# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of 8086 Addressing Modes

The 11 addressing modes are grouped into **four functional families**:

1. **Data / Operand Addressing Modes** (the bulk of them — 8 modes)
2. **Program Memory Addressing Modes** (used implicitly with IP)
3. **String Addressing Modes** (SI, DI implicit)
4. **I/O Port Addressing Modes** (Direct & Indirect)
5. **Implied / Implicit Addressing Mode** (no operand field, e.g., `CLC`, `STI`)

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Mode by Mode

### (A) Register Addressing Mode
- **Operands are CPU registers**, not memory.
- Fastest mode: no memory cycle is needed.
- Example: `MOV AX, BX`   →   copies the 16-bit contents of BX into AX.
- Example: `ADD AL, CL`   →   AL ← AL + CL.

> [!NOTE]
> Source and destination **must be of the same size** (e.g., 8-bit with 8-bit, 16-bit with 16-bit). This is a classic KTU pitfall.

### (B) Immediate Addressing Mode
- **Data is part of the instruction itself** (constant).
- Useful for loading constants and initializing registers.
- Example: `MOV CX, 2050H`  →  CX ← 2050H.
- Example: `ADD AL, 25H`   →  AL ← AL + 25H.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The immediate value can never be the **destination**. `MOV 2500H, AX` is **illegal** in 8086.

### (C) Direct (Absolute) Addressing Mode
- The instruction directly contains the **16-bit offset (effective address)** of the operand in memory.
- Example: `MOV AX, [2000H]`  →  AX ← word at physical address DS:2000H.
- Example: `MOV AL, [4500H]`.
- **Default Segment: Data Segment (DS).**

$$ \text{PA} = (\text{DS} \times 10\text{H}) + 2000\text{H} $$

### (D) Register Indirect Addressing Mode
- The instruction specifies a **register that holds the offset address** of the operand.
- The allowed pointer registers are: **BX, SI, DI** (and BP for stack access).
- Example: `MOV AX, [BX]`   →  offset = [BX].
- Example: `MOV AL, [SI]`.
- **Default Segment: DS** (for BX/SI/DI), **SS** (for BP).

### (E) Based Relative Addressing Mode
- The offset is computed as **[Base Register] + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement**.
- Base register: **BX** (default DS) or **BP** (default SS).
- Example: `MOV AX, [BX + 08H]`   →  EA = BX + 08H.
- Example: `MOV AL, [BP + 0500H]`  →  EA = BP + 0500H.

### (F) Indexed Addressing Mode
- The offset is computed as **[Index Register] + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement**.
- Index register: **SI** or **DI** (default DS).
- Example: `MOV AX, [SI + 04H]`.
- Example: `MOV CX, [DI + 1000H]`.
- **Commonly used for array access** with a known base offset.

### (G) Based Indexed Addressing Mode
- Offset = **[Base Register] + [Index Register]**.
- Example: `MOV AX, [BX + SI]`.
- Example: `MOV DX, [BP + DI]`.
- **Default Segment: DS** (if base is BX), **SS** (if base is BP).

### (H) Based Indexed with Displacement (Relative Based Indexed) Addressing Mode
- Offset = **[Base] + [Index] + 8/16-bit displacement**.
- The most powerful & most asked mode in KTU exams.
- Example: `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 06H]`.
- Example: `MOV CX, [BP + DI + 1200H]`.
- Used to access two-dimensional arrays and stack-frame-based local variables.

### (I) String Addressing Mode
- Used **implicitly** with string instructions (`MOVS`, `LODS`, `STOS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`).
- Source string → **DS:SI** (auto-increment/decrement per `DF` flag).
- Destination string → **ES:DI** (auto-increment/decrement per `DF` flag).
- Example: `MOVSB`  → moves 1 byte from [DS:SI] to [ES:DI], then SI±1, DI±1.

### (J) Implied (Implicit) Addressing Mode
- No explicit operand field; the opcode itself implies the operand.
- Example: `CLC` (clear carry flag), `STI` (set interrupt flag), `NOP`, `HLT`.

### (K) I/O Port Addressing Modes
- **Direct Port I/O:** `IN AL, 80H`  → fixed 8-bit port address (0–255).
- **Indirect Port I/O:** `IN AL, DX`  → DX holds 16-bit port address (0–65535), used when > 8-bit port ID is required.
- Same for `OUT`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **Effective Address (EA) formula** and the **default segment pair** for every mode. The exam is heavily numerical on this.

| # | Addressing Mode | Syntax | Effective Address (EA) | Default Segment | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Register | `reg` | N/A (data in reg) | None | `MOV AX, BX` |
| 2 | Immediate | `data` | N/A (data in instr) | None | `MOV AX, 2050H` |
| 3 | Direct | `[offset]` | `offset` | DS | `MOV AX, [2000H]` |
| 4 | Register Indirect | `[reg]` | `reg` | DS (BX/SI/DI), SS (BP) | `MOV AX, [BX]` |
| 5 | Based Relative | `[BX/BP + disp]` | `BX/BP + disp` | DS / SS | `MOV AX, [BX+4]` |
| 6 | Indexed | `[SI/DI + disp]` | `SI/DI + disp` | DS | `MOV AX, [SI+4]` |
| 7 | Based Indexed | `[BX/BP + SI/DI]` | `BX/BP + SI/DI` | DS / SS | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` |
| 8 | Based Indexed + Disp | `[BX/BP + SI/DI + disp]` | `BX/BP + SI/DI + disp` | DS / SS | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+4]` |
| 9 | String | implicit | SI (src), DI (dst) | DS, ES | `MOVSB` |
| 10 | Implied | none | none | none | `CLC` |
| 11 | I/O Port Direct | `IN AL, port8` | port in instruction | I/O space | `IN AL, 80H` |
| 12 | I/O Port Indirect | `IN AL, DX` | DX | I/O space | `IN AL, DX` |

### Universal Physical Address Formula

$$ \text{PA} = \text{Segment Register} \times 10\text{H} + \text{EA} $$

> Note: A 16-bit segment base is **shifted left by 4 bits** (i.e., multiplied by 16) before adding the offset. This is a frequent KTU 2-mark question.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Based Indexed with Displacement** is the workhorse for **structure access** in C compiled to 8086 assembly (e.g., a struct field offset is the displacement; pointer is the base; array index is the index).
- **Indexed mode** is what a C compiler lowers `a[i]` to: `MOV AX, [SI + i*size]`.
- **String addressing** is used in `memcpy`, `strcmp`, and `memset` style routines in DOS-era firmware.
- **I/O Indirect via DX** is mandatory in 8086 to access any port above 255 (used heavily in keyboard, PIC, and DMA programming).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Worked Examples — Physical Address Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *exact style* of numerical questions KTU asks. The trick is to **always identify the default segment first**, then compute the offset, then the PA.

### Example 1 — Direct Addressing
**Instruction:** `MOV AX, [2000H]` with `DS = 4000H`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset (EA)} &= 2000\text{H} \\
\text{Segment base} &= \text{DS} \times 10\text{H} = 4000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} = 40000\text{H} \\
\text{Physical Address (PA)} &= 40000\text{H} + 2000\text{H} = 42000\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The 16-bit word at memory location `42000H` (low byte at `42000H`, high byte at `42001H`) is loaded into AX.

### Example 2 — Register Indirect Addressing
**Instruction:** `MOV AX, [SI]` with `DS = 5000H`, `SI = 2050H`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{SI} = 2050\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= (\text{DS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{SI} = 50000\text{H} + 2050\text{H} = 52050\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 3 — Based Indexed with Displacement
**Instruction:** `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 06H]` with `DS = 6000H`, `BX = 1000H`, `SI = 2050H`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{BX} + \text{SI} + 06\text{H} \\
        &= 1000\text{H} + 2050\text{H} + 0006\text{H} \\
        &= 3056\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 60000\text{H} + 3056\text{H} = 63056\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 4 — Based Relative (using BP)
**Instruction:** `MOV AL, [BP + 0500H]` with `SS = 3000H`, `BP = 1010H`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{BP} + 0500\text{H} = 1010\text{H} + 0500\text{H} = 1510\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= (\text{SS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{EA} \\
        &= 30000\text{H} + 1510\text{H} = 31510\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the default segment switch:** When **BP** is used as the base register, the segment is **SS (Stack Segment)** by default, *not* DS. Forgetting this is a classic 1-mark loss.

### Example 5 — Indexed Addressing for Array
**Instruction:** `MOV AX, [SI + 1000H]` with `DS = 7000H`, `SI = 0500H`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{SI} + 1000\text{H} = 0500\text{H} + 1000\text{H} = 1500\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 70000\text{H} + 1500\text{H} = 71500\text{H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 I/O Port Addressing Worked Example

**Instruction:** `OUT 80H, AL` (Direct Port I/O)
- Port address = `80H` (fixed 8-bit).
- Data from AL is sent to port `0080H` of the **separate I/O address space** (not the memory address space).

**Instruction:** `OUT DX, AL` with `DX = 0300H` (Indirect Port I/O)
- Port address is in DX = `0300H` (16-bit, so ports 0–65535 accessible).
- Data from AL is sent to I/O port `0300H`.

## 3.3 The "Decoded-by-Decoded" Mode Recognition Table (Examiner's Style)

> [!TIP]
> A student should be able to look at a 3-character bracket and instantly name the mode.

| Bracket Pattern | Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| `[5000H]` | Direct | Bare 16-bit constant. |
| `[BX]` | Register Indirect | Single register inside. |
| `[BX + 4]` or `[4 + BX]` | Based Relative | Base + constant. |
| `[SI + 4]` | Indexed | Index + constant. |
| `[BX + SI]` | Based Indexed | Base + Index, no constant. |
| `[BX + SI + 4]` | Based Indexed + Disp | Base + Index + constant. |
| `[BP + DI + 2000H]` | Based Indexed + Disp (SS default) | BP forces SS. |

## 3.4 Size Mismatch Pitfall (Compulsory Conceptual)

The instruction `MOV AX, BL` is **illegal** because AX is 16-bit and BL is 8-bit. The assembler rejects the encoding. Similarly, `MOV [2000H], 5000H` is illegal because the immediate destination has no size hint — the assembler cannot infer whether the user meant byte or word. The correct form is `MOV WORD PTR [2000H], 5000H` or `MOV BYTE PTR [2000H], 50H`.

## 3.5 Default Segment Override Prefix (The "+2 Marks" Trick)

In 8086, you can **override** the default segment using a segment override prefix byte:
- `CS:`, `DS:`, `ES:`, `SS:` written before the operand.
- Example: `MOV AX, ES:[BX]` — operand at `ES × 10H + BX`, **not** `DS × 10H + BX`.
- **Cost:** 1 extra clock cycle and 1 extra byte in the instruction. **Benefit:** full control of data location. KTU loves this as a 2-mark sub-part.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree of 8086 Addressing Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[8086 Addressing Modes]:::root --> B[Data Operand Modes]:::branch
    A --> C[Program Memory Mode]:::branch
    A --> D[String Mode]:::branch
    A --> E[IO Port Modes]:::branch
    A --> F[Implied Mode]:::branch

    B --> B1[Register]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Immediate]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Direct]:::leaf
    B --> B4[Register Indirect]:::leaf
    B --> B5[Based Relative]:::leaf
    B --> B6[Indexed]:::leaf
    B --> B7[Based Indexed]:::leaf
    B --> B8[Based Indexed with Disp]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Relative IP]:::leaf

    D --> D1[DS SI and ES DI]:::leaf

    E --> E1[Direct Port 8 bit]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Indirect Port DX]:::leaf

    F --> F1[No Operand Field]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1a2f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#4a7ab5,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#a8c5e8,stroke:#4a7ab5,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — How the 8086 Computes the Effective Address

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch] --> B{Decode Opcode}
    B --> C[Identify Addressing Mode]
    C --> D{Any Memory Operand?}
    D -- No --> E[Execute in Registers Only]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute Effective Address EA]
    F --> G[Read Default Segment Register]
    G --> H["PA = Segment x 10H + EA"]
    H --> I[Read / Write Memory at PA]
    I --> J[Execute ALU Operation]
    J --> K[Update Flags if Applicable]
    K --> L[Fetch Next Instruction]
```

## 4.3 Memory Layout Diagram (Direct Addressing Example)

```
        Segment Base (DS)               Offset (EA)
        4000 0  H ───────────────┐     2000 H
                 │               │
                 ▼               ▼
  +────+────+────+────+────+────+────+────+
  │40000│40001│40002│ ... │42000│42001│42002│   (Physical Memory)
  +────+────+────+────+────+────+────+────+
                            ▲     ▲
                            │     │
                       Low Byte   High Byte
                       of AX      of AX
                       (42000H)   (42001H)
```

In the diagram above, the **logical address** `4000:2000` maps to the **physical address** `42000H`. The 16-bit word at that physical address is loaded into AX (little-endian: low byte first).

## 4.4 Block Architecture of EA Calculation (8086 Bus Interface Unit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIU[Bus Interface Unit]
        ID[Instruction Decoder]:::blk --> EU[EA Computation Logic]:::blk
        EU --> ADDR[20-bit Physical Address Bus]:::blk
        ADDR --> MEM[External Memory]:::ext
    end
    SEG[Segment Registers CS DS SS ES]:::blk --> EU
    OFF[Offset Registers BX BP SI DI IP]:::blk --> EU
    DISP[Displacement from Instruction]:::blk --> EU
    EU --> PA[PA Formula 10H Segment plus Offset]:::hl
    PA --> MEM

    classDef blk fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#000000
    classDef ext fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b08a00,color:#000000
    classDef hl fill:#f4b6b6,stroke:#a02020,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define addressing modes. List any six data-related addressing modes of the 8086 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Addressing modes* are the various methods by which the CPU specifies the operand(s) of a machine instruction — defining how the **Effective Address (EA)** of the data is determined.

Six data-related addressing modes of 8086:
1. **Register** addressing (`MOV AX, BX`)
2. **Immediate** addressing (`MOV AX, 5000H`)
3. **Direct** addressing (`MOV AX, [2000H]`)
4. **Register Indirect** addressing (`MOV AX, [BX]`)
5. **Indexed** addressing (`MOV AX, [SI + 4]`)
6. **Based Indexed with Displacement** addressing (`MOV AX, [BX + SI + 4]`)

*(+1 mark for definition, +1 mark for listing 6, +1 mark for one example each / or correct classification.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the role of the BP register in addressing modes? Why is the default segment for BP different from that of BX?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **BP (Base Pointer)** register is primarily used to access data within the **current stack frame** — typically function parameters and local variables. It is almost always paired with a displacement or an index register (e.g., `[BP + 4]`, `[BP + SI + 6]`).

The **default segment** for BP is the **Stack Segment (SS)**, while BX defaults to **DS**. This is by design: BP is meant to index into the stack, and the stack lives in the SS. Using BX, the programmer usually indexes into the data segment (DS).

*(+1 mark role, +1 mark default segment, +1 mark design rationale.)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]`

**(a) Explain the following addressing modes of 8086 with one example each:**
**(i) Register Indirect (ii) Based Indexed (iii) Based Indexed with Displacement (7 marks)**

**(b) For the instruction `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 0200H]`, with `DS = 2000H`, `BX = 0500H`, `SI = 1030H`, calculate the Effective Address (EA) and the Physical Address (PA). (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Register Indirect Addressing Mode (≈ 2.3 marks)**
The instruction specifies a **register that holds the effective address** of the operand. Allowed pointer registers: **BX, SI, DI** (default DS) and **BP** (default SS).
- *Example:* `MOV AX, [SI]`  → AX ← word at memory location whose offset is the content of SI, in segment DS.
- *EA formula:* $\text{EA} = \text{SI}$

**(ii) Based Indexed Addressing Mode (≈ 2.3 marks)**
The effective address is the **sum of a base register and an index register**.
- *Example:* `MOV AX, [BX + DI]`.
- *EA formula:* $\text{EA} = \text{BX} + \text{DI}$
- *Default segment:* DS (because BX is the base).

**(iii) Based Indexed with Displacement Mode (≈ 2.4 marks)**
EA = base register + index register + 8-bit or 16-bit displacement. The most flexible mode — supports 2D arrays and stack frames.
- *Example:* `MOV AX, [BP + SI + 08H]`.
- *EA formula:* $\text{EA} = \text{BP} + \text{SI} + 08\text{H}$
- *Default segment:* SS (because BP is base).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

Given: `DS = 2000H`, `BX = 0500H`, `SI = 1030H`, instruction `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 0200H]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{BX} + \text{SI} + 0200\text{H} \\
         &= 0500\text{H} + 1030\text{H} + 0200\text{H} \\
         &= 1730\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= (\text{DS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{EA} \\
         &= 20000\text{H} + 1730\text{H} \\
         &= 21730\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step marking scheme:**
| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Identifying the mode (Based Indexed + Disp) | 1 |
| Correct EA addition (with all three terms) | 2 |
| Correct segment base = DS × 10H = 20000H | 1 |
| Final PA = 21730H | 2 |
| Units / Hex convention | 1 |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the I/O port addressing modes of 8086 with examples. Compare direct and indirect port addressing. (7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the physical address generated by the following instruction: `MOV AL, [BP + 0500H]` given `SS = 5000H` and `BP = 1010H`. Also, briefly state why BP uses SS and not DS by default. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The 8086 has a **separate 16-bit I/O address space** (65536 ports). It supports two I/O addressing modes:

**1. Direct Port Addressing (≈ 3 marks)**
- The 8-bit port address is part of the instruction itself.
- Range: **00H to FFH** (0–255) only.
- *Syntax:* `IN accumulator, port8` or `OUT port8, accumulator`
- *Example:* `IN AL, 80H`  → reads a byte from I/O port 0080H into AL.
- *Example:* `OUT 20H, AL`  → sends AL's content to I/O port 0020H.

**2. Indirect Port Addressing (≈ 3 marks)**
- The 16-bit port address must be loaded into the **DX** register first.
- Range: **0000H to FFFFH** (0–65535).
- *Syntax:* `IN accumulator, DX` or `OUT DX, accumulator`
- *Example:*
  ```
  MOV DX, 0300H
  IN  AL, DX        ; reads from port 0300H
  ```

**Comparison Table (≈ 1 mark):**

| Feature | Direct Port | Indirect Port |
|---|---|---|
| Port size | 8-bit | 16-bit |
| Range | 0–255 | 0–65535 |
| Register used | None (port in instr) | DX (holds address) |
| Instruction size | 2 bytes (fixed) | 1 byte (varies w/ DX pre-load) |
| Typical use | Simple devices (e.g., 8255) | Wider systems (PIC, DMA, HDD) |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

Given: `SS = 5000H`, `BP = 1010H`, instruction `MOV AL, [BP + 0500H]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= \text{BP} + 0500\text{H} = 1010\text{H} + 0500\text{H} = 1510\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= (\text{SS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{EA} \\
         &= 50000\text{H} + 1510\text{H} = 51510\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mark split:**
- Correct identification of default segment = SS: **1 mark**
- EA calculation: **2 marks**
- Segment base = 50000H: **1 mark**
- Final PA = 51510H: **2 marks**
- Reason BP uses SS: **1 mark** (BP is the stack-frame pointer; the stack lives in SS by design. This avoids the programmer having to write an SS: override prefix on every stack access.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Using DS instead of SS** when BP is involved (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting the ×10H shift** on the segment base (treats it as 16-bit addition, loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Mixing up displacement width** — 8-bit disp (e.g., `04H`) vs 16-bit disp (`0200H`). They behave identically in EA computation but differ in instruction length.
> 4. **Treating `IN/OUT` port addresses as memory addresses** — they live in a *separate* I/O address space, not in main memory.
> 5. **Confusing *Register Indirect* with *Direct*** — `[BX]` is Register Indirect (register *holds* the address); `[2000H]` is Direct (address is the *constant* itself).
> 6. **Forgetting the `DF` flag's role** in string addressing (auto-increment vs auto-decrement).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total modes in 8086: 11** (often quoted as 8 data modes + 1 string + 1 implied + 2 I/O, or just "around 11" in syllabus notes).
- **The Universal Physical Address Formula:** $\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \times 10\text{H} + \text{EA}$ — *always*.
- **Default Segments Quick-Recall:**
  * `[BX], [SI], [DI], [BX+SI], [BX+DI], [disp]` → **DS**
  * `[BP], [BP+SI], [BP+DI], [BP+disp]` → **SS**
  * `[BP+BX+disp]` is illegal (two bases) — **no such mode**.
- **Pointer Register Rules:** Only **BX, BP, SI, DI** can appear inside `[ ... ]` for memory operands. **CX, DX, AX** are *not* allowed in memory pointer positions in 8086.
- **String Mode Magic:** Always uses `DS:SI` (source) and `ES:DI` (destination), auto-updated by `DF`.
- **Segment Override Prefix:** Can change default (e.g., `ES:[BX]` to access BX in ES). Costs 1 byte + 4 clocks.
- **I/O Port Direct:** 8-bit, embedded in instruction.
- **I/O Port Indirect (DX):** 16-bit, required for ports > 255.
- **Highest exam frequency:** Numerical PA calculations (≈ 7 marks) + theory (≈ 7 marks) = full 14-mark question coverage.
- **Watch out for the "address" units** — offsets are 16-bit (0–65535), physical addresses are 20-bit (0–1,048,575 = 1 MB), and I/O ports are 16-bit (0–65535) in their *own* space.
- **Practice 3–4 numerical problems** before the exam — the formula is identical for all modes; only the EA composition changes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set and Assembler Directives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set and Assembler Directives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Instruction Set: The Vocabulary of the Microprocessor

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instruction Set:** The complete collection of binary-coded machine-level commands that a microprocessor (such as the **Intel 8086**) is hard-wired to recognize, decode, and execute. It defines the functional capability, data types, registers, memory addressing modes, and the complete repertoire of operations a programmer can invoke through assembly or machine language.

The **8086 instruction set** is broadly classified into the following functional groups:

| Sl. No | Category | Typical Operation |
|:------:|:---------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Data Transfer Instructions** | Move data between registers, memory, and I/O |
| 2 | **Arithmetic Instructions** | Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division |
| 3 | **Bit Manipulation (Logical) Instructions** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shift, rotate |
| 4 | **String Instructions** | Operate on byte/word strings |
| 5 | **Control Transfer (Branch) Instructions** | Jumps, loops, calls, interrupts |
| 6 | **Processor Control Instructions** | Flags manipulation, HLT, NOP, WAIT |

### Conceptual Analogy — Think Like a Chef 👨‍🍳

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** If the **CPU is a chef**, the **Instruction Set is the chef's recipe book**. Each *recipe* (instruction) tells the chef *what ingredient* (operand/data) to use, *from where* to fetch it (addressing mode), and *what action* to perform (operation). The chef cannot invent a new recipe on the fly — the recipes are hard-wired into memory. Similarly, the CPU can only execute operations that exist in its instruction set. **Assembler Directives** are like the *head chef's kitchen notes* — they don't cook anything, but they tell the kitchen *where* the ingredients are stored, *how big* the containers are, and *how* to organize the pantry for the chef to work efficiently.

---

## 1.2 Assembler Directives: The Silent Helpers

> [!NOTE]
> **Assembler Directives** (also called *pseudo-instructions* or *pseudo-ops*) are special commands written to the **assembler**, not the microprocessor. They are **not converted into machine code** at runtime. Instead, they guide the assembler during the translation of source code (.ASM file) into object code (.OBJ file) — for example, by defining data, reserving memory, organizing segments, or declaring procedures.

### Why Do We Need Directives?

- The CPU operates on **binary**, but humans write in **mnemonics**.
- The assembler must know:
  - *Where* in memory to place code and data
  - *How much* memory to allocate for variables
  - *Which* segment registers to use
  - *Where* a procedure starts and ends
- **Directives answer these questions — not the CPU.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **instructions** (MOV, ADD) with **directives** (DB, EQU). Remember: **Instructions generate machine code; directives do not.** This is a frequent 1-mark trap in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 Instruction Format (8086 Machine Code Anatomy)

An 8086 instruction is variable in length (1 to 6 bytes). Its general structure is:

| Bytes | Field | Purpose |
|:-----:|:------|:--------|
| Byte 0–1 | **Opcode + Direction (D) / Word (W) bit** | Specifies the operation and operand size |
| Byte 1–2 | **MOD-REG-R/M** byte | Specifies addressing mode and registers |
| Byte 2–4 | **Displacement** | Optional 8-bit or 16-bit address offset |
| Byte 4–6 | **Immediate data** | Optional 8-bit or 16-bit constant |

The first byte is the **opcode byte**:

$$\text{Opcode Byte} = \underbrace{\text{OPCODE}}_{6 \text{ bits}} \; \underbrace{D}_{1 \text{ bit}} \; \underbrace{W}_{1 \text{ bit}}$$

Where:
- **OPCODE (6 bits)**: Defines the operation (e.g., 100010 for MOV)
- **D (1 bit)**: Direction — `0` = REG is source, `1` = REG is destination
- **W (1 bit)**: Width — `0` = byte operation, `1` = word operation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of 8086 Instruction Set

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Format | Operation |
|:--------:|:--------|:-------|:----------|
| **MOV** | Move | MOV dst, src | `dst ← src` |
| **PUSH** | Push to stack | PUSH src | `SP ← SP - 2; [SS:SP] ← src` |
| **POP** | Pop from stack | POP dst | `dst ← [SS:SP]; SP ← SP + 2` |
| **XCHG** | Exchange | XCHG op1, op2 | `op1 ↔ op2` |
| **IN** | Input from port | IN acc, port | `acc ← [port]` |
| **OUT** | Output to port | OUT port, acc | `[port] ← acc` |
| **LEA** | Load effective address | LEA reg, mem | `reg ← offset of mem` |
| **LDS** | Load DS & register | LDS reg, mem | `reg ← [mem]; DS ← [mem+2]` |
| **LES** | Load ES & register | LES reg, mem | `reg ← [mem]; ES ← [mem+2]` |
| **LAHF** | Load AH from flags | LAHF | `AH ← FLAGS` |
| **SAHF** | Store AH to flags | SAHF | `FLAGS ← AH` |
| **PUSHF** | Push flags | PUSHF | `SP ← SP - 2; [SP] ← FLAGS` |
| **POPF** | Pop flags | POPF | `FLAGS ← [SP]; SP ← SP + 2` |
| **XLAT** | Translate byte | XLAT | `AL ← [BX + AL]` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** `MOV` is the most tested instruction. Note that **MOV CS, reg** and **MOV mem/reg, imm-to-segment-register** are **illegal**. The **XCHG** instruction cannot exchange two memory operands directly.

---

### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions

| Category | Instructions | Operation |
|:---------|:-------------|:----------|
| **Addition** | ADD, ADC, INC, AAA, DAA, CBW | Adds binary or BCD |
| **Subtraction** | SUB, SBB, DEC, NEG, CMP, AAS, DAS, CWD | Subtracts, compares, negates |
| **Multiplication** | MUL, IMUL, AAM | Unsigned/signed multiply |
| **Division** | DIV, IDIV, AAD | Unsigned/signed divide |

#### Key Formulas (KTU High-Yield)

| Operation | Byte Form | Word Form | Description |
|:---------:|:---------:|:---------:|:------------|
| **ADD** | `ADD reg8/mem8, reg8/imm8` | `ADD reg16/mem16, reg16/imm16` | `dst ← dst + src` |
| **ADC** | Same as ADD | Same as ADD | `dst ← dst + src + CF` |
| **SUB** | Same as ADD | Same as ADD | `dst ← dst - src` |
| **SBB** | Same as ADD | Same as ADD | `dst ← dst - src - CF` |
| **CMP** | `CMP reg8/mem8, reg8` | `CMP reg16/mem16, reg16` | Performs `dst - src`, sets flags |
| **MUL** | `MUL r/m8` | `MUL r/m16` | `AX ← AL × r/m8` (Byte) or `DX:AX ← AX × r/m16` (Word) |
| **DIV** | `DIV r/m8` | `DIV r/m16` | `AL ← AX / r/m8; AH ← AX mod r/m8` (Byte) |

> [!NOTE]
> **MUL Byte Result Length:** 8 bits → 16 bits (AX). **MUL Word Result Length:** 16 bits → 32 bits (DX:AX). This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU ESE.

---

### 2.1.3 Bit Manipulation (Logical) Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Format |
|:--------:|:----------|:-------|
| **AND** | Bitwise AND | `AND dst, src` |
| **OR** | Bitwise OR | `OR dst, src` |
| **XOR** | Bitwise XOR | `XOR dst, src` |
| **NOT** | One's complement | `NOT dst` (single operand) |
| **TEST** | Logical compare (AND without storing) | `TEST op1, op2` |
| **SHL/SAL** | Shift left logical/arithmetic | `SHL dst, count` |
| **SHR** | Shift right logical | `SHR dst, count` |
| **SAR** | Shift right arithmetic | `SAR dst, count` |
| **ROL** | Rotate left | `ROL dst, count` |
| **ROR** | Rotate right | `ROR dst, count` |
| **RCL** | Rotate left through carry | `RCL dst, count` |
| **RCR** | Rotate right through carry | `RCR dst, count` |

#### Shift/Rotate Operation Logic

For a **1-bit left shift** on a byte operand:

$$B_7 \; B_6 \; B_5 \; B_4 \; B_3 \; B_2 \; B_1 \; B_0 \rightarrow CF \; B_7 \; B_6 \; B_5 \; B_4 \; B_3 \; B_2 \; B_1 \; B_0 \; 0$$

The bit shifted out goes into the **Carry Flag (CF)**, and a `0` enters from the right.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A left shift by `n` bits is equivalent to **multiplication by $2^n$**, and a right shift by `n` bits is equivalent to **division by $2^n$** (for unsigned numbers). This frequently appears in numerical problems.

---

### 2.1.4 String Instructions (String Primitives)

8086 has five primitive string operations. Each uses **SI** (source index) and **DI** (destination index) and auto-increments/decrements based on the **Direction Flag (DF)**.

| Instruction | Meaning | Operation |
|:------------|:--------|:----------|
| **MOVS** | Move string | `ES:DI ← DS:SI` |
| **CMPS** | Compare string | `DS:SI - ES:DI`, sets flags |
| **SCAS** | Scan string | `AL/AX - ES:DI`, sets flags |
| **LODS** | Load string | `AL/AX ← DS:SI` |
| **STOS** | Store string | `ES:DI ← AL/AX` |

Each of these must be prefixed with:
- **REP** (REPeat while CX ≠ 0)
- **REPE/REPZ** (Repeat while equal/zero)
- **REPNE/REPNZ** (Repeat while not equal/not zero)

---

### 2.1.5 Control Transfer (Branch) Instructions

| Type | Instructions | Description |
|:-----|:-------------|:------------|
| **Unconditional** | JMP, CALL, RET, INT n, IRET | Always alter control flow |
| **Conditional** | Jcc (JE, JNE, JG, JL, JGE, JLE, JA, JB, etc.) | Jump only if condition is true |
| **Iteration** | LOOP, LOOPE, LOOPNE | Loop while CX ≠ 0 and condition met |

#### Conditional Jump Distance Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula:** A conditional jump is encoded as a **2-byte instruction** (short jump) with a **relative displacement of 8 bits (signed)**. The reachable range is:
> $$\text{Range} = -128 \; \text{to} \; +127 \; \text{bytes from the next instruction}$$
> 
> The effective address is calculated as:
> $$IP_{new} = IP_{current} + \underbrace{\text{Displacement}}_{\text{signed 8-bit}} + 2$$
> 
> If the displacement does not fit in 8 bits, the assembler automatically generates a **NEAR jump** (3 bytes with 16-bit displacement).

#### Common Conditional Jump Mnemonics

| Mnemonic | Condition Tested | Signed/Unsigned |
|:--------:|:-----------------|:----------------|
| **JE / JZ** | ZF = 1 | Either |
| **JNE / JNZ** | ZF = 0 | Either |
| **JG / JNLE** | ZF=0 and SF=OF | Signed |
| **JL / JNGE** | SF ≠ OF | Signed |
| **JGE / JNL** | SF = OF | Signed |
| **JA / JNBE** | CF=0 and ZF=0 | Unsigned |
| **JB / JNAE** | CF = 1 | Unsigned |
| **JBE / JNA** | CF=1 or ZF=1 | Unsigned |

---

### 2.1.6 Processor Control Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|:--------:|:----------|
| **STC** | Set Carry Flag (CF ← 1) |
| **CLC** | Clear Carry Flag (CF ← 0) |
| **CMC** | Complement Carry Flag (CF ← NOT CF) |
| **STD** | Set Direction Flag (DF ← 1) |
| **CLD** | Clear Direction Flag (DF ← 0) |
| **STI** | Set Interrupt Flag (IF ← 1) |
| **CLI** | Clear Interrupt Flag (IF ← 0) |
| **NOP** | No operation (consumes 1 byte, 4 clock cycles) |
| **HLT** | Halt the processor |
| **WAIT** | Wait for TEST pin to go low |
| **ESC** | Escape to external coprocessor |
| **LOCK** | Bus lock prefix |

---

## 2.2 Comprehensive Reference of Assembler Directives

### 2.2.1 Data Definition Directives

| Directive | Purpose | Bytes per Item | Example |
|:---------:|:--------|:---------------:|:-------:|
| **DB** (Define Byte) | Allocates 1 byte per item | **1** | `DATA DB 25H, 'A', ?` |
| **DW** (Define Word) | Allocates 2 bytes per item | **2** | `WORD DW 1234H, 56H` |
| **DD** (Define Doubleword) | Allocates 4 bytes per item | **4** | `DWORD DD 12345678H` |
| **DQ** (Define Quadword) | Allocates 8 bytes per item | **8** | `QUAD DQ 1122334455667788H` |
| **DT** (Define Tenbytes) | Allocates 10 bytes per item | **10** | `BCD_VAL DT 1234567890H` |

> [!NOTE]
> The `?` symbol inside a data directive tells the assembler to **reserve memory but leave the content uninitialized**. Example: `BUFFER DB 100 DUP(?)` reserves 100 bytes of uninitialized space.

#### DUP Operator — The Memory Allocator

| Syntax | Meaning |
|:-------|:--------|
| `DB 5 DUP(0)` | 5 bytes, all initialized to 0 |
| `DW 10 DUP(?)` | 10 words (20 bytes), uninitialized |
| `DB 3 DUP(1, 2)` | Repeats pattern (1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2) — 6 bytes total |
| `DB 'HELLO'` | 5 bytes: ASCII codes for H, E, L, L, O |

---

### 2.2.2 Segment Definition Directives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite — The SEGMENT Model:** The 8086 memory model uses four logical segments. Each is defined using `SEGMENT ... ENDS`.

```asm
DATA_SEG    SEGMENT
            ; data variables declared here
DATA_SEG    ENDS

CODE_SEG    SEGMENT
            ASSUME CS:CODE_SEG, DS:DATA_SEG
START:      ; code starts here
CODE_SEG    ENDS
            END START
```

| Directive | Function |
|:---------:|:---------|
| **SEGMENT** | Begins a logical segment |
| **ENDS** | Ends a segment |
| **ASSUME** | Tells the assembler which segment register addresses which segment |
| **END** | Marks end of the entire source program; optional label specifies entry point |

---

### 2.2.3 Equate and Assignment Directives

| Directive | Function | Reassignment? | Example |
|:---------:|:---------|:--------------|:-------:|
| **EQU** | Assigns a constant or symbol | **No** (immutable) | `PORT equ 60H` |
| **= (=)** | Assigns a numeric value | **Yes** (redefinable) | `COUNT = 5` |
| **LABEL** | Assigns a name + size to current location | — | `NEXT_BYTE LABEL BYTE` |

---

### 2.2.4 Address Manipulation Directives

| Directive | Meaning | Example |
|:---------:|:--------|:--------|
| **OFFSET** | Returns the 16-bit displacement of a label/data | `MOV BX, OFFSET ARRAY` |
| **PTR** | Overrides default operand size (BYTE/WORD/DWORD) | `MOV BYTE PTR [BX], 5` |
| **SEG** | Returns the segment address of a label | `MOV AX, SEG DATA` |
| **TYPE** | Returns data size in bytes | `MOV CX, TYPE ARRAY` |
| **LENGTH** | Returns number of elements in a DUP | `MOV CX, LENGTH ARRAY` |
| **SIZE** | Returns total bytes (`LENGTH × TYPE`) | `MOV CX, SIZE ARRAY` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** `OFFSET` ≠ `LEA`. While both fetch the address of a label, `OFFSET` is an **assembler-time** calculation (constant), whereas `LEA` is a **run-time** instruction that can compute the address of an *expression* like `[BX+SI+10]`.

---

### 2.2.5 Procedure and Module Directives

| Directive | Function |
|:---------:|:---------|
| **PROC** | Begins a named procedure (subroutine) |
| **ENDP** | Ends a procedure |
| **NEAR** | Called within the same segment (intra-segment) |
| **FAR** | Called from a different segment (inter-segment) |
| **PUBLIC** | Declares symbols visible to other modules |
| **EXTERN** | Declares symbols defined in other modules |
| **INCLUDE** | Inserts contents of another source file |
| **NAME** | Assigns a name to the module |
| **TITLE** | Sets a title text for the listing file |
| **GROUP** | Groups several segments under one name |
| **ORG** | Sets the assembler location counter to a specific address |
| **EVEN** | Aligns the next data on an even address |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|:--------|:-------------------|:------------|
| **8086 Address Calculation (Physical)** | $PA = \text{Segment} \times 10H + \text{Offset}$ | Hexadecimal |
| **Segment Range** | 64 KB per segment | $2^{16}$ bytes |
| **Total Addressable Memory (8086)** | $1{,}048{,}576$ bytes = 1 MB | $2^{20}$ bytes |
| **Stack Pointer Change (PUSH)** | $SP_{new} = SP - 2$ | Word operation |
| **Stack Pointer Change (POP)** | $SP_{new} = SP + 2$ | Word operation |
| **MUL Byte result location** | $AX = AL \times r/m8$ | 16-bit result |
| **MUL Word result location** | $DX:AX = AX \times r/m16$ | 32-bit result |
| **DIV Byte operands** | $AL = \text{quotient}, AH = \text{remainder}$ | — |
| **Short Jump Range** | $-128 \text{ to } +127$ bytes | Signed 8-bit displacement |
| **Near Jump Range** | $\pm 32 \text{ KB}$ | Signed 16-bit displacement |
| **Shift Left by n = multiply by** | $2^n$ | — |
| **Shift Right by n = divide by** | $2^n$ | Unsigned only |
| **CS cannot be the destination of MOV** | Hardware restriction | KTU favorite |
| **REP prefix effect** | Decrements CX, repeats while condition true | Per iteration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Instruction sets form the **ISA (Instruction Set Architecture)** contract between hardware and software — the same role that **RISC-V**, **ARM**, and **x86-64** play in modern CPUs. Assembler directives are the spiritual ancestors of **modern linker scripts** and **section pragmas** in C compilers (`#pragma data_seg`, `.section .data`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address Calculation in 8086

The 8086 has a **20-bit physical address bus**, but its registers are only **16 bits wide**. To generate a 20-bit physical address, the **BIU (Bus Interface Unit)** uses the following scheme:

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Concept Setup**

A logical address is specified as `Segment : Offset` (e.g., `2000H : 1234H`).

**Step 2 — Segment Register Interpretation**

The segment register is treated as the **base address** of a 64 KB region. The CPU left-shifts the segment value by **4 bits** (equivalent to multiplying by $16_{10}$ or $10H$):

$$\text{Segment} \times 10H = \text{Segment} \ll 4$$

**Step 3 — Add the Offset**

The 16-bit offset is added to the shifted segment base to produce the 20-bit physical address:

$$PA = (\text{Segment Register} \times 10H) + \text{Effective Offset}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example**

Compute the physical address of `2000H : 1234H`:

$$PA = 2000H \times 10H + 1234H$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= 20000H + 1234H \\
   &= 21234H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification**

We can verify: $2000H \times 16 = 8192 \times 16 = 131072 = 20000H$. Adding offset $1234H = 4660$ gives $131072 + 4660 = 135732 = 21234H$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** The offset can range from $0000H$ to $FFFFH$ (0 to 65,535), allowing each segment to span **64 KB** in either direction. The same physical address can be represented by **4096 different** `Segment:Offset` pairs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Encoding of a MOV Instruction

Let us encode the instruction: `MOV AL, 0BH`  (move immediate byte to AL).

### Step-by-Step Encoding

**Step 1 — Identify the Operation**

`MOV` to an 8-bit register from immediate data falls in the opcode group. The 6-bit opcode is `101100` (binary) for `MOV reg, imm`.

**Step 2 — Determine the D and W Bits**

- We are moving a **byte (8-bit)** → **W = 0**
- The destination is a **register** → **D = 1**

**Step 3 — Identify the Register Code (REG field)**

Register encoding (3 bits):

| Code | 8-bit (W=0) | 16-bit (W=1) |
|:----:|:-----------:|:------------:|
| 000 | AL | AX |
| 001 | CL | CX |
| 010 | DL | DX |
| 011 | BL | BX |
| 100 | AH | SP |
| 101 | CH | BP |
| 110 | DH | SI |
| 111 | BH | DI |

For `AL`, the code is **000**.

**Step 4 — Build the Second Byte (MOD-REG-R/M)**

For immediate-to-register, the **MOD = 11** (register mode), REG = 000, R/M = 000:

$$\text{Byte 2} = \underbrace{11}_{\text{MOD}} \; \underbrace{000}_{\text{REG}} \; \underbrace{000}_{\text{R/M}} = 11000000 = C0H$$

**Step 5 — Append the Immediate Data**

The immediate is `0BH` → one byte.

**Step 6 — Final Machine Code**

$$10110000 \;\; 11000000 \;\; 00001011 = B0 \; C0 \; 0B$$

```
B0 C0 0B        ; MOV AL, 0BH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When a `MOV` to accumulator (AX/AL) uses an immediate, the opcode is a special 1-byte form (**B0–B7** for `MOV AL, imm8` and **B8–BF** for `MOV AX, imm16`). This is more efficient than the general `MOV reg, imm` form (which is **3 bytes**). Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Flags After a CMP Instruction

Given: `CMP AX, BX` where `AX = 0x1234` and `BX = 0x1235`. Determine the flag values.

**Step 1 — CMP is a Non-Storing SUB**

The CPU internally performs `AX - BX` and discards the result, setting flags only.

**Step 2 — Perform the Subtraction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AX - BX &= 1234H - 1235H \\
        &= 1234H + \overline{1235H} + 1 \\
        &= 1234H + (1235H \; \text{XOR} \; FFFFH) + 1 \\
        &= 1234H + EDCAH + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1234H + EDCAH &= 0xFFFFE \; \text{(21-bit, only 16 bits kept)} \\
              &= FFFEH \\
\text{FFF EH} + 1 &= FFFFH
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine Each Flag**

| Flag | Rule | Result | Value |
|:-----|:-----|:-------|:------|
| **CF** (Carry) | Borrow occurred | `1234H < 1235H` | **CF = 1** |
| **ZF** (Zero) | Result = 0 | `FFFFH ≠ 0` | **ZF = 0** |
| **SF** (Sign) | MSB of result = 1 | `F` (MSB=1) | **SF = 1** |
| **OF** (Overflow) | Signed overflow into sign bit | No wrap | **OF = 0** |
| **PF** (Parity) | Even parity of LSB byte? | `F` → 4 ones (even) | **PF = 1** |
| **AF** (Auxiliary) | Borrow from bit 4 | Yes | **AF = 1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Question:** Given two numbers and a CMP, list all six flags. The **JA** (jump if above) will be false because `CF=1 OR ZF=1` would be required to be FALSE for JA — but `CF=1` makes it false. Hence, `AX > BX` (unsigned) is **false**, which is correct since $1234H < 1235H$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Encoding Reference

### 3.4.1 Conditional Jump Encoding Logic

| Instruction | Opcode (Hex) | Condition | Length |
|:------------|:------------:|:----------|:------:|
| **JE / JZ** | `74` (short) | ZF = 1 | 2 bytes |
| **JNE / JNZ** | `75` (short) | ZF = 0 | 2 bytes |
| **JC / JB / JNAE** | `72` | CF = 1 | 2 bytes |
| **JNC / JAE / JNB** | `73` | CF = 0 | 2 bytes |
| **JS** | `78` | SF = 1 | 2 bytes |
| **JNS** | `79` | SF = 0 | 2 bytes |
| **JO** | `70` | OF = 1 | 2 bytes |
| **JNO** | `71` | OF = 0 | 2 bytes |
| **JP / JPE** | `7A` | PF = 1 | 2 bytes |
| **JNP / JPO** | `7B` | PF = 0 | 2 bytes |

### 3.4.2 Assembler Directive Memory Layout Analysis

Consider the following data block:

```asm
DATA SEGMENT
    A   DB  10H, 20H
    B   DW  1234H
    C   DD  12345678H
    D   DB  'HELLO'
    E   DW  5 DUP(0)
DATA ENDS
```

**Memory Map Derivation:**

| Symbol | Offset (Hex) | Content (Bytes) | Size (Bytes) | Type |
|:------:|:------------:|:----------------|:------------:|:-----|
| A | 0000 | `10 20` | 2 | Byte × 2 |
| B | 0002 | `34 12` | 2 | Word (little-endian: low byte first) |
| C | 0004 | `78 56 34 12` | 4 | Doubleword (little-endian) |
| D | 0008 | `48 45 4C 4C 4F` | 5 | ASCII of "HELLO" |
| E | 000D | `00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00` | 10 | 5 words of zero |
| **Next free** | 0017 | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little-Endian Rule:** The 8086 stores the **least significant byte** at the **lower address** and the **most significant byte** at the higher address. This is a 2-mark favorite in KTU.

### Step-by-Step Verification of the Little-Endian Storage

For `B DW 1234H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low byte of } 1234H &= 34H \quad \rightarrow \text{stored at offset 0002H} \\
\text{High byte of } 1234H &= 12H \quad \rightarrow \text{stored at offset 0003H}
\end{aligned}
$$

For `C DD 12345678H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0 (LSB)} &= 78H \rightarrow 0004H \\
\text{Byte 1} &= 56H \rightarrow 0005H \\
\text{Byte 2} &= 34H \rightarrow 0006H \\
\text{Byte 3 (MSB)} &= 12H \rightarrow 0007H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Little-Endian?** The 8086 was designed so that lower-order bytes could be fetched first when starting from the low address — a micro-optimization for 8-bit bus accesses of 16-bit data.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example: Stack Operation

```asm
PUSH AX       ; Assume AX = 1234H, SP = 2000H
PUSH BX       ; Assume BX = 5678H
POP CX        ; After this, CX = ?
```

**Step-by-Step Stack Simulation:**

**Step 1 — `PUSH AX` (AX = 1234H)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{new} &= SP - 2 = 2000H - 2 = 1FFEH \\
[SS:1FFEH] &= AL = 34H \quad (\text{low byte}) \\
[SS:1FFFH] &= AH = 12H \quad (\text{high byte})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — `PUSH BX` (BX = 5678H)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP_{new} &= 1FFEH - 2 = 1FFCH \\
[SS:1FFCH] &= BL = 78H \\
[SS:1FFDH] &= BH = 56H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — `POP CX`**

The top of stack is at `1FFCH` (current SP):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CL &\leftarrow [SS:1FFCH] = 78H \\
CH &\leftarrow [SS:1FFDH] = 56H \\
SP_{new} &= 1FFCH + 2 = 1FFEH \\
\therefore CX &= 5678H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LIFO Rule:** Stack follows **LIFO (Last-In First-Out)**. The last item pushed is the first item popped. After two `PUSH` and one `POP`, the value retrieved is the value of **BX**, not AX.

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: 8086 Instruction Execution Flow

This diagram illustrates the **complete instruction lifecycle** in the 8086, showing how instructions flow from memory through the BIU and EU to execution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MEMORY Unit<br/>Instruction Queue<br/>6 bytes] --> B[BIU Bus Interface Unit]
    B --> C[Instruction Queue FIFO<br/>6-byte pre-fetch buffer]
    C --> D[Instruction Decoder<br/>Parses opcode bytes]
    D --> E{Instruction Type?}
    E -->|Data Transfer| F[MOV PUSH POP XCHG<br/>LDS LES LEA]
    E -->|Arithmetic| G[ADD SUB MUL DIV<br/>INC DEC CMP]
    E -->|Logical Shift| H[AND OR XOR NOT<br/>SHL SHR ROL ROR]
    E -->|Control| I[JMP CALL RET<br/>Jcc LOOP INT]
    E -->|String| J[MOVS CMPS SCAS<br/>LODS STOS + REP]
    F --> K[EU Execution Unit<br/>ALU + Registers + Flags]
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Flag Register<br/>CF ZF SF OF PF AF]
    K --> M[General Registers<br/>AX BX CX DX]
    K --> N[Result written back<br/>to Register or Memory]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style N fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Assembler Translation Process

This diagram shows how **assembler directives** influence the translation pipeline from `.ASM` source to `.EXE` executable.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source File<br/>.ASM] --> T1[Pass 1<br/>Build Symbol Table]
    T1 --> ST[(Symbol Table<br/>Labels + Addresses)]
    T1 --> T2[Pass 2<br/>Generate Object Code]
    T2 --> OBJ[Object File<br/>.OBJ]
    ST --> T2
    DIR[Assembler Directives<br/>DB DW DD EQU SEGMENT<br/>ENDS ASSUME ORG] -.-> T1
    DIR -.-> T2
    OBJ --> LINK[Linker]
    LIB[Library Files<br/>.LIB] --> LINK
    LINK --> EXE[Executable File<br/>.EXE]

    style SRC fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style T2 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ST fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OBJ fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DIR fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LINK fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EXE fill:#385723,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that **directives** are processed in *both passes*. In **Pass 1**, directives like `EQU`, `DB`, `DW` help build the symbol table. In **Pass 2**, they determine code generation. This is why directives are sometimes called **meta-instructions**.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Conditional Jump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to compare two values] --> Q1{Signed or<br/>Unsigned?}
    Q1 -->|Signed| Q2{First value<br/>greater?}
    Q1 -->|Unsigned| Q3{First value<br/>greater?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| JG[Use JG<br/>Jump if Greater]
    Q2 -->|No| Q2B{Equal?}
    Q2B -->|Yes| JE[Use JE<br/>Jump if Equal]
    Q2B -->|No| JL[Use JL<br/>Jump if Less]

    Q3 -->|Yes| JA[Use JA<br/>Jump if Above]
    Q3 -->|No| Q3B{Equal?}
    Q3B -->|Yes| JE2[Use JE<br/>Jump if Equal]
    Q3B -->|No| JB[Use JB<br/>Jump if Below]

    style Start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style JG fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JA fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JL fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JB fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JE fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style JE2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Using **JA** for signed comparison or **JG** for unsigned comparison will cause wrong program behavior. The CPU's flags reflect *unsigned* borrow (CF) and *signed* overflow (OF) **differently** — choose jumps based on whether your data is signed or unsigned.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a Machine Instruction and an Assembler Directive. Give one example of each.**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Machine Instruction | Assembler Directive |
|:----------|:--------------------|:-------------------:|
| **Meaning** | A command executed by the CPU at run-time | A command for the assembler during translation |
| **Generates machine code?** | **Yes** | **No** |
| **When acted upon** | During program execution | During assembly (translation) phase |
| **Affects memory?** | Yes, at run-time | Yes, at assembly-time (reserves/defines data) |
| **Example** | `MOV AX, BX` | `DB`, `DW`, `EQU`, `SEGMENT` |

**Example of Machine Instruction:** `MOV AL, 25H` — The CPU fetches opcode `B0`, reads immediate byte `25H`, and stores it in `AL`.

**Example of Assembler Directive:** `COUNT DB 50H` — The assembler reserves **1 byte** of memory in the data segment, stores the value `50H` there, and records `COUNT` in the symbol table with the assigned offset. No machine code is generated for this line at run-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** "Directives **direct** the assembler, **not** the processor."

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Explain the function of the EQU and OFFSET directives with examples.**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) EQU Directive (Equate)**

The `EQU` directive assigns a **constant symbolic name** to a value, expression, or another symbol. It is **immutable** — the value cannot be redefined later in the program.

```asm
PORT     EQU  60H          ; Symbol PORT = 60H
MAX_LEN  EQU  256          ; Symbol MAX_LEN = 256
CTRL_REG EQU  PORT + 1     ; Computed value
```

**Utility:** Makes the program more readable. If `PORT` needs to change, only one line is modified.

**(b) OFFSET Directive**

The `OFFSET` directive returns the **16-bit displacement** (offset address) of a label or data variable within its segment. It is evaluated by the assembler at assembly time, producing a **constant** in the machine code.

```asm
MOV  BX, OFFSET ARRAY    ; Loads the offset of ARRAY into BX
                         ; e.g., if ARRAY is at DS:0124H, then BX = 0124H
```

**Key Distinction:**

| Feature | OFFSET | LEA |
|:--------|:-------|:----|
| When evaluated | At **assembly time** | At **run time** |
| Output type | Constant | Can compute complex expressions |
| Operates on | Labels only | Any memory expression |
| Example | `MOV BX, OFFSET X` | `LEA BX, [SI+10]` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The model below follows the official KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern with internal choice.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **8086 instruction format** with a neat diagram. Describe the function of the **opcode byte**, **MOD-REG-R/M byte**, and the role of the **D and W bits** with a suitable example. (7 marks)

**(b)** With neat examples, explain the following **assembler directives**: `DB`, `DW`, `DD`, `EQU`, `ASSUME`, `PROC`, `OFFSET`, `PTR`. Show the memory layout wherever applicable. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): 8086 Instruction Format

**Introduction:**
The 8086 instruction format is **variable-length**, ranging from **1 to 6 bytes**. Each instruction consists of optional fields that the BIU interprets to generate the correct operation.

**Diagram of General Instruction Format:**

```
+--------+--------+----------------+----------------+
| Byte 0 | Byte 1 |   Byte 2-3     |   Byte 4-5     |
+--------+--------+----------------+----------------+
| OPCODE |  MOD   |   Displacement |   Immediate    |
| + D + W| REG R/M|   (0/8/16 bit) |   (0/8/16 bit) |
+--------+--------+----------------+----------------+
```

**Detailed Structure of the Opcode Byte (Byte 0):**

| Field | Size | Function |
|:------|:----:|:---------|
| **OPCODE** | 6 bits | Defines the operation (e.g., 100010 for MOV) |
| **D** | 1 bit | Direction: 0 = REG is source, 1 = REG is destination |
| **W** | 1 bit | Width: 0 = byte (8-bit), 1 = word (16-bit) |

**Detailed Structure of Byte 1 (MOD-REG-R/M):**

| Field | Size | Function |
|:------|:----:|:---------|
| **MOD** | 2 bits | Addressing mode (00=Memory no disp, 01=Memory 8-bit disp, 10=Memory 16-bit disp, 11=Register) |
| **REG** | 3 bits | Register selection (AL/AX=000, CL/CX=001, etc.) |
| **R/M** | 3 bits | Register/Memory operand selection (combined with MOD) |

**Worked Example: `MOV AX, BX`**

**Step 1:** Identify opcode for `MOV reg, reg` = `100010` (6 bits)
**Step 2:** W = 1 (word operation)
**Step 3:** D = 1 (REG field is destination = AX)
**Step 4:** REG = 000 (AX), MOD = 11 (register mode), R/M = 011 (BX)
**Step 5:** Byte 1 = `11 000 011` = `C3H`
**Step 6:** Final machine code = `10001011 11000011` = `8B C3` (2 bytes total)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When explaining the D bit, always state that it is **don't-care (×)** for instructions with no explicit direction (e.g., arithmetic where order is fixed by the opcode).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Assembler Directives

**(i) DB — Define Byte**

Reserves **1 byte** per item. Can store numbers, ASCII characters, or `?` for uninitialized storage.

```asm
DATA DB 10H, 20H, 'A'    ; 3 bytes: 10H, 20H, 41H
BUFF DB 50 DUP(?)        ; 50 bytes reserved, uninitialized
```

**(ii) DW — Define Word**

Reserves **2 bytes** (1 word) per item. Stored in **little-endian** format.

```asm
WORD1 DW 1234H           ; Bytes: 34H 12H at offsets n, n+1
ARR    DW 10 DUP(0)      ; 20 bytes initialized to 0
```

**Memory Layout for `ARR`:**

| Offset | Byte |
|:------:|:----:|
| 0000 | 00 |
| 0001 | 00 |
| 0002 | 00 |
| ... | ... |
| 0013 | 00 |

**(iii) DD — Define Doubleword**

Reserves **4 bytes** per item.

```asm
DVAL DD 12345678H        ; Bytes: 78H 56H 34H 12H
```

**(iv) EQU — Equate**

Assigns a **constant** symbolic name. Immutable.

```asm
PORT_ADDR EQU 2000H      ; PORT_ADDR is now 2000H
```

**(v) ASSUME — Segment Register Binding**

Tells the assembler which segment register points to which segment. Does **not** load the segment register — that requires `MOV` at run-time.

```asm
DATA_SEG SEGMENT
        ; data declarations
DATA_SEG ENDS

CODE_SEG SEGMENT
        ASSUME CS:CODE_SEG, DS:DATA_SEG
START:  MOV AX, DATA_SEG
        MOV DS, AX        ; Explicit loading of DS
```

**(vi) PROC — Procedure Declaration**

Marks the start of a named subroutine.

```asm
ADD_NUM PROC NEAR
       ; procedure body
       RET
ADD_NUM ENDP
```

**(vii) OFFSET — Returns Address Offset**

```asm
MOV SI, OFFSET ARRAY     ; SI = address of ARRAY within its segment
```

**(viii) PTR — Type Override**

Forces the assembler to treat an operand as a specific size, useful when the size is ambiguous.

```asm
MOV BYTE PTR [BX], 25    ; Store 25 as a byte at [BX]
MOV WORD PTR [BX], 1000  ; Store 1000 as a word at [BX]
```

**Complete Memory Map Example:**

```asm
DATA SEGMENT
    X    DB  10H, 20H
    Y    DW  1234H
    Z    DD  0ABCDEF0H
DATA ENDS
```

| Symbol | Offset | Byte 0 | Byte 1 | Byte 2 | Byte 3 |
|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| X | 0000 | 10 | 20 | — | — |
| Y | 0002 | 34 | 12 | — | — |
| Z | 0004 | F0 | DE | BC | 0A |

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a)** Classify the **8086 instruction set** into different functional groups. List at least **three instructions** from each group with their syntax and function. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly program to **add two 8-bit numbers** stored in memory and store the result. Use appropriate **data definition directives** and explain the role of each line. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): 8086 Instruction Set Classification

The 8086 instruction set is classified into **six functional groups**:

**Group 1: Data Transfer Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `MOV` | `MOV dst, src` | Copies data from source to destination |
| `PUSH` | `PUSH src` | Pushes 16-bit value onto the stack |
| `POP` | `POP dst` | Pops 16-bit value from the stack into destination |

**Group 2: Arithmetic Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `ADD` | `ADD dst, src` | `dst ← dst + src`; updates flags |
| `SUB` | `SUB dst, src` | `dst ← dst - src`; updates flags |
| `MUL` | `MUL src` | `AX ← AL × src` (byte); `DX:AX ← AX × src` (word) |

**Group 3: Bit Manipulation (Logical) Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `AND` | `AND dst, src` | Bitwise AND; sets CF=OF=0 |
| `OR` | `OR dst, src` | Bitwise OR |
| `XOR` | `XOR dst, src` | Bitwise XOR; used to clear a register (`XOR AX, AX`) |

**Group 4: String Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `MOVSB` | `MOVSB` | Moves byte from `[DS:SI]` to `[ES:DI]` |
| `CMPSB` | `CMPSB` | Compares bytes and sets flags |
| `SCASB` | `SCASB` | Scans string for a byte equal to `AL` |

**Group 5: Control Transfer Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `JMP` | `JMP label` | Unconditional jump |
| `JE` | `JE label` | Jump if ZF=1 (equal) |
| `LOOP` | `LOOP label` | Decrements CX, jumps if CX ≠ 0 |

**Group 6: Processor Control Instructions**

| Instruction | Syntax | Function |
|:------------|:-------|:---------|
| `STC` | `STC` | Set CF=1 |
| `CLD` | `CLD` | Clear DF=0 (auto-increment for strings) |
| `NOP` | `NOP` | No operation (1 byte, 4 T-states) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Assembly Program to Add Two 8-Bit Numbers

```asm
; Program: Add two 8-bit numbers stored in memory
; Result stored in memory with appropriate carry handling

DATA    SEGMENT
        NUM1    DB  45H        ; First 8-bit number
        NUM2    DB  38H        ; Second 8-bit number
        RESULT  DB  ?          ; Reserve byte for sum
        CARRY   DB  ?          ; Reserve byte for carry flag
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:CODE, DS:DATA
START:  MOV     AX, DATA       ; Load address of DATA segment into AX
        MOV     DS, AX         ; Initialize DS register

        MOV     AL, NUM1       ; Load first number into AL
        ADD     AL, NUM2       ; Add second number to AL
        MOV     RESULT, AL     ; Store sum in RESULT

        JNC     SKIP           ; If no carry (CF=0), skip storing carry
        MOV     CARRY, 01H     ; Store 1 in CARRY to indicate overflow
SKIP:   MOV     AH, 4CH
        INT     21H            ; Return to DOS
CODE    ENDS
        END     START
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation:**

| Line | Directive/Instruction | Role |
|:-----|:----------------------|:-----|
| `DATA SEGMENT` | Directive | Begins data segment |
| `NUM1 DB 45H` | Directive | Allocates 1 byte initialized to `45H` |
| `NUM2 DB 38H` | Directive | Allocates 1 byte initialized to `38H` |
| `RESULT DB ?` | Directive | Allocates 1 byte (uninitialized) for result |
| `CARRY DB ?` | Directive | Allocates 1 byte for carry flag storage |
| `DATA ENDS` | Directive | Ends data segment |
| `CODE SEGMENT` | Directive | Begins code segment |
| `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA` | Directive | Binds CS to CODE, DS to DATA |
| `MOV AX, DATA` | Instruction | Loads offset of DATA into AX |
| `MOV DS, AX` | Instruction | Initializes DS register (required at run-time) |
| `MOV AL, NUM1` | Instruction | `AL ← 45H` |
| `ADD AL, NUM2` | Instruction | `AL ← 45H + 38H = 7DH` (CF=0, no overflow) |
| `MOV RESULT, AL` | Instruction | Stores `7DH` in RESULT |
| `JNC SKIP` | Instruction | Jump if no carry (CF=0) — skips the carry store |
| `MOV AH, 4CH` / `INT 21H` | Instruction | DOS interrupt to exit program |

**Verification of Arithmetic:**

$$
45H + 38H = \underbrace{0100 \; 0101}_{45H} + \underbrace{0011 \; 1000}_{38H} = \underbrace{0111 \; 1101}_{7DH}
$$

No carry out of bit 7 → **CF = 0**, hence `JNC SKIP` is taken, and `CARRY` remains uninitialized (or holds `00H` from BSS).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to initialize DS:** Writing `MOV AL, NUM1` without first loading `DS` will cause the assembler to generate code that reads from an *uninitialized* DS register, leading to **runtime crash** (Incorrect Segment). Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Conflating `MOV RESULT, AL` with `MOV AL, RESULT`:** The destination is on the **left** in Intel syntax. This is a 0.5 mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Using `MOV DS, DATA` directly:** The Intel syntax **prohibits** moving an immediate value directly into a segment register. You **must** use `MOV AX, DATA; MOV DS, AX`. This is a 1-mark penalty.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the `END START` directive:** Without it, the assembler does not know the entry point. This often causes the program to hang.
> 
> 5. **Confusing `OFFSET` with `SEG`:** `OFFSET` returns the **lower 16 bits** (displacement); `SEG` returns the **upper 16 bits** (segment base). Confusing them gives the wrong address.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring signed vs unsigned jumps:** Using `JA` (unsigned) when the data is signed gives wrong results for negative numbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Instruction Set and Assembler Directives**

### ✅ Instruction Set Essentials

- The 8086 instruction set has **6 functional groups**: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, String, Control Transfer, Processor Control.
- Instructions are **variable length** (1–6 bytes), with the **opcode byte** containing OPCODE + **D bit** (direction) + **W bit** (width).
- The **MOD-REG-R/M** byte specifies the addressing mode and registers.
- `MUL` byte result is in `AX`; word result is in `DX:AX`.
- `DIV` byte: `AL = quotient`, `AH = remainder`.
- `MUL` and `DIV` are **unsigned**; `IMUL` and `IDIV` are **signed**.
- Conditional jumps use **short (2-byte, 8-bit displacement)** format for distances of $-128$ to $+127$ bytes.
- **Signed jumps** (`JG`, `JL`, `JGE`, `JLE`) differ from **unsigned jumps** (`JA`, `JB`, `JAE`, `JBE`) — choose based on data type.
- `LOOP` uses `CX` as the counter and jumps if `CX ≠ 0`.
- The `REP` prefix is used with **string instructions** to repeat while `CX ≠ 0`.
- `MOV CS, reg` is **illegal** — you cannot write to CS directly.

### ✅ Assembler Directive Essentials

- **Directives do not generate machine code** — they guide the assembler.
- `DB` = 1 byte/item, `DW` = 2 bytes/item, `DD` = 4 bytes/item.
- `DUP(n)` repeats the pattern `n` times; `DUP(?)` reserves uninitialized memory.
- `EQU` assigns a **constant** (immutable); `=` assigns a **redefinable** value.
- `SEGMENT ... ENDS` defines a logical segment; `ASSUME` binds a segment register to it.
- `OFFSET` returns the displacement of a label (assembler-time constant).
- `PTR` overrides default operand size: `BYTE PTR`, `WORD PTR`, `DWORD PTR`.
- `PROC ... ENDP` defines a subroutine; use `NEAR` for intra-segment and `FAR` for inter-segment calls.
- `END <label>` marks the program end and specifies the entry point.
- 8086 uses **little-endian** storage: low byte at lower address, high byte at higher address.

### ✅ Cross-Cutting Tips for KTU Exams

- Always show the **flag-by-flag analysis** in arithmetic problems.
- For string instructions, mention the **DF flag** direction (auto-increment or auto-decrement).
- When asked about differences, use a **comparison table** — KTU examiners reward structured answers.
- The physical address formula $PA = \text{Seg} \times 10H + \text{Offset}$ is **mandatory** for any address-related question.
- For assembly programs, always include **full segment setup** with `ASSUME` and proper DS initialization — partial programs lose marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** Think of the 8086 instruction groups as **D.A.L.S.C.P** — **D**ata, **A**rithmetic, **L**ogical, **S**tring, **C**ontrol, **P**rocessor. Memorize this acronym for instant recall during exams!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Assembly Language Programming (ALP)** is a low-level, symbolic programming methodology in which mnemonics (short, human-readable codes such as `MOV`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `JMP`, `LOOP`) are used to represent the binary machine-level instructions executed by a microprocessor. Each assembly instruction maps **one-to-one** to a unique machine code opcode recognized by the CPU's instruction decoder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Verbatim):** *Assembly language is a low-level programming language specific to a particular microprocessor architecture (e.g., Intel 8086) in which mnemonics, labels, directives, and operands combine to produce executable machine code after being processed by an assembler.*

The **assembler** is the system software that translates this symbolic source code (.ASM file) into the object code (.OBJ file). The most common assembler used in KTU laboratories is the **Microsoft Macro Assembler (MASM)** or the **Turbo Assembler (TASM)** for the 16-bit Intel 8086 microprocessor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a tourist in France who speaks only English, but the locals (the CPU) understand only French (binary `01001011 10110000`).

- **Machine Code (Binary)** = French language understood only by the CPU.
- **Assembly Language** = A simple translation sheet: instead of saying "Bonjour, je voudrais du café" (machine code), you write `MOV AL, 01H` and the assembler does the rest.
- **High-Level Language (C/Java)** = Hiring a human interpreter (compiler) who converts entire paragraphs at once — convenient but you lose fine control.

**Why learn ALP at all in 2024?**
> [!NOTE]
> Assembly is still **irreplaceable** in:
> - **Real-time embedded systems** (ECUs in cars, pacemakers) where **latency < 1 µs** is mandatory.
> - **Bootloaders and BIOS/UEFI routines** that run *before* any compiler exists.
> - **Interrupt Service Routines (ISR)** where every clock cycle matters.
> - **Device drivers** that must manipulate hardware registers bit-by-bit.

## 1.3 Salient Features of 8086 Assembly Language

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Mnemonics** | Operation codes like `MOV`, `ADD`, `CMP` |
| **Labels** | Symbolic names for memory addresses (e.g., `START:`) |
| **Directives (Pseudo-ops)** | Non-executable instructions guiding the assembler (e.g., `DB`, `DW`, `SEGMENT`, `ENDS`) |
| **Comments** | Begin with `;` semicolon — ignored by assembler |
| **Case Insensitivity** | `mov` and `MOV` are identical |
| **1-to-1 Mapping** | One assembly instruction = one machine instruction (unlike C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** *Mnemonics produce machine code; Directives do NOT. Directives merely instruct the assembler how to translate the program.*

## 1.4 General Structure of an 8086 Assembly Program

A standard MASM/TASM ALP for the 8086 follows the **logical segment structure** required by the segmented memory model of the 8086:

```
DATA SEGMENT           ; Directive: Start of Data Segment
   VAR1 DB 10H         ; Define Byte variable VAR1 = 10H
DATA ENDS              ; End of Data Segment

CODE SEGMENT           ; Directive: Start of Code Segment
   ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA   ; Informs assembler of segment association
START:                 ; Label: Entry point
   MOV AX, DATA        ; Load data segment address
   MOV DS, AX          ; Initialize DS register
   ; ----- user instructions -----
   MOV AL, VAR1
   ADD AL, 05H
   MOV VAR1, AL
   ; ----- termination -----
   MOV AH, 4CH
   INT 21H             ; DOS interrupt: return control to OS
CODE ENDS              ; End of Code Segment
END START              ; Directive: End of program, entry point is START
```

> [!TIP]
> The `INT 21H` with `AH=4CH` is a **DOS function call** that terminates a program and returns control to the operating system — a mandatory KTU lab convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Programming Languages

Programming languages are categorized into five abstraction levels. KTU examiners frequently test this classification.

| Level | Language Type | Example | Translation Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Machine Code | `B4 05` | None (raw binary) |
| **L2** | Assembly Language | `MOV AH, 05H` | **Assembler** |
| **L3** | High-Level Language | C, Pascal | **Compiler** |
| **L4** | Very High-Level | SQL, MATLAB | Interpreter / JIT |
| **L5** | Natural / AI Language | English sentences | LLM / NLP |

> [!NOTE]
> The **assembler** is to assembly what the **compiler** is to C — but an assembler performs a **1:1 line-by-line translation**, whereas a compiler does **N:M translation with optimization**.

## 2.2 Assembler Directives — The "Grammar Rules" of ALP

Directives (also called **pseudo-instructions**) tell the assembler *how* to assemble the program, not *what* the CPU must execute. KTU 2024 mandates familiarity with the following:

### 2.2.1 Data Definition Directives

| Directive | Full Form | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DB` | Define Byte | Reserves 1 byte (8 bits) | `NUM DB 25` |
| `DW` | Define Word | Reserves 2 bytes (16 bits) | `CNT DW 1000H` |
| `DD` | Define Doubleword | Reserves 4 bytes (32 bits) | `ADDR DD 12345678H` |
| `DQ` | Define Quadword | Reserves 8 bytes (64 bits) | `BIG DQ ?` |
| `DT` | Define Tenbytes | Reserves 10 bytes (BCD) | `BCDVAL DT 1234567890H` |
| `DUP` | Duplication | Repeats a value | `ARR DB 10 DUP(0)` |
| `?` | Uninitialized | Reserves storage without value | `TEMP DB ?` |

### 2.2.2 Segment & Program Structure Directives

| Directive | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `SEGMENT name` | Begins a logical segment |
| `ENDS` | Ends a segment |
| `ASSUME` | Tells assembler which segment register points to which segment |
| `END label` | Marks physical end of source program |
| `ORG addr` | Sets the **Origin** — next instruction/data starts at `addr` |
| `OFFSET` | Returns the offset of a label/variable |
| `PTR` | Type override (BYTE PTR, WORD PTR) |

### 2.2.3 Equate & Macro Directives

| Directive | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `EQU` | Assigns a constant symbol | `PORT EQU 03H` |
| `=` | Reassignable symbol | `COUNT = 5` |
| `PROC` / `ENDP` | Define a procedure (subroutine) | `ADD1 PROC ... ENDP` |
| `MACRO` / `ENDM` | Define a text macro | See below |

**Example MACRO definition:**
```
SUM MACRO X, Y
   MOV AL, X
   ADD AL, Y
ENDM
```
**Invocation:** `SUM 05H, 03H` → expands to the two instructions inline.

## 2.3 Instruction Format of 8086 Machine Code

Every 8086 machine instruction is encoded as a variable-length sequence of **1 to 6 bytes**:

$$\text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{1\text{ or }2\text{ bytes}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Mod-R/M}}_{0\text{ or }1\text{ byte}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Displacement}}_{0,1,2\text{ bytes}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Immediate data}}_{0,1,2\text{ bytes}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable-length encoding** is why the 8086 is called a **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) architecture. Modern ARM/RISC-V processors use fixed 32-bit encoding.

### 2.3.1 The Mod-R/M Byte Explained

The **Mod-R/M** byte selects one of 8 registers and one of 8 addressing modes, giving **256 possible combinations** in a single byte.

$$\text{Mod-R/M} = \underbrace{bb}_{\text{Mode (2 bits)}} \;+\; \underbrace{ddd}_{\text{Register (3 bits)}} \;+\; \underbrace{rrr}_{\text{R/M (3 bits)}}$$

| Bits | Field | Possible Values |
|---|---|---|
| **bb (2 bits)** | Mode | `00` = Memory (no disp), `01` = Memory + 8-bit disp, `10` = Memory + 16-bit disp, `11` = Register |
| **ddd (3 bits)** | Reg / Opcode extension | `000`=AL, `001`=CL, `010`=DL, `011`=BL, `100`=AH, `101`=CH, `110`=DH, `111`=BH |
| **rrr (3 bits)** | R/M operand | `000`=BX+SI, `001`=BX+DI, `010`=BP+SI, `011`=BP+DI, `100`=SI, `101`=DI, `110`=BP, `111`=BX |

## 2.4 The Instruction Execution Cycle (Why ALP is "Close to Hardware")

Every assembly instruction, when fetched by the 8086, follows the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle:

$$\text{CPU Cycle} = T_{\text{Fetch}} + T_{\text{Decode}} + T_{\text{Execute}} + T_{\text{Store}}$$

For a simple `MOV AX, BX` (register-to-register, 2 bytes), this takes **2 clock cycles** (BIU fetch + EU execute). For `ADD AX, [BX+SI+1000H]`, it may take **17+ clock cycles** due to memory access and EA calculation.

> [!TIP]
> This is the fundamental reason **Assembly is used in real-time systems**: the programmer can predict *exact* timing because every cycle is visible.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Pin to Wall)

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $n_{\text{bytes}} = \lceil \text{bits} / 8 \rceil$ | Byte size of data | bytes |
| $T_{\text{inst}} = N_{\text{clocks}} \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Execution time of an instruction | µs |
| $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{CPU}}$ | Clock period | µs (8086: 0.125 µs @ 8 MHz) |
| $\text{EA} = [\text{Base}] + [\text{Index}] + \text{Disp}$ | Effective Address (8086) | 16-bit offset |
| $A_{20} = \underbrace{S}_{\text{Segment}} \times 16 + \underbrace{O}_{\text{Offset}}$ | 20-bit Physical Address | 0 to FFFFFH |
| $N_{\text{modes}} = 2^{bb} \times 2^{rrr} = 8 \times 8$ | Addressing mode combinations | 64 modes |
| $M_{\text{regs}} = 2^{ddd} \times 2^{rrr}$ | Register addressing combinations | 64 reg pairs |
| $\text{Code size} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert\text{Instruction}_i\vert_{\text{bytes}}$ | Total program size | bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> The **physical address** formula $A_{20} = S \times 16 + O$ is mathematically equivalent to a **left-shift-by-4** of the segment base, then addition of the offset. It produces a **20-bit address** despite the registers being only 16 bits — this is the famous 8086 segmented memory trick that yields the **1 MB address space**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Physical Address = Segment × 16 + Offset

The 8086 has 16-bit registers but needs to address **1,048,576 bytes** (1 MB) of memory, requiring **20 address lines (A0–A19)**. The solution is **segmented addressing**.

### Step 1: Identify the Problem
$$\text{Needed address space} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$
$$\text{Register width} = 16 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$
$$\text{Deficit} = 2^{20} - 2^{16} = 1{,}048{,}576 - 65{,}536 = 983{,}040 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2: Design the Solution
A 16-bit segment register is **shifted left by 4 bits** (i.e., multiplied by 16) to produce a 20-bit base, and a 16-bit offset is added.

$$A_{\text{physical}} = (S \ll 4) + O$$

### Step 3: Mathematical Expansion
$$A_{\text{physical}} = (S \times 2^4) + O = 16 \cdot S + O$$

### Step 4: Bit-Width Verification
$$A_{\text{physical}} = \underbrace{(S \times 16)}_{20 \text{ bits}} + \underbrace{O}_{16 \text{ bits}}$$
$$A_{\text{physical}} \le \text{FFFFFH} = 1{,}048{,}575 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 5: Worked Example
Given: `MOV AX, [2000H:3450H]` (Segment = 2000H, Offset = 3450H)

$$A_{\text{physical}} = (2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 3450\text{H}$$
$$A_{\text{physical}} = 20000\text{H} + 3450\text{H}$$
$$A_{\text{physical}} = 23450\text{H}$$

> [!TIP]
> The hex multiplication by 16 is trivial: just append a `0`. So `2000H × 10H = 20000H`. This is the **single most-tested KTU calculation**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address (EA) for All 8086 Addressing Modes

The Effective Address is computed by the **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** in the BIU. There are **8 canonical modes** (KTU 2024 emphasizes these):

| # | Mode | EA Formula | Example Instruction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct | $O$ | `MOV AL, [1234H]` |
| 2 | Register Indirect | $[\text{BX}]$ or $[\text{SI}]$ etc. | `MOV AL, [BX]` |
| 3 | Based | $[\text{BX}] + \text{disp}$ or $[\text{BP}] + \text{disp}$ | `MOV AL, [BX+10H]` |
| 4 | Indexed | $[\text{SI}] + \text{disp}$ or $[\text{DI}] + \text{disp}$ | `MOV AL, [SI+20H]` |
| 5 | Based-Indexed | $[\text{BX}]+[\text{SI}]+ \text{disp}$ | `MOV AL, [BX+SI+5]` |
| 6 | String | $[\text{SI}]$ or $[\text{DI}]$ auto-inc/dec | `MOVSB`, `LODSB` |
| 7 | Port Direct | `port` (8-bit) | `IN AL, 60H` |
| 8 | Port Indirect | $[\text{DX}]$ (16-bit) | `OUT DX, AL` |

### Master Formula for Memory Modes

$$\text{EA} = [\text{Base} \in \{\text{BX, BP}\}] + [\text{Index} \in \{\text{SI, DI}\}] + \text{Displacement}_{0,8,16}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Segment Association** (KTU-favorite trick question):
> - If **BP** is the base register → default segment is **SS** (Stack Segment)
> - All other memory modes (BX, SI, DI) → default segment is **DS** (Data Segment)
> - Code-fetch (IP) → default segment is **CS**
