## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Execution Time of Common 8086 Instructions

| Instruction | Bytes | Clock Cycles (T) | When Run @ 5 MHz → Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV reg, reg` | 2 | 2 | 0.4 µs |
| `MOV AL, [BX]` | 2 | 8 | 1.6 µs |
| `ADD reg, reg` | 2 | 3 | 0.6 µs |
| `ADD AL, [BX+SI+disp]` | 4 | 13 | 2.6 µs |
| `MUL reg` (8-bit) | 2 | 70–77 | 14–15.4 µs |
| `DIV reg` (16-bit) | 2 | 124–133 | 24.8–26.6 µs |
| `LOOP label` | 2 | 17 (taken) / 5 (not taken) | 3.4 µs / 1.0 µs |
| `JMP short label` | 2 | 15 | 3.0 µs |
| `CALL near proc` | 3 | 19 | 3.8 µs |

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem — Typical KTU Style

**Problem:** A 8086 ALP is to sum 100 bytes of data stored in an array `ARR` (defined in the data segment) and store the result in a word `SUM`. Calculate the total program size and the worst-case execution time on a 5 MHz 8086.

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Write the Assembly Program
```
DATA SEGMENT
   ARR DB 100 DUP(5)       ; 100 bytes, each initialized to 5
   SUM DW ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
   ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
START:
   MOV AX, DATA
   MOV DS, AX
   XOR AL, AL             ; AL = 0 (sum = 0)
   MOV CX, 100            ; Counter
   LEA SI, ARR            ; SI = offset of ARR
AGAIN:
   ADD AL, [SI]           ; Add array element
   INC SI                 ; Next byte
   LOOP AGAIN             ; Decrement CX, loop if CX ≠ 0
   MOV SUM, AX            ; Store result
   MOV AH, 4CH
   INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

### Step 2: Code Size Calculation
Using the cheat sheet:
$$\text{Code size} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \vert\text{Instruction}_i\vert_{\text{bytes}}$$
$$= \underbrace{2}_{\text{MOV AX,DATA}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{MOV DS,AX}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{XOR AL,AL}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{MOV CX,100}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{LEA SI,ARR}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{ADD AL,[SI]}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{INC SI}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{LOOP AGAIN}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{MOV SUM,AX}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{MOV AH,4CH}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{INT 21H}}$$
$$= 24 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3: Data Size Calculation
$$\text{Data size} = 100 \times 1 \text{ byte} + 2 \text{ bytes} = 102 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4: Total Program Size
$$\text{Total} = 24 + 102 = 126 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 5: Execution Time Calculation
Loop body executes **100 times**. Each iteration: `ADD AL,[SI]` + `INC SI` + `LOOP` (taken).

$$T_{\text{loop body}} = 13 + 2 + 17 = 32 \text{ cycles (worst case)}$$
$$T_{\text{total loop}} = 100 \times 32 = 3{,}200 \text{ cycles}$$
$$T_{\text{setup}} \approx 15 \text{ cycles (initialization)} + 5 \text{ cycles (termination)}$$
$$T_{\text{total}} \approx 3{,}220 \text{ cycles}$$

At $f_{\text{CPU}} = 5 \text{ MHz}$, $T_{\text{clk}} = 1/(5 \times 10^6) = 0.2 \mu s$:

$$t_{\text{execution}} = 3{,}220 \times 0.2 \mu s = 644 \mu s \approx 0.644 \text{ ms}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture Flow: 8086 Assembly Program Translation & Execution

The following Mermaid diagram depicts how an `.ASM` file travels through translation, linking, loading, and finally execution on the 8086 CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code\n .ASM file] --> B[Assembler\n MASM or TASM]
    B --> C[Object Code\n .OBJ file]
    C --> D[Linker\n LINK.EXE]
    D --> E[Executable Code\n .EXE file]
    E --> F[Loader\n DOS or TSR]
    F --> G[RAM\n Physical Memory\n 1 MB Space]
    G --> H[8086 CPU\n BIU plus EU]
    H --> I[Execution Output\n Registers or Memory]

    subgraph TRANSLATION_PHASE [Translation Phase offline]
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph EXECUTION_PHASE [Execution Phase runtime]
        F
        G
        H
        I
    end

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **assembler** and **linker** run *offline* (before the program is executed). The **loader** and **CPU** run *at runtime*. This is why the assembly→machine translation cost is **paid only once**, but the execution speed is paid every time the program runs.

## 4.2 Flow: Effective Address Calculation (AGU inside BIU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Opcode Fetched] --> B{Mod R/M byte}
    B --> C[Mode Field bb]
    B --> D[Reg Field ddd]
    B --> E[R/M Field rrr]
    C --> F{bb equals 11}
    F -->|Yes| G[Register Mode\n No EA needed]
    F -->|No| H[Memory Mode]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Select Base Register\n BX or BP]
    H --> J[Select Index Register\n SI or DI]
    I --> K[Add Base plus Index]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Add Displacement\n 0, 8-bit, or 16-bit]
    L --> M[Final 16-bit EA]
    M --> N[BIU adds Segment x 16]
    N --> O[20-bit Physical Address]
    D --> P[Operand Register Identified]
    G --> P

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Structure of a Typical ALP File

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[8086 Assembly Program] --> S1[STACK SEGMENT\n Stack storage]
    P --> S2[DATA SEGMENT\n Variables and constants]
    P --> S3[EXTRA SEGMENT\n Extra data area]
    P --> S4[CODE SEGMENT\n Instruction stream]
    S4 --> Q[ASSUME Directive\n Tell CPU which reg\n points to which segment]
    Q --> R[START label\n Entry point]
    R --> T[Initialization\n MOV AX, DATA\n MOV DS, AX]
    T --> U[Main Logic Block\n user instructions]
    U --> V[Termination\n MOV AH, 4CH\n INT 21H]
    V --> W[CODE ENDS]
    W --> X[END START]

    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style S2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style S4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style U fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU labs often test whether students can write a **complete working 8086 ALP** with all four segments. Although only DATA and CODE are strictly required, including STACK avoids stack-overflow bugs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define an Assembler. List any two commonly used 8086 assemblers.**

**Model Answer:**
An **assembler** is a system software that translates assembly-level mnemonic code (`.ASM`) into relocatable machine code (`.OBJ`), performing **one-to-one translation** of mnemonics, labels, and directives.

**Two commonly used 8086 assemblers:**
1. **MASM (Microsoft Macro Assembler)** — Industry standard, supports macros and complex segment handling.
2. **TASM (Turbo Assembler)** — Borland's product, IDE-friendly, backward compatible with MASM.

> [!TIP]
> Other valid answers accepted: **A86, NASM (not for 8086 but Intel-syntax), FASM.**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between an instruction and a directive in 8086 assembly language. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Instruction | Directive |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A statement executed by the CPU | A statement that guides the assembler |
| Execution | Translated to machine code (opcode) | NOT translated to machine code |
| Purpose | Performs data movement/computation | Defines data, segments, or constants |
| Example | `MOV AX, BX` | `DATA SEGMENT` |
| Effect at Runtime | Changes registers/memory flags | No runtime effect |

**Example:**
- Instruction: `ADD AL, 05H` → CPU adds 5 to AL and updates flags.
- Directive: `TEMP DB 10H` → Allocates 1 byte in memory, no execution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the three main categories of 8086 assembler directives with suitable examples for each. Draw the general format of an 8086 assembly language program.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Write an 8086 ALP to add two 16-bit numbers stored at locations `NUM1` and `NUM2` in the data segment and store the result in `RESULT`. Show the program execution for sample inputs 1234H + 5678H.**

#### Solution to (a):

The three categories of 8086 assembler directives are:

**1. Data Definition / Data Allocation Directives**
These allocate memory and optionally initialize it.
- `DB` (Define Byte): `X DB 25`
- `DW` (Define Word): `Y DW 1234H`
- `DD` (Define Doubleword): `Z DD 12345678H`
- `DUP`: `ARR DB 50 DUP(0)` → creates 50 bytes, all zero.
- `?`: `TEMP DB ?` → reserves 1 byte, uninitialized.

**2. Segment Definition Directives**
Used to define logical segments as required by the 8086's segmented memory architecture.
- `SEGMENT name` ... `ENDS` : Defines a segment.
- `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA` : Tells assembler the segment-register association.
- `GROUP`, `SEG`, `OFFSET` : Special segment operators.

**3. Program Control Directives**
Control the flow, organization, and termination of the source program.
- `END <label>` : Marks the physical end of source.
- `ORG <addr>` : Sets the location counter.
- `PROC name` ... `ENDP` : Defines a procedure/subroutine.
- `EQU`, `=` : Constant definition.
- `MACRO` ... `ENDM` : Defines a macro.

**General Program Format:**
```
STACK SEGMENT
   DB 100 DUP(0)   ; 100 bytes of stack space
STACK ENDS

DATA SEGMENT
   ; variables and constants
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
   ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
START:
   MOV AX, DATA
   MOV DS, AX
   ; user instructions
   MOV AH, 4CH
   INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

#### Solution to (b):

**Assembly Program:**
```
DATA SEGMENT
   NUM1   DW 1234H
   NUM2   DW 5678H
   RESULT DW ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
   ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
START:
   MOV AX, DATA
   MOV DS, AX            ; Initialize DS

   MOV AX, NUM1          ; AX = 1234H
   ADD AX, NUM2          ; AX = 1234H + 5678H = 68ACH
   MOV RESULT, AX        ; Store result

   MOV AH, 4CH
   INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Step | Instruction | AX | RESULT (memory) | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV AX, DATA` | F000H (assumed DS loaded) | — | None |
| 2 | `MOV DS, AX` | F000H | — | None |
| 3 | `MOV AX, NUM1` | 1234H | — | None |
| 4 | `ADD AX, NUM2` | 68ACH | — | CF=0, ZF=0, SF=0, OF=0 |
| 5 | `MOV RESULT, AX` | 68ACH | 68ACH | None |
| 6 | `MOV AH, 4CH` | 4CACH | 68ACH | None |
| 7 | `INT 21H` | 4CACH | 68ACH | Program exits |

**Hex Addition Verification:**
$$1234\text{H} + 5678\text{H} = 68\text{AC}\text{H}$$

Manual addition:
- 4 + 8 = C (no carry)
- 3 + 7 = A (no carry)
- 2 + 6 = 8
- 1 + 5 = 6
Result: `68ACH` ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the 8086 instruction format with a neat diagram. Show how the Mod-R/M byte is structured and explain its role in selecting operands and addressing modes.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Write an 8086 ALP to find the largest number in an array of 10 unsigned bytes stored in the data segment. Explain the algorithm with a flowchart.**

#### Solution to (a):

**8086 Instruction Format:**
```
| Opcode (1-2 bytes) | Mod-R/M (0-1 byte) | Displacement (0,1,2 bytes) | Immediate Data (0,1,2 bytes) |
```

**Mod-R/M Byte Structure (8 bits):**
```
| 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| Mod (2 bits) | Reg/Opcode (3 bits) | R/M (3 bits) |
|    Mode     |    Operand         |  Address Mode  |
```

- **Mod (bits 7-6):** Determines addressing mode.
  - `00` → Register indirect / direct memory
  - `01` → Memory + 8-bit displacement
  - `10` → Memory + 16-bit displacement
  - `11` → Register-to-register
- **Reg/Opcode (bits 5-3):** For two-operand instructions, specifies the source/destination register; for one-operand instructions, extends the opcode.
- **R/M (bits 2-0):** Specifies the second operand — either a register (Mod=11) or a memory address computed using BX, BP, SI, DI, and optional displacement.

**Role of Mod-R/M:**
1. Decides whether operands are registers or memory.
2. Selects the specific register out of 8 possible (AL, CL, DL, BL, AH, CH, DH, BH).
3. Triggers EA computation for memory operands (which combination of BX/BP/SI/DI/Disp).
4. Encodes extended opcodes (e.g., for `SHL`, `SHR`, `SAR`, `NOT`, `NEG`, `MUL`, `DIV`).

**Worked Example:** `ADD [BX+SI+1000H], AX` (16-bit displacement)
- Opcode: `01` (ADD reg/mem with reg)
- Mod-R/M = `10 000 000` = `80H` (Mod=10 → 16-bit disp, Reg=000 → AX, R/M=000 → [BX+SI])
- Displacement: `1000H` (2 bytes, little-endian → `00 10`)
- Total: 4 bytes: `01 80 00 10`

#### Solution to (b):

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**
1. Load the first element of the array as the initial maximum.
2. Set up a counter for the remaining (N-1) elements.
3. Use a pointer to scan the array.
4. For each element, compare with the current maximum using `CMP` and conditional jump `JLE` (Jump if Less or Equal).
5. If the new element is greater, update the maximum.
6. Decrement counter and loop until all elements are checked.
7. Store the maximum.

**Assembly Program:**
```
DATA SEGMENT
   ARR DB 45H, 12H, 89H, 33H, A7H, 05H, 67H, 1EH, 99H, 0BH
   MAX DB ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
   ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
START:
   MOV AX, DATA
   MOV DS, AX

   MOV CX, 10            ; Counter for 10 elements
   DEC CX                ; We compare 9 remaining
   LEA SI, ARR           ; SI points to first element
   MOV AL, [SI]          ; AL = first element (max so far)
   INC SI                ; Move to next element

NEXT:
   CMP AL, [SI]          ; Compare MAX with next element
   JGE SKIP              ; If MAX >= element, skip
   MOV AL, [SI]          ; Else, update MAX
SKIP:
   INC SI
   LOOP NEXT             ; Decrement CX, repeat if CX != 0

   MOV MAX, AL           ; Store result
   MOV AH, 4CH
   INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

| Iter | SI points to | [SI] | AL (MAX) | Comparison | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | ARR[0] | 45H | 45H | — | Initialize |
| 1 | ARR[1] | 12H | 45H | 45H ≥ 12H | Skip |
| 2 | ARR[2] | 89H | 45H | 45H < 89H | Update MAX = 89H |
| 3 | ARR[3] | 33H | 89H | 89H ≥ 33H | Skip |
| 4 | ARR[4] | A7H | 89H | 89H < A7H | Update MAX = A7H |
| 5 | ARR[5] | 05H | A7H | A7H ≥ 05H | Skip |
| 6 | ARR[6] | 67H | A7H | A7H ≥ 67H | Skip |
| 7 | ARR[7] | 1EH | A7H | A7H ≥ 1EH | Skip |
| 8 | ARR[8] | 99H | A7H | A7H < 99H | Update MAX = 99H |
| 9 | ARR[9] | 0BH | 99H | 99H ≥ 0BH | Skip |

**Final Result:** `MAX = 99H` (decimal 153) ✓

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgot to initialize DS register** — Program crashes with garbage segment value. Always include `MOV AX, DATA` then `MOV DS, AX`.
> 2. **Using `MOV` between two memory operands** — Illegal in 8086. Use a register as intermediate: `MOV AL, [SRC]` then `MOV [DST], AL`.
> 3. **Forgetting `END START`** — Assembler generates warnings, program may hang.
> 4. **Confusing `LOOP` (uses CX) with `JCXZ`** — `LOOP` decrements CX first; off-by-one errors.
> 5. **In `JGE`/`JLE`**: These are *signed* comparisons. For *unsigned* use `JAE`/`JBE` (above/below equal). Mixing them corrupts the maximum/minimum logic.
> 6. **Offset Calculation Error**: Forgetting that `OFFSET LABEL` returns the 16-bit offset, not the physical address. Must be added to `Segment × 16` for physical.
> 7. **Not terminating with `INT 21H / AH=4CH`** — Program returns to debugger instead of DOS. Examiner will mark as "incomplete execution."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Assembly language = mnemonics + labels + directives + comments.** Mnemonics become opcodes; directives do not.
- ✅ **8086 uses segmented memory**: Physical Address = `Segment × 16 + Offset`. The `× 16` is a 4-bit left shift.
- ✅ **Instruction format is variable-length** (1–6 bytes): Opcode + Mod-R/M + Displacement + Immediate.
- ✅ **Mod-R/M byte** decodes addressing mode (2 bits), register operand (3 bits), and EA mode (3 bits).
- ✅ **Default segments**: BX/SI/DI → DS, BP → SS, IP-fetch → CS. The override prefix `CS:`, `DS:`, `ES:`, `SS:` can change this.
- ✅ **Program structure**: STACK, DATA, EXTRA, CODE segments with `ASSUME` declaration. Always initialize DS before using data.
- ✅ **Termination**: `MOV AH, 4CH` followed by `INT 21H` is the standard DOS exit.
- ✅ **Assembler = 1:1 translator**; **Compiler = N:M translator with optimization**. ALP is faster and smaller, but harder to write.
- ✅ **For unsigned comparisons use** `JB`/`JAE`; for signed use `JL`/`JGE`. Mixing them is a top-3 KTU mistake.
- ✅ **`LOOP` decrements CX automatically**. If CX = 0 initially, `LOOP` decrements to FFFFH, so the loop executes 65,536 times.
- ✅ **`DUP` and `?`** are directive modifiers, not instructions. `DB 100 DUP(?)` reserves 100 bytes, none initialized.
- ✅ **ORG** changes the *location counter*, not the segment base. Used for absolute addressing (e.g., interrupt vector tables).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Modular Programming** is a software design technique that divides a complete program into smaller, independent, and manageable sub-programs or **modules** (also called procedures, subroutines, or functions). In the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, modular programming is implemented using **Procedures** declared with the `PROC` and `ENDP` directives in 8086 assembly language. Each module performs a specific, well-defined task and can be independently coded, tested, and debugged before being linked to the main program.

A module generally has three defining features:
1. **A single entry point** (the procedure name).
2. **A single exit point** (the `RET` instruction).
3. **The ability to be called repeatedly** from different parts of a program.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)
> [!NOTE]
> Think of Modular Programming like a **professional kitchen** in a hotel. The head chef (main program) doesn't cook every dish personally. Instead, the head chef calls upon specialized stations — the grill station, the salad station, the dessert station — each of which is an independent **module**. Each station (subroutine) knows its job perfectly, has its own ingredients (local data), and returns the finished plate (return value) to the head chef. If the dessert chef is absent, the rest of the kitchen still works. That's modularity in action.

### 1.3 Why Modular Programming?
- **Readability** — Programs become easier to read and understand.
- **Reusability** — A module written once can be called multiple times.
- **Debugging Ease** — Bugs can be isolated to specific modules.
- **Team Development** — Different programmers can code different modules simultaneously.
- **Memory Efficiency** — Shorter modules mean smaller, well-organized object code after linking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In 8086 assembly, modular programming relies on three pillars — (1) the `PROC`/`ENDP` directives, (2) the `CALL`/`RET` instruction pair, and (3) the `PUBLIC`/`EXTRN` directives for inter-module communication. These are mandatory items in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) for Module 1.

---

### 1.4 Types of Procedures in 8086
| Procedure Type | Pointer Size | Segment Behavior | Use Case |
|----------------|--------------|------------------|----------|
| **NEAR PROC** | 16-bit (IP only) | Within the same code segment | Subroutines used inside a single program |
| **FAR PROC** | 32-bit (CS:IP) | Across different code segments | Subroutines shared between independently assembled programs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CALL Instruction — The Trigger
The `CALL` instruction is used by the main program to **transfer control** to a procedure. Before transferring, the CPU automatically pushes the return address onto the **Stack**, so that the procedure can return to the calling point.

#### 2.1.1 Near CALL (Intra-Segment)
- Operand is a 16-bit signed displacement.
- Operates within the current code segment.
- The CPU pushes `IP` (16-bit) onto the stack.
- New `IP` is calculated as: `IP_new = IP_current + displacement`.

#### 2.1.2 Far CALL (Inter-Segment)
- Operand is a 32-bit full address (`segment:offset`).
- Used when the procedure is in a different code segment.
- The CPU pushes `CS` first, then `IP`, onto the stack.
- Both `CS` and `IP` are reloaded from the operand.

### 2.2 The RET Instruction — The Return
The `RET` (Return) instruction pops the return address from the stack and transfers control back to the instruction immediately following the `CALL` in the calling program.
- In a **NEAR** procedure, it pops the 16-bit `IP`.
- In a **FAR** procedure, it pops `IP` first, then `CS`.
- **Optional operand form:** `RET n` (e.g., `RET 4`) — pops the return address and *additionally* discards `n` bytes from the stack (used to clean up parameters passed via the stack).

### 2.3 Parameter Passing Techniques (KTU High-Weight Topic)
There are **three principal methods** to pass data to and from procedures:

| Method | Mechanism | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|--------|-----------|------------|---------------|
| **Using Registers** | Pass values in CPU registers (e.g., `AX`, `BX`) | Fastest; simplest for small data | Limited by number of registers |
| **Using Memory Variables** | Place data in a fixed memory location | Unlimited data size | Slow; risk of accidental overwriting |
| **Using the Stack** | Push parameters onto stack before `CALL` | Re-entrant; clean; standard for C calls | Slower; requires stack management |

### 2.4 Modular Programming Directives (Assembler-Level)
| Directive | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| `PROC` | Begins a procedure definition | `MY_PROC PROC NEAR` |
| `ENDP` | Ends a procedure definition | `MY_PROC ENDP` |
| `PUBLIC` | Declares a label **accessible** to other modules | `PUBLIC SUM, AVG` |
| `EXTRN` | Declares a label **defined in another module** | `EXTRN SUM:FAR` |
| `SEGMENT` | Begins a logical segment | `DATA_SEG SEGMENT` |
| `ENDS` | Ends a segment | `DATA_SEG ENDS` |
| `ASSUME` | Tells the assembler which segment register points to which segment | `ASSUME CS:CODE_SEG` |
| `END` | Marks the end of the source file and the entry point | `END START` |

### 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU Exam-Ready** and have appeared in past question papers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Type |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Near CALL Stack Effect | `SP ← SP - 2`, `SS:SP ← IP_next` | 16-bit push |
| 2 | Far CALL Stack Effect | `SP ← SP - 2`, `SS:SP ← CS`, `SP ← SP - 2`, `SS:SP ← IP` | Two 16-bit pushes (32-bit total) |
| 3 | Near RET | `IP ← SS:SP`, `SP ← SP + 2` | 16-bit pop |
| 4 | Far RET | `IP ← SS:SP`, `SP ← SP + 2`, `CS ← SS:SP`, `SP ← SP + 2` | Two 16-bit pops |
| 5 | Effective Address of Near CALL | $IP_{new} = IP_{current} + \text{displacement}_{16}$ | Signed 16-bit |
| 6 | Stack Frame of Nested Calls | Total stack usage = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{bytes pushed by call } i)$ | Bytes |
| 7 | RET n Effect | Pops return + adds `n` to SP | Bytes discarded |
| 8 | Re-entrancy Condition | All local data must reside on the **stack** (not in fixed memory) | Boolean |
| 9 | Parameter Position on Stack | Parameters are pushed in **reverse order** (last parameter first) for C-style calls | Convention |
| 10 | Return Value Convention (AX/DX) | 16-bit return → `AX`; 32-bit return → `DX:AX` | Registers |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
In real-time embedded systems (automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs, medical devices), modular programming allows firmware teams to:
- **Maintain safety-critical code** as discrete, certifiable modules (e.g., ISO 26262 compliance).
- **Re-use verified modules** across multiple product variants.
- **Perform unit testing** on individual modules before integration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Stack Trace: A NEAR CALL and RET Pair

Consider this 8086 assembly sequence:

```assembly
MAIN    PROC
        MOV  AX, 1234H
        CALL SUB1          ; <-- Line A: call subroutine
        ADD  BX, CX        ; <-- Line B: execution resumes here after RET
        ...
MAIN    ENDP

SUB1    PROC  NEAR
        PUSH BP            ; save BP
        MOV  BP, SP        ; establish stack frame
        MOV  CX, 0005H
        POP  BP
        RET                ; return to Line B
SUB1    ENDP
```

**Stack Trace (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

| Step | Instruction | Action | Stack (Top → Bottom) | SP Value |
|------|-------------|--------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | `MOV AX, 1234H` | Loads AX with 1234H | (empty) | 0FFFEH |
| 2 | `CALL SUB1` | Pushes IP of next instruction (Line B) | `[IP_B]` | 0FFFCH |
| 3 | | IP is loaded with address of SUB1 | `[IP_B]` | 0FFFCH |
| 4 | `PUSH BP` | Saves old BP onto stack | `[BP_old, IP_B]` | 0FFFAH |
| 5 | `MOV BP, SP` | BP now points to current top | `[BP_old, IP_B]` | 0FFFAH |
| 6 | `MOV CX, 0005H` | CX ← 5 | `[BP_old, IP_B]` | 0FFFAH |
| 7 | `POP BP` | Restores BP from stack | `[IP_B]` | 0FFFCH |
| 8 | `RET` | Pops IP_B into IP | (empty) | 0FFFEH |
| 9 | `ADD BX, CX` | Execution resumes at Line B | (empty) | 0FFFEH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The net effect of `CALL SUB1` + `RET` is that the stack pointer returns to **exactly the same value** it had before the `CALL`. This is the foundational invariant of every well-behaved subroutine.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Parameter Passing — Stack Method

Suppose the C-like call: `result = ADD_TWO(a, b);` where `a=5`, `b=7`. In 8086 assembly using the **stack method**:

**Step 1: Pushing parameters (reverse order)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 2 \\
\text{SS:SP} &\leftarrow 7 \quad \text{(push b first, because of reverse order convention)} \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 2 \\
\text{SS:SP} &\leftarrow 5 \quad \text{(push a)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: CALL instruction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 2 \\
\text{SS:SP} &\leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{return}} \quad \text{(NEAR call pushes only IP)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Inside the procedure, accessing parameters via `BP`**

After the standard prologue (`PUSH BP; MOV BP, SP`), the parameters can be accessed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &\rightarrow [\text{BP} + 4] \\
b &\rightarrow [\text{BP} + 6] \\
\text{Return Address} &\rightarrow [\text{BP} + 2] \\
\text{Old BP} &\rightarrow [\text{BP} + 0] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of Offsets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset of } a &= \text{(return address)} + \text{(old BP)} + \text{(offset of } b) \\
&= 2 + 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bytes above BP} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cleaning up — `RET 4`**

After computing the result (placed in `AX`), the procedure uses `RET 4`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP} &\leftarrow \text{SS:SP} \quad \text{(pop return address)} \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} + 2 \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} + 4 \quad \text{(discard 4 bytes of parameters)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Re-entrant Procedure — Formal Conditions
A procedure is **re-entrant** if it can be safely interrupted in the middle of its execution and re-entered by the same or a different caller. The formal conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re-entrant} \iff & (\text{All local data on stack}) \\
                        & \land (\text{No global/static variables modified}) \\
                        & \land (\text{No use of fixed memory addresses}) \\
                        & \land (\text{All I/O ports accessed via parameters or stack}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Re-entrancy is the key feature that allows **recursive procedures** and **interrupt service routines (ISRs)** to be safe. If a procedure is not re-entrant, an interrupt occurring mid-execution could corrupt its local state and crash the system.

### 3.4 Cross-Module Linkage: The EXTRN / PUBLIC Dance

When two separately assembled files (modules) are linked:

**Module 1 (Definer):**
```assembly
        PUBLIC  CALC_SUM
CODE1   SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:CODE1
CALC_SUM PROC FAR
        ; ... implementation ...
        RET
CALC_SUM ENDP
CODE1   ENDS
        END
```

**Module 2 (User):**
```assembly
        EXTRN   CALC_SUM:FAR
CODE2   SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:CODE2
START:  CALL    CALC_SUM   ; linker resolves this FAR call
        ...
CODE2   ENDS
        END     START
```

**Linker Resolution Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CALL FAR} \implies & \text{Push CS, Push IP} \\
\text{Linker patches} \implies & \text{FAR address} = \text{Segment of CALC_SUM : Offset of CALC_SUM} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The linker reads the `EXTRN` declaration in Module 2, locates the `PUBLIC` symbol in Module 1, computes a 32-bit physical address, and rewrites the `CALL` instruction's immediate operand accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: CALL and RET Execution Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Main Program executing"] -->|CALL SUB1| B["Push return address IP_next onto stack"]
    B --> C["Load IP with address of SUB1"]
    C --> D["Begin executing SUB1"]
    D --> E{"Is SUB1 a NEAR or FAR procedure?"}
    E -->|NEAR| F["Stack has 16-bit return IP"]
    E -->|FAR| G["Stack has 32-bit return CS:IP"]
    F --> H["Execute procedure body"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Encounter RET instruction"]
    I --> J["Pop return address from stack into IP and CS"]
    J --> K["Restore stack pointer SP"]
    K --> L["Resume execution in main program at instruction after CALL"]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Modular Program Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAIN["Main Module: Main.asm"]
        M1["Main Program"]
        M2["Call Sub1"]
        M3["Call Sub2"]
        M4["Call Sub3"]
    end

    subgraph LIB["Library Module: Lib.asm"]
        S1["Sub1: Input routine"]
        S2["Sub2: Computation routine"]
        S3["Sub3: Output routine"]
    end

    M1 --> M2 --> S1
    M1 --> M3 --> S2
    M1 --> M4 --> S3

    S1 --> R1["Return to main"]
    S2 --> R2["Return to main"]
    S3 --> R3["Return to main"]

    R1 --> M1
    R2 --> M1
    R3 --> M1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stack Frame During a Nested Call

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H["Higher Memory Addresses"] --> L["Lower Memory Addresses"]
    A["Old BP of Main"] --> B["Return Address to Main"]
    B --> C["Parameters passed to Sub1"]
    C --> D["Old BP of Sub1 saved by PUSH BP"]
    D --> E["Local variables of Sub1"]
    E --> F["Return Address to Sub1 from Sub2"]
    F --> G["Parameters passed to Sub2"]
    G --> I["Local variables of Sub2 TOP OF STACK"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Cross-Module Linkage

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Main Module
    participant L as Linker
    participant S as Subroutine Module
    M->>L: Declaration EXTRN CALC_SUM FAR
    S->>L: Declaration PUBLIC CALC_SUM
    L->>M: Patch CALL instruction with FAR address
    L->>S: Combine object code into single EXE
    M->>S: CALL CALC_SUM at runtime
    S->>M: RET returns control to Main
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is modular programming? List any two advantages of modular programming in 8086 assembly language.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Modular programming is a software design approach where a large program is divided into smaller, independent, and reusable sub-programs called modules or procedures. In 8086 assembly, each module is written as a separate `PROC` block and combined using the assembler and linker.

**Two advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Reusability:** A procedure can be called multiple times from different parts of the program, reducing code duplication.
2. **Easy debugging and maintenance:** Errors can be localized to a specific module, making the program easier to test and modify.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a NEAR procedure and a FAR procedure in 8086.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | NEAR Procedure | FAR Procedure |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **Pointer size** | 16-bit (IP only) | 32-bit (CS:IP) |
| **Segment** | Same code segment | Different code segment |
| **CALL type** | Intra-segment call | Inter-segment call |
| **Stack push** | Pushes only IP (2 bytes) | Pushes CS then IP (4 bytes) |
| **Use case** | Internal subroutines | External / library modules |

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the role of the `CALL` and `RET` instructions in implementing a subroutine in 8086. Differentiate between **Near CALL** and **Far CALL** with neat stack diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to find the **sum of two 16-bit numbers** using a procedure. Pass the parameters **using registers** and return the result in `AX`. Show the complete program with a `MAIN` module and a `SUM` procedure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the `CALL` instruction.
The `CALL` instruction is used to transfer program control from the main program to a procedure (subroutine). Before jumping, it automatically pushes the address of the next instruction (the return address) onto the stack.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the `RET` instruction.
The `RET` instruction, placed at the end of a procedure, pops the return address from the stack and transfers control back to the instruction immediately following the `CALL`.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Near CALL mechanics.
- Operates within the same code segment.
- Pushes a 16-bit IP onto the stack (`SP ← SP - 2; SS:SP ← IP`).
- Loads new IP from the displacement operand.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Far CALL mechanics.
- Operates across code segments.
- Pushes CS first, then IP, onto the stack (4 bytes total).
- Loads both CS and IP from the 32-bit operand.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Stack diagrams.

```
Near CALL stack:                Far CALL stack:

    [  IP of next instr  ]          [  CS of caller  ]
    [    Old SP →        ]          [  IP of next    ]
                                    [    Old SP →    ]
```

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

```assembly
; ------- MAIN MODULE -------
DATA    SEGMENT
        NUM1    DW  1234H
        NUM2    DW  00ABH
        RESULT  DW  ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:CODE, DS:DATA

START:  MOV     AX, DATA
        MOV     DS, AX
        MOV     AX, NUM1       ; AX = first number
        MOV     BX, NUM2       ; BX = second number
        CALL    ADD_TWO        ; call the procedure
        MOV     RESULT, AX     ; store the sum
        MOV     AH, 4CH
        INT     21H            ; exit to DOS

; ------- PROCEDURE MODULE -------
ADD_TWO PROC    NEAR
        ADD     AX, BX         ; sum = NUM1 + NUM2
        RET                    ; return to main
ADD_TWO ENDP

CODE    ENDS
        END     START
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The main program loads `NUM1` into `AX` and `NUM2` into `BX` — this is the **register-based parameter passing** method.
- `CALL ADD_TWO` transfers control to the procedure.
- Inside `ADD_TWO`, the sum `AX + BX` is computed and stored in `AX` — this is the **return value convention**.
- `RET` returns to the main program, which stores `AX` into `RESULT`.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, adapted]*

**(a)** Explain the **three methods of parameter passing** to procedures in 8086 with suitable examples. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of `PUBLIC` and `EXTRN` directives in modular programming. Write a short program showing how two separate modules (MAIN and HELPER) communicate with each other. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Method 1: Using Registers (2 marks)**
- **How:** Parameters are loaded into CPU registers (e.g., `AX`, `BX`, `CX`) before the `CALL`.
- **Example:** `MOV AX, NUM1; MOV BX, NUM2; CALL ADD_TWO`
- **Advantage:** Fastest execution; minimal memory access.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited to the small number of registers available.

**Method 2: Using Memory (2 marks)**
- **How:** Parameters are stored in fixed memory locations shared by caller and callee.
- **Example:** Variables `PARM1` and `PARM2` in a `DATA` segment, both modules access them.
- **Advantage:** No limit on data size or number of parameters.
- **Disadvantage:** Slower due to memory access; risk of side-effects from accidental modification.

**Method 3: Using the Stack (3 marks)**
- **How:** Parameters are pushed onto the stack in reverse order before `CALL`. The procedure uses `BP` to access them at `[BP+4]`, `[BP+6]`, etc.
- **Example:**
```assembly
        PUSH  NUM2
        PUSH  NUM1
        CALL  ADD_TWO
        ; Result in AX; cleanup with RET 4
```
- **Advantage:** Enables re-entrant and recursive procedures; clean separation of data.
- **Disadvantage:** Slower (stack accesses); requires careful stack management.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of PUBLIC and EXTRN (3 marks):**
- `PUBLIC` declares a symbol (label, variable, or procedure) as **accessible from other modules**. Without this directive, a label is private to its own source file.
- `EXTRN` declares a symbol that is **used in the current module but defined in another module**. The assembler needs this declaration to generate the correct instruction encoding (NEAR vs FAR).

**Two-Module Program (4 marks):**

**Module 1: `HELPER.asm`**
```assembly
        PUBLIC  CALC_AVG
HELPER  SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:HELPER

CALC_AVG PROC FAR
        ; takes sum in AX, count in BX; returns average in AX
        DIV     BX          ; AX = sum / count
        RET
CALC_AVG ENDP

HELPER  ENDS
        END
```

**Module 2: `MAIN.asm`**
```assembly
        EXTRN   CALC_AVG:FAR
MAIN    SEGMENT
        ASSUME  CS:MAIN

START:  MOV     AX, 0100H   ; sum = 256
        MOV     BX, 0010H   ; count = 16
        CALL    CALC_AVG    ; linker patches FAR address
        MOV     AH, 4CH
        INT     21H

MAIN    ENDS
        END     START
```

**Linker Action:** When the linker combines `MAIN.obj` and `HELPER.obj`, it sees the `EXTRN CALC_AVG:FAR` in MAIN and the `PUBLIC CALC_AVG` in HELPER, and rewrites the `CALL` instruction in MAIN with the 32-bit address of `CALC_AVG` in HELPER's code segment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting `RET` in a procedure** → Causes the program to fall through to whatever code follows, leading to crashes and full 0 marks on the procedure question.
> 2. **Confusing NEAR and FAR stack sizes** → NEAR pushes 2 bytes, FAR pushes 4 bytes. Mixing this up will lose 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Not preserving registers** → If a procedure modifies `BX`, `CX`, or `SI/DI`, it must push them at the start and pop at the end. Forgetting this corrupts the caller's data.
> 4. **Wrong offset in stack parameter access** → A common student error is `[BP+2]` for parameters, but the return address sits at `[BP+2]`. The first parameter is at `[BP+4]`.
> 5. **Forgetting to set `DS`** in multi-module programs → Causes the linker to produce code that crashes at runtime.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Modular Programming:**

- **Definition:** Modular programming breaks a program into independent procedures; each procedure is a `PROC` block ending with `ENDP`.
- **CALL** transfers control to a procedure; it pushes the return address automatically.
- **RET** returns control to the caller; it pops the return address automatically.
- **NEAR PROC** → 16-bit IP pushed, same segment.
- **FAR PROC** → 32-bit CS:IP pushed, different segment.
- **Parameter passing** — three methods: (1) Registers, (2) Memory, (3) Stack.
- **Stack parameter access** — first parameter is at `[BP+4]`, second at `[BP+6]`, etc., when `PUSH BP; MOV BP, SP` is used as prologue.
- **RET n** — used to discard `n` bytes of stack parameters after popping the return address.
- **PUBLIC** — makes a label visible to other modules; used in the defining module.
- **EXTRN** — declares a label used but defined elsewhere; used in the calling module.
- **Re-entrant procedures** — must store ALL local data on the stack, never in global/static memory.
- **Register preservation rule** — `BP`, `SI`, `DI`, `BX`, `CX`, `DX` are caller-saved by the callee using `PUSH`/`POP` pairs.
- **NEAR call instruction size** — 3 bytes (`opcode + 16-bit displacement`).
- **FAR call instruction size** — 5 bytes (`opcode + 16-bit offset + 16-bit segment`).
- **Standard C calling convention (cdecl)** — parameters pushed right-to-left, caller cleans up the stack with `RET n` or `ADD SP, n`.
- **Why modular?** — readability, reusability, debuggability, team development, and memory efficiency after linking.
- **KTU typical 14-mark question pattern** — (a) explain theory with diagrams, (b) write a complete program with separate `MAIN` and procedure modules, include comments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linking and Relocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linking and Relocation # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Linking** is the post-assembly process of combining multiple independently assembled object modules (programs and subroutines) into a single, unified executable program, while resolving all external symbol references (labels defined in other modules) and producing a coherent logical address space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Relocation** is the mechanism by which the **Linker/Loader** modifies the absolute or relative addresses within the object code so that the complete program can be loaded and executed from *any* available memory location, rather than being permanently fixed to the addresses assumed at assembly time. ### The Two Faces of the Same Coin | Process | Stage | Primary Function | Operates On | |---|---|---|---| | **Linking** | Assembly → Executable | Combines modules, resolves symbols | Object files (.OBJ) | | **Relocation** | Linking → Loading | Adjusts addresses for load location | Final code offsets | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego City" Analogy 🧱 Imagine you are an architect building a **Lego City**. The city has different sections: a hospital, a school, a fire station. You don't build the entire city at once. Instead: - **Each section (hospital, school) is built independently on a separate table** — these are your *source modules*. - Each section has instructions saying things like *"Place the red door on the main entrance"* (these are **local labels**). - Some sections need parts from another section: *"Connect the hospital's emergency exit to the school playground"* — this is an **external reference**. Now, when you finally combine all sections onto one big baseplate (the **single memory map**): 1. You must **decide the final position** of each section on the big baseplate → this is **relocation**. 2. You must **fix all cross-references** so doors connect to the right buildings → this is **linking**. If you moved the hospital from one corner to another, the connection to the school would break — *unless* the linker had already recorded offsets rather than fixed coordinates. This is exactly why **relocatable code** is essential in modern embedded systems like 8086, ARM, and 8051-based controllers. --- ## 1.3 Why KTU Emphasizes This Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU's outcome-based education framework, this topic directly maps to **CO1 (Understanding architecture and assembly programming)** of OECIT615. Every student must understand **why** an assembler cannot produce a fully executable file in a single pass when programs span multiple files — the linker/loader duo is the answer. ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Object File Magic Number**: Typically `0x01` (or vendor-specific) to identify relocatable code. - **Word Size for Address Calculations**: **16-bit** for 8086 (giving 1 MB address space = **1,048,576 bytes**), **8-bit** for 8051 (giving 64 KB address space). - **Standard Relocation Entry Size**: Typically **2 bytes** (relocation type + offset) per fixup record. # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Code Types ### 🔹 A. Absolute Code (Position-Dependent) - Addresses are **hard-coded** into the machine instructions at assembly time. - The program **can only run** at the exact memory location for which it was assembled. - **Zero relocation effort** is needed — the loader just copies bytes. - Used in: simple bootloaders, ROM-resident firmware, very small embedded systems with a fixed memory map. ### 🔹 B. Relocatable Code (Position-Independent) - Addresses are expressed as **offsets from a base address** (often `0000H`). - The final physical address is computed only at **link/load time** using the formula: $$\text{Actual Address} = \text{Base Address} + \text{Offset}$$ - **Mandatory** for modular programs, shared libraries, and position-independent executables (PIE). --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Linking & Relocation ### 🔸 Phase 1: Assembly of Each Module 1. The assembler reads the source file. 2. It performs **two passes**: - **Pass 1**: Builds the symbol table, calculates LC (Location Counter) for every label. - **Pass 2**: Generates the object code, leaving placeholders (`?????` or `0`) for external symbols. 3. Output: An **Object Module** containing: - The **Header Record** (program name, length, start address). - **Text Records** (machine code + relocatable addresses). - **Modification/Relocation Records** (which addresses must be patched). - **End Record** (marks file end). ### 🔸 Phase 2: Linking 1. The **Linker** reads all object modules specified in the input. 2. It builds a **Global Symbol Table (GST)** — a dictionary of every label exported by every module. 3. It assigns each module a unique **starting address** in the combined logical address space. 4. It **resolves all external references** by substituting placeholder values with computed real addresses. 5. Output: A single **Linked Executable File** (often in HEX or absolute binary format). ### 🔸 Phase 3: Loading & Relocation 1. The **Loader** reads the executable file. 2. It selects a **Base Address** in physical RAM where the program will reside. 3. It **walks through every relocation record** and patches each address: $$\text{New Address} = \text{Old Address} + (\text{New Base} - \text{Original Assumed Base})$$ 4. It transfers control to the program's **entry point**. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is your **exam-ready reference**. Memorize these before any Module 1 test. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Relocated Address** | $\text{Addr}_{\text{final}} = \text{Base}_{\text{load}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{link}}$ | Bytes/words | | **Address Space (8086)** | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes (per segment); $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes (total) | Segmented memory | | **Address Space (8051)** | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes (Harvard) | Code + XDATA combined | | **Block Relocation** | $\text{Shift} = \text{New\_Start} - \text{Original\_Start}$ | Single constant per module | | **External Reference Count** | $\text{ER} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Refs}(M_i)$ | Sum across all modules | | **Total Code Size** | $\text{Size} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Size}(M_i)$ | Bytes | | **Symbol Table Entry** | $(\text{Name}, \text{Value}, \text{Type}, \text{Scope})$ | Quaternary tuple | | **Relocation Type** | `Absolute` $\vert$ `Relative` $\vert$ `Base-Relative` $\vert$ `PC-Relative` | Field tag in record | > **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked "define relocation," **always** state the formula and explain that offsets are pre-computed by the assembler, and the **loader** performs the final addition. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of Linking/Relocation | |---|---| | **Embedded Firmware (8051/ARM)** | Modular driver libraries (UART, ADC) linked into a single HEX file | | **Operating Systems** | Dynamic linking of shared `.so`/`.dll` files at runtime | | **Bootloaders** | Relocating code from Flash to RAM for faster execution | | **IoT Devices** | Position-Independent Code (PIC) for over-the-air (OTA) updates | | **Real-Time Systems** | Static linking ensures deterministic load times (no dynamic resolution) | # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Linking Two 8051 Assembly Modules ### 🔹 Problem Setup We have **two source files** that will be linked together: **File 1: `MAIN.ASM`** ```asm ORG 0000H LJMP START ORG 0040H START: MOV DPTR, #TABLE ; External reference to TABLE MOVC A, @A+DPTR LCALL DELAY ; External reference to DELAY SJMP START END ``` **File 2: `UTILS.ASM`** ```asm ORG 0000H ; Assembled independently DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH LOOP1: MOV R1, #0FFH LOOP2: DJNZ R1, LOOP2 DJNZ R0, LOOP1 RET TABLE: DB 0C0H, 0F9H, 0A4H, 0B0H, 99H END ``` ### 🔹 Step 1: Independent Assembly Each file is assembled **as if it starts at 0000H**. The assembler produces two **relocatable object files**. ### 🔹 Step 2: Linker Creates a Unified Address Map Assume the linker assigns: | Module | Start Address | Size | |---|---|---| | `MAIN.OBJ` | `0000H` | 8 bytes (code) | | `UTILS.OBJ` | `0008H` | 9 bytes (code + data) | > Note: In real KTU exam problems, the linker's address assignments are **given to you** — you don't have to compute them. ### 🔹 Step 3: Relocation Table Generated The linker examines every **external reference** in `MAIN.OBJ`: | Reference | Type | Offset in MAIN | Final Address (Base 0000H + Offset) | |---|---|---|---| | `TABLE` | Data label (8-bit) | `4044H` field | `0008H + 6 = 000EH` | | `DELAY` | Code label (16-bit) | `4006H` field | `0008H + 0 = 0008H` | ### 🔹 Step 4: Address Patching — The Core Math The LCALL instruction at offset `0006H` in `MAIN.OBJ` originally contained the placeholder `????` for `DELAY`. The linker patches it: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Original Code at } & \text{offset 0006H in MAIN.OBJ:} \\ & 12 \; \text{?? ??} \quad \text{(LCALL opcode 12H + 16-bit address placeholder)} \\ \\ \text{Relocation Formula:} & \\ \text{Final Address} & = \text{Base}_{\text{UTILS}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{DELAY within UTILS}} \\ & = 0008\text{H} + 0 \\ & = 0008\text{H} \\ \\ \text{Patched Code:} & \quad 12 \; 08 \; 00 \quad \text{(LCALL 0008H)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 🔹 Step 5: Verification After linking, when the program is loaded at base `0000H` (or any other base), the `LCALL DELAY` instruction correctly jumps to the `DELAY` routine because **both the code and the reference have been adjusted by the same offset**. --- ## 3.2 Generic Relocation Algorithm — Exhaustive Derivation Given a relocatable object module $M_i$ assembled at base $B_i^{\text{asm}} = 0$ (relative), containing $k$ relocatable address fields, and loaded at physical base $B_i^{\text{load}}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Identify the load base } & B_i^{\text{load}} \text{ assigned by the loader.} \\ \\ \text{Step 2: For each relocatable field } & f_j \text{ in } M_i, \text{ read its offset } o_j. \\ \\ \text{Step 3: Compute the relocation delta:} & \\ \Delta_i & = B_i^{\text{load}} - B_i^{\text{asm}} \\ & = B_i^{\text{load}} - 0 \\ & = B_i^{\text{load}} \\ \\ \text{Step 4: Patch the field:} & \\ \text{Value}_{\text{final}}(f_j) & = \text{Value}_{\text{old}}(f_j) + \Delta_i \\ & = \text{Value}_{\text{old}}(f_j) + B_i^{\text{load}} \\ \\ \text{Step 5: Write the patched word back to memory.} & \\ \\ \text{Step 6: Repeat Step 2--5 for all } j \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}. & \\ \\ \text{Step 7: Jump to entry point } E_i = & B_i^{\text{load}} + o_{\text{entry}}. \end{aligned} $$ ### 🔹 Special Case: PC-Relative Relocation For branch instructions like `SJMP`, the assembler stores the **displacement**, not the absolute target. So: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Displacement}_{\text{old}} & = \text{Target}_{\text{asm}} - (\text{PC}_{\text{asm}} + 2) \\ \text{Displacement}_{\text{new}} & = \text{Target}_{\text{final}} - (\text{PC}_{\text{final}} + 2) \\ & = (\text{Target}_{\text{asm}} + \Delta) - (\text{PC}_{\text{asm}} + \Delta + 2) \\ & = \text{Target}_{\text{asm}} - \text{PC}_{\text{asm}} - 2 \\ & = \text{Displacement}_{\text{old}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **PC-relative branches need NO relocation** — the $\Delta$ cancels out. This is why most assemblers prefer short branches (SJMP, BNE, etc.) for relocatable code. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Absolute Code | Relocatable Code | |---|---|---| | **Assembly Output** | Fixed addresses | Base-relative offsets | | **External Reference Handling** | None (single file) | Symbol resolution required | | **Load Address Flexibility** | ❌ Fixed | ✅ Any free location | | **Relocation Cost** | $0$ operations | $k$ additions (where $k$ = #reloc fields) | | **Typical Use Case** | Boot ROM, tiny MCUs | Modular firmware, OS code | | **Size Overhead** | None | 1 relocation record per address | | **Linker Required?** | No (only loader) | Yes | | **KTU Question Likelihood** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Linking & Relocation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Source Module 1
MAIN.ASM"] --> B["Assembler Pass 1
Build Symbol Table"] A1["Source Module 2
UTILS.ASM"] --> B1["Assembler Pass 1
Build Symbol Table"] B --> C["Object File 1
MAIN.OBJ"] B1 --> C1["Object File 2
UTILS.OBJ"] C --> D["LINKER
Resolves Symbols"] C1 --> D D --> E["Global Symbol Table
GST"] D --> F["Address Map"] D --> G["Linked Executable
FINAL.HEX"] G --> H["LOADER
Patches Relocation Records"] H --> I["Physical Memory
Execution Ready"] I --> J["CPU Begins Execution
at Entry Point"] ``` ## 4.2 Relocation Record Structure (Block-Level View) ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Relocation Record"] --> R1["Field 1: Address Offset
2 bytes"] R --> R2["Field 2: Relocation Type
1 byte"] R2 --> R2a["Type 0: Absolute"] R2 --> R2b["Type 1: Base-Relative"] R2 --> R2c["Type 2: PC-Relative"] R2 --> R2d["Type 3: Segment-Relative"] R --> R3["Field 3: Symbol Index
2 bytes"] R --> R4["Field 4: Addend Constant
2 bytes"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Linker's Internal Workings ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT["Input: Object Files + Libraries"] --> SCAN["Scanner
Reads Headers"] SCAN --> GST["Global Symbol Table
Name Value Module"] GST --> ASSIGN["Address Assigner
Computes Base for Each Module"] ASSIGN --> RESOLVE["External Reference Resolver
Patches Address Fields"] RESOLVE --> EMIT["Emitter
Generates Output File"] EMIT --> OUTPUT["Linked Executable"] GST -.lookup.-> RESOLVE ASSIGN -.base address.-> RESOLVE ``` ## 4.4 Memory Map After Linking & Loading ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MEM["Physical Memory After Loading"] direction TB REGION1["Region A: MAIN Module
Base 0000H -- Size 8 bytes"] REGION2["Region B: UTILS Module
Base 0008H -- Size 9 bytes"] REGION3["Region C: Data Section
Base 0011H -- Free Space"] REGION4["Region D: Stack
Grows Downward"] end REGION1 -->|LCALL 0008H| REGION2 REGION2 -->|RET| REGION1 ``` ## 4.5 Decision Flow: Is Relocation Needed? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is the program
multifile?"} Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Is the load address
known at assembly?"} Q1 -->|Yes| R1["Relocatable code REQUIRED
Linker mandatory"] Q2 -->|Yes Fixed| A1["Absolute code
Simple loader only"] Q2 -->|No| R1 R1 --> R2["Assembler emits
relocation records"] R2 --> R3["Linker resolves
external symbols"] R3 --> R4["Loader patches
final addresses"] A1 --> A2["Loader copies bytes
No patching"] ``` # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the term "relocation" in the context of microprocessors. Why is it necessary?** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Relocation is the process of modifying the addresses used in a program (machine code) so that the program can be loaded and executed from any available memory location, rather than being restricted to the specific address for which it was originally assembled. > **Necessity (1.5 marks):** It is necessary because: > 1. Modular programs consist of multiple independently assembled object files whose final positions are not known until linking. > 2. The same program may be loaded at different addresses depending on system configuration (e.g., bootloader vs. main application). > 3. It enables **code reuse** — the same relocatable module can be linked into different projects. > 4. Embedded systems often need to run firmware from different memory regions (internal Flash, external ROM, etc.). --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between a Linker and a Loader. State one key function of each.** > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Linker | Loader | |---|---|---| | **Stage** | Post-assembly, pre-execution | Post-linking, execution time | | **Input** | Multiple `.OBJ` files | Single executable file (`.HEX`/`.EXE`) | | **Key Function** | Resolves external symbol references and combines object modules into one executable (1.5 marks) | Loads the executable into physical memory, performs final relocation (address patching), and transfers control to the entry point (1.5 marks) | --- --- ## 📚 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > ⚙️ **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You must attempt either **Q(A) OR Q(B)**. Solutions below show full KTU-style step-by-step work. --- ### **🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 — Adapted]** **Q(A)(a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a clear diagram, explain the **two-pass assembler** model and describe how the assembler generates the **relocation table** during Pass 2. List the typical fields of a relocation record. **(b) [7 marks]:** Two object modules `MOD1.OBJ` and `MOD2.OBJ` are to be linked. `MOD1` is assigned base `0200H` and contains an external call to a label `SUB` located at offset `0010H` inside `MOD2`. `MOD2` is assigned base `0300H`. Calculate the patched address that the linker will write into the LCALL instruction field. --- ### **✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A** #### Part (a): Two-Pass Assembler & Relocation Table **Step 1: Pass 1 of the Assembler (2 marks)** The first pass reads the entire source file **without generating any code**. Its jobs are: - Maintain a **Location Counter (LC)** that tracks the current address. - Build a **Symbol Table** mapping every label to its LC value. - Resolve all forward references (labels used before they are defined). - Handle assembler directives like `ORG`, `EQU`, `DB`, `DW`. **Step 2: Pass 2 of the Assembler (2 marks)** The second pass reads the source again, this time **generating machine code**: - Translates each mnemonic to its opcode. - Encodes operand addresses using the **offset from the module's base (0000H)**. - When an operand references an **external symbol** (defined in another module), the assembler emits a placeholder and creates a **relocation record**. **Step 3: Relocation Table — Typical Fields (3 marks)** | Field Name | Size (bytes) | Description | |---|---|---| | `Address Offset` | 2 | Location within the module where the fixup is needed | | `Relocation Type` | 1 | `0`=Absolute, `1`=Base-Rel, `2`=PC-Rel, `3`=Segment-Rel | | `Symbol Index` | 2 | Pointer into the External Symbol Table | | `Addend` | 2 | Constant to be added to the resolved address | **Diagram: Relocation Process** ``` ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Object Module │ │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Text Record │ │ │ │ 12 ?? ?? │◄──┼─── Relocation Record │ └──────────────┘ │ (points here, type=Base-Rel) │ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ Reloc Table │ │ │ │ Offset: 0006 │ │ │ │ Type: 01 │ │ │ │ SymIx: 0003 │ │ │ │ Addnd: 0000 │ │ │ └──────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────┘ ``` --- #### Part (b): Numerical Relocation Problem **Given Data:** - `MOD1.OBJ` base address: $B_1 = 0200\text{H}$ - `MOD2.OBJ` base address: $B_2 = 0300\text{H}$ - Target label `SUB` offset within `MOD2`: $o_{\text{SUB}} = 0010\text{H}$ - Instruction type: `LCALL` (16-bit absolute address required) **Step 1: Compute the final physical address of `SUB` (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Address}_{\text{SUB}} & = B_2 + o_{\text{SUB}} \\ & = 0300\text{H} + 0010\text{H} \\ & = 0310\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Determine the relocation delta for `MOD1` (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_1 & = B_1 - B_1^{\text{asm}} \\ & = 0200\text{H} - 0000\text{H} \\ & = 0200\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: State the patched value (2 marks)** The linker writes the bytes `10H 03H` (little-endian representation of `0310H`) into the LCALL instruction field at the corresponding offset in `MOD1`. **✅ Final Answer:** The LCALL instruction in `MOD1` will be patched to **`LCALL 0310H`**. --- ### **🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 — Model Paper]** **Q(B)(a) [7 marks]:** What is **Position-Independent Code (PIC)**? Explain with reference to 8051 assembly why PIC is difficult to achieve and how the linker compensates for it. **(b) [7 marks]:** A program consists of 3 modules with sizes 512 bytes, 1024 bytes, and 256 bytes. The loader places the first module at base `8000H`. Assuming sequential placement, compute the final physical addresses of: (i) the last byte of module 1, (ii) the first byte of module 3, and (iii) a label defined at offset `01F0H` in module 2. --- ### **✅ MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B** #### Part (a): Position-Independent Code (PIC) **Definition (2 marks):** Position-Independent Code is machine code that executes correctly **regardless of where it is loaded in memory**. All internal branches and data accesses use **PC-relative** or **base-register-relative** addressing rather than absolute addresses. **Why PIC is difficult in 8051 (3 marks):** - The 8051 has a **Harvard architecture** with separate code and data address spaces. - Instructions like `AJMP addr11` and `ACALL addr11` use an **11-bit absolute address** within a 2 KB page — these are inherently position-dependent. - Direct data accesses (`MOV A, 30H`) refer to the **internal RAM at fixed addresses 00H–7FH**, not the module base. - Only `SJMP rel` and conditional jumps (e.g., `JNZ rel`) are PC-relative and therefore position-independent. **How the linker compensates (2 marks):** - The linker scans all instructions and identifies those that use **absolute addressing** (like `AJMP`/`ACALL`/`LJMP`/`LCALL`). - For each such instruction, it emits a **relocation record**. - At load time, the loader adds the module's base address to the operand to produce the correct final target. - If the target falls outside the 2 KB page boundary, the linker may need to convert an `AJMP` into a 3-byte `LJMP` automatically. --- #### Part (b): Numerical Problem **Given Data:** - Module sizes: $S_1 = 512$ bytes, $S_2 = 1024$ bytes, $S_3 = 256$ bytes - Base address of Module 1: $B_1 = 8000\text{H}$ - Sequential placement: $B_{i+1} = B_i + S_i$ **Step 1: Compute base addresses (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} B_1 & = 8000\text{H} \\ B_2 & = B_1 + S_1 = 8000\text{H} + 200\text{H} = 8200\text{H} \\ B_3 & = B_2 + S_2 = 8200\text{H} + 400\text{H} = 8600\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Last byte of Module 1 (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Last}_{\text{M1}} & = B_1 + S_1 - 1 \\ & = 8000\text{H} + 200\text{H} - 1 \\ & = 81\text{FF}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: First byte of Module 3 (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{First}_{\text{M3}} & = B_3 = 8600\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Label at offset `01F0H` in Module 2 (1.5 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Addr}_{\text{label}} & = B_2 + 01\text{F0H} \\ & = 8200\text{H} + 01\text{F0H} \\ & = 83\text{F0H} \end{aligned} $$ **✅ Final Answers:** - (i) Last byte of Module 1: **`81FFH`** - (ii) First byte of Module 3: **`8600H`** - (iii) Label address: **`83F0H`** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks in Linking & Relocation questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Addend:** When the relocation record has a non-zero addend, students often write `Final = Base + Offset`, missing the `+ Addend` term. **Always read the relocation record fully.** > > 2. **Confusing PC-Relative with Absolute:** Students apply relocation to `SJMP rel` instructions. **DON'T** — PC-relative displacements are already position-independent. The $\Delta$ cancels out, as proved in Section 3.2. > > 3. **Little-Endian Mistake:** The 8051/8086 store multi-byte addresses in little-endian format. If the answer is `0310H`, write `10 03` in the memory dump, not `03 10`. KTU examiners explicitly check byte order. > > 4. **Missing the Difference Between Linker and Loader:** Many students write "linker loads the program." **NO.** The linker *generates* the executable; the *loader* places it in memory. This is a 1–2 mark penalty every time. > > 5. **Forgetting the Entry Point:** After loading, control transfers to $B_{\text{load}} + o_{\text{entry}}$. Not to address `0000H` unless explicitly stated. > > 6. **Skipping the Symbol Table:** A 7-mark question that asks "explain linking" **must** include the role of the Global Symbol Table (GST) to score full marks. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Linking & Relocation)** - ✅ **Linker** = combines object modules + resolves external symbols → produces a single executable. - ✅ **Loader** = places executable in physical memory + performs final address patching + jumps to entry point. - ✅ **Absolute code** = fixed addresses, no relocation, single-file programs. - ✅ **Relocatable code** = base-relative offsets, relocation records emitted, link/load-time address calculation. - ✅ **Core formula:** $\text{Final Address} = \text{Base}_{\text{load}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{link}}$ - ✅ **Two-pass assembler** — Pass 1 builds symbol table, Pass 2 generates code with relocatable offsets. - ✅ **Relocation record fields:** Address Offset, Type, Symbol Index, Addend. - ✅ **PC-relative branches** (SJMP, JNZ) need **NO relocation** — displacement is position-independent. - ✅ **Absolute branches** (LJMP, LCALL) **require relocation** — they encode 16-bit absolute targets. - ✅ **8051 constraint:** Harvard architecture makes PIC harder; `AJMP/ACALL` are page-dependent and need linker intervention. - ✅ **8086 quirk:** Segmented memory — relocation may apply to `segment:offset` pairs, not just flat addresses. - ✅ **Linker's GST** (Global Symbol Table) is the data structure that enables symbol resolution across modules. - ✅ **Sequential module placement:** $B_{i+1} = B_i + \text{Size}(M_i)$. - ✅ **Little-endian byte ordering** is standard for 8051 and 8086 — always write low byte first. - ✅ **KTU favourite marks-givers:** "Differentiate linker vs loader" (3 marks) and "compute final address after relocation" (7 marks) appear in nearly every exam cycle.

topics.title: Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack**: In the 8086 microprocessor, the *stack* is a dedicated region of read/write memory that operates on the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle. It is used by the processor for the temporary storage of data, return addresses, flags, and parameters during subroutine calls, interrupts, and certain data operations.

The stack in the 8086 is always **word-oriented** (16-bit / 2-byte). The stack grows **downward** in memory, meaning every new PUSH operation decrements the Stack Pointer before storing data, and every POP operation reads the data first and then increments the Stack Pointer.

Two dedicated registers govern the stack:

| Register | Width | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **SS (Stack Segment)** | 16-bit | Holds the base segment address of the stack region. |
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | 16-bit | Always points to the **Top-of-Stack (TOS)**, the last occupied location. |
| **BP (Base Pointer)** | 16-bit | Optional; used to access data within a stack frame without disturbing SP. |

The 20-bit physical address of the current TOS is generated by the Bus Interface Unit (BIU) using the formula:

$$PA_{stack} = SS \times 10H + SP$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stack of Plates"

Imagine a spring-loaded cafeteria plate dispenser:

- There is a **single opening** at the top — you can only add or remove a plate from this opening.
- The **most recent plate placed** is always the **first one taken out** (LIFO).
- A **counter (the spring)** automatically tracks the topmost plate; in the 8086, this counter is the **Stack Pointer (SP)**.
- The plates themselves are stored in a tall, fixed cabinet — the cabinet is the **Stack Segment (SS)**, and the cabinet's address label is the segment base.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Just as you cannot access a plate at the bottom of the dispenser without first removing the plates above it, the 8086 stack cannot be randomly indexed. Data is strictly accessed at the TOS pointed to by **SP**.

## 1.3 Salient Features of the 8086 Stack

- **LIFO Discipline**: Last pushed is the first to be popped.
- **Word-Sized Operations**: 8-bit PUSH/POP is illegal in 8086. (80186+ supports 8-bit.)
- **Downward Growth**: PUSH decrements SP, POP increments SP.
- **Resides in RAM**: Implemented in the standard memory map; no dedicated on-chip stack hardware.
- **Initialisation**: SS and SP must be initialised by the programmer/LOS (Loader) at the start of a program.
- **Used implicitly** by `CALL`, `RET`, `INT`, `IRET`, and `PUSHF`/`POPF` instructions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — PUSH and POP

### 2.1.1 PUSH Operation
When the 8086 executes `PUSH src` (where `src` may be a 16-bit register or a memory word):

1. The BIU **decrements SP by 2** — this allocates a new 16-bit slot pointing *below* the previous TOS.
2. The 16-bit source data is then **written to memory at SS:SP**.

Mathematically:

$$SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2$$

$$Memory[SS \times 10H + SP_{new}] \leftarrow \text{source word}$$

### 2.1.2 POP Operation
When the 8086 executes `POP dest`:

1. The 16-bit data is **read from memory at SS:SP** into the destination.
2. The BIU then **increments SP by 2** to point at the new TOS.

Mathematically:

$$\text{destination} \leftarrow Memory[SS \times 10H + SP_{old}]$$

$$SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Endianness Rule**: The 8086 is **little-endian**. In a PUSH, the low byte of the source goes to the lower address (the new SP), and the high byte to the higher address. For `PUSH AX` with AX = 1234H, AL = 34H is stored at SP and AH = 12H at SP+1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stack Physical Address | $PA = SS \times 10H + SP$ | 20-bit, range `00000H`–`FFFFFH` |
| SP after PUSH | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2$ | SP wraps modulo $2^{16}$ (65 536) |
| SP after POP | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2$ | Same wrap-around |
| Bytes per stack operation | 2 bytes (one word) | Always even-aligned at TOS |
| Max stack depth (SP space) | $2^{16} = 65\,536$ bytes | 32 768 words |
| Default stack size in DOS | 64 KB | Reserved in PSP at program load |
| CALL pushes IP only (near) | $SP \leftarrow SP - 2;\; SS:SP \leftarrow IP$ | Returns with RET |
| CALL pushes CS:IP (far) | Two PUSHes, total $SP - 4$ | Returns with RETF |
| INT pushes FLAGS, CS, IP | Three PUSHes, total $SP - 6$ | Returns with IRET |

## 2.3 Why Does the 8086 Stack Grow *Downward*?

The downward growth is **a design choice, not a constraint** of physics. The reasons are:

- **Efficiency in subroutine calls**: A CALL only needs to allocate space by decrementing SP; it does not have to search for free space upward. The downward region is virtually always free at the start of a routine.
- **Protection of program code**: The stack grows *away* from the code segment (CS), preventing accidental overwrites.
- **Convention**: It is the same model used by Intel 8080/8085 emulators running on 8086 in *8086 Emulation Mode*, preserving software compatibility.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler backend**: Compilers for C, Pascal, and Ada use the stack to pass parameters, allocate local (auto) variables, and save callee-saved registers.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)**: Hardware interrupts use the stack to save the FLAGS, CS, and IP, allowing nested interrupts and automatic return.
- **Operating System kernels**: Context switching in RTOS (e.g., VxWorks, FreeRTOS ported to 80186) saves task state on the per-task stack.
- **Embedded firmware**: Every function call in a `gcc -m16` or `Keil` C51-emulated 8051 hybrid stack uses the same PUSH/POP micro-operations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Physical Address Calculation

**Problem**: Given `SS = 4000H` and `SP = 0100H`, compute the physical address of the current Top-of-Stack.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula**.

$$PA = SS \times 10H + SP$$

**Step 2 — Convert the segment base to its physical form (append a hex `0`)**.

$$SS \times 10H = 4000H \times 10H = 40000H$$

> This works because appending a `0` in hexadecimal is mathematically identical to multiplying by 16 (10H).

**Step 3 — Add the offset (SP)**.

$$PA = 40000H + 0100H = 40100H$$

**Result**: `PA = 40100H` — the BIU accesses this 20-bit physical address whenever it reads or writes the stack.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PUSH AX Trace

**Initial Conditions**:

$$SS = 4000H, \quad SP = 0100H, \quad AX = 1234H$$

**Instruction**: `PUSH AX`

**Step 1 — Decrement SP by 2**.

$$SP_{new} = 0100H - 0002H = 00FEH$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new physical address**.

$$PA_{new} = 40000H + 00FEH = 400FEH$$

**Step 3 — Store the 16-bit word at SS:SP (little-endian)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory}[400FEH] &\leftarrow AL = 34H \\
\text{Memory}[400FFH] &\leftarrow AH = 12H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Snapshot**:

| Address | Byte |
| :---: | :---: |
| `400FEH` | `34H` |
| `400FFH` | `12H` |
| **SP** | **`00FEH`** |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — POP BX Trace (continuing from Example 2)

**Instruction**: `POP BX`

**Step 1 — Read the word at the current TOS into BX (little-endian)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BL &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[400FEH] = 34H \\
BH &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[400FFH] = 12H
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, `BX = 1234H`.

**Step 2 — Increment SP by 2**.

$$SP_{new} = 00FEH + 0002H = 0100H$$

**Result**: The original SP value is restored, and BX now holds `1234H`.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stack Interaction with Key Instructions

| Instruction | Bytes Pushed / Popped | Stack Order (top → bottom) | Return Mechanism |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `PUSH reg/mem` | 2 bytes (1 word) | data | `POP` |
| `POP reg/mem` | 2 bytes (1 word) | — | — |
| `CALL NEAR proc` | 2 bytes | IP | `RET` (near) |
| `CALL FAR proc` | 4 bytes | IP, CS | `RETF` |
| `INT n` | 6 bytes | IP, CS, FLAGS | `IRET` |
| `INTO` (overflow) | 6 bytes | IP, CS, FLAGS | `IRET` |
| `PUSHF` | 2 bytes | FLAGS | `POPF` |
| `POPF` | 2 bytes | — | — |

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Stack Effect of a Near CALL

**Initial Conditions**:

$$SS = 2000H, \quad SP = 1000H, \quad IP = 0050H$$

**Instruction**: `CALL NEAR SUB1` (call offset is, say, `0200H`)

**Step 1 — Push IP onto the stack**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &\leftarrow SP - 2 = 0FFEH \\
\text{Memory}[200FEH] &\leftarrow 50H \\
\text{Memory}[200FFH] &\leftarrow 00H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Load new IP**.

$$IP \leftarrow 0200H$$

**Step 3 — Subroutine ends with `RET`**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[200FEH:200FFH] = 0050H \\
SP &\leftarrow SP + 2 = 1000H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip**: A near `CALL` pushes only the **16-bit IP**. A far `CALL` pushes **IP first, then CS**, and the matching `RETF` pops **CS first, then IP**. Many students mistakenly reverse this order in memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8086 Stack Hardware Interface

The BIU generates the stack physical address using dedicated adder and segment-register logic. The relationship is shown in the following block-level architecture flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    segReg["SS 16-bit Stack Segment Register"]
    spReg["SP 16-bit Stack Pointer"]
    adder1["20-bit Adder: SS shifted left 4"]
    adder2["20-bit Adder: SS + SP"]
    paBus["20-bit Physical Address Bus"]
    mem["Stack Memory Region in RAM"]
    cpu["8086 Execution Unit EU"]

    segReg --> adder1
    adder1 --> adder2
    spReg --> adder2
    adder2 --> paBus
    paBus --> mem
    mem --> cpu
    cpu -- "PUSH/POP, CALL/RET, INT/IRET" --> mem
```

**Interpretation**: The `SS` register is shifted left by 4 bits (×10H) and added to `SP` inside the BIU. The 20-bit result is placed on the address bus to access the stack memory. Data flows bidirectionally between the stack memory and the EU.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — PUSH Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start: PUSH src encountered"]
    stepA1["Step 1: Fetch source 16-bit word into internal latch"]
    stepA2["Step 2: SP = SP - 2"]
    stepA3["Step 3: Compute PA = SS*10H + SP"]
    stepA4["Step 4: Write low byte to PA"]
    stepA5["Step 5: Write high byte to PA+1"]
    endA["End: TOS updated to new SP"]

    startA --> stepA1
    stepA1 --> stepA2
    stepA2 --> stepA3
    stepA3 --> stepA4
    stepA4 --> stepA5
    stepA5 --> endA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — POP Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB["Start: POP dest encountered"]
    stepB1["Step 1: Compute PA = SS*10H + SP"]
    stepB2["Step 2: Read low byte from PA into dest low byte"]
    stepB3["Step 3: Read high byte from PA+1 into dest high byte"]
    stepB4["Step 4: SP = SP + 2"]
    endB["End: TOS now points to next word"]

    startB --> stepB1
    stepB1 --> stepB2
    stepB2 --> stepB3
    stepB3 --> stepB4
    stepB4 --> endB
```

## 4.4 Memory Map — Stack Growth Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    highAddr["Higher Memory Address 20006H: Older data / unused"]
    addr5["20004H: CS value from far CALL"]
    addr4["20002H: IP value from CALL"]
    addr3["20000H: Top of Stack TOS (after CALL)"]
    spArrow["SP points here"]
    addr2["1FFFEH: Free / old data"]
    addr1["1FFFCH: Free / old data"]
    lowAddr["Lower Memory Address 1FFFAH: End of stack space"]
```
