    addr3 --- spArrow
    highAddr --> addr5 --> addr4 --> addr3 --> addr2 --> addr1 --> lowAddr
```

**Reading the diagram**: The downward arrows indicate that the stack *grows toward* lower addresses. The most recently pushed value sits at `20000H`, the current TOS, with `SP = 0000H` (relative to the `SS` base of `2000H`).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q**: Define Stack Pointer (SP) and Stack Segment (SS) registers. How are they used to generate the physical address of the stack?

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **Stack Segment (SS) — 1 mark**: The SS register (16-bit) holds the base address of the stack region in memory. It cannot be changed implicitly by any instruction other than those that load it directly.
- **Stack Pointer (SP) — 1 mark**: The SP register (16-bit) always points to the current Top-of-Stack (TOS). It is automatically decremented by 2 on a PUSH and incremented by 2 on a POP.
- **Physical Address Generation — 1 mark**: The BIU computes `PA = SS × 10H + SP`, producing a 20-bit address that accesses the stack memory.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q**: Differentiate between PUSH and POP operations in the 8086 with respect to SP behaviour and memory write/read order.

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **PUSH — 1.5 marks**: SP is first decremented by 2, then the 16-bit source data is written to the new SS:SP location (low byte at SP, high byte at SP+1, little-endian).
- **POP — 1.5 marks**: The 16-bit data is first read from the current SS:SP location into the destination (low byte from SP, high byte from SP+1), and then SP is incremented by 2.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)**: Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) Explain the stack structure of the 8086 microprocessor with a neat diagram. List the salient features of the 8086 stack. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

1. **Definition (1 mark)**: The 8086 stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) read/write memory region in RAM, used for temporary storage of return addresses, flags, and data.

2. **Registers (1.5 marks)**:
   - **SS (Stack Segment)** — base address of stack.
   - **SP (Stack Pointer)** — current TOS.
   - **BP (Base Pointer)** — for accessing stack data frames.

3. **Physical address (1 mark)**: `PA = SS × 10H + SP`, a 20-bit address generated by the BIU.

4. **Diagram (2 marks)** — refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for the memory map; the candidate should sketch a downward-growing stack with SP arrow.

5. **Salient Features (1.5 marks)**:
   - Word-sized operations (2 bytes each).
   - Grows downward.
   - LIFO discipline.
   - Used implicitly by CALL, RET, INT, IRET.
   - Initialised by the program loader at startup.

**(b) With a suitable example, explain the PUSH and POP operations of the 8086. Show the contents of SP, SS and the stack memory before and after each operation. Assume SS = 5000H, SP = 0200H, AX = ABCDH, BX = 1234H. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Initial state (0.5 mark)**:
`SS = 5000H`, `SP = 0200H`, `AX = ABCDH`, `BX = 1234H`.
PA of TOS = `50000H + 0200H = 50200H`.

**Step 2 — `PUSH AX` execution (2 marks)**:
- SP decreases: `SP = 0200H − 2 = 01FEH`.
- Memory write: `[500FEH] = CDH` (AL), `[500FFH] = ABH` (AH).
- New PA = `500FEH`.

**Step 3 — `PUSH BX` execution (1.5 marks)**:
- SP decreases: `SP = 01FEH − 2 = 01FCH`.
- Memory write: `[500FCH] = 34H` (BL), `[500FDH] = 12H` (BH).

**Step 4 — `POP BX` execution (2 marks)**:
- Memory read: `BL ← [500FCH] = 34H`, `BH ← [500FDH] = 12H` → `BX = 1234H`.
- SP increases: `SP = 01FCH + 2 = 01FEH`.

**Step 5 — `POP AX` execution (1 mark)**:
- Memory read: `AL ← [500FEH] = CDH`, `AH ← [500FFH] = ABH` → `AX = ABCDH`.
- SP increases: `SP = 01FEH + 2 = 0200H` (back to initial).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 1 mark lost if you mix this up**: Many students write that PUSH *first* writes the data and *then* decrements SP. **The correct order is: decrement SP first, then write data.** Writing the wrong order will be penalised in 8086 derivations.

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) Explain the physical address calculation of the stack in 8086. What is the size of the stack segment, and what is the maximum depth of the stack? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

1. **Formula (1.5 marks)**: The 20-bit physical address of the stack is:
$$PA = SS \times 10H + SP$$
The segment base `SS` is first shifted left by 4 bits (equivalently multiplied by 10H = 16) and then added to the 16-bit offset `SP` to produce a 20-bit physical address.

2. **Worked numeric example (2 marks)**:
If `SS = 3000H` and `SP = 0450H`,
$$PA = 30000H + 0450H = 30450H$$

3. **Segment size (1.5 marks)**: Since SP is 16 bits, the maximum offset is `FFFFH`. Hence, the maximum size of a stack segment is **64 KB** (65 536 bytes, or 32 768 words).

4. **Maximum stack depth (1 mark)**: With each PUSH consuming 2 bytes, the maximum depth of the stack is **32 768 words**. If the stack underflows (PUSH at SP = 0000H), it wraps around to FFFEH — a critical programming error in real-time systems.

5. **Practical note (1 mark)**: DOS conventionally allocates a 64 KB stack starting just below the PSP (Program Segment Prefix) of the loaded `.EXE`/`.COM` program.

**(b) Write notes on the role of the stack in subroutine calls and interrupts. Show the stack contents during a near CALL and an INT 3 instruction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

1. **Stack in subroutine calls (3 marks)**:
   - `CALL NEAR proc` pushes the 16-bit IP onto the stack (2 bytes) and loads the new IP.
   - `RET` pops the saved IP from the stack, returning control to the instruction after CALL.
   - `CALL FAR proc` pushes IP first, then CS (4 bytes total), and `RETF` pops CS first, then IP.
   - **Stack frame** for parameter passing: PUSH parameters before CALL; the callee may use BP to index them.

2. **Stack in interrupts (3 marks)**:
   - On a hardware `INTR` or software `INT n` (e.g., INT 3 for breakpoint), the CPU automatically pushes FLAGS, CS, and IP onto the stack — a 6-byte push.
   - The ISR ends with `IRET`, which pops IP, CS, and FLAGS in the reverse order.
   - This mechanism allows transparent nesting of interrupts (each ISR can itself be interrupted).

3. **Numerical illustration (1 mark)**:
   Suppose just before `INT 3`, `IP = 1234H`, `CS = 2000H`, `FLAGS = 0200H`, and `SP = 0100H` (with `SS = 4000H`).
   After the CPU recognises `INT 3`:
   - `SP ← 00FAH` (decrement by 6).
   - Memory `400FAH:400FBH ← 1234H` (IP).
   - Memory `400FCH:400FDH ← 2000H` (CS).
   - Memory `400FEH:400FFH ← 0200H` (FLAGS).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zone**: When asked to *show* stack contents, students often forget that the **low byte is stored at the lower address** (little-endian). For `IP = 1234H`, `34H` is at the lower address and `12H` is at the higher address. Drawing the memory map in big-endian order will cost you at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stacks in 8086**

- **LIFO** is the governing principle; think "stack of plates."
- **Word-only operations**: PUSH/POP always handle 16 bits (2 bytes).
- **Physical address**: $PA = SS \times 10H + SP$ — generated in the BIU.
- **Direction of growth**: Downward (toward lower addresses).
- **PUSH order**: *Decrement SP first*, then write data.
- **POP order**: *Read data first*, then increment SP.
- **Endianness**: Little-endian — low byte at lower address, high byte at higher address.
- **Stack segment size**: 64 KB max (16-bit SP).
- **Stack depth**: 32 768 words max.
- **Implicit users of the stack**: `CALL`, `RET`, `RETF`, `INT n`, `INTO`, `IRET`, `PUSHF`, `POPF`.
- **Near CALL** pushes only IP (2 bytes); **Far CALL** pushes IP and CS (4 bytes).
- **Interrupts** push IP, CS, and FLAGS (6 bytes), restored by IRET in reverse order.
- **BP register** is used to access stack data frames without altering SP.
- **Initialisation**: SS and SP must be set by the program/loader at startup — they default to undefined values after reset.
- **Stack overflow / underflow**: A PUSH at `SP = 0000H` wraps to `SP = FFFEH`; a POP at the maximum depth may overwrite code or data. Always leave guard space.
- **No 8-bit PUSH/POP** in 8086 — use `XCHG` or masking operations if you need to manipulate a single byte.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** (the canonical reference processor for KTU Module 1), a **Procedure** is a self-contained, named sequence of assembly language instructions that performs a specific, well-defined task and can be invoked (called) from any point in the program. In Intel/AMD architecture terminology, a procedure is functionally equivalent to a **subroutine** (other architectures call them *functions*, *methods*, or *routines*).

The 8086 instruction set provides two dedicated opcodes to implement the procedure call/return mechanism:

| Opcode | Mnemonic | Function |
|--------|----------|----------|
| `E8 xx xx` | `CALL` | Transfers control to a procedure and saves the return address on the stack |
| `C3` / `CB` | `RET` (Near/Far) | Pops the saved return address from the stack and transfers control back to the caller |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Procedures are classified as a **control-transfer / program-flow topic** in the 8086 assembly language unit. Students are expected to know the stack mechanics, the difference between **NEAR** and **FAR** calls, the various **parameter-passing conventions**, and the trade-offs between procedures and macros.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Phone Call"

Imagine you are cooking dinner (the **main program**) and a recipe needs you to pre-heat the oven to 180 °C (a specific **sub-task**). You have two choices:

- **Option A (No Procedures — Inline Cooking):** You stop cooking, go set the oven temperature, wait, then come back and continue — but you must *remember exactly where you left off* and the *order of every ingredient* in your head.
- **Option B (Procedures — A Phone Call):** You call a friend (**CALL** the procedure), tell them "I am at *step 4, adding salt*" (**push the return address onto the stack**). Your friend sets the oven (**executes the procedure body**) and tells you "I'm done" (**RET**). You hang up, **pop step 4 off the stack**, and resume.

The crucial element is the **return address saved on the stack** — it is the "reminder note" of *exactly* where the main program was paused. This is why 8086 procedures are intrinsically tied to the **Stack Segment (SS)** and the **Stack Pointer (SP)** register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** A procedure is *executed* at **run-time** by the CPU, while a **MACRO** is *expanded* at **assembly-time** by the assembler. This single difference is worth at least **3 marks** in a typical KTU question.

### 1.3 Why Use Procedures?

- **Modularity** — break a large monolithic assembly program into logical, testable units.
- **Code Reusability** — write once, call many times (reduces program size).
- **Maintainability** — bugs are isolated to a single procedure.
- **Abstraction** — the caller need not know *how* the procedure works, only its *interface* (parameters and return value).
- **Team Development** — different team members can author different procedures in parallel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CALL Instruction — Step-by-Step Mechanics

When the CPU executes a `CALL` instruction, the following **5 micro-operations** occur in sequence:

1. **Resolve the target address** of the procedure (either a 16-bit offset for *NEAR*, or a 32-bit `CS:IP` pair for *FAR*).
2. **Decrement** the Stack Pointer by **2** (for NEAR) or **4** (for FAR) to make room on the stack.
3. **Push** the current value of `IP` (and `CS` for FAR calls) onto the stack.
4. **Load** the target address into `IP` (and `CS` for FAR calls).
5. **Resume execution** from the new `CS:IP` — i.e., the first instruction of the procedure.

### 2.2 The RET Instruction — Step-by-Step Mechanics

The `RET` instruction is the symmetric inverse of `CALL`:

1. **Pop** the saved return address from the top of the stack into `IP` (and `CS` for FAR returns).
2. **Increment** SP by **2** (NEAR) or **4** (FAR) to "release" the stack space.
3. **Resume execution** at the popped address — back in the caller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"CALL → CArries, RET → Returns."* CALL **PUSH**es the address; RET **POP**s it. Always ask: *Did the number of pushes equal the number of pops?* If not, the stack will be **unbalanced** — a classic source of KTU full-mark killers.

### 2.3 NEAR vs. FAR Procedures

| Property | NEAR Procedure | FAR Procedure |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Pointer size** | 16-bit (offset only) | 32-bit (`CS:IP`) |
| **Bytes pushed on stack** | 2 (IP only) | 4 (CS then IP) |
| **Default in 8086** | Inside same segment | Across segments |
| **Mnemonic suffix** | `CALL NEAR PTR` or default | `CALL FAR PTR` |
| **RET form** | `RET` (opcode `C3`) | `RET FAR` (opcode `CB`) |
| **Assembled as** | 3-byte instruction (`E8 disp16`) | 5-byte instruction (`9A ptr16:16`) |
| **Speed** | Faster (no CS reload) | Slower (CS reload + segment cache flush) |

### 2.4 Parameter-Passing Conventions (High-Yield for KTU)

There are **three canonical ways** to pass data between a caller and a procedure:

1. **Register Method** — parameters are loaded into CPU registers (e.g., `MOV AL, value; CALL PROC`). Fastest, but limited by the **8** general-purpose registers of the 8086.
2. **Memory (Global Variable) Method** — parameters are stored in named memory locations (e.g., `MY_VAR`). Simple, but unsafe for re-entrant or recursive code.
3. **Stack Method** — parameters are pushed onto the stack before the call. This is the **industry standard** for high-level languages (C, Pascal) and the most exam-tested method in KTU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat for 8086:** The 8086 has **no `PUSH imm16`** instruction (added only in 80186+). Therefore, parameters are usually moved to a register first, then pushed: `MOV AX, val; PUSH AX`.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| **NEAR CALL stack effect** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2$ | Stack grows **downward** in 8086 |
| **FAR CALL stack effect** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4$ | Pushes CS, then IP |
| **PUSH operation** | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2;\quad [\text{SS:SP}] \leftarrow \text{src}$ | 16-bit source |
| **POP operation** | $\text{dst} \leftarrow [\text{SS:SP}];\quad \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2$ | 16-bit destination |
| **RET (Near) stack effect** | $\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{SS:SP}];\quad \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2$ | Pops 2 bytes |
| **RET FAR stack effect** | $\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{SS:SP}];\quad \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2;\quad \text{CS} \leftarrow [\text{SS:SP}];\quad \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2$ | Pops 4 bytes total |
| **RET n (with stack cleanup)** | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + n$ after pop | Used in C `stdcall` convention |
| **CALL NEAR encoding** | $\text{offset} = \text{disp16}$ (signed 16-bit) | Range: $-32768$ to $+32767$ |
| **CALL FAR encoding** | $\text{ptr} = \text{segment:offset}$ | 32-bit absolute address |
| **Code size of CALL** | 3 bytes (NEAR) / 5 bytes (FAR) | Excludes operands already in code |
| **Code size of RET** | 1 byte (Near) / 1 byte (Far) | Both are single-byte opcodes |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Procedures are the **building blocks of every modern firmware**:

- **Embedded C on 8051/ARM Cortex-M:** When you write a function in C, the compiler emits a `BL` (Branch-and-Link) instruction — which is *exactly* the ARM equivalent of 8086 `CALL`.
- **Operating Systems (Linux/Windows):** System calls, interrupt handlers, and driver routines are all procedures.
- **DSP Routines:** FFT, FIR filters, and matrix operations are written as procedures and called repeatedly from a main loop.
- **Bootloader Code:** BIOS and UEFI firmware consist of nested procedures that initialize hardware, then call the OS loader.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Procedure Type

| Decision Criterion | Use **NEAR** Procedure When | Use **FAR** Procedure When |
|--------------------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Code location | Procedure is in the **same code segment** | Procedure is in a **different code segment** |
| Performance | Critical inner loops, ISRs | Cross-segment library routines |
| Memory footprint | 1 byte saved per CALL site | Larger program, segmented memory model |
| TASM/MASM syntax | `PROC NEAR` (default in `.MODEL SMALL`) | `PROC FAR` |
| Recursion depth limit | Only stack space limits it (up to ~32 KB) | Same, but with extra CS load per level |
| Reentrancy | Possible if stack-only parameters used | Possible but more expensive |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Trace: A Procedure Call and Return

Consider the following 8086 assembly snippet. We will trace the **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **IP**, and the **stack contents** step by step.

**Assumption:** Initial `SP = 0xFFE0` and the procedure `MY_PROC` is a NEAR procedure at offset `0x0200`.

```asm
        MOV  SP, 0FFE0H      ; SP ← 0xFFE0
        CALL MY_PROC          ; Calls procedure at 0x0200
        MOV  AX, BX           ; Continues here after RET
        ...
MY_PROC PROC NEAR
        PUSH AX               ; Save AX
        ADD  AX, 5            ; Procedure body
        POP  AX               ; Restore AX
        RET                   ; Return to caller
MY_PROC ENDP
```

Let us trace the stack state at every critical point.

**Step 1: Initial State**

$$
\text{SP} = \text{0xFFE0}, \quad \text{IP} = \text{0x0000} \text{ (assume CALL is at 0x0000)}
$$

Stack is empty. SP points to the topmost free location.

**Step 2: Execute `CALL MY_PROC`**

The CPU calculates the target offset. The displacement is encoded as a signed 16-bit value:

$$
\text{disp16} = \text{Target IP} - \text{IP}_{\text{next instruction}}
$$

For a NEAR call, the CPU:
- First decrements SP by 2:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2 = \text{0xFFE0} - 0x0002 = \text{0xFFDE}
$$

- Then writes the return address (the IP of the instruction *after* CALL) to the new top of stack:

$$
[\text{SS:0xFFDE}] \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{next}}
$$

- Finally loads the new IP from the operand:

$$
\text{IP} \leftarrow \text{0x0200}
$$

**Step 3: Inside `MY_PROC` — execute `PUSH AX`**

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2 = \text{0xFFDE} - 0x0002 = \text{0xFFDC}
$$

$$
[\text{SS:0xFFDC}] \leftarrow \text{AX}
$$

**Step 4: Execute procedure body `ADD AX, 5`**

This is a register-only operation. **No stack change.**

**Step 5: Execute `POP AX`**

$$
\text{AX} \leftarrow [\text{SS:0xFFDC}]
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2 = \text{0xFFDC} + 0x0002 = \text{0xFFDE}
$$

**Step 6: Execute `RET`**

The CPU pops the saved return address:

$$
\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{SS:0xFFDE}] = \text{IP}_{\text{next (after CALL)}}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2 = \text{0xFFDE} + 0x0002 = \text{0xFFE0}
$$

**Final State:**

$$
\text{SP} = \text{0xFFE0} \text{ (back to original)}, \quad \text{AX} = \text{original value (preserved)}
$$

The stack is **balanced** — every PUSH has a matching POP, and CALL/RET correctly round-tripped the IP.

### 3.3 Analytical Trace — The Unbalanced Stack (Common Bug)

Suppose a student *forgets* the `POP AX` inside the procedure. Then:

- At `RET`, the value at `[SS:0xFFDC]` (which is the original `AX` value pushed earlier) is **popped into IP**.
- This causes the CPU to jump to an arbitrary address — almost certainly crashing the program.
- The stack pointer ends at `0xFFDE` instead of `0xFFE0`, and **every subsequent CALL/RET** will be off by 2 bytes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to trace a procedure, always draw a **stack diagram** showing `[high address → low address]` with arrows indicating push/pop direction. Examiners award full marks only when the stack evolution is clearly tabulated.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Stack Parameter Passing

We will write a complete procedure that adds two 16-bit numbers passed via the stack.

```asm
        MOV  AX, 1234H        ; First parameter
        PUSH AX
        MOV  AX, 5678H        ; Second parameter
        PUSH AX
        CALL ADD_TWO          ; Call the procedure
        ; Result (sum) is in AX
        ADD  SP, 4            ; Clean up 4 bytes from stack (stdcall style)

ADD_TWO PROC NEAR
        PUSH BP               ; Save caller's BP
        MOV  BP, SP           ; BP now points to saved BP
        MOV  AX, [BP+4]       ; Second parameter (last pushed)
        ADD  AX, [BP+6]       ; First parameter
        POP  BP               ; Restore BP
        RET                   ; Return
ADD_TWO ENDP
```

**Why `[BP+4]` and `[BP+6]`?**

Let us map the stack frame **after the `MOV BP, SP`** instruction:

| Stack Address | Contents | Offset from BP |
|---------------|----------|----------------|
| `SP+0` → `BP+0` | Saved BP (pushed by us) | `[BP+0]` |
| `SP+2` → `BP+2` | Return address (low word) | `[BP+2]` |
| `SP+4` → `BP+4` | 5678H (second parameter) | `[BP+4]` |
| `SP+6` → `BP+6` | 1234H (first parameter) | `[BP+6]` |

Therefore:

$$
[\text{BP+4}] = \text{0x5678}, \quad [\text{BP+6}] = \text{0x1234}
$$

$$
\text{AX} = \text{0x5678} + \text{0x1234} = \text{0x68AC}
$$

The **return address** occupies `[BP+2]`, and the **saved BP** is at `[BP+0]`. Every parameter is therefore offset by `+2` for the return address and `+2` per parameter above the return address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Frame Rule (KTU Favourite):** For a NEAR procedure with `n` 16-bit stack parameters, the $k$-th parameter (counted from 1, with parameter 1 being the *first* pushed) is at offset:

$$
\text{Offset of param}_k = 2k + 2 \quad (\text{in bytes from BP})
$$

This formula is worth **7 marks** in a typical KTU Part-B question on stack frame analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow of a CALL / RET Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Caller Program executing in CS:IP"] --> B{"Encounter CALL opcode"}
    B --> C["Resolve target offset for NEAR or CS:IP for FAR"]
    C --> D["Decrement SP by 2 NEAR or 4 FAR"]
    D --> E["Push return address CS:IP onto stack"]
    E --> F["Load new CS:IP from operand"]
    F --> G["Procedure Body executes"]
    G --> H{"Encounter RET opcode"}
    H --> I["Pop return address from stack into CS:IP"]
    I --> J["Increment SP by 2 NEAR or 4 FAR"]
    J --> K["Resume Caller after original CALL instruction"]
    K --> L["Stack pointer restored to pre CALL state"]
```

### 4.2 Stack-Frame Block Diagram (NEAR Procedure with 2 Parameters)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Higher["Higher Memory Addresses"]
        D0["Parameter 1 (1234H) at BP+6"]
        D1["Parameter 2 (5678H) at BP+4"]
        D2["Return Address (IP) at BP+2"]
        D3["Saved BP at BP+0"]
    end
    subgraph Lower["Lower Memory Addresses SP points here"]
        D4["Top of Stack SP = BP+0"]
    end
    D0 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Procedural Program Architecture (Modular Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["MAIN procedure - Main Program"] --> P1["INIT procedure - Hardware Initialization"]
    M --> P2["READ procedure - Input from Port"]
    M --> P3["COMPUTE procedure - Data Processing"]
    M --> P4["DISPLAY procedure - Output to LED"]
    P1 -.-> R1["RET to MAIN"]
    P2 -.-> R2["RET to MAIN"]
    P3 -.-> R3["RET to MAIN"]
    P4 -.-> R4["RET to MAIN"]
    R1 --> M
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    R4 --> M
```

### 4.4 Comparison — Procedure vs. Macro (Visual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PR["PROCEDURE - Runtime mechanism"]
        P1["Source code has ONE copy of body"]
        P2["Assembled into machine code ONCE"]
        P3["CALL instruction transfers control"]
        P4["Uses STACK to save return address"]
        P5["Saves memory if called multiple times"]
    end
    subgraph MC["MACRO - Assembly-time mechanism"]
        M1["Source code is EXPANDED at every call site"]
        M2["Assembler duplicates code inline"]
        M3["No CALL or RET generated"]
        M4["No stack usage - faster execution"]
        M5["Larger code size if called multiple times"]
    end
    PR --> VS["Decision Criterion: Reusability > Speed"]
    MC --> VS2["Decision Criterion: Speed > Code Size"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Q1. Define a procedure in 8086 assembly language. List any two advantages of using procedures.**

**Model Answer:**

A *procedure* (also called a *subroutine*) in 8086 assembly is a self-contained block of instructions identified by a `PROC` directive that performs a specific task and can be invoked from different points in the program using the `CALL` instruction. Control is returned to the caller via the `RET` instruction.

**Advantages:**
1. **Modularity** — Large programs can be divided into smaller, manageable logical units, improving readability and reducing development time.
2. **Code Reusability** — A procedure is written once in memory but can be invoked many times via `CALL`, which reduces the overall program size and memory footprint.

*(Alternate valid answers: easier debugging, team development, abstraction of implementation details, library creation.)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between a NEAR procedure and a FAR procedure in 8086.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NEAR Procedure | FAR Procedure |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| **Segment** | Same code segment as caller | Different code segment |
| **Address saved** | Only 16-bit IP | Both 16-bit CS and 16-bit IP |
| **Bytes pushed on stack** | 2 bytes | 4 bytes |
| **Instruction size** | 3 bytes (`E8 disp16`) | 5 bytes (`9A ptr16:16`) |
| **RET form** | `RET` (1 byte, opcode `C3`) | `RET FAR` (1 byte, opcode `CB`) |
| **Execution speed** | Faster (no segment reload) | Slower (CS reload) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain the stack operations performed by the `CALL` and `RET` instructions of the 8086 microprocessor. Illustrate with diagrams how the stack pointer and program counter are modified in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language procedure to find the factorial of a number `N` using recursion. Pass the parameter using a register and explain how the stack handles the recursive calls. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — The CALL Instruction (NEAR case):**

When the CPU executes a NEAR `CALL` (e.g., `CALL PROC_NAME`):

1. The CPU pushes the current value of the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — which points to the instruction *immediately after* the `CALL` — onto the stack. This is the **return address**.

   $$
   \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2
   $$

   $$
   [\text{SS:SP}] \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{return}}
   $$

2. The CPU then loads the target procedure's offset (a 16-bit signed displacement) into IP.

   $$
   \text{IP} \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{disp16}
   $$

3. Execution continues at the new IP (the first instruction of the procedure).

**Step 2 — The RET Instruction:**

1. The CPU pops the saved return address from the top of the stack into IP.

   $$
   \text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{SS:SP}]
   $$

   $$
   \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2
   $$

2. Execution resumes at the instruction following the `CALL` in the original program.

**Step 3 — Stack Diagram:**

```
         Before CALL              After CALL             After RET
         -----------              ----------             ---------
   High  |  ....  |              |  ....  |              |  ....  |
         |  ....  |              |  ....  |              |  ....  |
         |--------|      SP →     |  RET   |  SP →        |--------|
   Low   |  empty |              |  IP    |              |  empty |  SP
         |--------|              |--------|              |--------|
                                [SP+1] [SP]
```

**For a FAR call**, the same sequence applies, but **4 bytes** (CS first, then IP) are pushed/popped, and both `CS` and `IP` are modified.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Recursive Factorial Procedure:**

```asm
; Assumption: N is passed in CX register.
; Result (N!) is returned in DX:AX (for large N).

        MOV  CX, 05H           ; N = 5
        CALL FACT              ; Compute 5!
        ; Result in DX:AX
        HLT                    ; End program

FACT    PROC  NEAR
        CMP  CX, 1             ; Base case
        JE   BASE_CASE
        PUSH CX                ; Save N on stack
        DEC  CX                ; N = N - 1
        CALL FACT              ; Recursive call
        ; After return, N! for (N-1) is in DX:AX
        POP  CX                ; Restore N
        ; Now multiply DX:AX by CX
        ; (Assuming a 16x16 multiplication routine exists)
        CALL MUL16             ; DX:AX ← (DX:AX) * CX
        RET
BASE_CASE:
        MOV  AX, 1
        MOV  DX, 0
        RET
FACT    ENDP
```

**Explanation of Stack Behaviour:**

- For each recursive call, the current value of `N` (in `CX`) is **pushed** onto the stack, along with the **return address** (2 bytes).
- The stack grows by **4 bytes per level** (2 for IP, 2 for `CX`).
- The recursion unwinds in LIFO order: when `N=1` is reached, the procedure returns `1`, then each higher level pops its saved `N` and multiplies.

For `N=5`, the stack will grow to a maximum of **5 levels deep** before unwinding. Each level uses 4 bytes of stack, so the total stack consumption is `5 × 4 = 20` bytes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **forget to save and restore `CX`** in the recursive procedure, causing the base-case check to fail on subsequent calls. **Lose 2 marks** if you miss this.
> - Some students declare the procedure as **FAR** when it is clearly within the same segment. Use `NEAR` for in-segment calls. **Lose 1 mark** for incorrect `PROC` qualifier.
> - The 16-bit limit means `7! = 5040` fits in `AX`, but `8! = 40320` already requires 32-bit storage (`DX:AX`). Always state this assumption explicitly. **Lose 1 mark** for not mentioning overflow.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a)** Discuss three different methods of passing parameters to an 8086 procedure, with suitable code examples. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program that uses a procedure to convert a packed BCD number (passed via the stack) into its equivalent binary (hex) value. Show the complete stack frame. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Method 1 — Passing Parameters in Registers**

```asm
        MOV  AL, 25H           ; First parameter in AL
        MOV  BL, 10H           ; Second parameter in BL
        CALL ADD_PROC          ; Procedure computes AL + BL
        ; Result in AL
```

**Advantages:** Fastest execution (no memory access). **Disadvantages:** Limited by the 8 general-purpose registers of 8086; not suitable for more than a few parameters; caller must preserve register values.

**Method 2 — Passing Parameters in Memory (Global Variables)**

```asm
        .DATA
PARAM1  DB  25H
PARAM2  DB  10H
        .CODE
        MOV  AL, PARAM1
        MOV  BL, PARAM2
        CALL ADD_PROC
```

**Advantages:** Unlimited number of parameters; simple syntax. **Disadvantages:** Not re-entrant; cannot be called from interrupt service routines safely; uses fixed memory addresses.

**Method 3 — Passing Parameters on the Stack**

```asm
        MOV  AX, 25H
        PUSH AX
        MOV  AX, 10H
        PUSH AX
        CALL ADD_PROC
        ADD  SP, 4            ; Caller cleans up stack
        ; Result in AX
```

**Advantages:** Re-entrant; supports recursion; clean separation between caller and callee; standard for high-level language interfacing. **Disadvantages:** Slower (memory access); requires careful stack frame management; risk of unbalanced pushes/pops.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Registers | Memory | Stack |
|--------|-----------|--------|-------|
| Speed | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Re-entrancy | No | No | Yes |
| Recursion support | No | No | Yes |
| Parameter limit | ~6 | Unlimited | ~32K bytes |
| Code complexity | Low | Low | High |
| Standard for C interface | No | No | Yes |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Problem:** Convert a packed BCD number (e.g., `0x95`, representing decimal 95) into its binary equivalent (also `0x5F` = 95 decimal).

```asm
        .MODEL SMALL
        .STACK  100H
        .DATA
        .CODE

MAIN    PROC  FAR
        MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX

        MOV  AX, 95H           ; Packed BCD = 95 decimal
        PUSH AX                ; Push as parameter
        CALL BCD2BIN
        ADD  SP, 2             ; Clean up stack (caller cleanup)
        ; Result in AX = 005FH = 95 decimal
        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
MAIN    ENDP

BCD2BIN PROC  NEAR
        PUSH BP
        MOV  BP, SP
        MOV  AL, [BP+4]        ; Get packed BCD byte
        MOV  BL, AL
        AND  BL, 0FH           ; Extract low nibble (units)
        MOV  CL, 4
        SHR  AL, CL            ; Extract high nibble (tens)
        MOV  BH, 10
        MUL  BH                ; AX = tens * 10
        ADD  AL, BL            ; AX = (tens*10) + units
        MOV  AH, 0             ; Clear high byte
        POP  BP
        RET
BCD2BIN ENDP
        END  MAIN
```

**Stack Frame Analysis:**

After `MOV BP, SP`, the stack looks like:

| Address | Contents | Access via |
|---------|----------|------------|
| `BP+0` | Saved BP (low byte) | `[BP]` |
| `BP+1` | Saved BP (high byte) | `[BP+1]` |
| `BP+2` | Return address (IP low) | `[BP+2]` |
| `BP+3` | Return address (IP high) | `[BP+3]` |
| `BP+4` | 0x95 (BCD byte, low) | `[BP+4]` |
| `BP+5` | 0x00 (BCD byte, high) | `[BP+5]` |

**Trace for input `0x95`:**
- `AL = 0x95`, `BL = 0x95`
- `BL = BL AND 0x0F = 0x05` (units digit)
- `AL = AL SHR 4 = 0x09` (tens digit)
- `AX = 9 × 10 = 90 = 0x005A`
- `AX = 0x005A + 0x0005 = 0x005F = 95` ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget the `ADD SP, 2` (or `RET 2`) **stack cleanup** after a `CALL` when the procedure does not clean up its own parameters. **Lose 2 marks** for not balancing the stack.
> - Forgetting `MOV AH, 0` after the `MUL` leaves garbage in `AH`, corrupting the 16-bit result. **Lose 1 mark.**
> - Accessing `[BP+4]` without first setting up `BP` will read the wrong stack location. **Always** show the `PUSH BP; MOV BP, SP` prologue. **Lose 2 marks** otherwise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A procedure is a named, callable block of instructions in 8086 assembly, invoked by `CALL` and terminated by `RET`.
- **CALL saves the return address on the stack; RET pops it back.** This is the heart of the procedure mechanism.
- **NEAR procedures** push **2 bytes** (IP only) and stay in the same segment. **FAR procedures** push **4 bytes** (CS:IP) and can cross segments.
- **Three parameter-passing methods:** Registers (fast, limited), Memory (simple, non-reentrant), Stack (re-entrant, recursive, C-compatible).
- **Stack Frame Rule:** The $k$-th 16-bit parameter is at offset $[BP + 2k + 2]$ inside a NEAR procedure.
- **Procedure vs. Macro:** Procedures are *runtime* call-based (saves memory); macros are *assembly-time* text substitution (faster, larger code).
- **Always balance the stack:** Every `PUSH` must have a matching `POP`, and every `CALL` must have a matching `RET`.
- **Recursion** is possible in 8086 procedures but consumes 4+ bytes of stack per level — keep recursion depth shallow.
- **The 8086 has no `PUSH imm16`** — load to a register first, then push.
- **Default procedure type** in `.MODEL SMALL` is `NEAR`; explicitly use `PROC FAR` only when crossing segments.
- **Use `RET n`** (e.g., `RET 4`) to pop `n` bytes of parameters in a single instruction — a clean way to handle stack cleanup.
- **KBD-trap:** If your procedure modifies registers that the caller relies on, save and restore them with `PUSH`/`POP` pairs (e.g., `PUSH BX ... POP BX`) — this is the **callee-save convention** expected in KTU valuations.
- **For exam answers:** always draw a labeled **stack diagram** with addresses, contents, and arrow directions — examiners give **bonus partial credit** for clear visuals.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Procedures frequently appear in **Part B questions worth 7–14 marks** combined with topics like *interrupts*, *string instructions*, and *DOS function calls*. Always link the procedure topic with the **stack frame** and **interrupt service routine (ISR)** concepts — they share the same `PUSH/POP/CALL/RET/IRET` mechanism, and a unified answer covering all four mechanisms will guarantee full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8086 / MASM (Microsoft Assembler)** programming model prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Microprocessors and Microcontrollers*, a **Macro** is a **named, named-block of assembly language instructions, directives, and pseudo-operations** that is **statically expanded inline by the assembler at assembly time (preprocessing phase)** whenever the macro name is invoked in the source code.

Formally, the KTU syllabus defines a macro as a textual substitution mechanism governed by the directives:

$$\text{macro\_name} \; \texttt{MACRO} \; [\text{parameter\_list}]$$
$$\qquad \qquad \texttt{; --- body of the macro ---}$$
$$\text{macro\_name} \; \texttt{ENDM}$$

The block between `MACRO` and `ENDM` is registered in the assembler's **Symbol Table (Macro Definition Table / MDT)** and is **expanded (textually replicated)** each time the macro is *called* in the program. This is fundamentally different from a `PROCEDURE`, which transfers control at **run time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1:**
> A macro is a *textual expansion* tool of the assembler (compile-time substitution), whereas a *procedure* is a *control-transfer* tool of the CPU (run-time CALL/RET mechanism). This distinction is a frequently asked question in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **macro** like a **"pre-written template on a rubber stamp"**:

- Imagine you are an office clerk who has to write the same address ("Company XYZ, Kochi, Kerala - 682021") on **100 letters**.
- Instead of writing the address 100 times by hand, you carve the address onto a **rubber stamp (the macro definition)** once.
- Every time you need the address, you simply **press the stamp onto the letter (the macro call)** — the same content appears, *inline*, on the spot.
- The stamp never "calls" another letter to fetch the address — the text physically appears where you stamped it. That is exactly what a macro does in assembly language: the macro body is **copied into your program** at the location of the macro call.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway in Plain English:**
> - **Macro** = "Copy-paste shortcut" managed by the **assembler** (before the program runs).
> - **Procedure** = "Phone-call shortcut" managed by the **CPU** (while the program runs).

## 1.3 Why Macros Are Used in 8086 Programs

| Engineering Reason | Plain English Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Code Reusability** | Write once, reuse many times within the same `.asm` file. |
| **Readability** | Replaces long repetitive instruction sequences with a single meaningful name. |
| **Parameterization** | The same macro can behave differently for different operands passed to it. |
| **Performance (Faster than Procedures)** | Since expansion is inline, **no CALL/RET overhead**, no stack push/pop of IP/CS. |
| **Modularization** | Complex initialization sequences (e.g., setting up the stack segment) can be hidden behind one name. |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> The following terminology is **bold-marked** because they are the exact terms the KTU paper-setter uses:
>
> - **Macro Definition** — the block enclosed between `MACRO` and `ENDM`.
> - **Macro Call / Macro Invocation** — the line in the program where the macro name is written to trigger expansion.
> - **Macro Expansion** — the actual copy of the macro body inserted in place of the macro call by the assembler.
> - **Formal Parameters** — the dummy names (placeholders) listed in the macro definition.
> - **Actual Parameters / Arguments** — the real values/symbols supplied at the macro call.
> - **Macro Library** — a separate file containing commonly used macro definitions, included via the `INCLUDE` directive.
> - **Local Labels in Macros** — labels declared with the `LOCAL` directive inside a macro so that each expansion produces a *unique* label.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Macro Definition (MASM Syntax)

A macro in MASM for the 8086 is constructed using **two mandatory directives** and **one optional parameter list**:

$$\boxed{\;\texttt{macroname} \;\; \texttt{MACRO} \;\; [\texttt{p1}, \texttt{p2}, \dots, \texttt{pN}] \;}$$

$$\qquad \qquad \texttt{; --- macro body: legal 8086 instructions / directives ---}$$

$$\boxed{\;\texttt{; --- body statements referencing p1, p2, ... ---}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\texttt{ENDM}\;}$$

**Rules that govern the body (KTU Board-relevant):**

1. The macro name appearing on the `MACRO` line **must be unique** within the program (it is registered in the assembler's symbol table).
2. Parameters are **positional** — `p1` always receives the first argument of the call.
3. Parameters in the body are **concatenated** with surrounding text using the ampersand **`&`** operator.
4. To pass a string that contains commas or spaces, enclose the argument in the **angle-bracket `< >`** text-delimiter operator.
5. To force the assembler to evaluate an expression *before* substitution, prefix the argument with the **percent `%`** expression-evaluation operator.
6. To suppress macro-expansion of a symbol that happens to be a macro name, use the **exclamation `!`** literal-text operator.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Macro Expansion

The assembler performs the following sequence when it encounters a macro call:

- **Step 1 — Recognition:** The assembler scans the program line-by-line. When it finds a token that matches a registered macro name, it suspends normal instruction parsing.
- **Step 2 — Argument Binding:** The actual parameters of the call are bound to the formal parameters `p1, p2, …, pN` in the order they appear.
- **Step 3 — Textual Substitution:** Each occurrence of `&p1`, `p2`, etc. inside the macro body is **textually replaced** by the corresponding argument.
- **Step 4 — Local Label Renaming (if any):** If the body contains a `LOCAL` directive, the assembler appends a unique numeric suffix (e.g., `??0000`, `??0001`) so that no two expansions collide in label space.
- **Step 5 — Inline Insertion:** The fully-substituted body is **inserted at the exact position** of the call in the source stream.
- **Step 6 — Continuation:** The assembler resumes scanning. The next macro call (if any) triggers a *new* expansion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crucial point: **the original macro definition is NOT present in the final `.EXE` or `.OBJ` file.** What runs on the 8086 is the *expanded* code — exactly as if you had hand-typed the body yourself.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Macro Operators

The following table consolidates the **macro operators** the KTU paper regularly tests. Note: the vertical bar is rendered as `\vert` to comply with the markdown table syntax rule.

| Operator | Syntax | Function | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `&` (Substitution / Concatenation) | `p&num` | Forces substitution of parameter `p` inside an identifier | Building labels: `LOOP&p1` |
| `< >` (Text Delimiter) | `<ax, bx>` | Passes the *literal* string between the brackets as one argument | Passing strings with spaces/commas |
| `!` (Literal-Text Escape) | `!p1` | Prevents the assembler from treating `p1` as a macro/symbol | Forcing literal text |
| `%` (Expression Evaluator) | `%p1` | Evaluates the expression `p1` and substitutes its *value* | Passing constants: `%CTR` |
| `;;` (Macro Comment) | `;; comment` | A comment that is removed during macro expansion | In-body documentation |
| `LOCAL` directive | `LOCAL lbl1, lbl2` | Declares labels unique to each expansion | Internal loop/jump labels |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Macros vs. Procedures

This is the **single most asked comparative question** in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the distinctions below:

| Attribute | MACRO | PROCEDURE (Near/Far) |
|---|---|---|
| Directive / Keyword | `MACRO … ENDM` | `PROC … ENDP` |
| Invocation mechanism | Textual substitution | `CALL` instruction |
| When does it execute? | At **assembly time** (compile time) | At **run time** |
| Control transfer | **No** transfer of control — body is *inline* | Transfers IP (and CS if far) via `CALL`/`RET` |
| Stack usage | **None** for CALL/RET | Yes — pushes return address |
| Execution speed | **Faster** (no CALL/RET overhead) | Slower (CALL/RET + stack operations) |
| Code size | **Larger** (body copied at every call) | **Smaller** (single copy in memory) |
| Parameter passing | Through macro arguments (textual) | Through registers, stack, or memory |
| Local variable support | Only `LOCAL` directive for labels | Full stack-frame support |
| Visible in `.LST` file? | Yes (expansion is shown) | No (only `CALL` site) |
| Suitable for | Short, frequently used sequences | Long, complex, recursive sub-programs |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Firmware Development:** Embedded 8086 engineers package repetitive I/O port initializations (e.g., `INIT_PIO p1`) as macros to keep hand-written firmware compact.
- **Device Drivers:** Macros wrap low-level port-I/O sequences (`OUT`/`IN`) into readable operations.
- **BIOS/Assembly Boot Loaders:** Macros abstract the loading of segment registers (`CS`, `DS`, `ES`, `SS`) at boot.
- **Compiler Code-Generators:** Modern compilers internally use a macro-like textual-expansion pass before generating final machine code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since *Macros* is a **declarative / textual-substitution concept** rather than a physics/math derivation, this section presents **fully worked MASM examples** with line-by-line assembler behaviour, equivalent in rigour to an algebraic derivation.

---

## 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Simple Macro (No Parameters)

### 3.1.1 Source Program (Student submits this)

```asm
; PROGRAM: Demonstration of a parameter-less macro
.MODEL SMALL
.486

CLEAR_CARRY  MACRO
             CLC
             ENDM

.STACK 100H
.DATA
    MSG DB 'OK$'
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV  AX, @DATA
     MOV  DS, AX

     CLEAR_CARRY        ; <<< MACRO CALL #1
     STC                ; Set Carry
     CLEAR_CARRY        ; <<< MACRO CALL #2

     MOV  AH, 4CH
     INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Assembler Behaviour (Equivalence Derivation)

The assembler first **registers** the macro `CLEAR_CARRY` in its Macro Definition Table. It then proceeds to the `MAIN` procedure and encounters the **first invocation** on the line `CLEAR_CARRY`. The expansion algorithm is:

- **Step 1:** Match the token `CLEAR_CARRY` against the MDT → hit found.
- **Step 2:** No formal parameters exist, so no argument binding is required.
- **Step 3:** The single instruction `CLC` is copied inline.
- **Step 4:** No `LOCAL` directive, so no label renaming is needed.
- **Step 5:** The line `CLEAR_CARRY` in the source stream is **replaced by** `CLC`.
- **Step 6:** Scanning resumes at the next line (`STC`), then hits the second `CLEAR_CARRY`, which triggers a *new* independent expansion (again `CLC`).

### 3.1.3 Equivalent Expanded Code (What the CPU Actually Executes)

```asm
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV  AX, @DATA
     MOV  DS, AX
     CLC                  ; <-- Expansion of CLEAR_CARRY #1
     STC
     CLC                  ; <-- Expansion of CLEAR_CARRY #2
     MOV  AH, 4CH
     INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
```

**Verification:** The `.LST` (listing) file produced by MASM will explicitly show two `CLC` lines corresponding to the two calls — confirming the inline expansion.

---

## 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Parameterized Macro (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

### 3.2.1 Source Program — `MOVE` Macro

The goal: define a macro that **copies a 16-bit value from one register to another with a left-shift by 1** in a single line. The two registers are passed as **parameters**.

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.486

; MACRO DEFINITION
SHIFT_LEFT   MACRO   src, dst
             MOV    AX, src
             SHL    AX, 1
             MOV    dst, AX
             ENDM

.STACK 100H
.DATA
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV  AX, @DATA
     MOV  DS, AX

     MOV  BX, 1234H
     SHIFT_LEFT   BX, CX      ; <<< CALL 1:  src = BX, dst = CX

     MOV  SI, 00FFH
     SHIFT_LEFT   SI, DX      ; <<< CALL 2:  src = SI, dst = DX

     MOV  AH, 4CH
     INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Argument-Binding Derivation

#### ▶ Call 1: `SHIFT_LEFT BX, CX`

The assembler performs positional binding:

$$\texttt{Formal: src = BX}, \qquad \texttt{Formal: dst = CX}$$

Substitution rules are applied to every reference of `src` and `dst` in the body. Note that the body uses the bare tokens `src` and `dst` (no `&` is needed because the parameter is the *whole* token — `&` is required only when the parameter is **embedded inside another identifier**).

After substitution, the inlined body becomes:

```asm
             MOV    AX, BX
             SHL    AX, 1
             MOV    CX, AX
```

#### ▶ Call 2: `SHIFT_LEFT SI, DX`

A *fresh* positional binding is computed for this call:

$$\texttt{Formal: src = SI}, \qquad \texttt{Formal: dst = DX}$$

After substitution, the inlined body becomes:

```asm
             MOV    AX, SI
             SHL    AX, 1
             MOV    DX, AX
```

### 3.2.3 Equivalent Fully-Expanded Program (Run-Time View)

```asm
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV  AX, @DATA
     MOV  DS, AX

     MOV  BX, 1234H
     MOV  AX, BX          ; <-- expansion of CALL 1
     SHL  AX, 1
     MOV  CX, AX
     MOV  SI, 00FFH
     MOV  AX, SI          ; <-- expansion of CALL 2
     SHL  AX, 1
     MOV  DX, AX

     MOV  AH, 4CH
     INT  21H
MAIN ENDP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code-size consequence:** The two macro calls each produced **3 inlined instructions** → the `MAIN` procedure grew by 6 instructions. A procedure-based alternative would have added only 2 × `CALL` instructions. This is the classic *size-vs-speed trade-off* the KTU examiner often tests.

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 3 — Concatenation Operator `&` (Building Labels)

A common engineering need is to generate a *unique* loop label whose name depends on the macro argument. The `&` operator concatenates a parameter with surrounding text to form a single identifier.

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.486

DELAY       MACRO   count
            LOCAL   LOOP&count
            MOV     CX, count
LOOP&count: DEC     CX
            JNZ     LOOP&count
            ENDM

.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     DELAY  100H         ; <<< CALL 1
     DELAY  200H         ; <<< CALL 2
     MOV    AH, 4CH
     INT    21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

### 3.3.1 Derivation of the Concatenation Behaviour

For **Call 1** (`count = 100H`):
- The `LOCAL` directive registers the symbol `LOOP100H` as expansion-local.
- The label `LOOP&count` becomes `LOOP100H` after concatenation.
- The instruction `JNZ LOOP&count` becomes `JNZ LOOP100H`.

For **Call 2** (`count = 200H`):
- A *new* `LOCAL` registration produces a *different* unique label — in this case `LOOP200H`.
- The label `LOOP&count` becomes `LOOP200H`.

### 3.3.2 Equivalent Expanded Code

```asm
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV    CX, 100H
LOOP100H:  DEC     CX
     JNZ     LOOP100H
     MOV    CX, 200H
LOOP200H:  DEC     CX
     JNZ     LOOP200H
     MOV    AH, 4CH
     INT    21H
MAIN ENDP
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Writing `LOOPcount` (no `&`). The assembler will *not* substitute `count` because it does not recognize it as a parameter token — it will treat `LOOPcount` as an entirely unrelated identifier. The `&` is **mandatory** when the parameter is glued to other text.

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 4 — Macro with `< >` Text Delimiter

The angle-bracket operator is required whenever the argument contains a **comma, space, or special character** that would otherwise confuse the assembler's argument parser.

```asm
PRINT_MSG   MACRO   msg
            MOV     AH, 09H
            MOV     DX, OFFSET msg
            INT     21H
            ENDM

.DATA
    WELCOME  DB 'Hello, World!$'
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     PRINT_MSG   WELCOME              ; <<< Single-token argument: no < > needed
     ; PRINT_MSG   'Hello, World!$'   ; <-- WOULD FAIL without < >
     PRINT_MSG   <'Hello, World!$>   ; <<< Using < > to pass a literal
     MOV   AH, 4CH
     INT   21H
MAIN ENDP
```

### 3.4.1 Why `< >` Is Necessary

When the assembler parses the arguments of a macro call, it uses the **comma** as the *default separator* between positional parameters. The expression `PRINT_MSG 'Hello, World!$'` would be split into two arguments by the comma inside the string. Wrapping the entire literal in `< >` tells the assembler: *"Treat everything between the brackets as a single argument — do not split on commas."*

$$\text{arg1 of } \texttt{PRINT_MSG} = \begin{cases} \texttt{'Hello} & \text{(without < > — WRONG)} \\ \texttt{'Hello, World!\$'} & \text{(with < > — CORRECT)} \end{cases}$$

---

## 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 5 — Macro Library (INCLUDE Directive)

For large 8086 projects, macros are often placed in a **separate file** (a *macro library*) and pulled into the main source using `INCLUDE`.

**File: `mylib.mac`** (saved in the same folder as the main `.asm`)

```asm
;--- mylib.mac : reusable macro library ---
INIT_DATA   MACRO
            MOV     AX, @DATA
            MOV     DS, AX
            ENDM

EXIT_PROG   MACRO
            MOV     AH, 4CH
            INT     21H
            ENDM
```

**File: `main.asm`**

```asm
INCLUDE mylib.mac        ; <-- pulls INIT_DATA and EXIT_PROG into MDT
.MODEL SMALL
.STACK 100H
.DATA
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     INIT_DATA            ; expanded inline
     ; ... user code ...
     EXIT_PROG            ; expanded inline
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

The assembler logically **prepends** the contents of `mylib.mac` to the main source before beginning macro-expansion analysis. This is the basis of modular, reusable 8086 firmware.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — "Which Construct Should I Use?"

| Situation | Recommended Construct | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| A 2-instruction sequence called 20 times | **Macro** | Saves CALL/RET overhead, no stack use |
| A 50-instruction sorting routine called once | **Procedure** | Avoids bloating code size |
| Code that must be **recursive** | **Procedure** | Macros cannot recurse (no call stack) |
| Code that needs **runtime address** (e.g., far pointer arithmetic) | **Procedure (FAR)** | `CALL FAR`/`RETF` is required |
| Internal loop label that must not clash across expansions | **Macro + `LOCAL`** | `LOCAL` guarantees uniqueness |
| A routine shared across multiple `.asm` files | **Macro library + `INCLUDE`** | Promotes reusability |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Macro Lifecycle (Assembly-Time Pipeline)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **six-stage pipeline** the assembler follows for every macro call. This is a high-yield diagram for the KTU 2-mark question: *"Explain the working of a macro."*

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Source Code Line Read]) --> B{Macro Name in MDT?}
    B -- No --> Z[Treat as Instruction or Directive]
    Z --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Bind Actual Args to Formal Params]
    C --> D[Apply Substitution Operators &, lt, gt, !, percent]
    D --> E{Rename LOCAL Labels with ??xxxx suffix}
    E --> F[Insert Expanded Body Inline at Call Site]
    F --> G([Continue Scanning Next Line])
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Memory Layout After Macro Expansion

This Mermaid block diagram shows the **Code Segment (CS) layout** of a program containing two macro calls and one procedure call. It visually highlights the *inline* nature of macros versus the *single-copy* nature of procedures.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CS[8086 CODE SEGMENT CS]
        direction TB
        S1[START: MOV AX, @DATA]
        S2[MOV DS, AX]
        S3[--- Macro 1 expansion ---]
        S4["CLC (1 byte)"]
        S5[STC]
        S6[--- Macro 2 expansion ---]
        S7["CLC (1 byte)"]
        S8["--- Procedure CALL ---"]
        S9["CALL MY_PROC (3 bytes)"]
        S10["--- MY_PROC body (lives ONCE) ---"]
        S11["MOV AH, 4CH"]
        S12["INT 21H"]
        S13[RET]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Macro 1 and Macro 2 each contributed a *separate, in-place* `CLC` instruction at their respective call sites (lines S4 and S7). In contrast, the procedure body (S10–S13) appears **only once** in memory, with the call site (S9) using a `CALL` instruction to jump to it.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Macro Definition Table (MDT) Concept

The assembler maintains a **Macro Definition Table** in its internal data structures during assembly. The Mermaid block below models a snapshot of the MDT after defining the three macros from Sections 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MDT[Macro Definition Table]
    MDT --> M1["Entry 1: CLEAR_CARRY<br/>Params: none<br/>Body: CLC"]
    MDT --> M2["Entry 2: SHIFT_LEFT<br/>Params: src, dst<br/>Body: MOV AX,src / SHL AX,1 / MOV dst,AX"]
    MDT --> M3["Entry 3: DELAY<br/>Params: count<br/>Body: MOV CX,count / DEC CX / JNZ LABEL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each entry stores the **name**, **parameter list**, and a **pointer to the macro body** in the assembler's working memory. When the assembler encounters a macro call, it indexes into this table to fetch the body for expansion.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Programmer Workflow with Macro Libraries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Programmer writes mylib.mac] --> LIB[(Macro Library .mac file)]
    LIB -- INCLUDE directive --> ASM[main.asm source]
    ASM -- MASM TASM Assembler --> OBJ[.OBJ file with all macros expanded]
    OBJ -- LINK --> EXE[.EXE ready to execute on 8086]
```

This is a **block-level functional architecture flow** that captures the engineering workflow for a macro-library-based 8086 project — the visual fallback for diagrams that would otherwise require hand-drawn schematics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a macro. How is it different from a procedure in 8086 assembly language programming?**

**Model Answer:**

A **macro** is a named block of assembly language statements defined between the directives `MACRO` and `ENDM` that is **textually expanded inline** by the assembler at assembly time whenever the macro name is invoked in the program.

| Aspect | Macro | Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| Expansion | At assembly time | At run time via `CALL` |
| Stack usage | None | Yes (return address) |
| Code size | Larger (inlined) | Smaller (single copy) |
| Execution speed | Faster | Slower (CALL/RET overhead) |
| Recursion | Not possible | Possible |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the purpose of the `LOCAL` directive inside a macro. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The `LOCAL` directive is used to declare labels inside a macro body as **unique to each expansion**. Without it, if a macro is called multiple times, all the inlined copies would contain labels with **identical names**, causing the assembler to throw a **"Symbol redefinition"** error.

`LOCAL` instructs the assembler to append a unique numeric suffix (e.g., `??0000`, `??0001`) to each label declaration on every expansion. This guarantees label uniqueness across multiple macro invocations, allowing the same macro to be reused safely.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat example, explain the **general syntax of defining and invoking a macro** in 8086 assembly language. List **any four macro-time operators** with their syntax. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a macro named `SWAPXY MACRO A, B` that exchanges the contents of two 16-bit registers `A` and `B` *without* using the `XCHG` instruction. **Show the full MASM program** that calls the macro twice with different arguments, and **show the equivalent expanded code** that the CPU will actually execute. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**General Syntax of Macro Definition and Invocation:**

A macro is **defined** once and **called** multiple times. The general MASM syntax is:

```asm
macroname  MACRO   [p1, p2, ... , pN]      ; definition header
            ; -------- macro body ----------
            ; instructions / directives
            ; referencing p1, p2, ...
            ; --------------------------------
            ENDM                            ; definition terminator
```

**Invocation (Call):**

```asm
macroname   arg1, arg2, ... , argN         ; assembler expands inline
```

**Four Macro-Time Operators:**

| Operator | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `&` | `p&num` | Concatenates parameter `p` with adjacent text to form one identifier |
| `< >` | `<text, with, commas>` | Forces a multi-token literal to be passed as a single argument |
| `!` | `!symbol` | Escapes a symbol from macro-expansion (treats it literally) |
| `%` | `%expr` | Evaluates the expression `expr` and substitutes its numeric value |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Macro Definition for `SWAPXY`:**

```asm
SWAPXY      MACRO   A, B
            MOV     AX, A
            MOV     CX, B
            MOV     B,  AX
            MOV     A,  CX
            ENDM
```

**Full Program with Two Calls:**

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.486
SWAPXY      MACRO   A, B
            MOV     AX, A
            MOV     CX, B
            MOV     B,  AX
            MOV     A,  CX
            ENDM
.STACK 100H
.DATA
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV   AX, @DATA
     MOV   DS, AX

     MOV   BX, 1111H
     MOV   DX, 2222H
     SWAPXY  BX, DX        ; <<< CALL 1

     MOV   SI, 3333H
     MOV   DI, 4444H
     SWAPXY  SI, DI        ; <<< CALL 2

     MOV   AH, 4CH
     INT   21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

**Equivalent Expanded Code (CPU-Visible):**

```asm
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV   AX, @DATA
     MOV   DS, AX

     MOV   BX, 1111H
     MOV   DX, 2222H
     MOV   AX, BX          ; <-- expansion of SWAPXY BX,DX
     MOV   CX, DX
     MOV   DX, AX
     MOV   BX, CX

     MOV   SI, 3333H
     MOV   DI, 4444H
     MOV   AX, SI          ; <-- expansion of SWAPXY SI,DI
     MOV   CX, DI
     MOV   DI, AX
     MOV   SI, CX

     MOV   AH, 4CH
     INT   21H
MAIN ENDP
```

**Verification of CALL 1:** Before swap, `BX = 1111H`, `DX = 2222H`. After the inlined instructions:
- `AX ← 1111H`, `CX ← 2222H`, `DX ← 1111H`, `BX ← 2222H`. ✓ Swapped.

**Verification of CALL 2:** Before swap, `SI = 3333H`, `DI = 4444H`. After the inlined instructions:
- `AX ← 3333H`, `CX ← 4444H`, `DI ← 3333H`, `SI ← 4444H`. ✓ Swapped.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **macros and procedures** in 8086 assembly language. Mention **at least six points of difference** and state when you would prefer a macro over a procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a MASM program that defines a parameterized macro `ADD3 MACRO op1, op2, op3, dest` which computes `dest ← op1 + op2 + op3` using 16-bit registers. Demonstrate the macro with **two calls** using different register sets, and present the **fully expanded source** that the assembler will produce. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table — Six Differences:**

| S.No. | Macro | Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Defined with `MACRO`/`ENDM` | Defined with `PROC`/`ENDP` |
| 2 | Expanded at **assembly time** (compile time) | Executed at **run time** via `CALL`/`RET` |
| 3 | **No** control transfer — body is inserted inline | Control is transferred via `CALL` instruction |
| 4 | **No** stack push/pop of return address | Stack is used to save/restore IP (and CS if far) |
| 5 | Generates **larger** code (body copied at every call) | Generates **smaller** code (single copy in memory) |
| 6 | **Faster** execution (no CALL/RET overhead) | Slower due to CALL/RET overhead |
| 7 | **Cannot** be recursive | Can be recursive |
| 8 | Visible as expanded code in `.LST` file | Visible only as a `CALL` site in `.LST` file |

**When to prefer a macro over a procedure:**
A macro is preferred when the body is **short** (typically 2–5 instructions) and is invoked **frequently** in a *time-critical* section of code, where eliminating the CALL/RET overhead is more important than the extra memory consumed by inlined copies.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Macro Definition:**

```asm
ADD3        MACRO   op1, op2, op3, dest
            MOV     AX, op1
            ADD     AX, op2
            ADD     AX, op3
            MOV     dest, AX
            ENDM
```

**Full Program with Two Calls:**

```asm
.MODEL SMALL
.486
ADD3        MACRO   op1, op2, op3, dest
            MOV     AX, op1
            ADD     AX, op2
            ADD     AX, op3
            MOV     dest, AX
            ENDM
.STACK 100H
.DATA
.CODE
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV   AX, @DATA
     MOV   DS, AX

     MOV   BX, 0010H
     MOV   CX, 0020H
     MOV   DX, 0030H
     ADD3  BX, CX, DX, SI      ; <<< CALL 1:  SI ← BX+CX+DX

     MOV   BP, 0A0AH
     MOV   DI, 0B0BH
     MOV   SP_TEMP, 0C0CH      ; (assumed symbolic)
     ADD3  BP, DI, SP_TEMP, BX ; <<< CALL 2:  BX ← BP+DI+SP_TEMP

     MOV   AH, 4CH
     INT   21H
MAIN ENDP
END MAIN
```

**Fully Expanded Code (CPU-Visible):**

```asm
MAIN PROC FAR
     MOV   AX, @DATA
     MOV   DS, AX

     MOV   BX, 0010H
     MOV   CX, 0020H
     MOV   DX, 0030H
     MOV   AX, BX              ; <-- expansion of CALL 1
     ADD   AX, CX
     ADD   AX, DX
     MOV   SI, AX

     MOV   BP, 0A0AH
     MOV   DI, 0B0BH
     MOV   SP_TEMP, 0C0CH
     MOV   AX, BP              ; <-- expansion of CALL 2
     ADD   AX, DI
     ADD   AX, SP_TEMP
     MOV   BX, AX

     MOV   AH, 4CH
     INT   21H
MAIN ENDP
```

**Verification of CALL 1:** `AX ← 0x0010`, then `AX ← 0x0010 + 0x0020 = 0x0030`, then `AX ← 0x0030 + 0x0030 = 0x0060`, then `SI ← 0x0060`. ✓

**Verification of CALL 2:** `AX ← 0x0A0A`, then `AX ← 0x0A0A + 0x0B0B = 0x1515`, then `AX ← 0x1515 + 0x0C0C = 0x2121`, then `BX ← 0x2121`. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Macros questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `&` operator** when building labels with parameters — the assembler will not substitute and your label will not be unique.
> 2. **Forgetting `LOCAL`** when a macro containing a label is invoked more than once — you will get a *"Symbol already defined"* error at assembly time.
> 3. **Confusing macro CALL with procedure CALL** — saying "macro transfers control to the body" is wrong. Macro body is *copied*; there is no transfer of control.
> 4. **Omitting angle brackets `< >`** when passing strings with commas or spaces — the assembler splits the argument incorrectly.
> 5. **Failing to show the expanded code** in 14-mark answers. The KTU examiner *always* expects both the **macro source** and the **assembler-expanded equivalent code**. Marks are split roughly 50/50 between them.
> 6. **Using a macro where a procedure is required** (e.g., recursive or far-pointer code) — leads to a *logically incorrect* program even if it assembles.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **macro** is a **named, parameterizable block of assembly text** that is **expanded inline by the assembler at assembly time** — there is **no `CALL`/`RET`** involved.
- Definition uses `MACRO` … `ENDM`; invocation is just the macro name followed by positional arguments.
- Macro expansion is performed by the **assembler (preprocessor)**, not the CPU — the final `.EXE` contains only the inlined code.
- The **`&`** operator concatenates a parameter with surrounding text to form one identifier (used to build labels).
- The **`< >`** text-delimiter operator passes a multi-token literal (containing commas/spaces) as a single argument.
- The **`!`** operator escapes a symbol from macro-expansion.
- The **`%`** operator forces pre-evaluation of an expression to its numeric value before substitution.
- The **`LOCAL`** directive declares labels as unique to each expansion by appending a numeric suffix — mandatory when a macro is called more than once and contains internal labels.
- A **macro library** is a separate file (e.g., `mylib.mac`) of macro definitions pulled into a program using `INCLUDE mylib.mac`.
- **Macro vs Procedure — key contrasts:**
  - Macro = compile-time inline, larger code, faster, no stack, no recursion.
  - Procedure = run-time `CALL`, smaller code, slower, uses stack, supports recursion.
- The KTU `.LST` (listing) file explicitly shows macro expansions — always refer to it when verifying your program.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **interrupt** is a hardware- or software-initiated signal/event that causes the microprocessor to temporarily suspend the execution of its current program, save the state of the CPU (status, instruction pointer, and registers) on the **stack**, and transfer control to a special routine known as the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR finishes execution, the CPU restores the saved state from the stack and resumes the original program from the exact point where it was interrupted. In the context of the **8086 microprocessor**, the Interrupt Structure is centralized through the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** — a 1 KB block of memory located at the physical address range **`00000H` to `003FFH`** — which holds the **CS:IP** (segmented addresses) of up to **256** different interrupt service routines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8086 supports **256 interrupt types**, numbered from `00H` to `FFH`. Each vector is exactly **4 bytes** long: 2 bytes for IP (offset) followed by 2 bytes for CS (segment). Total IVT size = 256 × 4 = 1024 bytes = **1 KB**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Phone Call" Analogy Imagine you are a KTU student solving an exam paper in your study room: 1. You are the **CPU**, deeply focused on the main task (your program). 2. Suddenly, your phone rings. The ring is the **interrupt signal**. 3. You stop writing, mark the page with a bookmark (this is **pushing the IP, CS, and flags onto the stack**). 4. You pick up the phone and attend to the caller — this is the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. 5. After hanging up, you look at the bookmark (stack pop) and continue writing exactly from where you left off. This perfectly mirrors how a CPU handles interrupts. Without interrupts, the CPU would have to "poll" every device continuously, wasting enormous clock cycles. Interrupts enable **event-driven execution**, drastically improving efficiency. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** | A signal sent to the CPU to request attention. | | **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** | The subroutine executed in response to an interrupt. Also called the **interrupt handler**. | | **Interrupt Vector** | The memory address (in IVT) that contains the address of the ISR. | | **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** | A lookup table in low memory holding ISR addresses. | | **Maskable Interrupt** | An interrupt that can be disabled (masked) using the **Interrupt Flag (IF)**. | | **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** | A high-priority interrupt that **cannot** be disabled by clearing IF. | | **Interrupt Latency** | The time delay between the interrupt request and the start of ISR execution. | | **Interrupt Nesting** | The ability of an ISR to be interrupted by another (higher-priority) interrupt. | > [!NOTE] > **Why do we need interrupts?** Without interrupts, the CPU must constantly check (poll) every I/O device. This wastes CPU time. Interrupts let the CPU execute useful code and respond to peripherals **only when needed**, achieving efficient multitasking and real-time response. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of 8086 Interrupts The 8086 interrupt system is broadly classified into three categories: ### A. Hardware Interrupts (External) Originate from external devices via physical pins on the processor. 1. **NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt)** — Pin 17 - Edge-triggered (rising edge), **cannot be masked** by clearing the IF flag. - Vectored to **Type 2 interrupt** (vector address `00008H`). - Used for catastrophic events (e.g., power failure, memory parity error). 2. **INTR (Interrupt Request)** — Pin 18 - Level-triggered, **maskable** using the `CLI` instruction (Clear Interrupt Flag). - Vector number is supplied externally via the data bus by an **interrupt controller** (e.g., 8259A PIC). - The CPU acknowledges via the `\overline{INTA}` (Interrupt Acknowledge) signal. ### B. Software Interrupts (Internal / Exceptions) Generated by executing the `INT n` instruction. 1. **Predefined (Dedicated) Interrupts** — Types 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 - Type 0: Divide-by-zero error (e.g., `DIV` by zero). - Type 1: Single-step (used by debuggers, triggered by TF = 1). - Type 2: NMI hardware pin. - Type 3: Breakpoint (one-byte `INT 3` instruction). - Type 4: Overflow (triggered by `INTO` when OF = 1). 2. **User-Defined Interrupts** — Types 5 to 255 - `INT n` where `n` ranges from `5` to `FFH`. - Useful for invoking OS-level services or custom routines. ### C. Exceptions (Processor-Generated) Raised automatically by the CPU upon detecting an error condition during instruction execution (e.g., divide error, segment fault). ## 2.2 The Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) — Detailed Structure The IVT occupies memory locations `00000H` to `003FFH`. Each of the **256** interrupt types occupies **4 bytes** structured as follows: $$\text{Vector Address} = \text{Type Number} \times 4$$ For an interrupt of type `n`: $$\text{IP address stored at: } (n \times 4) \text{ and } (n \times 4) + 1$$ $$\text{CS address stored at: } (n \times 4) + 2 \text{ and } (n \times 4) + 3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **IVT Memory Layout Example (Lowest 32 bytes):** > - `00000H` – `00003H` → Type 0 (Divide Error) — IP at 0,1; CS at 2,3 > - `00004H` – `00007H` → Type 1 (Single Step) — IP at 4,5; CS at 6,7 > - `00008H` – `0000BH` → Type 2 (NMI) — IP at 8,9; CS at A,B > - `0000CH` – `0000FH` → Type 3 (Breakpoint) — IP at C,D; CS at E,F > - `00010H` – `00013H` → Type 4 (Overflow) — IP at 10,11; CS at 12,13 ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Equations | S.No. | Formula / Rule | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{IVT Size} = 256 \times 4 = 1024 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ KB}$ | Total memory occupied by IVT. | | 2 | $\text{Vector Address} = \text{Type} \times 4$ | Physical address in IVT for given interrupt type. | | 3 | $\text{Number of supported interrupts} = 2^8 = 256$ | Ranges from `00H` to `FFH`. | | 4 | $\text{IP storage offset} = 4n$ and $4n+1$ | Low word of ISR pointer. | | 5 | $\text{CS storage offset} = 4n+2$ and $4n+3$ | High word (segment) of ISR pointer. | | 6 | $\text{Interrupt Acknowledge cycle} = 2 \text{ INTA pulses}$ | CPU sends two `\overline{INTA}` signals for INTR. | | 7 | $\text{TRAP (80286+)} = \text{Software-generated NMI}$ | Used for debugging faults in protected mode. | ## 2.4 The 8086 Interrupt Processing Sequence — Step by Step When an interrupt is triggered (whether hardware or software), the 8086 CPU executes the following sequence: 1. **Decode the interrupt type** (`n`) — either from instruction, hardware, or pre-defined source. 2. **Push the FLAGS register** onto the stack (decrement SP by 2). 3. **Clear the Interrupt Flag (IF)** and **Trap Flag (TF)** — to prevent further maskable interrupts and single-stepping during ISR. 4. **Push CS register** onto the stack (decrement SP by 2). 5. **Push IP (Instruction Pointer)** onto the stack (decrement SP by 2). 6. **Fetch the new IP** from IVT location $(n \times 4)$ and $(n \times 4)+1$. 7. **Fetch the new CS** from IVT location $(n \times 4)+2$ and $(n \times 4)+3$. 8. **Jump** to the new CS:IP and begin ISR execution. 9. At ISR end, execute `IRET` (Interrupt Return), which: - Pops IP from stack. - Pops CS from stack. - Pops FLAGS from stack. - Resumes the original program. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This structured sequence is the foundation of modern **Operating Systems** (Windows, Linux) and **Real-Time Systems (RTOS)**. Every keyboard keystroke, mouse click, network packet, and timer tick uses this exact mechanism in some form. # 3.
