Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing the IVT Vector Address for a Given Interrupt Type **Problem:** Find the physical address in the IVT where the CS:IP of the ISR for interrupt type `INT 21H` is stored. **Step 1 — Identify the interrupt type number.** $$n = 21\text{H} = 2 \times 16 + 1 = 33 \text{ (decimal)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the vector address formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Vector Address} &= n \times 4 \\ &= 33 \times 4 \\ &= 132 \text{ (decimal)} \\ &= 84\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — State the memory layout for the CS:IP pair.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IP (low byte)} &\rightarrow \text{Address } 00084\text{H} \\ \text{IP (high byte)} &\rightarrow \text{Address } 00085\text{H} \\ \text{CS (low byte)} &\rightarrow \text{Address } 00086\text{H} \\ \text{CS (high byte)} &\rightarrow \text{Address } 00087\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verify total IVT range check.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximum vector address} &= \text{FFH} \times 4 = 1020 \text{ (decimal)} = 3\text{FC}_{\text{H}} \\ \text{IVT ends at } & 00000\text{H} + 3\text{FFH} = 003\text{FFH} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The ISR pointer for `INT 21H` resides in the 4-byte block starting at physical address **`00084H`**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculating the Physical Address of the ISR from CS:IP **Problem:** Suppose the IVT for `INT 10H` contains `IP = 4A2CH` and `CS = 1234H`. Compute the actual physical address at which the ISR begins execution. **Step 1 — Recall the 8086 physical address formula (segmented memory model).** $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{CS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{IP}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Physical Address} &= (1234\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 4\text{A2C}\text{H} \\ &= 12340\text{H} + 4\text{A2C}\text{H} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the hexadecimal addition column-wise.** $$ \begin{aligned} 12340\text{H} &+ 4\text{A2C}\text{H} \\ &= 12340 + 4\text{A2C} \\ &= 16\text{D6C}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to decimal for verification.** $$ \begin{aligned} 1234\text{H} &= 4660_{10} \\ 4\text{A2C}\text{H} &= 18988_{10} \\ 4660 \times 16 + 18988 &= 74560 + 18988 = 93548_{10} \\ 16\text{D6C}\text{H} &= 1 \times 16^4 + 6 \times 16^3 + 13 \times 16^2 + 6 \times 16 + 12 \\ &= 65536 + 24576 + 3328 + 96 + 12 \\ &= 93548_{10} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** The ISR for `INT 10H` begins executing at physical address **`16D6CH`**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stack Frame Built During Interrupt Processing **Problem:** Show the contents of the stack (from top to bottom) just after the CPU enters the ISR for a hardware interrupt on the INTR line, given that the original SP = `1000H`. **Step 1 — CPU pushes FLAGS first.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SP after pushing FLAGS} &= 1000\text{H} - 2 = 0\text{FFE}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — CPU pushes CS next.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SP after pushing CS} &= 0\text{FFE}\text{H} - 2 = 0\text{FFC}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — CPU pushes IP last (on top of stack).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SP after pushing IP} &= 0\text{FFC}\text{H} - 2 = 0\text{FFA}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Final stack frame (from top to bottom).** | Memory Address (Top → Bottom) | Contents | Remarks | |---|---|---| | `0FFAH`, `0FFBH` | IP (return offset) | Top of stack | | `0FFCH`, `0FFDH` | CS (return segment) | Middle | | `0FFEH`, `0FFFH` | FLAGS register | Bottom of frame | **Step 5 — On `IRET`, the order is reversed.** IP is popped first, then CS, then FLAGS — exactly restoring the pre-interrupt CPU state. > [!NOTE] > **Stack grows downward** in the 8086 architecture. Each PUSH decrements SP by 2, and each POP increments it. This is critical for the **IRET** instruction to restore the state in the correct reverse order. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Interrupt Types | Feature | Hardware (NMI) | Hardware (INTR) | Software (`INT n`) | Software (Exception) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Source** | External pin | External pin + 8259A | Instruction in code | CPU-detected error | | **Type Number** | Fixed (Type 2) | Variable (0–255) | Explicit in opcode | Fixed (e.g., 0, 4) | | **Maskable?** | No | Yes (via `CLI`) | No (always accepted) | No | | **Vector Source** | Internal (IVT) | External data bus | Internal (IVT) | Internal (IVT) | | **Acknowledge Signal?** | No | Yes (`\overline{INTA}`) | No | No | | **Priority** | Highest (after DIV err) | Programmable (via 8259) | Lowest | High (varies) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 8086 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Physical Address 00000H - 003FFH
Total: 1 KB = 256 Interrupts"] --> B["Type 00H: Divide Error
Addr 00000H-00003H"] A --> C["Type 01H: Single Step
Addr 00004H-00007H"] A --> D["Type 02H: NMI
Addr 00008H-0000BH"] A --> E["Type 03H: Breakpoint INT 3
Addr 0000CH-0000FH"] A --> F["Type 04H: INTO Overflow
Addr 00010H-00013H"] A --> G["Types 05H-1FH: Reserved
by Intel"] A --> H["Types 20H-FFH: User / DOS
e.g., INT 21H at 00084H"] B --> B1["Bytes 0,1: IP
Bytes 2,3: CS"] C --> C1["Bytes 4,5: IP
Bytes 6,7: CS"] D --> D1["Bytes 8,9: IP
Bytes A,B: CS"] H --> H1["DOS Function Calls
Keyboard, Disk, Display"] ``` ## 4.2 Interrupt Processing Flowchart (8086) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Normal Program Execution"]) --> Check{"Interrupt
Request?"} Check -- "No" --> Start Check -- "Yes" --> Push1["Push FLAGS onto Stack"] Push1 --> Clear["Clear IF and TF Flags"] Clear --> Push2["Push CS onto Stack"] Push2 --> Push3["Push IP onto Stack"] Push3 --> Fetch["Fetch new IP from IVT
at address Type x 4"] Fetch --> Fetch2["Fetch new CS from IVT
at address Type x 4 + 2"] Fetch2 --> Jump["Jump to ISR at CS:IP"] Jump --> ISR["Execute Interrupt Service Routine"] ISR --> IRET["Execute IRET Instruction"] IRET --> Pop1["Pop IP from Stack"] Pop1 --> Pop2["Pop CS from Stack"] Pop2 --> Pop3["Pop FLAGS from Stack"] Pop3 --> Resume(["Resume Original Program"]) Resume --> Start ``` ## 4.3 8086 Interrupt Architecture — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["8086 Microprocessor"] EU["Execution Unit
Registers, ALU"] BIU["Bus Interface Unit
Instruction Queue, CS,IP"] IF["Interrupt Logic
IF, TF Flags"] end subgraph Pins["External Pins"] NMI["NMI Pin
Type 2"] INTR["INTR Pin
Type via Bus"] INTA["INTA Output
Acknowledge"] end subgraph IVT["Interrupt Vector Table
00000H-003FFH"] V0["256 Vectors x 4 bytes"] end subgraph ISR["Interrupt Service Routines"] ISR0["ISR for Type 0"] ISR1["ISR for Type 1"] ISRN["ISR for Type n"] end NMI --> IF INTR --> IF IF -- "Trigger" --> BIU BIU -- "Read Vector" --> IVT INTA -- "Two Pulses" --> INTR IVT -- "CS:IP Loaded" --> BIU BIU -- "Execute" --> ISR ISR -- "IRET" --> EU ``` ## 4.4 IVT Entry Structure (4 Bytes per Interrupt) ```mermaid flowchart LR Addr["Address: Type x 4"] --> IP_L["Low Byte of IP"] IP_L --> IP_H["High Byte of IP"] IP_H --> CS_L["Low Byte of CS"] CS_L --> CS_H["High Byte of CS"] CS_H --> Next["Next Interrupt Type
at Type x 4 + 4"] style IP_L fill:#e1f5ff style IP_H fill:#e1f5ff style CS_L fill:#ffe1e1 style CS_H fill:#ffe1e1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is an interrupt? List any four types of interrupts supported by the 8086 microprocessor.** **Model Answer:** An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that temporarily halts the main program execution, causing the CPU to execute a special routine called the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). After ISR completion, the CPU returns to the main program. Four types of interrupts in 8086: 1. **Hardware Interrupts** — NMI (Type 2) and INTR (maskable). 2. **Software Interrupts** — `INT n` instruction (e.g., `INT 21H`). 3. **Internal Interrupts / Exceptions** — Type 0 (Divide Error), Type 4 (Overflow). 4. **Single-Step Interrupt** — Type 1 (triggered when TF = 1). *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing types)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain the role of the Interrupt Flag (IF) and Trap Flag (TF) in the 8086.** **Model Answer:** - **Interrupt Flag (IF):** Controls the **masking of the INTR pin**. When `IF = 1`, maskable interrupts are **enabled** (accepted). When `IF = 0`, they are **disabled** (masked). It is set/cleared using `STI` and `CLI` instructions. - **Trap Flag (TF):** When `TF = 1`, the CPU automatically generates a **Type 1 (single-step) interrupt** after every instruction. This is used by debuggers to execute programs one instruction at a time. *(3 marks: 1.5 marks each)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake #1:** Students often confuse the **vector address** (location in IVT) with the **ISR address** (where the ISR actually runs). The vector address is computed as `Type × 4`; the ISR address is loaded *from* the IVT. > - **Mistake #2:** Forgetting that `CLI` only masks **INTR**, **not NMI** or software interrupts. > - **Mistake #3:** Writing `STI` instead of `IRET` at the end of an ISR — this will crash the program because flags/IP/CS are not restored. > - **Mistake #4:** Miscalculating vector addresses (especially crossing the `0FFH` boundary). --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** of 8086 and describe how the CPU computes the vector address for a given interrupt type. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **complete sequence of operations performed by the 8086** when it receives an interrupt on the INTR pin. Also explain the role of the `INTA` signal. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a 1 KB block located at physical address `00000H` to `003FFH`. It contains 256 entries, one for each interrupt type. Each entry is 4 bytes long, holding the IP (2 bytes, low word) and CS (2 bytes, high word) of the ISR. **Computation of Vector Address:** For an interrupt of type `n`, the IVT entry starts at: $$\text{Vector Address} = 4 \times n$$ **Example:** For `n = 21H = 33`: $$\text{Vector Address} = 33 \times 4 = 132 = 84\text{H} \text{ (i.e., physical address 00084H)}$$ The IP is stored at `00084H`–`00085H` and CS at `00086H`–`00087H`. The CPU fetches this CS:IP and jumps to the ISR. *(Diagram: refer Section 4.1.)* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (b): When the 8086 receives an interrupt on the **INTR pin**, the following sequence occurs: 1. The CPU **checks IF**. If `IF = 1`, it accepts the interrupt; if `IF = 0`, it is masked. 2. The CPU **activates the INTA (Interrupt Acknowledge) bus cycle** in two pulses. 3. During the **first `INTA` pulse**, the CPU sends a lock signal to the bus and floats the data bus to allow the external **8259A PIC** to place the interrupt type number on the bus. 4. During the **second `INTA` pulse**, the CPU reads the type number from the data bus. 5. The CPU **pushes FLAGS, CS, and IP** onto the stack in that order. 6. The CPU **clears IF and TF** to disable further maskable interrupts. 7. The CPU **fetches the new CS:IP** from the IVT at address `4 × type`. 8. Execution jumps to the ISR. 9. At the end, the `IRET` instruction pops IP, CS, and FLAGS, returning to the main program. **Role of `INTA`:** It is an active-low output signal that informs the external interrupt controller (8259A) that the CPU has acknowledged the interrupt. The 8259A uses this to place the interrupt type number onto the data bus. **(7 marks)** --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Differentiate between **maskable and non-maskable interrupts** in 8086. Explain the dedicated interrupts Type 0 to Type 4. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An interrupt `INT 50H` is executed in an 8086 system. The IVT entry for this interrupt contains `IP = ABCDH` and `CS = 1000H`. Calculate: (i) The physical address in the IVT where this entry is stored. (ii) The physical address at which the ISR begins execution. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): | Parameter | Maskable Interrupt (INTR) | Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) | |---|---|---| | Pin | Pin 18 (INTR) | Pin 17 (NMI) | | Maskable via IF? | Yes (`CLI` disables it) | No — cannot be disabled | | Type number | Variable (0–255) supplied via bus | Fixed: Type 2 | | Trigger | Level-triggered | Edge-triggered (rising) | | Acknowledge signal | Two `INTA` pulses required | No `INTA` required | | Priority | Lower than NMI | Highest priority | | Use | I/O devices, peripherals | Power failure, memory parity | **Dedicated Interrupts (Types 0 to 4):** - **Type 0 (Divide Error):** Generated when a `DIV` or `IDIV` instruction attempts division by zero or produces a quotient too large for the destination register. Vector at `00000H`. - **Type 1 (Single Step):** Generated after every instruction if `TF = 1`. Vector at `00004H`. Used for debugging. - **Type 2 (NMI):** Hardware non-maskable interrupt. Vector at `00008H`. - **Type 3 (Breakpoint):** Triggered by the one-byte `INT 3` instruction. Vector at `0000CH`. Used by debuggers. - **Type 4 (Overflow):** Triggered by `INTO` instruction if `OF = 1`. Vector at `00010H`. Used after signed arithmetic. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Physical address in IVT for INT 50H:** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 50\text{H} = 80 \text{ decimal} \\ \text{Vector Address} &= 4 \times n = 4 \times 80 = 320 \text{ decimal} = 140\text{H} \\ \text{Physical Address in IVT} &= 00140\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ Layout: - `00140H`, `00141H` → Low, High bytes of IP - `00142H`, `00143H` → Low, High bytes of CS **(3 marks)** **(ii) Physical address at which ISR begins execution:** The 8086 computes the physical address using the segmented formula: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Physical Address} &= (\text{CS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{IP} \\ &= (1000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{ABCD}\text{H} \\ &= 10000\text{H} + \text{ABCD}\text{H} \\ &= 1\text{ABCD}\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ Verification in decimal: - $1000\text{H} = 4096$, $\text{ABCD}\text{H} = 43981$ - $4096 \times 16 + 43981 = 65536 + 43981 = 109517_{10}$ - $1\text{ABCD}\text{H} = 1 \times 65536 + 10 \times 4096 + 11 \times 256 + 12 \times 16 + 13 = 109517_{10} \checkmark$ **Final Answers:** - (i) IVT entry is at physical address **`00140H`**. - (ii) ISR begins execution at physical address **`1ABCDH`**. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The 8086 supports **256 interrupts** (`00H` to `FFH`), with the IVT occupying the **first 1 KB** of memory (`00000H`–`003FFH`). - **Vector address** = `Type × 4`. This is the location in the IVT, **not** the ISR address. - The **CPU pushes FLAGS first, then CS, then IP** onto the stack. `IRET` reverses this order. - **NMI is non-maskable**; it always gets serviced, even if `IF = 0`. - **INTR requires two `INTA` pulses** for the 8259A PIC to deliver the type number on the data bus. - **Software interrupts (`INT n`) cannot be masked** by `IF` — they execute immediately. - The **`IRET` instruction is mandatory** at the end of every ISR to restore the CPU state. - The 8086 **clears IF and TF** automatically upon entering an ISR to prevent nested maskable interrupts and single-stepping. - **Dedicated interrupts** are Types 0–4; **Types 5–31** are reserved by Intel; **Types 32–255** are available for user/OS use. - For the KTU exam, always write the **CS:IP → Physical Address** conversion as: **PA = (CS × 16) + IP**, in hexadecimal, with clear step-by-step arithmetic. - Distinguish between **Interrupt Acknowledge (`INTA`)** (output from CPU) and **Interrupt Request (`INTR`)** (input to CPU). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU Exams:** Always **underline the final answer** and **show the formula before substituting values**. Examiners award step-marks generously if the method is clear, even if the final arithmetic has minor errors.

topics.title: Byte and String Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Byte and String Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Byte Manipulation** in the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** refers to a class of assembly language instructions that operate on **8-bit (1-byte)** data operands. These instructions perform arithmetic, logical, shift, rotate, and data transfer operations on individual bytes stored in registers, memory locations, or as immediate values. The **8086 microprocessor** has a **16-bit data bus internally** but processes bytes efficiently through its dual-architecture design (BIU and EU).

**String Manipulation** refers to a powerful set of instructions specifically designed by Intel to handle **sequences (arrays) of bytes or words** stored in memory. A "string" in 8086 assembly is a contiguous block of memory containing elements of equal size (byte or word), and special instructions like `MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `LODS`, and `STOS` — combined with the `REP` prefix — allow the processor to perform operations on entire arrays with single-instruction execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8086 is a **16-bit microprocessor** with a **16-bit data bus** (8088 has 8-bit external data bus but 16-bit internal). String instructions are one of the most exam-favored topics in Module 1 of OECIT615.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

### The "Words and Sentences" Analogy

Imagine you are reading a book:
- A **single letter** in the book = **1 Byte** (8 bits)
- A **word** in the book = **1 Word** (16 bits = 2 bytes)
- A **sentence** in the book = **A String** (a sequence of bytes/words)

**Byte manipulation** is like editing individual letters — you change one character at a time (changing an 'A' to a 'B').

**String manipulation** is like copying an entire sentence, finding a misspelled word in a paragraph, or comparing two sentences letter-by-letter. Instead of processing letter-by-letter with hundreds of instructions, the 8086 has **dedicated instructions** that do this in a **single, automatic loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A "string" in 8086 is **NOT** the same as a high-level language string (which ends with a null character `\0`). In 8086, a string is simply a **contiguous memory block**, and the programmer must know the **count** of elements in advance (stored in `CX` register).

## 1.3 Standard 8086 Metrics and Boundaries

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Data Word Size | **16 bits** (2 bytes) | Internal register width |
| Byte Size | **8 bits** | Smallest addressable unit |
| Address Bus | **20 bits** | Address up to **1 MB** (2²⁰ = 1,048,576) |
| Segment Size | **64 KB** (65,536 bytes) | One segment |
| String Index Registers | **SI** (Source), **DI** (Destination) | Auto-increment/decrement |
| String Counter | **CX** Register | Holds element count |
| Direction Flag | **DF** (bit 10 of FLAGS) | Controls SI/DI auto-direction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Address Calculation (KTU High-Yield Formula):**
> $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Segment Register} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$$
> $$\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \ll 4 + \text{Offset}$$

The `\times 16` is equivalent to a **4-bit left shift** (`<< 4`), which is why the segment register contributes only the upper 16 bits of the 20-bit physical address.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Byte Manipulation Instructions — Complete Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions

**MOV (Move) Instruction**
- **Syntax:** `MOV destination, source`
- **Function:** Copies data from source to destination. **Source is unchanged.**
- **Restrictions:** Both operands cannot be memory; segment registers cannot be moved directly to/from memory; immediate-to-segment-register not allowed.
- **Flags Affected:** **None**

**MOVZX (Move with Zero-Extend)**
- **Syntax:** `MOVZX reg16, reg8/mem8` or `MOVZX reg32, reg16/reg8/mem8`
- **Function:** Moves a smaller operand into a larger one, **filling upper bits with 0**.
- **KTU Use Case:** Sign-extending safely when the value is known to be unsigned.

**MOVSX (Move with Sign-Extend)**
- **Syntax:** `MOVSX reg16, reg8/mem8`
- **Function:** Moves a smaller operand into a larger one, **replicating the sign bit (MSB)** into the upper bits.
- **KTU Use Case:** Converting signed byte to signed word.

**XCHG (Exchange)**
- **Syntax:** `XCHG operand1, operand2`
- **Function:** Swaps contents of two operands.
- **Special Form:** `XCHG AX, reg16` is encoded in a single byte (most efficient).
- **Flags Affected:** **None**

### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|
| `ADD dest, src` | dest = dest + src | All arithmetic flags |
| `ADC dest, src` | dest = dest + src + CF | All (used in multi-byte addition) |
| `SUB dest, src` | dest = dest − src | All |
| `SBB dest, src` | dest = dest − src − CF | All (multi-byte subtraction) |
| `CMP dest, src` | dest − src (result discarded) | All (only flags change) |
| `NEG dest` | dest = 0 − dest (two's complement) | All (CF=1 except when dest=0) |
| `INC dest` | dest = dest + 1 | All except CF |
| `DEC dest` | dest = dest − 1 | All except CF |
| `MUL src` | AX = AL × src (unsigned byte) | CF, OF (undefined for SF, ZF, AF, PF) |
| `IMUL src` | AX = AL × src (signed byte) | CF, OF |
| `DIV src` | AL = AX/src, AH = AX MOD src | Undefined (all) |
| `IDIV src` | Signed division | Undefined (all) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** `MUL` and `DIV` operate on **unsigned** numbers. `IMUL` and `IDIV` operate on **signed** (two's complement) numbers. The choice affects how the result is interpreted, NOT the bit pattern.

### 2.1.3 Logical Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Truth Table Highlight |
|---|---|---|
| `AND dest, src` | Bitwise AND | 1·1=1, else 0 (used to mask bits) |
| `OR dest, src` | Bitwise OR | 0·0=0, else 1 (used to set bits) |
| `XOR dest, src` | Bitwise XOR | Different=1, Same=0 (used to clear reg: `XOR AX,AX`) |
| `NOT dest` | One's complement (invert bits) | 1→0, 0→1 |
| `TEST dest, src` | dest AND src (result discarded) | Flags only, no operand change |

- **AND, OR, XOR, TEST:** All **clear CF and OF**, set ZF based on result, leave AF undefined, modify SF and PF.
- **NOT:** Affects **no flags**.

### 2.1.4 Shift and Rotate Instructions

Shift and rotate instructions operate on bits within an 8-bit or 16-bit operand.

**Shift Left (SHL) / Shift Arithmetic Left (SAL)**
- **Operation:** Bits shifted left; LSB filled with 0; MSB shifted into CF.
- **Effect:** Equivalent to unsigned multiplication by 2ⁿ.
- **Visual:**
  ```
  CF ← [MSB ... LSB] ← 0
  ```

**Shift Right (SHR)**
- **Operation:** Bits shifted right; MSB filled with 0; LSB shifted into CF.
- **Effect:** Equivalent to unsigned division by 2ⁿ.

**Shift Arithmetic Right (SAR)**
- **Operation:** Bits shifted right; **MSB is preserved (sign extension)**; LSB into CF.
- **Effect:** Signed division by 2ⁿ (preserves sign).

**Rotate Left (ROL)**
- **Operation:** Bits shifted left; MSB wraps around to LSB AND to CF.

**Rotate Right (ROR)**
- **Operation:** Bits shifted right; LSB wraps around to MSB AND to CF.

**Rotate Through Carry Left (RCL)**
- **Operation:** 17-bit rotation (CF included); MSB → CF, old CF → LSB.

**Rotate Through Carry Right (RCR)**
- **Operation:** 17-bit rotation; LSB → CF, old CF → MSB.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For a 16-bit operand, `RCL` and `RCR` operate on a **17-bit field** (16 operand bits + CF). This is often a 2-mark question.

### 2.1.5 Sign Extension Instructions

- **CBW (Convert Byte to Word):** If AL < 80H, AH = 00H; else AH = FFH. Extends sign of AL into AH.
- **CWD (Convert Word to Doubleword):** If AX < 8000H, DX = 0000H; else DX = FFFFH. Extends sign of AX into DX.
- **Flags Affected:** **None**

## 2.2 String Manipulation Instructions — Complete Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 The Five Primitive String Instructions

| Mnemonic | Full Form | Operation | Memory Operands |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOVS` / `MOVSB` / `MOVSW` | Move String | `[DI] ← [SI]`, then SI/DI ± 1 or 2 | Source: DS:SI, Dest: ES:DI |
| `CMPS` / `CMPSB` / `CMPSW` | Compare String | `[SI] − [DI]`, flags set, then SI/DI ± | Source: DS:SI, Dest: ES:DI |
| `SCAS` / `SCASB` / `SCASW` | Scan String | `AL/AX − [DI]`, flags set, then DI ± | Dest: ES:DI only |
| `LODS` / `LODSB` / `LODSW` | Load String | `AL/AX ← [SI]`, then SI ± | Source: DS:SI only |
| `STOS` / `STOSB` / `STOSW` | Store String | `[DI] ← AL/AX`, then DI ± | Dest: ES:DI only |

### 2.2.2 The Direction Flag and Auto-Increment/Decrement

- **`CLD` (Clear Direction Flag):** DF = 0 → **SI and DI auto-INCREMENT** after each string operation.
- **`STD` (Set Direction Flag):** DF = 1 → **SI and DI auto-DECREMENT**.
- **Increment size:** ±1 for byte variants, ±2 for word variants.

### 2.2.3 The REP Prefix Family

| Prefix | Condition | Termination | Used With |
|---|---|---|---|
| `REP` | — | When `CX = 0` | `MOVS`, `STOS` |
| `REPE` / `REPZ` | ZF = 1 | When `CX = 0` OR `ZF = 0` | `CMPS`, `SCAS` |
| `REPNE` / `REPNZ` | ZF = 0 | When `CX = 0` OR `ZF = 1` | `CMPS`, `SCAS` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** Before ANY string instruction with REP, you **MUST** initialize:
> 1. `CX` — number of repetitions
> 2. `DS:SI` — source address (if used)
> 3. `ES:DI` — destination address (if used)
> 4. `DF` — direction (via `CLD` or `STD`)
> 5. `AL`/`AX` — for `SCAS` and `LODS`

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical Address | $PA = Segment \times 16 + Offset$ | 20-bit |
| 2 | Segment Shift | $PA = (Segment \ll 4) + Offset$ | Logical left shift 4 |
| 3 | Byte addition | $CF = 1$ if unsigned sum > **255** | Byte overflow |
| 4 | Word addition | $CF = 1$ if unsigned sum > **65,535** | Word overflow |
| 5 | Signed byte range | $-128$ to $+127$ | Two's complement |
| 6 | Signed word range | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ | Two's complement |
| 7 | OF flag (signed) | $OF = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ of MSB | XOR of carry bits |
| 8 | CBW extension | If $AL \geq 80H$ then $AH = FFH$ | Sign bit = 1 |
| 9 | MUL byte | $AX = AL \times r/m8$ | 8-bit × 8-bit = 16-bit |
| 10 | DIV byte | $AL = AX \div r/m8$, $AH = AX \bmod r/m8$ | Quotient and remainder |
| 11 | SAR preserves sign | MSB unchanged, LSB → CF | Signed right shift |
| 12 | String index update | If $DF=0$: $SI \mathrel{+}= 1$ or $2$ | Auto-increment |
| 13 | REP termination | When $CX = 0$ (or condition fails) | CX decremented each rep |
| 14 | Two's complement negation | $\overline{X} + 1$ | NEG computes this |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pitfall:** Inside the above table, all `|` and absolute-value markers have been replaced with `\vert`-style wording to keep the markdown table parser stable. Never write `|x|` in a table row — write `absolute value of x`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Byte and string manipulation instructions form the **heartbeat of low-level software**:
- **Operating Systems:** Memory block copying (e.g., `memcpy` in C uses `REP MOVSB` internally).
- **Data Communication:** Frame parsing, packet scanning (`SCAS` finds delimiters).
- **Compilers:** String literal copying, table lookup, code generation.
- **Embedded Systems (Module 5 connection):** LCD/serial buffer manipulation in 8051/ARM.
- **Cryptography & DSP:** Bit rotation for shift-register ciphers (LFSRs) and convolution.
- **Bootloaders:** Sector-by-sector memory copy during BIOS-to-RAM handoff.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two's Complement and NEG Instruction

**Objective:** Prove that `NEG X` is equivalent to `NOT X` followed by `ADD X, 1`.

**Step 1 — Definition of Two's Complement Negation:**
The mathematical negation of a number $X$ in two's complement representation is defined as:

$$
-X = 2^n - X
$$

where $n$ is the number of bits (8 for byte, 16 for word).

**Step 2 — Rewrite the Right-Hand Side:**
Observe that $2^n$ for $n=8$ equals $256 = 0x100$. We can write:

$$
-X = (2^n - 1) - X + 1
$$

The expression $(2^n - 1)$ is the bitwise **NOT** of $X$ (all 1s minus $X$ flips every bit). Therefore:

$$
-X = \overline{X} + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Connect to Assembly:**
This is exactly what the 8086's microcoded `NEG` instruction performs in hardware:

$$\text{NEG } X \equiv \text{NOT } X \; ;\; \text{ADD } X, 1$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example:** Let $X = 0x5A$ (binary $0101\,1010$).

- $\overline{X} = 0xA5$ (binary $1010\,1010$)
- $\overline{X} + 1 = 0xA5 + 0x01 = 0xA6$ (binary $1010\,0110$)
- Verification: $0x5A + 0xA6 = 0x100 = 256$ ✓ (which is $2^8$ as required)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The `NEG` instruction is **not** simply multiplication by $-1$ in a generic sense; it is a **bit-pattern transformation** that the ALU performs in one microcycle. Recognizing this is the foundation of subtractor design in digital electronics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Overflow Flag (OF) for Signed Addition

**Objective:** Show that for two signed numbers $A$ and $B$ added in 8-bit, the overflow flag is:

$$
OF = C_{in}^{MSB} \oplus C_{out}^{MSB}
$$

**Step 1 — When does signed overflow occur?**
Signed overflow happens when the **sign of the result is mathematically impossible** — i.e., the result exceeds the representable range $[-128, +127]$ for 8-bit.

**Step 2 — Two scenarios that cause overflow:**
- Positive + Positive = Negative (carry INTO MSB = 0, carry OUT of MSB = 1)
- Negative + Negative = Positive (carry INTO MSB = 1, carry OUT of MSB = 0)

**Step 3 — Formal XOR rule:**
When the two carry bits (into bit 7 and out of bit 7) **differ**, the sign bit has been corrupted:

$$
OF = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}
$$

**Step 4 — Example:** $0x70 + 0x60$ (both positive).
- $0x70 = 0110\,0000$, $0x60 = 0110\,0000$
- Sum = $0xE0 = 1110\,0000$ (interpreted as $-32$, but $112 + 96 = 208$ which is positive)
- $C_{in}$ to bit 7 = 0, $C_{out}$ from bit 7 = 1
- $OF = 0 \oplus 1 = 1$ ✓ (overflow detected)

## 3.3 Worked Example: MOVS with REP (Memory Block Copy)

**Problem:** Copy 10 bytes from source `SRC` to destination `DST` in the same data segment.

**Assembly Program (with line-by-line analysis):**

```assembly
        ASSUME DS:DATA, ES:DATA
DATA    SEGMENT
SRC     DB  10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H
        DB  60H, 70H, 80H, 90H, 0A0H
DST     DB  10 DUP(?)
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
        ASSUME CS:CODE
START:  MOV  AX, DATA        ; AX = segment address
        MOV  DS, AX          ; DS points to data segment
        MOV  ES, AX          ; ES also points to data (must, for MOVS)
        CLD                  ; DF = 0, auto-increment mode
        LEA  SI, SRC         ; SI = offset of source
        LEA  DI, DST         ; DI = offset of destination
        MOV  CX, 10          ; Counter = 10 bytes
        REP  MOVSB           ; Copy [SI]→[DI], SI++, DI++, CX--; repeat until CX=0
        MOV  AH, 4CH         ; DOS terminate
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

**Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Iteration | CX (before) | [SI] value | [DI] before | [DI] after | SI after | DI after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 10H | ? | 10H | SRC+1 | DST+1 |
| 2 | 9 | 20H | ? | 20H | SRC+2 | DST+2 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 1 | 0A0H | ? | 0A0H | SRC+10 | DST+10 |
| Exit | 0 | — | — | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** The `REP MOVSB` instruction **does NOT** handle overlapping memory regions correctly if SI < DI. For overlapping copies, you must use `STD` (backward copy) to avoid corruption. This is the same issue addressed by `memmove` vs `memcpy` in C.

## 3.4 Worked Example: SCASB to Find a Character

**Problem:** Find the first occurrence of character 'K' (ASCII $0x4B$) in a 100-byte string at `BUFFER`.

**Program:**

```assembly
        CLD
        LEA  DI, BUFFER      ; Point DI to start
        MOV  AL, 'K'         ; Search key
        MOV  CX, 100         ; Max length
        REPNE SCASB          ; Scan while ZF=0 (mismatch)
        JZ   FOUND           ; ZF=1 means match found
NOTFOUND:
        ; ... not found handler
        JMP  DONE
FOUND:  DEC  DI              ; DI points PAST the match; back up by 1
        ; DI now holds offset of 'K'
DONE:
```

**Flag Analysis During Execution:**
- Each iteration: `AL - [DI]`
- If `AL = [DI]`: result is 0 → ZF = 1 → `REPNE` terminates.
- If `AL ≠ [DI]`: result is non-zero → ZF = 0 → `REPNE` continues.
- After termination, `CX` holds the number of characters **NOT scanned** (remaining count).
- `DI` points to the byte **after** the match; subtract 1 to get the match address.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using `REPE SCASB` instead of `REPNE SCASB`. `REPE` terminates on the **first mismatch** (wrong direction for "find a character"). Use `REPNE` to find a matching character.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Multiplication and Division

**Problem:** Compute $AL \times BL$ (unsigned byte multiply), then divide AX by CL.

```assembly
        MOV  AL, 25         ; AL = 0x19
        MOV  BL, 4          ; BL = 0x04
        MUL  BL             ; AX = 0x19 × 0x04 = 0x0064 (100)
        ; AX = 0064H, flags: CF=0, OF=0 (high half is zero)
        MOV  CL, 7          ; CL = 0x07
        DIV  CL             ; AL = 100 / 7 = 14 (0x0E)
                             ; AH = 100 MOD 7 = 2 (0x02)
```

**Bit Pattern Verification:**
- $25 \times 4 = 100 = 64H$ → fits in 8 bits, so CF=0 and OF=0. ✓
- $100 \div 7 = 14$ remainder $2$. ✓

## 3.6 Comprehensive Flag-Affecting Matrix (Analytical Table)

| Instruction | OF | SF | ZF | AF | PF | CF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ADD/ADC | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SUB/SBB/CMP | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| INC | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| DEC | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| MUL/IMUL | ✓ | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| DIV/IDIV | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| AND/OR/XOR/TEST | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0 |
| NOT/NEG | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| SHL/SHR/SAL/SAR | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | ✓ |
| ROL/ROR/RCL/RCR | ✓ | — | — | — | — | ✓ |
| MOV/XCHG/CBW/CWD | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| String ops (MOVS etc.) | — | — | ✓ | — | — | — |
| CLD/STD | — | — | — | — | — | DF only |

✓ = affected, — = unchanged/undefined, 0 = always cleared.

## 3.7 End-to-End Engineering Problem: ASCII to Upper-Case Conversion

**Problem:** Convert a 20-character lowercase string to uppercase using byte manipulation.

**Logic:** In ASCII, `'a' = 0x61`, `'A' = 0x41` → bit pattern difference is `0x20 = 0010\,0000B`. Clearing bit 5 converts lowercase to uppercase.

```assembly
        CLD
        LEA  SI, STRING     ; Source
        LEA  DI, STRING     ; Same location (in-place)
        MOV  CX, 20
AGAIN:  LODSB               ; AL ← [SI], SI++
        AND  AL, 0DFH       ; Clear bit 5 (mask 1101 1111)
        STOSB               ; [DI] ← AL, DI++
        LOOP AGAIN
```

**Bit Pattern Demonstration:** Character 'k' = `0110\,1011`. After `AND 0xDF`:
- `0x6B = 0110\,1011`
- `0xDF = 1101\,1111`
- Result: `0100\,1011 = 0x4B = 'K'` ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: 8086 String Operation Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the micro-operational flow when a `REP MOVSB` instruction is executed. The BIU fetches the opcode, the EU interprets the REP prefix, and the internal pointer registers (`SI`, `DI`, `CX`, `DF`) drive the iteration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch: REP MOVSB opcode] --> B[Initialize CX from instruction context]
    B --> C{CX equals 0?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Exit: No operation performed]
    C -- No --> D[BIU reads byte at DS:SI]
    D --> E[BIU writes byte to ES:DI]
    E --> F{DF flag state?}
    F -- DF equals 0 --> G[Increment SI by 1]
    F -- DF equals 1 --> H[Decrement SI by 1]
    G --> I[Increment DI by 1]
    H --> J[Decrement DI by 1]
    I --> K[Decrement CX by 1]
    J --> K
    K --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each "Iteration" loop reads **one byte**, writes **one byte**, updates **two pointer registers** (SI and DI), and decrements **CX**. The `CLD`/`STD` instruction before `REP MOVSB` decides whether the pointers move forward (low-to-high address) or backward (high-to-low address).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of String Instruction Categories

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StringPrimitiveOps[String Primitive Instructions]
        opA[MOVS: Memory to Memory Copy]
        opB[CMPS: Memory to Memory Compare]
        opC[SCAS: Register to Memory Compare]
        opD[LODS: Memory to Register Load]
        opE[STOS: Register to Memory Store]
    end
    subgraph REPPrefixFamily[REP Prefix Family]
        rep1[REP: Unconditional Loop]
        rep2[REPE or REPZ: Loop if ZF equals 1]
        rep3[REPNE or REPNZ: Loop if ZF equals 0]
    end
    subgraph ControlRegisters[Control and Pointer Registers]
        reg1[SI: Source Index]
        reg2[DI: Destination Index]
        reg3[CX: Counter]
        reg4[DF: Direction Flag]
        reg5[AL or AX: Accumulator]
    end
    opA --- reg1
    opA --- reg2
    opB --- reg1
    opB --- reg2
    opC --- reg2
    opC --- reg5
    opD --- reg1
    opD --- reg5
    opE --- reg2
    opE --- reg5
    rep1 --- reg3
    rep2 --- reg3
    rep3 --- reg3
    rep1 --- reg4
    rep2 --- reg4
    rep3 --- reg4
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct REP Prefix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Need to repeat a string instruction] --> q1{What is the operation?}
    q1 -- Copy or Store --> repUse[Use REP]
    q1 -- Compare or Scan --> q2{What is the stopping condition?}
    q2 -- Stop at first MATCH --> repE[Use REPE or REPZ]
    q2 -- Stop at first MISMATCH --> repNE[Use REPNE or REPNZ]
    repUse --> final[Initialize CX, SI, DI, DF, then execute]
    repE --> final
    repNE --> final
```

## 4.4 Logical Flow: Signed Multiplication Overflow Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a[IMUL src: Signed Multiply] --> b{Is the source a byte?}
    b -- Yes --> c[AX equals AL times src as signed]
    b -- No --> d[DX:AX equals AX times src as signed]
    c --> e{Is result sign-extended}
    d --> f{Is the result sign-extended}
    e -- Yes, AH equals sign of AL --> g[OF equals 0, CF equals 0]
    e -- No, sign extension fails --> h[OF equals 1, CF equals 1]
    f -- Yes --> g
    f -- No --> h
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For `MUL`/`IMUL`, the `CF` and `OF` flags are **both set to 1** if the upper half of the result is non-zero, and **both cleared to 0** if the upper half is zero. The upper half is `AH` for byte operands and `DX` for word operands.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the difference between `MOV` and `LEA` instructions in 8086 with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

`MOV` is a **data transfer instruction** that copies the **content** of the source operand into the destination. `LEA` (Load Effective Address) is an **address calculation instruction** that loads the **offset (effective address)** of a memory operand into a register, without performing any memory access.

| Aspect | `MOV AX, BX` | `LEA AX, BX` |
|---|---|---|
| Operation | Copies value of BX into AX | Loads address of BX into AX (illegal in 8086) |
| Memory access | Yes (if mem operand) | No (computes offset) |
| Typical use | Data movement | Offset calculation |

**Practical Example:**
```assembly
MYDATA DB 10H, 20H, 30H
        ...
        LEA  SI, MYDATA     ; SI = offset of MYDATA (e.g., 0200H), NOT 10H
        MOV  AL, [SI]       ; AL = 10H (the actual byte value)
```

`MOV SI, MYDATA` would attempt to load the **value** at address MYDATA into SI, which is an immediate-to-register move and is **not legal** for a memory source — `LEA` is the correct choice.

**Word count justification:** The 8086 has a `MOV reg, mem` form, but it copies the 16-bit value at the memory location. `LEA` always returns the **offset** of the label.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the Direction Flag (DF) in 8086 string operations? How is it set or cleared?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Direction Flag (DF)** is bit 10 of the **FLAGS register** in 8086. It controls the **auto-update direction** of the index registers `SI` and `DI` during string primitive instructions (`MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `LODS`, `STOS`).

- **DF = 0** → `SI` and `DI` are **auto-incremented** (low-to-high address traversal). Set by the `CLD` instruction (opcode `FC`).
- **DF = 1** → `SI` and `DI` are **auto-decremented** (high-to-low address traversal). Set by the `STD` instruction (opcode `FD`).

The increment value is **1** for byte instructions and **2** for word instructions.

**Default Convention:** Most software uses `CLD` (forward direction) because strings are conventionally stored from low to high memory addresses. The 8086 does **not** initialize DF on reset — it remains at its last set value, so explicit `CLD` is good practice before any string operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **part (a) = 7 marks** and **part (b) = 7 marks**. The student answers **one** of the two alternatives. Full model solutions are given for each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different logical instructions available in 8086 with suitable examples. Also explain the effect of these instructions on the status flags. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to **find the number of occurrences of a particular character** in a given string of 50 characters. Use the `SCASB` instruction. Explain the algorithm step by step. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

The 8086 provides **five logical instructions** operating at the bit level:

**1. AND (Logical AND)**
- **Syntax:** `AND dest, src`
- **Operation:** Performs bitwise AND; result stored in `dest`.
- **Use:** Masking (clearing) specific bits. `AND AL, 0FH` isolates the lower nibble.
- **Example:** `AL = 0xF0` and `BL = 0x0F` → `AND AL, BL` → `AL = 0x00`.

**2. OR (Logical OR)**
- **Syntax:** `OR dest, src`
- **Operation:** Bitwise OR.
- **Use:** Setting specific bits. `OR AL, 01H` forces the LSB to 1.
- **Example:** `AL = 0x30` and `BL = 0x0F` → `OR AL, BL` → `AL = 0x3F`.

**3. XOR (Logical Exclusive-OR)**
- **Syntax:** `XOR dest, src`
- **Operation:** Bitwise XOR.
- **Use:** Clearing a register (`XOR AX, AX` is the canonical idiom to set AX = 0 efficiently), toggling bits.
- **Example:** `AL = 0xFF`, `XOR AL, AL` → `AL = 0x00`.

**4. NOT (Logical NOT / One's Complement)**
- **Syntax:** `NOT dest`
- **Operation:** Inverts every bit.
- **Example:** `AL = 0x0F` → `NOT AL` → `AL = 0xF0`.

**5. TEST (Logical Compare)**
- **Syntax:** `TEST dest, src`
- **Operation:** Computes `dest AND src` and **discards** the result, but updates **flags**.
- **Use:** Testing specific bits without changing the operand. `TEST AL, 01H` checks if LSB is set.
- **Example:** After `TEST AL, 0x80`, **ZF=1** means bit 7 was 0; **ZF=0** means bit 7 was 1.

**Effect on Flags:**

| Instruction | OF | SF | ZF | AF | PF | CF |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AND | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0 |
| OR  | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0 |
| XOR | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0 |
| NOT | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| TEST | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | — | ✓ | 0 |

OF and CF are **always cleared** by AND, OR, XOR, and TEST. NOT affects **no flags**.

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Algorithm (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Initialize** ES = DS = data segment.
2. **CLD** to set forward direction.
3. **LEA DI, STRING** to point DI at the string.
4. **MOV AL, CHAR** to load the search character into AL.
5. **MOV CX, 50** to set the maximum scan length.
6. **MOV BL, 0** to initialize the occurrence counter.
7. **REPNE SCASB** — scans while AL ≠ [DI]; stops on match OR when CX = 0.
8. After each SCASB, **JCXZ NOT_FOUND** to check if CX reached 0.
9. If match found (ZF=1), **INC BL** and **DEC DI** to re-examine the same byte next iteration.
10. After loop, store BL in `COUNT` and terminate.

**Complete Program:**

```assembly
DATA    SEGMENT
STRING  DB  'MICROPROCESSOR ENGINEERING IS FUN'
COUNT   DB  ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA, ES:DATA
START:  MOV  AX, DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  ES, AX
        CLD
        LEA  DI, STRING
        MOV  AL, 'E'         ; Search for letter 'E'
        MOV  CX, 30          ; String length
        MOV  BL, 0           ; Counter = 0
AGAIN:  REPNE SCASB
        JCXZ DONE            ; If CX = 0, no more matches
        INC  BL              ; One more occurrence
        JMP  AGAIN
DONE:   MOV  COUNT, BL
        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

**Why DEC DI is not needed here:** After a match, `SCASB` already advances DI by 1. To keep scanning from the same position, we decrement DI back. In the program above, the simpler approach of just incrementing BL and continuing naturally works because `REPNE` resumes from the new DI position. Some textbooks insert `DEC DI` to re-examine the matched character if overlapping matches are possible (rare in ASCII text).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Model Paper 2]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of the `REP MOVSB` instruction. Show how the SI, DI, and CX registers are updated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly program using string instructions to **compare two strings of length 20 bytes** and determine whether they are equal or not. Store result 'Y' in `MATCH` if equal, else 'N'. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution:

`REP MOVSB` is a string primitive combined with a repeat prefix. It performs **byte-by-byte memory-to-memory copy** from `[DS:SI]` to `[ES:DI]`, auto-updating `SI`, `DI`, and `CX`.

**Step-by-Step Operation (Flow Diagram in text form):**

1. **Initial Setup (Programmer's responsibility):**
   - `DS:SI` = source address
   - `ES:DI` = destination address
   - `CX` = number of bytes
   - `DF` = 0 (CLD) for forward direction

2. **Pre-check:** If `CX = 0`, no operation occurs and execution proceeds to the next instruction.

3. **Main Iteration Body (executed once per byte):**
   - **Step A:** BIU reads byte at `DS:SI`.
   - **Step B:** BIU writes that byte to `ES:DI`.
   - **Step C:** If `DF = 0`, then `SI ← SI + 1` and `DI ← DI + 1`. If `DF = 1`, both are decremented.
   - **Step D:** `CX ← CX − 1`.

4. **Loop Control:** Go back to **Pre-check**. Exit when `CX = 0`.

**Register Update Table (DF = 0, copying 5 bytes from 0200H to 0300H):**

| Step | CX (before) | SI (before) | DI (before) | [SI] | [DI] after write | SI after | DI after | CX after |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 0200H | 0300H | 0x11 | 0x11 | 0201H | 0301H | 4 |
| 2 | 4 | 0201H | 0301H | 0x22 | 0x22 | 0202H | 0302H | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 0202H | 0302H | 0x33 | 0x33 | 0203H | 0303H | 2 |
| 4 | 2 | 0203H | 0303H | 0x44 | 0x44 | 0204H | 0304H | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 0204H | 0304H | 0x55 | 0x55 | 0205H | 0305H | 0 |

**Hardware Note:** The 8086 BIU contains a dedicated **string handling unit** that performs steps A–D in a single microcoded loop, far faster than a software loop using `LODSB` and `STOSB`.

#### Part (b) Model Solution:

**Algorithm:**
1. Load DS and ES with the same data segment.
2. `CLD` for forward direction.
3. `LEA SI, STR1` and `LEA DI, STR2`.
4. `MOV CX, 20` — string length.
5. `REPE CMPSB` — compare byte by byte; stop on first mismatch or when CX=0.
6. After loop, **check ZF**: if `ZF = 1`, strings are equal.
7. Store 'Y' or 'N' in `MATCH` accordingly.

**Complete Program:**

```assembly
DATA    SEGMENT
STR1    DB  'HELLO WORLD 12345  '
STR2    DB  'HELLO WORLD 12345  '
MATCH   DB  ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA, ES:DATA
START:  MOV  AX, DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  ES, AX
        CLD
        LEA  SI, STR1
        LEA  DI, STR2
        MOV  CX, 20
        REPE CMPSB
        JNE  NOTEQUAL
        MOV  BYTE PTR MATCH, 'Y'
        JMP  DONE
NOTEQUAL:
        MOV  BYTE PTR MATCH, 'N'
DONE:   MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

**Explanation of Critical Instructions:**

- `REPE CMPSB` performs `[SI] − [DI]`, sets flags, and increments both SI and DI. It repeats **as long as ZF=1** (matching bytes) and CX>0.
- On the **first mismatch**, ZF becomes 0 and the loop exits early — saving time. This is why `REPE` is the correct choice for "find first difference" comparison.
- If the loop completes with `CX = 0` AND `ZF = 1`, the strings are byte-for-byte identical.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to initialize **both DS and ES** — MOVS/CMPS/SCAS/LODS/STOS all use ES for the destination, and an uninitialized ES causes wild memory writes and program crashes.
> 2. Forgetting `CLD` — if DF=1 (from a previous `STD`), the pointers move backwards and the program behaves incorrectly.
> 3. Confusing `REPE` and `REPNE` — for **comparison**, use `REPE` (continue if equal); for **searching**, use `REPNE` (continue if not equal).
> 4. Forgetting to save DI's match position — `REPNE SCASB` leaves DI pointing **past** the match; use `DEC DI` to back up.
> 5. Using `MOV AX, MYDATA` instead of `LEA AX, MYDATA` — illegal in 8086, common 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam — Byte and String Manipulation**

### Byte Manipulation Essentials
- ✅ `MOV` copies data; **never** memory-to-memory, never immediate-to-segment-register.
- ✅ `MOVZX` zero-extends; `MOVSX` sign-extends.
- ✅ `MUL`/`DIV` are **unsigned**; `IMUL`/`IDIV` are **signed** — both use the same opcode form, semantics differ in interpretation.
- ✅ `AND` masks bits to 0; `OR` sets bits to 1; `XOR` toggles bits; `NOT` inverts; `TEST` is non-destructive AND.
- ✅ `SHL`/`SAL` = unsigned multiply by 2ⁿ; `SHR` = unsigned divide by 2ⁿ; `SAR` = signed divide by 2ⁿ (preserves sign).
- ✅ `ROL`/`ROR` rotate within the operand; `RCL`/`RCR` rotate through **CF** (17-bit rotation for 16-bit operand).
- ✅ `NEG X` ≡ `NOT X` followed by `ADD X, 1` (two's complement identity).
- ✅ `CBW` extends AL's sign into AH; `CWD` extends AX's sign into DX — both are flag-safe.

### String Manipulation Essentials
- ✅ Five primitives: `MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `LODS`, `STOS` — each has **B** (byte) and **W** (word) variant.
- ✅ Source always at `DS:SI`; destination always at `ES:DI` (with `LODS` and `STOS` being one-sided).
- ✅ `CLD` → DF=0 → auto-increment; `STD` → DF=1 → auto-decrement.
- ✅ Three REP prefixes: `REP` (unconditional), `REPE`/`REPZ` (loop if equal), `REPNE`/`REPNZ` (loop if not equal).
- ✅ `CX` holds the iteration count; decremented automatically each repetition.
- ✅ After `REPNE SCASB`, **DI points past the match** — use `DEC DI` to get the match address.
- ✅ For overlapping memory copies, use `STD` (backward direction) to avoid corruption.

### Flag-Quick-Reference
- ✅ `CF`: set by carry/borrow in arithmetic; cleared by `SHL` if last shift-out was 0.
- ✅ `ZF`: set if result is zero; `REPE`/`REPNE` depend on it.
- ✅ `SF`: sign flag = MSB of result.
- ✅ `OF`: signed overflow; cleared by `AND`/`OR`/`XOR`.
- ✅ `DF`: direction flag; only affected by `CLD`/`STD`.

### High-Yield Numerical Facts
- ✅ Byte signed range: **−128 to +127**; unsigned: **0 to 255**.
- ✅ Word signed range: **−32,768 to +32,767**; unsigned: **0 to 65,535**.
- ✅ 8086 has **14 × 16-bit registers**: 4 data (AX, BX, CX, DX), 4 segment (CS, DS, SS, ES), 4 pointer/index (SP, BP, SI, DI), 2 control (IP, FLAGS).
- ✅ `MUL` byte: result in `AX`; high half in `AH`; **CF=OF=1** if `AH ≠ 0`.
- ✅ `REP` family internally uses the BIU's dedicated string unit — a single `REP MOVSB` is faster than a software loop of `LODSB`/`STOSB`.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exam
- ❌ Using `MOV` between two memory operands.
- ❌ Forgetting `CLD` before `REP` instructions.
- ❌ Confusing `REPE` (continue on equal) with `REPNE` (continue on not-equal).
- ❌ Not setting `ES` equal to `DS` (mandatory for `MOVS`/`CMPS`/`SCAS`/`STOS`).
- ❌ Reading `SCASB` result from `AL` (the scan compares `AL` with `[DI]`; the result is in flags, not a register).
- ❌ Writing `|x|` in markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead (compiler pitfall).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8086 System Bus Structure: 8086 Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8086 System Bus Structure: 8086 Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8086 microprocessor** is a **16-bit** HMOS device manufactured by Intel, packaged in a **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)**. It features a **16-bit internal data bus** and a **20-bit external address bus**, giving it a physical memory addressing capability of **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes (1 MB)**. The 8086 is architecturally divided into two independent processing units: the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** and the **Execution Unit (EU)**, a hallmark feature that enables **pipelined instruction prefetching** via a **6-byte instruction queue**.

The **8086 System Bus Structure** refers to the set of physical pins and signal lines that connect the CPU to external memory, I/O devices, and co-processors. The 8086 uses a **multiplexed address/data bus** to fit 20 address bits, 16 data bits, and numerous control/status signals into a 40-pin package, requiring external demultiplexing (typically using the **8282/8283 address latch** and **8286/8287 bus transceiver**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must master pin-by-pin signal functions in **both Minimum Mode (MN/MX' = 1) and Maximum Mode (MN/MX' = 0)** configurations, since the 24th pin's logic level re-defines the function of pins 24–31.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Model

Imagine the 8086 as a **central post office sorting room** that processes 1 million P.O. boxes (memory locations).

- The **20-bit address bus** is the **mail carrier's route list** — it must carry the unique 20-bit house number to find any of the 1 MB boxes.
- The **16-bit data bus** is the **mailbag** that carries the actual letter (data) back and forth.
- The **multiplexing** is like a courier who **shouts the house number AND carries the parcel on the same bicycle** at different times — first announcing "I am at box number X!" then "Here is your parcel!". A helper (the 8282 latch) **remembers** the address while the parcel is being delivered.
- **Control signals** (RD', WR', M/IO') are the **paperwork stamps** that tell the system: "Is this a read or a write? Is it a mailbox (memory) or a post-office counter (I/O)?"
- **Status signals** (S2', S1', S0') are the **flags the courier waves** so the supervisor (8288 Bus Controller in Max Mode) knows what kind of operation is happening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 40 pins for so many signals?** With 20 address + 16 data + ~10 control signals, the 8086 needs at least 46 pins. Intel solved this by **time-multiplexing** the lower 16 address lines with the data bus (AD15–AD0), saving 16 pins and reducing the package to a manageable 40 pins.

## 1.3 The Two Operating Modes

The 8086 has a single dedicated mode-select pin:

- **MN/MX' = 1 (High, +5V)** → **Minimum Mode** (single processor, like a stand-alone post office)
- **MN/MX' = 0 (Low, GND)** → **Maximum Mode** (multi-processor with 8288 Bus Controller, like a regional sorting hub)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pin 24 is **MN/MX'** and pin **24 is the ONLY mode-select pin**. Pins **24 through 31 change meaning** based on this mode. This is the single most-tested concept in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pin Groupings — The 40 Pins Categorized

The 40 pins of the 8086 are organized into **seven functional groups**:

| Group | Pin Count | Pin Names | Function |
|:------|:---------:|:----------|:---------|
| Power Supply | 2 | Vcc (Pin 40), GND (Pin 1, 20) | +5V power and ground |
| Address/Data Bus | 16 | AD15–AD0 (Pins 39–34, 32–28, 21, 22, 15–12) | Time-multiplexed address (T1) / data (T2–T4) |
| Address/Status Bus | 4 | A19/S6–A16/S3 (Pins 35–38) | Multiplexed address (T1) / status (T2–T4) |
| Bus High Enable | 1 | BHE'/S7 (Pin 34) | High-byte enable (T1) / status S7 (T2–T4) |
| Control Bus (Common) | 8 | RD', READY, TEST', INTR, NMI, RESET, CLK, M/IO' | Read, wait-state, test, interrupts, clock |
| Control Bus (Min Mode) | 8 | WR', INTA', ALE, DEN', DT/R', HOLD, HLDA, MN/MX' | Write, int-ack, latch-enable, data-enable, DMA |
| Control Bus (Max Mode) | 8 | WR', INTA', ALE, DEN', DT/R', LOCK', QS1, QS0, RQ'/GT0', RQ'/GT1' | Replaced HOLD/HLDA with LOCK', queue status, request/grant |

## 2.2 The Multiplexed Bus Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why multiplexing?** With 40 pins, Intel had no room for 20 address + 16 data = 36 bus pins. So:

- **T1 (Address Phase):** AD15–AD0 carry the **lower 16 bits of the 20-bit address**; A19/S6–A16/S3 carry the **upper 4 bits of the 20-bit address**.
- **T2–T4 (Data Phase):** AD15–AD0 carry the **16-bit data**; A19/S6–A16/S3 carry **status bits S6–S3**.

A **D-type latch (8282)** triggered by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal freezes the T1 address into its outputs, freeing AD15–AD0 to be reused for data in T2–T4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Status Bit Significance (KTU favourite):**
> - **S5** = Interrupt Enable Flag (IF) — reflects the current state of the IF flag.
> - **S6** = Always **0** on 8086 (reserved; 0 on 8088, 1 on 8086 actually for S6).
> - **S3, S4** = Segment register used by the current bus cycle (00=ES, 01=SS, 10=CS, 11=DS).

## 2.3 Status Signal Encoding (S2', S1', S0')

In **Maximum Mode**, the 8086 outputs three status signals that the external **8288 Bus Controller** decodes to generate the actual memory/I/O read/write strobes.

| S2' | S1' | S0' | Cycle Type | 8288 Command Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:-----------|:--------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Interrupt Acknowledge | INTA' |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | I/O Read | IORC' |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Write | IOWC' |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Halt (8086 only) | None (passive) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Opcode Fetch | MRDC' |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Memory Read | MRDC' |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Write | MWTC' |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Passive (no bus cycle) | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The status signals are **active LOW** and are valid from T2 to T4. The 8288 uses an internal **status decoder + timing generator** to produce 8 command outputs: IORC', IOWC', MRDC', MWTC', AMWC', AIOWC', INTA', and MCE/PDEN.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Encoding Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Encoding | Units / Notes |
|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------|
| Physical Memory | $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ | **1 MB** addressable space |
| I/O Address Space | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | **64 KB** separate I/O space |
| Physical Address Calculation | $PA = (Segment \times 16) + Offset$ | Segment shifted left 4 bits, then add 16-bit offset |
| Data Bus Width | **16 bits** | Lower byte D7–D0, Upper byte D15–D8 |
| Addressing Modes | Register, Immediate, Direct, Indirect, Based, Indexed, Based-Indexed | Total **17** addressing modes |
| Operating Frequency | **5 MHz** (8086), **8 MHz** (8086-2) | Clock period T = **200 ns** minimum |
| Bus Cycle States | T1, T2, T3, T4, plus Tw (wait) | Each state = **2 clock cycles** (2T) in original 8086 |
| Pipe-line Stage | 6-byte instruction queue | BIU prefetches while EU executes |
| Interrupt Vector | Type $\times 4$ | Each vector occupies 4 bytes in IVT (256 types × 4 = 1024 = 1 KB) |
| Even Address Boundary | Lower byte on D7–D0, upper byte on D15–D8 | Saves **1 bus cycle** (4T → 4T, no penalty) |
| Odd Address Boundary | Lower byte on D15–D8 first, then upper byte on D7–D0 | **2 bus cycles** (8T, penalty of 4T) |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing BHE' and A0' combinations in tables, use `BHE' = 0, A0' = 0` etc. — never use the pipe symbol `\|` in table cells. Use LaTeX `\vert` if separation is needed: $A_0 = 0 \vert BHE' = 0$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8086 bus structure is the **ancestor of every modern x86 CPU**. The techniques introduced here — multiplexed buses, address latching, bus transceivers, and bus controllers — directly evolved into:
- **ISA Bus** (IBM PC/AT, 1984) — used the 80286 with 24-bit address and 16-bit data.
- **PCI Bus** — added bus mastering and arbitration concepts from 8086's HOLD/HLDA.
- **PCI Express** — modern serial descendant using the same DMA and interrupt primitives.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address from Segment:Offset Pair

The 8086 stores a 20-bit physical address in two 16-bit registers: a **segment** and an **offset**. The BIU computes the physical address as follows.

### Step 1: Identify the Registers
The segment register holds the upper 16 bits of the base address of a 64 KB logical segment. The offset is a 16-bit displacement into that segment.

### Step 2: Left-Shift the Segment
Shift the segment register's contents left by **4 bit positions** (i.e., multiply by $2^4 = 16$).

### Step 3: Add the Offset
Add the 16-bit offset to the shifted segment value.

### Step 4: Truncate to 20 Bits
Discard any carry beyond bit 19 (wrap-around at 1 MB boundary).

**Example Computation:** Let CS = `0x1000` and IP = `0x0450`. Find the physical address of the next instruction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment} &= 0x1000 \\
\text{Shift Left by 4} &= 0x1000 \ll 4 = 0x10000 \\
\text{Offset} &= 0x0450 \\
\text{Physical Address} &= 0x10000 + 0x0450 = 0x10450
\end{aligned}
$$

In decimal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
4096 \times 16 + 1104 &= 65536 + 1104 \\
&= 66640 \\
&= 0x10450
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each segment is aligned to a **16-byte (paragraph) boundary** because the lowest 4 bits of the segment are always 0 after shifting. This is the origin of the "paragraph" terminology in x86 assembly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Odd vs Even Address Bus Cycles

The 8086 can fetch a 16-bit word in either **one bus cycle (4T)** or **two bus cycles (8T)** depending on alignment.

### Case A: Even-Aligned Word Access

If the address $A_0 = 0$ (even), the lower byte sits on D7–D0 and the upper byte sits on D15–D8. Both are accessed simultaneously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus cycles} &= 1 \\
\text{Time} &= 4T \\
\text{BHE' line} &= 0, \quad A_0 = 0 \\
\text{Result} &= \text{Single 16-bit transfer}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Case B: Odd-Aligned Word Access

If the address $A_0 = 1$ (odd), the lower byte sits on D15–D8 (the high byte lane), requiring the CPU to:
1. First, enable the high byte lane ($\overline{BHE} = 0$, $A_0 = 1$) to read the **lower** byte from the high lane.
2. Increment the address, then enable the low byte lane ($A_0 = 0$) to read the **upper** byte from the low lane.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus cycles} &= 2 \\
\text{Time} &= 8T \\
\text{Penalty} &= 4T = 800 \text{ ns @ 5 MHz} \\
\text{Result} &= \text{Two 8-bit transfers, swapped}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Performance Tip for KTU:** Aligned accesses (even addresses) are 2× faster. Compilers insert padding to keep stack-allocated data even-aligned, and the `EVEN` directive in MASM forces alignment.

## 3.3 BHE' and A0' Selection Matrix

The 8086 uses **BHE' (Bus High Enable)** together with **A0** to select which byte lane(s) are active:

| $\overline{BHE}$ | $A_0$ | Selected Lanes | Operation | Address Type |
|:---------------:|:-----:|:---------------|:----------|:-------------|
| 0 | 0 | D15–D0 | Word (16-bit) transfer | Even-aligned word |
| 0 | 1 | D15–D8 | High byte to/from odd address | Odd byte access |
| 1 | 0 | D7–D0 | Low byte to/from even address | Even byte access |
| 1 | 1 | None | Idle / no transfer | — |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Selected Byte} &= (\overline{BHE'} = 0 \text{ AND } A_0 = 0) \rightarrow \text{Both bytes} \\
&\lor (\overline{BHE'} = 0 \text{ AND } A_0 = 1) \rightarrow \text{Upper byte only} \\
&\lor (\overline{BHE'} = 1 \text{ AND } A_0 = 0) \rightarrow \text{Lower byte only} \\
&\lor (\overline{BHE'} = 1 \text{ AND } A_0 = 1) \rightarrow \text{No selection}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Bus Cycle Timing Derivation

A standard 8086 bus cycle consists of **4 clock states (T1–T4)**, each of duration **2 internal clock periods (2Tclk)**. The 8086's internal clock generator divides the external crystal by 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 4 \times T_{state} \\
T_{state} &= 2 \times T_{clk} \\
T_{cycle} &= 4 \times 2 \times T_{clk} = 8 \times T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **5 MHz crystal** ($T_{clk} = 200$ ns):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 8 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 1600 \text{ ns} = 1.6 \text{ μs} \\
\text{Bus bandwidth} &= \frac{16 \text{ bits}}{1.6 \text{ μs}} = 10 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **READY** input inserts **Tw (wait) states** of one Tclk each, allowing slow memory/IO devices to extend the bus cycle. The number of wait states is unlimited; each adds 200 ns.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Explain the function of the BHE' pin in the 8086 microprocessor. How is it used along with A0 to select different byte lanes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **BHE' (Bus High Enable)** is an **active-low** output signal of the 8086 used in conjunction with the **A0** address line to select the appropriate byte lane of the 16-bit data bus.

**(1 mark) — BHE' Function:**
BHE' = 0 enables the **upper byte lane D15–D8** during a read/write operation. It is valid only during the **T1 state** (address phase) when the address is multiplexed onto the bus, and must be latched externally along with A0.

**(1 mark) — A0 Function:**
A0 = 0 selects the **lower byte lane D7–D0**, A0 = 1 indicates an odd address.

**(1 mark) — Selection Matrix (must be memorized):**
- BHE' = 0, A0 = 0 → **Word transfer** (both bytes, even-aligned).
- BHE' = 0, A0 = 1 → **High byte** (odd address byte).
- BHE' = 1, A0 = 0 → **Low byte** (even address byte).
- BHE' = 1, A0 = 1 → **No selection** (idle).

> [!TIP]
> The BHE' signal is necessary because the 8086 can address individual bytes within a 16-bit word, and the memory bank must know which byte lane to activate. Without BHE', the 8086 would need 24 address lines (3 × 8 banks) to control 3 banks separately.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**What is the difference between Minimum Mode and Maximum Mode operation of 8086? When is each preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(1 mark) — Definition:**
The 8086 has a single mode-select pin **MN/MX' (Pin 24)**.
- **Minimum Mode:** MN/MX' = 1 (tied to +5V). The CPU generates control signals directly.
- **Maximum Mode:** MN/MX' = 0 (tied to GND). An external **8288 Bus Controller** generates control signals based on status S2'–S0'.

**(1 mark) — Key Difference:**
In **Minimum Mode**, pins 24–31 function as **HOLD, HLDA, WR', M/IO', DT/R', DEN', ALE, INTA'**.
In **Maximum Mode**, the same pins become **RQ'/GT0', RQ'/GT1', LOCK', S2', S1', S0', QS0, QS1**.

**(1 mark) — Preference:**
- **Minimum Mode** is preferred for **single-CPU embedded systems** where simplicity and lower cost matter.
- **Maximum Mode** is preferred for **multi-processor systems** (e.g., 8086 + 8087 NDP) requiring **bus arbitration** and **co-processor synchronization**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Draw the pin diagram of 8086 and explain the function of any **10 signals** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the 8086 **minimum mode system configuration** with a neat block diagram, showing the 8282 latch, 8286 transceiver, and 8284A clock generator. Justify why external demultiplexing is necessary. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Pin Diagram and Signal Functions (7 marks)**

The 8086 has 40 pins arranged in a DIP package. Below are 10 important signals:

**1. AD15–AD0 (Pins 39–34, 32–28, 21, 22, 15–12):** Address/Data bus. Multiplexed. During T1, they carry the lower 16-bit address; during T2–T4 they carry the 16-bit data.

**2. A19/S6–A16/S3 (Pins 35–38):** Address/Status bus. During T1, they carry the upper 4 address bits; during T2–T4 they carry status bits S3–S6 (S3–S4 = segment register, S5 = IF flag, S6 = 0).

**3. BHE'/S7 (Pin 34):** Bus High Enable in T1 (active low) enables the D15–D8 byte lane; in T2–T4 it carries status S7 (always 1 in 8086).

**4. M/IO' (Pin 25):** Distinguishes memory (HIGH) from I/O (LOW) access. Valid throughout the bus cycle.

**5. RD' (Pin 26):** Active low read strobe. Indicates that the selected memory or I/O device must place data on the bus.

**6. WR' (Pin 29 in Min Mode):** Active low write strobe. Indicates the CPU is sending data to the selected device.

**7. ALE (Pin 39 in Min Mode):** Address Latch Enable. HIGH pulse in T1; falling edge tells the 8282 latch to capture the address.

**8. DEN' (Pin 32 in Min Mode):** Data Enable. Active low in T2–T4 to enable the 8286 transceiver for data transfer.

**9. DT/R' (Pin 33 in Min Mode):** Data Transmit/Receive. HIGH = CPU sending data, LOW = CPU receiving data.

**10. INTR (Pin 18):** Maskable Interrupt Request. Sampled at the end of each instruction; acknowledged if IF = 1. Vectored via INTA' bus cycle.

**(1 mark for each signal × 10 = 6 marks + 1 mark for diagram structure)**

**Part (b) — Minimum Mode System Configuration (7 marks)**

The minimum mode system requires three support chips to demultiplex and buffer the bus.

**Block Diagram (Functional Description):**
- **8084A Clock Generator** → provides CLK, RESET, and READY synchronization from a 15 MHz crystal (internal divide-by-3 gives 5 MHz).
- **Three 8282 Octal Latches** → demultiplex AD15–AD0 (×2) and A19–A16 (×1). Each latch has **STB (Strobe)** input connected to ALE.
- **Two 8286 Octal Bus Transceivers** → buffer the data bus; T-pin connected to DT/R', OE' connected to DEN'.

**Why External Demultiplexing? (2 marks)**
The 8086 reduces 40 pins by **time-multiplexing** the lower 16 address lines with the 16 data lines. This means during T1, the bus carries the address, but during T2–T4, the address lines must hold their values **stable** for memory while the same physical pins now carry data. Since the CPU cannot drive the address on one set of pins and data on the same pins simultaneously, an **external latch** (8282) is mandatory to "freeze" the T1 address and present it to memory continuously. Without the latch, the memory chip would see the address only momentarily and then data, leading to incorrect reads.

**8286 Transceiver Role (1 mark):**
The 8286 provides **bidirectional buffering** so the data bus can be driven both by the CPU (write) and by external devices (read). DT/R' selects the direction, DEN' enables the output.

**(0.5 marks for signal interconnect, 0.5 marks for crystal divider explanation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **maximum mode configuration** of 8086. Describe the role of the **8288 Bus Controller** and the function of status signals S2', S1', S0'. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **HOLD, HLDA, and RQ'/GT'** signals. Compare how DMA and bus arbitration are handled in minimum versus maximum mode. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Maximum Mode and 8288 Bus Controller (7 marks)**

In maximum mode (MN/MX' = 0), the 8086 does **not** generate control signals like RD', WR', DEN', DT/R' directly. Instead, it outputs **three status signals S2', S1', S0'** that are decoded by the external **8288 Bus Controller** to produce all the necessary memory and I/O control strobes. **(1 mark)**

**Status Signal Encoding (3 marks — must show the full table):**

| S2' | S1' | S0' | Cycle Type |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:-----------|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Interrupt Acknowledge |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | I/O Read |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Write |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Halt |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Code Fetch |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Memory Read |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Write |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Passive |

**Role of 8288 (2 marks):**
- Decodes S2'–S0' to produce **MRDC'** (Memory Read), **MWTC'** (Memory Write), **IORC'** (I/O Read), **IOWC'** (I/O Write), **INTA'**.
- Generates **advanced** versions: **AMWC'** and **AIOWC'** (one T-state earlier than normal).
- Generates **ALE** and **DEN'** for latching and transceiver control.
- Includes a **bus arbiter** for multi-CPU systems.

**Why Maximum Mode is Used (1 mark):**
- Allows multiple 8086s to share a common bus (multiprocessing).
- Allows connection of co-processors (8087 NDP, 8089 IOP).
- Offloads control-signal generation, freeing the CPU from bus management.

**Part (b) — HOLD, HLDA, and RQ'/GT' (7 marks)**

**HOLD and HLDA in Minimum Mode (3 marks):**
- **HOLD** is an **input** to the CPU from an external device (e.g., DMA controller) requesting bus mastership.
- The CPU completes the current bus cycle, then floats (tri-states) its address, data, and control buses.
- The CPU then activates **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)** as an **output** to inform the requester that it has the bus.
- The DMA controller now drives the bus directly. When HOLD is de-asserted, the CPU resumes control.

**RQ'/GT' in Maximum Mode (2 marks):**
- The maximum mode has **two** bidirectional pins: **RQ'/GT0'** and **RQ'/GT1'**.
- RQ'/GT0' has **higher priority** than RQ'/GT1'.
- These are open-drain, active-low signals. A requester (other CPU or DMA) pulls the line LOW to request the bus; the CPU responds with a **GRANT pulse**; later the requester sends a **RELEASE pulse** to return control.
- This is more compact than HOLD/HLDA and supports two simultaneous requests.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Minimum Mode | Maximum Mode |
|:--------|:-------------|:-------------|
| Request mechanism | HOLD (input) | RQ'/GT0', RQ'/GT1' (bidirectional) |
| Acknowledge | HLDA (output) | Grant pulse on same line |
| Number of simultaneous requests | 1 (HOLD) | 2 (RQ0', RQ1') |
| External logic required | None | 8288 bus controller, optional 8289 arbiter |
| Use case | Simple embedded | Multi-CPU, DMA, co-processor systems |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify Min/Max mode for dual-function pins (24–31).** A common 2-mark loss. Always state "In **Minimum Mode**, pin 27 functions as HOLD; in **Maximum Mode**, it becomes RQ'/GT0'."
> 2. **Confusing RD' and WR' polarity.** RD' and WR' are **active-LOW** strobes (the apostrophe indicates inversion). Writing "RD high to read" loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Status signal S6 value.** On the 8086, **S6 = 0** (always), but on 8088 it is held HIGH. Many students answer "S6 = 1" for 8086, losing a mark.
> 4. **ALE timing.** ALE is HIGH during T1 and falls at the T1–T2 boundary. Saying "ALE is HIGH during T2" loses marks.
> 5. **READY signal origin.** READY is an **input** to the CPU (from memory), not an output. Students often reverse this.
> 6. **Memory address calculation.** Forgetting that the **segment is shifted left by 4 bits (multiply by 16)**, not added directly to the offset. Writing `PA = Segment + Offset` is wrong.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 8086 System Bus & Signals Quick-Fire Points:**

- The 8086 is a **16-bit CPU** with a **20-bit address bus** in a **40-pin DIP** package, using **multiplexed address/data lines** to fit all signals.
- The **20-bit address bus** gives **1 MB (2²⁰ bytes)** of physical memory; **16-bit I/O address** gives **64 KB (2¹⁶)** of I/O space.
- The physical address formula is **PA = (Segment × 16) + Offset**, with wrap-around at 1 MB.
- The CPU is split into **BIU** (handles bus, has a 6-byte instruction queue) and **EU** (executes instructions), enabling **pipelining**.
- **Pin 24 (MN/MX')** determines mode: HIGH = Minimum, LOW = Maximum.
- **Minimum Mode pins 24–31:** HOLD, HLDA, WR', M/IO', DT/R', DEN', ALE, INTA'.
- **Maximum Mode pins 24–31:** RQ'/GT0', RQ'/GT1', LOCK', S2', S1', S0', QS0, QS1.
- **BHE' = 0 + A0 = 0** → word access; **BHE' = 0 + A0 = 1** → high byte; **BHE' = 1 + A0 = 0** → low byte.
- **ALE** latches the address at T1; **DEN'** enables the data transceiver at T2–T4; **DT/R'** controls transceiver direction.
- **Status bits S3–S4** indicate the segment register; **S5 = IF flag**; **S6 = 0** on 8086; **S7 = 1** (from BHE' line in T2–T4).
- **S2'–S0'** in Max Mode encode 8 bus cycle types, decoded by the **8288 Bus Controller**.
- **Standard bus cycle = 4T states × 2Tclk = 8Tclk**; each wait state (Tw) is **2Tclk = 200 ns** at 5 MHz.
- **READY** is an **input** sampled at T2; it inserts Tw states for slow memory.
- **TEST'** is an input sampled only by the **WAIT** instruction; it has no effect on memory or I/O.
- **HOLD/HLDA** (Min Mode) is for **DMA**; **RQ'/GT0', RQ'/GT1'** (Max Mode) is for **bus arbitration** with priority GT0' > GT1'.
- **INTR** is maskable (controlled by IF); **NMI** is non-maskable (Type 2, vector address `0008:0008`); **INTA'** is the two-pulse interrupt acknowledge bus cycle.
- The 8086 uses **support chips 8282 (latch), 8286 (transceiver), 8284A (clock), 8288 (bus controller)** — all are examinable in Min vs Max context.
- A **10-bit I/O address** (A0–A9) is decoded for 8/16-bit port access; **A0 = 0** selects even ports, **BHE' = 0 + A0 = 1** selects odd ports.
- The **6-byte instruction queue** prefetches when the EU is busy — this is why 8086 is faster than 8088 (which has a 4-byte queue and 8-bit external bus).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Basic Configurations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Basic Configurations of 8086** refer to the two standard hardware arrangement schemes (Minimum Mode and Maximum Mode) selected by strapping the **MN/MX̄** pin of the 8086 microprocessor to logic HIGH (1) or LOW (0). These configurations define how the CPU generates its **Control Bus** signals, how it arbitrates the system bus, and whether external bus controllers (like the 8288) are required. The 8086 microprocessor (a **16-bit** CPU with a **20-bit** address bus) can operate in two distinct hardware modes. Each mode generates a different set of control signals and supports a different system architecture—from a simple single-processor setup to a complex multi-processor environment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Two-Role Manager:** > Think of the 8086 CPU as a **busy manager of a large office building**. * In **Minimum Mode**, the manager is a **small business owner** who handles all tasks personally — answering calls, opening doors, and managing the small team directly.
