There is no co-manager, no secretary. * In **Maximum Mode**, the manager runs a **large corporate office** with a dedicated **Executive Secretary (the 8288 Bus Controller)**. The manager gives high-level commands, and the secretary translates them into specific, organized actions for the staff. The manager can even collaborate with other managers (other CPUs) without chaos. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standards > [!TIP] > * **Operating Frequency:** Standard **5 MHz** (8086), **8 MHz** (8086-2), **10 MHz** (8086-1). > * **Data Bus Width:** **16 bits** (bidirectional). > * **Address Bus Width:** **20 bits** (capable of addressing **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 = 1 MB** of memory). > * **I/O Address Space:** **16-bit** I/O address → **64 KB** I/O ports. > * **Mode Select Pin:** **MN/MX̄** (Pin 33) — HIGH for Minimum, LOW for Maximum. > * **Multiplexed Pins:** **AD₁₅–AD₀** and **A₁₉/S₆–A₁₆/S₃** (address and data share the same physical pins, time-division multiplexed — TDM). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 8086 Pin Classification The 8086 comes in a **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)**. To fit 20 address bits, 16 data bits, and numerous control signals into only 40 pins, Intel multiplexed the address and data buses. **Three categories of pins:** 1. **Common Pins (Exist in both modes)** — Power (Vcc, GND), Clock (CLK), Reset (RESET), Ready (READY), Test (TEST̄), NMI, INTR, INTĀ, HOLD, HLDA, AD₁₅–AD₀, A₁₉/S₆–A₁₆/S₃, BHĒ/S₇, RD̄, M/IŌ, WR̄, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄. 2. **Minimum Mode Pins (MN/MX̄ = 1)** — M/IŌ, WR̄, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, INTĀ, HOLD, HLDA. 3. **Maximum Mode Pins (MN/MX̄ = 0)** — S₂̄, S₁̄, S₀̄ (Status), RQ̄/GT₀̄, RQ̄/GT₁̄ (Request/Grant), LOCK̄, QS₁, QS₀ (Queue Status). ### 2.2 Minimum Mode Configuration (MN/MX̄ = 1) **Operational Steps:** * **Why used:** Small, single-CPU systems where the 8086 directly controls all bus operations. * **How it works:** The 8086 itself generates **all control signals** directly (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, INTĀ). * **Buses formed:** * **Address Bus (20 bits):** Demultiplexed from **AD₁₅–AD₀** + **A₁₉–A₁₆** using **3 × 74LS373** octal latches (latch controlled by ALE). * **Data Bus (16 bits):** Buffered using **2 × 74LS245** octal bidirectional transceivers (controlled by DEN̄ and DT/R̄). * **Control Bus:** Direct from 8086 pins (M/IŌ, RD̄, WR̄). * **Bus Arbitration:** Hardware-based using **HOLD** and **HLDA** pins for DMA (Direct Memory Access). ### 2.3 Maximum Mode Configuration (MN/MX̄ = 0) **Operational Steps:** * **Why used:** Multi-processor systems (e.g., 8086 + 8087 coprocessor, or 8086 + 8089 I/O processor, or multiple 8086s). * **How it works:** The 8086 outputs **3 status lines (S₂̄, S₁̄, S₀̄)** which feed an external **8288 Bus Controller**. The 8288 decodes these status bits and generates a *richer* set of control signals. * **Signals generated by 8288:** MRDC̄, MWTC̄, AMWC̄ (memory), IORC̄, IOWC̄, AIOWC̄ (I/O), INTĀ, CEN̄ (cascade enable), DEN, DT/R̄. * **Bus Arbitration:** Priority-based using **RQ̄/GT₀̄ and RQ̄/GT₁̄** (Request/Grant) — a 3-wire handshake protocol (no separate HOLD/HLDA pins). * **LOCK̄ pin:** Allows the CPU to lock the bus during critical multi-instruction sequences. * **QS₁, QS₀:** Output the internal **6-byte instruction queue** status (for the 8087 to track instructions). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Key equations, conversions, and address calculations for 8086 system design:** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Physical Address Generation** | $\text{PA} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$ | 20-bit result, range **00000H – FFFFFH** | | **Memory Capacity** | $2^n$ bytes, where $n$ = address lines | $2^{20}$ = **1,048,576 B = 1 MB** | | **I/O Port Capacity** | $2^n$ ports, where $n$ = I/O address bits | $2^{16}$ = **65,536 = 64K ports** | | **Bus Bandwidth** | $\text{Bus BW} = \text{Data Width} \times \text{Frequency}$ | bits/sec (e.g., 16 bits × 5 MHz = **80 Mbps**) | | **Wait State Insertion** | $\text{Cycle Time} = (N + W) \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | $N$ = base states, $W$ = wait states, $T_{\text{clk}}$ = clock period | | **Memory Access Time** | $t_{\text{acc}} \le (N + W - 1) \times T_{\text{clk}}$ | Must be satisfied or READY must be activated | | **Status Encoding (Max Mode)** | $\overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0}$ decoded by 8288 | 8 possible states, 6 used | | **BHĒ Selection Logic** | $\overline{\text{BHE}} = 0$ for **D₁₅–D₈**, A₀ = 0 for **D₇–D₀** | Enables upper/lower byte selection | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Minimum Mode** → Used in embedded controllers, dedicated instruments, and educational kits. * **Maximum Mode** → Used in original IBM PC, industrial PCs, and mainframes where numerical co-processing (8087 Math Coprocessor) or I/O co-processing (8089) was essential for performance. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address Calculation in 8086 The 8086 uses **segmented memory architecture** because it has 16-bit internal registers but a 20-bit external address bus. **Given:** * Segment Register Content = `CS = 2000H` (Code Segment) * Offset = `IP = 0450H` (Instruction Pointer) **Goal:** Find the Physical Address of the next instruction to be fetched. **Step 1 — Conceptual Basis:** The 20-bit physical address is constructed by left-shifting the 16-bit segment base by 4 bit positions (multiplying by 2⁴ = 16 = 10H), then adding the 16-bit offset. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Physical Address} &= (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset} \\ &= (\text{Segment} \times 16_{10}) + \text{Offset} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Hex Values:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PA} &= (2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 0450\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Perform the Hexadecimal Multiplication:** $$ \begin{aligned} 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} &= 2000\text{H} \times 16_{10} \end{aligned} $$ Explanation: Multiplying a hex number by 10H simply appends a zero (since base 16, multiplying by 16 shifts left by one hex digit). $$ \begin{aligned} 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} &= 20000\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Add the Offset:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PA} &= 20000\text{H} + 0450\text{H} \\ &= 20450\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Verify Range:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Result} &= 20450\text{H} = 132{,}176_{10} \end{aligned} $$ Since $0 \le \text{PA} \le \text{FFFFFH}$, the address `20450H` is a valid 20-bit physical memory location. ✓ **Result:** The next instruction will be fetched from physical memory address **`20450H`**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Wait State Calculation **Given:** * Clock Frequency = $5 \text{ MHz}$ → $T_{\text{clk}} = 200 \text{ ns}$ * Memory Access Time $t_{\text{acc}} = 450 \text{ ns}$ * Base states for memory read $N = 4$ (typical T-state count for a bus cycle) * Propagation delay of decoder + buffer ≈ $50 \text{ ns}$ **Goal:** Find the number of wait states $W$ required. **Step 1 — Compute the maximum allowed memory access:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{acc, max}} &= (N + W - 1) \times T_{\text{clk}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute known values:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{acc, max}} &= (4 + W - 1) \times 200 \text{ ns} \\ &= (3 + W) \times 200 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Effective available time (after subtracting delays):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{effective}} &= t_{\text{acc, max}} - t_{\text{delay}} \\ &= (3 + W) \times 200 - 50 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Set $t_{\text{effective}} \ge t_{\text{acc}}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} (3 + W) \times 200 - 50 &\ge 450 \\ (3 + W) \times 200 &\ge 500 \\ 3 + W &\ge 2.5 \\ W &\ge -0.5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Round up to nearest integer wait state:** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= 0 \text{ wait states} \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The memory is fast enough; **0 wait states** are required. If the memory had been 600 ns, then: $$ \begin{aligned} (3 + W) \times 200 - 50 &\ge 600 \\ 3 + W &\ge 3.25 \\ W &\ge 0.25 \Rightarrow W = 1 \end{aligned} $$ So **1 wait state** would be inserted by activating the **READY** pin. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Minimum Mode vs. Maximum Mode > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison matrix is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU board exams. Memorize it thoroughly. | Parameter | Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 1) | Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 0) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Mode Select** | Pin 33 tied to Vcc (+5V) | Pin 33 tied to GND (0V) | | **Bus Controller** | None required (8086 self-controls) | External **8288 Bus Controller** required | | **Control Signal Outputs** | $\overline{\text{RD}}, \overline{\text{WR}}, \text{M/IO}, \overline{\text{INTA}}$ directly | Status $\overline{S_2}\overline{S_1}\overline{S_0}$ → 8288 generates $\overline{\text{MRDC}}, \overline{\text{MWTC}}, \overline{\text{IORC}}, \overline{\text{IOWC}}$, etc. | | **Bus Arbitration Pins** | HOLD, HLDA (2 pins) | $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}_0, \overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}_1$ (3-wire protocol) | | **LOCK̄ Pin** | Not available | Available (pin 29) | | **Queue Status Pins** | Not available | QS₁, QS₀ available (used by 8087) | | **Typical Application** | Single-CPU, small embedded | Multi-CPU (8086 + 8087 + 8089) | | **System Cost** | Low | Higher (extra 8288 chip) | | **Control Flexibility** | Limited, fixed signals | Rich, programmable advanced commands | | **ALE** | Output from 8086 directly | Output from 8288 (8288's ALE) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 System Block Diagram — Minimum Mode Configuration > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the architectural flow of a Minimum Mode 8086 system. Notice the demultiplexing latches and the buffering transceivers. ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["8086 CPU
MN MX bar = 1
40 pin DIP"] LATCH1["74LS373 Latch 1
A7 A0 Demux"] LATCH2["74LS373 Latch 2
A15 A8 Demux"] LATCH3["74LS373 Latch 3
A19 A16 Demux"] TRANS1["74LS245 Transceiver 1
D7 D0 Buffer"] TRANS2["74LS245 Transceiver 2
D15 D8 Buffer"] MEM["Memory
1 MB Address Space
FFFFFH to 00000H"] IO["I O Devices
64K Ports
FFFFH to 0000H"] DECODER["Address Decoder
3 to 8 or similar"] ABUS["20 Bit Address Bus"] DBUS["16 Bit Data Bus"] CBUS["Control Bus
RD bar WR bar M IO"] CPU -- AD0 AD7 --> LATCH1 CPU -- AD8 AD15 --> LATCH2 CPU -- A16 A19 --> LATCH3 CPU -- AD0 AD7 --> TRANS1 CPU -- AD8 AD15 --> TRANS2 LATCH1 --> ABUS LATCH2 --> ABUS LATCH3 --> ABUS ABUS --> DECODER DECODER --> MEM DECODER --> IO TRANS1 --> DBUS TRANS2 --> DBUS CPU -- RD WR M IO --> CBUS CBUS --> MEM CBUS --> IO DBUS --> MEM DBUS --> IO ``` ### 4.2 System Block Diagram — Maximum Mode Configuration > [!TIP] > In Maximum Mode, an **8288 Bus Controller** sits between the CPU and the system buses. The CPU outputs only 3 status lines ($\overline{S_2}, \overline{S_1}, \overline{S_0}$); the 8288 decodes them and produces the rich control signal set. ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU8086["8086 CPU
MN MX bar = 0
Max Mode Selected"] BUS8288["8288 Bus Controller
Status Decoder
Command Generator"] CPU8087["8087 Math Coprocessor"] CPU8089["8089 I O Processor"] LATCH1["74LS373 Latch
A19 A0 Demux"] TRANS["74LS245 Transceiver
D15 D0 Buffer"] MEM["Memory
1 MB Space"] IO["I O Devices
64K Ports"] ABUS["20 Bit Address Bus"] DBUS["16 Bit Data Bus"] CBUS["Advanced Control Bus
MRDC MWTC IORC IOWC"] STATUS["3 Status Lines
S2 bar S1 bar S0 bar"] RQGT["RQ GT0 and RQ GT1
Bus Arbitration Wires"] QS["Queue Status
QS1 QS0"] CPU8086 -- STATUS --> BUS8288 CPU8086 -- QS --> CPU8087 CPU8086 -- RQGT --> CPU8087 CPU8086 -- RQGT --> CPU8089 BUS8288 --> CBUS CPU8086 -- AD15 AD0 and A19 A16 --> LATCH1 CPU8086 -- AD15 AD0 --> TRANS LATCH1 --> ABUS TRANS --> DBUS ABUS --> MEM ABUS --> IO DBUS --> MEM DBUS --> IO CBUS --> MEM CBUS --> IO ``` ### 4.3 Pin Function Reassignment Table (Pin 24–31) > [!IMPORTANT] > Pins 24 through 31 are **dual-function** — their meaning depends on the mode. This is a classic KTU exam short-answer question. | Pin | Minimum Mode Name | Maximum Mode Name | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pin 24** | $\overline{\text{INTA}}$ (Interrupt Acknowledge) | $\overline{\text{QS}}_1$ (Queue Status 1) | | **Pin 25** | ALE (Address Latch Enable) | $\overline{\text{QS}}_0$ (Queue Status 0) | | **Pin 26** | $\overline{\text{DEN}}$ (Data Enable) | $\overline{S}_0$ (Status 0) | | **Pin 27** | DT/$\overline{\text{R}}$ (Data Transmit/Receive) | $\overline{S}_1$ (Status 1) | | **Pin 28** | M/$\overline{\text{IO}}$ (Memory/I/O select) | $\overline{S}_2$ (Status 2) | | **Pin 29** | $\overline{\text{WR}}$ (Write) | $\overline{\text{LOCK}}$ (Bus Lock) | | **Pin 30** | HLDA (Hold Acknowledge) | $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}_0$ (Request/Grant 0) | | **Pin 31** | HOLD (Hold Request) | $\overline{\text{RQ}}/\overline{\text{GT}}_1$ (Request/Grant 1) | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > Differentiate between Minimum Mode and Maximum Mode configurations of the 8086 microprocessor. Mention the role of the MN/MX̄ pin. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The MN/MX̄ (pin 33) is a **mode select** pin. When tied HIGH (Vcc = +5V), the 8086 operates in **Minimum Mode**, where it directly generates all control signals (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ) — suitable for **single-CPU** systems. When tied LOW (GND = 0V), it operates in **Maximum Mode**, where the CPU outputs only **3 status lines** ($\overline{S_2}, \overline{S_1}, \overline{S_0}$) that feed an external **8288 Bus Controller**, which generates advanced control signals (MRDC̄, MWTC̄, IORC̄, IOWC̄) — suitable for **multi-CPU** systems with 8087/8089. Maximum mode also provides bus arbitration via RQ̄/GT₀/₁ and a LOCK̄ signal. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** > Why is the address bus of the 8086 demultiplexed? List the ICs used for this purpose. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 8086 multiplexes the lower 16 address lines with the 16-bit data bus onto pins **AD₁₅–AD₀** to fit into a 40-pin package. During the **first T-state (T1)**, these pins carry the address; during **T2–T4**, they carry data. To separate the address permanently, **3 × 74LS373 octal transparent latches** are used, controlled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal — HIGH during T1 latches the address; LOW holds it. A 4th latch (74LS373) handles the **BHĒ/S₇** signal. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Practice (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > * **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse the function of **ALE** in minimum vs. maximum mode. In **minimum mode**, ALE is generated by the **8086 directly**; in **maximum mode**, ALE is generated by the **8288**. > * **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to mention that **74LS373** is a **transparent latch** (vs. 74LS374 which is an edge-triggered flip-flop) costs 1 mark. > * **Pitfall 3:** Confusing **DT/R̄** (Data Transmit/Receive) with **DEN̄** (Data Enable) when describing transceiver control. > * **Pitfall 4:** Writing **MN/MX̄** as `MN/MX` — the bar (negation) is mandatory; without it, the meaning inverts. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]` > **Q. (a)** Explain the Minimum Mode configuration of 8086 with a neat block diagram. List the functions of the signals M/IŌ, RD̄, WR̄, ALE, DEN̄, and DT/R̄. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Describe the Maximum Mode configuration of 8086. Explain the role of the 8288 bus controller and the RQ̄/GT signals. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]** **Architecture (3 marks):** In Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ = +5V), the 8086 acts as the sole bus master. The system consists of: * **8086 CPU** (40 pins) * **74LS373 latches × 3** — demultiplex address from AD₁₅–AD₀ and A₁₉/S₆–A₁₆/S₃, latched by **ALE = HIGH** during T1. * **74LS245 transceivers × 2** — buffer the 16-bit data bus; direction controlled by **DT/R̄**, enabling by **DEN̄**. * **Address decoder** (74LS138) — generates chip selects for memory and I/O. **Signal Functions (4 marks — 2/3 marks each):** | Signal | Function | | :--- | :--- | | **M/IŌ** | HIGH = memory access; LOW = I/O access. Acts as the most significant bit of the address in I/O space. | | **RD̄** | Active LOW read strobe — CPU reads data from memory/I/O. | | **WR̄** | Active LOW write strobe — CPU writes data to memory/I/O. | | **ALE** | Address Latch Enable — HIGH in T1; 74LS373 captures the address on its falling edge. | | **DEN̄** | Data Enable — Active LOW; enables the 74LS245 transceivers onto the data bus. | | **DT/R̄** | Data Transmit/Receive — HIGH = CPU transmitting (write); LOW = CPU receiving (read). | **Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 above. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]** **Maximum Mode Setup (2 marks):** In Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 0V), the 8086 supports **multiprocessor configurations**. Pins 24–31 are reassigned (see Section 4.3 table). An external **8288 Bus Controller** decodes the status signals and issues advanced commands. **Role of 8288 Bus Controller (3 marks):** The 8288 receives the **3 status lines** $\overline{S_2}, \overline{S_1}, \overline{S_0}$ and decodes them into: $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 000 \rightarrow \text{INTA} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 001 \rightarrow \text{IORC} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 010 \rightarrow \text{IOWC} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 011 \rightarrow \text{HALT} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 100 \rightarrow \text{Instruction Fetch} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 101 \rightarrow \text{Memory Read (MRDC)} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 110 \rightarrow \text{Memory Write (MWTC)} \\ \overline{S_2}\,\overline{S_1}\,\overline{S_0} &= 111 \rightarrow \text{Passive (no bus cycle)} \end{aligned} $$ The 8288 also generates separate **Advanced Write** signals (AMWC̄, AIOWC̄) which activate **one T-state earlier** than normal writes — useful for slow peripherals. Additionally, 8288 provides **CEN̄ (Cascade Enable)** to allow daisy-chained bus controllers. **RQ̄/GT Signals (2 marks):** The two **RQ̄/GT₀̄ and RQ̄/GT₁̄** lines implement a **3-wire handshake** protocol: 1. Requesting master **pulses RQ̄/GT̄ LOW** (request). 2. CPU responds by **pulsing the same line LOW** (grant), releases it HIGH. 3. After bus use, requesting master **pulses LOW again** (release). RQ̄/GT₀̄ has **higher priority** than RQ̄/GT₁̄. The 8087 typically connects to RQ̄/GT₁̄, while RQ̄/GT₀̄ is reserved for the 8089 I/O processor or DMA controllers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]` > **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the demultiplexing of the address and data bus of 8086. Mention the role of 74LS373 and 74LS245. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Explain the function of the following 8086 signals: BHĒ/S₇, $\overline{\text{TEST}}$, READY, RESET, MN/MX̄, and $\overline{\text{LOCK}}$. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]** **Need for Demultiplexing (2 marks):** The 8086 has only 40 pins, so it multiplexes the 16-bit data bus with the lower 16 address lines on pins AD₁₅–AD₀ (time-division multiplexing). During T1, these pins carry the address; during T2–T4, they carry the data bus traffic. External circuitry must separate them. **74LS373 — Address Latch (2 marks):** * 8-bit transparent D-type latch. * When **LE (Latch Enable, tied to ALE) = HIGH**, the latch is transparent — output follows input. * When **LE = LOW** (falling edge of ALE), the address is **frozen** at the output. * Three 74LS373 ICs are used: one for AD₇–AD₀, one for AD₁₅–AD₈, and one for A₁₉/S₆–A₁₆/S₃. **74LS245 — Data Bus Transceiver (2 marks):** * 8-bit bidirectional non-inverting transceiver. * **DIR pin** tied to **DT/R̄** — HIGH for CPU→Memory (write), LOW for Memory→CPU (read). * **OĒ (Output Enable)** pin tied to **DEN̄** — active LOW to connect the transceiver to the system data bus. * Two 74LS245 ICs handle the 16-bit data bus (D₇–D₀ and D₁₅–D₈). **Block Diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.1 of these notes. --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]** | Signal | Pin | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **$\overline{\text{BHE}}$/S₇** | 34 | **Bus High Enable** — active LOW in T1 enables data on the upper byte (D₁₅–D₈); in T2–T4, pin carries status bit S₇. Used with A₀ to select odd/even banks. | | **$\overline{\text{TEST}}$** | 23 | Input. Wait-for-Test instruction (`WAIT`) checks this pin. If HIGH, the CPU idles until it goes LOW — used for **8087 coprocessor synchronization**. | | **READY** | 22 | Input. If LOW, the CPU inserts **wait states** (TW) into the bus cycle to accommodate slow memory/peripherals. | | **RESET** | 21 | Input. HIGH for at least **4 clock cycles** to reset the 8086; clears IP, DS, SS, ES, PSW; sets CS = FFFFH; execution begins at physical address **FFFF0H**. | | **MN/MX̄** | 33 | Mode Select. HIGH (Vcc) = Minimum Mode; LOW (GND) = Maximum Mode. | | **$\overline{\text{LOCK}}$** | 29 | Active LOW output in **Maximum Mode** only. Asserted during a **LOCK-prefixed instruction** (e.g., `XCHG reg, MEM` with LOCK) to prevent other processors from gaining bus control during the instruction. | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and memorize before every KTU exam:** * **Two modes** of 8086 are selected by the **MN/MX̄** pin (pin 33): HIGH = Minimum, LOW = Maximum. * **Minimum Mode** = 8086 self-generates control signals (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, INTĀ). No 8288. * **Maximum Mode** = 8086 outputs 3 status lines; **8288 Bus Controller** generates MRDC̄, MWTC̄, IORC̄, IOWC̄, AMWC̄, AIOWC̄. * **Bus Arbitration:** Min Mode uses **HOLD/HLDA**; Max Mode uses **RQ̄/GT₀̄/RQ̄/GT₁̄** (3-wire pulse protocol). * **Demultiplexing:** **74LS373** (latch, controlled by ALE) for address; **74LS245** (transceiver, controlled by DT/R̄ and DEN̄) for data. * **LOCK̄** pin exists **only in Maximum Mode** (pin 29). * **QS₁/QS₀** pins exist **only in Maximum Mode** (pins 24, 25) — used by 8087 to track the instruction queue. * **BHĒ** is used along with A₀ to select upper byte (D₁₅–D₈), lower byte (D₇–D₀), or full 16-bit word — enabling the **8086's memory banking** architecture. * **Memory capacity** = $2^{20}$ = **1 MB**; **I/O port capacity** = $2^{16}$ = **64 KB**. * **Physical Address** = (Segment × 10H) + Offset → **20-bit** address. * **Read cycle** = minimum **4 T-states**; **Write cycle** = minimum **4 T-states** (can insert wait states via READY). * **Reset vector** = **FFFF0H** (CS:IP = F000:FFF0 → PA = FFFF0H). * In a **Maximum Mode multiprocessor system**, the 8087 (Math Coprocessor) connects to RQ̄/GT₁̄ and reads QS₁/QS₀ — the **8086 acts as the bus master**, the 8087 as a **slave bus master** (passive until granted).

topics.title: System Bus Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Bus Timing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Bus Timing** refers to the **precise, deterministic sequence of events and signal transitions** that occur on the address, data, and control buses of a microprocessor (specifically the **8086** in this module) during a bus cycle. A *bus cycle* is the time taken by the processor to fetch an instruction byte from memory or perform a read/write data transfer. The unit of timing is the **T-state**, which equals one **clock period (T = 1/f)** of the system clock fed to the **CLK** pin of the 8086.

The 8086 bus cycle is fundamentally divided into **four T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4)** with an optional **wait state (Tw)** inserted between T3 and T4 if the addressed memory or I/O device is too slow to respond within the standard timing window.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** You must be able to draw and label the **Read cycle** and **Write cycle** timing diagrams for the 8086 in **Minimum Mode**, identify every active-high/active-low control signal, and explain the role of the **READY** pin in introducing **Wait States (Tw)**.

## 1.2 The Architectural Big Picture

The 8086 is a **16-bit microprocessor** with a **20-bit address bus** and a **16-bit data bus**. Because it is packaged in a **40-pin DIP**, it cannot dedicate separate pins for the entire address and data buses. Therefore, it uses a **multiplexed bus architecture**:

- **Pins AD15–AD0** carry **Address** during the first part of the cycle and **Data** during the later part of the cycle.
- **Pins A19/S6–A16/S3** carry the **upper 4 bits of address** multiplexed with **status information (S6–S3)**.

External **latches (74LS373 / 74LS245)** are used to demultiplex these lines, controlled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Synchronised Theatre"

Imagine a **theatre ticket counter** where the cashier (the **8086 CPU**) must serve a customer (the **memory/I/O device**). The system bus timing is the **choreographed script** of the play:

| Role in Analogy | Hardware Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Theatre Manager's whistle | **CLK (System Clock)** — sets the rhythm |
| Cashier calling out the counter number | **Address bus (A19–A0)** with **ALE** — "Where are you going?" |
| Customer handing over the ticket | **Data bus (D15–D0)** — "Here is the data" |
| Sign above counter: "OPEN" or "CLOSED" | **M/IO, RD, WR** — the operation type |
| Slow customer asking "wait a moment" | **READY = 0** — inserts a **Wait State (Tw)** |
| End of transaction bell | **T4 state** — bus cycle complete |

Each T-state is a **single beat of the clock**. Every signal must change at the **rising or falling edge** of CLK, ensuring that all devices in the system stay perfectly synchronised.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Metric to Remember:** A standard 8086 bus cycle is **4 clock periods long (4T)**. With a **5 MHz clock (8086)**, one bus cycle = 4 × 200 ns = **800 ns**. With a **10 MHz clock (8086-2)**, the bus cycle = 4 × 125 ns = **500 ns**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bus Cycle – T-State Architecture

A bus cycle is composed of a finite number of **T-states (Clock States)**. Each T-state is one **period (T)** of the CLK input pin. The transitions of all external signals are referenced to the **falling edge of CLK** in the 8086.

### 2.1.1 Standard T-State Map

| T-State | Activity on Address Bus | Activity on Data Bus | Control Signals Activated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T1** | 20-bit address placed on AD15–AD0 and A19/S6–A16/S3 | Tri-stated (high impedance) | **ALE = 1** (latches address), **BHE = status**, **DT/R** set |
| **T2** | Address removed; AD lines go high-Z | For **Read**: still high-Z. For **Write**: data placed on bus. | **M/IO, RD (Read)** or **WR (Write)** activated; **DEN = 1** (enables transceiver) |
| **T3** | High-Z | **Read**: Memory/IO places valid data. **Write**: Data held. | Data transfer occurs. |
| **Tw** (optional) | High-Z | Data held; bus waits for slow device. | If **READY = 0**, wait states inserted. |
| **T4** | High-Z | Read: Data latched into CPU. Write: Data removed. | **RD/WR** deactivated, bus cycle ends. |

## 2.2 Detailed Signal Analysis (Minimum Mode MN/MX = High)

### 2.2.1 The Control Signal Dictionary

| Signal | Pin # | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CLK** | 19 | Input | System clock; bus timing reference. Frequency: **5 MHz (8086), 8 MHz (8086-2), 10 MHz (8086-1)**. |
| **ALE** | 25 | Output | Address Latch Enable. Goes HIGH in T1; latches address into 74LS373. |
| **M/IO** | 28 | Output | **HIGH = Memory access**, **LOW = I/O access**. Set in T1. |
| **BHE/S7** | 34 | Output | Bus High Enable. LOW in T1 when accessing upper byte (D15–D8). |
| **RD** | 32 | Output | Read strobe. **Active LOW**. Goes LOW in T2. |
| **WR** | 29 | Output | Write strobe. **Active LOW**. Goes LOW in T2 (write cycle). |
| **DT/R** | 27 | Output | Data Transmit/Receive. **HIGH = Write** (CPU transmits), **LOW = Read** (CPU receives). |
| **DEN** | 26 | Output | Data Enable. **Active LOW** (in 8086). Enables 74LS245 transceiver. |
| **READY** | 22 | Input | Ready input. **HIGH = device ready**, **LOW = insert wait state**. Sampled at rising edge of T3. |
| **HOLD** | 31 | Input | Bus request from DMA/other master. |
| **HLDA** | 30 | Output | Hold Acknowledge. |

### 2.2.2 The Multiplexing Scheme

During **T1** of any bus cycle:

$$A_{19}A_{18}A_{17}A_{16} \quad A_{15}A_{14}\ldots A_1A_0 \quad \overline{BHE}$$

These **20 address bits + BHE = 21 address lines** are required to address the full **1 MB memory** of the 8086. Since the data bus is only 16 bits, the lower 16 bits are multiplexed with the address on pins **AD15–AD0**. The demultiplexing latch (typically **74LS373** for address and **74LS245** for data) is enabled by the **ALE** pulse.

## 2.3 Wait States (Tw) – The Synchronisation Bridge

The 8086 is the **bus master**, but memory and I/O devices vary in speed. To prevent data corruption when accessing a slow device, the 8086 samples the **READY** pin at the **end of T2 / start of T3**:

- If **READY = 1 (HIGH)**: Proceed directly to T3. Bus cycle = 4T.
- If **READY = 0 (LOW)**: Insert one **Tw state**. CPU re-checks READY in each Tw. Continue until READY = 1, then proceed to T3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calculation Rule:** If `N` wait states are inserted, the total bus cycle duration = **(4 + N) × T** clock periods.

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| 2 | Standard bus cycle duration | $T_{cycle} = 4T$ | For 0 wait states |
| 3 | Bus cycle with N wait states | $T_{cycle} = (4 + N) \cdot T$ | Where N ≥ 0 is integer |
| 4 | Maximum addressable memory | $M = 2^{20}$ | = **1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** |
| 5 | I/O port space | $2^{16}$ ports | = **65,536 ports** (64K I/O) |
| 6 | Bandwidth (bytes/sec, no wait) | $BW = \dfrac{2}{4T}$ | **2 bytes per 4T cycle** (16-bit) |
| 7 | Address latch enable pulse width | $t_{AL} \geq 2T - 60$ ns | 8086 AC spec |
| 8 | Read data setup time | $t_{DR} \geq 20$ ns | Before T4 falling edge |
| 9 | Ready sampling window | End of T2 | If LOW → Tw inserted |
| 10 | Minimum pulse width of RD/WR | $2T - 60$ ns | Active LOW strobe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** System bus timing is the foundation of **memory interfacing, peripheral design, and DMA coordination**. Every engineer designing an 8086-based embedded system (industrial controllers, legacy avionics, lab instruments) must compute wait states to match the CPU's speed with the memory's access time. A mismatch causes the CPU to read **stale or invalid data** — a classic fault in microprocessor labs.

## 2.5 Minimum Mode vs Maximum Mode Bus Timing

| Feature | Minimum Mode (MN/MX = 1) | Maximum Mode (MN/MX = 0) |
|---|---|---|
| Control signals | RD, WR, M/IO, ALE, DEN, DT/R | S2, S1, S0, LOCK, RQ/GT |
| Bus arbitration | HOLD / HLDA pins | RQ/GT0, RQ/GT1 pins |
| Status encoding | None (direct signals) | 3-bit status from 8288 bus controller |
| Multiprocessor support | Limited | Full (with 8288 + 8087 + 8089) |
| KTU focus | **Primary** | Secondary |

In **Maximum Mode**, the 8086 outputs **S2, S1, S0** instead of M/IO, RD, WR. An external **8288 Bus Controller** decodes these three status lines into the actual control signals. The timing structure (T1–T4, Tw) remains identical — only the *signal names* change.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — 8086 Read Cycle (Minimum Mode)

Let us trace the **Read Bus Cycle** step-by-step, the way a KTU 14-mark question expects.

### Step 1: T1 State — Address Phase
- The 8086 places the **20-bit physical address** on:
  - **A19/S6 – A16/S3** (pins 35–38) — upper 4 address bits
  - **AD15 – AD0** (pins 2–16, 39) — lower 16 address bits
- The **BHE** signal (pin 34) is driven LOW to select the upper data byte (D15–D8).
- **ALE** (pin 25) goes **HIGH** for the duration of T1. This rising edge causes external **74LS373 latches** to capture the address.
- **M/IO** is set HIGH (memory) or LOW (I/O) — this signal is valid from the **start of T1** so external address decoders have time to settle.
- **DT/R** is driven LOW (CPU will be *receiving* data in the upcoming read).

**Result at end of T1:** External latches hold a stable, demultiplexed 20-bit address.

### Step 2: T2 State — Command Phase
- The falling edge of CLK during T2 causes the address on AD15–AD0 to be **removed**; these lines enter **high-impedance (tri-state)**.
- The 8086 activates the **RD** signal (pin 32) by driving it **LOW**. This is the "Read Strobe" for memory or I/O.
- **DEN** (pin 26) is driven **LOW** to enable the **74LS245 data bus transceiver**, allowing the device's data to flow onto the CPU's local bus.
- The 8086 now begins to **wait for the memory device's access time** to elapse.

### Step 3: Sampling the READY Pin
- At the **rising edge of T2's clock**, the 8086 samples the **READY** pin.
- **Case A — READY = HIGH**: The addressed device is fast enough. The CPU moves directly to T3.
- **Case B — READY = LOW**: The device is slow. The CPU inserts a **Wait State (Tw)**.

### Step 4: Tw State(s) — Wait Phase
- The CPU remains in the **Tw** state, holding RD LOW, and continues to sample READY on each rising clock edge.
- For each clock tick where READY = 0, another Tw is inserted.
- When READY finally goes HIGH, the CPU exits the wait loop and enters T3 on the next clock cycle.

**Number of wait states:** $N = $ number of consecutive Tw cycles inserted.

### Step 5: T3 State — Data Phase
- The addressed memory or I/O device must place valid data on **D15–D0** at least **20 ns** before the falling edge of T3 (this is the data setup time $t_{DR}$).
- The CPU reads the data into its internal bus interface unit.

### Step 6: T4 State — Completion Phase
- At the beginning of T4, the **RD** signal is driven **HIGH** (deactivated).
- **DEN** is also driven HIGH, disabling the data transceiver.
- The data lines return to high-impedance.
- The bus cycle ends. The next bus cycle can begin immediately after T4 (pipelining) or after additional idle states (TI).

### Step 3.7 — Total Time Calculation

$$
T_{bus\_cycle} = T_1 + T_2 + \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{w,i} \right) + T_3 + T_4
$$

For a **single Tw** ($N = 1$) with **5 MHz clock** ($T = 200$ ns):

$$
T_{cycle} = 200 + 200 + 200 + 200 + 200 = 1000 \; \text{ns} = 1.0 \; \mu s
$$

Compared to the **zero-wait** case of 800 ns, the wait state adds **25% overhead**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — 8086 Write Cycle (Minimum Mode)

The write cycle differs in three key aspects:

### Step 1: T1 — Address Phase (identical to Read)
- Address is placed on the bus.
- **ALE = 1**, address latched.
- **DT/R is driven HIGH** (CPU will be *transmitting* data).
- **M/IO** indicates memory or I/O.

### Step 2: T2 — Data and Command Phase
- Crucially, **the data is placed on AD15–AD0 very early** — typically by the end of T2.
- **WR** (pin 29) is driven **LOW**. This is the "Write Strobe."
- **DEN** is driven LOW to enable the 74LS245 transceiver.
- **RD remains HIGH** (inactive).

### Step 3: T3 — Write Strobe Active
- The WR signal remains LOW, providing the device with sufficient time to latch the data.
- READY is sampled; if slow, Tw states are inserted here.

### Step 4: T4 — Completion
- WR goes HIGH (deactivated).
- The data is removed from the bus.
- DEN goes HIGH (transceiver disabled).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Subtle Difference:** In a **Read cycle**, data flows *from* the device *to* the CPU. In a **Write cycle**, data flows *from* the CPU *to* the device. The signal **DT/R (Data Transmit/Receive)** controls the direction of the **74LS245 transceiver** accordingly. **DT/R = 1** for write, **DT/R = 0** for read.

## 3.3 Detailed Wait State Calculation Example

**Problem:** An 8086 system runs at **8 MHz**. An EPROM with access time of **450 ns** is interfaced. How many wait states are required?

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the clock period.

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 125 \; \text{ns}
$$

**Step 2:** A standard 4T bus cycle lasts:

$$
T_{cycle} = 4 \times 125 = 500 \; \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with EPROM access time.

Since EPROM (450 ns) is slightly **faster** than the 500 ns bus cycle, it can theoretically respond within the standard T3. However, in real designs we must subtract **data setup time** ($t_{DR} = 20$ ns) and **propagation delays** through buffers. Effective margin:

$$
t_{margin} = 500 - 450 - 20 = 30 \; \text{ns} \geq 0
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

$$
\boxed{N_{wait} = 0 \;\; \text{wait states required.}}
$$

**Counter-example:** If the EPROM were **650 ns**:

$$
t_{margin} = 500 - 650 = -150 \; \text{ns} < 0
$$

We need additional time of 150 ns. Each wait state adds **125 ns** (one clock). So:

$$
N_{wait} = \left\lceil \frac{150}{125} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.2 \rceil = 2 \;\; \text{wait states}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Signal Activity at Each T-State

The following table consolidates the entire read cycle (Minimum Mode, 0 wait states):

| Signal | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CLK** | ↑↓ | ↑↓ | ↑↓ | ↑↓ |
| **A19–A16/S6–S3** | Address (A19–A16) | Status (S6–S3) | Status | Status |
| **AD15–AD0** | Address (A15–A0) | High-Z | Data from device | High-Z |
| **ALE** | **HIGH** | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| **BHE** | Valid (LOW for D15–D8) | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| **M/IO** | Valid | Valid | Valid | Valid |
| **RD** | HIGH | **LOW** | LOW | HIGH |
| **WR** | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH |
| **DT/R** | LOW (Read) | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| **DEN** | HIGH | **LOW** | LOW | HIGH |
| **READY** | X | Sampled | HIGH | X |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of 8086 Bus Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Bus Cycle] --> B[T1: Address Phase]
    B --> B1[Place A19-A0 on Multiplexed Bus]
    B --> B2[ALE Pulse HIGH to Latch Address]
    B --> B3[Set M/IO and BHE]
    B --> B4[DT/R set: 0 for Read, 1 for Write]
    B1 --> C[T2: Command Phase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D{Operation Type}
    D -->|Read| E1[RD goes LOW]
    D -->|Write| E2[WR goes LOW]
    E1 --> F[DEN goes LOW, Enable Transceiver]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Sample READY pin at end of T2]
    G --> H{READY = 1?}
    H -->|NO| I[Insert Tw: Wait State]
    I --> J[Re-sample READY in next clock]
    J --> H
    H -->|YES| K[T3: Data Transfer]
    K --> L[Read: Latch data into CPU]
    K --> M[Write: Device latches data]
    L --> N[T4: Completion]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Deactivate RD or WR]
    O --> P[DEN goes HIGH]
    P --> Q[Bus returns to High-Z]
    Q --> R[End of Bus Cycle]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#ef4444,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — 8086 Read Bus Cycle (0 Wait States)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8086 CPU
    participant Latch as 74LS373 Latch
    participant MEM as Memory / IO Device
    participant XCVR as 74LS245 Transceiver

    Note over CPU: T1 begins
    CPU->>Latch: Drive A19-A0 and BHE on bus
    CPU->>Latch: ALE = HIGH
    Latch-->>Latch: Latch address internally
    CPU->>CPU: Set M/IO, DT/R=0
    Note over CPU: T1 ends (falling CLK)

    Note over CPU: T2 begins
    CPU->>MEM: RD goes LOW
    CPU->>XCVR: DEN goes LOW
    CPU->>CPU: Remove address from AD15-AD0
    MEM-->>MEM: Begin access cycle
    CPU->>CPU: Sample READY = 1
    Note over CPU: T2 ends

    Note over CPU: T3 begins
    MEM->>XCVR: Place valid data on D15-D0
    XCVR->>CPU: Forward data through transceiver
    CPU->>CPU: Latch data internally
    Note over CPU: T3 ends

    Note over CPU: T4 begins
    CPU->>MEM: RD goes HIGH
    CPU->>XCVR: DEN goes HIGH
    CPU->>CPU: Data lines go High-Z
    Note over CPU: T4 ends / Bus cycle complete
```

## 4.3 Signal-Level Timing State Machine (Minimum Mode Read)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> T1
    T1: T1 - Address Phase\nALE = HIGH\nAddress driven on bus
    T1 --> T2
    T2: T2 - Command Phase\nRD = LOW, DEN = LOW\nSample READY pin
    T2 --> Tw: READY = 0
    T2 --> T3: READY = 1
    Tw: Tw - Wait State\nHold RD LOW\nRe-sample READY
    Tw --> Tw: READY = 0
    Tw --> T3: READY = 1
    T3: T3 - Data Phase\nDevice places data on D15-D0\nCPU reads data
    T3 --> T4
    T4: T4 - Completion\nRD = HIGH, DEN = HIGH\nBus returns to High-Z
    T4 --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Architecture of the Bus Interface Unit (BIU) – The Timing Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIU[8086 Bus Interface Unit]
    Q[Instruction Queue\n6 bytes FIFO] --> EU[Execution Unit]
    EU --> Q
    BIU_CTRL[BIU Control Logic] --> Q
    SUM[Address Adder\n+ Segment Registers]
    SUM --> A1[20-bit Physical Address]
    A1 --> BUS[External Multiplexed Bus]
    BIU_CTRL --> BUS
    end

    EXT[External 74LS373\nAddress Latch] --> MEM[Memory\n1 MB Space]
    BUS --> EXT
    EXT --> IO[IO Devices\n64K Ports]
    BUS --> XCVR[74LS245\nData Transceiver]
    XCVR --> MEM
    XCVR --> IO

    style BIU fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style EU fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style BIU_CTRL fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style SUM fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style EXT fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style XCVR fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a T-state and a bus cycle. How are wait states related to bus cycles? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **T-state** is the time period of one clock cycle, equal to the reciprocal of the CLK input frequency. A **bus cycle** is the complete sequence of events required by the CPU to perform a single memory or I/O read/write operation. In the 8086, a standard bus cycle consists of **4 T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4)**. **Wait states (Tw)** are additional T-states inserted between T3 and T4 (or before T3) when the addressed memory or I/O device is too slow to respond within the standard timing. The READY pin controls wait state insertion. Mathematically:

$$
T_{bus} = (4 + N) \cdot T \quad \text{where N = number of wait states}
$$

---

### Question 2: What is the function of the ALE pin in 8086? Why is it necessary? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**ALE (Address Latch Enable)** is an **active-HIGH output pin (pin 25)** of the 8086 that signals the presence of a valid address on the multiplexed address/data bus (**AD15–AD0** and **A19/S6–A16/S3**). During **T1**, the CPU places the 20-bit physical address on these pins and pulses ALE HIGH. External **74LS373 octal latches** use this HIGH pulse to capture and hold the address. After T1, the address pins are reused for **data** (AD15–AD0) or **status** (S6–S3). ALE is necessary because the 8086 has only **40 pins** and must multiplex address and data on the same physical lines. The latches, once triggered, hold the demultiplexed address stable for the rest of the bus cycle, providing it to memory and I/O address decoders.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** Draw and explain the **Read cycle timing diagram** of the 8086 in **Minimum Mode**. Clearly label all major signals in each T-state. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** An 8086 system operates at **10 MHz**. Interface an SRAM with an access time of **250 ns**. Determine the number of wait states required. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The 8086 Read cycle in Minimum Mode consists of four T-states (assuming 0 wait states):

**T1 State:**
- The 20-bit address is placed on the multiplexed bus: A19–A16 on pins A19/S6–A16/S3, and A15–A0 on pins AD15–AD0.
- The **ALE** signal goes HIGH, latching the address into external 74LS373 latches.
- The **BHE** signal goes LOW (for upper byte access).
- The **M/IO** signal goes HIGH (memory) or LOW (I/O).
- **DT/R** is set LOW (CPU will receive data).

**T2 State:**
- The address is removed from AD15–AD0 (these lines go HIGH-Z).
- The **RD** signal is driven LOW (active read strobe).
- **DEN** is driven LOW, enabling the 74LS245 data bus transceiver.
- The CPU begins to wait for memory's access time.
- At the rising edge of T2, the CPU samples the **READY** pin.

**T3 State:**
- The memory device places valid data on D15–D0.
- The data passes through the enabled 74LS245 to the CPU.
- The CPU latches the data into its internal bus interface.

**T4 State:**
- The **RD** signal returns HIGH.
- **DEN** returns HIGH, disabling the transceiver.
- The data lines return to high-Z.
- The bus cycle terminates.

```
         T1      T2      T3      T4
CLK  ___|‾|___|‾|___|‾|___|‾|___
ALE  ___|‾‾|_________________
A    __|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___________
AD   __|ADDRESS|‾Z‾|DATA|‾Z‾|__
M/IO __|VALID|‾‾‾‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
RD   _________|‾‾‾|‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾
WR   ________________________
DEN  _________|‾‾‾|‾‾‾|‾‾‾‾‾
DT/R _|LOW|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $f_{CLK} = 10$ MHz, $t_{access} = 250$ ns (SRAM).

**Step 1:** Compute the clock period.

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 100 \; \text{ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the no-wait bus cycle duration.

$$
T_{cycle} = 4T = 4 \times 100 = 400 \; \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the SRAM access time.

Since SRAM access time (250 ns) is **less than** the bus cycle (400 ns), the device can respond in time. The data will be ready by the time T3 begins.

**Step 4:** Account for data setup time ($t_{DR} = 20$ ns).

$$
t_{available} = 400 - 250 = 150 \; \text{ns} \quad \text{(margin)}
$$

Since $150 \text{ ns} > 20 \text{ ns}$, the system has sufficient timing margin.

$$
\boxed{N_{wait} = 0 \;\; \text{wait states required.}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The SRAM can be interfaced with **0 wait states** at 10 MHz.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the role of the **READY** signal in the 8086 bus cycle. Describe how wait states are inserted. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare the **Read cycle** and **Write cycle** timing of the 8086 in Minimum Mode, highlighting the differences in DT/R, RD, WR, and data placement on the bus. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **READY** signal is an **active-HIGH input to the 8086 on pin 22**. It is used to synchronise the CPU with slower memory or I/O devices.

**Mechanism of Wait State Insertion:**

1. During a bus cycle, the 8086 places the address and activates the RD or WR strobe by **T2**.
2. At the **end of T2 (rising clock edge)**, the CPU samples the **READY** pin.
3. If **READY = 1 (HIGH)**: The device is fast. The CPU proceeds to T3 directly. Total cycle = 4T.
4. If **READY = 0 (LOW)**: The device is slow. The CPU enters a **Wait State (Tw)**.
5. In each Tw state, the CPU continues to hold RD/WR active and **re-samples READY**.
6. As long as READY = 0, additional Tw states are inserted.
7. When READY finally goes HIGH, the CPU exits Tw and enters T3 on the next clock.
8. The total bus cycle becomes $(4 + N)T$, where $N$ is the number of Tw states.

**Hardware Implementation:** A **wait state generator** circuit (using a flip-flop or counter triggered by RD/WR) pulls READY LOW for a programmable number of clocks, then releases it HIGH.

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Aspect | Read Cycle | Write Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **RD pin** | Goes LOW in T2 (active) | Stays HIGH (inactive) |
| **WR pin** | Stays HIGH (inactive) | Goes LOW in T2 (active) |
| **DT/R pin** | LOW (CPU is *receiving*) | HIGH (CPU is *transmitting*) |
| **Data placement on AD15–AD0** | Placed by *external device* in T3 | Placed by *CPU* in T2 |
| **DEN pin** | LOW in T2–T3 (enables transceiver for data flow to CPU) | LOW in T2–T3 (enables transceiver for data flow from CPU) |
| **Wait state sampling** | End of T2 | End of T2 |
| **T-state count** | 4T (or 4T + N·Tw) | 4T (or 4T + N·Tw) |

The common elements in both cycles are: address placement in T1, ALE pulse, M/IO and BHE validity, and READY sampling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing DT/R polarity:** Many students write DT/R = 1 for read and 0 for write. **Correct: DT/R = 1 for WRITE (Transmit), DT/R = 0 for READ (Receive).**
> 2. **Forgetting that DEN is active-LOW in 8086:** It is opposite to 8085's logic.
> 3. **Skipping the data phase in Write cycle:** Some students assume the data is placed on the bus in T1. **Correct: Data is placed on the bus in T2, not T1.**
> 4. **Confusing BHE with A0:** BHE is the **Bus High Enable**, not Bank High Enable. It selects the upper byte D15–D8.
> 5. **Not labeling ALE as active-HIGH:** Writing ALE as negative-edge triggered loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **bus cycle** of the 8086 is **4 T-states** long by default: **T1, T2, T3, T4**.
- Each **T-state** = one clock period = $\dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ seconds.
- **T1** is the **Address Phase** — address placed, ALE pulsed HIGH, BHE and M/IO set.
- **T2** is the **Command Phase** — RD or WR activated, DEN enabled, address removed.
- **T3** is the **Data Phase** — actual data transfer occurs.
- **T4** is the **Termination Phase** — all strobes deactivated, bus returns to high-Z.
- **Wait States (Tw)** are inserted when **READY = 0**, sampled at the end of T2. They extend the cycle to $(4+N)T$.
- **ALE** is **active-HIGH** and demultiplexes the address bus using 74LS373 latches.
- **BHE** (active-LOW) enables the **upper data byte (D15–D8)** for 16-bit transfers.
- **DT/R = 0** for **Read** (CPU receives); **DT/R = 1** for **Write** (CPU transmits).
- **DEN is active-LOW** in the 8086 (unlike 8085's READY which is active-HIGH for the same logical sense).
- **M/IO = 1** means **Memory access**; **M/IO = 0** means **I/O access**.
- The **8086 has a 20-bit address bus** → **1 MB memory space**.
- The **8086 has a 16-bit data bus** → can transfer **2 bytes per bus cycle** in word-aligned accesses.
- **Maximum Mode** uses status signals **S2, S1, S0** decoded by the **8288 bus controller**; timing structure remains identical.
- For wait state calculation: $N_{wait} = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{access} - t_{margin}}{T} \right\rceil$ where $t_{margin}$ accounts for setup times.
- The **8086 pipelining** allows the BIU to fetch the next instruction while the EU executes the current one, but bus cycle timing itself is unchanged.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Design Using 8086
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Design Using 8086

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**System Design Using 8086** refers to the structured hardware engineering methodology of interconnecting the **8086 microprocessor** with external memory devices (RAM/ROM), Input/Output (I/O) peripherals, address latches, data transceivers, and clock generation circuits to form a fully functional microcomputer system. It encompasses two primary operating architectures defined by the MN/MX̅ pin:

1. **Minimum Mode (MN/MX̅ = 1):** A uniprocessor configuration where the 8086 itself generates all control signals (RD̅, WR̅, IO/M̅, INTA̅, ALE, DEN̅, DT/R̅) directly. Suited for single-CPU embedded boards.
2. **Maximum Mode (MN/MX̅ = 0):** A multiprocessor configuration (e.g., with the 8087 NDP or 8089 IOP) where control signals are decoded by an external **Bus Controller IC (8288)** from the status lines S0̅, S1̅, S2̅.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU board examiner expects students to draw **complete system block diagrams**, explain **address decoding** (absolute / linear / block), and solve numerical problems on **memory map calculation** and **chip select logic generation** using combinational logic gates or a **3-to-8 decoder (74LS138)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the 8086 as the **manager of a large office building**:

- **Address Bus (20-bit, A0–A19):** The manager calls out room numbers (each memory location has a unique 20-bit address, allowing 2²⁰ = **1,048,576 = 1 MB** of addressable space).
- **Data Bus (16-bit, AD0–AD15):** Multiplexed with the lower 16 bits of the address (AD0–AD15). Because the pins are shared, the manager needs a **stenographer (Address Latch - 74LS373)** to "remember" the address while the data is being transferred. This is why the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal exists.
- **Control Bus (RD̅, WR̅, IO/M̅):** The "instructions" — read, write, or talk to I/O devices.
- **Address Decoder (74LS138):** The **reception desk** that hears the room number and directs the manager to the correct office (chip) by activating that chip's CS̅ (Chip Select) line.

The minimum mode system is like a **single-manager office**, while the maximum mode is like a **co-managed office** where a deputy (Bus Controller 8288) issues orders on behalf of the manager.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **System Clock Frequency:** Standard 8086 operates at **5 MHz**, 8086-2 at **8 MHz**, 8086-1 at **10 MHz**. (Modern equivalents like V20/V30 can run up to 16 MHz.)
- **Address Bus Width:** **20 bits** → Physical memory space = **1 MB** (00000H to FFFFFH).
- **Data Bus Width:** **16 bits** (multiplexed with A0–A15 on AD0–AD15).
- **Memory Organization:** Divided into **two 512 KB banks**:
  - **Even (Lower) Bank:** Connected to D0–D7, selected by A0 = 0 and BHE̅ = 1.
  - **Odd (Upper) Bank:** Connected to D8–D15, selected by A0 = 1 and BHE̅ = 0.
  - Both banks active (16-bit read/write) when A0 = 0 **and** BHE̅ = 0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Quick Fact:** Always specify the **bus cycle time (T-state count)**. A standard 8086 memory read/write bus cycle is **4 T-states** (4 clock pulses). If wait states are inserted via the **READY** pin, additional T-states (Tw) are appended.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The 8086 system design is built upon **five foundational blocks**. Each must be correctly implemented to pass KTU board examinations:

### Block 1: Clock Generation (8284A)
- The 8086 requires a **33% duty cycle** clock at one-third the crystal frequency.
- The **8284A clock generator** takes a fundamental crystal (e.g., **15 MHz**) and divides it by 3 to produce a **5 MHz** processor clock.
- It also synchronizes the **READY** and **RESET** inputs to the system clock.

### Block 2: Address Latching (74LS373 / 8282)
- Because the lower 16 address lines (AD0–AD15) are **time-multiplexed** with the data bus, they must be demultiplexed at the very beginning of every bus cycle.
- The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal goes HIGH at T1, instructing the transparent latch (74LS373) to capture the address.
- After T1, ALE goes LOW, the latch "freezes" the address, and the pins are reused for data transfer.

### Block 3: Data Bus Buffering (74LS245)
- The 16 data lines must be buffered to provide sufficient drive current (fan-out) for multiple peripherals.
- The **DEN̅ (Data Enable)** and **DT/R̅ (Data Transmit/Receive)** signals control direction:
  - **DT/R̅ = 1:** CPU → Memory/IO (Write).
  - **DT/R̅ = 0:** Memory/IO → CPU (Read).
- A **74LS245** bidirectional transceiver is used, with DIR connected to DT/R̅ and OE̅ connected to DEN̅.

### Block 4: Address Decoding (74LS138 Decoder)
- The 8086 has 20 address lines producing 1 MB of address space.
- Individual memory chips (e.g., 32 KB) cannot cover the whole space, so we must partition memory into **blocks** and enable only the appropriate chip.
- A **74LS138 (3-to-8 line decoder)** is a standard decoder. It takes 3 inputs (A, B, C) and 3 enable pins (G1, G2A̅, G2B̅) and produces 8 active-LOW outputs (Y0̅ to Y7̅).
- Decoding techniques:
  - **Absolute (Full) Decoding:** All 20 address lines are used → unique address for every memory location. No aliasing.
  - **Linear (Partial) Decoding:** Fewer address lines are used → multiple addresses map to the same location (address aliasing). Saves hardware but is non-standard.
  - **Block Decoding:** A combination; groups of memory are decoded together.

### Block 5: Memory & I/O Interfacing
- **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Used to store boot firmware / BIOS. Connected to the upper end of memory (e.g., F0000H–FFFFFH) for the cold-start jump vector (CS:IP = FFFF:0000).
- **RAM (Random Access Memory):** Volatile working memory. Connected to lower addresses.
- **I/O Ports:** 8086 supports **64K I/O ports** (16-bit I/O address) or **32K in 8-bit mapped mode** using the IO/M̅ signal.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Address | $\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$ | Hex | 20-bit computation (always 1 MB max) |
| Memory Size | $2^N$ bytes, where N = number of address lines used | Bytes | Calculate total addressable space |
| Memory Block Size | $\frac{\text{Total Memory}}{2^k}$, where k = number of decoded lines | Bytes | Address range per chip |
| Number of Chips | $\frac{\text{Required Memory}}{\text{Chip Capacity}}$ | Integer | Bill of materials |
| Bank Selection | A0 = 0 → Even Bank, BHE̅ = 0 → Odd Bank | Logic | Critical for 16-bit access |
| 74LS138 Output Active | $\text{Y}\overline{\text{n}} = 0$ when G1=1, G2A=0, G2B=0 and C,B,A match n | Boolean | Generate CS̅ signal |
| 74LS138 Logic | $\overline{\text{Yn}} = \overline{\text{G1} \cdot \overline{\text{G2A}} \cdot \overline{\text{G2B}} \cdot \text{(CBA in binary)}}$ | Boolean | Truth table for chip select |
| Clock Frequency | $f_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{f_{\text{crystal}}}{3}$ | MHz | 8284A divider |
| Bus Cycle T-states | Read/Write = **4T**; with Wait = 4T + nTw | T-states | Determines READY insertion |
| I/O Port Space | $2^{16} = \textbf{65,536}$ (8-bit) or 32K (16-bit) | Ports | I/O map sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** When asked "Design a memory system of X KB using Y KB chips", always (1) calculate the number of chips, (2) draw the chip array, (3) derive the address range, and (4) design the decoder logic. Missing any one step costs 2-3 marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

System design using 8086 is the **bedrock of legacy industrial control**. Even today, **embedded x86 SBCs (Single Board Computers)** in CNC machines, medical infusion pumps, avionics, and POS terminals use 8086-compatible bus architecture. The chip-select decoding technique you learn here directly translates to designing FPGA-based memory controllers and SoC address maps (AXI/AHB bus decoding). Mastering 8086 system design gives you the architectural intuition for any modern processor-based product.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: System Design Decision Tree

The following matrix maps the KTU 2024 expected solution structure for any system design problem:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify total memory requirement and chip capacity | N = Number of chips |
| 2 | Group chips into rows × columns (word/byte organization) | Memory array schematic |
| 3 | Identify the number of address lines required for each chip | $\log_2(\text{chip capacity})$ |
| 4 | Determine the number of address lines available for decoding | $20 - \text{(chip lines)} - 1$ (for A0/BHE̅) |
| 5 | Choose decoder (74LS138) and map each chip's CS̅ to a Yn̅ output | CS̅ equations |
| 6 | Compute the starting and ending address of each block | Hex range |
| 7 | Validate that no two chips overlap and that the entire 1 MB is logically partitioned | Verification |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation: KTU Board-Type Problem

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2024 Pattern):**
Design a memory system for an 8086-based microcomputer with the following specifications:
- 32 KB ROM using 8 KB chips.
- 64 KB RAM using 16 KB chips.
- ROM must start at physical address **E0000H**.
- Use a 74LS138 decoder.

**Step 1: Count the Number of Chips**

For ROM (Read-Only Memory):
$$\text{Number of ROM chips} = \frac{\text{Total ROM size}}{\text{Chip size}} = \frac{32 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

For RAM (Random Access Memory):
$$\text{Number of RAM chips} = \frac{\text{Total RAM size}}{\text{Chip size}} = \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

> **Logic Note:** Since the 8086 has a 16-bit data bus, in real KTU board problems we often pair two 8-bit chips (one for even bank, one for odd bank). For simplicity in this derivation, we assume 8-bit wide chips arranged in pairs to form 16-bit words.

**Step 2: Compute Address Lines Per Chip**

For 8 KB chips:
$$8 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 \text{ bytes} = 2^{13} \text{ bytes}$$
So each 8 KB chip requires **13 address lines** (A0–A12).

For 16 KB chips:
$$16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ bytes} = 2^{14} \text{ bytes}$$
So each 16 KB chip requires **14 address lines** (A0–A13).

**Step 3: Identify Lines Available for Decoding**

Total address lines on the 8086: **20** (A0–A19).

For 8 KB chips (13 address lines used for internal memory):
$$\text{Decoding lines} = 20 - 13 = 7 \text{ lines (A13–A19)}$$
These 7 lines can be fed to the decoder (only 3 used for 74LS138, others as enables).

For 16 KB chips (14 address lines used for internal memory):
$$\text{Decoding lines} = 20 - 14 = 6 \text{ lines (A14–A19)}$$

**Step 4: Establish the Starting Address for ROM**

ROM starts at **E0000H**. Convert to binary to identify the A19–A13 pattern:

$$\text{E0000H} = 1110\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2$$

In binary grouping (5-bit segments):
$$\text{E} = 1110$$
$$\text{0} = 0000$$
$$\text{0} = 0000$$
$$\text{0} = 0000$$
$$\text{0} = 0000$$

So the top 7 lines **A19 A18 A17 A16 A15 A14 A13** = **1 1 1 0 0 0 0** for the start of ROM.

**Step 5: Define the ROM Address Map**

Since each chip is 8 KB, the addresses for the 4 ROM chips are:

| Chip | A19 A18 A17 A16 A15 A14 A13 | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|
| ROM1 | 1 1 1 0 0 0 0 | E0000H – E1FFFH |
| ROM2 | 1 1 1 0 0 0 1 | E2000H – E3FFFH |
| ROM3 | 1 1 1 0 0 1 0 | E4000H – E5FFFH |
| ROM4 | 1 1 1 0 0 1 1 | E6000H – E7FFFH |

(End of 32 KB ROM block: **E7FFFH**.)

**Step 6: Define the RAM Address Map**

Let us place the 64 KB RAM immediately after the ROM (at E8000H), or alternatively at a typical location like **00000H**. Placing it at 00000H (simpler):

| Chip | A19 A18 A17 A16 A15 A14 | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|
| RAM1 | 0 0 0 0 0 0 | 00000H – 03FFFH |
| RAM2 | 0 0 0 0 0 1 | 04000H – 07FFFH |
| RAM3 | 0 0 0 0 1 0 | 08000H – 0BFFFH |
| RAM4 | 0 0 0 0 1 1 | 0C000H – 0FFFFH |

**Step 7: Generate the Chip Select (CS̅) Equations using 74LS138**

The 74LS138 has inputs **C, B, A** (with C = MSB) and outputs **Y0̅ to Y7̅**. We connect:
- C = A19, B = A18, A = A17
- G1 = +5V (always enabled)
- G2A̅ = A16, G2B̅ = IO/M̅ = 1 (memory operation, M̅ = 0 → G2B̅ = 0; in 8086 IO/M̅ = 1 means I/O, so for memory we tie IO/M̅ = 0 to G2B̅ to enable ONLY during memory ops)

Let us assume we use only the upper 3 bits (A19, A18, A17) for the C, B, A inputs:

- **For ROM1–ROM4 (E0000H–E7FFFH):** A19A18A17 = **111**. This selects Y7̅ of 74LS138-1.
- **For RAM1–RAM4 (00000H–0FFFFH):** A19A18A17 = **000**. This selects Y0̅ of 74LS138-1.

For fine-grained decoding within each group, we use the next 3 bits (A16, A15, A14) on a second 74LS138 to decode the 8 sub-chips.

**CS̅ Equations for the Second-Level Decoder (74LS138-2) driven by A16, A15, A14:**

| Chip | A16 A15 A14 | Y Output Active | CS̅ Connection |
|---|---|---|---|
| ROM1 | 0 0 0 | Y0̅ | CS̅ of ROM1 |
| ROM2 | 0 0 1 | Y1̅ | CS̅ of ROM2 |
| ROM3 | 0 1 0 | Y2̅ | CS̅ of ROM3 |
| ROM4 | 0 1 1 | Y3̅ | CS̅ of ROM4 |
| RAM1 | 0 0 0 | Y0̅ | CS̅ of RAM1 |
| RAM2 | 0 0 1 | Y1̅ | CS̅ of RAM2 |
| RAM3 | 0 1 0 | Y2̅ | CS̅ of RAM3 |
| RAM4 | 0 1 1 | Y3̅ | CS̅ of RAM4 |

**Final Boolean CS̅ Expression (KTU 2024 expects this exact form):**

$$\overline{\text{CS}_{\text{ROM1}}} = \overline{\text{Y7̅}_{\text{decoder1}}} \cdot \overline{\text{Y0̅}_{\text{decoder2}}}$$

$$\overline{\text{CS}_{\text{ROM1}}} = \overline{(A19 \cdot A18 \cdot A17)} \cdot \overline{(\overline{A16} \cdot \overline{A15} \cdot \overline{A14})}$$

> **Logic Note:** The double-bar indicates the CS̅ is active LOW. KTU expects the bar over each Y̅ output to be clearly drawn.

**Step 8: Verification of Memory Map**

Total ROM (E0000H–E7FFFH) = 8 K × 4 = 32 KB ✓
Total RAM (00000H–0FFFFH) = 16 K × 4 = 64 KB ✓
Total system = 96 KB out of 1 MB maximum ✓
No overlap detected ✓

This completes the rigorous KTU-pattern system design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: 8086 Minimum Mode System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    node1[Crystal 15 MHz]
    node2["8284A Clock Generator"]
    node3["8086 CPU CLK input"]
    node4["74LS373 Address Latch x3"]
    node5["74LS245 Data Transceiver"]
    node6["74LS138 Decoder Upper A19-A17"]
    node7["74LS138 Decoder Lower A16-A14"]
    node8["ROM Bank 32 KB E0000H-E7FFFH"]
    node9["RAM Bank 64 KB 00000H-0FFFFH"]
    node10["I/O Ports 64K"]
    node11[Control Bus RD WR IO-M]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 -- 5 MHz CLK --> node3
    node3 -- AD0-AD15 multiplexed --> node4
    node3 -- AD0-AD15 data --> node5
    node3 -- A16-A19 --> node6
    node4 -- A0-A15 latched --> node7
    node6 -- Y0-Y7 select --> node7
    node7 -- CS signals --> node8
    node7 -- CS signals --> node9
    node3 -- RD WR IO-M --> node11
    node11 --> node8
    node11 --> node9
    node11 --> node10
    node5 -- 16-bit data --> node8
    node5 -- 16-bit data --> node9
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Memory Map Layout (Hex Address Allocation)

```mermaid
graph TB
    blockFF[FFFFFH: Top of 1 MB Memory]
    blockF0[F0000H-FFFFFH: System BIOS 64 KB]
    blockE0[E0000H-E7FFFH: User ROM 32 KB]
    blockD0[D0000H-DFFFFH: Reserved 64 KB]
    blockA0[A0000H-AFFFFH: Video RAM 64 KB]
    block9[00000H-9FFFFH: User RAM 640 KB]
    block00[00000H: Cold Reset Vector CS:IP FFFF:0000H]

    blockFF --> blockF0
    blockF0 --> blockE0
    blockE0 --> blockD0
    blockD0 --> blockA0
    blockA0 --> block9
    block9 --> block00
```

> **Explanation:** This is the standard IBM PC-compatible memory map. KTU problems may ask you to compute the "gap" between two chips or verify that an address is in ROM or RAM.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Address Decoding Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A19[A19] --> DEC1C[74LS138 C input]
    A18[A18] --> DEC1B[74LS138 B input]
    A17[A17] --> DEC1A[74LS138 A input]
    DEC1[74LS138 Primary Decoder]
    DEC1C --> DEC1
    DEC1B --> DEC1
    DEC1A --> DEC1
    DEC1 -- Y0 00000H-1FFFFH --> RAM[RAM CS Active]
    DEC1 -- Y1 20000H-3FFFFH --> RAM2[RAM2 CS Active]
    DEC1 -- Y7 E0000H-FFFFF --> ROM[ROM CS Active]
    DEC1 -- Y2-Y6 --> NULL[Unused / Reserved]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Read Bus Cycle T-State Timing (4T)

```mermaid
graph TB
    T1["T1: ALE HIGH, AD0-AD15 carries Address, A16-A19 valid"]
    T2["T2: ALE LOW, AD0-AD15 goes High-Z, RD goes LOW"]
    T3["T3: Memory places data on bus, CPU samples READY"]
    T4["T4: CPU latches data, RD goes HIGH, bus cycle ends"]
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
```

> **Note on Wait States:** If READY = 0 at the end of T3, the CPU inserts **Tw (Wait States)** until READY = 1. KTU frequently asks: "How many wait states are needed for a 600 ns memory in an 8086 at 5 MHz?" Answer: $T = 200 \text{ ns}$, so we need $\lceil 600/200 \rceil = 3$ wait states.
