The total number of unique combinations (and hence unique addresses) is calculated by the fundamental counting principle. **Step 1:** Identify the number of independent address lines. $$n = 20 \text{ lines}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the base-2 combinatorial formula. Each line contributes a factor of 2 choices, so the total address space is: $$N_{\text{addresses}} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots \text{ (20 times)} = 2^{20}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the power of 2. $$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ unique byte addresses}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to conventional units (1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes). $$N_{\text{addresses}} = 1 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 5:** Account for the data bus width. If 16 bits are accessed per cycle, the total bit capacity is: $$C_{\text{bits}} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 16 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ Mbit} = 2 \text{ MB}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Memory Bandwidth Calculation **Given:** 8086 system with clock frequency $f_{\text{CLK}} = 5 \text{ MHz}$ and data bus width $w = 16 \text{ bits}$. **Step 1:** Identify the formula for theoretical maximum memory bandwidth. $$BW = f_{\text{CLK}} \times w$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known values. $$BW = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} \times 16 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3:** Perform the multiplication. $$BW = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/second} = 80 \text{ Mbit/s}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to bytes per second for clarity. $$BW = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{8} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/second} = 10 \text{ MB/s}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the *theoretical maximum*. In practice, due to bus cycle overhead (T1, T2, T4 idle states) and the fact that 8086 uses 4 clock cycles per bus cycle, the **effective throughput** is closer to one transfer per ~800 ns. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effective Transfer Rate (with 4T Bus Cycle) **Step 1:** Determine the bus cycle period. A standard 8086 bus cycle consumes 4 clock states (T1, T2, T3, T4) for a zero-wait-state memory access. $$T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times T_{\text{CLK}} = 4 \times \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the clock frequency. $$T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 800 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the number of bus cycles per second. $$N_{\text{cycles}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{1}{800 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.25 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles/s}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply by data transferred per cycle (16 bits = 2 bytes). $$R_{\text{effective}} = 1.25 \times 10^6 \times 2 = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} = 2.5 \text{ MB/s}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Address Calculation for Physical Memory The 8086 uses **segmented memory addressing**. A physical address is calculated as: **Step 1:** Write the standard formula. $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Base} \times 10_H) + \text{Offset}$$ The shift by $10_H$ (i.e., multiplication by 16) occurs because the segment base is left-shifted by 4 bits. **Step 2:** Worked example. Suppose CS = $2000_H$ and IP = $4530_H$. $$\text{PA} = (2000_H \times 10_H) + 4530_H$$ **Step 3:** Compute $2000_H \times 10_H$. $$2000_H \times 10_H = 20000_H$$ **Step 4:** Add the offset. $$\text{PA} = 20000_H + 4530_H = 24530_H$$ **Step 5:** Verify no segment boundary crossing (offset $< 10^4_H$): $4530_H < 10000_H$ ✓ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: 8086 Bus Signals Summary | Pin | Type | Direction | Function | Active Level | |-----|------|-----------|----------|--------------| | $AD_{15}$–$AD_0$ | Address/Data | Bidirectional | Multiplexed lower address + data | — | | $A_{19}$–$A_{16}/S_6$–$S_3$ | Address/Status | Output (address) / Output (status) | Upper address in T1, status in T2-T4 | — | | $\overline{BHE}/S_7$ | Control/Status | Output | Bus High Enable in T1, status in T2-T4 | Active LOW | | $\overline{RD}$ | Control | Output | Read strobe to memory/I/O | Active LOW | | $\overline{WR}$ | Control | Output | Write strobe to memory/I/O | Active LOW | | $M/\overline{IO}$ | Control | Output | 1 = Memory access, 0 = I/O access | Active HIGH/LOW | | $ALE$ | Control | Output | Address Latch Enable (latches address on H→L) | HIGH pulse in T1 | | $DT/\overline{R}$ | Control | Output | Data Transmit (1) / Receive (0) | — | | $\overline{DEN}$ | Control | Output | Data Enable (activates external bus transceiver) | Active LOW | | $READY$ | Control | Input | Insert wait states when LOW | Active HIGH | | $INTR$ | Control | Input | Maskable Interrupt Request | Active HIGH | | $NMI$ | Control | Input | Non-Maskable Interrupt | Rising edge | | $\overline{TEST}$ | Control | Input | Wait for coprocessor (8087) | Active LOW | | $HOLD$, $HLDA$ | Control | I/O | DMA request/acknowledge | HIGH/HIGH | | $CLK$ | Clock | Input | System clock (33% duty cycle for 8086) | — | | $RESET$ | Control | Input | Reset CPU (CS=$FFFF$, IP=$0000$) | HIGH (min 4 CLK) | | $MN/\overline{MX}$ | Configuration | Input | Minimum/Maximum mode select | — | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: 8086 System Bus Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["8086 CPU
ALU + Registers + CU"] ABUS["20-bit Address Bus
A0 to A19"] DBUS["16-bit Data Bus
D0 to D15"] CBUS["Control Bus
RD WR M/IO ALE BHE DEN"] LATCH["74LS373 Latch
Demultiplexer"] MEM["Memory Unit
1 MB Byte-Addressable
ROM + RAM Banks"] IO["I/O Devices
64 KB I/O Space
8255 PPI + 8253 Timer"] DECODER["Address Decoder
74LS138 3-to-8"] CPU -- "T1: Address out" --> ABUS ABUS --> LATCH LATCH -- "Latched A0-A19" --> MEM ABUS --> DECODER DECODER -- "Chip Select Signals" --> MEM DECODER -- "Chip Select Signals" --> IO CPU -- "T2-T4: Data in/out" --> DBUS DBUS <--> MEM DBUS <--> IO CPU --> CBUS CBUS -- "RD, WR, M/IO" --> MEM CBUS -- "RD, WR, M/IO" --> IO LATCH -- "ALE control" --> CPU ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 8086 Bus Cycle Timing (Read Operation) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["T1 State
Address placed on AD bus
ALE goes HIGH
BHE and A19-A16 driven"] T2["T2 State
AD bus floated
DTR set to receive
RD goes LOW
DEN goes LOW"] T3["T3 State
Data sampled at trailing edge
RD remains LOW
Memory drives AD bus"] T4["T4 State
Data latched into CPU
RD goes HIGH
DEN goes HIGH
ALE stays LOW"] TW["Tw State Optional
READY LOW
Inserts wait state"] T1 --> T2 T2 --> TW TW --> T3 T3 --> T4 T4 --> NEXT["Next Bus Cycle or Idle"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Address/Data Demultiplexing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB MUX["Multiplexed Pins
AD0-AD15 carry
Address in T1
Data in T2-T4"] LATCHIN["Inputs to 74LS373"] ENABLE["ALE Signal from 8086
HIGH in T1, falls at end of T1"] CAPTURE["Falling edge of ALE
latches address"] LATCHOUT["Latched Outputs
A0-A15 held stable"] DATTX["From T2 onwards
AD pins become D0-D15"] MUX --> LATCHIN ENABLE --> LATCHIN LATCHIN --> CAPTURE CAPTURE --> LATCHOUT MUX --> DATTX LATCHOUT --> MEMDECOD["Memory Address Decoder"] DATTX --> MEMDATA["Memory Data Lines"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Bank Organization (8086) ```mermaid flowchart TB BANKL["Even Address Bank
8-bit wide
D0-D7 connected
Selected when A0 = 0"] BANKH["Odd Address Bank
8-bit wide
D8-D15 connected
Selected when BHE = 0"] COMBINED["Full 16-bit Word Access
A0 = 0 AND BHE = 0
Both banks active
One bus cycle"] LOWERONLY["8-bit Lower Access
A0 = 0, BHE = 1
Even bank only"] UPPERONLY["8-bit Upper Access
A0 = 1, BHE = 0
Odd bank only
Takes 2 bus cycles"] BANKL --> COMBINED BANKH --> COMBINED BANKL --> LOWERONLY BANKH --> UPPERONLY ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the three-bus architecture of the 8086 microprocessor with neat diagrams.** **Model Answer:** The 8086 microprocessor employs three distinct bus groups for communication: 1. **Address Bus (20-bit):** Lines $A_0$–$A_{19}$ carry the physical address of the memory or I/O location to be accessed. It is **unidirectional** (output only from CPU) and provides an addressable range of $2^{20} = 1 \text{ MB}$ of byte-addressable memory. 2. **Data Bus (16-bit):** Lines $D_0$–$D_{15}$ transfer actual data to/from the CPU. It is **bidirectional** and uses tri-state buffers to allow multiple devices to share the bus. A 16-bit data bus enables one word transfer per bus cycle. 3. **Control Bus:** A collection of individual signal lines ($\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $ALE$, $\overline{BHE}$, $DT/\overline{R}$, $\overline{DEN}$, $READY$, etc.) that synchronize and direct bus operations. They determine *when* and *what kind* of transfer occurs (read/write, memory/I/O, byte/word). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The 8086 uses a **multiplexed bus** — $AD_0$–$AD_{15}$ pins carry both address and data at different time slots (T1 vs T2–T4), reducing pin count from 36 to 16 for the address+data bus. An external 74LS373 latch with the ALE signal demultiplexes the address. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the function of the ALE and $\overline{BHE}$ signals in the 8086?** **Model Answer:** **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** This is an output signal from the 8086 that goes HIGH during the T1 state of every bus cycle. The falling edge of ALE is used by an external address latch (74LS373) to capture the address present on the multiplexed address/data bus. Without ALE-controlled demultiplexing, the address would be lost as soon as T2 begins (when the same pins switch to data mode). **$\overline{BHE}$ (Bus High Enable):** This is an active-LOW output signal that, when LOW, enables the upper 8 bits ($D_8$–$D_{15}$) of the data bus — the "odd" memory bank. Combined with $A_0$, four transfer combinations are possible: | $\overline{BHE}$ | $A_0$ | Function | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | Whole 16-bit word (both banks) | | 0 | 1 | Upper byte from/to odd bank | | 1 | 0 | Lower byte from/to even bank | | 1 | 1 | No operation (bus idle) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **system bus structure of the 8086 microprocessor**. List the functions of any **eight control signals**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **total memory addressable by 8086 in bytes and bits**. If the clock frequency is **10 MHz**, compute the **theoretical bandwidth** and **effective transfer rate** assuming a 4T bus cycle. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a)** *[Refer to Section 4.1 mermaid diagram for visual reference]* The 8086 system bus consists of three groups: 1. **Address Bus (20 lines):** $A_0$–$A_{19}$ carry the physical memory address. Unidirectional output from the CPU. 2. **Data Bus (16 lines):** $D_0$–$D_{15}$ carry data bidirectionally. Multiplexed with lower address lines ($AD_0$–$AD_{15}$). 3. **Control Bus:** Coordinates the operation. **Functions of Eight Control Signals:** - **$\overline{RD}$ (Read):** Active LOW output. When asserted, the selected memory/I/O device places data on the bus. - **$\overline{WR}$ (Write):** Active LOW output. CPU writes data onto the bus to the selected device. - **$M/\overline{IO}$:** Output signal. HIGH = memory access; LOW = I/O access. - **$ALE$ (Address Latch Enable):** HIGH pulse in T1; falling edge latches address externally. - **$\overline{BHE}/S_7$:** Bus High Enable; enables upper data byte ($D_8$–$D_{15}$). - **$DT/\overline{R}$ (Data Transmit/Receive):** Controls direction of external bus transceiver (HIGH = CPU sending, LOW = CPU receiving). - **$\overline{DEN}$ (Data Enable):** Active LOW; enables external data transceivers. - **$READY$:** Input. If LOW, the CPU inserts wait states (Tw) to accommodate slow peripherals. **(b) Memory and Bandwidth Calculations:** **Step 1: Total addressable memory in bytes** $$M = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 2: Total memory in bits (assuming full 16-bit data words)** $$C_{\text{bits}} = 2^{20} \times 16 = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ Mbit} = 2 \text{ MB}$$ **Step 3: Clock period at 10 MHz** $$T_{\text{CLK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ ns}$$ **Step 4: Theoretical Bandwidth** $$BW = f_{\text{CLK}} \times w = 10 \times 10^6 \times 16 = 160 \text{ Mbit/s} = 20 \text{ MB/s}$$ **Step 5: Effective Transfer Rate (4T bus cycle)** $$T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times T_{\text{CLK}} = 4 \times 100 \text{ ns} = 400 \text{ ns}$$ $$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{16 \text{ bits}}{400 \text{ ns}} = \frac{16}{400 \times 10^{-9}} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} = 5 \text{ MB/s}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Module Choice] **(a)** Explain the **concept of multiplexed address/data bus** in 8086. How is the address demultiplexed using external hardware? Describe the role of the **74LS373 latch and ALE signal** with a timing diagram description. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An 8086 system operates at a clock frequency of **5 MHz**. If the CPU accesses memory in **word (16-bit) mode**, calculate: (i) the **T-state duration**, (ii) the **bus cycle duration** (4T), (iii) **wait states needed** if a memory device has access time of **450 ns**, and (iv) the **effective data throughput** in bytes/sec. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Multiplexed Bus & Demultiplexing:** The 8086 has 16 pins labeled $AD_0$–$AD_{15}$ that serve **dual purposes** — they carry the lower 16 bits of the address during the T1 state of a bus cycle, and then the 16-bit data during the T2–T4 states. Similarly, pins $A_{16}/S_3$, $A_{17}/S_4$, $A_{18}/S_5$, $A_{19}/S_6$, and $\overline{BHE}/S_7$ share address and status functions. **Why multiplexing?** It reduces pin count. A non-multiplexed 8086 would need $20 + 16 = 36$ pins just for address+data, exceeding the practical DIP-40 package limit. **Demultiplexing Process:** 1. During **T1**, the 8086 places the 20-bit address on the multiplexed lines ($AD_0$–$AD_{15}$, $A_{16}$–$A_{19}$). 2. The **ALE** signal goes HIGH, opening the transparent latch inputs of the **74LS373** (an 8-bit D-type transparent latch). 3. On the **falling edge of ALE** (end of T1), the latch captures and holds the address stable at its outputs. 4. From **T2 onwards**, the AD pins are free to carry data, but the latched address remains available to memory and decoders throughout the cycle. **Timing Sequence:** | State | AD0-AD15 | ALE | 74LS373 Output | BHE/A16-A19 | |-------|----------|-----|----------------|-------------| | T1 | Address (lower 16) | HIGH | Address | Address | | T1→T2 | — | Falling edge | Latches | — | | T2 | Floating | LOW | Held address | Status | | T3-T4 | Data (16-bit) | LOW | Held address | Status | **(b) Numerical Calculations:** **Given:** $f_{\text{CLK}} = 5 \text{ MHz}$, 16-bit data bus, memory access time = 450 ns. **(i) T-state duration:** $$T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 200 \text{ ns}$$ **(ii) Bus cycle duration (4T):** $$T_{\text{bus}} = 4 \times 200 = 800 \text{ ns}$$ **(iii) Wait states calculation:** The memory device requires 450 ns to respond. The standard bus cycle allocates data sampling near the end of T3 (after 3T = 600 ns of address + ALE setup). However, the critical constraint is the **time from address valid to data valid**. Standard 8086 bus cycle without wait states provides data ready time of: $$T_{\text{ready}} = 3 \times 200 = 600 \text{ ns (from T1 start)}$$ Wait states needed: $$n = \left\lceil \frac{T_{\text{memory}} - T_{\text{available}}}{T_{\text{CLK}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{450 - 600}{200} \right\rceil$$ Since 450 < 600, the memory is fast enough → **0 wait states required**. > [!NOTE] > If memory access time were, e.g., 850 ns, then $n = \lceil (850-600)/200 \rceil = \lceil 1.25 \rceil = 2$ wait states. **(iv) Effective throughput:** With 0 wait states, each bus cycle transfers 2 bytes (16 bits) in 800 ns. $$R_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2 \text{ bytes}}{800 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} = 2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s} = 2.5 \text{ MB/s}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "1 MB memory" with "1 Mbit memory":** 8086 addresses **1 MB (MegaBYTE)**, not 1 Mb. The total bit capacity is **16 Mbit** (or **2 MB logical**). > > 2. **Forgetting demultiplexing:** A diagram of the 8086 bus *without* the 74LS373 latch and ALE signal is incomplete. Examiners expect this demultiplexing arrangement in any full marks answer. > > 3. **Confusing $A_0$ with byte-enable logic:** $\overline{BHE}$ is what enables the upper byte, not $A_{19}$. $A_0$ enables the lower byte. > > 4. **Stating bus width = memory size:** Memory size = $2^n$ locations, not $n$ bytes. The 20-bit bus = **1,048,576 locations**, not 20 bytes. > > 5. **Mixing up "word" and "byte":** 8086 word = 16 bits. 1 word = 2 bytes. > > 6. **Skipping T-state timing:** When asked for "bandwidth," always specify whether it's **theoretical** ($f \times w$) or **effective** (accounting for bus cycle overhead). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Buses:** Address (unidirectional, 20-bit), Data (bidirectional, 16-bit), Control (mixed, ~16 signals). - **8086 Addressing:** 20-bit bus → $2^{20} = \mathbf{1{,}048{,}576}$ byte addresses = **1 MB** byte-addressable memory. - **Multiplexing:** $AD_0$–$AD_{15}$ carry address in T1 and data in T2–T4. - **Demultiplexing Latch:** **74LS373** (octal transparent latch) controlled by **ALE** (Address Latch Enable). - **$\overline{BHE}$:** Active-LOW signal enables upper 8-bit data bus ($D_8$–$D_{15}$). - **$M/\overline{IO}$:** HIGH = memory access; LOW = I/O access. - **I/O Space:** Separate 8-bit (or 16-bit) port addressing space of $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$. - **Bus Cycle:** 4 clock states (T1, T2, T3, T4); T1 = address phase, T2–T4 = data phase. - **Wait States ($T_w$):** Inserted when READY is held LOW to slow memory access. - **Tri-state Buffers:** Used on data bus to allow multiple devices to share lines without contention. - **Memory Banks:** 8086 organizes memory into **even bank** (D0–D7, selected by $A_0=0$) and **odd bank** (D8–D15, selected by $\overline{BHE}=0$). - **Bandwidth Formula:** $BW = f_{\text{CLK}} \times w$ (theoretical); $R_{\text{eff}} = w / T_{\text{cycle}}$ (effective). - **Minimum vs Maximum Mode:** Pin $MN/\overline{MX}$ selects mode; minimum mode is for single-CPU systems (8086 alone), maximum mode is for multiprocessor (8086 + 8087 + 8089). - **Reset Vector:** On RESET, CS=$FFFF_H$, IP=$0000_H$ → first instruction fetched from physical address $FFFF0_H$ (last 16 bytes of 1 MB memory). --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — System Bus Structure** > *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OECIT615 syllabus, mapped to Course Outcomes CO2 and Bloom's Taxonomy Levels L2–L4.*

topics.title: Multiprocessor Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessor Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Multiprocessor Configurations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiprocessor Configuration** refers to a system architecture in which **two or more processors** (such as a CPU and a specialized co-processor) operate cooperatively while sharing a **common system bus, memory, and I/O resources**. In the Intel 8086 family, this involves pairing the **8086 CPU** with specialized support processors — most notably the **Intel 8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP)** and the **Intel 8089 I/O Processor (IOP)** — to offload specific computational or I/O-intensive tasks.

The 8086 microprocessor, despite being a powerful 16-bit processor, has inherent limitations in handling **floating-point arithmetic** and **high-speed I/O transfers**. Multiprocessor configurations overcome these limitations by allowing **dedicated processors** to function alongside the host 8086, communicating through shared buses and a coordinated handshaking protocol.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen.
> - The **Head Chef (8086 CPU)** takes customer orders, decides the menu, and coordinates the kitchen.
> - The **Pastry Chef (8087 NDP)** specializes only in desserts and complex baking — tasks the head chef would otherwise struggle with.
> - The **Dishwasher / Supply Manager (8089 IOP)** handles the cleaning and bringing in raw materials — repetitive, time-consuming I/O tasks.
>
> All three work in the **same kitchen (common system bus and memory)**, but each has a **specialized role**. The head chef never wastes time washing dishes, just as the 8086 never wastes cycles on I/O housekeeping.

This is the essence of multiprocessing — **task specialization under shared resources**.

### 1.3 Key Participating Processors (Intel Family)

| Processor | Full Name | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|
| **8086** | 16-bit Host CPU | Main control, instruction fetch, integer arithmetic |
| **8087** | Numeric Data Processor (NDP) | Floating-point, trigonometric, logarithmic math |
| **8089** | I/O Processor (IOP) | Direct memory access, channel-based I/O control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 8087 is classified as a **Coprocessor** (tightly coupled, instruction-level coupling), while the 8089 is classified as an **I/O Processor** (loosely coupled, bus-master capable).

### 1.4 Two Fundamental Categories of Multiprocessor Systems

1. **Closely Coupled Configuration** — The 8086 + 8087 pair, sharing the **same bus interface logic, clock, and memory**. The 8087 monitors the 8086 instruction stream passively.
2. **Loosely Coupled Configuration** — The 8086 + 8089 pair, each acting as an **independent bus master**, arbitrating access to the shared system bus through hardware arbitration pins (e.g., `RQ/` and `GT`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Closely Coupled Configuration (8086 + 8087)

### 2.1.1 Architectural Philosophy

In a closely coupled system, the **8087 NDP** is *not* a standalone processor. It is a **passive monitor** of the 8086's instruction queue. The 8086 and 8087 share:

- The **same system bus**
- The **same clock generator (8284A)**
- The **same address/data bus interface logic (8288)**
- The **same memory and I/O space**

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic Flow (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1:** The 8086 fetches instructions from memory into its **6-byte prefetch queue**.
- **Step 2:** The 8087 simultaneously monitors the **data bus** for opcodes that begin with the **ESCAPE (ESC)** sequence — instructions with the bit pattern `11011xxx`.
- **Step 3:** When the 8087 detects an ESC opcode, it executes the floating-point instruction in **parallel** with the 8086.
- **Step 4:** The 8086 proceeds to fetch the next instruction (unless it is `WAIT`).
- **Step 5:** A `WAIT` instruction in the 8086 program forces synchronization — the 8086 enters an idle state until the 8087 raises its **`BUSY` signal HIGH** (signaling completion).
- **Step 6:** The 8087 may raise **`INT` (interrupt request)** to inform the host of exceptional results (e.g., divide-by-zero, overflow, invalid operation).

### 2.1.3 Critical Handshaking Signals

| Signal | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `BUSY` | 8087 → 8086 | Active LOW = 8087 is executing; tested by `WAIT` |
| `INT` | 8087 → 8086 | Interrupt request from 8087 for exception handling |
| `RQ/` and `GT` (RQB/GTB) | Bidirectional | Request/Grant bus access for temporary bus control |
| `TEST` | 8086 → 8087 | Tested by `WAIT` instruction; must be LOW for 8086 to proceed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without the `WAIT` + `BUSY` protocol, the 8086 might read a memory operand *before* the 8087 has finished processing it — a classic race condition. The KTU examiner frequently tests this synchronization logic.

### 2.1.4 The 8087 Data Types — Performance Metrics

The 8087 supports **seven data types**, with the following key parameters:

| Data Type | Bits | Significant Digits | Range (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Word Integer | 16 | 4 | $-32,768$ to $+32,767$ |
| Short Integer | 32 | 9 | $\approx \pm 2 \times 10^9$ |
| Long Integer | 64 | 19 | $\approx \pm 9 \times 10^{18}$ |
| Packed BCD | 80 | 18 | $\pm 10^{4932}$ |
| Short Real | 32 | 7 | $\approx \pm 10^{\pm 38}$ |
| Long Real | 64 | 16 | $\approx \pm 10^{\pm 308}$ |
| Temporary Real | 80 | 19 | $\approx \pm 10^{\pm 4932}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **80-bit Temporary Real** format is the 8087's internal working precision — all operations are performed at this extended precision, then rounded to the destination format.

## 2.2 Loosely Coupled Configuration (8086 + 8089)

### 2.2.1 Architectural Philosophy

In a loosely coupled configuration, the 8089 IOP is a **fully independent bus master** with its own channel program execution capability. The 8086 and 8089 communicate via **shared memory** (mailbox-based handshaking).

### 2.2.2 Key Structural Differences

- The 8089 has its **own internal bus arbitration logic** and can take control of the system bus.
- The 8086 sends an **IOP initialization message** to a fixed memory location, which the 8089 reads to determine its task.
- Two 8089s can be used simultaneously — one on the **local bus** (with 8086), one on the **system bus** — acting as a bridge.

### 2.2.3 Request/Grant Protocol (RQ/GT)

The 8086 has two dedicated pins: **`RQ/`/ `GT_0` and `RQ/`/ `GT_1`**. The 8089 requests the bus via a single line that carries **pulsed** request and grant signals:

1. **8089 pulls line LOW** → Bus request pulse (1 clock)
2. **8086 grants** by sending a LOW pulse on the same line (1 clock)
3. **8089 acknowledges** with another LOW pulse (1 clock) → bus ownership transferred
4. **8089 releases bus** by sending a final LOW pulse → ownership returned to 8086

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet — Multiprocessor Timing & Bus Equations**

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum system bus frequency ($f_{\text{bus}}$) | $f_{\text{bus}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | MHz |
| Memory operand access time ($T_{\text{acc}}$) | $T_{\text{acc}} = k \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ where $k$ = wait states + base cycles | ns / cycles |
| Effective CPU throughput (with coprocessor) | $\eta_{\text{eff}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{WAIT}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8087 relative performance (vs 8086) | $S_{87} = \dfrac{T_{86}}{T_{87}}$ (typically 50–100× for FP) | ratio |
| 8089 DMA transfer rate (maximum) | $R_{\text{IOP}} = \dfrac{B_{\text{burst}}}{T_{\text{cycle}} \cdot N_{\text{cycles}}}$ | bytes/s |
| 8087 bus request pulse width | $t_{\text{RQ}} = 1 \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ | ns |
| 8086 prefetch queue size | $Q_{86} = 6$ bytes | bytes |
| 8088 prefetch queue size | $Q_{88} = 4$ bytes | bytes |

**Note on notation:** In the table above, $\vert$ symbols (e.g., absolute value) are rendered using the standard $\vert$ delimiter for clarity in KTU exam scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Multiprocessor Configurations Are Used Today:**
> - **Scientific computing** (CAD/CAM, weather modeling) — legacy of 8087 NDP designs.
> - **Embedded control systems** — modern descendants include SoCs with dedicated **DSP cores** paired with general-purpose **ARM Cortex-A** cores (the architectural philosophy is identical).
> - **GPU-CPU heterogeneous computing** — modern GPUs are the spiritual successors of the 8089 IOP: specialized processors that handle parallel, repetitive tasks while the CPU orchestrates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 8086 + 8087 Synchronization Delay Analysis

When the 8086 encounters a floating-point instruction, the synchronization overhead can be derived. Let us denote:

- $T_c$ = clock cycle time
- $T_f$ = 8087 floating-point operation time
- $T_w$ = 8086 `WAIT` instruction cycles
- $T_m$ = memory operand fetch time

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the WAIT Synchronization Delay

$$
T_{\text{WAIT}} = T_w \cdot T_c
$$

This is the **base WAIT instruction execution time** (each `WAIT` instruction occupies 3 clock cycles minimum and continuously tests the `TEST` pin until the 8087 de-asserts `BUSY`).

$$
T_{\text{sync}} = n \cdot T_{\text{WAIT}} + T_m
$$

Where $n$ is the number of `WAIT` instructions inserted by the assembler (typically $n=1$ per ESC sequence). $T_m$ represents the **memory operand access delay** — the time for the 8087 to read/write the floating-point operand from memory.

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = T_f + T_{\text{sync}}
$$

The **total effective execution time** for the 8087 to complete a floating-point instruction (as seen by the 8086 program) is the sum of the actual processing time and the synchronization overhead.

### 3.1.2 Performance Gain Derivation

The **speedup factor** when using an 8087 coprocessor versus a software emulation on the 8086 is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{86,\text{emulated}}}{T_{87,\text{hardware}}} = \frac{T_{86,\text{emulated}}}{T_f + T_{\text{sync}}}
$$

For a transcendental function like $\sin(x)$:

- Software emulation on 8086: $T_{86,\text{emulated}} \approx 10,000 \cdot T_c$
- Hardware on 8087: $T_f + T_{\text{sync}} \approx 200 \cdot T_c$

Therefore:

$$
S = \frac{10,000 \cdot T_c}{200 \cdot T_c} = 50
$$

This means the 8087 delivers approximately **50× speedup** for transcendental operations.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of 8089 DMA Transfer Throughput

The 8089 IOP performs DMA transfers in **burst mode**. The throughput is given by:

$$
R_{\text{DMA}} = \frac{B_{\text{burst}}}{T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{transfer}}}
$$

Where:

- $B_{\text{burst}}$ = bytes per burst (typically **64 bytes** per memory-to-I/O cycle)
- $T_{\text{setup}}$ = bus arbitration + channel command fetch time
- $T_{\text{transfer}}$ = data movement time

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} = N_{\text{bytes}} \cdot T_{\text{cycle}} \cdot k
$$

Where $k$ is the number of clock cycles per byte transfer (typically $k=2$ for the 8089).

For a transfer of $N = 1024$ bytes with $T_c = 200$ ns:

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} = 1024 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2 = 409.6 \, \mu s
$$

$$
R_{\text{DMA}} = \frac{1024}{409.6 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.5 \text{ MB/s}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Closely Coupled vs. Loosely Coupled

| Parameter | Closely Coupled (8086+8087) | Loosely Coupled (8086+8089) |
|---|---|---|
| **Coupling Strength** | Tight (instruction-level) | Loose (bus-master level) |
| **Number of Bus Masters** | 1 at a time (8086 owns bus, 8087 monitors) | 2 (8086 and 8089 arbitrate) |
| **Communication Mechanism** | `WAIT`/`BUSY`/`TEST` handshake | Shared memory mailbox + I/O channel |
| **Memory Access** | Shared; 8087 reads via 8086's bus cycles | Independent bus arbitration |
| **Synchronization** | `WAIT` instruction polls `TEST` | Channel program completion flag |
| **Scalability** | Limited (one coprocessor per host) | High (multiple IOPs possible) |
| **Programming Model** | ESC instructions inline in 8086 code | Channel programs in IOP memory |
| **Use Case** | Floating-point math acceleration | High-speed I/O, disk controllers |
| **Example Instruction** | `ESC 30, [BX]` (FADD via 8087) | `XFER` channel command in 8089 |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Bus Arbitration Pulse Timing

The 8089 IOP uses a **pulsed request/grant protocol** on a single wire. Let us derive the **minimum time** required for a complete bus handover:

$$
T_{\text{handover}} = T_{\text{RQ}} + T_{\text{GT8086}} + T_{\text{GT8089}} + T_{\text{release}}
$$

Each pulse occupies **one clock cycle** ($T_c$):

$$
T_{\text{handover}} = 4 \cdot T_c
$$

Therefore, for $T_c = 200$ ns:

$$
T_{\text{handover}} = 4 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-9} = 800 \text{ ns}
$$

The **bus utilization efficiency** of the 8086 in a loosely coupled system is:

$$
\eta_{8086} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{handover}} \cdot f_{\text{IOP}}}{T_{\text{total}}}
$$

Where $f_{\text{IOP}}$ is the frequency of IOP bus requests. For $f_{\text{IOP}} = 10,000$ requests/s and $T_{\text{total}} = 1$ s:

$$
\eta_{8086} = 1 - \frac{800 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10{,}000}{1} = 1 - 0.008 = 0.992 = 99.2\%
$$

The 8086 retains **99.2% bus utilization** — proving the arbitration overhead is negligible for typical workloads.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closely Coupled Configuration (8086 + 8087)

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU["8086 HOST CPU"]
    NDP["8087 NUMERIC COPROCESSOR NDP"]
    CLCK["8284A CLOCK GENERATOR"]
    BCL["8288 BUS CONTROLLER"]
    LATCH["ADDRESS LATCH 74LS373"]
    BUF["BUS BUFFER 74LS245"]
    MEM["SHARED MEMORY 1 MB"]
    IO["I/O DEVICES"]

    CLCK -- CLOCK SIGNAL --> CPU
    CLCK -- CLOCK SIGNAL --> NDP
    CPU -- 16 BIT DATA BUS --> BUF
    NDP -- 16 BIT DATA BUS --> BUF
    CPU -- 20 BIT ADDRESS BUS --> LATCH
    NDP -- MONITORS DATA BUS --> CPU
    CPU -- STATUS S0 S1 S2 --> BCL
    BCL -- CONTROL SIGNALS --> BUF
    LATCH -- 20 BIT ADDRESS --> MEM
    BUF -- 16 BIT DATA --> MEM
    MEM -- DATA --> IO

    CPU -- WAIT INSTRUCTION TESTS --> NDP
    NDP -- BUSY SIGNAL TO CPU --> CPU
    NDP -- INTERRUPT REQUEST INT --> CPU
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Loosely Coupled Configuration (8086 + 8089)

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU1["8086 HOST CPU"]
    IOP1["8089 IOP LOCAL"]
    IOP2["8089 IOP SYSTEM"]
    ARB["BUS ARBITER 8289"]
    BCL1["8288 BUS CONTROLLER LOCAL"]
    BCL2["8288 BUS CONTROLLER SYSTEM"]
    LMEM["LOCAL MEMORY 1 MB"]
    SMEM["SYSTEM MEMORY 1 MB"]
    LIO["LOCAL I/O DEVICES"]
    SIO["SYSTEM I/O DEVICES DISK CONTROLLER"]

    CPU1 -- LOCAL BUS --> IOP1
    CPU1 -- RQ GT PINS --> IOP1
    IOP1 -- BUS REQUEST --> ARB
    CPU1 -- STATUS --> BCL1
    IOP1 -- STATUS --> BCL1
    BCL1 -- CONTROL --> LMEM
    BCL1 -- CONTROL --> LIO

    IOP1 -- SYSTEM BUS BRIDGE --> IOP2
    IOP2 -- ARBITRATION --> ARB
    IOP2 -- STATUS --> BCL2
    BCL2 -- CONTROL --> SMEM
    BCL2 -- CONTROL --> SIO

    CPU1 -- MAILBOX IN SHARED MEMORY --> IOP1
    IOP1 -- COMPLETION FLAG --> CPU1
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — 8087 Instruction Execution Sequence

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["8086 FETCHES INSTRUCTION"]
    B["CHECK IF OPCODE STARTS WITH ESCAPE 11011XXX"]
    C["8086 EXECUTES INTEGER OR NON FP INSTRUCTION"]
    D["8087 DETECTS ESC OPCODE"]
    E["8087 READS MEMORY OPERAND USING 8086 BUS CYCLES"]
    F["8087 EXECUTES FLOATING POINT OPERATION"]
    G["8087 WRITES RESULT BACK TO MEMORY OR REGISTER"]
    H["8086 ENCOUNTERS WAIT INSTRUCTION"]
    I["8086 TESTS TEST PIN POLLING BUSY"]
    J["8087 COMPLETES AND DEASSERTS BUSY"]
    K["8086 RESUMES NEXT INSTRUCTION FETCH"]

    A --> B
    B -- NO --> C
    B -- YES --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I -- BUSY LOW WAIT --> I
    I -- BUSY HIGH READY --> J
    J --> K
    K --> A
```

## 4.4 RQ/GT Pulsed Handshaking Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1["STATE 1 8086 OWNS BUS IDLE"]
    S2["STATE 2 8089 SENDS REQUEST PULSE LOW FOR 1 CLK"]
    S3["STATE 3 8086 SENDS GRANT PULSE LOW FOR 1 CLK RELEASES BUS"]
    S4["STATE 4 8089 SENDS ACKNOWLEDGE PULSE LOW FOR 1 CLK TAKES BUS"]
    S5["STATE 5 8089 EXECUTES DMA TRANSFER"]
    S6["STATE 6 8089 SENDS RELEASE PULSE LOW FOR 1 CLK RETURNS BUS"]
    S7["STATE 7 8086 RESUMES EXECUTION"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the role of the 8087 Numeric Data Processor in a closely coupled multiprocessor configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8087 NDP is a specialized **floating-point coprocessor** designed to work alongside the 8086 CPU in a closely coupled multiprocessor configuration. Its primary roles are:

1. **Offloading floating-point arithmetic** — It executes floating-point, trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential operations **50 to 100 times faster** than software emulation on the 8086.
2. **Passive bus monitoring** — The 8087 monitors the 8086's data bus for **ESC (Escape) opcodes** (bit pattern `11011xxx`) and executes them in parallel.
3. **Extended precision** — It provides **80-bit Temporary Real** internal precision with an 8×80-bit register stack.
4. **Synchronization via WAIT/BUSY** — It signals completion to the 8086 using the `BUSY` pin, which the 8086 polls through `WAIT` instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Role of NDP — 1 mark; ESC detection — 1 mark; WAIT/BUSY — 1 mark.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between closely coupled and loosely coupled multiprocessor configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| Coupling Type | Tight (instruction-level) | Loose (bus-master level) |
| Bus Masters | 8086 only; 8087 monitors | 8086 and 8089 (both arbitrate) |
| Communication | `WAIT`/`BUSY` handshake | Shared memory mailbox |
| Processor | 8087 NDP | 8089 IOP |
| Number of CPUs | One coprocessor per host | Multiple IOPs possible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Distinction table — 2 marks; example processors — 1 mark.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a **closely coupled multiprocessor configuration** using 8086 and 8087. Discuss the handshaking signals in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **functioning of the 8087 NDP**. Discuss the role of the `WAIT` instruction and the `BUSY` signal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Closely Coupled Block Diagram (3 marks):**
- The 8086 CPU and 8087 NDP share the **same clock** (from 8284A), the **same bus controller** (8288), the **same address latches** (74LS373), and the **same data buffers** (74LS245).
- Both access a **common 1 MB memory** and common I/O devices.
- The 8086 is the **sole bus master**; the 8087 is a **passive monitor** that only activates when ESC instructions are detected.

**Handshaking Signals (4 marks):**
- `BUSY` (8087 → 8086): Active LOW indicates the 8087 is executing. The 8086's `WAIT` instruction keeps polling this status.
- `TEST` (8086 → 8087): Tested by the 8086's `WAIT` instruction. When LOW, 8086 resumes.
- `RQ/`/ `GT` (Bidirectional): Allows the 8087 to request temporary bus control (e.g., to fetch long operands).
- `INT` (8087 → 8086): Interrupt request to handle exceptions like overflow, divide-by-zero, invalid operand, or denormal.

**(b) 8087 Functioning (4 marks):**
- The 8087 contains a **Control Unit**, an **8×80-bit register stack**, and an **arithmetic unit**.
- When the 8086 fetches an ESC instruction, the 8087 simultaneously **decodes and executes** the floating-point operation.
- The 8086, after fetching the ESC opcode, encounters a `WAIT` instruction (inserted by the assembler) and **idles**, repeatedly testing the `TEST` pin.
- The 8087, after completion, **de-asserts `BUSY`**, causing the 8086 to resume.

**WAIT and BUSY Protocol (3 marks):**
- `WAIT` is a **3-clock minimum** instruction that continuously tests the `TEST` input.
- The 8086 cannot proceed until `TEST` = LOW, which occurs only when the 8087 has finished.
- This **synchronization prevents race conditions** where the 8086 might read a memory location before the 8087 has stored its result.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students incorrectly draw the 8087 as a **separate bus master** in a closely coupled system. **The 8087 NEVER arbitrates for the bus in tightly coupled mode** — the 8086 owns the bus exclusively, and the 8087 piggybacks on its cycles. Confusing this with the 8089's behavior costs **2–3 marks**.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **loosely coupled multiprocessor configuration** with a block diagram. How does the 8089 IOP communicate with the 8086? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **RQ/GT pulsed handshaking protocol** between the 8086 and 8089 in detail. Derive the total handover time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Loosely Coupled Configuration (4 marks):**
- In a loosely coupled system, the 8086 and 8089 IOP operate as **independent bus masters**.
- Each processor has its **own bus controller (8288)**, **local bus**, and **local memory**.
- The 8086 and 8089 communicate via a **shared system bus** arbitrated by the 8289 bus arbiter.
- A typical dual-IOP setup uses one 8089 on the **local bus** (with the 8086) and another on the **system bus**, with the local IOP serving as a **bridge**.

**8086–8089 Communication (3 marks):**
- The 8086 prepares an **IOP initialization block** (a structure in shared memory containing the channel program pointer, control flags, and data pointers).
- The 8086 writes a **CA (Channel Attention) signal** to the 8089, alerting it to read the initialization block.
- The 8089 reads the channel program, executes it autonomously, and **updates a status byte** in shared memory upon completion.
- The 8086 polls this status byte (or receives an interrupt) to confirm task completion.

**(b) RQ/GT Handshaking (4 marks):**
The protocol uses **three pulses** on a single wire (`RQ/GT_0` or `RQ/GT_1`):

1. **Request pulse (8089 → 8086):** 1 clock cycle LOW — 8089 requests the bus.
2. **Grant pulse (8086 → 8089):** 1 clock cycle LOW — 8086 releases bus ownership.
3. **Release pulse (8089 → 8086):** 1 clock cycle LOW — 8089 returns bus to 8086.

**Handover Time Derivation (3 marks):**
$$
T_{\text{handover}} = 3 \cdot T_c
$$

For $T_c = 200$ ns:
$$
T_{\text{handover}} = 3 \cdot 200 = 600 \text{ ns}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is to assume the 8089 uses **separate RQ and GT lines** like the 8086's HOLD/HLDA. The 8089 uses a **single bidirectional line with pulsed signaling** — a critical distinction. Mixing this up costs **2 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Multiprocessor Configurations**

- **Two configuration types:** Closely coupled (8086+8087) and Loosely coupled (8086+8089).
- **8087 = Coprocessor** (passive monitor, instruction-level coupling).
- **8089 = IOP** (active bus master, channel-program execution).
- **ESC opcode pattern:** Bit pattern `11011xxx` signals the 8087.
- **`WAIT` instruction:** Causes 8086 to idle and poll `TEST` pin.
- **`BUSY` signal:** Active LOW = 8087 is executing; tested by 8086.
- **`RQ/GT` protocol:** Uses **3 pulses** (request, grant, release) on a single line.
- **8087 data types:** 7 types including 80-bit Temporary Real.
- **8086 prefetch queue:** 6 bytes (8088 has only 4 bytes).
- **Bus arbitration chip:** **8289** in loosely coupled systems.
- **Bus controller chip:** **8288** in both configurations.
- **Clock generator:** **8284A** provides CLK, RESET, READY signals.
- **Closely coupled advantage:** Simpler, faster for FP math.
- **Loosely coupled advantage:** Scalable, supports multiple IOPs.
- **Communication in loosely coupled:** Shared memory mailbox + Channel Attention (CA) signal.
- **8087 register stack:** 8 × 80-bit registers, acts as a stack.
- **KTU key term:** "**Co-processor configuration**" = 8086 + 8087.
- **KTU key term:** "**I/O processor configuration**" = 8086 + 8089.
- **Performance gain:** 8087 delivers **50–100× speedup** for FP operations.
- **Synchronization goal:** Prevent race conditions between host and coprocessor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coprocessor, Closely Coupled and Loosely Coupled Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coprocessor, Closely Coupled and Loosely Coupled Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Coprocessor

A **Coprocessor** is a specialized auxiliary processor designed to extend the instruction set and computational capabilities of the host (main) microprocessor. It works in conjunction with the CPU to offload specific, computationally intensive tasks such as high-precision floating-point arithmetic, signal processing, or graphics operations, thereby accelerating overall system throughput.

In the **Intel 8086/8088 family**, the **8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP)** — often called the *Math Coprocessor* — is the classic textbook example. The 8087 directly supports 80-bit extended precision floating-point arithmetic (the IEEE 754 floating-point standard) and trigonometric, logarithmic, and exponential functions that the 8086/8088 cannot natively perform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECIT615 - Module 2):**
> Students must clearly distinguish between the *Closely Coupled* (tight integration) and *Loosely Coupled* (independent multiprocessor) coprocessor configurations. KTU 2024 Scheme questions typically test the **architectural differences, signal interactions, and use-case scenarios** between the two.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Chefs in One Kitchen"

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen with one Head Chef (the main CPU, 8086) who is brilliant at preparing regular dishes (integer, logic, control, I/O instructions) but is slow when it comes to baking elaborate pastries (floating-point math).

- **Closely Coupled Analogy**: The Head Chef and a Pastry Chef stand at the *same stove*, sharing the same recipe book. The Head Chef reads out the order; if it's a pastry, the Pastry Chef immediately takes over that step. They share the same heat, the same ingredients, and finish dishes together. *(One bus, shared queue monitoring.)*

- **Loosely Coupled Analogy**: The Pastry Chef works at a *completely separate station* with their own oven, own ingredients, and own recipe book. The Head Chef writes a note on the order ticket, places it in a shared "pantry," and the Pastry Chef fetches it, bakes the pastry, and leaves the result in the pantry for the Head Chef to plate. *(Independent buses, communication through shared memory.)*

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & System Constants

- **8087 Data Width**: **80 bits** internal registers (ST(0)–ST(7))
- **8086 Queue Size**: **6 bytes** (8086) / **4 bytes** (8088)
- **Standard Operating Frequency**: **5 MHz** to **10 MHz** (original family)
- **Numeric Data Types Supported**: 16-bit integer, 32-bit integer, 64-bit integer, 16-bit BCD, 32-bit (short real), 64-bit (long real), 80-bit (temporary real)

> [!NOTE]
> The coprocessor **does not** have its own conventional instruction-fetch mechanism in a *closely coupled* setup — it *passively monitors* the host CPU's instruction queue and activates only when it recognizes an ESC (Escape) opcode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8087 Numeric Data Processor — Internal Architecture

The 8087 coprocessor internally contains:

1. **Control Unit (CU)**: Decodes instructions and manages the overall operation. It monitors the status of the host CPU's instruction queue and synchronizes execution.
2. **Numeric Execution Unit (NEU)**: Performs all numeric computations. It contains:
   - **8 × 80-bit data registers** (organized as a register stack, ST(0) at top)
   - The arithmetic logic unit
   - The exponent and mantissa processing units
3. **Bus Interface Logic**: Connects to the system bus, manages data transfer, and handles the **BUSY** and **TEST** handshake signals.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How a Coprocessor Instruction is Executed

The execution flow of a coprocessor instruction (ESC opcode) is a precisely orchestrated sequence:

- **Step 1 — Instruction Fetch**: The host CPU fetches the next instruction from memory into its internal instruction queue.
- **Step 2 — Queue Monitoring**: The 8087 continuously monitors the instruction queue by snooping the bus. If the **first byte of an instruction is an ESC opcode (9-bit: 11011 × × ×)**, the 8087 identifies the instruction as its own.
- **Step 3 — Operand Fetch**: The 8087 uses the remaining bytes of the ESC instruction to determine whether the operands are in registers or memory. If memory operands are required, the host CPU performs the bus cycle and the 8087 latches the data.
- **Step 4 — Parallel Execution**: The host CPU is free to continue executing subsequent instructions. Meanwhile, the 8087 begins executing the numeric instruction **in parallel**.
- **Step 5 — Synchronization**: When the main program later needs the result (e.g., a subsequent FSTSW or FWAIT instruction), the CPU checks the 8087's **BUSY** pin. If BUSY = 1, the CPU enters a wait state using the **TEST** pin handshake.

### 2.3 Two Modes of Coprocessor Configuration

#### A. Closely Coupled Configuration

In the **Closely Coupled** arrangement, the coprocessor is **physically and logically tied to the host CPU**. They share the **same system bus and the same clock**, and the coprocessor monitors the CPU's instruction queue.

- **Single shared system bus** for both processors
- **Coprocessor operates in lockstep** with the host CPU
- **One coprocessor per host CPU** (cannot have multiple coprocessors easily)
- Communication is via the **shared instruction queue** and **shared memory address space**
- The **TEST** signal is used by the host CPU to wait for coprocessor completion

#### B. Loosely Coupled Configuration

In the **Loosely Coupled** arrangement, the coprocessor is treated as a **completely independent processor** with its **own clock generator, its own local bus, and even its own copy of the system bus interface**.

- **Two independent system buses** (one for CPU, one for coprocessor)
- The **8288 bus controller and 8289 bus arbiter** are used to arbitrate bus access
- **Multiple coprocessors** can be connected simultaneously (each with its own independent operations)
- Communication occurs exclusively through **shared memory** (using a dual-ported memory or a memory-access arbitration scheme like the 8289)
- The coprocessor **fetches its own instructions** independently

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Configurations?**
> The **Closely Coupled** design is simpler, cheaper, and provides tight synchronization — ideal for a single-user PC with one math workload. The **Loosely Coupled** design is used in multiprocessor systems where multiple independent processors must run concurrently, demanding higher throughput at the cost of hardware complexity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| **System Buses Used** | **1** (shared) | **2** (independent + arbiter bus) |
| **Clock Source** | Common clock | Independent clock |
| **Coprocessors Allowed** | 1 per host CPU | Multiple (2 or more) |
| **Bus Controller Needed?** | No | **Yes (8288 + 8289)** |
| **Instruction Fetch by Coprocessor** | Snoops host queue | Fetches independently |
| **Communication Method** | Shared queue + memory | **Shared memory / I/O ports** |
| **Synchronization Signal** | **TEST** pin (active LOW) | Semaphore flags in memory |
| **System Cost** | Low | High |
| **Application Domain** | Single-user PCs, desktop math workstations | Multiprocessor servers, real-time embedded |
| **Performance Under Load** | Limited (single bus bottleneck) | High (parallel independent operations) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the Closely Coupled configuration, the coprocessor must recognize the ESC (Escape) opcode pattern. In the Loosely Coupled configuration, the coprocessor has its **own program counter (IP)** and is a fully autonomous processor — it does **not** monitor the host CPU's queue at all.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closely Coupled Handshake Protocol

The synchronization between the 8086 (host) and 8087 (coprocessor) is governed by two critical signals: **BUSY** and **TEST**. Let us derive the exact operational sequence.

**Step 1 — Define the signal polarity**

The 8087 pulls the **BUSY** pin **HIGH** for the entire duration it is executing a numeric instruction. The host CPU uses the **WAIT** instruction, which internally checks the **TEST** pin.

**Step 2 — Establish the initialization condition**

At power-on, the 8087's **BUSY = LOW** (idle) and the host CPU's internal flip-flop is reset. The 8087 then monitors the instruction queue.

**Step 3 — Instruction recognition**

When the 8087 detects an ESC opcode in the queue, it begins execution. Immediately, it raises the **BUSY** signal to **HIGH**.

$$
\text{BUSY} = 1 \quad \text{(during coprocessor execution)}
$$

**Step 4 — CPU encounters dependent instruction**

The host CPU's main program may subsequently need the result. The compiler/assembler automatically inserts a **WAIT (FWAIT)** mnemonic before any instruction that reads the coprocessor's status or result. The WAIT instruction performs the following check in hardware:

$$
\text{WAIT} \rightarrow \text{CPU samples } \overline{\text{TEST}} \text{ pin continuously}
$$

**Step 5 — Synchronization condition**

The coprocessor, upon completing its operation, drives the **TEST** pin **LOW** to signal readiness. The relationship between BUSY and TEST is:

$$
\overline{\text{TEST}} = \overline{\text{BUSY}} \quad \text{(at completion only)}
$$

**Step 6 — CPU resumes execution**

When the CPU samples $\overline{\text{TEST}} = 0$, the WAIT instruction terminates and the CPU proceeds to the next instruction.

**Step 7 — Example Timing Diagram Logic**

Let us represent the time taken by the coprocessor as $T_{\text{exec}}$. The host CPU is free to execute non-dependent instructions for a duration up to:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}} = T_{\text{exec}} - T_{\text{setup}}
$$

where $T_{\text{setup}}$ is the time required to detect the ESC opcode and latch the operands. This represents the **overlap** between host CPU and coprocessor execution — the central reason coprocessors accelerate numeric computation.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Configuration Selection

Below is a structured analytical framework for determining when to choose each configuration:

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended Configuration | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single user, single math workload | Closely Coupled | Cheaper, simpler, sufficient throughput |
| Multiple coprocessors in parallel | **Loosely Coupled** | Requires independent bus arbitration |
| Tight real-time synchronization needed | Closely Coupled | Shared clock enables deterministic timing |
| High throughput multiprocessing server | **Loosely Coupled** | Independent buses eliminate single-bus contention |
| Cost-sensitive embedded product | Closely Coupled | No 8288/8289 arbiter chips needed |
| Distributed processing with shared data pool | **Loosely Coupled** | Multiple processors can write/read shared memory concurrently |
| Floating-point heavy scientific calculation | Closely Coupled (8086 + 8087) | Standard single-workstation numeric computing |
| Industrial multiprocess control | **Loosely Coupled** | Independent fault isolation per processor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Modern CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, ARM Cortex) integrate the math coprocessor *on-die* as a dedicated FPU execution unit. However, the same architectural principles — *parallel execution units, handshake synchronization, and shared register files* — are direct descendants of the 8086/8087 closely coupled design. The loosely coupled concept survives today in **multi-core SoCs** where independent cores arbitrate for shared L3 cache and memory controllers using bus arbiter logic analogous to the 8289.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closely Coupled Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu1[HOST CPU 8086]
    cp1[COPROCESSOR 8087]
    mem1[SYSTEM MEMORY]
    ioport1[I/O DEVICES]
    clk1[COMMON CLOCK]
    bus1[SHARED SYSTEM BUS]
    test1[TEST PIN LINK]
    busy1[BUSY PIN LINK]
    queue1[6 BYTE INSTRUCTION QUEUE]

    clk1 --> cpu1
    clk1 --> cp1
    cpu1 --- bus1
    cp1 --- bus1
    mem1 --- bus1
    ioport1 --- bus1
    cpu1 --> queue1
    cpu1 --> test1
    cp1 --> busy1
    queue1 -.MONITORED BY.-> cp1
    test1 <-. HANDSHAKE .-> busy1
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Loosely Coupled Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    host2[HOST CPU 8086]
    coproc2[COPROCESSOR 8087 A]
    coproc2b[COPROCESSOR 8087 B]
    bc1[8288 BUS CONTROLLER 1]
    bc2[8288 BUS CONTROLLER 2]
    arbiter1[8289 BUS ARBITER]
    sysbus2[SYSTEM BUS]
    shared2[SHARED DUAL PORT MEMORY]
    hostbus2[HOST LOCAL BUS]
    copbus2[COPROCESSOR LOCAL BUS]
    hostclk2[HOST CLOCK]
    copclk2[COPROCESSOR CLOCK]

    hostclk2 --> host2
    copclk2 --> coproc2
    copclk2 --> coproc2b
    host2 --> hostbus2
    hostbus2 --> bc1
    coproc2 --> copbus2
    coproc2b --> copbus2
    copbus2 --> bc2
    bc1 --> sysbus2
    bc2 --> sysbus2
    sysbus2 --> arbiter1
    arbiter1 --> shared2
    arbiter1 --> sysbus2
    shared2 --- sysbus2
```

### 4.3 Signal Interaction Flowchart — Closely Coupled Handshake

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[POWER ON 8087 BUSY 0]
    step2[HOST FETCHES INSTRUCTION]
    step3{IS OPCODE ESC 11011}
    step4[HOST EXECUTES NORMALLY]
    step5[8087 LATCHES ESC INSTRUCTION]
    step6[8087 SETS BUSY 1]
    step7[8087 EXECUTES NUMERIC OPERATION]
    step8[8087 COMPLETES SETS TEST 0]
    step9[HOST CPU WAITS IF TEST 1]
    step10[HOST RESUMES EXECUTION]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 -- NO --> step4
    step3 -- YES --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 -- BUSY STILL 1 --> step9
    step9 -- TEST 0 --> step10
```

### 4.4 Architectural Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLOSE[CLOSELY COUPLED]
        ca[8086 CPU]
        cb[8087 NDP]
        cc[SHARED BUS]
        ca --- cc
        cb --- cc
    end

    subgraph LOOSE[LOOSELY COUPLED]
        la[8086 HOST]
        lb[8087 COPROC 1]
        lc[8087 COPROC 2]
        ld[8289 ARBITER]
        le[SHARED MEMORY]
        la --- la_bus[HOST BUS]
        lb --- lb_bus[COPROC BUS 1]
        lc --- lc_bus[COPROC BUS 2]
        la_bus --> ld
        lb_bus --> ld
        lc_bus --> ld
        ld --> le
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define a coprocessor. Mention the name of the coprocessor designed to work with the 8086 microprocessor.**

> **[Model Answer — KTU Pattern, 3 Marks]**
>
> A **coprocessor** is a specialized auxiliary processor that works in conjunction with a host microprocessor to perform specific computational tasks — typically floating-point arithmetic — that the main CPU cannot execute efficiently by itself. It extends the host's instruction set and runs in parallel with it.
>
> The coprocessor designed for the **8086 microprocessor is the 8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP)**, also called the *Math Coprocessor*. It performs high-precision floating-point operations at 80-bit extended precision, far exceeding the 8086's 16-bit integer-only native capability. **[3 Marks]**

**Q2. Differentiate between Closely Coupled and Loosely Coupled configurations of a coprocessor (any three points).**

> **[Model Answer — KTU Pattern, 3 Marks]**
>
> | Sl. No. | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | A single shared system bus is used by both the host CPU and the coprocessor. | Two independent system buses exist, one for the host CPU and one for the coprocessor. |
> | 2 | The coprocessor monitors the host CPU's instruction queue by snooping for ESC opcodes. | The coprocessor fetches its own instructions independently from shared memory. |
> | 3 | The coprocessor shares the clock signal of the host CPU. | The coprocessor has its own independent clock generator. |
>
> **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Both sub-parts typically carry **7 marks each**. Solutions below follow the exact step-by-step valuation pattern KTU examiners use.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the internal architecture of the 8087 numeric data coprocessor in detail. (7 Marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the closely coupled configuration of the 8086–8087 system. Explain the role of the TEST and BUSY signals. (7 Marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — 8087 Internal Architecture [7 Marks]**

The 8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP) is internally partitioned into two major functional units, as enumerated below:

**1. Control Unit (CU) — [3 Marks]**

The Control Unit is responsible for:
- Decoding the ESC (Escape) opcodes fetched by the host CPU.
- Generating the necessary control and timing signals to coordinate data transfer between the 8087 and the system bus.
- Maintaining synchronization with the host CPU through the **BUSY** and **TEST** signals.
- Managing the operand fetch and result write-back cycles.

**2. Numeric Execution Unit (NEU) — [3 Marks]**

The NEU contains:
- A **register stack of eight 80-bit floating-point data registers**, labelled **ST(0) through ST(7)**. ST(0) is the top-of-stack, providing stack-style operand access.
- An **arithmetic logic unit** capable of 80-bit addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, square root, and transcendental operations (sin, cos, tan, log, exp).
- **Status, Control, and Tag word registers** for managing exception flags, precision control, rounding mode, and stack register occupancy.

**3. Bus Interface Unit — [1 Mark]**

Provides the physical interface to the local data bus of the host CPU (in closely coupled mode) or to an independent bus (in loosely coupled mode), and manages data latching during memory operand transfers.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Closely Coupled Configuration [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram (describe verbally as in exam):**

In the Closely Coupled configuration, the 8086 host CPU and the 8087 coprocessor are connected to a **single shared system bus**. Both processors use the **same clock signal**. The 8087's bus interface pins are connected directly to the 8086's local bus (in maximum mode) or system bus (in minimum mode).

**Operational Sequence: [3 Marks]**

1. The 8086 fetches instructions from memory in the normal manner and stores them in its 6-byte internal instruction queue.
2. The 8087 *passively monitors* this instruction queue in parallel.
3. When the 8087 detects an instruction beginning with the ESC opcode (9-bit pattern: 11011 × × ×), it identifies the instruction as a coprocessor instruction.
4. The 8087 latches the operand address from the ESC instruction (if the operand is in memory, the 8086 performs the bus read cycle and the 8087 captures the data from the bus).
5. The 8087 begins executing the numeric operation *in parallel* with the 8086, which is free to continue executing subsequent non-dependent instructions.
6. The 8087 raises the **BUSY = 1** signal for the entire execution duration.
7. When a dependent instruction is reached (a WAIT or FWAIT is auto-inserted by the assembler), the 8086 checks the **TEST** pin. The 8087 pulls $\overline{\text{TEST}}$ LOW upon completion.
8. The 8086 exits the WAIT state and reads the result from the 8087's register stack.

**Role of TEST and BUSY signals: [4 Marks]**

- **BUSY** is an *output* from the 8087 and *input* to the 8086. BUSY = 1 means the coprocessor is busy executing; BUSY = 0 means it is idle.
- **TEST** ($\overline{\text{TEST}}$) is an *output* from the 8087 and *input* to the 8086. It is the active-low complement of BUSY used for handshake.
- The 8086 executes the **WAIT** instruction, which repeatedly samples the $\overline{\text{TEST}}$ pin until it goes LOW.
- This two-signal handshake guarantees **synchronization without polling overhead**, allowing true parallel execution.
- The **8086's WAIT instruction is opcode 9B H**, and on the 8087 side, this maps to the FWAIT mnemonic — both are functionally identical.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Draw and explain the loosely coupled multiprocessor configuration with the 8086 and two 8087s. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Compare closely coupled and loosely coupled configurations on the basis of: (i) number of buses, (ii) number of coprocessors supported, (iii) clock signal, (iv) bus arbitration, (v) synchronization mechanism, (vi) cost, and (vii) typical application. (7 Marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution — Loosely Coupled Configuration [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram (describe):**

In the Loosely Coupled configuration, the host 8086 and each 8087 coprocessor operate as **fully independent processors**, each with its **own clock generator** and its **own bus controller (8288)**. All processors share access to a common system bus, arbitrated by an **8289 Bus Arbiter**. A **shared memory** (often dual-ported) serves as the communication medium between processors.

**Components involved: [3 Marks]**

- **One 8086** host CPU with its own 8288 bus controller and clock generator (8284).
- **Two 8087** coprocessors, each with their own 8284 clock generator and 8288 bus controller.
- **One 8289 Bus Arbiter** for granting bus access among the three processors.
- **Shared memory** (system RAM) accessible to all three processors through the arbitrated system bus.
- Each processor has its **own copy** of the address, data, and control bus locally.

**Operation: [2 Marks]**

Each processor fetches and executes its **own independent instruction stream** from the shared memory. Communication between the host and the coprocessors occurs through:
- **Mailbox locations** in shared memory (semaphore flags).
- The **LOCK prefix** on the 8086 to ensure atomic bus access.
- **Multiprocessor interrupt management** (using the 8259A PIC).

**Bus Arbitration: [2 Marks]**

The 8289 bus arbiter implements one of three modes:
- **Parallel Priority Mode** — fixed priority.
- **Rotating Priority Mode** — dynamic, time-shared priority.
- **Cascaded Mode** — for systems with multiple 8289s.

When a processor needs the system bus, it requests access via the 8289. The 8289 grants access to the highest-priority requester. The processor then performs its bus cycle independently.

---

**(b) Model Solution — Detailed Comparison [7 Marks — 1 Mark Each]**

| Comparison Parameter | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Number of System Buses** | One shared system bus | Independent bus for each processor, arbitrated |
| (ii) **Coprocessors Supported** | Only one coprocessor per host CPU | Multiple coprocessors (and even co-processors of different types) |
| (iii) **Clock Signal** | Common clock shared by CPU and coprocessor | Independent clock generator for each processor |
| (iv) **Bus Arbitration** | Not required; single bus, no contention | Required; uses the 8289 bus arbiter chip |
| (v) **Synchronization Mechanism** | TEST/BUSY handshake pins; WAIT instruction | Semaphore flags in shared memory; LOCK prefix |
| (vi) **Cost** | Low (no arbiter chips) | High (additional 8288, 8289, and clock chips per processor) |
| (vii) **Typical Application** | Desktop PCs, scientific calculators, workstations | Multiprocessor servers, real-time industrial control, parallel processing systems |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BUSY and TEST polarities**: Many students write "BUSY = 0 means the coprocessor is executing." This is **wrong**. BUSY = **1** (HIGH) indicates the coprocessor is busy. $\overline{\text{TEST}} = 0$ (LOW) indicates the coprocessor has **finished** and the CPU may proceed. **Loses 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the WAIT / FWAIT mnemonic**: Students often omit that the assembler automatically inserts a WAIT (9BH) before every coprocessor read instruction. The CPU does not magically know to wait — it is the WAIT instruction that performs the $\overline{\text{TEST}}$ pin polling. **Loses 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Saying "closely coupled has multiple buses"**: This is a fatal error. Closely coupled has **ONE shared system bus**. Loosely coupled has **multiple independent buses** arbitrated by the 8289. **Loses 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Mixing up ESC opcode pattern**: The ESC opcode is the 9-bit pattern **11011 × × ×** (the first 5 bits are 11011, followed by 3 bits that the 8087 decodes). Stating it as "any opcode starting with D8" is acceptable shorthand, but stating "8086 instruction starting with 9B" is **wrong** (9BH is the WAIT opcode). **Loses 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Skipping the 8289 arbiter**: In loosely coupled diagrams, students frequently draw the 8087 directly on the system bus without an 8289 bus arbiter. This is **incomplete and loses 2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Not mentioning dual-ported memory**: In loosely coupled systems, shared memory is often implemented as **dual-ported** to allow simultaneous access. Omitting this detail loses a mark in long-answer questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2**

- **Coprocessor**: Specialized auxiliary processor; 8087 NDP for the 8086/8088 family.
- **8087 Register Stack**: Eight 80-bit registers ST(0)–ST(7), stack-organized.
- **ESC Opcode**: The 9-bit pattern **11011xxx** triggers the 8087 to take over a fetched instruction.
- **Closely Coupled Configuration**:
  - **One** shared system bus.
  - **Single** clock shared between CPU and coprocessor.
  - 8087 *monitors* the 8086 instruction queue.
  - **Synchronization**: BUSY + $\overline{\text{TEST}}$ + WAIT instruction.
  - Only **one** coprocessor per host CPU.
  - Lower cost, simpler hardware.
- **Loosely Coupled Configuration**:
  - **Multiple independent buses**, each with its own 8288 bus controller.
  - **Independent clock generators** (8284) per processor.
  - **Bus arbitration** via the **8289 bus arbiter**.
  - Each processor has its **own program counter** and **fetches its own instructions**.
  - Communication via **shared memory** (often dual-ported) with semaphore synchronization.
  - **Multiple coprocessors** can operate concurrently.
  - Higher cost, used in multiprocessor systems.
- **Key Handshake Signals**:
  - **BUSY = 1** → 8087 is executing.
  - $\overline{\text{TEST}} = 0$ → 8087 has finished, CPU may proceed.
  - **WAIT (9BH / FWAIT)** → CPU instruction that polls $\overline{\text{TEST}}$.
- **Numeric Data Types**: 16-bit int, 32-bit int, 64-bit int, 16-bit BCD, 32-bit short real, 64-bit long real, **80-bit temporary real** (internal format).
- **Modern Relevance**: On-die FPUs in modern CPUs are descendants of the closely coupled design; multi-core SoCs extend the loosely coupled philosophy.
- **KTU Hot-Question Tip**: Expect a 7-mark question asking for a **neat block diagram** of either configuration — practice drawing both clearly with proper signal labels.
- **Examiner's Trick**: The phrase "the coprocessor has its own program counter" is **only true in loosely coupled mode** — never state it for closely coupled mode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit, single-chip microcontroller** introduced by Intel in 1980, featuring an embedded **8-bit CPU**, on-chip **program memory (ROM/EPROM)**, on-chip **data memory (RAM)**, parallel and serial I/O ports, timers/counters, and an interrupt controller — all integrated on a single silicon die using **Harvard architecture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> "The 8051 is an 8-bit Harvard-architecture microcontroller with a 16-bit Program Counter, 8-bit ALU, 4 KB on-chip ROM, 128 bytes on-chip RAM, four 8-bit I/O ports, two 16-bit timers/counters, a full-duplex UART, and a 5-source, 2-priority interrupt structure."

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Class:** 8-bit, CISC-style, **Harvard** (separate code + data buses), single-address-space MCS-51 family.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **fully self-sufficient, pocket-sized office**:

- The **CPU (Central Brain)** is the office manager — it reads instructions, thinks, and issues orders.
- The **ROM (4 KB)** is the **filing cabinet with rule books** — it permanently stores the program that the manager always follows. Power off? The rules remain.
- The **RAM (128 bytes)** is the **worker's desk** — temporary scratchpad memory used while a job is running. Power off? The desk is cleared.
- The **four I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** are the **four doors** of the office — sensors and actuators plug in through them.
- The **timers** are the **stopwatches** on the wall — they count time precisely.
- The **UART (Serial Port)** is the **telephone** — it talks to other offices/devices one bit at a time.
- The **interrupt controller** is the **receptionist** — it routes urgent knocks on the door to the manager immediately.

Just as a small office does not need a massive mainframe computer, an **embedded device** (washing machine, microwave, car ECU) does not need a desktop CPU — the 8051 is built exactly for that role: **small, fast, cheap, and self-contained**.

## 1.3 Salient Features of 8051 (Board-Examiner High-Weightage)

| S.No. | Feature | Specification |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Data Bus Width | **8-bit** |
| 2 | Address Bus (External) | **16-bit** (64 KB addressing) |
| 3 | On-chip Program Memory (ROM) | **4 KB** |
| 4 | On-chip Data Memory (RAM) | **128 bytes** |
| 5 | I/O Ports | **4 × 8-bit = 32 lines** |
| 6 | Timers/Counters | **2 × 16-bit** |
| 7 | Serial Port (UART) | **1, Full-duplex** |
| 8 | Interrupt Sources | **5** (2 external + 3 internal) |
| 9 | Interrupt Priority Levels | **2** (programmable) |
| 10 | Register Banks | **4 × 8-byte banks** (00H–1FH) |
| 11 | Power Saving Modes | **Idle** and **Power-Down** |
| 12 | Operating Frequency | **12 MHz** (typical, max 24 MHz) |
| 13 | Machine Cycle | **12 oscillator periods** |
| 14 | Boolean Processor | **Yes (bit-addressable logic)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize (Board Favourite):**
> - External address space = **64 KB** code + **64 KB** data
> - On-chip RAM = **128 bytes** (00H–7FH)
> - Bit-addressable RAM = **16 bytes** (20H–2FH) = **128 addressable bits**
> - Special Function Registers (SFRs) = **128 bytes** (80H–FFH)
> - **16 bytes** of SFRs are bit-addressable

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Block Diagram — Functional Components

The 8051 silicon die is organised into the following major functional blocks, all interconnected by the **internal bus system** (8-bit data + 16-bit address):

### A. Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- **8-bit ALU** — performs arithmetic, logic, and Boolean operations
- **Accumulator (A)** — 8-bit primary operand and result register
- **B Register** — used implicitly in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`
- **Program Status Word (PSW)** — flag register (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P)
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** — 8-bit, points to top of stack in RAM (default value = **07H**)
- **Data Pointer (DPTR)** — 16-bit register (DPH high byte + DPL low byte) for external data memory addressing
- **Program Counter (PC)** — 16-bit, auto-incremented, initialized to **0000H** on reset

### B. On-chip Memory
- **ROM (4 KB):** 0000H–0FFFH internally; external code memory accessible via `EA̅` pin
- **RAM (128 B):** Lower 128 bytes — register banks + bit-addressable + general purpose

### C. Oscillator & Clock Circuit
- On-chip **inverting amplifier** + external **quartz crystal** between **XTAL1** and **XTAL2**
- Internal clock = **Crystal frequency ÷ 2** (some variants) — but in the classic 8051, the **oscillator frequency = crystal frequency**
- **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → at 12 MHz crystal, one machine cycle = **1 μs**

### D. Four I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)
- Each port is **8 bits** with latches, drivers, and an alternate function
- **P0:** Acts as **multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is used
- **P2:** Provides **high-byte address (A8–A15)** when external memory is used
- **P1:** Pure general-purpose I/O (no alternate function in basic 8051)
- **P3:** Pins carry alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅

### E. Serial Port (UART)
- One **full-duplex** UART, mode-controlled by **SCON** register
- 4 working modes (Mode 1 — 8-bit UART, 9600 baud typical)

### F. Timers/Counters
- Two **16-bit** up-counters: **T0 (TH0/TL0)** and **T1 (TH1/TL1)**
- 4 operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) — controlled by **TMOD** register
- Can be **timer** (internal clock) or **counter** (external pin T0/T1)

### G. Interrupt Controller
- **5 sources:** External 0, External 1, Timer 0, Timer 1, Serial Port
- **2 priority levels** via **IP (Interrupt Priority)** register
- Vector addresses: 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H

## 2.2 Memory Organisation of 8051 (Harvard — Separate Spaces)

The 8051 has **three distinct address spaces**:

| Memory Type | Size | Address Range | Notes |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Internal Code (ROM) | 4 KB | 0000H–0FFFH | Program memory |
| Internal Data (RAM) | 128 B | 00H–7FH | Lower 128 bytes |
| Special Function Registers | 128 B | 80H–FFH | Higher 128 bytes — overlapping addresses only via direct addressing |
| External Code (when EA̅=0) | 64 KB max | 0000H–FFFFH | Accessed via PSEN̅ |
| External Data (XDATA) | 64 KB max | 0000H–FFFFH | Accessed via MOVX instruction |

### Internal RAM (128 Bytes) Sub-Organisation
| Range | Use |
|:---|:---|
| 00H–07H | **Register Bank 0** (default after reset) |
| 08H–0FH | **Register Bank 1** |
| 10H–17H | **Register Bank 2** |
| 18H–1FH | **Register Bank 3** |
| 20H–2FH | **Bit-addressable area (16 bytes → 128 bits)** |
| 30H–7FH | General-purpose RAM (scratchpad, 80 bytes) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exam)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Time | $T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Internal Timer Tick Rate | $f_{tick} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Timer Overflow Period (Mode 1) | $T_{overflow} = \frac{12 \times (2^{16} - count)}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Timer Overflow Frequency | $f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (2^{16} - count)}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Timer Reload Value (Mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload) | $TH = 256 - \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times 2 \times f_{desire}}$ | integer |
| 6 | Baud Rate (Mode 1 UART, Timer 1 overflow) | $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps |
| 7 | TH1 Reload for desired Baud (SMOD=0) | $TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{OSC}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | integer |
| 8 | External Address Space | $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\text{ KB}$ | bytes |
| 9 | On-chip RAM | $2^7 = 128$ bytes | |
| 10 | Bit-addressable bits | $16 \times 8 = 128$ bits | 20H–2FH |

> [!NOTE]
