> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Timer 1 reload formula** is used universally to generate **standard baud rates** (9600, 4800, 2400) in real RS-232 communication with PCs and GSM modules.
> - **Machine cycle formula** dictates the **execution time of every instruction** — critical for **time-critical real-time systems** (anti-lock brakes, motor control, signal generation).
> - **Memory map** is used when porting code from 8051 to **larger derivatives** like 8052 (256 B RAM) or modern **Silicon Labs / Nuvoton** 8051 cores.

## 2.4 PSW (Program Status Word) Register — Bit Anatomy

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | — | **P** |
| Name | Carry | Aux. Carry | User Flag 0 | Reg. Bank Sel 1 | Reg. Bank Sel 0 | Overflow | Reserved | Parity |

- **RS1, RS0** select active register bank: `00`→Bank0, `01`→Bank1, `10`→Bank2, `11`→Bank3
- **P** is set/cleared by hardware to make accumulator have **even parity** (board favourite: *parity of A*)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic step, every substitution, every conversion is fully shown. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Machine Cycle Time at 11.0592 MHz Crystal

The 8051 design rule states that **1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

$$
f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}
$$

The time period of one oscillator clock is:

$$
T_{OSC} = \frac{1}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds}
$$

One machine cycle takes 12 such periods:

$$
T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{OSC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11059200}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = 1.085 \,\mu s
$$

**Why this specific crystal (11.0592 MHz)?** — Because $11.0592 \times 10^6 / 12 = 921{,}600$ Hz, which divides evenly to give **exact 9600 baud** with integer TH1 reload.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Timer 1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud (SMOD = 0)

**Given:** $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz, target $\text{Baud} = 9600$, SMOD bit = 0.

**Step 1 — Start with the standard UART Mode 1 baud rate formula:**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute SMOD = 0 → $2^{SMOD} = 2^0 = 1$:**

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by 32:**

$$
9600 \times 32 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

$$
307200 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply (denominator of left is 1):**

$$
307200 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1) = 11.0592 \times 10^6
$$

$$
3686400 \times (256 - TH1) = 11059200
$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by 3,686,400:**

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11059200}{3686400}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = 3
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for TH1:**

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}
$$

**Conclusion:** To obtain 9600 baud, the program must execute:

$$
\boxed{TH1 = 253 = 0\text{xFD}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most famous calculation in 8051 textbooks** — appearing in nearly every KTU previous-year paper.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Delay Generated by Timer 0 in Mode 1

**Given:** $f_{OSC} = 12$ MHz, Timer 0 in Mode 1, count value = FC18H (i.e. 64536 decimal).

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle time:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total timer tick count from count value to overflow:**

In Mode 1, the timer is 16-bit, so it overflows at $2^{16} = 65536$.

$$
\text{Ticks to overflow} = 65536 - \text{count value}
$$

$$
\text{Ticks} = 65536 - 64536 = 1000
$$

**Step 3 — Convert ticks to time (1 tick = 1 machine cycle = 1 μs):**

$$
T_{delay} = 1000 \times 1 \,\mu s = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Forgetting loop (e.g., 5 iterations):**

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times 1 \text{ ms} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The general formula: $T_{delay} = (65536 - \text{count}) \times 1.085 \,\mu s$ when $f_{OSC} = 11.0592$ MHz.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Verifying External Memory Addressing

The 8051 external address bus is **16 bits** wide. Number of uniquely addressable bytes:

$$
N = 2^{16} = 65536 = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

This is split into two independent 64 KB windows:

$$
\underbrace{0000\text{H} - \text{FFFFH}}_{\text{External Code Memory (read via PSEN̅)}} \quad \bigg\vert \quad \underbrace{0000\text{H} - \text{FFFFH}}_{\text{External Data Memory (read/write via MOVX)}}
$$

The **same addresses** are reachable by both — but they are **physically separate buses** thanks to separate control signals (PSEN̅ for code, RD̅/WR̅ for data).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Problems

| Problem Statement | First Action | Key Formula |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Find execution time of 1 machine cycle | Identify crystal | $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$ |
| Find timer delay | Identify mode + count | $T = (65536 - \text{count}) \times T_{MC}$ |
| Find TH1 for a baud rate | Set SMOD | $TH1 = 256 - f_{OSC} / (384 \times \text{Baud})$ |
| Find external memory size | 16-bit address | $2^{16} = 64$ KB |
| Find bit-addressable memory size | 16 bytes × 8 bits | **128 bits** |
| Identify register bank of R3 | Check RS1, RS0 in PSW | Banks at 00, 08, 10, 18 H |
| Find default SP after reset | — | **SP = 07H** |
| Find reset vector | Lowest code address | **0000H** |
| Timer 0 overflow vector | Timer 0 ISR address | **000BH** |
| Serial port vector | UART ISR address | **0023H** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix (e.g., `cpuA`, `romB`).
- No reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
- Node labels contain **plain uppercase text** only — no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside double quotes.

## 4.4 PSW Register — Bit Layout Diagram


| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Total)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three features of 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU-expected depth):**

The 8051 microcontroller has the following salient features:

1. **8-bit CPU with 8-bit ALU** — Operates on 8-bit data words with an integrated Arithmetic Logic Unit supporting arithmetic, logical, and Boolean (bit-level) operations.

2. **4 KB on-chip ROM (Program Memory)** — Internal non-volatile program storage; external program memory up to 64 KB is accessible using the `EA̅` and `PSEN̅` pins.

3. **128 bytes on-chip RAM (Data Memory)** — Divided into 4 register banks (32 bytes), 16 bytes of bit-addressable memory, and 80 bytes of general-purpose scratchpad.

*(Award 1 mark per feature; 1 additional mark for mentioning size/unit precision.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) in 8051? What is its reset value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an 8-bit register that holds the address of the **top of the stack** in the internal data RAM. The stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses) in the 8051.

- **Role 1:** SP automatically increments before a `PUSH` and decrements after a `POP` operation.
- **Role 2:** SP points to the next free location for the stack during `LCALL`, `ACALL`, and interrupt service routines.
- **Role 3:** SP value determines where the stack physically resides in RAM (must be initialised).

**Reset value of SP = 07H** (i.e., the stack begins at location 08H after the first PUSH). For real applications, **SP is reinitialised to a higher address (e.g., 30H or 60H)** to avoid corrupting register bank 1.

*(Award 1 mark each for: SP definition, function/use, and reset value = 07H.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (Choose ONE of TWO; 14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a) Draw the architectural block diagram of 8051 and explain the function of each block.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above.)**

**Functions of each block (1 mark each for any 5–7 blocks):**

1. **CPU + ALU** — Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logical (AND, OR, XOR), rotate, swap, and Boolean (bit) operations. The accumulator A is the primary operand and result register.

2. **Program Counter (PC)** — 16-bit register that points to the next instruction to be fetched from code memory. Auto-increments after each fetch; jumps/branches modify it explicitly.

3. **Data Pointer (DPTR)** — 16-bit register (DPH : DPL) used to point to data in **external data memory** (XDATA) for `MOVX` instructions, and to look up tables in code memory via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.

4. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — 8-bit, points to top of internal RAM stack. Initialised to 07H on reset; used by `LCALL`, `ACALL`, `PUSH`, `POP`, and interrupt handlers.

5. **PSW (Program Status Word)** — Holds flags: CY (carry), AC (auxiliary carry for BCD), OV (overflow), P (parity), RS1/RS0 (register bank select), F0 (user flag).

6. **Internal ROM (4 KB)** — Stores the program; non-volatile. Address range 0000H–0FFFH internally; external code memory from 1000H–FFFFH (if `EA̅ = 0`).

7. **Internal RAM (128 B)** — Volatile working memory. Lower 32 B is 4 register banks; 20H–2FH is bit-addressable; 30H–7FH is general purpose.

8. **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — 128 bytes at 80H–FFH. Control port latches, timer modes, serial port, interrupt enables, etc.

9. **Oscillator & Clock** — On-chip amplifier with external crystal at XTAL1/XTAL2. Generates the master clock; 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.

10. **I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** — Four 8-bit bidirectional ports. P0 and P2 multiplex external address/data bus when accessing external memory.

11. **Timers T0 & T1** — Two 16-bit up-counters, usable in timer or counter mode, with 4 operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3).

12. **Serial Port (UART)** — Full-duplex asynchronous receiver-transmitter. 4 modes; standard 8051 uses Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud).

13. **Interrupt Controller** — 5 sources (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial); 2 priority levels; vector locations 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.

**Key features to add (1 mark for 2 of them):**
- Harvard architecture (separate program + data buses)
- Boolean processor (bit-level operations)
- On-chip oscillator
- Power saving (Idle & Power-Down)

---

**(b) Explain the internal RAM organisation of 8051 with a neat diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051's 128-byte internal data RAM (00H–7FH) is partitioned into three distinct functional regions:

| Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Register Banks** | 00H–1FH | 32 B | Four banks of 8 registers (R0–R7) |
| **Bit-Addressable Area** | 20H–2FH | 16 B | 128 individually addressable bits |
| **General Purpose RAM** | 30H–7FH | 80 B | Stack + scratchpad variables |

**Register Banks Detail:**

| Bank | Address | Selected by RS1,RS0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Bank 0 | 00H–07H | 0, 0 (default) |
| Bank 1 | 08H–0FH | 0, 1 |
| Bank 2 | 10H–17H | 1, 0 |
| Bank 3 | 18H–1FH | 1, 1 |

**Why multiple banks?** — During context switching, the CPU can switch banks to save a full set of R0–R7 instantly without PUSH/POP. Only **one bank is active** at a time, chosen by PSW bits RS1, RS0.

**Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):**

This 16-byte region allows **direct single-bit access** using instructions like `SETB 20H.0`, `CLR 22H.5`. The 128 bits are numbered **00H–7FH** (bit addresses), separate from byte addresses. This feature is heavily used in **Boolean processing** for control applications.

**General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):**

Free for user variables and for the **stack** (the stack starts at the SP value and grows upward). Best practice: initialise SP to **30H or higher** to keep the stack out of the register bank area.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.3 — Internal Data RAM subgraph.)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model]

**(a) Explain the PSW (Program Status Word) register of 8051. List the function of each flag bit.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **PSW (Program Status Word)** is an 8-bit SFR at address **D0H** that contains the **flag bits** of the CPU and the **register-bank-select bits**.

| Bit | Symbol | Address (bit) | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 7 | **CY** | D7H | **Carry Flag** — Set when an arithmetic operation produces a carry out of bit 7 (addition) or borrow (subtraction). Used in multi-byte arithmetic. |
| 6 | **AC** | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry Flag** — Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4. Used in **BCD arithmetic** with `DA A` instruction. |
| 5 | **F0** | D5H | **User-defined Flag 0** — Available for general-purpose software use. |
| 4 | **RS1** | D4H | **Register Bank Select 1** — High bit of bank selector. |
| 3 | **RS0** | D3H | **Register Bank Select 0** — Low bit of bank selector. |
| 2 | **OV** | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — Set when a signed arithmetic operation overflows. Used by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. |
| 1 | — | D1H | **Reserved** (undefined; user must not rely on this bit). |
| 0 | **P** | D0H | **Parity Flag** — Automatically set/cleared to make the accumulator contain an **even number of 1s** (even parity). |

**Register Bank Selection Logic:**

$$
\text{Bank} = 2 \times RS1 + RS0
$$

| RS1 | RS0 | Active Bank | Address |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

**Important Notes:**
- The **P flag is updated only on instructions that write to A** (the accumulator).
- The **AC flag is only meaningful in BCD operations** (Decimal Adjust).
- The **OV flag** is set when both carry into MSB and carry out of MSB differ (XOR of C7 and C6 in addition).

**Example:** After `MOV A, #0FFH` followed by `ADD A, #01H`, CY = 1 (overflow out of 8 bits), OV = 0 (no signed overflow because 255 + 1 = 256, which fits the unsigned range).

---

**(b) Explain the Timer/Counter operation of 8051 in Mode 1. Show how delay is generated with a calculation example.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 1: 16-bit Timer/Counter**

In Mode 1, the timer register is **16 bits wide** — formed by **THx (high byte)** and **TLx (low byte)**, where x is 0 or 1.

- When the **TRx** bit in **TCON** is set, the timer increments on every machine cycle (timer mode) or every external pulse on Tx pin (counter mode).
- The timer **overflows** when it rolls over from **FFFFH to 0000H**, setting the **TFx** flag and generating an interrupt (if enabled).
- After overflow, the timer **does not auto-reload** — the program must re-write THx and TLx.

**TMOD configuration for Mode 1 (Timer 0, software control, internal clock):**

$$
\text{TMOD} = 0\text{x}01
$$

(where `M1 = 0`, `M0 = 1` for Mode 1, and `C/T̅ = 0` for timer mode)

**Delay Calculation Formula:**

$$
T_{delay} = (2^{16} - \text{count value}) \times T_{MC}
$$

$$
T_{delay} = (65536 - \text{count value}) \times \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}
$$

**Worked Example:**

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **50 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with $f_{OSC} = 12$ MHz.

**Step 1 — Machine cycle time:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Required number of timer ticks:**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Initial count value:**

$$
\text{count value} = 65536 - 50{,}000 = 15{,}536
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to TH0 and TL0:**

$$
15{,}536 \text{ in hex} = 0\text{x}3\text{CB}0
$$

$$
\therefore TH0 = 0\text{x}3C, \quad TL0 = 0\text{x}B0
$$

**Step 5 — Assembly code snippet:**

```assembly
MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, timer mode
MOV TH0, #3CH      ; Load high byte
MOV TL0, #0B0H     ; Load low byte
SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
HERE: JNB TF0, HERE ; Wait until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
CLR TR0            ; Stop timer
CLR TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
```

**Result:** The CPU waits at the `JNB` loop for exactly 50 ms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing machine cycle with oscillator period** — many students write $T = 1/f_{OSC}$ (1 mark lost). The correct relation is $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$.
> 2. **Writing the reload value in decimal** — always give the **hex value** for THx and TLx, with both bytes shown.
> 3. **Forgetting to clear TFx** — if you don't clear TF0/TR0, the timer never re-triggers.
> 4. **Missing SMOD bit in baud-rate questions** — default SMOD = 0; explicitly mention it in the solution.
> 5. **Confusing internal code memory with external code memory** — internal is 4 KB; addressing 64 KB refers to **external** memory.
> 6. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling arrows / data flow** — examiners deduct for unlabeled diagrams.
> 7. **Writing `PSW` flags in wrong order** — the standard order is CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P (bit 7 to bit 0).
> 8. **Using 8255 PIA addresses (80H, etc.) for SFR discussion** — 8051 SFRs have **specific meanings** (P0=80H, SP=81H, etc.), not generic port addresses.
> 9. **Forgetting that the stack grows UP** — many students assume it grows down. This is a guaranteed mark-losing error.
> 10. **Not specifying 11.0592 MHz vs 12 MHz** — when the question mentions serial communication, default to **11.0592 MHz** unless told otherwise.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Memorisation List for KTU ESE:**

- **Architecture type:** Harvard (separate code & data buses), CISC, 8-bit.
- **Crystal & machine cycle:** $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$. At 11.0592 MHz → **1.085 μs**; at 12 MHz → **1 μs**.
- **On-chip RAM:** **128 bytes** (00H–7FH).
- **Register banks:** 4 banks × 8 bytes = 32 bytes (00H–1FH).
- **Bit-addressable RAM:** 16 bytes (20H–2FH) = **128 bits**.
- **Bit-addressable SFRs:** 16 SFRs = 128 bits.
- **SFR space:** 80H–FFH (128 bytes, only 21 SFRs defined in basic 8051).
- **Reset values:** PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, all ports = FFH, PSW = 00H.
- **Reset vector:** 0000H; **Interrupt vectors:** 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.
- **P0** → multiplexed AD0–AD7 (open drain — needs external pull-ups).
- **P2** → A8–A15 high address byte.
- **P3** alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅.
- **EA̅ = 0** → all code from external ROM; **EA̅ = 1** → internal 4 KB, then external above 0FFFH.
- **PSEN̅** → Program Store Enable (used to read external code memory).
- **ALE** → Address Latch Enable (latches the low byte of address from P0).
- **8051 has 5 interrupt sources, 2 priority levels.**
- **Baud rate formula (Mode 1, SMOD=0):** $\text{Baud} = f_{OSC} / [32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)]$.
- **Standard reload for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz:** TH1 = 0xFD (253).
- **DPTR** is the only 16-bit data pointer for `MOVX` and `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`.
- **MUL AB** → result in B:A (16-bit). **DIV AB** → quotient in A, remainder in B.
- **Stack grows UP** (SP increments on PUSH, decrements on POP).
- **Power saving modes:** Idle (CPU clock stopped, peripherals run) and Power-Down (entire oscillator stopped).
- **Bit instructions:** `SETB bit`, `CLR bit`, `ANL C, bit`, `ORL C, bit`, `MOV C, bit`.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Board Trick:** "8051 = 8-bit, 4K ROM, 128B RAM, 4 Ports, 2 Timers, 1 UART, 5 Interrupts, 2 Priorities." This single line answers 60% of definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers (SFRs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Special Function Registers (SFRs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated **byte-addressable on-chip memory locations** in the 8051 microcontroller (addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**) that control and interface with the **integrated peripheral hardware** of the device. Each SFR directly maps to a specific hardware function such as I/O ports, timers, serial communication, interrupt system, accumulator, stack pointer, and program status word.

In the standard **8051 architecture**, the internal RAM is divided into:
- **Lower 128 bytes** (0x00 – 0x7F) — General Purpose RAM (user-accessible).
- **Upper 128 bytes** (0x80 – 0xFF) — **SFR region** (hardware control).
- The same addresses 0x80–0xFF are accessed using *different addressing modes* — direct addressing reaches SFRs, while indirect addressing reaches upper RAM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Control Room of a Building**

Imagine a large office building (the **8051 microcontroller**). The lower RAM is the **office workspace** where employees (your variables) do their jobs. The **SFRs** are the **manager's control room** on the top floor, containing dedicated switches, gauges, and levers — one switch controls the elevator (Timer 0), another controls the intercom system (Serial Port), and another handles the fire alarm (Interrupt System). You cannot move these switches to another room; they have **fixed addresses** in the control room, and flipping them operates the corresponding hardware. The **PSW** is the manager's status clipboard showing the current condition flags, and the **Accumulator (ACC)** is the primary desk where the most important paperwork (data) is processed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Key Metrics

- **SFR Address Range:** 0x80 – 0xFF (128 bytes)
- **Number of Defined SFRs in standard 8051:** **21 SFRs** occupying **26 byte locations** (some SFRs are bit-addressable).
- **Bit-Addressable SFRs:** Those whose addresses end in 0x0 or 0x8 (multiples of 8): ACC (0xE0), B (0xF0), PSW (0xD0), IP (0xB8), IE (0xA8), SCON (0x98), TCON (0x88), P0 (0x80), P1 (0x90), P2 (0xA0), P3 (0xB0).
- **Total Bit-Addressable Bits in SFRs:** **83 bits** (from 0x80 to 0xFF, excluding gaps).

### 1.4 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Outcomes (CO Mapping):**
> - **CO3:** Understand the internal architecture and memory organization of 8051.
> - **CO4:** Interface and program on-chip peripherals using SFRs.
> - **Bloom's Level:** *Apply / Analyze* — Students must be able to identify and configure SFRs for I/O, timer, and serial operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of SFRs

The 21 standard SFRs of the 8051 are grouped into functional categories:

1. **CPU Registers:** A (Accumulator), B Register, PSW, SP, DPTR (DPH+DPL)
2. **I/O Port Latches:** P0, P1, P2, P3
3. **Timer Registers:** TCON, TMOD, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1
4. **Serial Port Registers:** SCON, SBUF, PCON (SMOD bit)
5. **Interrupt Registers:** IE, IP
6. **Power Control:** PCON

### 2.2 Detailed SFR Map (KTU High-Yield Table)

| SFR Symbol | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable? | Reset Value (Binary) | Primary Function |
|:-----------|:-------------:|:----------------:|:--------------------:|:-----------------|
| **P0**     | 0x80          | Yes (P0.0–P0.7)  | 1111 1111            | Port 0 latch (also AD0–AD7 / data bus) |
| **SP**     | 0x81          | No               | 0000 0111            | Stack Pointer (resets to 0x07) |
| **DPL**    | 0x82          | No               | 0000 0000            | Data Pointer Low byte |
| **DPH**    | 0x83          | No               | 0000 0000            | Data Pointer High byte |
| **PCON**   | 0x87          | No               | 0xxx 0000            | Power Control (SMOD, PD, IDL) |
| **TCON**   | 0x88          | Yes              | 0000 0000            | Timer/Counter Control flags |
| **TMOD**   | 0x89          | No               | 0000 0000            | Timer/Counter Mode register |
| **TL0**    | 0x8A          | No               | 0000 0000            | Timer 0 Low byte |
| **TL1**    | 0x8B          | No               | 0000 0000            | Timer 1 Low byte |
| **TH0**    | 0x8C          | No               | 0000 0000            | Timer 0 High byte |
| **TH1**    | 0x8D          | No               | 0000 0000            | Timer 1 High byte |
| **P1**     | 0x90          | Yes              | 1111 1111            | Port 1 latch |
| **SCON**   | 0x98          | Yes              | 0000 0000            | Serial Control register |
| **SBUF**   | 0x99          | No               | xxxx xxxx            | Serial Data Buffer (Tx/Rx) |
| **P2**     | 0xA0          | Yes              | 1111 1111            | Port 2 latch (A8–A15) |
| **IE**     | 0xA8          | Yes              | 0xx0 0000            | Interrupt Enable register |
| **P3**     | 0xB0          | Yes              | 1111 1111            | Port 3 latch (alternate functions) |
| **IP**     | 0xB8          | Yes              | xxx0 0000            | Interrupt Priority register |
| **PSW**    | 0xD0          | Yes              | 0000 0000            | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **ACC**    | 0xE0          | Yes              | 0000 0000            | Accumulator |
| **B**      | 0xF0          | Yes              | 0000 0000            | B Register (multiplication/division) |

### 2.3 PSW (Program Status Word) — Bit Structure

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Symbol | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | **—** | **P** |
| Name | Carry | Aux Carry | Flag 0 | Register Bank Select 1 | Register Bank Select 0 | Overflow | Reserved | Parity |

> [!NOTE]
> **RS1:RS0** Register Bank Selection: `00` → Bank 0 (0x00–0x07), `01` → Bank 1 (0x08–0x0F), `10` → Bank 2 (0x10–0x17), `11` → Bank 3 (0x18–0x1F).

### 2.4 TCON (Timer Control Register) — Bit Structure

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Symbol | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TR0/TR1** — Timer Run control bits (1 = Start, 0 = Stop).
- **TF0/TF1** — Timer Overflow flags (set by hardware on overflow).
- **IT0/IT1** — Interrupt Type (1 = Edge triggered, 0 = Level triggered).

### 2.5 TMOD (Timer Mode Register) — Byte Structure

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| **GATE** | **C/T̅** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̅** | **M1** | **M0** |
| Timer 1 ←—————————→ |   |   | Timer 0 ←—————————→ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer, **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer, **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload, **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 only).

### 2.6 SCON (Serial Control Register) — Bit Structure

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Symbol | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |

### 2.7 IE (Interrupt Enable) — Bit Structure

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Symbol | **EA** | **—** | **—** | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |

- **EA** — Global enable (must be 1 for any interrupt to respond).
- **ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0** — Individual enables for Serial, Timer 1, External 1, Timer 0, External 0.

### 2.8 Timer Count Formula (Key Equation)

For Mode 1 (16-bit timer):

$$T_{delay} = (2^{16} - N) \times T_{machine}$$

Where:
- $N$ = Timer initial value (TH + TL)
- $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ (for standard 8051, 12 clock cycles per machine cycle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This formula is **critical** for KTU board problems involving generating precise delays, square wave generation, and event scheduling using 8051 timers.

### 2.9 Baud Rate Formula (Serial Port Mode 1)

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

Where $SMOD$ is the PCON.7 bit. This formula is **essential** for KTU serial communication problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation for Mode 1

**Problem Statement:** Compute the timer reload value $N$ required to generate a delay of **50 ms** on an 8051 system with crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, configured in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**.

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period:**

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Required Number of Timer Ticks:**

$$N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46082 \text{ ticks}$$

**Step 3 — Timer Initial Value:**

In Mode 1, the timer counts up from the loaded value to overflow at $2^{16} = 65536$. So the initial value $N$ is:

$$N = 2^{16} - N_{ticks} = 65536 - 46082 = 19454$$

**Step 4 — Splitting into High and Low Bytes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{decimal} &= 19454 \\
\text{Convert to hex:} \quad 19454 \div 16 &= 1215 \text{ remainder } 14 \;(E) \\
1215 \div 16 &= 75 \text{ remainder } 15 \;(F) \\
75 \div 16 &= 4 \text{ remainder } 11 \;(B) \\
4 \div 16 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \;(4) \\
\therefore 19454_{10} &= 4BFE_{H} \\
\text{TH} &= 0x4B \\
\text{TL} &= 0xFE
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Counts from } N &= 0x4BFE = 19454 \\
\text{Total counts to overflow} &= 65536 - 19454 = 46082 \\
\text{Delay} &= 46082 \times 1.085 \mu s \approx 50.0 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Baud Rate Calculation for Serial Communication

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the TH1 reload value to achieve a **9600 baud** rate in **Mode 1** with $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ and $SMOD = 0$.

**Step 1 — Substitute Known Values into the Baud Rate Formula:**

$$9600 = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify Constants:**

$$\begin{aligned}
9600 &= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} \\
9600 \times 32 &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} \\
307200 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1) &= 11.0592 \times 10^{6} \\
(256 - TH1) &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{307200 \times 12} \\
(256 - TH1) &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}} \\
(256 - TH1) &= 3 \\
TH1 &= 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{1152} = 9600 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz crystal?** This exact frequency is used in 8051 systems because it produces integer divisor values for **standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200)**. At 12 MHz, only Mode 0 gives a clean baud rate.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bit-Addressable vs Byte-Addressable SFRs

| Property | Bit-Addressable SFRs | Non-Bit-Addressable SFRs |
|:---------|:--------------------:|:------------------------:|
| Addresses | End in 0x0 or 0x8 | All other addresses |
| Individual bit access | Yes (`SETB P1.0`) | No (must operate on full byte) |
| Used for | Control flags, I/O pins | Data buffers, count values |
| Examples | P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, PSW, ACC, B | SP, TMOD, TH0/1, TL0/1, SBUF, PCON, DPH, DPL |
| KTU tip | Often tested in bit-level assembly | Often tested for initialization values |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SFR Access vs Internal RAM Access (Same Address)

| Property | SFR Region (0x80–0xFF) | Upper Internal RAM (0x80–0xFF) |
|:---------|:----------------------:|:------------------------------:|
| Addressing Mode | **Direct Addressing** | **Indirect Addressing** (using @R0/@R1) |
| Example | `MOV A, 0x90` → reads P1 | `MOV A, @R0` (R0=0x90) → reads upper RAM byte at 0x90 |
| 8051 ASM mnemonic | `MOV A, P1` (assembler translates) | `MOV A, @R0` |
| Coexistence | Both share the **same physical addresses**; selected by addressing mode |
| KTU trap | Forgetting the addressing mode distinction loses full marks |

### 3.5 Reset Values of Critical SFRs (Important for Initialization)

| SFR | Reset Value | Implication |
|:----|:-----------:|:-----------:|
| SP | 0x07 | Stack starts at 0x08 — **must reinitialize to 0x70** for safe operation |
| P0, P1, P2, P3 | 0xFF | All ports = 1 (high-impedance inputs ready) |
| ACC, B, PSW | 0x00 | Bank 0 selected, all flags cleared |
| PCON | 0x00 | SMOD = 0 (no baud rate doubling) |
| SCON, TCON, IE, IP | 0x00 | All interrupts disabled, timers stopped |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Internal Memory Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[8051 Internal Memory Architecture] --> B[Internal RAM 256 Bytes - 0x00 to 0xFF]
    A --> C[Program Memory - Up to 64KB]

    B --> D[Lower 128 Bytes - 0x00 to 0x7F]
    B --> E[Upper 128 Bytes - 0x80 to 0xFF]

    D --> D1[Register Banks R0-R7 - 0x00 to 0x1F]
    D --> D2[Bit Addressable Region - 0x20 to 0x2F - 16 Bytes - 128 Bits]
    D --> D3[General Purpose RAM - 0x30 to 0x7F]

    D1 --> D1a[Bank 0: 0x00-0x07]
    D1 --> D1b[Bank 1: 0x08-0x0F]
    D1 --> D1c[Bank 2: 0x10-0x17]
    D1 --> D1d[Bank 3: 0x18-0x1F]

    E --> E1[SFR Region - Direct Addressing - 0x80 to 0xFF]
    E --> E2[Upper General RAM - Indirect Addressing - 0x80 to 0xFF]

    E1 --> F1[CPU Registers: ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPH, DPL]
    E1 --> F2[I/O Ports: P0, P1, P2, P3]
    E1 --> F3[Timer: TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1]
    E1 --> F4[Serial: SCON, SBUF, PCON]
    E1 --> F5[Interrupt: IE, IP]

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2d5a87,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style E2 fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#e67e22,color:#ffffff
    style F2 fill:#16a085,color:#ffffff
    style F3 fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
    style F4 fill:#d35400,color:#ffffff
    style F5 fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 SFR Functional Grouping Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SFR[8051 SFR Block - 0x80 to 0xFF]

    SFR --> CPU[CPU Control Group]
    SFR --> IO[I/O Port Group]
    SFR --> TIM[Timer Group]
    SFR --> SER[Serial Group]
    SFR --> INT[Interrupt Group]
    SFR --> PWR[Power Group]

    CPU --> A[ACC - 0xE0]
    CPU --> B[B - 0xF0]
    CPU --> P[PSW - 0xD0]
    CPU --> S[SP - 0x81]
    CPU --> D[DPTR - 0x82-0x83]

    IO --> P0[P0 - 0x80]
    IO --> P1[P1 - 0x90]
    IO --> P2[P2 - 0xA0]
    IO --> P3[P3 - 0xB0]

    TIM --> TC[TCON - 0x88]
    TIM --> TM[TMOD - 0x89]
    TIM --> T0[TH0/TL0 - 0x8C/0x8A]
    TIM --> T1[TH1/TL1 - 0x8D/0x8B]

    SER --> SC[SCON - 0x98]
    SER --> SB[SBUF - 0x99]
    SER --> PC[PCON - 0x87]

    INT --> IE[IE - 0xA8]
    INT --> IP[IP - 0xB8]

    style SFR fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff
    style CPU fill:#e74c3c,color:#ffffff
    style IO fill:#27ae60,color:#ffffff
    style TIM fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
    style SER fill:#d35400,color:#ffffff
    style INT fill:#c0392b,color:#ffffff
    style PWR fill:#7f8c8d,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 PSW Bit-Field Functional Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSW[PSW Register - 0xD0 - Bit Addressable]

    PSW --> CY[Bit 7 - CY - Carry Flag - Set on arithmetic carry/borrow]
    PSW --> AC[Bit 6 - AC - Auxiliary Carry - BCD operations]
    PSW --> F0[Bit 5 - F0 - User-defined Flag]
    PSW --> RS1[Bit 4 - RS1 - Bank Select High]
    PSW --> RS0[Bit 3 - RS0 - Bank Select Low]
    PSW --> OV[Bit 2 - OV - Overflow Flag - Signed arithmetic]
    PSW --> RS[Bit 1 - Reserved]
    PSW --> P[Bit 0 - P - Parity Flag - Auto set by hardware]

    RS1 --> BA[Banks Selection Logic]
    RS0 --> BA
    BA --> BA0[RS1=0,RS0=0: Bank 0 at 0x00]
    BA --> BA1[RS1=0,RS0=1: Bank 1 at 0x08]
    BA --> BA2[RS1=1,RS0=0: Bank 2 at 0x10]
    BA --> BA3[RS1=1,RS0=1: Bank 3 at 0x18]

    style P fill:#8e44ad,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Timer System Block — SFR Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[Crystal Oscillator f_osc] --> MC[Machine Cycle Generator - Divide by 12]
    MC --> T0[Timer 0 Counter]
    MC --> T1[Timer 1 Counter]

    T0 --> TH0[TH0 - 0x8C]
    T0 --> TL0[TL0 - 0x8A]
    T1 --> TH1[TH1 - 0x8D]
    T1 --> TL1[TL1 - 0x8B]

    TMOD[TMOD - 0x89 - Mode Select] -.Controls.-> T0
    TMOD -.Controls.-> T1
    TCON[TCON - 0x88 - Control Flags] -.Controls.-> T0
    TCON -.Controls.-> T1

    T0 -.Sets TF0.-> TCON
    T1 -.Sets TF1.-> TCON

    TCON -.Triggers.-> IE[IE - 0xA8 - Interrupt Enable]
    IE -.Enables.-> CPU[CPU - Interrupt Service Routine]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Define Special Function Registers (SFRs) in the 8051 microcontroller. List any six SFRs with their addresses.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** SFRs are dedicated on-chip memory-mapped registers in the 8051 located in the address range **0x80 to 0xFF**. They are used to control the on-chip peripherals (I/O ports, timers, serial port, interrupts) and the CPU core functions (accumulator, stack pointer, program status word).

**Six SFRs:**

| SFR | Address (Hex) |
|:----|:-------------:|
| P0 | 0x80 |
| SP | 0x81 |
| P1 | 0x90 |
| SCON | 0x98 |
| IE | 0xA8 |
| PSW | 0xD0 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Explain the bit structure of the PSW register and the function of each bit.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PSW (0xD0) is a bit-addressable SFR that holds CPU status flags. Its bit structure:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|:---:|:------:|:--------|
| 7 | CY | Carry flag — set on arithmetic carry/borrow |
| 6 | AC | Auxiliary carry — for BCD arithmetic |
| 5 | F0 | General-purpose user flag |
| 4 | RS1 | Register bank select MSB |
| 3 | RS0 | Register bank select LSB |
| 2 | OV | Overflow flag for signed arithmetic |
| 1 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | P | Parity flag — automatically set/cleared by hardware |

**RS1:RS0 = 00** selects Bank 0 (0x00–0x07); **01** → Bank 1 (0x08–0x0F); **10** → Bank 2 (0x10–0x17); **11** → Bank 3 (0x18–0x1F).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the bit structure of the **TMOD and TCON** registers. Discuss the four operating modes of the 8051 timers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an 8051 system with crystal frequency **12 MHz**, calculate the value to be loaded into the **TH0 and TL0** registers to generate a delay of **5 ms** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1**. Write the corresponding 8051 assembly program. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Q.A(a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **TMOD (0x89) — Timer Mode Register (NOT bit-addressable):**
>
> High nibble controls **Timer 1**, low nibble controls **Timer 0**.
>
> | Bit | Name | Function |
> |:---:|:----:|:---------|
> | 7/3 | GATE | 1 = Hardware control via INTx pin, 0 = Software control (TRx) |
> | 6/2 | C/T̅ | 1 = Counter mode (external pulses), 0 = Timer mode (internal clock) |
> | 5/1 | M1 | Mode select MSB |
> | 4/0 | M0 | Mode select LSB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCON (0x88) — Timer Control Register (bit-addressable):**
>
> | Bit | Symbol | Function |
> |:---:|:------:|:---------|
> | 7 | TF1 | Timer 1 overflow flag (set by hardware) |
> | 6 | TR1 | Timer 1 run control (1 = start) |
> | 5 | TF0 | Timer 0 overflow flag |
> | 4 | TR0 | Timer 0 run control |
> | 3 | IE1 | External interrupt 1 edge flag |
> | 2 | IT1 | External interrupt 1 type (edge/level) |
> | 1 | IE0 | External interrupt 0 edge flag |
> | 0 | IT0 | External interrupt 0 type |

**Four Timer Modes:**

| Mode | M1:M0 | Description | Max Count |
|:----:|:-----:|:------------|:---------:|
| Mode 0 | 00 | 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility) | $2^{13} = 8192$ |
| Mode 1 | 01 | **16-bit timer** (most common) | $2^{16} = 65536$ |
| Mode 2 | 10 | **8-bit auto-reload** (TH holds reload value) | $2^{8} = 256$ |
| Mode 3 | 11 | **Split timer** (Timer 0 only — TL0 + TH0 act as two 8-bit timers) | 256 each |

---

#### **Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Number of Ticks Required:**

$$N_{ticks} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 5000 \text{ ticks}$$

**Step 3 — Initial Timer Value (16-bit Mode 1):**

$$N = 2^{16} - 5000 = 65536 - 5000 = 60536$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hex:**

$$\begin{aligned}
60536 \div 16 &= 3783 \text{ R } 8 \\
3783 \div 16 &= 236 \text{ R } 7 \\
236 \div 16 &= 14 \text{ R } 12 \;(C) \\
14 \div 16 &= 0 \text{ R } 14 \;(E) \\
\therefore 60536_{10} &= EC78_{H} \\
\therefore TH0 &= 0xEC \\
\therefore TL0 &= 0x78
\end{aligned}$$

**8051 Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Software control
        MOV TH0, #0ECH    ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #078H    ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0          ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE     ; Wait for overflow (TF0 = 1)
        CLR TR0           ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0           ; Clear overflow flag
        END
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **SCON** and **PCON** registers of the 8051. List the four serial communication modes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **TH1** value required to generate a baud rate of **4800 bps** in **Mode 1** with crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz** and **SMOD = 1**. Show the assembly code to initialize the serial port and transmit a byte. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Q.B(a):**

> [!NOTE]
> **SCON (0x98) — Serial Control Register (bit-addressable):**
>
> | Bit | Symbol | Function |
> |:---:|:------:|:---------|
> | 7 | SM0 | Serial mode MSB |
> | 6 | SM1 | Serial mode LSB |
> | 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable |
> | 4 | REN | Receive enable (must be 1 to receive) |
> | 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2, 3) |
> | 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (Modes 2, 3) |
> | 1 | TI | Transmit interrupt flag (set after byte sent) |
> | 0 | RI | Receive interrupt flag (set after byte received) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCON (0x87) — Power Control Register (NOT bit-addressable):**
>
> | Bit | Symbol | Function |
> |:---:|:------:|:---------|
> | 7 | SMOD | Baud rate doubler (1 = double baud rate) |
> | 6 | — | Reserved |
> | 5 | — | Reserved |
> | 4 | — | Reserved |
> | 3 | GF1 | General-purpose flag |
> | 2 | GF0 | General-purpose flag |
> | 1 | PD | Power Down mode (set to 1 for sleep) |
> | 0 | IDL | Idle mode (set to 1 for idle) |

**Serial Modes:**

| Mode | SM0:SM1 | Description | Baud Rate |
|:----:|:-------:|:------------|:---------:|
| Mode 0 | 00 | 8-bit Shift Register | $f_{osc}/12$ (fixed) |
| Mode 1 | 01 | 8-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1) |
| Mode 2 | 10 | 9-bit UART | $f_{osc}/32$ or $f_{osc}/64$ |
| Mode 3 | 11 | 9-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1) |

---

#### **Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Baud Rate Formula with SMOD = 1:**

$$4800 = \frac{2^{1}}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for TH1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
4800 &= \frac{2}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} \\
4800 &= \frac{1}{16} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} \\
4800 \times 16 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1) &= 11.0592 \times 10^{6} \\
921600 \times (256 - TH1) &= 11.0592 \times 10^{6} \\
(256 - TH1) &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{921600} \\
(256 - TH1) &= 12 \\
TH1 &= 256 - 12 = 244 = 0xF4
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{2304} = 4800 \quad \checkmark$$

**8051 Assembly Code:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN enabled, TI=0, RI=0
        MOV PCON, #80H    ; SMOD = 1 (baud rate doubled)
        MOV TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
        MOV TH1, #0F4H    ; Reload value for 4800 baud
        SETB TR1          ; Start Timer 1
TRANSMIT:
        MOV SBUF, #'A'    ; Load character into serial buffer
WAIT:   JNB TI, WAIT      ; Wait until transmission complete
        CLR TI            ; Clear TI flag for next transmission
        SJMP TRANSMIT     ; Repeat
        END
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI flags** after serial operations — costs 1–2 marks consistently.
> 2. **Wrong timer mode selection** — Mode 0 is 13-bit, NOT 16-bit. Many students confuse this.
> 3. **Not resetting SP** — Stack defaults to 0x07 which collides with register Bank 1. Always initialize `MOV SP, #70H`.
> 4. **SFR vs Upper RAM confusion** — Address 0x80 can be P0 (direct) or upper RAM byte (indirect). Examiners **specifically test** this distinction.
> 5. **Missing bit-addressability** — Using `SETB ACC.0` is valid; using `SETB TMOD.0` is **illegal** because TMOD is not bit-addressable.
> 6. **Baud rate formula misuse** — Forgetting the $2^{SMOD}$ factor or using $f_{osc}/12$ instead of $\times 12$ in the denominator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **SFR address range:** 0x80 – 0xFF (128 bytes) — **always quote this in definitions.**
- ✅ **Total SFRs:** 21 named, occupying 26 byte locations.
- ✅ **Bit-addressable SFRs (11 total):** P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B.
- ✅ **Same address, different addressing:** Direct addressing → SFR; Indirect addressing (`@R0/@R1`) → Upper 128 RAM.
- ✅ **ACC address:** 0xE0 | **B address:** 0xF0 | **PSW address:** 0xD0 | **SP address:** 0x81.
- ✅ **DPTR is a 16-bit register** formed by DPH (0x83) + DPL (0x82) — used for external data memory access via `MOVX`.
- ✅ **PSW RS1:RS0** controls register bank selection — only one bank is active at a time.
- ✅ **P0** is open-drain and needs external pull-ups; **P1, P2, P3** have internal pull-ups.
- ✅ **P3 alternate functions:** P3.0 (RxD), P3.1 (TxD), P3.2 (INT0), P3.3 (INT1), P3.4 (T0), P3.5 (T1), P3.6 (WR̅), P3.7 (RD̅).
- ✅ **TMOD is non-bit-addressable** — write the entire byte at once (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #01H`).
- ✅ **TCON is bit-addressable** — individual bits like `TR0`, `TF0` can be set/cleared separately.
- ✅ **Timer delay formula:** $N = 2^{16} - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}}$ (Mode 1).
- ✅ **Baud rate formula:** $BR = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$.
- ✅ **SBUF** is a single address (0x99) but operates as **two registers** — write to it (sends) and read from it (receives).
- ✅ **Reset value of SP = 0x07** — always reinitialize to 0x70 for safe stack placement.
- ✅ **IE register must have EA = 1** to enable any interrupt; individual bits enable specific sources.
- ✅ **SCON reset value = 0x00** — serial port is OFF; must initialize with `MOV SCON, #50H` for Mode 1 reception.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Pins Ports and Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** I/O Pins Ports and Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **MCS-51 family of microcontrollers** (the 8051 architecture, which is the canonical KTU 2024 syllabus target), the term **I/O Pins, Ports, and Circuits** refers to the structured hardware interface between the CPU core and the external physical world. Specifically:

- **I/O Pin**: An individual physical metal contact on the microcontroller package through which a single binary signal enters or exits the silicon die. Each pin is bidirectional or quasi-bidirectional.
- **I/O Port**: A logical grouping of **8 I/O pins** (P0, P1, P2, P3) under the control of a **Special Function Register (SFR)** with the same name. The port SFR latches the logical value driven on, or read from, the corresponding pins.
- **I/O Circuit**: The internal transistor-level network (latch, output driver, input buffer) embedded on-chip that connects the SFR to the physical pin. In the 8051, this circuit is a unique **quasi-bidirectional** I/O structure with internal pull-ups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**: The student must be able to draw the internal structure of an 8051 I/O port pin, explain its quasi-bidirectional nature, distinguish the role of each of the four ports (P0, P1, P2, P3), and list the **alternate functions** multiplexed onto Port 3 and Port 0/Pins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large office building** (the 8051 chip):

- The **CEO's office** is the **CPU/ALU** that processes information.
- The **CEO does not walk outside to talk to customers**; instead, the company employs **receptionists** at the front desk.
- Each **receptionist = 1 I/O pin** — a single point of contact with the outside world.
- The **reception desk counter = I/O Port** (a group of 8 receptionists working together, each handling one bit of an 8-bit message, like 8 operators on a switchboard).
- The **intercom system and desk telephone wiring = I/O circuit** — the internal hardware that lets the CEO "talk" to the receptionist, who then "shouts" the message outside. Importantly, the receptionist can both *speak out* (output) and *listen in* (input), but with a quirky rule: while listening, there's a small **weak internal spring** (pull-up) that gently tugs the line HIGH unless someone actively pulls it LOW. This is the famous **quasi-bidirectional** behavior.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Voltage ($V_{CC}$):** **+5 V DC** (standard 8051; modern variants like AT89C51 also operate at 5 V).
- **Logic HIGH output voltage ($V_{OH}$):** typically **≥ 2.4 V** (P1, P2, P3); P0 requires external pull-up.
- **Logic LOW output voltage ($V_{OL}$):** typically **≤ 0.45 V**.
- **Logic HIGH input voltage ($V_{IH}$):** typically **≥ 2.0 V**.
- **Logic LOW input voltage ($V_{IL}$):** typically **≤ 0.8 V**.
- **Internal pull-up resistance ($R_{pull-up}$):** approximately **5 kΩ to 40 kΩ** (weak).
- **Sink current capability (per pin):** ≈ **20 mA**; whole port: **≈ 26.5 mA (PORT 0)** and **≈ 15 mA (Ports 1, 2, 3)** — exceeding this damages the chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition to Memorize for KTU**: A **quasi-bidirectional pin** is an I/O pin that can be used as both input and output without reconfiguring its data direction register (unlike modern microcontrollers such as ARM/AVR which use DDR/TRIS). In the 8051, the *same pin* serves as input or output, and writing a '1' to the port latch prepares the pin to act as a high-impedance input while the internal weak pull-up keeps it at logic 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four 8051 Ports at a Glance

| Port | Pins | SFR Address | Internal Pull-up | Special Role | Typical Use |
|------|------|-------------|------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **P0** | 32–39 | **0x80** | **NO pull-up** (open-drain) | Multiplexed **Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)** | External memory interface |
| **P1** | 1–8 | **0x90** | **YES (weak)** | Pure general-purpose I/O | GPIO, user I/O |
| **P2** | 21–28 | **0xA0** | **YES (weak)** | Multiplexed **Higher Address bus (A8–A15)** | External memory interface |
| **P3** | 10–17 | **0xB0** | **YES (weak)** | Has **alternate functions** | Serial, Interrupts, Timers, RD, WR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why P0 has no internal pull-up**: When P0 is used to drive the multiplexed address/data bus during external memory access, it must be able to drive a strong logic HIGH to fully swing the bus. Internal pull-ups are too weak for this bus-driving role. Therefore, P0 is **open-drain**, and the 8051 development boards add an external **10 kΩ pull-up resistor pack**.

## 2.2 The Quasi-Bidirectional I/O — Step by Step Logic

1. **CPU writes '1' to port latch**: The lower transistor (Q1 in textbooks) is OFF. The internal weak pull-up (≈ 5–40 kΩ) gently raises the pin toward $V_{CC}$. The pin is now **ready to be read as input** — it is in a high-impedance state with a small pull-up, so external devices can pull it LOW if needed. This is the "default" or "input-ready" state.
2. **CPU writes '0' to port latch**: The lower transistor turns ON, actively pulling the pin hard to **GND (0 V)**. The pin can sink up to 20 mA. The internal pull-up is overpowered.
3. **CPU reads the port pin**: The internal input buffer samples the voltage on the *physical pin* (not the latch). This is why one must first write '1' to the latch before reading — otherwise the latch output would force a '0' and the read would always return 0.
4. **Port 0 quirk**: Writing '1' to P0 disables the pull-up entirely (P0 has none). Therefore, when P0 is used as input, it **floats** — external pull-ups are mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The "quasi" prefix captures the asymmetry — the pin drives LOW strongly (good sink) and HIGH weakly (good input-readiness). It is a clever single-transistor-per-pin design that saves silicon area, which is why the 8051 was revolutionary in 1980.

## 2.3 Port 3 Alternate Functions (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| P3 Pin | Alternate Function | Direction | Function |
|--------|--------------------|-----------|----------|
| **P3.0** | **RxD** | Input | Serial data receive |
| **P3.1** | **TxD** | Output | Serial data transmit |
| **P3.2** | **$\overline{INT0}$** | Input | External interrupt 0 |
| **P3.3** | **$\overline{INT1}$** | Input | External interrupt 1 |
| **P3.4** | **T0** | Input | Timer 0 external input |
| **P3.5** | **T1** | Input | Timer 1 external input |
| **P3.6** | **$\overline{WR}$** | Output | External memory write strobe |
| **P3.7** | **$\overline{RD}$** | Output | External memory read strobe |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Expression / Value | Description |
|------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Number of I/O ports in 8051 | **4** | P0, P1, P2, P3 |
| Pins per port | **8** | Each port is 8-bit wide |
| Total I/O pins | **32** | 4 × 8 |
| $V_{CC}$ | **+5 V** | Standard supply |
| $V_{IH}$ (min) | **2.0 V** | Recognized as logic 1 |
| $V_{IL}$ (max) | **0.8 V** | Recognized as logic 0 |
| $I_{OL}$ (per pin, max) | **≈ 20 mA** | Sink current |
| $I_{OH}$ (P0 only) | weak (open-drain) | Needs external pull-up |
| Internal pull-up resistor | **≈ 5 kΩ – 40 kΩ** | Weak, varies with temperature |
| SFR address P0 | **0x80** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address P1 | **0x90** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address P2 | **0xA0** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR address P3 | **0xB0** | Bit-addressable |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded product design**: Engineers connect LEDs (via current-limiting resistors ≈ 330 Ω for 5 V), switches (with pull-ups), 7-segment displays, and relays to these ports.
- **External memory expansion**: P0 and P2 together form the 16-bit external address bus to access up to **64 KB** of external ROM/RAM.
- **Communication**: P3.0/P3.1 enable UART-based communication with PCs, sensors, and Bluetooth modules (HC-05).
- **Industrial control**: Port pins drive opto-isolators, MOSFET gates, and buzzer circuits — but never exceed the per-pin current rating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistor for an LED on an 8051 Port Pin

A classic KTU numerical. Suppose an **LED** (forward voltage $V_F = 2.0$ V, desired forward current $I_F = 10$ mA) is connected between a 8051 port pin and ground. Compute the series resistor value $R_S$.

### Given
- Supply voltage from pin HIGH: $V_{OH} \approx V_{CC} = 5$ V (when driving logic 1 on P1/P2/P3; for P0 an external pull-up to $V_{CC}$ is required).
- LED forward voltage: $V_F = 2.0$ V.
- Desired LED current: $I_F = 10$ mA = $0.010$ A.

### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$V_{CC} - V_{R_S} - V_F = 0$$

This means the supply voltage equals the sum of the voltage drop across the resistor and the LED.

### Step 2 — Solve for the voltage drop across the series resistor:

$$V_{R_S} = V_{CC} - V_F$$

$$V_{R_S} = 5\text{ V} - 2.0\text{ V} = 3.0\text{ V}$$

The resistor must drop the *excess* voltage (3.0 V) that the LED does not consume.

### Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to find $R_S$:

$$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_F}$$

$$R_S = \frac{3.0\text{ V}}{0.010\text{ A}} = 300\ \Omega$$

### Step 4 — Choose the nearest standard E12 resistor value:

$$R_S \approx 330\ \Omega \quad \text{(standard E12 value)}$$

### Step 5 — Verify the actual LED current with the chosen resistor:

$$I_{F,actual} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R_S} = \frac{5 - 2.0}{330}$$

$$I_{F,actual} = \frac{3.0}{330} \approx 9.09\ \text{mA}$$

This is safely within the **20 mA per-pin sink current limit** of the 8051, so the design is valid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU**: This derivation is a frequently asked 7-mark question. Always (i) state KVL, (ii) state Ohm's Law, (iii) substitute numeric values, and (iv) choose a standard resistor.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reading vs. Writing a Port

| Action | Code Statement | Internal Operation | Pin State | Trap to Avoid |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Write 0 | `P1 = 0x00;` | Lower FET ON, pin pulled hard to GND | LOW (drives) | None — safe |
| Write 1 | `P1 = 0xFF;` | Lower FET OFF, weak pull-up active | HIGH (weak) or Input-ready | If external load > pull-up strength, voltage sags |
| Read pin (input) | `x = P1;` | Input buffer samples physical pin voltage | Depends on external circuit | **Must write 1 first**, else reads back the latch (always 0) |
| Read port SFR | `x = 0x90;` (direct) | Reads the *latch* contents | Not a pin read | Used in read-modify-write instructions (e.g., `P1 \|= 0x01;`) |

## 3.3 Derivative-Style Reasoning: Why P0 Has No Pull-up

Suppose the internal pull-up existed in P0 with $R_{pull-up} = 10$ kΩ. When the 8051 accesses external memory, the bus is loaded with multiple memory chips' inputs, each presenting a small capacitive load plus leakage. The effective bus load capacitance $C_L$ might be ≈ 100 pF, and to switch this bus in time $t_{rise} \le 20$ ns:

$$I_{required} = \frac{C_L \cdot \Delta V}{t_{rise}}$$

$$I_{required} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 5}{20 \times 10^{-9}} = 25\ \text{mA}$$

But a 10 kΩ pull-up can only supply:

$$I_{pull-up} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{pull-up}} = \frac{5}{10000} = 0.5\ \text{mA}$$

The pull-up is **50× too weak** to switch the bus quickly. Hence, P0 is open-drain and either driven LOW actively (during address time) or pulled HIGH by an external pull-up resistor (typically 4.7 kΩ – 10 kΩ) in real hardware.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8051 Port Pin Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef sfr fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef fet fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef pullup fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000

    Latch["Port Latch D Q - SFR Bit"]:::sfr
    WriteCtrl["Write Pulse from CPU Bus"]:::sfr
    InBuf["Input Buffer to CPU Read Bus"]:::sfr
    Qup["Upper FET P-Channel - Pull-up Driver"]:::fet
    Qdn["Lower FET N-Channel - Sink Driver"]:::fet
    Pullup["Weak Internal Pull-up 5k to 40k ohm"]:::pullup
    Pin["Physical Pin PAD"]:::ext
    ExtCircuit["External Circuit - LED - Switch - Bus Load"]:::ext

    WriteCtrl --> Latch
    Latch -- Q = 1 turns OFF --> Qdn
    Latch -- Q = 0 turns ON --> Qdn
    Latch -- Q = 0 disables --> Qup
    Latch -- Q = 1 enables --> Qup
    Pullup --> Pin
    Qup --> Pin
    Qdn --> Pin
    Pin --> InBuf
    Pin --> ExtCircuit
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: LED + Switch Interfacing on P1 (Classic Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef vcc fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000

    VCC -- unused open drain P0 needs pull up --> R1
    R1 --> LED1
    LED1 --> P10
    P10 --> GND1

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Writing `P1.0 = 0` sinks current through the LED, lighting it. The switch on P1.1 is *active-low* — pressing the switch grounds the pin. The internal weak pull-up of P1 holds P1.1 HIGH when the switch is open, so the program must first write `P1 = 0xFF` and then read `P1` to detect the switch state.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**What is a quasi-bidirectional I/O port? Why is it used in the 8051?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A quasi-bidirectional I/O port is an I/O pin that can act as both an input and an output **without** the need to reconfigure a data direction register. The 8051 uses this design to **save silicon area** and **simplify programming** — the same port can source a small current and sink a large current. When '1' is written to the port latch, the lower transistor is turned OFF and the internal weak pull-up holds the pin HIGH, making it ready to be used as an input. When '0' is written, the lower transistor turns ON, pulling the pin to ground. Thus, the same hardware serves both directions with a simple software write. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors, while Port 1 does not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Port 0 has an **open-drain output structure** with no internal pull-up transistor. This is because P0 is multiplexed with the external address/data bus (AD0–AD7) and must be able to drive a heavily loaded bus quickly — an internal pull-up would be too weak. Hence external pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ) are connected to P0. Port 1, on the other hand, is dedicated to general-purpose I/O and contains a built-in weak pull-up transistor (5–40 kΩ) that can hold the pin HIGH when '1' is written, so it does not need an external pull-up for simple GPIO use. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: For the I/O port pin diagram question, students commonly lose **3–4 marks** for: (i) forgetting to label the **internal pull-up resistor**, (ii) not mentioning the **read latch vs. read pin** distinction, (iii) drawing only the upper transistor and missing the lower one, and (iv) failing to mention that P0 has **no internal pull-up**. Always include the latch (D flip-flop), the upper FET, the lower FET, the internal pull-up, and the input buffer in your diagram.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice for Average Students

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the internal structure of an 8051 Port 1 pin. Discuss how it functions as both input and output.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The Port 1 pin is a **quasi-bidirectional** I/O pin consisting of the following internal elements:

1. **D Latch**: Stores 1 bit of data written by the CPU into the P1 SFR (address 0x90). The Q output of the latch drives the output transistor network.
2. **Upper FET (Pull-up driver)**: A weak p-channel transistor that connects the pin to $V_{CC}$ when the latch holds '1'. It acts as a **weak current source**.
3. **Lower FET (Sink driver)**: A strong n-channel transistor that connects the pin to GND when the latch holds '0'. It is a **strong current sink** (up to 20 mA).
4. **Internal Pull-up Resistor ($R_{pull-up} \approx 5$–$40$ kΩ)**: Always connected, providing a passive pull-up even when the upper FET is OFF.
5. **Input Buffer**: A tri-state buffer that, when activated by a "Read Pin" signal, passes the *physical pin voltage* to the internal CPU read bus.

**Operation as Output**:
- To output a '0': CPU writes 0 to P1.x latch. Lower FET turns ON; pin is actively driven to GND.
- To output a '1': CPU writes 1 to P1.x latch. Lower FET turns OFF; upper FET (or internal pull-up) weakly pulls the pin to $V_{CC}$. Suitable only for very light loads (e.g., CMOS input of another IC).

**Operation as Input**:
- CPU first writes '1' to the latch (this is the critical step). The lower FET is OFF, putting the pin in high-impedance mode. The internal pull-up holds the pin at $V_{CC}$ (logic 1) by default. External circuitry can pull the pin LOW to feed data into the 8051.
- The CPU then issues a "read pin" instruction; the input buffer transfers the *pin voltage* to the internal bus. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**List the alternate functions of Port 3. Explain the role of P3.6 ($\overline{WR}$) and P3.7 ($\overline{RD}$) during external memory access.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

| Pin | Alternate Function | Purpose |
|-----|--------------------|---------|
| P3.0 | RxD | Serial data input (UART) |
| P3.1 | TxD | Serial data output (UART) |
| P3.2 | $\overline{INT0}$ | External interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | $\overline{INT1}$ | External interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 external clock input |
| P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 external clock input |
| P3.6 | $\overline{WR}$ | External data memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | $\overline{RD}$ | External data memory read strobe |

**Role of $\overline{WR}$ (P3.6) and $\overline{RD}$ (P3.7) in External Data Memory Access**:
When the 8051 executes an instruction like `MOVX A, @DPTR` (read) or `MOVX @DPTR, A` (write) to access external data memory:

- The address is placed on **Port 0 (low byte, multiplexed)** and **Port 2 (high byte)**.
- For a **read** operation, the 8051 drives **P3.7 ($\overline{RD}$) LOW** for one machine cycle. External memory places the addressed byte on the bus, and the 8051 reads it. (active-low strobe)
- For a **write** operation, the 8051 drives **P3.6 ($\overline{WR}$) LOW**. The byte from the accumulator is written into the external memory at the address. (active-low strobe)
- These signals are *active-low* — meaning the action occurs when the pin is pulled to 0 V. This is the standard 8085/8051 control-bus convention. (7 marks)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare the structures and uses of Port 0, Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3 in the 8051.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 | Port 2 | Port 3 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| SFR address | 0x80 | 0x90 | 0xA0 | 0xB0 |
| Pins | P0.0–P0.7 | P1.0–P1.7 | P2.0–P2.7 | P3.0–P3.7 |
| Internal pull-up | **No** (open drain) | Yes (weak) | Yes (weak) | Yes (weak) |
| Primary role | Address/Data bus | General-purpose I/O | High-address bus | Alternate functions + GPIO |
| Special use | Multiplexed AD0–AD7 with ALE | Pure I/O | Provides A8–A15 | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$ |
| Requires external pull-up for I/O | **Yes** | No | No | No |

**Detailed Explanation**:
- **Port 0** is the only true open-drain port. It serves as the lower byte of the address bus and the bidirectional data bus for both external program and data memory. The byte is latched externally using the **ALE** signal because the address and data are time-multiplexed. External 10 kΩ pull-ups are mandatory when P0 is used as GPIO.
- **Port 1** is the "cleanest" general-purpose port — purely for I/O, no alternate functions, with built-in pull-ups. It is the first choice for interfacing LEDs, switches, and simple peripherals.
- **Port 2** provides the upper 8 bits of the address (A8–A15) when external memory is used. When external memory is absent (as in the 8051 with internal ROM, e.g., 8751), P2 acts as a normal quasi-bidirectional port.
- **Port 3** has the most versatility — every pin has an alternate function essential to the 8051's peripheral set. When the alternate function is not used, the pin can act as a normal quasi-bidirectional GPIO, provided the corresponding peripheral is disabled. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An LED (forward voltage $V_F = 1.8$ V, forward current $I_F = 12$ mA) is to be connected to pin P1.0 of an 8051. Calculate the value of the current-limiting resistor. Justify whether this is safe for the 8051.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply KVL**:

$$V_{CC} - V_{R_S} - V_F = 0$$

$$V_{R_S} = V_{CC} - V_F = 5 - 1.8 = 3.2\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law**:

$$R_S = \frac{V_{R_S}}{I_F} = \frac{3.2\ \text{V}}{0.012\ \text{A}} \approx 266.7\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Choose standard E12 value**: $R_S = 270\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 — Verify the actual current**:

$$I_{F,actual} = \frac{3.2}{270} \approx 11.85\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Safety check**:
- 8051 per-pin sink current limit: ≈ **20 mA**.
- 11.85 mA < 20 mA ✓
- Total port current limit (all 8 pins of P1 sinking together): ≈ **26.5 mA**. Single LED of 12 mA is well within budget ✓
- **Conclusion**: The design is **safe** for the 8051. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Punchlist — I/O Pins, Ports, and Circuits**

- [ ] The 8051 has **4 ports (P0–P3)**, **32 I/O pins**, each port is **8 bits**, each port has an SFR with the same name.
- [ ] All 4 port SFRs are **bit-addressable** (addresses ending in 0 or 8): 0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0.
- [ ] The 8051 I/O is **quasi-bidirectional** — no DDR register; direction is controlled by writing '1' to the latch (input-ready) or '0' (output LOW).
- [ ] **P0 = open-drain, NO internal pull-up.** External pull-up (4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ) needed.
- [ ] **P1, P2, P3 = quasi-bidirectional with internal weak pull-up** (5 kΩ–40 kΩ).
- [ ] **P0 and P2 are multiplexed** with the external address/data bus. P0 = AD0–AD7, P2 = A8–A15.
- [ ] **P3 alternate functions** (memorize all 8): RxD, TxD, $\overline{INT0}$, $\overline{INT1}$, T0, T1, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$.
- [ ] Always **write '1' to the latch before reading** a port as input; otherwise the strong pull-down dominates and the read returns 0.
- [ ] Maximum per-pin sink current ≈ **20 mA**; per-port sink limit ≈ **26.5 mA** for P0, **15 mA** for P1/P2/P3.
- [ ] For an LED on a port pin: $R_S = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F$. Choose nearest standard value (220, 270, 330, 470 Ω).
- [ ] Voltage levels: $V_{IH} \geq 2.0$ V, $V_{IL} \leq 0.8$ V, $V_{OH} \geq 2.4$ V, $V_{OL} \leq 0.45$ V.
- [ ] Switch interfacing: Use **active-low** (switch to GND) and rely on the internal pull-up; or use external 10 kΩ pull-up for noise immunity.
- [ ] Common KTU diagram must show: **D-latch, upper FET, lower FET, internal pull-up resistor, input buffer, external pin**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set - Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set - Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Addressing Modes (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** The way in which the **operand** (data on which the operation is to be performed) is specified in an instruction is called the **Addressing Mode**. It defines the rule for interpreting or modifying the address field of the instruction before the operand is actually referenced/executed.

The **8086 microprocessor** supports a powerful and flexible set of addressing modes that allow operands to be located in **registers**, embedded directly in the **instruction** (immediate), in **memory** (data segment, stack segment, extra segment, or code segment), or at **I/O ports**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Addressing Modes are Critical in 8086:**
> 1. They provide **programming flexibility** (compact code, efficient memory access).
> 2. They enable handling of **arrays, structures, stacks, and pointers** in high-level languages.
> 3. They determine the **Effective Address (EA)** — the final 20-bit physical address generated after segment arithmetic.
> 4. KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy weightage on **EA calculation** and **default segment pairing** questions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are giving directions to a friend to reach a specific book in a huge library:

| Analogy | Computer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **floor + rack** (e.g., "3rd floor, Rack B") is a fixed, known location. | **Direct Addressing** (the instruction contains the exact offset) |
| You say "the book is in my **right hand**" | **Register Addressing** (data is inside a CPU register) |
| You say "**this** book" while pointing to it (value itself, not a location) | **Immediate Addressing** (operand is embedded in instruction) |
| You say "look in the **rack number** stored on this paper" | **Register Indirect Addressing** (register holds the offset) |
| You say "start from **Section A**, then skip 5 racks" | **Based Addressing** (Base register + displacement) |
| You say "combine **Rack B** of **Section A**" | **Based Indexed Addressing** (Base + Index) |

The 8086 offers **8 distinct addressing modes** for data operands. Let us master each one.

---

### 1.3 The 8086 Data-Operand Addressing Modes — Quick Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Data Addressing Modes in 8086 = 8**
> They are categorized as: **Register, Immediate, Direct, Register Indirect, Based Relative, Indexed Relative, Based Indexed Relative,** and **Based Indexed** (with no displacement — i.e., displacement = 0).

All of these are computed using the **Effective Address (EA)** formula:

$$EA = \text{Base} + \text{Index} + \text{Displacement}$$

where **Base** ∈ {BX, BP}, **Index** ∈ {SI, DI}, and **Displacement** is a signed 8-bit or 16-bit constant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The 8 Addressing Modes of 8086

#### **Mode 1: Register Addressing**
- The operand is present **inside a CPU register**.
- No memory access is required → **fastest** mode.
- Example: `MOV AX, BX` → copies content of BX into AX.
- `MOV CX, DX` → another classic example.

#### **Mode 2: Immediate Addressing**
- The operand (an 8-bit or 16-bit **constant**) is part of the instruction itself.
- The data is fetched along with the instruction from the code segment.
- Example: `MOV AL, 25H` → loads the constant `25H` into AL.
- Example: `MOV CX, 1234H` → loads `1234H` into CX.

#### **Mode 3: Direct Addressing**
- The instruction contains the **16-bit offset (Effective Address)** of the operand in memory.
- Default segment: **Data Segment (DS)** for data, **Code Segment (CS)** for jumps.
- Example: `MOV AL, [2000H]` → loads AL from memory location `DS:2000H`.
- Example: `MOV [5000H], BL` → stores BL into `DS:5000H`.

#### **Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing**
- A register **holds the offset** (not the data). The data resides in memory.
- Allowed registers: **SI, DI, BX** (default segment = DS) and **BP** (default segment = SS).
- Example: `MOV AX, [SI]` → loads AX from address `DS:SI`.
- Example: `MOV [BX], AL` → stores AL at `DS:BX`.

#### **Mode 5: Based Relative Addressing**
- Uses a **Base Register (BX or BP)** plus an 8-bit or 16-bit signed displacement.
- Example: `MOV AL, [BX + 1000H]` → loads AL from `DS:(BX + 1000H)`.
- Example: `MOV [BP + 200H], CX` → stores CX at `SS:(BP + 200H)`.

#### **Mode 6: Indexed Relative Addressing**
- Uses an **Index Register (SI or DI)** plus an 8-bit or 16-bit signed displacement.
- Example: `MOV AL, [SI + 50H]` → loads AL from `DS:(SI + 50H)`.
- Example: `MOV [DI + 100H], BX` → stores BX at `DS:(DI + 100H)`.

#### **Mode 7: Based Indexed Addressing** (no displacement, displacement = 0)
- Combines a **Base Register (BX or BP)** with an **Index Register (SI or DI)**.
- Example: `MOV AL, [BX + SI]` → loads AL from `DS:(BX + SI)`.
- Example: `MOV [BP + DI], AX` → stores AX at `SS:(BP + DI)`.

#### **Mode 8: Based Indexed Relative Addressing**
- The most powerful mode: **Base + Index + Displacement** (all three combined).
- Example: `MOV AL, [BX + SI + 50H]` → loads AL from `DS:(BX + SI + 50H)`.
- Example: `MOV [BP + DI + 1234H], CX` → stores CX at `SS:(BP + DI + 1234H)`.

---

### 2.2 Default Segment Register Table — *HIGH-YIELD KTU FAVOURITE*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The #1 KTU Favourite Question:** "Identify the default segment for the addressing mode used in the given instruction." Memorize this table.

| Addressing Mode | Registers Used | Default Segment | Alternate Segment (with prefix) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Register | AX, BX, CX, DX, etc. | None (Register) | — |
| Immediate | — | None (Embedded) | — |
| Direct | [offset] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Register Indirect (BX, SI, DI) | [BX], [SI], [DI] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Register Indirect (BP) | [BP] | **SS** | DS, ES, CS |
| Based Relative (BX) | [BX + disp] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Based Relative (BP) | [BP + disp] | **SS** | DS, ES, CS |
| Indexed Relative (SI) | [SI + disp] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Indexed Relative (DI) | [DI + disp] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Based Indexed (BX + SI/DI) | [BX + SI/DI] | **DS** | CS, ES, SS |
| Based Indexed (BP + SI/DI) | [BP + SI/DI] | **SS** | DS, ES, CS |
| Based Indexed Relative (BP + Index + disp) | [BP + SI/DI + disp] | **SS** | DS, ES, CS |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Effective Address & Physical Address

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Address (EA)** is the 16-bit offset computed from registers and displacement.
> **Physical Address (PA)** is the **20-bit** address put on the address bus.

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Address (EA) | $\text{EA} = \text{Base} + \text{Index} + \text{Displacement}$ | 16-bit, 0000H–FFFFH |
| Physical Address (PA) | $\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{EA} \; \text{(i.e., PA = Segment} \ll 4 \text{+ EA)}$ | 20-bit, 00000H–FFFFFH |
| Segment × 16 | $\text{Segment} \ll 4$ (append one hex 0 to right) | — |
| Maximum Memory | $2^{20} = 1,048,576$ bytes $= 1 \text{ MB}$ | Bytes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Array Traversal:** Indexed addressing (e.g., `MOV AL, [SI]`) is used to step through array elements by incrementing SI/DI.
- **Stack Frame Access:** BP-based addressing (e.g., `MOV AX, [BP+4]`) is used in C compilers to access function parameters and local variables on the stack.
- **Structure Access:** Based Indexed Relative addressing maps directly to C's `struct` member access: `array[i].field` → `[BX + SI + offset]`.
- **Pointer Dereferencing:** Register Indirect addressing is the low-level analog of C's `*ptr`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — EA and PA Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Question Pattern:** Given segment and register values, compute EA, PA, and identify the addressing mode.

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
Assume `DS = 2000H`, `SS = 4000H`, `BX = 0200H`, `SI = 0010H`, `DI = 0030H`, `BP = 0100H`.
Find the **Physical Address** for each of the following instructions, and identify the addressing mode:

1. `MOV AL, [SI]`
2. `MOV CX, [BP + 100H]`
3. `MOV AX, [BX + SI + 50H]`
4. `MOV [DI + 200H], BL`

---
