topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Processor Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — ARM Processor Fundamentals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of 32-bit (and 64-bit in newer variants) reduced instruction set computer (RISC) processor architectures developed by **ARM Holdings (Cambridge, UK)**. It is the most widely used processor architecture in embedded systems, mobile devices, IoT endpoints, and increasingly in desktops, servers, and supercomputers.

### 1.1 Historical Evolution of ARM

| Year | Milestone | Key Development |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **1985** | ARM1 | First prototype by **Acorn Computers**, UK |
| **1987** | ARM2 | First commercial silicon — pioneering the **26-bit address space** |
| **1990** | ARM6 | Joint venture forms **Advanced RISC Machines Ltd.** (Apple, Acorn, VLSI) |
| **1998** | ARM7TDMI | The legendary 3-stage pipelined core used in early mobile phones and the original **iPod** |
| **2004** | ARMv7 | Introduction of the **Cortex** family (Cortex-A, -R, -M) |
| **2011** | ARMv8 | First 64-bit architecture (AArch64) with Apple’s Cyclone leading adoption |
| **2020+** | ARMv9 | Adds Scalable Vector Extension 2 (SVE2) and Realm Management Extension (RME) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The acronym **ARM** originally stood for **Acorn RISC Machine** (1985–1990) and was later re-branded to **Advanced RISC Machine** after the joint venture in 1990. Examiners love asking this!

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Specialist Carpenter"

Imagine two carpenters building identical chairs:
- A **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** carpenter has a single, enormous Swiss-Army knife with 200 tools. Some tools are highly specialised (a built-in chair-leg-shaper), but the knife is heavy, slow to open each blade, and most blades go unused on any given day. (Example: Intel x86).
- An **ARM (RISC) carpenter** carries a slim belt with 30 simple, razor-sharp tools. Every tool does **one job perfectly and fast**. He performs the chair-leg-shaping with three quick, simple cuts. The toolkit is tiny, light, and the carpenter works very fast. (Example: ARM Cortex).

**Takeaway:** ARM trades rich single-instruction complexity for **simple, fast, energy-efficient, and pipelinable** primitives.

### 1.3 Why ARM Dominates the Embedded & Mobile World

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core ARM Design Philosophy — 8 Pillars:**
> 1. **Large, uniform register file** (load–store architecture).
> 2. **Fixed-length, simple instructions** (typically 32-bit, easily decoded).
> 3. **Load/Store architecture** — only load/store instructions access memory; data processing operates on registers.
> 4. **Simple addressing modes** (most instructions use base + offset).
> 5. **Hardware-scheduled pipelines** with low pipeline penalties.
> 6. **High code density** via Thumb (16-bit compressed instructions) and Thumb-2.
> 7. **Low power consumption** — small die size and architectural simplicity.
> 8. **Scalability** — from Cortex-M0 (32 K gates) to Cortex-A78 (huge out-of-order core).

### 1.4 Physical / Architectural Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Typical ARM Value | Why It Matters |
|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Register width** | **32 bits** (ARM state) / 16 bits (Thumb state) | Defines native data size |
| **Address bus** | **32 bits** (4 GB linear) | Standard for ARMv7 |
| **Word size** | **32 bits = 4 bytes** | Memory addressing unit |
| **Half-word** | **16 bits = 2 bytes** | For Thumb instructions |
| **Byte** | **8 bits** | Smallest addressable unit |
| **Modes** | **7 operating modes** (in ARMv7) | Privilege/security control |
| **Registers** | **37 physical 32-bit registers** (banked) | 30 general-purpose + 6 status |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARM vs RISC vs CISC — The Architectural Triangle

| Feature | **ARM (RISC)** | **Traditional RISC (MIPS/SPARC)** | **CISC (x86)** |
|---------|----------------|-----------------------------------|----------------|
| Instruction size | Fixed 32-bit (or 16-bit Thumb) | Fixed 32-bit | Variable 1–15 bytes |
| Memory access | Load/Store only | Load/Store only | Register–Memory & Memory–Memory |
| Addressing modes | ~9 simple modes | ~3–4 | 12+ complex modes |
| Decoding | Hardwired, single-cycle | Hardwired | Microcode ROM |
| Register count | 37 (banked) | 32 flat | 16 (GPR) + hidden micro-ops |
| Power | **Very low** | Medium | High |
| Pipeline | 3 → 5 → 8+ stages | 5 stages typical | 12–20+ deep |
| Code density | High (Thumb) | Lower | Highest |
| Examples | Cortex-M, Cortex-A | MIPS R3000, RISC-V | Intel Core i7, AMD Ryzen |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU One-Liner to Memorize:** *"ARM is a RISC architecture that uses a load-store model with a uniform 32-bit instruction word (with a 16-bit Thumb compressed subset) and 37 banked registers to support 7 operating modes."*

## 2.2 The 5 Core RISC Principles (Why ARM Is a RISC)

1. **Single-cycle execution** — most instructions complete in one clock cycle.
2. **Load/Store architecture** — data-processing instructions never touch memory; only `LDR` and `STR` do.
3. **Large register file** — 37 registers minimise memory traffic (the **von Neumann bottleneck**).
4. **Hardwired control** — no microcode ROM; control logic is combinational (faster, smaller).
5. **Fixed instruction format** — simplifies the decoder, enabling pipelining.

## 2.3 ARM Processor Family Tree (KTU Must-Know)

```
                        ARM Architecture Family
                                │
        ┌───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐
   Classic ARM                 Cortex                  Neoverse
        │                       │                       │
   ┌────┴────┐           ┌──────┼──────┐          Server/Cloud
 ARM7TDMI   ARM9          A     R     M
   │         │         Cortex  Cortex  Cortex          E1, N1, V1
ARM7TDMI-S  ARM920T       -A53  -R4    -M0/M3/M4/M7
                     ARM11    -A57  -R5     -M33
                                -A72          -M55 (Helium)
                                -A78          -M85
```

### 2.3.1 The Three Cortex Profiles

| Profile | Target | Features | Typical Use |
|---------|--------|----------|-------------|
| **Cortex-A (Application)** | High-performance apps, OS | MMU, full Thumb-2, NEON SIMD, virtualization | Smartphones, tablets, Linux |
| **Cortex-R (Real-time)** | Hard real-time, deterministic | MPU, low-latency interrupt, lockstep | Automotive brakes, HDD controllers |
| **Cortex-M (Microcontroller)** | Low-power MCUs | MPU, NVIC, Thumb-2 only, deterministic | IoT, sensors, wearables, drones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** "Differentiate between ARM7, ARM9, and Cortex-M3 cores." Know the **pipeline depth** and **architecture version** for each.

## 2.4 Pipeline Architecture in ARM

The pipeline is **the heart of ARM’s performance story**. Each generation evolves the pipeline.

| Core | Pipeline Stages | Architectural Version |
|------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **ARM7TDMI** | **3-stage** (Fetch → Decode → Execute) | ARMv4T |
| **ARM9** | **5-stage** (F → D → E → M → WB) | ARMv5TE |
| **ARM11** | **8-stage** (issue ×2 out-of-order) | ARMv6 |
| **Cortex-A8** | 13-stage dual-issue in-order | ARMv7-A |
| **Cortex-A53** | 8-stage dual-issue in-order | ARMv8-A |
| **Cortex-A78** | 11-stage wide out-of-order | ARMv8.2-A |

### 2.4.1 The Classic 3-Stage Pipeline (ARM7TDMI)

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
Instr 1 │  Fetch  │ Decode  │ Execute │       │       │
Instr 2 │         │  Fetch  │ Decode  │Execute│       │
Instr 3 │         │         │  Fetch  │Decode │Execute│
        └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        t0   t1   t2   t3   t4   t5   t6
```

* **Fetch (F):** Instruction is read from memory using the PC.
* **Decode (D):** Instruction decoded; operands read from register bank.
* **Execute (E):** ALU operation or address calculation; registers written back.

> [!NOTE]
> The 3-stage pipeline gives ARM7 a **CPI ≈ 1** for ideal code. The classic equation holds: **Time per instruction = 1 cycle** (ignoring stalls).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|-------------------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | Seconds |
| 2 | **Average CPI** | $CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times IC_i)$ | Cycles/instruction |
| 3 | **MIPS Rating** | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million instr/sec |
| 4 | **Pipeline Speedup (ideal)** | $S_{ideal} = k$ | For k-stage pipeline |
| 5 | **Pipeline Speedup (real)** | $S_{real} = \dfrac{k}{1 + (k-1)\cdot p}$ | p = prob. of stall per stage |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $TP = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Instructions/second |
| 7 | **Code size (Thumb benefit)** | $\Delta Size = \dfrac{Size_{ARM} - Size_{Thumb}}{Size_{ARM}} \times 100\%$ | % reduction, often 25–35% |
| 8 | **Amdahl’s Law (parallel fraction f)** | $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | N = parallel resources |
| 9 | **Branch Penalty (3-stage)** | Penalty = 1 cycle (taken) | Flush one fetched instr |
| 10 | **Address Bus Capacity (32-bit)** | $Capacity = 2^{32} = 4\,GB$ | Linear addressing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table. Always use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) inside any `|...|` row.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of ARM Fundamentals

* **Mobile industry:** > 99% of smartphones use ARM cores (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, Samsung Exynos all license ARM IP).
* **IoT/MCU market:** Cortex-M cores power billions of STM32, NXP LPC, Nordic nRF, and ESP32 devices.
* **Automotive:** Cortex-R cores are used in **ASIL-D** braking and steering ECUs.
* **Server/Cloud:** AWS Graviton, Apple M-series, and Ampere Altra all use ARM Neoverse cores — power efficiency is the key driver.
* **Aerospace & Defence:** SPARC and ARM are common in space-graded rad-hard processors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why CPI = 1 in an Ideal 3-Stage Pipeline

Let us mathematically justify the claim that the ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline achieves **CPI = 1** for an ideal program (no stalls, no branches, no data hazards).

**Given:**
* Pipeline depth $k = 3$ stages: Fetch (F), Decode (D), Execute (E).
* Clock period $T_{clk}$.
* Number of instructions in the program = $N$.

### Step 1 — Time for the First Instruction

The first instruction must traverse all 3 stages before completing. So its latency is:

$$
T_{first} = 3 \times T_{clk}
$$

### Step 2 — Time for Subsequent Instructions

Once the pipeline is **filled**, a new instruction is *issued* every single cycle. So each of the remaining $(N-1)$ instructions adds only one cycle:

$$
T_{others} = (N-1) \times T_{clk}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Time to Execute N Instructions

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{first} + T_{others} \\
          &= 3 \, T_{clk} + (N-1) \, T_{clk} \\
          &= (N+2) \, T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Average CPI

Average cycles per instruction is total cycles divided by instruction count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \dfrac{T_{total}}{N \times T_{clk}} = \dfrac{N+2}{N} = 1 + \dfrac{2}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Limit as N → ∞

As $N$ becomes large (a real program has thousands of instructions), the constant 2 becomes negligible:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} CPI_{avg} = \lim_{N \to \infty}\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{N}\right) = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** For a non-trivial program, the ARM7 3-stage pipeline executes nearly **1 instruction per cycle**, i.e., $CPI \approx 1$. This is why ARM7TDMI achieves ~130 MIPS @ 130 MHz.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Pipeline Speedup vs Non-Pipelined Execution

**Given:**
* Non-pipelined ARM execution: each instruction takes $k \cdot T_{clk} = 3 \, T_{clk}$.
* Pipelined execution: $T_{pipe} = (N + k - 1) \, T_{clk}$.

### Step 1 — Non-Pipelined Time

$$
T_{nonpipe} = N \times k \times T_{clk} = N \times 3 \, T_{clk}
$$

### Step 2 — Pipelined Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pipe} &= (N + k - 1)\,T_{clk} \\
         &= (N + 2)\,T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{T_{nonpipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{3N}{N+2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Limit as $N \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \lim_{N \to \infty}\dfrac{3N}{N+2} = 3
$$

**Conclusion:** The theoretical maximum speedup equals the number of pipeline stages $k$. For ARM7, $S_{max} = 3$. The Cortex-A8 (13 stages) theoretically achieves $S_{max} = 13$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Realistic Speedup with Stalls (Amdahl-style for pipeline)

Let $p$ be the probability that any given instruction causes a **one-cycle stall** (e.g., load-use hazard, branch taken). The total stall cycles = $N \cdot p$.

### Step 1 — Total Cycles with Stalls

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{stall} &= T_{pipe} + N \cdot p \cdot T_{clk} \\
          &= (N+2) \, T_{clk} + N \cdot p \, T_{clk} \\
          &= \bigl(N(1+p) + 2\bigr) \, T_{clk}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Realistic Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{real} &= \dfrac{3N}{N(1+p)+2} = \dfrac{3}{1+p+\tfrac{2}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Limit as $N \to \infty$

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S_{real} = \dfrac{3}{1+p}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $p = 0.2$ (20% instructions cause a stall), then

$$
S_{real} = \dfrac{3}{1+0.2} = \dfrac{3}{1.2} = 2.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even a small stall probability halves the benefit of deep pipelining. This is why **branch prediction** and **hazard forwarding** are critical in modern cores.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pipeline Stages vs Architectural Features

| Feature | ARM7TDMI (3-stage) | ARM9 (5-stage) | Cortex-M3 (3-stage) | Cortex-A53 (8-stage) |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Pipeline stages | **3** | **5** | **3** | **8** |
| Architecture | ARMv4T | ARMv5TE | ARMv7-M | ARMv8-A |
| Thumb support | Yes | Yes | Yes (Thumb-2 only) | Yes (AArch32 + AArch64) |
| Clock (typical) | 80–130 MHz | 150–300 MHz | 50–200 MHz | 1.2–2.0 GHz |
| MIPS @ 100 MHz | ~100 | ~115 | ~125 | ~200 (per core, dual-issue) |
| MMU/MPU | None | MMU (920T) | MPU (optional) | MMU full |
| Branch prediction | None | None (BTAC optional) | Static only | Dynamic, 2-level |
| Out-of-order | No | No | No | No (in-order dual-issue) |
| Power (typ. core) | 0.5 mW/MHz | 1 mW/MHz | 0.3 mW/MHz | ~1.5 mW/MHz |
| Use case | Legacy MCU, toys | Set-top box, early smartphones | Modern MCUs, IoT | Smartphones, servers |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — MIPS Calculation (KTU-style)

**Problem:** An ARM7TDMI runs at $f_{clk} = 60\,\text{MHz}$ with an average CPI of 1.2. Compute:
1. MIPS rating.
2. CPU time to execute a 10 million instruction program.

**Given:**
* $f_{clk} = 60 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
* $CPI = 1.2$
* $N_{inst} = 10 \times 10^{6}$

### Step 1 — MIPS

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} \\
     &= \dfrac{60 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{6}} \\
     &= \dfrac{60}{1.2} \\
     &= 50 \,\text{MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — CPU Execution Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}} \\
        &= \dfrac{10 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2}{60 \times 10^{6}} \\
        &= \dfrac{12}{60} \\
        &= 0.2 \,\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** 50 MIPS, 200 ms execution time. This is a typical KTU Part-A 3-mark numerical.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ARM Core

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Program Counter PC] --> F1[Fetch Stage F]
    F1 --> D1[Decode Stage D]
    D1 --> E1[Execute Stage E]
    E1 --> W1[Writeback to Register Bank]
    E1 --> M1[Memory Address for LDR STR]
    M1 --> MEM[External Memory]
    MEM --> W1
    W1 --> RFBANK[Register Bank 37x32bit]
    D1 <--> RFBANK
    CPSR[Current Program Status Register CPSR] --> D1
    SPSR[Saved Program Status Register SPSR] --> E1
    INTCON[Interrupt Controller VIC NVIC] --> PCINT[PC update on IRQ FIQ]
    PCINT --> A
```

## 4.2 ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline Instruction Flow
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instruction 1
    Fetch 1       :a1, 0, 1
    Decode 1      :a2, 1, 1
    Execute 1     :a3, 2, 1
    section Instruction 2
    Fetch 2       :b1, 1, 1
    Decode 2      :b2, 2, 1
    Execute 2     :b3, 3, 1
    section Instruction 3
    Fetch 3       :c1, 2, 1
    Decode 3      :c2, 3, 1
    Execute 3     :c3, 4, 1
    section Instruction 4
    Fetch 4       :d1, 3, 1
    Decode 4      :d2, 4, 1
    Execute 4     :d3, 5, 1
    section Instruction 5
    Fetch 5       :e1, 4, 1
    Decode 5      :e2, 5, 1
    Execute 5     :e3, 6, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** After cycle 2, one new instruction **enters** the pipeline every cycle. That is the “one instruction per cycle” steady-state behaviour of a 3-stage pipeline.

## 4.3 ARM Architecture Version Evolution Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V4T[ARMv4T] --> V5TE[ARMv5TE]
    V5TE --> V6[ARMv6]
    V6 --> V7A[ARMv7-A]
    V6 --> V7R[ARMv7-R]
    V6 --> V7M[ARMv7-M]
    V7A --> V8A[ARMv8-A 64bit]
    V7M --> V8M[ARMv8-M]
    V8A --> V9A[ARMv9-A SVE2 RME]
    V4T --- C1[ARM7TDMI]
    V5TE --- C2[ARM9E]
    V6 --- C3[ARM11]
    V7A --- C4[Cortex A8 A9 A15]
    V7M --- C5[Cortex M0 M3 M4]
    V8A --- C6[Cortex A53 A57 A78]
    V8M --- C7[Cortex M23 M33 M55]
    V9A --- C8[Cortex X1 X2 Neoverse N2]
```

## 4.4 ARM Operating Modes and Register Banking

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USR[User Mode USR] --> SYS[System Mode SYS]
    FIQ[Fast Interrupt FIQ] -->|banked R8-R14| RFIQ[FIQ Banked Registers]
    IRQ[Interrupt IRQ] -->|banked R13 R14| RIRQ[IRQ Banked Registers]
    SVC[Supervisor SVC] -->|banked R13 R14| RSVC[SVC Banked Registers]
    ABT[Abort ABT] -->|banked R13 R14| RABT[ABT Banked Registers]
    UND[Undefined UND] -->|banked R13 R14| RUND[UND Banked Registers]
    CPSR1[CPSR Current Status] -.shared.-> USR
    SPSRF[SPSR_fiq] -.saved.-> FIQ
    SPSRI[SPSR_irq] -.saved.-> IRQ
    SPSRS[SPSR_svc] -.saved.-> SVC
    SPSRA[SPSR_abt] -.saved.-> ABT
    SPSRU[SPSR_und] -.saved.-> UND
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Only **FIQ mode** banks **R8–R14** (i.e., 7 extra registers) so that the FIQ handler can begin execution *without* saving registers — giving it the lowest latency of any exception. All other privileged modes bank only **R13 (SP)** and **R14 (LR)**.

## 4.5 The 37-Register File Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UNBANKED["Unbanked Registers visible in all modes"]
        R0[R0] --- R1[R1] --- R2[R2] --- R3[R3]
        R4[R4] --- R5[R5] --- R6[R6] --- R7[R7]
        R8[R8] --- R9[R9] --- R10[R10] --- R11[R11]
        R12[R12] --- R15[R15 PC]
    end
    subgraph BANKED["Banked Registers per mode"]
        R13[R13 SP] --- R14[R14 LR]
        R8F[R8_fiq] --- R9F[R9_fiq] --- R10F[R10_fiq]
        R11F[R11_fiq] --- R12F[R12_fiq] --- R13F[R13_fiq] --- R14F[R14_fiq]
    end
    CPSR[CPSR] --- SPSR[SPSR x5 modes]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting:** Unbanked common = 15 (R0–R7, R8–R12 unbanked, R15=PC) + CPSR = 16. Plus R8_fiq to R14_fiq (7) + (R13,R14) × 5 privileged modes = 10 + 5 SPSR = 5. Total physical registers = **16 + 7 + 10 + 5 ≈ 37 + 1 PC overlap** = the famous 37 × 32-bit physical registers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is ARM? How is it different from a traditional CISC processor?**

**Model Answer:**

**ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a 32-bit RISC processor architecture originally developed by Acorn Computers (1985) and now managed by ARM Holdings, Cambridge. It is widely used in mobile and embedded systems.

**Differences from CISC (e.g., x86):**

| Aspect | ARM (RISC) | CISC (x86) |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| Instruction set | Small, fixed 32-bit | Large, variable length |
| Memory access | Load–Store only | Memory–Memory allowed |
| Decoding | Hardwired | Microcode |
| Power | Low | High |
| Pipeline | Simple, deep | Complex, many hazards |

ARM is also **licensable IP** (Intellectual Property), while Intel designs and manufactures its own x86 chips.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the features of the ARM7TDMI core.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ARM7TDMI** is a 32-bit RISC core with the following key features:
1. **T** — supports **Thumb** 16-bit compressed instruction set.
2. **D** — has on-chip **Debug** extensions (JTAG boundary scan).
3. **M** — enhanced **Multiplier** (produces 64-bit result in one cycle).
4. **I** — includes the **EmbeddedICE** in-circuit emulator logic.
5. **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute) giving $CPI \approx 1$.
6. **Von Neumann** single 32-bit bus for instructions and data.
7. **37 physical 32-bit registers** with 7 operating modes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **3-stage pipeline of ARM7TDMI**. Discuss the effect of a branch instruction on pipeline performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ARM processor family** with an emphasis on the **Cortex-A, Cortex-R, and Cortex-M** profiles. Mention at least two cores per profile. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q. (a)

**ARM7 3-Stage Pipeline:**

The ARM7TDMI uses a 3-stage pipeline — **Fetch (F), Decode (D), Execute (E)**.

```
        Cycle:   1    2    3    4    5    6
Inst 1:         F    D    E
Inst 2:              F    D    E
Inst 3:                   F    D    E
Inst 4:                        F    D    E
```

* **Fetch (F):** Instruction is read from the address held in the **Program Counter (PC)**. PC is incremented by 4 (one word).
* **Decode (D):** The instruction is decoded and operands are read from the **register bank** (R0–R15). The barrel shifter is set up if required.
* **Execute (E):** The **ALU** performs the operation, the **multiplier** runs (if MUL/MLA), or a memory address is computed for `LDR`/`STR`. The result is written back to the destination register (or to memory).

**Effect of a Branch on Performance:**

A **conditional or unconditional branch** changes the PC. In a 3-stage pipeline, the branch is decided during the **Execute (E)** stage, but the next instruction has already been **fetched** in cycle 2 (assuming the branch was at cycle 1 decode, decided at cycle 3 execute). So the fetched instruction must be **flushed**.

* **Penalty = 1 cycle per taken branch** in ARM7.
* The CPI equation becomes:

$$
CPI_{eff} = 1 + (p_{branch} \times 1)
$$

where $p_{branch}$ is the probability of taking a branch. If 25% of instructions are taken branches, $CPI_{eff} = 1.25$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speedup lost** by branch flushing: If the program has $N$ instructions and branch frequency $f_b$ = 0.25, the **stall cycles** = $N \times f_b \times 1 = 0.25N$. The effective time becomes $T_{eff} = (N+0.25N+2)T_{clk} = 1.25N \, T_{clk}$, reducing the speedup from 3× to $3/1.25 = 2.4 \times$.

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b)

**ARM Processor Family — Cortex Profiles:**

ARM licenses cores in three main “profile” families, each optimised for a target domain.

**1. Cortex-A (Application Profile)** — *High-performance, full OS, virtualisation.*
* **Cortex-A53:** 64-bit, 8-stage in-order dual-issue; the workhorse of mid-range smartphones.
* **Cortex-A78:** 11-stage out-of-order, 2.1 GHz+, used in flagship Snapdragon and Exynos SoCs.
* Used in: Smartphones, tablets, Linux SBCs (Raspberry Pi 4 uses A72), smart TVs.

**2. Cortex-R (Real-time Profile)** — *Deterministic, low-latency, safety-critical.*
* **Cortex-R4:** 8-stage, dual-core lockstep, used in automotive ECUs.
* **Cortex-R52:** Hardware virtualization support, ASIL-D capable.
* Used in: Automotive brakes, airbags, HDD/SSD controllers, 5G modems.

**3. Cortex-M (Microcontroller Profile)** — *Low-power, deterministic, Thumb-2 only.*
* **Cortex-M0/M0+:** Smallest (12 K gates), for ultra-low-power IoT nodes.
* **Cortex-M3:** Used in STM32, NXP LPC, popular for general MCU work.
* **Cortex-M4:** Adds DSP extensions and optional FPU.
* **Cortex-M7:** 6-stage superscalar, with FPU, for high-performance MCUs.
* **Cortex-M33:** Adds TrustZone security.
* **Cortex-M55:** First core with Helium (M-profile Vector Extension).

**Summary Table:**

| Profile | Bit width | Pipeline | Typical MHz | Memory Mgmt | Typical Use |
|---------|-----------|----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Cortex-A** | 32/64 | 8–13 stage | 1–3 GHz | MMU | Smartphones, Linux |
| **Cortex-R** | 32 | 8-stage dual | 0.5–1.5 GHz | MPU + lockstep | Automotive, storage |
| **Cortex-M** | 32 | 2–3 stage | 50–400 MHz | MPU (optional) | IoT, MCUs |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **ARM programmer’s model** in detail. Discuss the **register banking** concept and its role in exception handling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **ARM7TDMI** and **Cortex-M3** cores in terms of architecture, pipeline, instruction set, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q. (a)

**ARM Programmer’s Model:**

The programmer’s model describes the **registers** visible to software at any time. It includes:
* **15 general-purpose 32-bit registers** (R0–R14)
* **1 program counter** (R15)
* **1 Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**

Total = **17 registers visible at any time**, but physically there are 37 registers (because of banking).

**R0–R12:** General-purpose scratch registers. R0–R3 are used for **argument passing** in the AAPCS (ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard). R7 often holds the **frame pointer (FP)**. R11 is the **FP** in some toolchains.

**R13 — SP (Stack Pointer):** Points to the top of the current stack. Each mode has its **own banked SP** so that mode switches do not corrupt the user stack.

**R14 — LR (Link Register):** Holds the **return address** when a function call (`BL`) or exception is taken. Each mode has its own banked LR.

**R15 — PC (Program Counter):** Read address + 8 in ARM state (two-stage prefetch) and PC + 4 in Thumb state.

**CPSR — Current Program Status Register:**

| 31 30 29 28 | 27 26 25 24 23 | 22–8 | 7 6 | 5 | 4–0 |
|-------------|---------------|------|-----|---|-----|
| N Z C V | Q (sat) IT[1:0] J | IT[7:2] | I F T | M[4:0] |
| Condition flags | Sticky overflow / IT / Jazelle | (continued) | IRQ/FIQ disable / Thumb | Mode bits |

* **N, Z, C, V** — condition code flags set by data-processing ops.
* **I** — disables IRQ when set.
* **F** — disables FIQ when set.
* **T** — Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb).
* **M[4:0]** — operating mode (5-bit field).

**SPSR (Saved Program Status Register):** On entering an exception, the **CPSR is copied into the SPSR** of the new mode. On return (`SUBS PC, LR, #4` etc.), the SPSR is copied back to CPSR — restoring the original state cleanly.

**Register Banking & Exception Handling:**

Each privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) has its **own R13 (SP), R14 (LR), and SPSR**. This means:

* When an IRQ is taken, the CPU switches to **IRQ mode**, uses **IRQ-mode SP** (so the user SP is untouched), saves the return address in **IRQ-mode LR**, and saves CPSR in **IRQ-mode SPSR**.
* The handler can therefore push registers onto its own stack and return cleanly with `SUBS PC, LR, #4` which atomically restores both PC and CPSR.
* **FIQ mode additionally banks R8–R14** (7 registers), so the FIQ ISR can run without saving any registers — fastest possible latency.

```
      USER MODE                  FIQ MODE
  R8  R9  R10 R11 R12       R8_fiq  R9_fiq  R10_fiq  R11_fiq  R12_fiq
  R13 (SP_user)             R13_fiq (SP_fiq)
  R14 (LR_user)             R14_fiq (LR_fiq)
  R15 PC                    R15 PC
  CPSR                      CPSR
                            SPSR_fiq  <- saved CPSR
```

**Banking in Action during an Exception (IRQ example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. IRQ asserted.} \\
\text{2. CPU: } R14_{irq} \leftarrow PC_{return}, \quad SPSR_{irq} \leftarrow CPSR, \quad CPSR[4:0] \leftarrow 10010_b (IRQ). \\
\text{3. PC} \leftarrow 0x00000018 \text{ (IRQ vector).} \\
\text{4. Handler runs, uses its banked R13 (SP\_irq) and R14 (LR\_irq).} \\
\text{5. On return: } SUBS \, PC, LR, \#4 \Rightarrow \text{ restores PC and CPSR atomically.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q. (b)

**Comparison: ARM7TDMI vs Cortex-M3**

| Feature | **ARM7TDMI** | **Cortex-M3** |
|---------|--------------|---------------|
| Architecture version | **ARMv4T** | **ARMv7-M** |
| Instruction sets | ARM (32-bit) + Thumb (16-bit) | **Thumb-2 only** (mixes 16- and 32-bit) |
| Pipeline stages | **3** (F, D, E) | **3** (F, D, E) — but redesigned |
| Bus | Von Neumann (single 32-bit) | **Harvard** (separate I/D buses via AHB-Lite) |
| Interrupt controller | External **VIC** (vectored) | On-chip **NVIC** with 1–240 IRQs, deterministic latency |
| Wake-up interrupt controller | No | **WIC** for sleep mode |
| Bit-banding | No | **Yes** — bit-band alias region (0x22000000–0x23FFFFFF) |
| Memory protection | None | **Optional MPU** (8 regions) |
| Code density | Good (with Thumb) | **Excellent** (Thumb-2 = best of both) |
| Interrupt latency | ~24 cycles (no tail-chain) | **12 cycles** standard, **6 cycles tail-chain**, **zero jitter** |
| Multiply | 32-bit result, multi-cycle | **32-bit × 32-bit = 32-bit**, single cycle |
| Divide | Software (no hardware divide) | Hardware `SDIV`/`UDIV` (2–12 cycles) |
| Power | No sleep mode hardware | Integrated **WFI/WFE** and Sleep-on-Exit |
| Debug | JTAG + EmbeddedICE | **JTAG + SWD (2-wire) + CoreSight** |
| FPU | No | No (but Cortex-M4F adds single-precision FPU) |
| Typical use | Legacy mobile, MP3, GPS | Modern MCUs: STM32, NXP LPC, TI Tiva-C, Nordic nRF |
| Power/performance | ~0.5 mW/MHz, ~100 MIPS @ 100 MHz | ~0.3 mW/MHz, ~125 MIPS @ 100 MHz |

**Key Take-aways:**

* Both have 3-stage pipelines, but the **Cortex-M3 is Harvard** (separate I and D buses), allowing simultaneous fetch and memory access — useful for `LDR` from program memory.
* The **NVIC** in M3 gives a deterministic, low-latency interrupt (12 cycles nominal, 6 with tail-chaining) vs ARM7’s external VIC.
* The M3 is **Thumb-2 only**, so its code density is superior, but it loses the original 32-bit ARM instruction set (a one-way street for migration).
* ARM7TDMI remains in production for ultra-low-cost legacy designs; **Cortex-M3 is the modern replacement**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU examiners deduct marks on ARM Fundamentals questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the “37 registers” with “37 visible registers.”** Only **17** are visible at any one time; 37 is the **total physical count** across all banked copies. Stating “ARM has 37 GPRs visible” will cost full credit.
> 2. **Saying ARM7 has a 5-stage pipeline.** It is **3-stage**; only ARM9 onwards introduced the 5-stage pipeline. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 3. **Mixing up R13/R14 roles** with R15 (PC). R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC. Get this wrong and the question is failed.
> 4. **Forgetting SPSR** exists per privileged mode and is *not* accessible in User mode. Writing “CPSR is saved in the handler” without saying “into SPSR of the new mode” is incomplete.
> 5. **Treating Thumb and ARM as different architectures** — they are both **ARM architecture**, just different *instruction set encodings* under the same CPSR T-bit.
> 6. **Pipeline hazard diagrams:** Always show the **time axis** explicitly. Drawing a vertical stack without the time axis loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Numerical answers:** Always show units (MHz, MIPS, seconds) and **write the formula first, then substitute**. A correct number with no formula gets zero in KTU valuation.
