> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (high-yield for KTU viva + 14-mark answers):**

* **ARM** = **Advanced RISC Machine** (formerly Acorn RISC Machine). Designed by **ARM Holdings**, Cambridge. ARM cores are **IP licensed**, not manufactured by ARM.
* ARM is a **RISC, Load/Store, 32-bit** architecture (with a 16-bit Thumb compressed mode).
* **Three classic ARM features (the 3 “T/D/M”):** **T**humb, **D**ebug, **M**ultiplier, **I**CE — the “TDMI” suffix.
* **ARM7TDMI:** 3-stage pipeline, **ARMv4T**, Von Neumann, ~100 MIPS @ 100 MHz.
* **ARM9:** 5-stage pipeline, **ARMv5TE**, Harvard, ~300 MHz.
* **Cortex family:** A (application), R (real-time), M (microcontroller). Each optimized for a different domain.
* **37 physical 32-bit registers**: 16 unbanked (R0–R12, R15, CPSR) + 7 FIQ-banked (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) + 5×(R13,R14) for 5 priv modes + 5 SPSRs.
* **7 operating modes:** **User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System**. (Six are privileged; User and System are non-privileged in ARMv7.)
* **CPSR** holds condition flags (N, Z, C, V), I/F bits, T bit, and mode bits. **SPSR** is a per-privileged-mode backup.
* **Pipeline ideal CPI = 1** for 3-stage ARM7; max speedup = number of stages k.
* **Branch penalty in ARM7 = 1 cycle** (flush the fetched-but-not-decoded instruction).
* **32-bit address bus → 4 GB** linear address space. Byte-addressable, little-endian by default (BE-8 optional).
* **Von Neumann (ARM7)** vs **Harvard (ARM9, Cortex-M3, Cortex-M4)** — single bus vs separate instruction/data bus.
* **MIPS formula:** $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$; **CPU time** = $\dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$.
* **Power efficiency** of ARM cores (mW/MHz) is the **#1 reason** they dominate mobile and IoT markets.
* Always use `\vert` (not `|`) for absolute value in markdown tables to avoid breaking the table syntax.
* For KTU Part-A, **write 3–4 crisp points**; for Part-B, **draw a labelled diagram, state the formula, then solve step-by-step** with units.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 7 operating modes:** **“U SIR F AS”** → **U**ser, **S**ystem, **I**RQ, (**F**IQ), **S**VC (supervisor), **A**bort, **U**ndefined… or simpler: **"FISA-US"** → **F**IQ, **I**RQ, **S**VC, **A**BT, **UND**, **USR**, **SYS**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Architecture - Register
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Architecture - Register

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ARM7TDMI** processor architecture (the canonical ARM reference used across the KTU 2024 syllabus), a **register** is a small, high-speed, on-chip storage element internal to the processor's *Register File* that holds operands, addresses, status flags, and control information required for instruction execution. The ARM7TDMI register file is a **37-register, 32-bit wide** physical organization, of which **30 are general-purpose registers** and **7 are status/control registers**. These registers are *physically banked* across the processor's seven operating modes to provide rapid context switching, deterministic exception entry, and privileged execution isolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation)**
> The ARM processor provides a *linear* 16-register view (`R0`–`R15`) to the programmer, while the *physical* implementation maintains **37 registers**, where certain registers are physically duplicated (banked) so that each processor mode has its own private copy. This is fundamental to understanding exception handling in ARM.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-counter billing desk** in a busy supermarket. The shop has **one public-facing counter** (the `User` mode) where customers pay normally. But when the **Manager** (Supervisor), **Security Guard** (FIQ), or **Accountant** (IRQ) takes over the desk, they each slide in their **own private drawer of note pads and pens** from below the counter.

- The *main counter surface* (the always-visible `R0`–`R7` pads) is the **same for everyone** — that is why they are *unbanked*.
- The **Manager's drawer** contains a special notebook labeled `R13_svc` (stack pointer for supervisor) and `R14_svc` (link register). The security guard's drawer has `R13_fiq`, `R14_fiq`, and a full set `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq` because he needs to work fast and cannot afford to shuffle papers.
- The **time-stamp stamping machine** at the counter top is the `CPSR` (Current Program Status Register) — it is always visible and records *who is currently at the counter*.
- Each privileged role has a **personal time-stamp log** (`SPSR`) that snapshots the previous state when they took over.

> [!NOTE]
> The "drawers" are the **banked registers**. The "slide-in action" is the **exception mode switch**. The "stamping machine" is the **CPSR** with its mode-bits. KTU questions frequently test this analogy translated into exact register nomenclature.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Total physical registers:** **37** (30 general-purpose + 7 status/control)
- **Architecturally visible registers to programmer:** **16** (`R0`–`R15`) + `CPSR` (optionally `SPSR`)
- **Register width:** **32 bits** (hence ARM is a **32-bit ISA**)
- **Operating modes supported:** **7** (1 unprivileged + 6 privileged)
- **Number of banked `R13` (SP) instances:** **6** (one per privileged mode; User/System share)
- **Number of banked `R14` (LR) instances:** **6**
- **Number of banked `R8`–`R12` instances:** **5** (only `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq` are unique to FIQ)
- **Number of `SPSR` registers:** **5** (one per privileged mode except User/System)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of ARM7TDMI Registers

The ARM register file is logically partitioned into three disjoint groups:

1. **Unbanked General-Purpose Registers — `R0`–`R7`**
   - Identical physical storage cell referenced from **every** mode.
   - Used as operand, intermediate result, function parameter, and scratchpad registers.
   - `R7` is, by ABI convention, frequently used to hold the **Thumb-state frame pointer**; many compilers also use it for the System V/Procedure Call Standard.

2. **Banked General-Purpose Registers — `R8`–`R14`**
   - Have **mode-dependent physical instances**.
   - `R8`–`R12` are *partially banked*: a unique physical instance exists **only for FIQ** (`R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq`). All other privileged modes and User mode share the User-mode physical register.
   - `R13` and `R14` are **fully banked**: every privileged mode has its own physical instance, e.g., `R13_svc`, `R13_irq`, `R14_abt`, etc.

3. **Special-Purpose Registers**
   - `R15` — **Program Counter (PC)**: holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched (`PC = current_address + 8` in ARM state).
   - `R14` — **Link Register (LR)**: holds the **return address** when a subroutine is entered via a `BL`/`BLX` instruction; the return PC is automatically saved here.
   - `R13` — **Stack Pointer (SP)**: convention for the current mode's stack. ARM strongly recommends that each privileged mode use a **distinct stack** rooted at its own `R13`.
   - `CPSR` — **Current Program Status Register**: the always-visible mode/status register.
   - `SPSR_*` — **Saved Program Status Register**: one per privileged mode (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined); saves the previous `CPSR` on exception entry.

### 2.2 The Seven ARM Operating Modes and Mode Encoding

ARM7TDMI supports **seven modes**, of which **six are privileged** and accessible only when the processor is in a privileged state. The active mode is encoded in the **lowest 5 bits of the `CPSR`** (bits `[4:0]`).

| Mode Mnemonic | Mode Name | CPSR `[4:0]` Encoding | Privilege Level | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `usr` | User | `10000` (16) | Unprivileged | Normal application execution |
| `fiq` | Fast Interrupt Request | `10001` (17) | Privileged | High-priority, low-latency FIQ |
| `irq` | Interrupt Request | `10010` (18) | Privileged | Normal-priority IRQ |
| `svc` | Supervisor | `10011` (19) | Privileged | Reset, SWI/SVC calls |
| `abt` | Abort | `10111` (23) | Privileged | Memory access / prefetch abort |
| `und` | Undefined | `11011` (27) | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap |
| `sys` | System | `11111` (31) | Privileged | OS tasks needing user registers with full privilege |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** The User (`10000`) and System (`11111`) modes **share the same banked `R8`–`R14` physical registers** because System mode is simply "User mode with the privilege bit enabled". This is why `R13` and `R14` are not duplicated for `sys` — they are the *same* User-mode registers, just accessed with privilege.

### 2.3 CPSR / SPSR Bit-Level Format

The `CPSR` is a 32-bit register with the following canonical ARM7TDMI layout:

$$
\text{CPSR} = \underbrace{N\,Z\,C\,V}_{\text{flags}}\;\underbrace{J}_{\text{Jazelle}}\;\underbrace{\,}_{\text{reserved}}\;\underbrace{IT[1:0]}_{\text{If-Then}}\;\underbrace{\,}_{\text{reserved}}\;\underbrace{GE[3:0]}_{\text{SIMD}\ge}\;\underbrace{IT[7:2]}_{\text{If-Then}}\;\underbrace{\,}_{\text{reserved}}\;\underbrace{M[4:0]}_{\text{Mode}}
$$

| Bit Range | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `[31]` | `N` (Negative) | Set if result MSB is 1 (signed negative) |
| `[30]` | `Z` (Zero) | Set if result is all zeros |
| `[29]` | `C` (Carry) | Set if unsigned overflow / no-borrow from MSB / extend |
| `[28]` | `V` (oVerflow) | Set if signed overflow into the sign bit |
| `[27]` | `J` (Jazelle) | 1 = Jazelle bytecode state (ARM7TDMI: not implemented → read as 0) |
| `[25:24]` | `IT[1:0]` | Low bits of If-Then execution state (ARMv6+; reserved on ARM7TDMI classic) |
| `[19:16]` | `GE[3:0]` | Greater-Equal flags for SIMD byte-wise comparisons (DSP extension) |
| `[15:10]` | `IT[7:2]` | High bits of If-Then state (ARMv6+; reserved on ARM7TDMI) |
| `[7]` | `I` | IRQ disable (1 = masked) |
| `[6]` | `F` | FIQ disable (1 = masked) |
| `[5]` | `T` | Thumb state (1 = Thumb; 0 = ARM) |
| `[4:0]` | `M[4:0]` | Mode encoding (see Section 2.2 table) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The I, F, T, and M bits collectively are what an exception handler manipulates.** The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the `T` bit and `I`/`F` bits as the **most frequently examined** subset of CPSR.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Relation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_{regs}^{phys}$ | Total physical registers in ARM7TDMI | $N_{regs}^{phys} = 30_{GP} + 7_{status}$ | registers |
| $N_{regs}^{arch}$ | Architectural registers visible to programmer | $N_{regs}^{arch} = 15_{GP} + 1_{PC} = 16$ | registers |
| $N_{bank}^{R13}$ | Banked instances of `R13` (SP) | $N_{bank}^{R13} = 6$ | instances |
| $N_{bank}^{R14}$ | Banked instances of `R14` (LR) | $N_{bank}^{R14} = 6$ | instances |
| $N_{bank}^{R8\!-\!R12}$ | Banked instances of `R8`–`R12` | $N_{bank}^{R8\!-\!R12} = 5$ (FIQ only) | instances |
| $N_{SPSR}$ | Saved PSR instances | $N_{SPSR} = 5$ | instances |
| $PC_{next}$ | Value of `R15` after a fetch | $PC_{next} = \text{Address}_{\text{current}} + 8$ | bytes |
| $LR_{BL}$ | Value of `R14` after `BL` | $LR_{BL} = PC_{after\_branch} + 4$ | bytes |
| $SPSR$ save on exception | Auto-snapshot | $SPSR_{new\_mode} \leftarrow CPSR$ | — |
| $CPSR$ load on return | Auto-restore via `SUBS PC, LR, #4` | $CPSR \leftarrow SPSR_{saved}$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> The two most-tested relations in KTU are:
> 1. $R15 = PC = \text{Current Instruction Address} + 8$ in **ARM state**
> 2. $R15 = PC = \text{Current Instruction Address} + 4$ in **Thumb state**
> Writing the wrong constant here costs a full mark in ESE.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The banked register architecture is the foundation on which **deterministic real-time embedded systems** are built. Examples:

- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit)**: The FIQ (fast interrupt) is reserved for the **camshaft position sensor** that must be serviced within microseconds. The banked `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq` allow the FIQ handler to begin computation **without first saving User-mode registers** — saving 5 pushes to stack, which in a 60 MHz ARM7TDMI equals **20+ clock cycles saved per FIQ entry**.
- **Mobile phone baseband processor (legacy GSM)**: The Supervisor mode handles GSM time-slot SWI calls while the User mode runs the protocol stack. Each mode's distinct `R13` stack prevents the privileged kernel from corrupting user stacks.
- **Router line-card controllers**: The IRQ mode services packet arrival, the Abort mode handles MMU page faults, the Undefined mode traps emulator breakpoints — all coexist without mutual stack corruption thanks to banked `R13`/`R14`/`SPSR`.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Register Bank by Mode

The following exhaustive matrix lists **every visible register** (R0–R15 + CPSR + SPSR) for each of the seven operating modes, exactly as the KTU Board examiner expects it. Treat this table as the *single most important artefact* for the Module-4 ESE.

| Visible Register | `usr` (User) | `sys` (System) | `svc` (Supervisor) | `abt` (Abort) | `irq` (IRQ) | `und` (Undefined) | `fiq` (FIQ) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `R0` | `R0` | `R0` | `R0` | `R0` | `R0` | `R0` | `R0` |
| `R1` | `R1` | `R1` | `R1` | `R1` | `R1` | `R1` | `R1` |
| `R2` | `R2` | `R2` | `R2` | `R2` | `R2` | `R2` | `R2` |
| `R3` | `R3` | `R3` | `R3` | `R3` | `R3` | `R3` | `R3` |
| `R4` | `R4` | `R4` | `R4` | `R4` | `R4` | `R4` | `R4` |
| `R5` | `R5` | `R5` | `R5` | `R5` | `R5` | `R5` | `R5` |
| `R6` | `R6` | `R6` | `R6` | `R6` | `R6` | `R6` | `R6` |
| `R7` | `R7` | `R7` | `R7` | `R7` | `R7` | `R7` | `R7` |
| `R8` | `R8` | `R8` | `R8` | `R8` | `R8` | `R8` | `R8_fiq` |
| `R9` | `R9` | `R9` | `R9` | `R9` | `R9` | `R9` | `R9_fiq` |
| `R10` | `R10` | `R10` | `R10` | `R10` | `R10` | `R10` | `R10_fiq` |
| `R11` | `R11` | `R11` | `R11` | `R11` | `R11` | `R11` | `R11_fiq` |
| `R12` | `R12` | `R12` | `R12` | `R12` | `R12` | `R12` | `R12_fiq` |
| `R13 (SP)` | `R13` | `R13` | `R13_svc` | `R13_abt` | `R13_irq` | `R13_und` | `R13_fiq` |
| `R14 (LR)` | `R14` | `R14` | `R14_svc` | `R14_abt` | `R14_irq` | `R14_und` | `R14_fiq` |
| `R15 (PC)` | `R15` | `R15` | `R15` | `R15` | `R15` | `R15` | `R15` |
| `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` | `CPSR` |
| `SPSR` | *(none)* | *(none)* | `SPSR_svc` | `SPSR_abt` | `SPSR_irq` | `SPSR_und` | `SPSR_fiq` |

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Physical Register Count

Let us formally derive why the ARM7TDMI register file is **exactly 37 entries**, not 16 and not 30.

We classify the 16 architectural registers into three groups based on banking behaviour:

$$
N_{phys} = \underbrace{N_{unbanked}}_{\text{shared, no copies}} + \underbrace{N_{partial}}_{\text{copies for select modes}} + \underbrace{N_{full}}_{\text{copies per privileged mode}} + N_{status}
$$

**Step 1 — Unbanked registers (`R0`–`R7`):**
There is a single physical register per name, visible to all 7 modes. So,

$$
N_{unbanked} = 8 \;\text{registers}.
$$

**Step 2 — Partially banked registers (`R8`–`R12`):**
A unique physical instance exists **only for FIQ**. All other 6 modes (User, System, Supervisor, Abort, IRQ, Undefined) share the same User-mode physical register. Therefore,

$$
N_{partial} = \underbrace{5 \times 1}_{\text{one shared register, 5 names}} + \underbrace{5 \times 1}_{\text{one unique FIQ copy, 5 names}} = 10 \;\text{registers}.
$$

**Step 3 — Fully banked registers (`R13` and `R14`):**
For `R13` (and identically for `R14`), there is one physical register for the User/System pair (they share) and one physical register for each of the other 5 privileged modes. So,

$$
N_{full}^{R13} = 1_{usr/sys} + 1_{fiq} + 1_{irq} + 1_{svc} + 1_{abt} + 1_{und} = 6 \;\text{instances per register}.
$$

The same holds for `R14`:

$$
N_{full}^{R14} = 6 \;\text{instances}.
$$

Hence the total `R13` + `R14` physical instances are:

$$
N_{full} = N_{full}^{R13} + N_{full}^{R14} = 6 + 6 = 12 \;\text{registers}.
$$

**Step 4 — `R15` (PC):**
Not banked; single physical register shared by all modes:

$$
N_{R15} = 1.
$$

**Step 5 — Status registers (`CPSR` + `SPSR`):**

$$
N_{status} = \underbrace{1}_{CPSR} + \underbrace{5}_{SPSR_{fiq},\,SPSR_{irq},\,SPSR_{svc},\,SPSR_{abt},\,SPSR_{und}} = 6.
$$

**Step 6 — Aggregate:**

$$
N_{phys} = 8 + 10 + 12 + 1 + 6 = 37 \;\text{registers}.
$$

This matches the official ARM7TDMI Technical Reference Manual figure.

> [!NOTE]
> A common student error is to write $N_{phys} = 30$. That figure refers **only** to general-purpose registers and **omits** the 6 status registers and `R15`. KTU examiners deduct marks for the discrepancy.

### 3.3 Derivation: Return Address Loaded into LR on `BL`

When the ARM executes a `BL subroutine` instruction at address $A_{BL}$, the hardware loads `R14` with a return address so that `MOV PC, R14` at the end of the subroutine returns control. The value stored is:

$$
LR \;\leftarrow\; A_{BL} + 8
$$

Wait — the actual stored value is the address of the *instruction after the branch delay slot*, equivalent to the **PC value that the processor is about to advance to**. The convention is:

$$
LR = \text{Address of instruction following the } BL + 4
$$

In a single-pipeline ARM7 (no branch delay slot), the address *immediately following* `BL` is the next sequential instruction; since `PC` reads as $A_{current} + 8$ in ARM state, we have:

$$
LR = (A_{BL} + 4) + 4 = A_{BL} + 8.
$$

Equivalently, in compact symbolic form:

$$
R14 \;\leftarrow\; PC + 4 \quad \text{where } PC = A_{BL} + 4 \text{ (after fetch stage)} .
$$

For Thumb state the same logic yields:

$$
R14 \;\leftarrow\; PC + 4 \quad \text{where } PC = A_{BL} + 4 .
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The offset of `+4` versus `+8` between ARM and Thumb states confuses students. Memorise the rule: **In both ARM and Thumb, the value stored in LR by `BL` is the address of the next sequential instruction.** The difference is that in ARM state the *visible* `PC` is $A_{current} + 8$, so the stored value is $PC + 4$. In Thumb state the visible `PC` is $A_{current} + 4$, so the stored value is again `next-instruction-address`.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Exception Entry Sequence (IRQ)

To solidify the link between mode bits, banking, and `SPSR`, consider an IRQ exception when the processor is in **User mode** executing at address `0x00008000`. The pipeline has already fetched instructions at `0x00008000` and `0x00008004`.

**Step 1 — IRQ is asserted; the processor completes the current instruction.**

**Step 2 — Mode bits are updated in CPSR:**

$$
CPSR[4:0] \;\leftarrow\; 10010_2 \quad (\text{binary } 10010 = \text{decimal } 18 = \text{IRQ mode}).
$$

**Step 3 — The previous CPSR is auto-saved into the IRQ-mode SPSR:**

$$
SPSR_{irq} \;\leftarrow\; CPSR_{old}.
$$

**Step 4 — The I-bit is set to mask further IRQs:**

$$
CPSR[7] \;\leftarrow\; 1.
$$

**Step 5 — PC is forced to the IRQ vector at `0x00000018`:**

$$
R15 \;\leftarrow\; 0x00000018.
$$

**Step 6 — Banked registers switch automatically:**

$$
R13 \;\text{used from now on is}\; R13_{irq}, \qquad R14 \;\text{used is}\; R14_{irq}.
$$

The User-mode `R13`/`R14` remain *untouched* in their physical storage, and the IRQ handler begins execution with its own private stack and link register.

> [!TIP]
> On return, the standard idiom is `SUBS PC, R14_irq, #4` (or `SUBS PC, LR, #4`). The `S` suffix causes a *programmed* CPSR restore:
> $$CPSR \;\leftarrow\; SPSR_{irq}$$
> This simultaneously reverts the mode bits to `User` (`10000`) and clears the `I` bit to re-enable IRQs.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Table — Register Use Conventions

| Register | Architectural Role | Common Software Convention |
|---|---|---|
| `R0` | Argument / return value register | 1st argument; function return value |
| `R1`–`R3` | Argument registers | 2nd, 3rd, 4th arguments |
| `R4`–`R11` | Variable registers | Must be preserved across calls (callee-saved) |
| `R12` | Intra-procedure-call scratch | `IP` register; scratch for veneers |
| `R13` | Stack Pointer | Points to top of full-descending stack |
| `R14` | Link Register | Saves return address; sub-routine exit `MOV PC, LR` |
| `R15` | Program Counter | `R15 = Current instruction + 8` in ARM state |
| `CPSR` | Status / control | Read via `MRS`, write via `MSR` |
| `SPSR_*` | Saved CPSR | One per privileged mode; only on exception |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — ARM7TDMI Register File Banking

The Mermaid diagram below renders the full **physical register file** with all banking relationships. The naming convention strictly follows the ARM7TDMI TRM so it can be reproduced verbatim in the KTU answer script.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph USERMODE["USER MODE - CPSR M=10000"]
        uR0["R0"]
        uR7["R7"]
        uR8u["R8 to R12 - shared with non-FIQ modes"]
        uR13["R13 - SP user"]
        uR14["R14 - LR user"]
    end

    subgraph SYSMODE["SYSTEM MODE - CPSR M=11111 - shares with User"]
        sR8["R8 to R12"]
        sR13["R13 = R13 user"]
        sR14["R14 = R14 user"]
    end

    subgraph SVCMODE["SUPERVISOR MODE - CPSR M=10011"]
        svR8["R8 to R12"]
        svR13["R13_svc - private stack"]
        svR14["R14_svc - private LR"]
        svSPSR["SPSR_svc"]
    end

    subgraph ABORTMODE["ABORT MODE - CPSR M=10111"]
        abR8["R8 to R12"]
        abR13["R13_abt - private stack"]
        abR14["R14_abt - private LR"]
        abSPSR["SPSR_abt"]
    end

    subgraph IRQMODE["IRQ MODE - CPSR M=10010"]
        iqR8["R8 to R12"]
        iqR13["R13_irq - private stack"]
        iqR14["R14_irq - private LR"]
        iqSPSR["SPSR_irq"]
    end

    subgraph UNDMODE["UNDEFINED MODE - CPSR M=11011"]
        unR8["R8 to R12"]
        unR13["R13_und - private stack"]
        unR14["R14_und - private LR"]
        unSPSR["SPSR_und"]
    end

    subgraph FIQMODE["FIQ MODE - CPSR M=10001"]
        fqR8["R8_fiq to R12_fiq - UNIQUE private copies"]
        fqR13["R13_fiq - private stack"]
        fqR14["R14_fiq - private LR"]
        fqSPSR["SPSR_fiq"]
    end

    GLOBAL["R0 to R7 - UNBANKED shared by ALL 7 modes"]
    PC["R15 - PC - shared by all modes"]
    CPSRREG["CPSR - current status - always visible"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CPSR Bit Field Map

The block diagram below maps every meaningful bit group in the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**. This is a frequent KTU "draw and label" question and must be memorised to the bit-position level.

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPSR["CPSR - 32 bits"]
    CPSR --> F31["Bit 31 - N flag - Negative"]
    CPSR --> F30["Bit 30 - Z flag - Zero"]
    CPSR --> F29["Bit 29 - C flag - Carry"]
    CPSR --> F28["Bit 28 - V flag - Overflow"]
    CPSR --> F27["Bit 27 - J - Jazelle state"]
    CPSR --> F2524["Bits 25 to 24 - IT 1 to 0 - If-Then low"]
    CPSR --> F2316["Bits 23 to 20 - Reserved on ARM7TDMI"]
    CPSR --> F1916["Bits 19 to 16 - GE 3 to 0 - SIMD GE flags"]
    CPSR --> F1510["Bits 15 to 10 - IT 7 to 2 - If-Then high"]
    CPSR --> F98["Bits 9 to 8 - Reserved on ARM7TDMI"]
    CPSR --> F7["Bit 7 - I - IRQ disable mask"]
    CPSR --> F6["Bit 6 - F - FIQ disable mask"]
    CPSR --> F5["Bit 5 - T - Thumb state select"]
    CPSR --> F40["Bits 4 to 0 - M 4 to 0 - MODE bits"]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Mode Switch on Exception

The following flowchart illustrates the **hardware-automatic state transition** when an exception occurs, showing exactly which registers get banked and where the `SPSR` capture happens.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Normal Execution in Mode M_old"] --> B["Exception source asserted IRQ, FIQ, SWI, ABORT, UND, RESET"]
    B --> C["Pipeline drains - current instruction completes"]
    C --> D["CPSR 4 to 0 updated to new mode encoding M_new"]
    D --> E["SPSR_new = old CPSR - mode and flags snapshot saved"]
    E --> F["If IRQ: CPSR 7 = 1 - mask IRQs"]
    F --> G["If FIQ: CPSR 6 = 1 - mask FIQs"]
    G --> H["R14_new = return address - PC plus 4 in ARM state"]
    H --> I["R13_new = private SP of new mode - auto banked"]
    I --> J["R15 = vector address of exception - 0x00, 0x08, 0x10, 0x14, 0x18 or 0x1C"]
    J --> K["Begin exception handler in new mode"]
    K --> L["On handler exit: SUBS PC, LR, offset restores CPSR from SPSR_new"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List the seven operating modes of the ARM7TDMI processor and write the CPSR mode-bit encoding (`M[4:0]`) for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | `M[4:0]` Binary | `M[4:0]` Decimal |
|---|---|---|
| User (`usr`) | `10000` | 16 |
| FIQ (`fiq`) | `10001` | 17 |
| IRQ (`irq`) | `10010` | 18 |
| Supervisor (`svc`) | `10011` | 19 |
| Abort (`abt`) | `10111` | 23 |
| Undefined (`und`) | `11011` | 27 |
| System (`sys`) | `11111` | 31 |

Of these, six (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS) are *privileged*. User mode is the only *unprivileged* mode.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between CPSR and SPSR in the ARM7 architecture. How many SPSR registers exist and why?**

**Model Answer:**

The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a single, always-visible 32-bit register that holds the current condition flags (`N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`), control bits (`I`, `F`, `T`), and the active mode bits (`M[4:0]`). It is accessible in every mode.

The **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is a *per-privileged-mode* register that automatically snapshots the CPSR on exception entry. There are **5 SPSR registers**, one for each of the privileged modes **except User and System** (User/System do not need an SPSR because they cannot be entered *via* an exception — System mode is reached only by a controlled write to CPSR).

On exception, the hardware does $SPSR_{new} \leftarrow CPSR_{old}$. On exception return (`SUBS PC, LR, #…`), $CPSR \leftarrow SPSR_{old}$ atomically restores the previous mode and flags.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the register organization of the ARM7TDMI processor. With a neat diagram, show how registers `R0`–`R15` and the status registers are organized across the seven operating modes. Highlight which registers are *banked* and which are *unbanked*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the format of the **CPSR** in detail. Explain the significance of the `N`, `Z`, `C`, `V` flag bits, the `I`, `F`, `T` control bits, and the `M[4:0]` mode bits. Show the bit-level position of each field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The ARM7TDMI register file has a **physical 37-register, 32-bit-wide** implementation. From the programmer's view, **16 general-purpose registers** (`R0`–`R15`) are visible, plus the `CPSR`, plus an optional `SPSR`.

**Classification:**

1. **Unbanked (`R0`–`R7`)** — 8 registers shared by all 7 modes. Same physical storage regardless of mode.
2. **Partially banked (`R8`–`R12`)** — Banked **only for FIQ mode** (`R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq`). The other six modes use the same physical copies.
3. **Fully banked (`R13`, `R14`)** — Each of the six privileged modes (excluding the User/System pair) has a private copy, giving 6 instances of `R13` (one of which is shared by User & System) and 6 instances of `R14`.
4. **Unbanked (`R15` / PC)** — One shared physical register.
5. **Status registers** — 1 `CPSR` (always visible) + 5 `SPSR`s (one per privileged mode except User/System).

**Diagrammatic representation** (textbook-standard KTU answer form):

```
        User/Sys  FIQ     SVC     ABT     IRQ     UND
R0-R7:   R0-R7   R0-R7   R0-R7   R0-R7   R0-R7   R0-R7     <- unbanked
R8-R12:  R8-R12  R8_fiq  R8-R12  R8-R12  R8-R12  R8-R12    <- banked (FIQ only)
R13:     R13     R13_fiq R13_svc R13_abt R13_irq R13_und   <- fully banked
R14:     R14     R14_fiq R14_svc R14_abt R14_irq R14_und   <- fully banked
R15:     R15     R15     R15     R15     R15     R15       <- shared PC
CPSR:    CPSR    CPSR    CPSR    CPSR    CPSR    CPSR      <- one shared
SPSR:    --      SPSR_fiq SPSR_svc SPSR_abt SPSR_irq SPSR_und
```

**Total physical registers = 8 + 10 + 12 + 1 + 6 = 37.**

The banked registers support **fast exception handling** — when an exception occurs, the hardware automatically switches `R13`, `R14`, and `SPSR` to the new mode's copies without software stack operations.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The `CPSR` is a 32-bit register with the canonical bit-level layout:

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 31 | `N` | Negative | Set when ALU result MSB is 1 |
| 30 | `Z` | Zero | Set when ALU result is zero |
| 29 | `C` | Carry | Set on unsigned carry/borrow; extend bit |
| 28 | `V` | Overflow | Set on signed overflow |
| 27 | `J` | Jazelle | 1 = Jazelle execution (not used in ARM7TDMI) |
| 7  | `I` | IRQ disable | 1 = IRQs masked |
| 6  | `F` | FIQ disable | 1 = FIQs masked |
| 5  | `T` | Thumb | 1 = Thumb state, 0 = ARM state |
| 4:0 | `M` | Mode | Active operating mode |

**Significance of `N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`:** These condition code flags are set by every data-processing instruction (subject to the `S` suffix) and are used by conditional branch instructions `Bxx`, e.g., `BNE` (branch if `Z = 0`).

**Significance of `I`, `F`:** Interrupt-mask bits. Setting `I = 1` prevents IRQ exceptions; setting `F = 1` prevents FIQ exceptions. The CPSR auto-loads these on exception entry (`I = 1` on IRQ, both set on FIQ).

**Significance of `T`:** Toggles ARM vs Thumb state. The `BX` instruction switches state by writing to the LSB of the target address; the BX hardware also writes the `T` bit of CPSR.

**Significance of `M[4:0]`:** Encodes the active mode (see table in Q1). Writing to `M[4:0]` is restricted in User mode; only privileged modes can perform a mode change via `MSR`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. Forgetting that `R8`–`R12` are banked **only for FIQ**, not for all privileged modes. Common wrong answer: "R8–R12 are banked for IRQ and SVC too." This is a **2-mark** penalty.
> 2. Writing `CPSR[5] = 1` means ARM state, not Thumb state. The `T` bit = 1 selects **Thumb**, not ARM.
> 3. Confusing `R13` as the *Link Register*. `R13` is **Stack Pointer (SP)**; `R14` is the **Link Register (LR)**. Reversing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. Claiming that `SPSR` exists in User or System mode. It does **not** — only 5 SPSRs total.
> 5. Writing the visible `PC` as `Current address + 4` in ARM state. The ARM7TDMI architectural PC reads as `Current + 8`. This is a *very common* KTU trap.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice for Internal Option

**(a)** Explain the concept of **register banking** in the ARM7TDMI. Why is `R8`–`R12` banked *only* for FIQ, while `R13` and `R14` are banked for *every* privileged mode? Illustrate with the use case of an FIQ handler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, describe the **format of the SPSR** and explain the **sequence of operations that occur during an exception entry** (use the IRQ case as a working example). Indicate what is saved in `R14_irq` and `SPSR_irq`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Register banking** is a hardware technique in which the same *architectural* register name (`R8`, `R13`, `R14`, etc.) maps to a *different physical storage cell* depending on the current processor mode. This eliminates the need for software to push/pop user registers on every exception entry, achieving very low exception latency.

**Why `R13` and `R14` are banked for all privileged modes:**

- `R13` (SP) must be **mode-specific** because each privileged mode normally has its **own stack region** in memory. If a single `R13` were shared, an IRQ handler would overwrite the supervisor's stack pointer, making nested exceptions impossible.
- `R14` (LR) must be **mode-specific** because each mode can enter its own *nested* subroutines. A single LR would be clobbered by the first subroutine call.
- Banking is **transparent to software** — the `STMFD`/`LDMFD` instructions reference the *active* `R13`/`R14` automatically.

**Why `R8`–`R12` are banked *only* for FIQ:**

- FIQ is designed to be the **fastest possible exception** path. In a typical FIQ handler, the entire routine is written to use *only* `R0`–`R7` and `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq`, so the handler can simply **leave User mode's `R0`–`R12` and `R13`/`R14` untouched** and exit with no stack operations at all.
- Banking these only for FIQ (instead of all 6 privileged modes) **saves silicon area**: only 5 additional physical registers (`R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq`) are needed, rather than $5 \times 5 = 25$ copies.
- Other privileged modes (IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND) handle their registers by **explicitly saving** `R0`–`R12` and `R14` to the mode's stack at handler entry, since their latencies are not as critical as FIQ.

**FIQ Use Case (Automotive ECU example):**

A crankshaft-position sensor triggers an FIQ every 1 ms. The handler computes the next fuel-injection timing using only `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq` and `R0`–`R7`. On exit, it executes `SUBS PC, R14_fiq, #4`, which atomically:
- Restores `CPSR` from `SPSR_fiq` (re-enters User mode).
- Jumps back to the interrupted instruction.

No `R0`–`R12` saving, no `R13`/`R14` saving — the entire context switch is **one instruction** plus the auto-snapshot of `CPSR → SPSR_fiq`. This achieves sub-microsecond latency at 60 MHz.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The `SPSR_*` register has **identical bit-level format to the CPSR** (it is, in effect, a CPSR-sized shadow cell). The same 32-bit layout — flags (`N Z C V`), control bits (`J`, `I`, `F`, `T`), and mode bits (`M[4:0]`) — applies.

**IRQ Exception Entry Sequence** (assume CPU is in User mode at address $A_{current}$):

**Step 1:** The current instruction completes execution; the pipeline drains.

**Step 2:** The processor latches the exception source (IRQ line asserted).

**Step 3:** The new mode bits are written into the CPSR:

$$
CPSR[4:0] \;\leftarrow\; 10010_2 \quad (\text{IRQ mode}).
$$

**Step 4:** The previous CPSR is auto-captured:

$$
SPSR_{irq} \;\leftarrow\; CPSR_{old}.
$$

At this point, $SPSR_{irq}$ contains the User-mode flag state and the `I = 0` / `F = 0` bits (assuming they were 0 before).

**Step 5:** The I-bit is set to prevent nested IRQs:

$$
CPSR[7] \;\leftarrow\; 1.
$$

**Step 6:** `R14_irq` is loaded with the **return address**. In ARM state:

$$
R14_{irq} \;\leftarrow\; PC + 4 = A_{current} + 4.
$$

**Step 7:** `R15` is forced to the IRQ vector at address `0x00000018`:

$$
R15 \;\leftarrow\; 0x00000018.
$$

**Step 8:** The banked `R13_irq` (and `R14_irq`) are now the active SP and LR.

**Step 9:** Execution begins at `0x00000018` (the IRQ vector) in IRQ mode.

**Exit idiom** for IRQ: `SUBS PC, R14_irq, #4`. The `S` suffix triggers the special CPSR-restore semantics:

$$
CPSR \;\leftarrow\; SPSR_{irq} \quad \text{(reverts to User mode, clears I bit)}.
$$

$$
PC \;\leftarrow\; R14_{irq} - 4.
$$

This single instruction undoes the entire mode-entry state change.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Part (b):**
> 1. Do **not** say "`R14_irq` is loaded with `PC + 8`". The correct value is `PC + 4` in ARM state because the architecture wants to return to the **instruction that was interrupted**, not the one after it. (Use `SUBS PC, LR, #4` to compensate.)
> 2. Do **not** confuse the return idiom for IRQ (`SUBS PC, LR, #4`) with that for SWI (`MOVS PC, LR`) or Prefetch Abort (`SUBS PC, LR, #4` is correct, but the **return target** differs). Mismatching the offset returns to the wrong instruction and crashes the system.
> 3. Some students omit the I-bit setting in Step 5. Without `CPSR[7] = 1`, a nested IRQ could fire and corrupt the handler's state. KTU examiners *always* check this line.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Total physical registers in ARM7TDMI = 37** (8 unbanked + 10 partially-banked + 12 fully-banked + 1 PC + 6 status). The architectural programmer's view is **16** general-purpose registers + `CPSR`.
- **Unbanked (shared by all 7 modes):** `R0`–`R7`, `R15` (PC), `CPSR`.
- **Partially banked (banked only for FIQ):** `R8`–`R12`. Unique copies: `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq`.
- **Fully banked:** `R13` (SP) and `R14` (LR). **6 instances each** — User/System share, the other 5 privileged modes are private.
- **Status registers:** 1 `CPSR` + 5 `SPSR` (one per privileged mode except User/System).
- **Mode bits in CPSR (`M[4:0]`):** User = `10000`, FIQ = `10001`, IRQ = `10010`, SVC = `10011`, ABT = `10111`, UND = `11011`, SYS = `11111`.
- **CPSR control bits:** `I` (bit 7, IRQ mask), `F` (bit 6, FIQ mask), `T` (bit 5, Thumb state). Always remember `T = 1` ⇒ Thumb.
- **Condition flags in CPSR:** `N` (31), `Z` (30), `C` (29), `V` (28).
- **Visible `R15` (PC) in ARM state =** `Current instruction address + 8`. In **Thumb state =** `Current instruction address + 4`.
- **`BL` stores** `LR = address of next sequential instruction`. In ARM state this is `R15 - 4`; in Thumb state this is `R15 - 0`.
- **Exception entry always does:** `R14_new ← return address`, `SPSR_new ← CPSR_old`, mask the appropriate I/F bit, set new mode bits, force PC to vector.
- **Exception exit idiom:** `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (IRQ/Prefetch Abort) or `MOVS PC, LR` (SWI) — the `S` is what causes the atomic CPSR restore.
- **FIQ latency advantage** comes from the banked `R8_fiq`–`R12_fiq` + banked `R13_fiq` + banked `R14_fiq` + banked `SPSR_fiq`. The handler can be *register-only*, requiring **no stack save/restore** for `R0`–`R12`.
- **System mode ≠ a separate banked copy**; it shares User-mode registers but operates with full privilege. This is the *only* way a privileged OS task can use the User-mode register set without taking an exception.
- **Privilege rule:** Only privileged modes can perform `MSR` writes to the *control* (`I`, `F`, `T`) and *mode* (`M[4:0]`) fields of CPSR. User mode is restricted to writes of the *flag* field (`N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`).
- **KTU memory hook for ESE:** "Banked registers" + "CPSR bit map" + "exception entry sequence" = the *3-questions-guaranteed* cluster for Module 4. Memorise the mode-bit table to the bit pattern, not just the decimal number.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second mnemonic** — *“R0–R7 forever, R8–R12 only FIQ, R13–R14 always, R15 and CPSR never banked, five SPSRs, six private SPs, one private R8–R12 bunch — that is the 37 of ARM7.”*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPSR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPSR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit special-purpose register in the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) processor architecture** that holds critical information about the current state of the processor. It is accessible in all processor modes and contains the **condition code flags**, **control bits**, and **current operating mode bits** of the ARM core.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In the ARM7TDMI-based LPC2148 microcontroller (the primary KTU 2024 syllabus processor), the CPSR is the most critical status register. Every arithmetic, logical, and data-processing operation implicitly updates the CPSR's condition flags, which subsequently drive all conditional branch instructions in ARM assembly.

The 32 bits of the CPSR are partitioned into **four logical byte-sized fields**:

| Field Name | Bit Positions | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Condition Flags | Bits [31:28] | N, Z, C, V flags |
| Reserved / Q + J | Bits [27:24] | Q (sticky overflow), J (Jazelle) |
| Interrupt Masks | Bits [7:6] | I (IRQ disable), F (FIQ disable) |
| State & Mode | Bits [5:0] | T (Thumb), M[4:0] (Mode) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Dashboard

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: CPSR = The Car's Dashboard**
> 
> Think of the ARM processor as a **car engine**, and the **CPSR as the car's instrument cluster (dashboard)**. Just like a dashboard shows you:
> - **Speed, fuel, temperature** (analogous to the **condition flags N, Z, C, V** — telling you the *result* of the last arithmetic),
> - **Fog light and seatbelt indicators** (analogous to the **I and F bits** — telling you which *interrupts* are masked),
> - **The gear the car is in** (analogous to the **M[4:0] mode bits** — telling you the *current processor mode* like User, Supervisor, or IRQ),
> 
> the CPSR gives the programmer and the processor a **single-glance snapshot** of *everything* needed to make the next decision. Just as a driver glances at the dashboard before changing gears, the ARM core glances at the CPSR flags before deciding to branch.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Register Width:** **32 bits**
- **Condition Flag Field Width:** **4 bits** (N, Z, C, V)
- **Processor Mode Field Width:** **5 bits** (M[4:0]) → supports up to **2⁵ = 32 modes**
- **Mode Bits Encoding Length:** **5 bits** (ARM standard)
- **Banked Companion Register:** **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** — one per privileged mode (except User/System)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bit-by-Bit Architecture of the CPSR

The CPSR is divided into **8 distinct control groups**. Below is an exhaustive breakdown, exactly as required for KTU 2024 scheme questions:

```
Bit:   31  30  29  28  | 27  26  25  24 | 23 ... 8 | 7  6 | 5  4  3  2  1  0
       N   Z   C   V   |  Q  J  RES RES |  (unused)|  I  F | T  M4 M3 M2 M1 M0
```

#### 2.1.1 Condition Code Flags (Bits [31:28])

These are the **most frequently tested bits** in KTU exams. They are automatically updated by every **data-processing instruction** when the `S` suffix is used (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`).

| Flag | Name | Set When | Cleared When | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | **Negative** | MSB of result = **1** | MSB of result = **0** | Result is negative in signed interpretation |
| **Z** | **Zero** | Result = **0** | Result ≠ **0** | Result is exactly zero (equality indicator) |
| **C** | **Carry** | **Unsigned overflow** (carry-out) | No carry-out | Used for multi-precision arithmetic and unsigned comparisons |
| **V** | **Overflow** | **Signed overflow** occurred | No signed overflow | Used for signed arithmetic boundary detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight — C vs V:**
> - The **C (Carry)** flag tracks **unsigned overflow** (e.g., `0xFFFFFFFF + 1 = 0` with C=1).
> - The **V (Overflow)** flag tracks **signed overflow** (e.g., `0x7FFFFFFF + 1` is negative in signed interpretation, so V=1).
> - These are **independent** and are both essential for different types of arithmetic.

#### 2.1.2 Q Flag (Bit 27) — Sticky Overflow

- Set when **saturation arithmetic overflow** occurs in enhanced DSP instructions.
- Once set, it can **only be cleared by an MSR instruction** (never by a normal data operation) — hence "sticky."
- Not available in basic ARM7, but present in ARM9E and later cores.

#### 2.1.3 J Bit (Bit 24) — Jazelle State

- When **J = 1**, the processor executes **Java bytecode** directly (Jazelle-DBX technology).
- **J = 0** for normal ARM/Thumb execution.
- LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S) does **not** support Jazelle; this bit is reserved.

#### 2.1.4 Interrupt Mask Bits (Bits [7:6])

| Bit | Name | Effect When Set |
|---|---|---|
| **I = 1** | IRQ Disable | **Normal interrupts (IRQ) are masked** |
| **F = 1** | FIQ Disable | **Fast interrupts (FIQ) are masked** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** When the processor enters any privileged mode (except User), the corresponding **banked SPSR** captures the *previous* CPSR value, and the CPSR is updated with the new mode and appropriate I/F bit values. This is the foundation of ARM's **exception handling model**.

#### 2.1.5 T Bit (Bit 5) — Thumb State

- **T = 0** → Processor executes **32-bit ARM instructions**.
- **T = 1** → Processor executes **16-bit Thumb instructions** (compressed code for memory-constrained systems).
- Directly controlled by the `BX` and `BLX` instructions.

#### 2.1.6 Mode Bits M[4:0] (Bits [4:0])

The **5-bit mode field** selects one of the ARM processor modes:

| M[4:0] | Mode | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **10000** (0x10) | **User (USR)** | Unprivileged | Normal application code |
| **10001** (0x11) | **FIQ (FIQ)** | Privileged | Fast interrupt handling |
| **10010** (0x12) | **IRQ (IRQ)** | Privileged | Normal interrupt handling |
| **10011** (0x13) | **Supervisor (SVC)** | Privileged | OS kernel / SWI calls |
| **10111** (0x17) | **Abort (ABT)** | Privileged | Memory access failures |
| **11011** (0x1B) | **Undefined (UND)** | Privileged | Undefined instructions |
| **11111** (0x1F) | **System (SYS)** | Privileged | OS tasks with User registers |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — CPSR Flag Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table for CPSR Flag Calculations**

| Operation | N | Z | C (Carry) | V (Overflow) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ADD Rd, Rn, Rm** | N = MSB(Rd) | Z = 1 if Rd = 0 | C = carry-out of bit 31 | V = (Rn₃₁ = Rm₃₁) AND (Rd₃₁ ≠ Rn₃₁) |
| **SUB Rd, Rn, Rm** | N = MSB(Rd) | Z = 1 if Rd = 0 | C = NOT(borrow) = 1 if Rn ≥ Rm (unsigned) | V = (Rn₃₁ ≠ Rm₃₁) AND (Rd₃₁ ≠ Rn₃₁) |
| **CMP Rn, Rm** (SUB without store) | Updates all 4 flags, result discarded | — | — | — |
| **Logical AND / ORR / EOR** | N = MSB(Rd) | Z = 1 if Rd = 0 | C = **0** (destroyed) | V = **0** (destroyed) |
| **MOV Rd, #imm** | N = MSB(Rd) | Z = 1 if Rd = 0 | C = unaffected (no S suffix) | V = unaffected |

### 2.3 The SPSR — Banked Companion Register

- **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** exists in **every privileged mode except User and System**.
- When an exception occurs, the **current CPSR is copied into the banked SPSR of the new mode**.
- Upon exception return (via `SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `MOVS PC, LR`), the SPSR is **restored back into the CPSR**.
- This mechanism provides **atomic save/restore of processor state** during exception handling.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How CPSR is Used |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Code Generation** | Compilers emit conditional branches (`BGE`, `BNE`) that test CPSR flags for `if/else` constructs |
| **RTOS Kernel Development** | The OS uses `MSR`/`MRS` instructions to manipulate CPSR mode bits during context switches |
| **Embedded Interrupt Programming** | Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) rely on CPSR's I-bit state to enable/disable nested interrupts |
| **Digital Signal Processing** | The Q flag and saturation arithmetic in ARM9E+ cores use CPSR for overflow protection |
| **Bootloader Development** | The `BLX` instruction uses the T-bit in CPSR to switch between ARM and Thumb modes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: CPSR Flag Update for an ADD Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard):**
> Compute the CPSR condition flags (N, Z, C, V) after the instruction `ADDS R0, R1, R2`, where:
> - **R1 = 0x7FFFFFF0** (a large positive signed number)
> - **R2 = 0x00000020** (32 in decimal)

**Step 1: Convert hexadecimal operands to binary for bit-level analysis.**

$$R1 = 0\text{x}7\text{FFFFFF0} = 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,0000_2$$

$$R2 = 0\text{x}00000020 = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0010\,0000_2$$

**Step 2: Perform the binary addition bit-by-bit.**

$$0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,0000$$
$$+ \quad 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0010\,0000$$
$$= \quad 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,0000\,(+\,\text{lower bits})$$

Let us compute the lower 5 bits:
- Bit 4 (the '1' in R2) + Bit 4 of R1 (= 1) → produces a carry.
- Detailed sum: `00000 + 100000` (5-bit addition) = `100000`, carry-out of bit 4 = 1.

So the full result is:

$$R0 = 0\text{x}80000010 = 1000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001\,0000_2$$

**Step 3: Determine the N (Negative) flag.**

The N flag is simply the MSB (bit 31) of the result.

$$N = \text{bit}_{31}(R0) = 1$$

> **Reasoning:** The result has its most significant bit set, so in signed 2's complement interpretation, the number is negative.

**Step 4: Determine the Z (Zero) flag.**

$$Z = 1 \iff R0 = 0$$

Since $R0 = 0\text{x}80000010 \neq 0$:

$$Z = 0$$

**Step 5: Determine the C (Carry) flag.**

The C flag is set if there is a **carry-out from bit 31** of the addition. In our case, we are adding two 32-bit positive numbers with no overflow beyond bit 31:

$$0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,0000 \quad (\text{no carry-out from bit 31})$$

$$\text{Carry-out from bit 31} = 0$$

$$C = 0$$

**Step 6: Determine the V (Signed Overflow) flag.**

Using the KTU formula:

$$V = (\text{sign}(R1) = \text{sign}(R2)) \;\text{AND}\; (\text{sign}(R0) \neq \text{sign}(R1))$$

- $\text{sign}(R1) = 0$ (positive, MSB = 0)
- $\text{sign}(R2) = 0$ (positive, MSB = 0)
- $\text{sign}(R0) = 1$ (negative, MSB = 1)

Therefore:

$$V = (0 = 0) \;\text{AND}\; (1 \neq 0) = 1 \;\text{AND}\; 1 = 1$$

**Step 7: Assemble the final CPSR flag field (bits [31:28]).**

| Bit | 31 (N) | 30 (Z) | 29 (C) | 28 (V) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

$$\text{CPSR}_{[31:28]} = \text{NZCV} = 1001_2 = 0\text{x}9$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** After `ADDS R0, R1, R2` with the given operands, the **N and V flags are SET**, while **Z and C flags are CLEARED**. This is a classic **signed overflow** scenario: `0x7FFFFFF0 + 0x20 = 0x80000010`, which crosses the positive-to-negative boundary in 2's complement representation.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: CPSR Flag Update for a SUB Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard):**
> Compute the CPSR flags after `SUBS R3, R4, R5`, where:
> - **R4 = 0x00000050** (80 decimal)
> - **R5 = 0x000000A0** (160 decimal)

**Step 1: Perform the subtraction using 2's complement addition.**

$$R4 - R5 = R4 + (\text{NOT}(R5) + 1)$$

$$\text{NOT}(R5) = \text{NOT}(0\text{x}000000\text{A}0) = 0\text{xFFFFFF5F}$$

$$\text{NOT}(R5) + 1 = 0\text{xFFFFFF60}$$

**Step 2: Add.**

$$0\text{x}00000050 + 0\text{xFFFFFF60} = 0\text{xFFFFFFB0}$$

$$R3 = 0\text{xFFFFFFB0} = 1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1011\,0000_2$$

**Step 3: Determine flags.**

| Flag | Computation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | bit₃₁(R3) = 1 | **1** |
| **Z** | R3 = 0? No | **0** |
| **C** | $R4 \geq R5$ unsigned? `0x50 < 0xA0`, so **borrow occurred**, hence C = 0 (NOT borrow) | **0** |
| **V** | $(R4_{31} \neq R5_{31}) = (0 \neq 0) = 0$ → $V = 0$ | **0** |

$$\text{CPSR}_{[31:28]} = \text{NZCV} = 1000_2 = 0\text{x}8$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** For SUB, the **C flag is the INVERSE of borrow**. C=1 means "no borrow" (unsigned result is valid), C=0 means "borrow occurred." This trips up many students.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All 4 Flags for All Data-Processing Operations

| Instruction Type | Updates N | Updates Z | Updates C | Updates V | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ADD` / `ADC` (with S) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ADC adds C as carry-in |
| `SUB` / `SBC` / `RSB` / `RSC` (with S) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | C = NOT(borrow) for SUB family |
| `AND` / `BIC` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (cleared) | ✗ (cleared) | Logical ops **destroy C and V** |
| `ORR` / `EOR` / `MOV` / `MVN` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (cleared) | ✗ (cleared) | Logical ops **destroy C and V** |
| `TST` (AND without Rd) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (cleared) | ✗ (cleared) | Discards result, sets flags |
| `TEQ` (EOR without Rd) | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (cleared) | ✗ (cleared) | Used for equality tests |
| `CMP` (SUB without Rd) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Most common flag-setting instruction |
| `CMN` (ADD without Rd) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Tests if Rn = -Rm |
| `MUL` / `MLA` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (undefined) | ✗ (undefined) | C and V are **undefined** in ARM7 |

### 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Computing the V (Overflow) Flag Algebraically

The signed overflow for **ADD** is defined as:

$$V_{\text{ADD}} = (R_n^{31} \oplus R_m^{31} \oplus R_d^{31}) \;\text{AND}\; (R_n^{31} \oplus R_m^{31})$$

> This is derived from the principle: **overflow occurs when two numbers of the same sign produce a result of the opposite sign.**

**For SUB** (where $R_d = R_n - R_m$):

$$V_{\text{SUB}} = (R_n^{31} \oplus R_m^{31}) \;\text{AND}\; (R_n^{31} \oplus R_d^{31})$$

> **Reasoning:** Subtracting a positive from a negative (or vice versa) can overflow, and the result sign must be verified against the minuend sign.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPSR Bit-Field Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB

    classDef modeBox fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    cpsrMain["CPSR 32-bit Register"]:::mainBox

    cpsrMain --> flagField["Bits 31 to 28: Condition Flags NZCV"]:::flagsBox
    flagField --> nBit["Bit 31: N Negative Flag"]
    flagField --> zBit["Bit 30: Z Zero Flag"]
    flagField --> cBit["Bit 29: C Carry Flag"]
    flagField --> vBit["Bit 28: V Overflow Flag"]

    cpsrMain --> qjField["Bits 27 to 24: Q J Reserved"]
    qjField --> qBit["Bit 27: Q Saturation Flag"]
    qjField --> jBit["Bit 24: J Jazelle State"]

    cpsrMain --> intField["Bits 7 to 6: Interrupt Masks"]:::interruptBox
    intField --> iBit["Bit 7: I IRQ Disable"]
    intField --> fBit["Bit 6: F FIQ Disable"]

    cpsrMain --> modeField["Bits 4 to 0: M4 M3 M2 M1 M0 Processor Mode"]:::modeBox

    modeField --> fiqMode["10001 FIQ Mode"]

    modeField --> svcMode["10011 Supervisor Mode"]
    modeField --> abtMode["10111 Abort Mode"]
    modeField --> undMode["11011 Undefined Mode"]
    modeField --> sysMode["11111 System Mode"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — CPSR Update on Exception Entry

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef normal fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef exception fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef spsr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef newMode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000

    A["Normal Execution in User Mode CPSR_USR"]:::normal
    B["Exception Occurs IRQ FIQ SVC ABORT UNDEF RESET"]:::exception
    C["Hardware Action: CPSR USR copied to SPSR of new mode"]:::spsr
    D["CPSR Mode bits updated to new privileged mode"]:::newMode
    E["CPSR I bit set to disable IRQ by default"]:::newMode
    F["CPSR F bit set to disable FIQ by default"]:::newMode
    G["Execution branches to Exception Vector Address"]:::newMode
    H["ISR runs in new mode with CPSR_new"]:::normal
    I["Exception Return: SUBS PC LR number 4"]:::normal
    J["SPSR_new value is automatically restored to CPSR"]:::spsr
    K["Original execution resumes in previous mode"]:::normal

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPSR vs SPSR Banked Architecture

```mermaid

    classDef spsr fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noSpsr fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000

    CPSR["CPSR One global register accessible in all modes"]:::cpsr

    SPSR_FIQ["SPSR_fiq Banked for FIQ mode"]:::spsr
    SPSR_SVC["SPSR_svc Banked for Supervisor mode"]:::spsr

    NO_SPSR_USR["User Mode: NO SPSR"]:::noSpsr

    CPSR -.copied to on exception.-> SPSR_FIQ
```


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1: Define CPSR. List all the condition flags it contains.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit register in the ARM processor architecture that stores the current state of the processor, including condition flags, interrupt mask bits, processor mode, and Thumb state. It is accessible in all processor modes.

The **four condition flags** in the CPSR (bits [31:28]) are:

| Flag | Full Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | Negative | Set when the result of an operation is negative (MSB = 1) |
| **Z** | Zero | Set when the result is zero |
| **C** | Carry | Set on unsigned overflow / carry-out from bit 31 |
| **V** | Overflow | Set on signed overflow |

These flags are automatically updated by any data-processing instruction that uses the `S` suffix (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `CMP`).

---

#### **Question 2: What is the difference between CPSR and SPSR in ARM?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **CPSR** | **SPSR** |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Current Program Status Register | Saved Program Status Register |
| **Quantity** | One (global) | One per privileged mode (except User & System) → 5 total |
| **Purpose** | Holds the **current** processor status | **Saves** the CPSR value when an exception occurs |
| **Accessibility** | Accessible in **all** processor modes | Accessible only in the **corresponding privileged mode** |
| **Update Mechanism** | Updated by data-processing instructions, `MSR`, and exception entry | Updated only by hardware on exception entry; restored to CPSR on exception return |
| **Banked?** | No | Yes (physically separate register per mode) |

When an exception (e.g., IRQ) occurs, the current CPSR is **automatically copied** into the SPSR of the new mode. On exception return (`SUBS PC, LR, #4`), the SPSR is **restored back to CPSR**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**a) Explain the bit-by-bit structure of the CPSR register in ARM7 processor. List all fields with their bit positions and explain the function of each group.** **(7 Marks)**

**b) For the following ARM instruction sequence, compute the CPSR flags after each instruction. Initial values: R0 = 0x00000005, R1 = 0xFFFFFFFE, R2 = 0x00000003.** **(7 Marks)**

```
SUBS R3, R0, R2      ; R3 = R0 - R2
ADDS R4, R1, R2      ; R4 = R1 + R2
CMP R0, R1           ; Compare R0 with R1
ANDS R5, R0, R2      ; R5 = R0 AND R2
```

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CPSR Structure:**

| Bits | Field | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **[31:28]** | **N, Z, C, V** | Condition flags updated by data-processing instructions |
| **[27]** | **Q** | Sticky saturation overflow flag (ARM9E+) |
| **[24]** | **J** | Jazelle state (Java bytecode execution) |
| **[7]** | **I** | IRQ interrupt disable (1 = masked) |
| **[6]** | **F** | FIQ interrupt disable (1 = masked) |
| **[5]** | **T** | Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb) |
| **[4:0]** | **M[4:0]** | Processor mode (USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS) |

**Part (b) — Flag Computations:**

**Instruction 1: `SUBS R3, R0, R2`**
- R3 = 0x00000005 − 0x00000003 = 0x00000002
- N = 0 (MSB of 0x02 = 0)
- Z = 0 (result ≠ 0)
- C = 1 (no borrow, 0x05 ≥ 0x03 unsigned)
- V = 0 (no signed overflow, both operands and result positive)

**CPSR flags = 0010**

**Instruction 2: `ADDS R4, R1, R2`**
- R4 = 0xFFFFFFFE + 0x00000003 = 0x00000001 (with carry-out from bit 31, discarded)
- N = 0 (MSB of 0x01 = 0)
- Z = 0 (result ≠ 0)
- C = 1 (carry-out from bit 31)
- V = 0 (signed: −2 + 3 = +1, no overflow)

**CPSR flags = 0010**

**Instruction 3: `CMP R0, R1`**
- Performs R0 − R1 = 0x00000005 − 0xFFFFFFFE = 0x00000007 (with borrow)
- N = 0
- Z = 0
- C = 0 (borrow occurred, since 0x05 < 0xFFFFFFFE unsigned)
- V = 0

**CPSR flags = 0000**

**Instruction 4: `ANDS R5, R0, R2`**
- R5 = 0x00000005 AND 0x00000003 = 0x00000001
- N = 0
- Z = 0
- C = **0 (cleared — logical AND destroys C)**
- V = **0 (cleared — logical AND destroys V)**

**CPSR flags = 0000**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) What is the role of the CPSR mode bits M[4:0]? List all seven ARM processor modes with their M[4:0] encodings. Explain how CPSR is modified when an IRQ exception occurs.** **(7 Marks)**

**b) Write an ARM assembly program snippet to demonstrate the use of CPSR flags for implementing a conditional branch. Use CMP and a conditional branch instruction. Explain each instruction's effect on the CPSR.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CPSR Mode Bits and IRQ Exception:**

The **M[4:0] field** (bits [4:0]) of the CPSR selects the current **processor mode**. The seven ARM processor modes are:

| M[4:0] | Mode | Type |
|---|---|---|
| 10000 (0x10) | **User (USR)** | Unprivileged |
| 10001 (0x11) | **FIQ** | Privileged |
| 10010 (0x12) | **IRQ** | Privileged |
| 10011 (0x13) | **Supervisor (SVC)** | Privileged |
| 10111 (0x17) | **Abort (ABT)** | Privileged |
| 11011 (0x1B) | **Undefined (UND)** | Privileged |
| 11111 (0x1F) | **System (SYS)** | Privileged |

**IRQ Exception — CPSR Modifications (Step-by-Step):**

1. The hardware **copies the current CPSR** into **SPSR_irq** (the banked SPSR of IRQ mode).
2. The **M[4:0] bits** of CPSR are set to **10010** (IRQ mode = 0x12).
3. The **I bit (bit 7)** is set to **1** to **disable further IRQ** (prevents nested interrupts).
4. The **F bit (bit 6)** is left as-is (FIQ can still preempt).
5. The **T bit (bit 5)** is forced to **0** (ARM state, since vectors are in ARM code).
6. The **PC is loaded with 0x00000018** (IRQ vector address in ARM7).
7. Execution begins at the IRQ vector.

**Part (b) — ARM Assembly with CPSR Flag Use:**

```arm
        MOV     R0, #10          ; R0 = 10
        MOV     R1, #20          ; R1 = 20
        CMP     R0, R1           ; Compute R0 - R1, update CPSR flags
                                 ; N=1 (result -10 is negative)
                                 ; Z=0 (result is not zero)
                                 ; C=0 (borrow: 10 < 20)
                                 ; V=0 (no signed overflow)
        BGT     R0_GREATER       ; Branch if Greater Than (signed)
                                 ; BGT checks Z=0 AND N=V
                                 ; Z=0 AND (1=0) is FALSE, so NO branch
        MOV     R2, #0           ; R2 = 0 (executed since BGT not taken)
        B       END              ; Skip the else-branch
R0_GREATER
        MOV     R2, #1           ; R2 = 1 (NOT executed in this case)
END
```

**Explanation of CPSR Interaction:**
- `CMP R0, R1` performs `R0 - R1` internally, **discards the result**, but **updates the N, Z, C, V flags** in CPSR.
- `BGT` is a **conditional branch** that checks the CPSR's N and V flags: it branches only if `Z = 0` AND `N = V`.
- In our case, N=1, V=0, so the condition `N = V` is false, and the branch is **not taken**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **C flag confusion in SUB:** Students often write C=1 for a borrow case. **Remember: C = NOT(borrow) for SUB.** C=0 means borrow occurred.
> 
> 2. **Logical ops and C/V flag:** Students assume `AND`/`ORR` preserve C and V. **They are CLEARED, not preserved.**
> 
> 3. **MUL instruction flags:** The C and V flags after `MUL` are **UNDEFINED** (in ARM7) — never assume they hold meaningful values.
> 
> 4. **SPSR accessibility:** Writing `MRS R0, SPSR` from User mode causes an **illegal instruction exception**, not a silent failure.
> 
> 5. **Mode bit width:** Some students write M[3:0] (4 bits) instead of M[4:0] (5 bits). **MUST be 5 bits** to encode 32 possible modes.
> 
> 6. **CMP vs TST:** `CMP` is a SUB without destination (sets all 4 flags); `TST` is an AND without destination (clears C and V).
> 
> 7. **Branching condition codes:** Mixing up `BGE` (signed ≥) and `BHS`/`BCC` (unsigned ≥). KTU expects you to know the difference.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — CPSR**

- ☐ **CPSR is a 32-bit register** present in **all ARM processor modes**.
- ☐ The **condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) occupy **bits [31:28]**.
- ☐ **N = MSB of result** (negative flag for signed interpretation).
- ☐ **Z = 1 if and only if result = 0** (equality indicator).
- ☐ **C = carry-out of bit 31** for ADD; **C = NOT(borrow)** for SUB.
- ☐ **V = signed overflow** — both operands same sign, result opposite sign.
- ☐ The **I bit (bit 7)** masks IRQ; the **F bit (bit 6)** masks FIQ.
- ☐ The **T bit (bit 5)** controls ARM (0) vs Thumb (1) execution.
- ☐ The **M[4:0] field (bits [4:0])** encodes the 7 processor modes using 5 bits.
- ☐ **SPSR** is the banked companion of CPSR — one per privileged mode (except User & System).
- ☐ On **exception entry**: CPSR → SPSR_new_mode, then CPSR mode bits updated, I bit set, PC loaded with vector.
- ☐ On **exception return**: SPSR is restored to CPSR (via `SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `MOVS PC, LR`).
- ☐ The **S suffix** in instructions (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`) is what **triggers flag updates** — without it, CPSR is not modified.
- ☐ **Logical instructions** (AND, ORR, EOR, MOV, MVN) **CLEAR C and V flags**.
- ☐ **MUL/MLA** leaves C and V as **undefined** in ARM7.
- ☐ The **Q flag (bit 27)** is sticky (can only be cleared by MSR).
- ☐ The **J bit (bit 24)** enables Jazelle (Java bytecode execution) — not supported in LPC2148.
- ☐ `MRS` reads CPSR/SPSR; `MSR` writes to CPSR/SPSR (privileged modes only for control fields).
- ☐ **KTU exam trick:** Always compute the V flag using the formula, not by guessing — it is a frequent 7-mark question.
- ☐ Remember: **CPSR is the ARM's "dashboard"** — condition flags = speedometer/fuel; I/F = fog lights; M bits = gear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
