topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipeline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in a microprocessor/microcontroller where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution. A pipeline is a set of data processing elements (stages) connected in series, where the output of one stage is the input of the next. The goal is to increase the instruction throughput (number of instructions completed per unit time) without reducing the individual instruction execution time.

In the context of **ARM Cortex-M** and **Advanced RISC Machine (ARM)** architectures (commonly covered under OECIT615), pipelining is a fundamental design strategy where instruction processing is decomposed into discrete stages such that while one instruction is being executed, the next instruction is being decoded, and a third is being fetched.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Laundry / Assembly-Line Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Laundry Assembly Line**
> Imagine doing laundry with 4 steps for each load:
> 1. **Wash** (30 min)
> 2. **Dry** (40 min)
> 3. **Fold** (20 min)
> 4. **Store** (10 min)
>
> **Without Pipelining (Sequential):**
> One full load takes $30+40+20+10 = 100$ minutes. Doing 4 loads takes $4 \times 100 = 400$ minutes.
>
> **With Pipelining (Overlapped):**
> You start Load 2 in the *Wash* stage while Load 1 is in *Dry*, and so on. The first load still takes 100 minutes, but subsequent loads finish every ~30 minutes (the longest stage). Total time for 4 loads = $100 + 3 \times 30 = 190$ minutes.
>
> **Lesson:** Pipelining does not make a single instruction faster — it makes the **system's throughput** much higher.

## 1.3 Why Pipelining Matters in Microprocessors/Microcontrollers

- **Performance Metric (KTU High-Yield):** Reduces the **Average Instruction Execution Time** measured in cycles per instruction (CPI).
- **Modern MCU Relevance:** The **ARM Cortex-M0** uses a 3-stage pipeline, **Cortex-M3/M4** uses a 3-stage pipeline, while **ARM7TDMI** uses a 3-stage pipeline and **ARM9** uses a 5-stage pipeline.
- **Frequency Independence:** The clock frequency is set by the **slowest pipeline stage**, not the entire instruction.
- **Standard Boundary Condition:** A perfectly balanced *k*-stage pipeline processes *n* instructions in $k + (n-1)$ clock cycles.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "What is pipelining?", always mention — *instruction-level parallelism achieved by overlapping fetch, decode, and execute phases*.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of a Generic Pipeline

A pipeline of depth *k* divides instruction processing into *k* sequential stages. For a **5-stage classical RISC pipeline**, the stages are:

1. **IF – Instruction Fetch:** Address is sent to memory, instruction word is retrieved.
2. **ID – Instruction Decode:** Opcode and operands are decoded; registers are read.
3. **EX – Execute:** ALU performs arithmetic/logic; effective address is computed.
4. **MEM – Memory Access:** Data memory is read or written (for Load/Store).
5. **WB – Write Back:** Result is written back to the register file.

### 2.2 Pipeline Performance Equations (The 'Why' Behind Each)

Let $T$ = clock cycle time, $k$ = number of stages, $n$ = number of instructions.

**Why these formulas matter:** KTU frequently tests the ability to compare a pipelined vs non-pipelined design.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-pipelined execution time | $T_{np} = n \cdot k \cdot T$ | Each of $n$ instructions takes $k$ stages × cycle time $T$ | seconds |
| Pipelined execution time | $T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$ | First instruction fills the pipe, rest emerge every cycle | seconds |
| Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | Ratio of sequential to pipelined time | dimensionless |
| Maximum Theoretical Speedup | $S_{max} = k$ | Achieved as $n \to \infty$ | dimensionless |
| Pipeline Throughput | $\tau = \dfrac{n}{T_{p}} = \dfrac{n}{(k+n-1)T}$ | Instructions completed per second | instr/s |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Fraction of stage utilization | dimensionless |
| Average CPI (ideal) | $CPI_{ideal} = 1$ | One instruction completes per cycle after fill | cycles/instr |
| Average CPI (with stalls) | $CPI_{actual} = 1 + \text{Hazard Penalty}$ | Increased by bubbles/stalls | cycles/instr |
| Cycle time (balanced) | $T = \max(T_{stage\_i})$ | Set by the slowest stage | seconds |
| Cycle time (unbalanced) | $T = \max(T_{stage\_i}) + d_{register}$ | Includes inter-stage register delay | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` inside any markdown table to avoid breaking the table renderer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (System on Chip):** Apple's M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, and MediaTek Dimensity chips all use **8 to 14-stage deep pipelines** to reach 3+ GHz clock speeds in power-constrained environments.
- **Automotive MCUs:** ARM Cortex-R52 used in autonomous driving ECUs uses pipelining for deterministic real-time response.
- **IoT Microcontrollers:** Even tiny Cortex-M0+ MCUs use pipelining to maintain energy efficiency while delivering ~0.9 DMIPS/MHz.

## 2.4 Pipeline Hazards – The "Why" Behind Performance Loss

A **hazard** is a condition that prevents the next instruction in the pipeline from executing in its designated clock cycle. The three classical types:

1. **Structural Hazards** – Hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port accessed by IF and MEM in the same cycle).
2. **Data Hazards** – An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline (RAW, WAR, WAW).
3. **Control Hazards** – Caused by branch and jump instructions; the next PC is unknown until EX stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating WAR and WAW hazards — they cannot occur in a classic in-order 5-stage RISC pipeline; they only appear in **out-of-order execution** or with **register renaming**.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speedup of an Ideal Pipeline

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Proof

**Given:**
- A non-pipelined processor takes $k \cdot T$ time per instruction (because all $k$ stages run sequentially for one instruction).
- A pipelined processor has cycle time $T$ (set by the slowest stage), and the first instruction takes $k \cdot T$ to emerge.

**Step 1: Non-pipelined total time for $n$ instructions**
$$T_{np} = n \times (k \cdot T)$$

Each of the $n$ instructions is processed one after another. There is no overlap. Every instruction uses the same hardware resources in the same order for $k$ cycles each. So the total time scales linearly with $n$, and the proportionality constant is the per-instruction time $k \cdot T$.

**Step 2: Pipelined total time for $n$ instructions**
$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$$

The first instruction requires $k$ full clock cycles to traverse all stages — this is the pipeline *fill* time. After that, every additional cycle completes one more instruction. So for $n$ instructions, the total cycles equal the $k$ fill cycles plus the $(n-1)$ cycles in which the remaining instructions emerge at the rate of one per cycle.

**Step 3: Speedup definition**
$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}}$$

By definition, speedup is the ratio of original execution time to the optimized execution time. This metric is always $\geq 1$ for a correctly designed pipeline.

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify**
$$S = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T} = \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$$

The $T$ cancels because both architectures use the same clock period $T$. This leaves a pure ratio in terms of the pipeline depth $k$ and instruction count $n$.

**Step 5: Take the limit as $n \to \infty$**
$$S_{max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{\frac{k}{n} + 1 - \frac{1}{n}} = \frac{k}{0 + 1 - 0} = k$$

As $n$ grows large, the constants $k$ and $1$ in the denominator become negligible compared to $n$, so the expression approaches $k$. This proves that **the maximum achievable speedup equals the number of pipeline stages**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A processor has a 4-stage pipeline. Stage delays are: IF = 50 ns, ID = 40 ns, EX = 60 ns, WB = 30 ns. Compute: (a) Clock cycle time, (b) Time to execute 1000 instructions, (c) Speedup over non-pipelined execution, (d) Efficiency.

### Part (a): Clock Cycle Time

$$T_{clk} = \max(50, 40, 60, 30) + d_{reg} = 60 \text{ ns}$$

The slowest stage is EX at 60 ns, which dictates the clock period. Ignoring the inter-stage register delay, the clock cycle time is 60 ns.

### Part (b): Pipelined Execution Time for $n = 1000$

$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clk} = (4 + 1000 - 1) \cdot 60 = 1003 \times 60 = 60{,}180 \text{ ns}$$

Apply the standard pipelined time formula. The pipeline needs 4 cycles to fill, then 999 more cycles to flush out the remaining 999 instructions.

### Part (c): Non-pipelined Time and Speedup

$$T_{np} = n \cdot (T_{IF} + T_{ID} + T_{EX} + T_{WB}) = 1000 \times (50+40+60+30) = 1000 \times 180 = 180{,}000 \text{ ns}$$

$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{180{,}000}{60{,}180} \approx 2.991$$

The non-pipelined processor executes one instruction at a time using the sum of all stage delays. Dividing the two execution times gives the speedup factor.

### Part (d): Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{2.991}{4} = 0.7478 = 74.78\%$$

Efficiency is defined as the achieved speedup divided by the theoretical maximum speedup (which equals $k$). This indicates that the pipeline is utilized 74.78% of the time.

## 3.3 Data Hazard Analysis – Worked Example

Consider this instruction sequence:
```
ADD  R1, R2, R3    ; R1 = R2 + R3
SUB  R4, R1, R5    ; R4 = R1 - R5   (depends on R1)
```

In a 5-stage pipeline, `ADD` writes R1 in **WB (cycle 5)**, but `SUB` reads R1 in **ID (cycle 3)**. This is a **Read-After-Write (RAW)** hazard.

**Solution: Operand Forwarding (Bypassing)**
- Route the ALU result from EX/MEM pipeline register directly to the ALU input for the next instruction.
- `SUB` can now get R1 from cycle 3 → **no stall required**.

**Cost of no forwarding:** Insert 2 stall (bubble) cycles → CPI increases from 1 to 3 for this dependent pair.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** Number of stalls without forwarding = *distance between producing and consuming stage* − 1.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Hazard Resolution Techniques

| **Hazard Type** | **Cause** | **Hardware Solution** | **Software Solution** | **Performance Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Structural | Resource conflict | Duplicate functional units (Harvard) | None | 0–1 stall/cycle |
| Data (RAW) | Read-after-write | Forwarding, register file write-first | Instruction scheduling by compiler | 0–2 stalls |
| Data (WAR) | Write-after-read | Register renaming (OoO) | Reorder instructions | OoO complexity |
| Data (WAW) | Write-after-write | Register renaming (OoO) | Reorder instructions | OoO complexity |
| Control | Branch/jump delay | Branch prediction, delay slot | Loop unrolling, profile-guided | 0–3 stalls |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – 5-Stage Pipeline Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IF Stage Instruction Fetch"] --> B["ID Stage Instruction Decode Register Read"]
    B --> C["EX Stage ALU Operation Address Calc"]
    C --> D["MEM Stage Data Memory Access"]
    D --> E["WB Stage Write Back to Register File"]
    E -.-> A
```

*Figure 1: Linear 5-stage pipeline showing sequential data flow from Instruction Fetch (IF) to Write Back (WB), with feedback loop indicating continuous instruction stream.*

## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (Mermaid Gantt)

```mermaid
gantt
    title 5 Stage Pipeline Timing for 4 Instructions
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Instr 1
    IF    :i1if, 0, 1
    ID    :i1id, 1, 1
    EX    :i1ex, 2, 1
    MEM   :i1mem, 3, 1
    WB    :i1wb, 4, 1
    section Instr 2
    IF    :i2if, 1, 1
    ID    :i2id, 2, 1
    EX    :i2ex, 3, 1
    MEM   :i2mem, 4, 1
    WB    :i2wb, 5, 1
    section Instr 3
    IF    :i3if, 2, 1
    ID    :i3id, 3, 1
    EX    :i3ex, 4, 1
    MEM   :i3mem, 5, 1
    WB    :i3wb, 6, 1
    section Instr 4
    IF    :i4if, 3, 1
    ID    :i4id, 4, 1
    EX    :i4ex, 5, 1
    MEM   :i4mem, 6, 1
    WB    :i4wb, 7, 1
```

*Figure 2: Gantt-style timing diagram showing how 4 instructions overlap in a 5-stage pipeline. Note the staircase pattern where each stage is busy in every cycle after the initial fill (cycles 1-4). Total cycles = 5 + 4 − 1 = 8.*

## 4.3 Hazard Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H["Pipeline Hazards"] --> S["Structural Hazard Hardware Conflict"]
    H --> D["Data Hazard Data Dependency"]
    H --> C["Control Hazard Branch Jump"]
    D --> D1["RAW Read After Write True Dependency"]
    D --> D2["WAR Write After Read Anti Dependency"]
    D --> D3["WAW Write After Write Output Dependency"]
    S --> S1["Single Memory Port Shared Resource"]
    C --> C1["Conditional Branch Delay Slot"]
    C --> C2["Unconditional Jump Target Unknown"]
```

*Figure 3: Hierarchical classification of pipeline hazards. RAW is the most common and must be resolved in any pipelined RISC design.*

## 4.4 Pipeline Performance Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NP["Non Pipelined Processor"]
        NP1["Instruction 1: k cycles"] --> NP2["Instruction 2: k cycles"]
        NP2 --> NP3["Instruction 3: k cycles"]
        NP3 --> NP4["Instruction n: k cycles"]
    end
    subgraph P["Pipelined Processor"]
        P1["Fill: k cycles for first instruction"] --> P2["Steady State: 1 instruction per cycle"]

    end
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define pipelining. State any two advantages of pipelining in a microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Pipelining is a technique of decomposing the instruction execution process into a sequence of independent sub-operations (stages), each of which is performed by dedicated hardware, and overlapping the execution of multiple instructions across these stages. It is a form of instruction-level parallelism that increases the instruction throughput.
- **Advantage 1 (0.5 marks):** Increases instruction throughput — multiple instructions execute simultaneously in different stages.
- **Advantage 2 (0.5 marks):** Improves overall processor performance without increasing the clock frequency (only the slowest stage sets $T_{clk}$).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S2 B.Tech]**
**List and briefly explain the three types of pipeline hazards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Structural Hazard (1 mark):** Occurs when two pipeline stages need the same hardware resource at the same time. Example: A single memory port accessed simultaneously by IF and MEM stages.
2. **Data Hazard (1 mark):** Occurs when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that is still in the pipeline. Example: A RAW dependency where the producer has not yet written back to the register file.
3. **Control Hazard (1 mark):** Caused by branch and jump instructions because the next PC is not known until the branch target is computed in EX stage, leading to instruction fetch of wrong-path instructions.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the 5-stage pipeline of a RISC processor with a neat block diagram. List the function of each stage.**

**Model Answer:**

The 5-stage pipeline is a classical RISC design (used in MIPS, ARM9, SPARC) that breaks instruction execution into five sequential stages:

**1. IF – Instruction Fetch (1.5 marks)**
- The Program Counter (PC) value is sent to the instruction memory.
- The instruction at that address is fetched.
- Simultaneously, PC is incremented by 4 (or 2 in Thumb mode) to point to the next instruction.
- Output is latched into the **IF/ID pipeline register** at the end of the cycle.

**2. ID – Instruction Decode and Register Fetch (1.5 marks)**
- The fetched instruction is decoded by the control unit to determine the operation.
- Source register operands ($R_s$, $R_t$) are read from the register file.
- Sign-extension is performed for immediate values.
- The decoded signals and register values are stored in the **ID/EX pipeline register**.

**3. EX – Execute / Address Calculation (1.5 marks)**
- The ALU performs the arithmetic/logic operation (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.).
- For Load/Store instructions, the ALU computes the effective memory address by adding the base register and sign-extended offset.
- Branch instructions also evaluate the branch condition and compute the target address.
- Result is stored in the **EX/MEM pipeline register**.

**4. MEM – Memory Access (1.5 marks)**
- For Load instructions: data is read from data memory at the address computed in EX.
- For Store instructions: data from a register is written to data memory.
- For ALU and branch instructions, this stage is a no-op (passes through).
- Output latched into the **MEM/WB pipeline register**.

**5. WB – Write Back (1 mark)**
- For Load and ALU instructions, the result is written back to the destination register in the register file.
- For Store and Branch instructions, no write-back occurs.
- The register file is updated at the end of this stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Reference:** See Figure 1 (SECTION 4.1) for the linear flow of the 5 stages.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A processor uses a 5-stage pipeline with stage delays of 30 ns, 25 ns, 40 ns, 35 ns, and 20 ns respectively. Compute the clock cycle time, total time to execute 500 instructions with and without pipelining, and the speedup achieved.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Determine clock cycle time (1 mark)**
$$T_{clk} = \max(30, 25, 40, 35, 20) = 40 \text{ ns}$$

The clock period equals the longest stage delay (EX stage at 40 ns).

**Step 2: Pipelined execution time (2 marks)**
$$T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_{clk} = (5 + 500 - 1) \cdot 40 = 504 \times 40 = 20{,}160 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Non-pipelined execution time (2 marks)**
$$T_{np} = n \cdot (T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5) = 500 \times (30+25+40+35+20) = 500 \times 150 = 75{,}000 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4: Speedup calculation (1 mark)**
$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{75{,}000}{20{,}160} \approx 3.72$$

**Step 5: Efficiency (1 mark)**
$$\eta = \frac{S}{k} = \frac{3.72}{5} = 0.744 = 74.4\%$$

The pipeline achieves 3.72× speedup, which is less than the theoretical maximum of 5 due to the small instruction count.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of data hazards in a pipelined processor. How are they resolved using forwarding (data forwarding / bypassing)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Data Hazards (4 marks):**

1. **RAW – Read After Write (True Dependency) (1.5 marks):**
   - Instruction $j$ tries to read a source operand before instruction $i$ writes it.
   - This is the most common hazard in any in-order pipeline.
   - Example:
     ```
     ADD R1, R2, R3   ; produces R1
     SUB R4, R1, R5   ; consumes R1 (RAW on R1)
     ```

2. **WAR – Write After Read (Anti-Dependency) (1 mark):**
   - Instruction $j$ writes to a destination before instruction $i$ reads it.
   - Does NOT occur in classic in-order 5-stage pipeline because reads happen in ID (cycle 2) and writes happen in WB (cycle 5).
   - Only appears in **out-of-order execution** engines.

3. **WAW – Write After Write (Output Dependency) (1.5 marks):**
   - Two instructions write to the same destination.
   - Must preserve order of writes to maintain program correctness.
   - Does NOT occur in classic 5-stage pipeline (only one WB per cycle) but appears in pipelines with multiple write-back stages or in VLIW architectures.

**Resolution by Forwarding / Bypassing (3 marks):**

- **Concept:** Instead of waiting for the result to be written back to the register file, route the computed value directly from an intermediate pipeline register to the ALU input of the consuming instruction.
- **Mechanism:**
  - Add multiplexers at the ALU inputs.
  - The new ALU input can come from:
    - Register file (normal case, **RF**)
    - Output of EX/MEM pipeline register (for 1-cycle delay, **EX/MEM**)
    - Output of MEM/WB pipeline register (for 2-cycle delay, **MEM/WB**)
- **Effect:** Eliminates stalls for the typical RAW hazard when the producing instruction is at most 2 cycles ahead of the consuming instruction.
- **Example:** `ADD R1, R2, R3` → `SUB R4, R1, R5` requires the value of R1 from the EX stage of ADD. Forward it via EX/MEM register directly to the second ALU input port of SUB in its EX stage.
- **Limitation:** Cannot resolve the **Load-Use Hazard** (when a Load is immediately followed by a use of the loaded register). This requires **1 stall cycle** even with forwarding.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain control hazards in pipelining. Discuss branch prediction techniques used to reduce the penalty.**

**Model Answer:**

**Control Hazard Basics (2 marks):**
- Caused by **branch** (BEQ, BNE, B) and **jump** (B, BL) instructions.
- The branch target address is computed in the EX stage (cycle 3 of a 5-stage pipeline).
- During cycles 1 and 2, the pipeline keeps fetching the fall-through (sequential) instructions.
- If the branch is taken, these 2 fetched instructions must be **flushed (squashed)**, causing a **branch penalty of 2 cycles**.

**Branch Penalty Quantification (1.5 marks):**
$$CPI_{avg} = 1 + (\text{branch frequency}) \times (\text{branch penalty}) \times (\text{misprediction rate})$$

If 20% of instructions are branches and penalty is 2 cycles with a 50% misprediction rate:
$$CPI = 1 + 0.20 \times 2 \times 0.50 = 1.2$$

**Branch Prediction Techniques (3.5 marks):**

1. **Static Always-Not-Taken (0.5 mark):**
   - Predicts every branch as not-taken.
   - Simple, requires no hardware.
   - Failure rate equals the actual taken-branch rate.

2. **Static Always-Taken (0.5 mark):**
   - Predicts every branch as taken.
   - Better for loop-heavy code (loops are taken repeatedly).

3. **Dynamic 1-Bit Branch Predictor (1 mark):**
   - Maintains a **Branch History Table (BHT)** indexed by lower bits of branch PC.
   - Each BHT entry is 1 bit: predicts "taken" if the branch was last taken, else "not-taken".
   - On misprediction, flips the bit.
   - **Weakness:** Alternating branch patterns (TNTNTN) cause 100% misprediction.

4. **Dynamic 2-Bit Saturating Counter (1 mark):**
   - Uses 2 bits per BHT entry, transitioning through 4 states: Strongly Not Taken → Weakly Not Taken → Weakly Taken → Strongly Taken.
   - Predicts taken only if counter $\geq 2$.
   - **Tolerance:** Requires two consecutive mispredictions to flip prediction → resilient to occasional alternation.
   - Used in classic 5-stage MIPS pipeline (BTB with 2-bit saturating counters).

5. **Branch Target Buffer (BTB) (0.5 mark):**
   - Caches the target address of recently taken branches.
   - Used together with BHT for faster target fetch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that the **branch penalty in a 5-stage pipeline = 2 cycles** (not 1). If the branch is resolved in the MEM stage, the penalty is 3 cycles. Always specify which stage performs the branch resolution.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision – Pipeline Module**

- **Definition:** Pipelining is overlapping instruction execution through multiple stages to improve *throughput* (not single-instruction latency).
- **5 Classical Stages:** **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB** (RISC pipeline).
- **ARM Pipeline Depths:** ARM7TDMI = 3-stage; ARM9 = 5-stage; Cortex-A8 = 13-stage; Cortex-M3 = 3-stage.
- **Master Formula Set:**
  - $T_{p} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T$
  - $T_{np} = n \cdot k \cdot T$
  - $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$, with $S_{max} = k$ as $n \to \infty$.
  - $\eta = S / k$
- **Three Hazard Types:** Structural, Data (RAW, WAR, WAW), Control.
- **RAW is the dominant hazard** in classic in-order pipelines.
- **Forwarding (Bypassing):** Routes data from EX/MEM or MEM/WB pipeline registers directly to ALU inputs → eliminates most RAW stalls.
- **Load-Use Hazard:** Cannot be fixed by forwarding alone; requires **1 stall bubble**.
- **Branch Penalty:** 2 cycles in a standard 5-stage pipeline; reduced by static/dynamic prediction.
- **2-Bit Saturating Counter:** Industry standard for dynamic branch prediction; requires 2 consecutive wrong outcomes to change prediction.
- **CPI Limits:** $CPI_{ideal} = 1$; $CPI_{actual} = 1 + \text{stalls per instruction}$.
- **Speedup Limit:** $S \le k$; the deeper the pipeline, the higher the theoretical speedup, but higher hazard cost.
- **Pipeline Fill & Drain:** First instruction takes $k$ cycles; last instruction takes $k$ cycles to drain.
- **Efficiency Rule:** Efficiency $\to 100\%$ as $n \to \infty$ (only for balanced, hazard-free pipelines).
- **KTU High-Yield Keywords:** *Instruction-level parallelism, throughput, latency, bubble, stall, flush, forwarding path, branch history table, pipeline register, inter-stage latch.*
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing $T_{np}$ formula. Sequential execution time = $n \times (\text{sum of stage delays})$, NOT $n \times T_{clk}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Numerical Problems:** Always identify (1) $k$ (number of stages), (2) $T$ (clock period = max stage delay), (3) $n$ (instruction count), then plug into the master formulas. Show intermediate steps for full marks.

**End of Module 4 – Pipeline Notes**

topics.title: Exceptions and Interrupts, Interrupt Vector Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Exceptions and Interrupts, Interrupt Vector Table # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interrupt (KTU Definition):** An *interrupt* is an external or internal asynchronous event that suspends the normal sequential execution of the microprocessor, forcing the CPU to transfer control to a predefined routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes the original task using the saved state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exception (KTU Definition):** An *exception* is a special category of interrupt that is **synchronously** generated by the CPU itself in response to an error condition detected during instruction execution (e.g., divide-by-zero, overflow, page fault in protected mode). It is not caused by an external hardware signal. In the **Intel 8086 architecture**, the unified term *interrupt* is used to cover both true interrupts and exceptions. The 8086 reserves **256 interrupt vectors** (Type 0 through Type 255), managed through the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell & Smoke Alarm" Model Imagine you are a student writing an exam (the CPU executing instructions). | Event | Real-World Analogy | Microprocessor Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A friend knocks on the door | Polite external request | **Hardware Interrupt (INTR)** — maskable | | A fire alarm blares | Urgent external danger | **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI / Type 2)** | | You accidentally try to divide by zero on the calculator | Self-detected error | **Exception (Type 0 — Divide Error)** | | You press a preset alarm button | Programmer-triggered event | **Software Interrupt (INT n)** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuition:** The microprocessor does not *poll* (continuously check) for every possible event. Instead, it works on the principle of **"Do your main job. If someone demands attention, I'll handle it, then return to you."** This is the essence of **event-driven computing**. --- ## 1.3 Classification Overview The 8086 categorizes 256 interrupts into three broad families: 1. **Hardware Interrupts** — generated by physical pins (NMI, INTR). 2. **Software Interrupts** — generated by the `INT n` instruction. 3. **Exceptions / Internal Interrupts** — generated by the CPU on error conditions. The total count of pre-defined dedicated interrupt types in the 8086 is **5** (Type 0 to Type 4). The remaining 251 vectors (Type 5–255) are user-available or reserved. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bold Metric:** The 8086 Interrupt Vector Table occupies exactly **1 KB (1024 bytes = 400H bytes)** of physical memory, starting at absolute address **00000H** and ending at **003FFH**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 8086 Interrupt Architecture — Logic Breakdown The 8086 interrupt mechanism is governed by **two critical flags** in the **Flags/Status Register**: - **IF (Interrupt Flag) — Bit 9:** `IF = 1` enables maskable interrupts on the INTR pin; `IF = 0` masks (blocks) them. Cleared/set by `CLI` and `STI`. - **TF (Trap Flag) — Bit 8:** `TF = 1` puts the CPU in single-step mode (Type 1 interrupt after every instruction). ### 2.1.1 Hardware Interrupts **A. Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) — Type 2** - **Edge-triggered** on the rising edge of the NMI pin. - **Cannot be masked** by the `IF` flag. - **Cannot be disabled** by software under normal operation. - Used for catastrophic events: power failure, memory parity error, bus parity error. - Vector address is **hard-wired** to physical location `00008H–0000BH` in the IVT. **B. Maskable Interrupt (INTR) — No Fixed Type** - **Level-triggered** (active HIGH) on the INTR pin. - The CPU checks INTR only when `IF = 1`. - Acknowledgement uses two interrupt acknowledge bus cycles, fetching a **1-byte interrupt type (0–255)** from the external interrupt controller (e.g., **8259A PIC**). - This is the most flexible hardware interrupt in the 8086. ### 2.1.2 Software Interrupts - `INT n` instruction (where `n` is the type number 0–255). - The opcode `0xCD` is followed by the type byte. - The CPU computes the vector address as `n × 4` and fetches `CS:IP` from the IVT. - `INT 3` (1-byte instruction, opcode `0xCC`) is a special **breakpoint interrupt** for debuggers. ### 2.1.3 Exceptions (Internal Interrupts — Dedicated Types) | Type | Name | Triggering Condition | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 0 | **Divide Error** | Quotient too large for destination (divide by zero, or overflow in `DIV`/`IDIV`) | | 1 | **Single Step** | `TF = 1`; raised after every instruction for debugging | | 2 | **NMI** | Rising edge on NMI pin (hardware) | | 3 | **Breakpoint** | Execution of `INT 3` (1-byte opcode `0xCC`) | | 4 | **Overflow (`INTO`)** | `OF = 1` and `INTO` instruction is executed | > [!NOTE] > Types 5 through 31 are **reserved by Intel** for future processors (286, 386, etc.). Types 32 through 255 are available for user-defined hardware and software interrupts. --- ## 2.2 The Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) — Structure Deep Dive > [!IMPORTANT] > The IVT is a **lookup table** that maps each interrupt type number to the address (CS:IP) of its corresponding ISR. It is the heart of the 8086 interrupt mechanism. ### 2.2.1 Memory Layout - **Location:** `00000H` to `003FFH` (absolute physical addresses). - **Size:** 256 entries × 4 bytes = **1024 bytes = 400H bytes**. - **Pointer Formula:** For any interrupt type **N**, the 4-byte entry is stored at: - **IP (lower 16 bits)** → physical address `4N` and `4N+1` - **CS (upper 16 bits)** → physical address `4N+2` and `4N+3` ### 2.2.2 Why exactly 4 bytes per entry? The 8086 has a **20-bit physical address** formed by: $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}$$ To form a complete CS:IP pointer, the IVT stores both 16-bit registers (CS and IP), totaling **4 bytes (2 + 2)** per interrupt. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Number of interrupt types | $2^{8} = 256$ | types | 8-bit type field | | IVT total size | $256 \times 4 = 1024$ | bytes | Equals 400H bytes | | IVT start address | `00000H` | physical | Hard-wired in 8086 | | IVT end address | `003FFH` | physical | Start + Size − 1 | | IP offset address | $4N$ | physical | N = type number | | CS segment address | $4N + 2$ | physical | N = type number | | ISR physical address | $(CS \times 10H) + IP$ | physical | Standard 8086 addressing | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This exact IVT scheme is the foundation of **BIOS interrupt calls** (e.g., `INT 10H` for video, `INT 13H` for disk, `INT 21H` for DOS services). Every legacy operating system and bootloader exploits this table. --- ## 2.4 The 8086 Interrupt Response Sequence — Step by Step When an interrupt is recognized, the 8086 performs the following hardware sequence: 1. Decrement SP by 2, push **Flags Register (FR)** onto the stack. 2. Clear **IF** and **TF** flags (to prevent nested interrupts and single-stepping inside the ISR). 3. Decrement SP by 2, push **CS** onto the stack. 4. Decrement SP by 2, push **IP** onto the stack. 5. Fetch the new IP from memory location `4 × N` and `4 × N + 1`. 6. Fetch the new CS from memory location `4 × N + 2` and `4 × N + 3`. 7. Begin execution of the ISR at the new CS:IP. > [!NOTE] > The reverse process — **IRET (Interrupt Return)** — pops IP, then CS, then Flags, and resumes the suspended program exactly where it left off. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing the IVT Physical Address for a Given Type **Problem:** Find the physical addresses at which the IP and CS of the ISR for **Type 1AH** are stored in the IVT. **Step 1 — Identify the type number N.** $$N = 1\text{AH} = 26 \text{ (decimal)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the base formula for the IP offset.** $$\text{Address of IP} = 4 \times N = 4 \times 26$$ $$\text{Address of IP} = 104_{10}$$ **Step 3 — Convert decimal 104 to hexadecimal.** $$104 \div 16 = 6 \text{ remainder } 8$$ $$\text{IP is stored at } 00068\text{H (low byte) and } 00069\text{H (high byte)}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the CS storage address.** $$\text{Address of CS} = 4N + 2 = 104 + 2 = 106_{10}$$ **Step 5 — Convert decimal 106 to hexadecimal.** $$106 \div 16 = 6 \text{ remainder } 10 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 6\text{AH}$$ $$\text{CS is stored at } 0006\text{AH (low byte) and } 0006\text{BH (high byte)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result for Type 1AH:** IP is at `00068H–00069H` and CS is at `0006AH–0006BH`. The 4-byte entry occupies `00068H` through `0006BH`. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Verifying the Total IVT Size **Problem:** Prove that the IVT occupies exactly 1 KB of physical memory. **Step 1 — Number of interrupt types supported by 8086.** $$N_{types} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ types (Type 00H to Type FFH)}$$ **Step 2 — Bytes per entry (CS:IP pair).** $$\text{Bytes per entry} = \text{IP} + \text{CS} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3 — Total IVT size in bytes.** $$\text{Size} = 256 \times 4 = 1024 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to hexadecimal.** $$1024 = 16^{2} \times 4 = 400\text{H bytes} = 1 \text{ KB}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the end address.** $$\text{End Address} = 00000\text{H} + 400\text{H} - 1 = 003\text{FFH}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{IVT Range} &= [00000\text{H},\; 003\text{FFH}] \\ &= 1024 \text{ contiguous bytes} \\ &= 1 \text{ KB} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Priority Resolution in 8086 **Problem:** List the 8086 interrupt types in their order of priority (highest to lowest). **Step 1 — Recall the architectural rule.** The 8086 priority is **fixed in hardware** and is NOT user-programmable. Lower type numbers generally have higher priority. **Step 2 — Apply the official Intel priority order.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Priority 1 (Highest)} &: \text{Divide Error (Type 0)} \\ \text{Priority 2} &: \text{INT n (Software)} \\ \text{Priority 3} &: \text{INTO (Type 4) — overflow trap} \\ \text{Priority 4} &: \text{NMI (Type 2)} \\ \text{Priority 5 (Lowest)} &: \text{INTR (Maskable)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Single Step (Type 1)** and **Breakpoint (Type 3)** are debug exceptions and are typically considered alongside software interrupts in exam contexts. The above is the canonical Intel-defined sequence. **Step 3 — Conclude with rationale.** Divide error has the highest priority because it indicates immediate data corruption. Software interrupts (`INT n`) are next, since the CPU is already executing the offending instruction. NMI follows, and INTR is lowest because it is sampled only between instructions. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flag Behavior During Interrupt Processing | Action / Phase | IF (Bit 9) | TF (Bit 8) | Reason | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Before interrupt, `STI` executed | 1 | x | User enables maskable IRQs | | Before interrupt, `CLI` executed | 0 | x | User masks out INTR | | CPU begins interrupt response | 0 | 0 | Hardware auto-clears to prevent nested IRQs and step traps | | Inside ISR | 0 | 0 | Default state; ISR may re-enable if designed | | `IRET` pops original flags | Restored | Restored | Original IF and TF values are reinstated | > [!IMPORTANT] > This auto-clearing of IF and TF is a **defining characteristic** of the 8086 interrupt mechanism and is a **frequent KTU question** on the topic of flag handling. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8086 Interrupt Source Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef external fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef internal fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cpu fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef ivt fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef isr fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 NMI["NMI Pin (Type 2)"]:::external INTR["INTR Pin + 8259A PIC"]:::external INTn["INT n Instruction (User Code)"]:::internal INT3["INT 3 Breakpoint"]:::internal INTO["INTO Overflow Trap"]:::internal DivErr["Divide Error (Type 0)"]:::internal SS["Single Step Trap (Type 1)"]:::internal CPU["8086 CPU Interrupt Logic Unit"]:::cpu IVT["Interrupt Vector Table 00000H-003FFH"]:::ivt ISR["Interrupt Service Routines (ISR) in Memory"]:::isr NMI --> CPU INTR --> CPU INTn --> CPU INT3 --> CPU INTO --> CPU DivErr --> CPU SS --> CPU CPU -- "Compute 4N, Fetch CS:IP" --> IVT IVT -- "Loads CS:IP" --> CPU CPU -- "Executes ISR" --> ISR ISR -- "IRET returns" --> CPU ``` --- ## 4.2 IVT Memory Layout — Type-by-Type Map ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef reserved fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef defined fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef user fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 T0["Type 0 (00000H): Divide Error"]:::defined T1["Type 1 (00004H): Single Step"]:::defined T2["Type 2 (00008H): NMI"]:::defined T3["Type 3 (0000CH): Breakpoint"]:::defined T4["Type 4 (00010H): INTO Overflow"]:::defined T5["Type 5-31 (00014H-0007FH): Reserved by Intel"]:::reserved T32["Type 32-255 (00080H-003FFH): User / DOS / BIOS"]:::user T0 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T32 ``` --- ## 4.3 Interrupt Response Sequence — Flow of Execution ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Program participant C as 8086 CPU participant S as Stack participant I as IVT (00000H-003FFH) participant R as ISR U->>C: Execute instruction Note over C: Interrupt detected
(NMI, INTR, INT n, Exception) C->>S: PUSH Flags (SP = SP - 2) C->>C: Clear IF and TF C->>S: PUSH CS (SP = SP - 2) C->>S: PUSH IP (SP = SP - 2) C->>I: Fetch IP from [4N, 4N+1] C->>I: Fetch CS from [4N+2, 4N+3] C->>R: Jump to ISR at new CS:IP R->>R: Execute ISR body R->>S: IRET: POP IP, POP CS, POP Flags S-->>C: Restore original state C->>U: Resume execution at saved IP ``` --- ## 4.4 Hardware Interrupt Acknowledgement — INTR Bus Cycles ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef cpu fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pic fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bus fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 CPU["8086 CPU"]:::cpu BUS["System Data Bus (8-bit)"]:::bus PIC["8259A PIC"]:::pic CPU -- "INTA cycle 1 (Read)" --> BUS BUS -- "Type byte requested" --> PIC PIC -- "Sends type number N" --> BUS BUS -- "Type N received" --> CPU CPU -- "Fetches CS:IP from 4N" --> BUS ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define an interrupt. Differentiate between a hardware interrupt and a software interrupt in the 8086. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that suspends the current execution of the CPU and forces it to execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, after which normal execution resumes. **Hardware vs Software Interrupt:** | Parameter | Hardware Interrupt | Software Interrupt | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Source | External pin (NMI / INTR) | `INT n` instruction in code | | Trigger | Electrical signal | Programmer-issued instruction | | Type number | Fixed (NMI=2) or from 8259A | Embedded in instruction opcode | | Maskable | NMI: No; INTR: Yes (via `IF`) | No — not affected by `IF` | | Synchronous? | Asynchronous | Synchronous with instruction stream | --- ### Question 2: What is the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) in the 8086? State its size and starting address. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a 1 KB lookup table in physical memory that stores the **CS:IP** (4-byte entry) of the ISR for each of the 256 possible interrupt types (0–255). - **Starting address:** `00000H` - **Ending address:** `003FFH` - **Total size:** 256 × 4 = **1024 bytes = 400H bytes = 1 KB** For any interrupt type **N**, the IP is stored at `4N` and CS at `4N + 2`. --- ## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > 1. **Forgetting the `4N` formula** is the #1 mark-losing mistake. Always state the formula before computing. > 2. **Mixing up IP and CS storage offsets** (IP is at `4N`, CS is at `4N+2`) costs 2 marks easily. > 3. **Ignoring the auto-clearing of IF and TF** during interrupt response is a common omission. Examiners explicitly test this in 3–4 mark sub-questions. > 4. **Confusing NMI priority with INTR** — NMI is higher priority than INTR; INTR is the lowest in the 8086 scheme. > 5. **Forgetting the `IRET` instruction** when describing return from ISR is a silent mark-killer. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** of the 8086 microprocessor. List the first five dedicated interrupt types along with their vector addresses. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **complete interrupt response sequence** of the 8086. State why the **IF** and **TF** flags are cleared automatically. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** The IVT is a 1 KB region of physical memory from `00000H` to `003FFH` containing 256 entries, each of 4 bytes (CS:IP). The 8086 uses the type number N to compute the entry location using the formula: $$\text{Base address} = 4 \times N$$ The IP word is stored at `4N` and `4N+1`, while CS is stored at `4N+2` and `4N+3`. **First Five Dedicated Types:** | Type | Name | Vector Address (4N) | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 0 | Divide Error | `00000H` | | 1 | Single Step | `00004H` | | 2 | NMI | `00008H` | | 3 | Breakpoint | `0000CH` | | 4 | Overflow (INTO) | `00010H` | ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["00000H: IP of Type 0"] --> A1["00002H: CS of Type 0"] A1 --> A2["00004H: IP of Type 1"] A2 --> A3["00006H: CS of Type 1"] A3 --> A4["00008H: IP of Type 2 (NMI)"] A4 --> A5["0000AH: CS of Type 2 (NMI)"] A5 --> A6["0000CH: IP of Type 3"] A6 --> A7["0000EH: CS of Type 3"] A7 --> A8["00010H: IP of Type 4 (INTO)"] A8 --> A9["00012H: CS of Type 4 (INTO)"] ``` **Model Solution (b):** When the 8086 detects an interrupt, it performs the following sequence of micro-operations: 1. **Decrement SP by 2** and **PUSH the Flags Register** onto the stack. 2. **Hardware clears the IF (Interrupt Flag) and TF (Trap Flag)**. 3. **Decrement SP by 2** and **PUSH CS** onto the stack. 4. **Decrement SP by 2** and **PUSH IP** onto the stack. 5. Multiply the type number **N by 4** to obtain the vector base address. 6. Load the **new IP** from memory locations `4N` and `4N+1`. 7. Load the **new CS** from memory locations `4N+2` and `4N+3`. 8. Begin executing the ISR at the loaded CS:IP. **Why are IF and TF cleared?** - **IF is cleared** to **prevent further maskable interrupts** (via INTR) from interrupting the current ISR, ensuring atomic and predictable execution. The ISR can re-enable interrupts using `STI` if nested interrupts are desired. - **TF is cleared** to **disable single-step mode** inside the ISR. Otherwise, the debugger would single-step through the entire ISR, which is not the desired behavior during normal interrupt servicing. The ISR concludes with the **`IRET`** instruction, which pops IP, CS, and Flags (in that order), restoring the CPU to its pre-interrupt state. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate between **NMI and INTR** in the 8086. List the dedicated internal interrupts of the 8086. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For an interrupt of **Type 49H**, calculate the exact physical addresses in the IVT where the IP and CS of the corresponding ISR are stored. Show all steps. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **NMI vs INTR:** | Parameter | NMI (Type 2) | INTR (No fixed type) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Trigger | Rising edge | Level-high (active HIGH) | | Maskable? | No (non-maskable) | Yes — controlled by `IF` | | Type number | Fixed (Type 2) | Fetched from 8259A via INTA | | Vector address | Fixed at `00008H` | Computed as `4 × N` | | Acknowledgement | Not required | Requires 2 INTA bus cycles | | Use case | Catastrophic events (power fail) | General I/O devices | **Dedicated Internal Interrupts of 8086:** 1. **Type 0 — Divide Error** (`DIV` / `IDIV` overflow) 2. **Type 1 — Single Step** (when `TF = 1`) 3. **Type 2 — NMI** (hardware pin) 4. **Type 3 — Breakpoint** (`INT 3`) 5. **Type 4 — Overflow** (`INTO` when `OF = 1`) **Model Solution (b):** Given: **Type N = 49H** **Step 1 — Convert 49H to decimal.** $$49\text{H} = 4 \times 16 + 9 = 64 + 9 = 73_{10}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the IVT base address formula for IP.** $$\text{IP base address} = 4 \times N = 4 \times 73$$ $$4 \times 73 = 292_{10}$$ **Step 3 — Convert 292 decimal to hexadecimal.** $$292 \div 16 = 18 \text{ remainder } 4$$ $$18 \div 16 = 1 \text{ remainder } 2$$ $$1 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 1$$ $$\text{IP base address} = 124\text{H} = 00000124\text{H}$$ **Step 4 — State the IP storage range.** $$\text{IP low byte} = 00000124\text{H}, \quad \text{IP high byte} = 00000125\text{H}$$ **Step 5 — Apply the CS base address formula.** $$\text{CS base address} = 4N + 2 = 292 + 2 = 294_{10}$$ **Step 6 — Convert 294 decimal to hexadecimal.** $$294 \div 16 = 18 \text{ remainder } 6$$ $$18 \div 16 = 1 \text{ remainder } 2$$ $$1 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 1$$ $$\text{CS base address} = 126\text{H} = 00000126\text{H}$$ **Step 7 — State the CS storage range.** $$\text{CS low byte} = 00000126\text{H}, \quad \text{CS high byte} = 00000127\text{H}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** The 4-byte IVT entry for **Type 49H** spans from `00000124H` to `00000127H`. IP occupies `0124H–0125H`; CS occupies `0126H–0127H`. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **256 total interrupt types** in 8086; **5 dedicated** (0–4); rest are user-defined or reserved. - ⭐ **IVT location:** `00000H` to `003FFH` (1 KB). **Memory formula:** IP at `4N`, CS at `4N+2`. - ⭐ **NMI** is **edge-triggered**, **non-maskable**, fixed at **Type 2** → vector `00008H`. - ⭐ **INTR** is **level-triggered**, **maskable by IF**, type fetched from **8259A** during **2 INTA cycles**. - ⭐ **Type 0** = Divide Error; **Type 1** = Single Step; **Type 2** = NMI; **Type 3** = Breakpoint; **Type 4** = Overflow. - ⭐ **IF and TF are auto-cleared** during interrupt response to prevent nesting and single-stepping. - ⭐ **Stack pushing order during interrupt:** Flags → CS → IP. - ⭐ **Stack popping order during IRET:** IP → CS → Flags. - ⭐ **Priority order (highest to lowest):** Divide Error → INT n → INTO → NMI → INTR. - ⭐ **INT 3** is a **1-byte instruction** (`0xCC`) reserved for debugger breakpoints. - ⭐ **Types 5–31** are **reserved by Intel**; **Types 32–255** are free for user applications. - ⭐ **BIOS/DOS interrupts** like `INT 10H`, `INT 13H`, `INT 21H` are all Type ≥ 32 software interrupts that exploit the IVT. - ⭐ **8086 in minimum mode** has no dedicated INTA pin; **maximum mode** uses the `LOCK`-equivalent INTA bus cycle through the 8288 bus controller.

topics.title: ARM Instruction Set - Data Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Instruction Set - Data Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**ARM Data Processing Instructions** are a class of 32-bit ARM core instructions that operate exclusively on the contents of the general-purpose registers (R0–R15), without any direct memory access in their primary execution path. They are the arithmetic logic unit (ALU) operations of the ARM core and are responsible for all computational, logical, comparison, and data-movement activities. Each instruction follows the standardized ARM format:

> **[COND] [opcode] [S] Rd, Rn, Operand2**

where:
- **[COND]** is a 4-bit condition code field that allows conditional execution.
- **[opcode]** denotes the operation (e.g., ADD, SUB, AND, MOV).
- **[S]** is an optional bit that updates the CPSR (Current Program Status Register) flags.
- **Rd** is the destination register.
- **Rn** is the first source register.
- **Operand2** is the flexible second operand — either an **8-bit immediate with 4-bit barrel-rotated shift**, or a **register optionally combined with a shift/rotation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Data Processing instructions in ARM are **register-only** by default — they never touch memory. Memory access requires separate Load/Store instructions (LDR/STR). This strict RISC philosophy is a high-weightage concept in the module.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **scientific calculator with 16 memory slots (R0–R15)**. To perform any calculation — add, subtract, AND, compare — you must first have *both* numbers already sitting in those memory slots. You cannot directly multiply "the number in memory slot 5" with "the number printed on page 42 of a book"; the book value must first be **copied (LDR)** into a memory slot. Once the operands are inside the calculator, the Data Processing instruction is the **"=" key** that performs the operation and (optionally) writes the result back into a memory slot.

The unique twist of ARM is the **"second operand"** (Operand2). It is not just a plain number — it is a small **barrel-shifter sub-system** that can pre-rotate, pre-shift, or pre-mask a value *before* the ALU ever sees it. Think of it as a **small pre-processing conveyor belt** that prepares the ingredient just before it enters the mixing bowl.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics (Bold):** The standard ARM data processing instruction is **32 bits wide**, has a **3-operand format** (2 sources, 1 destination), executes in a single cycle in the ideal pipeline, and supports **16 condition codes** for conditional execution.

---

### Why "Data Processing" Matters in the KTU Curriculum

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome mapping for OECIT615, this topic primarily addresses:
- **CO2:** *Apply the architectural concepts of ARM to write assembly programs using data processing instructions.*
- It is the foundation for higher modules involving **branching, multiplication, and assembly language programming in Keil µVision** (covered later in the syllabus).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Instruction Encoding (32-bit Word)

Every ARM Data Processing instruction is a 32-bit word. The bit-fields are arranged as follows:

| Bits [31:28] | Bits [27:26] | Bits [25] | Bits [24:21] | Bits [20] | Bits [19:16] | Bits [15:12] | Bits [11:8] | Bits [7:0] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| COND | 00 | I | Opcode | S | Rn | Rd | Operand2 field | (Immediate/Shift) |

**Decoding logic:**
- **COND** → condition for execution.
- **I = 0** → Operand2 is a *register with shift* (11 bits: 4-bit shift amount, 3-bit shift type, 1-bit shift register Rm, plus 3 unused bits).
- **I = 1** → Operand2 is an *8-bit immediate value rotated right by an even number* (0–30).

> [!NOTE]
> The **opcode field [24:21]** distinguishes the four major categories — **Arithmetic, Logical, Comparison, and Move**. Within each category, the exact 4-bit opcode selects the specific instruction (e.g., 0100 = ADD, 0010 = SUB).

---

### 2.2 Categories of Data Processing Instructions

#### A. Arithmetic Operations

| Mnemonic | Operation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **ADD** | Rd = Rn + Operand2 | Plain addition |
| **ADC** | Rd = Rn + Operand2 + C | Add with Carry |
| **SUB** | Rd = Rn − Operand2 | Plain subtraction |
| **SBC** | Rd = Rn − Operand2 + C − 1 | Subtract with Carry (NOT) |
| **RSB** | Rd = Operand2 − Rn | Reverse Subtract |
| **RSC** | Rd = Operand2 − Rn + C − 1 | Reverse Subtract with Carry |

#### B. Logical Operations

| Mnemonic | Operation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **AND** | Rd = Rn AND Operand2 | Bitwise AND |
| **ORR** | Rd = Rn OR Operand2 | Bitwise OR |
| **EOR** | Rd = Rn XOR Operand2 | Bitwise XOR |
| **BIC** | Rd = Rn AND (NOT Operand2) | Bit Clear |

#### C. Comparison Operations (no Rd — flags only)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **CMP** | Rn − Operand2 | Compare (sets N, Z, C, V) |
| **CMN** | Rn + Operand2 | Compare Negative |
| **TST** | Rn AND Operand2 | Test bits (sets N, Z) |
| **TEQ** | Rn XOR Operand2 | Test Equivalence (sets N, Z) |

#### D. Data Movement Operations (Rn field is ignored)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **MOV** | Rd = Operand2 | Move (Rn unused) |
| **MVN** | Rd = NOT Operand2 | Move NOT (bitwise complement) |

---

### 2.3 The Barrel Shifter and Operand2

The **barrel shifter** is a dedicated hardware unit that can perform five operations on Operand2 in a *single clock cycle*:

| Shift Type Code | Name | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **00 LSL** | Logical Shift Left | Multiply by 2ⁿ (vacated bits filled with 0) |
| **01 LSR** | Logical Shift Right | Divide by 2ⁿ (unsigned) |
| **10 ASR** | Arithmetic Shift Right | Divide by 2ⁿ preserving sign |
| **11 ROR** | Rotate Right | Circular bit rotation |
| **11 RRX** | Rotate Right Extended | 33-bit rotate through C flag |

**Key KTU Insight:** The barrel shifter sits *between* the register file and the ALU. This means **the shift is "free"** — it does not cost extra cycles, which is why ARM assembly can express operations like `ADD R0, R1, R2 LSL #4` in a single cycle.

---

### 2.4 The Suffix "S" and CPSR Flag Updates

When the **S bit is set**, the instruction updates the four condition flags in the **CPSR** (Current Program Status Register):

| Flag | Meaning | Set by |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | Negative | Logical NOT of MSB of result |
| **Z** | Zero | Result is zero |
| **C** | Carry | Unsigned overflow/underflow |
| **V** | Overflow | Signed overflow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In comparison instructions (CMP, TST, etc.), **S is always implied** — the S bit is effectively forced ON.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Immediate constant value | $\text{Imm} = \text{8-bit value} \times 2^{(2 \times \text{rotate})}$ | rotate is **even** (0,2,…,30) |
| Range of immediate | 0 to $2^{32}-1$ via rotation | All 32-bit constants representable in practice |
| LSL n equivalent | $R \times 2^n$ | n ∈ [0..31] |
| LSR n equivalent | $\lfloor R \div 2^n \rfloor$ (unsigned) | n ∈ [1..32] |
| ASR n equivalent | $R \div 2^n$ (sign-extended) | n ∈ [1..32] |
| ROR n equivalent | bitwise rotation by n | n ∈ [1..31] |
| RRX | R[31:1] = R[32:1], R[0] = old C, C = old R[0] | 33-bit rotate |
| CMP sets flags | Result of (Rn − Operand2) discarded | N=MSB, Z=1 if diff=0 |
| ADC (carry) | $\text{Rd} = \text{Rn} + \text{Op2} + C_{\text{in}}$ | $C_{\text{in}} \in \{0,1\}$ |
| SBC (carry) | $\text{Rd} = \text{Rn} - \text{Op2} + C_{\text{in}} - 1$ | Equivalent to $\text{Rn} - \overline{\text{Op2}} - 1 + C_{\text{in}}$ |
| BIC (bit clear) | $\text{Rd} = \text{Rn} \text{ AND } (\text{NOT Op2})$ | Mask 1 bits in Op2 clear matching bits in Rn |

> **Note on LaTeX:** The pipe character `|` in expressions like `|x|` is intentionally written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table rendering.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

ARM data processing instructions form the **computational backbone** of:
- **Embedded control systems** (washing machines, drones, automotive ECUs) — high code density with barrel-shifter idioms.
- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** — LSL/ASR emulate fixed-point multiplications cheaply.
- **Cryptography & bit manipulation** — EOR, BIC, RRX are staples of CRC, parity, and AES code.
- **Compiler code generation** — modern ARM C compilers emit these idioms for power-of-2 divides, mod-2ⁿ, and bit-field extraction.
- **IoT firmware** — Cortex-M microcontrollers rely on the same Thumb-2 derived data processing for ultra-low-power operation.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Decoding an Immediate Operand

**Problem:** Decode the ARM immediate `0x3F0` expressed as `&0xFF000000` in the instruction:

```
    ORR R0, R0, #0xFF000000
```

**Step 1 — Identify the 8-bit base value and rotation.**

The assembler stores the immediate as: `0xFF` rotated right by how many bit-positions?

$$
\begin{aligned}
0\text{xFF000000}_{\text{hex}} &= 1111\,1111\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_{2} \\
\text{The byte 0xFF is at the } & \text{MSB end of the word.} \\
\text{To bring it to bits [7:0], rotate} & \text{ right by 32 − 24 = 8? No — rotate by 24.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: The hardware rotates an 8-bit value *right* by $2 \times n$ positions, where n is the 4-bit rotate field. A right rotation by 24 brings the 0xFF from bits [31:24] down to bits [7:0], which is *not* what we want. We want 0xFF at [31:24], which is a rotation by 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{imm8} &= \text{0xFF} \\
\text{rotate\_field} &= 0 \quad \text{(even, 0..30)} \\
\text{Stored as} &= \text{(rotate\_field :: imm8)} = \text{0xFF} \\
\text{Decoded value} &= \text{0xFF rotated right by } 0 = \text{0x000000FF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Wait, re-evaluate:** The constant `0xFF000000` is a 32-bit mask. In ARM, this is encoded as 8-bit `0xFF` rotated **right by 8** positions (rotate_field = 4, because 2×4 = 8):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{imm8} &= \text{0xFF} \\
\text{rotate} &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ bits to the right} \\
\text{Value} &= \text{0xFF} \ggg 8 \;\text{within a 32-bit word, then OR'd into high byte.} \\
&= 0\text{xFF000000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The instruction `ORR R0, R0, #0xFF000000` sets the top 8 bits of R0 to 1, leaving the lower 24 bits unchanged.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multiplication by a Constant Using Barrel Shifter

**Problem:** Evaluate `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #4` if R1 = 0x00000010 and R2 = 0x00000003.

**Step 1 — Apply LSL #4 to Operand2 (R2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R2_{shifted} &= R2 \ll 4 \\
&= 0\text{x00000003} \ll 4 \\
&= 0\text{x00000030} \quad \text{(3 × 16 = 48)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — ALU performs addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &= R1 + R2_{shifted} \\
&= 0\text{x00000010} + 0\text{x00000030} \\
&= 0\text{x00000040} \quad (16 + 48 = 64)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle count:** **1 cycle** (barrel shift is "free").

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Subtract with Carry (SBC) for Multi-Precision Subtraction

**Problem:** Compute 64-bit subtraction: `R2:R1 − R4:R3` (high:low word pairs), and store result in `R6:R5`. Use the SBC chain.

**Step 1 — Low word subtraction (plain SUB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R5 &= R1 - R3 \\
\text{If borrow occurred, set } C &= 0; \text{ else } C &= 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — High word subtraction (SBC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R6 &= R2 - R4 - (1 - C) \\
   &= R2 - R4 - \overline{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked numeric example** (R2=0x00000002, R1=0xFFFFFFFF, R4=0x00000001, R3=0x00000002):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R5 &= \text{0xFFFFFFFF} - \text{0x00000002} = \text{0xFFFFFFFD} \\
C &= 1 \quad \text{(no borrow)} \\
R6 &= \text{0x00000002} - \text{0x00000001} - (1-1) = \text{0x00000001}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result = 0x00000001FFFFFFFD**, which equals $131071$. Verify: $(2 \times 2^{32} + \text{0xFFFFFFFF}) - (1 \times 2^{32} + 2) = 2^{33} - 1 - 2^{32} - 2 = 2^{32} - 3 = \text{0xFFFFFFFD}$ for the low word, plus the high-word result. ✅

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operand2 Encodings

| Mode | Syntax | Hardware Action | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | `#imm` | imm8 ROR (2×rotate) | Loading constants |
| Immediate shifted | `#imm, ASR n` | Decoded then shifted | Rare in practice |
| Register | `Rm` | Pass through | Copy or simple op |
| Register, LSL #n | `Rm, LSL #n` | Logical shift left by n | Multiply by 2ⁿ |
| Register, LSR #n | `Rm, LSR #n` | Logical shift right by n | Divide by 2ⁿ |
| Register, ASR #n | `Rm, ASR #n` | Arithmetic shift right | Signed divide by 2ⁿ |
| Register, ROR #n | `Rm, ROR #n` | Rotate right by n | Bit-field packing |
| Register, RRX | `Rm, RRX` | 33-bit rotate through C | Carry propagation |
| Register, shift Rs | `Rm, LSL Rs` | Variable shift by register | Dynamic scaling |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Using BIC to Clear Bits

**Problem:** Clear bits 7, 4, and 0 of R0 without affecting other bits.

**Step 1 — Construct the mask.** To *clear* a bit, the mask bit must be 0; to *keep* a bit, the mask bit must be 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits to clear} &: 7, 4, 0 \\
\text{Mask (clear positions = 0)} &= \text{0b ... 0\,1110\,1110} \\
&= 0\text{xFFFFFFEE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Execute `BIC R0, R0, #0xFFFFFFEE` (assuming constant fits via rotation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0_{new} &= R0_{old} \;\text{AND}\; (\text{NOT } 0\text{xFFFFFFEE}) \\
         &= R0_{old} \;\text{AND}\; 0\text{x00000011} \\
         &\Rightarrow \text{Bits 7, 4, 0 forced to 0.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Data Processing Instruction Execution Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram captures the **flow of data** through the ARM core when a data processing instruction executes, showing the role of the barrel shifter and ALU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Register IR 32-bit] --> B[Decode Unit]
    B --> C{COND field check}
    C -- Condition false --> Z[Instruction skipped no flags]
    C -- Condition true --> D[Read Rn from Register Bank]
    B --> E[Barrel Shifter Operand2 processor]
    E -->|Register with shift| F1[Shift amount from imm4 or Rs]
    E -->|Immediate with rotation| F2[Rotate imm8 right by 2n]
    F1 --> G[Shifter Output]
    F2 --> G
    D --> H[ALU]
    G --> H
    B --> I[Opcode selector]
    I --> H
    H --> J[Result bus]
    J --> K[Write back to Rd]
    B --> L{S bit set}
    L -- Yes --> M[Update CPSR flags N Z C V]
    L -- No --> N[Flags unchanged]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The instruction enters the **Decode Unit** which extracts COND, opcode, S bit, Rd, Rn, and Operand2 fields.
- The **barrel shifter** prepares Operand2 *in parallel* with reading Rn.
- The **ALU** combines Rn and the shifter output using the chosen opcode.
- The result is written to **Rd**, and *optionally* the **CPSR flags** are updated.

---

### 4.2 Operand2 Encoding Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Operand2 decode] --> B{I bit}
    B -- I equals 1 --> C[Immediate operand]
    C --> D[8-bit imm8 value]
    D --> E[4-bit rotate right by 2n]
    E --> F[Sign-extended 32-bit constant to ALU]

    B -- I equals 0 --> G[Register operand]
    G --> H{Shift mode field}
    H -- LSL --> I1[Logical shift left by imm5 or Rs]
    H -- LSR --> I2[Logical shift right]
    H -- ASR --> I3[Arithmetic shift right]
    H -- ROR --> I4[Rotate right]
    H -- RRX --> I5[Rotate right extended with C]
    I1 --> J[Shifter output to ALU]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    F --> J
```

---

### 4.3 CPSR Flag Update Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ALU Result 32 bits] --> B{N equals result 31}
    B -->|bit 31 of result equals 1| N1[N flag equals 1]
    B -->|bit 31 equals 0| N0[N flag equals 0]

    A --> C{Z check: result is 0}
    C -- yes --> Z1[Z flag equals 1]
    C -- no --> Z0[Z flag equals 0]

    A --> D{C carry borrow logic}
    D -- ADD ADC --> E1[C equals carry out of MSB]
    D -- SUB SBC --> E2[C equals NOT borrow]
    A --> F{V signed overflow}
    F --> G1[V equals 1 if signs of operands same and sign of result differs]
    F --> G0[V equals 0 otherwise]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*Explain the role of the **barrel shifter** in ARM data processing instructions. Why is it considered "free" in terms of cycle count?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The barrel shifter is a dedicated hardware sub-block in the ARM core that performs shift and rotate operations (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) on the second operand *before* it enters the ALU.
2. **Location (1 mark):** It sits between the register file and the ALU, processing Operand2 in parallel with the register read of Rn.
3. **"Free" justification (1 mark):** Because the shift happens in the same clock cycle as the ALU operation (parallel hardware, not sequential microcode), the instruction still completes in a single cycle. Hence the shift incurs no additional latency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
*Distinguish between the **BIC** and **AND** instructions in ARM with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **AND** performs bitwise logical conjunction: `Rd = Rn AND Operand2`. *Example:* `AND R0, R1, #0x0F` extracts the low nibble of R1.
2. **BIC** (Bit Clear) is `Rd = Rn AND (NOT Operand2)`. *Example:* `BIC R0, R1, #0x0F` clears the low nibble of R1.
3. **Key difference (1 mark):** BIC is equivalent to AND with an inverted mask — it is preferred over `AND R0, R1, #0xFFFFFFF0` because it expresses intent more clearly and is functionally identical.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. KTU examiners follow strict step-marking.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **general format of an ARM data processing instruction** with a neat diagram of the 32-bit encoding. List the four major categories of data processing instructions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Decode the following instructions and state the result if R1 = 0xAAAAAAAA and R2 = 0x55555555:
  1. `AND R0, R1, R2`
  2. `ORR R3, R1, R2`
  3. `EOR R4, R1, R2`
  4. `BIC R5, R1, R2`
  5. `MVN R6, R1`

---

##### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Format:** `[COND][opcode][S][Rn][Rd][Operand2]`

| Field | Bits | Description |
|---|---|---|
| COND | [31:28] | 4-bit condition code |
| (00) | [27:26] | Always 00 for data processing |
| I | [25] | 0=register Operand2, 1=immediate |
| Opcode | [24:21] | 4-bit operation code |
| S | [20] | Update CPSR flags if 1 |
| Rn | [19:16] | First source register |
| Rd | [15:12] | Destination register |
| Operand2 | [11:0] | Second operand (shifter) |

**Four major categories (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**
1. **Arithmetic** — ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, RSB, RSC
2. **Logical** — AND, ORR, EOR, BIC
3. **Comparison** — CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ
4. **Data Movement** — MOV, MVN

**Encoding diagram (2 marks):**

```
[31:28] [27:26] [25] [24:21] [20] [19:16] [15:12] [11:0]
 COND     00     I   Opcode   S    Rn      Rd    Operand2
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks — 1.4 each]**

Given: **R1 = 0xAAAAAAAA = 1010 1010 1010 ... 1010₂**, **R2 = 0x55555555 = 0101 0101 0101 ... 0101₂**.

**1. AND R0, R1, R2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &= R1 \text{ AND } R2 \\
   &= \text{1010...}\;\text{AND}\;\text{0101...} \\
   &= \text{0000...0} = \text{0x00000000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**2. ORR R3, R1, R2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R3 &= R1 \text{ OR } R2 \\
   &= \text{1010...}\;\text{OR}\;\text{0101...} \\
   &= \text{1111...} = \text{0xFFFFFFFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. EOR R4, R1, R2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R4 &= R1 \text{ XOR } R2 \\
   &= \text{1010...}\;\text{XOR}\;\text{0101...} \\
   &= \text{1111...} = \text{0xFFFFFFFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**4. BIC R5, R1, R2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R5 &= R1 \text{ AND } (\text{NOT } R2) \\
   &= R1 \text{ AND } \text{0xAAAAAAAA} \\
   &= \text{0xAAAAAAAA}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Because R2 and R1 are exact bitwise complements, NOT R2 = R1.)

**5. MVN R6, R1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R6 &= \text{NOT } R1 \\
   &= \text{NOT 0xAAAAAAAA} = \text{0x55555555}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CPSR flag update rules** for ADD, SUB, and CMP instructions. How does the S bit control flag updates?

**(b) [7 Marks)** A 16-bit value is stored across two registers: R1 (high byte) and R0 (low byte). Write an ARM instruction sequence to **double the 16-bit value using only data processing instructions**, handling the carry from the low-byte doubling into the high byte.

---

##### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**CPSR Flags: N, Z, C, V** (2 marks for identification).

**ADD (Rd = Rn + Op2) flag rules (2 marks):**
- **N = result[31]** (1 if MSB is 1)
- **Z = 1 if result == 0, else 0**
- **C = carry out from bit 31** (1 if unsigned overflow)
- **V = 1** if both operands have the same sign and the result has the opposite sign (signed overflow)

**SUB (Rd = Rn − Op2) flag rules (2 marks):**
- **N = result[31]**
- **Z = 1 if result == 0**
- **C = 0 if borrow occurred (i.e., unsigned underflow), else 1** (NOT of borrow)
- **V = 1** if signs of operands differ and sign of result differs from Rn

**CMP = SUB with S always set, result discarded (1 mark).**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Approach:** Use ADC chain — `LSL #1` is multiply by 2.

```
    MOV   R2, R0, LSL #1      ; R2 = R0 << 1 (low byte doubled)
    ADC   R3, R1, R1          ; R3 = R1 + R1 + C  (high byte doubled + carry)
    MOV   R0, R2              ; low byte result to R0
    MOV   R1, R3              ; high byte result to R1
```

**Step-by-step verification (5 marks):**

Let R1:R0 = 0x1234 (high:low). R0 = 0x34, R1 = 0x12.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line 1: } R2 &= R0 \ll 1 = 0\text{x68}, \; C_{\text{out}} = 0 \\
\text{Line 2: } R3 &= R1 + R1 + C = 0\text{x12} + 0\text{x12} + 0 = 0\text{x24} \\
\text{Result: } R1:R0 &= 0\text{x2468} = 2 \times 0\text{x1234} = 0\text{x2468}. \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Edge case — carry propagation:** Let R1:R0 = 0x80FF. R0 doubling produces 0x1FE with C = 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R2 &= 0\text{x1FE}, \; C = 1 \\
R3 &= 0\text{x80} + 0\text{x80} + 1 = 0\text{x101} \\
\text{Final 32-bit result} &= 0\text{x0001\,01FE} \\
&= 2 \times 0\text{x80FF} = 0\text{x101FE}. \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The `ADC` correctly absorbs the carry from the low-byte doubling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the S bit semantics:** Many students write `ADD R0, R1, R2` and then assert flags incorrectly. Remember: **S is OFF by default**; only `ADDS` (or instructions with implicit S) updates CPSR.
> 2. **Mis-decoding `0x` constants:** ARM immediates are limited to 8-bit values rotated by an *even* amount. Constants like `0xFFFFFF00` fit (`0xFF` rotated right by 8) but arbitrary 32-bit values like `0x12345678` **cannot** be expressed as a single immediate — use `MOV/MVNV/ORR` with rotated pieces (MOV/MVNV pseudo-instructions).
> 3. **BIC confusion:** Students often confuse BIC with BICS (flag update). BIC only clears bits when the corresponding mask bit is **1**; a 0 in the mask *preserves* the original bit.
> 4. **Carry flag for SUB:** The C flag after SUB is **set if NO borrow occurred**, i.e., the inverted logic from ADD. This is a classic mark-loser.
> 5. **Comparison instructions have no Rd:** Forgetting that CMP, TST, TEQ, CMN do not write to a destination register is a recurring valuation error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ ARM data processing instructions are **32-bit, 3-operand, register-only** with no direct memory access.
- ✅ Four categories: **Arithmetic, Logical, Comparison, Move.**
- ✅ **Operand2** is the "secret weapon" — it is a *barrel-shifted register OR a rotated 8-bit immediate*.
- ✅ The **barrel shifter** is parallel to the ALU → shifts are **free** (single-cycle execution).
- ✅ Immediate constants: **8-bit value rotated right by an even amount (0–30)** — yields a 32-bit constant.
- ✅ The **S bit** controls **CPSR flag updates**; comparison instructions **force S on**.
- ✅ **ADC / SBC** chains are used for **multi-precision (64/128-bit)** arithmetic.
- ✅ **BIC** = `Rn AND (NOT Op2)` — the standard idiom for clearing individual bits.
- ✅ **MVN** = bitwise NOT — the canonical way to load `−1` (`MVN R0, #0`).
- ✅ **CMP sets C = 1 if no borrow** (inverted carry logic vs ADD).
- ✅ The 16 condition codes in COND allow **conditional execution without branches**, saving pipeline flushes.
- ✅ Multiply instructions (**MUL, MLA**) are *separate* from basic data processing — covered in the next module sub-topic.
- ✅ Modern Thumb-2 / Cortex-M code reuses these data processing semantics in compressed 16-bit form.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Branch Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch Instructions** in the **8086 microprocessor** are a class of program control transfer instructions that alter the normal sequential flow of execution by loading the **Instruction Pointer (IP)**, and optionally the **Code Segment Register (CS)**, with a new target address. They are the architectural mechanism used to implement **decision-making**, **looping**, **subroutine calls**, and **interrupts** in assembly language programs.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, branch instructions are categorized into four primary families:

| Category | Purpose | Example Mnemonics |
|---|---|---|
| **Unconditional Transfers** | Always alter control flow | `JMP`, `CALL`, `RET`, `INT` |
| **Conditional Transfers** | Transfer only if a flag condition is true | `JE`, `JNE`, `JG`, `JL`, `JC` |
| **Iteration Control** | Loop based on `CX` register | `LOOP`, `LOOPE`, `LOOPNE` |
| **Interrupt Control** | Hardware/software interrupt handling | `INT n`, `INTO`, `IRET` |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy: Reading a Book with Choices**
>
> Imagine you are reading a choose-your-own-adventure novel. Normally, you read page 1, then page 2, then page 3 — that's **sequential execution**. But when you reach a "If you go left, turn to page 40. If right, turn to page 55," you have just encountered a **branch instruction**. The "If" check is the **flag condition**, and turning to a different page is **loading a new value into the IP register**.

In microprocessors, every instruction automatically advances the **IP** to the next memory location. Branch instructions are special because they **overwrite** this automatic IP advancement with a new address based on either a programmer's directive (unconditional) or a runtime condition check (conditional).

### 1.3 Key Architectural Entities

> [!NOTE]
> The **8086 Instruction Pointer (IP)** is a **16-bit register** that always points to the **next instruction to be fetched**. Branch instructions manipulate this register either directly (intra-segment) or together with **CS** (inter-segment).

The two registers involved in branch target calculation are:

- **IP (Instruction Pointer)** — Holds the 16-bit offset of the next instruction within the current code segment.
- **CS (Code Segment Register)** — Holds the 16-bit base address of the current code segment.

Together, the **20-bit physical address** of the next instruction is computed as:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP}$$

### 1.4 Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 4 Weightage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Module 4 (Instruction Set of 8086)** carries significant marks in the End Semester Examination (ESE). Branch instructions typically appear as a **Part B (14-mark) question** involving classification, flag analysis, and instruction encoding. Expect combinations with **addressing modes** and **flag register** concepts.
