## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Branch Instructions Work

> [!NOTE]
> Every branch instruction in the **8086** operates on a single fundamental principle: **modify the IP (and optionally CS) to redirect the CPU's fetch unit to a new memory location.**

The execution follows this structured logic:

1. **Fetch Stage:** The 8086's Bus Interface Unit (BIU) prefetches the next instruction into the 6-byte instruction queue.
2. **Decode Stage:** The Execution Unit (EU) decodes the opcode and identifies it as a control transfer instruction.
3. **Condition Evaluation (Conditional Only):** The EU tests the relevant bits of the **Flag Register** (or `CX` for loops).
4. **Target Address Calculation:** The Effective Address (EA) or segment:offset of the target is computed based on the addressing mode and displacement.
5. **Register Update:** IP (and CS if inter-segment) is overwritten with the calculated target.
6. **Queue Flush:** The BIU flushes the instruction queue and begins prefetching from the new address.

### 2.2 Classification Hierarchy

#### A. Unconditional Branch Instructions

| Instruction | Syntax | Operation | Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short Jump | `JMP rel8` | `IP ← IP + 8-bit displacement` | 2 bytes |
| Near Jump | `JMP rel16` | `IP ← IP + 16-bit displacement` | 3 bytes |
| Direct Far Jump | `JMP addr16:addr16` | `CS:IP ← target segment:offset` | 5 bytes |
| Indirect Near Jump | `JMP reg16` or `JMP mem16` | `IP ← contents of operand` | 2 bytes |
| Intra-segment CALL | `CALL rel16` | Push IP, then `IP ← IP + 16-bit disp` | 3 bytes |
| Inter-segment CALL | `CALL proc-name` | Push CS, push IP, then load both | 5 bytes |
| Near RET | `RET` | Pop IP from stack | 1 byte |
| Far RET | `RETF` | Pop IP, then pop CS from stack | 1 byte |
| Software Interrupt | `INT n` | Push flags, CS, IP; load from vector table | 2 bytes |

#### B. Conditional Jump Mnemonics (Flag-Based)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8086 provides **18 conditional jump instructions**. All conditional jumps in the 8086 are **SHORT jumps** (8-bit displacement, range **−128 to +127** bytes from the next instruction).

| Mnemonic | Condition Tested | Flag Bits Checked | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JZ` | ZF = 1 | ZF | Jump if Equal / Zero |
| `JNE` / `JNZ` | ZF = 0 | ZF | Jump if Not Equal / Not Zero |
| `JC` | CF = 1 | CF | Jump if Carry |
| `JNC` | CF = 0 | CF | Jump if No Carry |
| `JS` | SF = 1 | SF | Jump if Sign (negative) |
| `JNS` | SF = 0 | SF | Jump if No Sign (positive) |
| `JO` | OF = 1 | OF | Jump if Overflow |
| `JNO` | OF = 0 | OF | Jump if No Overflow |
| `JP` / `JPE` | PF = 1 | PF | Jump if Parity Even |
| `JNP` / `JPO` | PF = 0 | PF | Jump if Parity Odd |
| `JA` / `JNBE` | CF=0 AND ZF=0 | CF, ZF | Above (unsigned) |
| `JAE` / `JNB` / `JNC` | CF = 0 | CF | Above or Equal (unsigned) |
| `JB` / `JNAE` / `JC` | CF = 1 | CF | Below (unsigned) |
| `JBE` / `JNA` | CF=1 OR ZF=1 | CF, ZF | Below or Equal (unsigned) |
| `JG` / `JNLE` | ZF=0 AND SF=OF | ZF, SF, OF | Greater (signed) |
| `JGE` / `JNL` | SF = OF | SF, OF | Greater or Equal (signed) |
| `JL` / `JNGE` | SF ≠ OF | SF, OF | Less (signed) |
| `JLE` / `JNG` | ZF=1 OR SF≠OF | ZF, SF, OF | Less or Equal (signed) |

#### C. Iteration / Loop Instructions

| Instruction | Operation | Termination |
|---|---|---|
| `LOOP label` | `CX ← CX − 1`; if `CX ≠ 0`, jump to label | When `CX = 0` |
| `LOOPE label` / `LOOPZ` | Decrement CX; jump if CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 1 | When CX = 0 or ZF = 0 |
| `LOOPNE label` / `LOOPNZ` | Decrement CX; jump if CX ≠ 0 AND ZF = 0 | When CX = 0 or ZF = 1 |
| `JCXZ label` | Jump to label if `CX = 0` (no decrement) | — |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical calculations on branch instructions in the KTU exam revolve around three core formulas. Memorize them thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Short Jump Displacement | $\text{disp8} \in [-128, +127]$ | Bytes (signed 2's complement) |
| 2 | Near Jump Displacement | $\text{disp16} \in [-32768, +32767]$ | Bytes (signed 2's complement) |
| 3 | Physical Address of Branch Target | $\text{PA} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP}_{\text{new}}$ | Hex / Decimal bytes |
| 4 | New IP after relative jump | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{old}} + \text{instruction length} + \text{disp}$ | Offset in segment |
| 5 | Stack pointer after CALL | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2$ (or −4 for far) | Words pushed |
| 6 | Stack pointer after RET | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 2$ (or +4 for far) | Words popped |
| 7 | Loop iterations | $N = \text{initial CX value}$ | Count |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Alert:** When calculating `IP_new` for a relative jump, **always add the instruction's own length** (2 bytes for short, 3 bytes for near) to the current IP **before** applying the displacement. The displacement is measured from the **next** instruction, not the current one.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Branch instructions form the bedrock of every embedded control system:

- **Automotive ECUs:** Conditional branches decide fuel injection timing based on sensor flags.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Loop instructions iterate over scan cycles.
- **IoT Devices:** CALL instructions invoke firmware subroutines for sensor polling.
- **Digital Signal Processing:** Unconditional jumps implement circular buffers and state machines.

### 2.5 Comparison — Jump vs. CALL vs. Loop

| Feature | JMP | CALL | LOOP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saves return address? | No | Yes (on stack) | No |
| Affects stack? | No | Yes (pushes 2 or 4 bytes) | No |
| Needs matching RET? | No | Yes | No |
| Uses CX register? | No | No | Yes (decrements) |
| Conditional form? | Yes (18 variants) | No | Yes (3 variants) |
| Typical use | Skip sections, end loops | Subroutine invocation | Counting iterations |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — IP Recalculation for Relative Jumps

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **exhaustive KTU-style derivation** for computing the new IP value after a short relative jump. This is the **single most repeated calculation** in KTU board exams on this topic.

**Given:**
- Current IP = `0200H`
- Instruction: `JMP SHORT 05H` (opcode `EB`, displacement `05H`)
- Instruction size = **2 bytes**

**Step 1 — Identify the instruction length.**
A short jump is encoded as `EB disp8`, occupying **2 bytes** total.

**Step 2 — Compute IP of the next instruction (the "fetch pointer" before displacement).**
The CPU's IP after fetching this 2-byte instruction points to the byte immediately after it:

$$\text{IP}_{\text{next}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{Instruction Size}$$

$$\text{IP}_{\text{next}} = 0200\text{H} + 0002\text{H} = 0202\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the signed 8-bit displacement.**
Since the displacement is `05H` (positive, 2's complement), the new IP is:

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{next}} + \text{disp8}$$

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0202\text{H} + 0005\text{H} = 0207\text{H}$$

**Step 4 — Express in physical address form (if CS is given, say CS = `1000H`):**

$$\text{PA}_{\text{old}} = 1000\text{H} \times 16 + 0200\text{H} = 10200\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 1000\text{H} \times 16 + 0207\text{H} = 10207\text{H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The displacement represents a forward jump of 5 bytes from the **next** instruction. The next instruction starts at `0202H`, and 5 bytes ahead is `0207H`. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Example — Backward Short Jump with Negative Displacement

**Given:**
- Current IP = `0300H`
- Instruction: `JMP SHORT F2H` (which is `−14` in signed 2's complement)
- CS = `2000H`

**Step 1 — Decode the negative displacement.**
Hex `F2H` in signed 8-bit 2's complement:

$$\text{disp8} = \text{F2H} = 11110010_2 = -14_{10}$$

**Step 2 — Compute IP of next instruction.**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{next}} = 0300\text{H} + 0002\text{H} = 0302\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the signed displacement.**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0302\text{H} + \text{FFE}2\text{H (sign-extended)}$$

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0302\text{H} - 0014\text{H} = 02EE\text{H}$$

**Step 4 — Physical address calculation.**

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 02EE\text{H} = 202EE\text{H}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — CALL and RET Stack Analysis

**Scenario:** A `CALL NEAR PROC` instruction is executed when:
- IP before CALL = `1000H`
- SP = `FFFEH`
- SS = `3000H`
- Target Procedure is at `IP = 1050H`

**Step 1 — During CALL execution, the return address is pushed onto the stack.**

$$\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2 = \text{FFFEH} - 2 = \text{FFFCH}$$

The value pushed is the IP of the **next instruction after CALL** (i.e., the return address):

$$\text{Return Address Pushed} = 1000\text{H} + 3 = 1003\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — IP is updated to the procedure's address.**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 1050\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Inside the procedure, when `RET` is executed:**

$$\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 2 = \text{FFFCH} + 2 = \text{FFFEH}$$

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 1003\text{H} \quad \text{(value popped from stack)}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example — LOOP Instruction Trace

**Initial State:** `CX = 0005H`, IP of `LOOP` instruction = `0400H`, target label is at `IP = 0380H`.

**Encoding of `LOOP label`:** Opcode `E2` followed by an 8-bit signed displacement.

**Step 1 — Compute the displacement (offset) for a backward jump.**

$$\text{disp} = \text{Target IP} - (\text{Current IP} + \text{Instruction Size})$$

$$\text{disp} = 0380\text{H} - (0400\text{H} + 2) = 0380\text{H} - 0402\text{H}$$

$$\text{disp} = 0380\text{H} - 0402\text{H} = \text{FF7EH (which is} -132_{10} \text{ in signed 2's complement)}$$

**Step 2 — Trace the loop iterations:**

| Iteration | CX before | CX after (CX−1) | Condition (CX ≠ 0) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0005 | 0004 | True | Jump to 0380H |
| 2 | 0004 | 0003 | True | Jump to 0380H |
| 3 | 0003 | 0002 | True | Jump to 0380H |
| 4 | 0002 | 0001 | True | Jump to 0380H |
| 5 | 0001 | 0000 | False | Fall through to next instruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The LOOP instruction decrements CX **first**, then tests. So a `LOOP` with initial `CX = 1` will execute the body **once** but will **not** loop back (CX becomes 0).

### 3.5 Comprehensive Algorithm — Choosing the Correct Conditional Jump

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students can select the **right conditional jump** given an arithmetic comparison. Follow this decision matrix strictly.

**Algorithm for unsigned comparison (after `CMP A, B`):**
- If `A = B` → use `JE` (ZF=1)
- If `A ≠ B` → use `JNE` (ZF=0)
- If `A > B` → use `JA` (CF=0, ZF=0)
- If `A ≥ B` → use `JAE` (CF=0)
- If `A < B` → use `JB` (CF=1)
- If `A ≤ B` → use `JBE` (CF=1 or ZF=1)

**Algorithm for signed comparison (after `CMP A, B`):**
- If `A = B` → use `JE` (ZF=1)
- If `A ≠ B` → use `JNE` (ZF=0)
- If `A > B` → use `JG` (ZF=0, SF=OF)
- If `A ≥ B` → use `JGE` (SF=OF)
- If `A < B` → use `JL` (SF≠OF)
- If `A ≤ B` → use `JLE` (ZF=1 or SF≠OF)

### 3.6 Analytical Table — Machine Encoding of Common Jumps

| Instruction | Opcode (Hex) | Bytes | Machine Code Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JMP SHORT +5` | EB | 2 | `EB 05` |
| `JMP SHORT −10` | EB | 2 | `EB F6` |
| `JE label` | 74 | 2 | `74 08` |
| `JNE label` | 75 | 2 | `75 FC` |
| `JC label` | 72 | 2 | `72 10` |
| `JNC label` | 73 | 2 | `73 08` |
| `JG label` | 7F | 2 | `7F 04` |
| `JL label` | 7C | 2 | `7C 06` |
| `LOOP label` | E2 | 2 | `E2 FA` |
| `CALL NEAR` | E8 | 3 | `E8 03 00` |
| `RET` | C3 | 1 | `C3` |
| `INT 3` | CD | 2 | `CD 03` |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Branch Instruction Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Instruction Fetch] --> B[Decode Opcode]
    B --> C{Is it a Branch Instruction?}
    C -->|No| D[Execute Normal Instruction]
    C -->|Yes| E{Unconditional or Conditional?}
    E -->|Unconditional| F[Compute Target Address]
    E -->|Conditional| G[Test Flag Register]
    G -->|Condition False| H[Continue Sequential Execution]
    G -->|Condition True| F
    F --> I{Intra or Inter Segment?}
    I -->|Intra| J[Update IP only]
    I -->|Inter| K[Update CS and IP]
    J --> L[Flush Prefetch Queue]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Begin New Fetch from Target]
    H --> D
    D --> N[End]
    M --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The **flag test** block (node `G`) is the critical decision gate for all 18 conditional jumps. For `LOOP` instructions, this block is replaced by a **CX register test** after automatic decrement.

### 4.2 Classification Tree — Branch Instruction Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Branch Instructions 8086] --> B[Unconditional Transfers]
    A --> C[Conditional Transfers]
    A --> D[Iteration Control]
    A --> E[Interrupt Control]
    B --> B1[JMP Short]
    B --> B2[JMP Near]
    B --> B3[JMP Far]
    B --> B4[CALL Near]
    B --> B5[CALL Far]
    B --> B6[RET Near]
    B --> B7[RET Far]
    C --> C1[Flag Tests]
    C --> C2[Sign-Based]
    C --> C3[Unsigned Compare]
    C --> C4[Signed Compare]
    C1 --> C11[JE JZ]
    C1 --> C12[JNE JNZ]
    C2 --> C21[JS JNS]
    C2 --> C22[JO JNO]
    C2 --> C23[JP JNP]
    C3 --> C31[JA JNBE]
    C3 --> C32[JAE JNB]
    C3 --> C33[JB JNAE]
    C3 --> C34[JBE JNA]
    C4 --> C41[JG JNLE]
    C4 --> C42[JGE JNL]
    C4 --> C43[JL JNGE]
    C4 --> C44[JLE JNG]
    D --> D1[LOOP]
    D --> D2[LOOPE LOOPZ]
    D --> D3[LOOPNE LOOPNZ]
    D --> D4[JCXZ]
    E --> E1[INT n]
    E --> E2[INTO]
    E --> E3[IRET]
```

### 4.3 Flowchart — CALL/RET Subroutine Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Main1[Main Program] -->|CALL PROC| Push1[Push IP to Stack]
    Push1 --> UpdIP[Update IP to Procedure Start]
    UpdIP --> Proc[Execute Procedure Body]
    Proc -->|RET| PopIP[Pop IP from Stack]
    PopIP --> Main2[Resume Main Program]
    Main2 --> Next1[Next Instruction]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — LOOP Instruction State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init: CX = N
    Init --> Decrement: LOOP encountered
    Decrement --> CheckZero: CX = CX - 1
    CheckZero --> Jump: CX is not 0
    CheckZero --> Exit: CX equals 0
    Jump --> Init: IP set to target label
    Exit --> [*]: Fall through to next instruction
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note on Memory Map Visualization:** Branch instructions affect the **CS:IP register pair**, which the 8086's Bus Interface Unit (BIU) uses to generate the 20-bit physical address. The 1 MB address space is divided into 64 KB code segments. A near jump remains inside one segment; a far jump crosses to a new segment, requiring both CS and IP to be reloaded — a costly operation that **flushes the 6-byte prefetch queue** entirely.

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Explain the difference between a near jump and a short jump in the 8086 microprocessor. State the displacement size and the range of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Short Jump (`JMP rel8`) | Near Jump (`JMP rel16`) |
|---|---|---|
| **Displacement Size** | 8-bit signed | 16-bit signed |
| **Range** | −128 to +127 bytes | −32,768 to +32,767 bytes |
| **Instruction Length** | 2 bytes (opcode + disp) | 3 bytes (opcode + 2 disp) |
| **Segment** | Intra-segment (IP only) | Intra-segment (IP only) |
| **Common Use** | Small conditional branches | Larger internal jumps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for range/displacement comparison.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List the flag bits that are tested by the conditional jump instructions `JA` and `JG`. Under what data type conditions is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **`JA` (Jump if Above):** Tests the **CF** and **ZF** flags. Condition: `CF = 0 AND ZF = 0`. Used for **unsigned** comparison (e.g., comparing two `BYTE` or `WORD` values where the MSB is not interpreted as a sign).
- **`JG` (Jump if Greater):** Tests the **ZF**, **SF**, and **OF** flags. Condition: `ZF = 0 AND SF = OF`. Used for **signed** comparison (e.g., comparing `INTEGER` values where MSB represents the sign).

| Jump | Flags Tested | Data Type |
|---|---|---|
| `JA` | CF, ZF | Unsigned |
| `JG` | ZF, SF, OF | Signed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for flag identification, 1 mark for condition logic, 1 mark for data type distinction.

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Branch Instruction Mechanics & IP Recalculation**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the execution sequence of an **unconditional `JMP SHORT`** instruction in the 8086. How is the target address computed? **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Consider the following 8086 instruction sequence. The current **IP = `0150H`** and **CS = `2000H`**. Calculate the new physical address after each of the following instructions is executed: **\[7 marks\]**

```asm
i.   JMP SHORT  +25
ii.  JMP SHORT  -30
iii. CALL NEAR  0200H
iv.  JMP WORD PTR [BX]   ; BX = 0300H
v.   LOOP BACK            ; assuming CX = 0001H
```

---

**Complete Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Flowchart & Target Address Computation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the instruction:**
A `JMP SHORT` (unconditional short jump) is encoded as `EB disp8`. It always transfers control to the address calculated by adding a **signed 8-bit displacement** to the IP of the next instruction.

**Step 2 — Execution Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fetch JMP SHORT instruction, 2 bytes] --> B[Increment IP by 2]
    B --> C[Add sign-extended disp8 to IP]
    C --> D[New IP = IP_next + disp8]
    D --> E[Flush BIU Prefetch Queue]
    E --> F[Begin Instruction Fetch from New IP]
    F --> G[Continue Execution at Target]
```

**Step 3 — Target Address Formula:**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{old}} + 2 + \text{disp8 (signed)}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP}_{\text{new}}$$

**Step 4 — Why flush the queue?** The 8086's BIU prefetches the next 6 bytes into the queue. After a jump, these bytes are invalid, so the queue is discarded and refilled from the new address.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations [7 marks]

**Given:** `IP_old = 0150H`, `CS = 2000H`

**Sub-part (i): `JMP SHORT +25`**

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0150\text{H} + 2 + 25 = 0150\text{H} + 001\text{BH} = 016\text{BH}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 016\text{BH} = 20000\text{H} + 016\text{BH} = 2016\text{BH}$$

**Sub-part (ii): `JMP SHORT −30`**

Convert `−30` to signed 8-bit hex: `−30 = E2H` (since `30H + CEH = 100H` and 2's complement gives `100H − 30H = E2H`).

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0150\text{H} + 2 + \text{FFE}2\text{H} = 0152\text{H} - 30 = 0134\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 0134\text{H} = 20134\text{H}$$

**Sub-part (iii): `CALL NEAR 0200H`**

For a near CALL, the displacement is interpreted as relative to the instruction **after** the CALL (which is 3 bytes long).

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0150\text{H} + 3 + 0200\text{H} = 0353\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 0353\text{H} = 20353\text{H}$$

**Sub-part (iv): `JMP WORD PTR [BX]`, where `BX = 0300H`**

This is an **indirect near jump**. The instruction length is 2 bytes (assuming `JMP mem16` encoding). The target address is the **contents of memory location** pointed to by `BX`, not `BX` itself.

For this problem, we assume the value at `[BX]` is given or interpret the operand as the offset:

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0150\text{H} + 2 = 0152\text{H} \text{ (just sequential, since target is indirect)}$$

If the problem intends `BX` content to be the target IP directly:

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0300\text{H}, \quad \text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 0300\text{H} = 20300\text{H}$$

**Sub-part (v): `LOOP BACK` with `CX = 0001H`**

The `LOOP` decrements CX first, then tests: `CX = 1 − 1 = 0`. Since `CX = 0`, the loop **does not jump**. Control falls through to the next instruction.

$$\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = 0150\text{H} + 2 = 0152\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 0152\text{H} = 20152\text{H}$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Conditional Jumps & Flag Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **18 conditional jump instructions** of the 8086 with respect to the flag bits tested. Provide a clear tabular classification based on **unsigned** and **signed** comparisons. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** After executing the instruction sequence below, the flag register contains: `CF = 1`, `ZF = 0`, `SF = 0`, `OF = 0`, `PF = 0`, `AF = 0`. Determine which of the following conditional jumps will be **taken** and which will **not be taken**. Justify each with the flag conditions. **\[7 marks\]**

```asm
MOV AL, 0F5H
CMP AL, 0A0H
```

Then evaluate: `JE`, `JNE`, `JA`, `JBE`, `JG`, `JL`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JS`, `JNS`, `JO`, `JNO`, `JP`, `JNP`, `JAE`, `JB`, `JGE`, `JLE`.

---

**Complete Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Classification Table [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive classification of all 18 conditional jumps:**

**Category 1: Single Flag Tests (6 instructions)**

| Mnemonic | Condition | Flag Tested |
|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JZ` | Equal / Zero | ZF = 1 |
| `JNE` / `JNZ` | Not Equal / Not Zero | ZF = 0 |
| `JC` | Carry | CF = 1 |
| `JNC` | No Carry | CF = 0 |
| `JS` | Sign (negative) | SF = 1 |
| `JNS` | No Sign (positive) | SF = 0 |
| `JO` | Overflow | OF = 1 |
| `JNO` | No Overflow | OF = 0 |
| `JP` / `JPE` | Parity Even | PF = 1 |
| `JNP` / `JPO` | Parity Odd | PF = 0 |

**Category 2: Unsigned Comparisons (4 instructions)**

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| `JA` / `JNBE` | Above / Not Below or Equal | CF = 0 AND ZF = 0 |
| `JAE` / `JNB` | Above or Equal / Not Below | CF = 0 |
| `JB` / `JNAE` | Below / Not Above or Equal | CF = 1 |
| `JBE` / `JNA` | Below or Equal / Not Above | CF = 1 OR ZF = 1 |

**Category 3: Signed Comparisons (4 instructions)**

| Mnemonic | Meaning | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| `JG` / `JNLE` | Greater / Not Less or Equal | ZF = 0 AND SF = OF |
| `JGE` / `JNL` | Greater or Equal / Not Less | SF = OF |
| `JL` / `JNGE` | Less / Not Greater or Equal | SF ≠ OF |
| `JLE` / `JNG` | Less or Equal / Not Greater | ZF = 1 OR SF ≠ OF |

#### Part (b) — Jump Evaluation [7 marks]

**Given flag state after `CMP AL, 0F5H, 0A0H`:**
- CF = 1, ZF = 0, SF = 0, OF = 0, PF = 0, AF = 0

| Jump | Condition Required | Evaluation | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JE` | ZF = 1 | ZF = 0 ≠ 1 | **Not Taken** |
| `JNE` | ZF = 0 | ZF = 0 = 0 | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JA` | CF=0 AND ZF=0 | CF=1, fails first | **Not Taken** |
| `JBE` | CF=1 OR ZF=1 | CF=1, satisfied | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JG` | ZF=0 AND SF=OF | ZF=0 ✓, SF=0, OF=0, SF=OF ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JL` | SF ≠ OF | SF=0, OF=0, equal | **Not Taken** |
| `JC` | CF = 1 | CF = 1 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JNC` | CF = 0 | CF = 1 ≠ 0 | **Not Taken** |
| `JS` | SF = 1 | SF = 0 ≠ 1 | **Not Taken** |
| `JNS` | SF = 0 | SF = 0 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JO` | OF = 1 | OF = 0 ≠ 1 | **Not Taken** |
| `JNO` | OF = 0 | OF = 0 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JP` | PF = 1 | PF = 0 ≠ 1 | **Not Taken** |
| `JNP` | PF = 0 | PF = 0 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JAE` | CF = 0 | CF = 1 ≠ 0 | **Not Taken** |
| `JB` | CF = 1 | CF = 1 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JGE` | SF = OF | SF = 0, OF = 0 ✓ | **Taken** ✓ |
| `JLE` | ZF=1 OR SF≠OF | ZF=0, SF=OF, condition false | **Not Taken** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixed signed/unsigned confusion:** Students often use `JA` for signed values or `JG` for unsigned values. Remember: **JA/JB family = unsigned**, **JG/JL family = signed**. This is the **#1 mark-loss area** in KTU exams.
> 2. **Off-by-one in displacement:** Forgetting to add the instruction length (2 or 3 bytes) before applying the signed displacement. This causes a 2-byte error in the calculated IP.
> 3. **Loop termination logic:** The `LOOP` instruction decrements CX **first**, then tests for zero. Initial CX = 1 means the body executes **once**, then exits.
> 4. **CALL stack imbalance:** Failing to balance `CALL` with a corresponding `RET`, or using a near `RET` after a far `CALL`, corrupts the stack.
> 5. **Flag register interpretation:** The `CMP` instruction performs subtraction **without** storing the result, but it **does** update the flags. Students sometimes forget this and look for wrong values.
> 6. **Displacement sign extension:** Forgetting to sign-extend 8-bit displacement to 16 bits when computing effective addresses.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **Branch instructions modify IP (and optionally CS)** to alter the sequential flow of execution.
- [x] **Short jump = 2 bytes (EB disp8), range ±127.** Near jump = 3 bytes (E9 disp16), range ±32,767. Far jump = 5 bytes (EA addr).
- [x] **All 18 conditional jumps are SHORT** (8-bit displacement only) in the 8086.
- [x] **Unsigned comparisons:** use `JA`, `JAE`, `JB`, `JBE` (based on CF and ZF).
- [x] **Signed comparisons:** use `JG`, `JGE`, `JL`, `JLE` (based on ZF, SF, OF).
- [x] **Sign-based single jumps:** `JS`, `JNS`, `JO`, `JNO`.
- [x] **LOOP family:** decrements `CX` first, then jumps if `CX ≠ 0`. Variants: `LOOPE`, `LOOPNE`, `JCXZ`.
- [x] **CALL pushes return address** (2 bytes for near, 4 bytes for far) onto the stack. **RET pops** it back into IP (and CS).
- [x] **The IP offset is measured from the NEXT instruction**, not the current one. Always add instruction length to current IP before applying displacement.
- [x] **Physical address formula:** $\text{PA} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP}_{\text{new}}$.
- [x] **Far jumps and calls require both CS and IP** to be reloaded, which flushes the BIU's 6-byte instruction queue.
- [x] **Software interrupts** (`INT n`) push FLAGS, CS, and IP onto the stack before loading from the interrupt vector table at physical address `n × 4`.
- [x] **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Flag analysis after `CMP`, displacement calculations, classification tables, and subroutine stack mechanics.
- [x] **Common error:** Confusing `JNC` (CF=0) with `JNE` (ZF=0) — they are completely different flag tests.
- [x] **Engineering application:** Branch instructions are the heart of all decision-making firmware, from automotive ECUs to IoT sensor loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Store Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load Store Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture**, a **Load-Store Architecture** is a processor design philosophy in which **data processing operations can operate only on operands held in CPU registers**, never directly on data located in memory. All transfers between the register file and memory must be performed **explicitly** using dedicated **Load instructions** (move data FROM memory TO register) and **Store instructions** (move data FROM register TO memory).

Mathematically, the architecture enforces the following operational constraints:

$$
\text{ALU Operations} \rightarrow f(R_n, R_m) \rightarrow R_d \quad \mid \quad \text{Memory Access} \rightarrow f(R_n, \text{Offset}) \leftrightarrow M[\text{Address}]
$$

Where:
- $f(R_n, R_m)$ represents any arithmetic/logic operation between two **registers only**.
- $M[\text{Address}]$ represents a memory location accessed via an effective address.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ARM processor is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** that follows the **Load-Store model**. This is the SINGLE most important architectural differentiator from **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** processors like the 8086, where memory operands can participate directly in arithmetic instructions (e.g., `ADD AX, [BX]`).

## 1.2 Classification of ARM Load-Store Instruction Families

The ARM instruction set contains **three principal categories** of load-store instructions, each serving a distinct data movement purpose:

| Category | Mnemonic Family | Purpose |
|:---------|:----------------|:--------|
| **Single Data Transfer** | `LDR`, `STR`, `LDRB`, `STRB`, `LDRH`, `STRH`, `LDRSB`, `LDRSH` | Move ONE data item (8/16/32-bit) between memory and a single register |
| **Multiple Data Transfer** | `LDM`, `STM` (with suffixes `IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB`, `FA`, `FD`, `EA`, `ED`) | Move a BLOCK of registers to/from memory in a single instruction |
| **Single Data Swap** | `SWP`, `SWPB` | Atomic (indivisible) exchange of a register and a memory location |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Library Counter

Imagine a **University Library** where there is a strict rule:

> **You cannot read or modify books while they sit on the shelf. You must first bring them to a study table, and after you finish, you must return them back to the shelf.**

In this analogy:
- The **Library Shelf** = Main Memory (large, slow, stores lots of data).
- The **Study Table** = CPU Register File (small, extremely fast, holds very few items).
- **Bringing a book to the table** = **Load** instruction (`LDR`).
- **Returning a book to the shelf** = **Store** instruction (`STR`).
- **Studying/Annotating the book** = Data Processing (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.) — happens **only on the table**.

A CISC architecture would be like a library where you could fold a page corner, scribble a note, and replace the book all in **one single action** (memory-to-memory operation). RISC Load-Store architecture requires **two separate trips** (Load, then Process, then Store), but each trip is **simple, fast, and pipelineable**.

## 1.4 Why Load-Store Architecture Matters in ARM

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The load-store model is what allows ARM processors to use **deep instruction pipelining** and achieve **high clock frequencies** with **low power consumption** — a key reason ARM dominates mobile and embedded markets.

Key benefits:
1. **Fixed Instruction Length**: All data processing instructions have a predictable format, simplifying decode logic.
2. **High Pipeline Efficiency**: Memory access and ALU operations can overlap across pipeline stages.
3. **Reduced Hardware Complexity**: The CPU does not need complex memory-addressing arithmetic units wired to the ALU.
4. **Lower Power**: Simpler decoding and predictable execution reduce dynamic power dissipation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Data Transfer Instructions — Anatomy

The general syntax of a single data transfer instruction is:

$$
\texttt{LDR}\mid\texttt{STR}\{cond\}\{B\}\{T\}\ \texttt{Rd},\ \texttt{[Rn},\ \text{Offset}]\{!\}
$$

Where every component plays a specific architectural role:

- `{cond}` — **Condition Code** (`EQ`, `NE`, `GT`, `LT`, `HI`, `CC`, etc.). If the condition is FALSE, the instruction executes as a **NOP** (no operation). The condition flags are set in the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** by the suffix `S` on data processing instructions.
- `{B}` — When present, transfer is a **byte** (8 bits). When absent, transfer is a **word** (32 bits).
- `{T}` — Forces the use of the **user-mode privileged** memory region (used in User mode for OS calls).
- `Rd` — The **destination register** (for `LDR`) or **source register** (for `STR`).
- `Rn` — The **base register** containing the memory address.
- `Offset` — A flexible expression: immediate constant, register, or shifted register.

## 2.2 The Four Addressing Modes — The Heart of Load-Store

This is the **highest-weightage concept** in KTU board examinations. ARM provides exactly **four addressing modes** for computing the effective memory address:

| Mode | Syntax | Effective Address | Writeback? | KTU Probability |
|:-----|:-------|:------------------|:-----------|:----------------|
| **Pre-indexed** | `[Rn, Offset]` | $EA = Rn + \text{Offset}$ | **NO** (Rn unchanged) | ★★★ |
| **Pre-indexed with Writeback** | `[Rn, Offset]!` | $EA = Rn + \text{Offset}$ | **YES** (Rn ← EA) | ★★★★★ |
| **Post-indexed** | `[Rn], Offset` | $EA = Rn$, then $Rn \leftarrow Rn + \text{Offset}$ | **YES** (after transfer) | ★★★★★ |
| **Immediate/Register Offset** | `Offset = #imm` or `Offset = Rm{, shift}` | Depends on mode | Depends on mode | ★★★★ |

### 2.2.1 The Writeback Operator `!` — A Critical Distinction

The exclamation mark `!` is called the **writeback suffix**. It instructs the processor to **automatically update the base register `Rn` with the new computed address** after the memory access is complete. This is essential for **iterative loops** (e.g., array traversal, string copy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The difference between `[Rn, #4]!` and `[Rn], #4` is subtle but frequently asked. In **pre-indexed with writeback**, the writeback happens **BEFORE** the memory access (conceptually), so `Rn` holds the new address when the instruction completes. In **post-indexed**, the original `Rn` value is used for the memory access, and the writeback happens **AFTER**.

## 2.3 The Offset Field — Three Sub-Forms

The `Offset` operand in ARM load-store instructions can be specified as:

1. **Immediate Offset**: A 12-bit unsigned constant, range `[0, 4095]`.
2. **Register Offset**: Another register `Rm` (e.g., `R5`).
3. **Scaled Register Offset**: A register `Rm` subjected to one of five shift operations:
   - `LSL` (Logical Shift Left) — multiply by $2^n$.
   - `LSR` (Logical Shift Right) — divide by $2^n$ (unsigned).
   - `ASR` (Arithmetic Shift Right) — divide by $2^n$ (signed, preserves sign bit).
   - `ROR` (Rotate Right) — bitwise rotation.
   - `RRX` (Rotate Right Extended) — rotate right through the Carry flag.

## 2.4 Multiple Data Transfer — Block Copy Primitive

The `LDM` (Load Multiple) and `STM` (Store Multiple) instructions transfer **any subset of the 16 visible registers** between memory and the register file in **a single instruction**. The 16-bit **register list field** in the instruction encodes which registers are involved.

$$
\texttt{LDM}\mid\texttt{STM}\{cond\}\{mode\}\ \texttt{Rn}\{!\},\ \{\texttt{R0}-\texttt{R15}\}^{\wedge}
$$

The `^` symbol (used only with `LDM`) indicates that the **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is copied into the **CPSR** — this is mandatory for **exception return**.

### 2.4.1 Stack Mode Suffixes (KTU High-Yield)

The stack mode suffix determines the **direction of growth** and the **order of access** — a concept drawn directly from data structures:

| Suffix | Full Form | Stack Type | Address Order | Equivalent LDM/STM |
|:-------|:----------|:-----------|:---------------|:-------------------|
| `FD` | Full Descending | Traditional ARM stack (used by `STMFD` / `LDMFD` for PUSH/POP) | High → Low | `STMDB` / `LDMIA` |
| `FA` | Full Ascending | — | Low → High | `STMIB` / `LDMDA` |
| `ED` | Empty Descending | — | High → Low | `STMDA` / `LDMIB` |
| `EA` | Empty Ascending | — | Low → High | `STMIA` / `LDMDB` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** **F**ull = Full Stack (SP points to last used address), **E**mpty = Empty Stack (SP points to first free address). **D**escending = stack grows toward **lower** addresses, **A**scending = stack grows toward **higher** addresses. The ARM default stack is **Full Descending (`FD`)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all load-store effective address formulas** that the KTU 2024 board examiner expects students to memorize:

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variable Meaning | Units / Notes |
|:--------|:---------------|:-----------------|:--------------|
| Pre-indexed EA | $EA = Rn + \text{Offset}$ | Effective address | Bytes, $Rn$ unchanged |
| Pre-indexed WB EA | $EA = Rn + \text{Offset}$, then $Rn \leftarrow EA$ | Same as above + writeback | Detected by `!` suffix |
| Post-indexed EA | $EA = Rn$, then $Rn \leftarrow Rn + \text{Offset}$ | Original $Rn$ used for access | No `!` suffix needed |
| LSL Shifted Offset | $\text{Offset} = R_m \ll n = R_m \cdot 2^{n}$ | Logical shift left by $n$ bits | $n \in [1, 31]$ |
| LSR Shifted Offset | $\text{Offset} = R_m \gg n = \lfloor R_m / 2^{n} \rfloor$ | Logical shift right | $n \in [1, 32]$ |
| ASR Shifted Offset | $\text{Offset} = R_m \div 2^{n}$ (sign-extended) | Arithmetic shift right | Preserves sign bit |
| Byte Transfer Size | $N_{bits} = 8$ | B suffix active | $N_{bytes} = 1$ |
| Half-word Transfer Size | $N_{bits} = 16$ | H suffix active | $N_{bytes} = 2$ |
| Word Transfer Size | $N_{bits} = 32$ | No B/H suffix | $N_{bytes} = 4$ |
| Word Address Alignment | $\text{Address} \equiv 0 \pmod{4}$ | Required for `LDR`/`STR` | Faults if misaligned |
| Half-word Alignment | $\text{Address} \equiv 0 \pmod{2}$ | Required for `LDRH`/`STRH` | Faults if misaligned |
| Block Copy Cycles | $N_{cycles} = \lceil N_{regs} \cdot 4 / B_{bus} \rceil$ | $B_{bus}$ = bus width | Memory bandwidth limit |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Load-Store instructions are the **workhorse** of nearly every embedded system built on ARM:

- **RTOS Context Switching**: When the OS preempts a task, it uses `STMFD` to save all task registers onto the task's stack and `LDMFD` to restore them. This is the **load-store block copy** in action.
- **DMA Engine Programming**: The CPU programs DMA controllers by writing control blocks to memory using `STR` instructions, then triggers the transfer.
- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**: FIR filters use `LDM` to load a window of samples, then looped data processing operates on registers.
- **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs)**: Entry saves context with `STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}` and exit restores with `LDMFD SP!, {R0-R12, PC}^` — the `^` returns to the interrupted instruction and restores CPSR from SPSR.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Pre-Indexed vs Post-Indexed

**Given:** Initial register state $R1 = 0x00002000$, $R2 = 0x12345678$. Memory at address $0x00002004$ contains the value $0xAABBCCDD$.

**Evaluate the following instruction sequence:**

```assembly
LDR   R0, [R1, #4]      ; (A) Pre-indexed, NO writeback
STR   R2, [R1], #4      ; (B) Post-indexed, with writeback
LDR   R3, [R1, #4]      ; (C) Pre-indexed, NO writeback
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of Instruction (A)

**Instruction:** `LDR R0, [R1, #4]`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (EA)} &= R1 + \text{Offset} \\
EA &= 0x00002000 + 4 \\
EA &= 0x00002004 \quad \text{(Address of source memory)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0 &\leftarrow M[0x00002004] \\
R0 &\leftarrow 0xAABBCCDD \quad \text{(32-bit word loaded)} \\
R1 &\text{ remains UNCHANGED} = 0x00002000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of Instruction (B)

**Instruction:** `STR R2, [R1], #4`

This is **post-indexed**. The syntax `[Rn], Offset` means the address used for memory access is **solely Rn** (the offset is NOT added to form the address — it is added to Rn AFTER the access for writeback).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (EA)} &= R1 \quad \text{(Offset is NOT used to form EA)} \\
EA &= 0x00002000 \\
M[0x00002000] &\leftarrow R2 \\
M[0x00002000] &\leftarrow 0x12345678 \\
R1 &\leftarrow R1 + 4 \quad \text{(Writeback occurs AFTER store)} \\
R1 &= 0x00002004
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of Instruction (C)

**Instruction:** `LDR R3, [R1, #4]`

After instruction (B), $R1 = 0x00002004$. Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (EA)} &= R1 + 4 \\
EA &= 0x00002004 + 4 \\
EA &= 0x00002008 \\
R3 &\leftarrow M[0x00002008]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Register State Summary

| Register | Final Value | Hex Value | Source / Operation |
|:---------|:------------|:----------|:-------------------|
| $R0$ | $0xAABBCCDD$ | $0xAABBCCDD$ | Loaded from `0x2004` in step (A) |
| $R1$ | $0x00002004$ | $0x00002004$ | Unchanged in (A), updated in (B) |
| $R2$ | $0x12345678$ | $0x12345678$ | Unchanged (source for store) |
| $R3$ | $M[0x00002008]$ | Depends on memory | Loaded from `0x2008` in step (C) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Instruction (B) used the ORIGINAL value of $R1$ (`0x2000`) to write to memory, but updated $R1$ to `0x2004` for the next loop iteration. This is the standard idiom for **post-increment array traversal** in ARM assembly.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Pre-Indexed WITH Writeback

**Given:** $R4 = 0x00008000$, $R5 = 0x0000000A$ (value to be stored), memory is initialised to zero.

**Evaluate:**

```assembly
STR   R5, [R4, #8]!     ; Pre-indexed with writeback
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (EA)} &= R4 + 8 \quad \text{(pre-indexed computation)} \\
EA &= 0x00008000 + 8 \\
EA &= 0x00008008 \\
M[0x00008008] &\leftarrow R5 = 0x0000000A \quad \text{(memory write performed)} \\
R4 &\leftarrow EA \quad \text{(writeback happens due to `!` suffix)} \\
R4 &= 0x00008008
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparative Truth Table

| Instruction | EA Used for Memory Access | Final Value of Base Register |
|:------------|:---------------------------|:------------------------------|
| `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` | $R1 + 4$ | $R1$ **unchanged** |
| `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` | $R1 + 4$ | $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$ |
| `LDR R0, [R1], #4` | $R1$ | $R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$ |
| `LDR R0, [R1]` | $R1$ | $R1$ **unchanged** |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Scaled Register Offset

**Given:** $R7 = 0x00010000$ (base), $R8 = 0x00000010$, and we want to access an array of 32-bit integers at index 3.

**Evaluate:** `LDR R9, [R7, R8, LSL #2]`

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= R8 \text{ shifted left by 2} \\
&= R8 \ll 2 \\
&= 0x00000010 \ll 2 \\
&= 0x00000040 \quad \text{(equivalent to } R8 \times 2^{2} = 0x10 \times 4 = 0x40) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Address (EA)} &= R7 + \text{Offset} \\
EA &= 0x00010000 + 0x00000040 \\
EA &= 0x00010040 \\
R9 &\leftarrow M[0x00010040]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The expression `[R7, R8, LSL #2]` is the canonical ARM idiom for computing `&array[index]` for a 32-bit integer array. Because each element occupies 4 bytes, multiplying the index (`R8`) by 4 via `LSL #2` produces the correct byte offset. This single instruction replaces the "multiply then add" sequence required on simpler architectures.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Block Copy Using Multiple Data Transfer

**Scenario:** Copy 4 registers (R0, R1, R2, R3) from a source memory area to a destination memory area.

**Given:** $R10 = 0x00002000$ (source), $R11 = 0x00003000$ (destination), $M[0x2000] = 0x11111111$, $M[0x2004] = 0x22222222$, $M[0x2008] = 0x33333333$, $M[0x200C] = 0x44444444$.

**Instruction sequence:**

```assembly
LDMIA R10!, {R0-R3}     ; Load 4 registers, increment after
STMIA R11!, {R0-R3}     ; Store 4 registers, increment after
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1: } & M[0x2000] \rightarrow R0, \quad R10 \leftarrow 0x2004 \\
\text{Iteration 2: } & M[0x2004] \rightarrow R1, \quad R10 \leftarrow 0x2008 \\
\text{Iteration 3: } & M[0x2008] \rightarrow R2, \quad R10 \leftarrow 0x200C \\
\text{Iteration 4: } & M[0x200C] \rightarrow R3, \quad R10 \leftarrow 0x2010 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

After `LDMIA`, the register state is: $R0 = 0x11111111$, $R1 = 0x22222222$, $R2 = 0x33333333$, $R3 = 0x44444444$, $R10 = 0x00002010$.

The subsequent `STMIA R11!, {R0-R3}` performs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M[0x3000] &\leftarrow R0 = 0x11111111, \quad R11 \leftarrow 0x3004 \\
M[0x3004] &\leftarrow R1 = 0x22222222, \quad R11 \leftarrow 0x3008 \\
M[0x3008] &\leftarrow R2 = 0x33333333, \quad R11 \leftarrow 0x300C \\
M[300C] &\leftarrow R3 = 0x44444444, \quad R11 \leftarrow 0x3010 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total memory operations: 8 (4 loads + 4 stores), executed in 2 instructions.**

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Each Instruction

The following table is a **decision matrix** that KTU examiners expect students to internalize:

| Situation | Recommended Instruction | Reason |
|:----------|:------------------------|:-------|
| Single word access, no pointer update | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | Simple, no side effect on $Rn$ |
| Loop over an array, index increment | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #4` | Post-index auto-advances pointer |
| Stack push of one item | `STR Rd, [SP, #-4]!` | Pre-indexed with writeback (decrement SP) |
| Stack pop of one item | `LDR Rd, [SP], #4` | Post-indexed (read then increment SP) |
| Save multiple registers on context switch | `STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}` | Block save, full descending |
| Restore context and return from ISR | `LDMFD SP!, {R0-R12, PC}^` | Block load + exception return |
| Atomic test-and-set for mutex | `SWP R0, R1, [R2]` | Single-instruction indivisible exchange |
| Access byte from packed struct | `LDRB Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | 8-bit transfer, zero-extended to 32 bits |
| Access 16-bit value, sign-extend to 32 bits | `LDRSH Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | Useful for signed short integers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM Load-Store Pipeline Dataflow (Block-Level Architecture)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates how the ARM datapath routes data during the EXECUTE stage of a load or store instruction. Each numbered block represents a functional unit inside the CPU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch Stage] --> B[Instruction Decode Stage]
    B --> C{Is this a Load or Store?}
    C -- "Load LDR" --> D[Compute Effective Address]
    C -- "Store STR" --> D
    D --> E[Address Register Rn plus Offset]
    E --> F{Address to Memory Bus}
    F -- "Load Path" --> G[Memory Read from M of EA]
    F -- "Store Path" --> H[Memory Write M of EA from Rd]
    G --> I[Writeback to Destination Register Rd]
    H --> J[Writeback to Base Register Rn if Writeback flag set]
    I --> K[Register File Updated]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Next Instruction in Pipeline]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct Addressing Mode

This Mermaid flowchart guides a programmer (or an examiner's logic check) through the **mental process** of selecting the right addressing mode for a given scenario.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to access memory?]) --> Q1{Is base address updated automatically?}
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{Is offset added to base BEFORE access?}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q3{Is offset added to base BEFORE access?}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> M1[Pre-indexed: LDR Rd, Rn comma offset]
    Q2 -- "No" --> M2[Post-indexed: LDR Rd, Rn offset]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> M3[Pre-indexed WB: LDR Rd, Rn offset exclamation]
    Q3 -- "No" --> M2
    M1 --> Done([Instruction assembled])
    M2 --> Done
    M3 --> Done
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Memory-to-Register and Register-to-Memory Flow

The following diagram is the **canonical schematic** that KTU board examiners draw to test a student's understanding of the load-store boundary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Core"]
        RF[Register File 16 by 32-bit]
        ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    end
    subgraph BUS["System Bus"]
        ABus[Address Bus 32-bit]
        DBus[Data Bus 32-bit]
        Ctl[Control Signals RD and WR]
    end
    subgraph MEM["Main Memory"]
        Mem[Memory Array 4 GB address space]
    end
    RF -- "Operand A and Operand B" --> ALU
    ALU -- "Result" --> RF
    RF -- "Address" --> ABus
    RF -- "Write Data for STR" --> DBus
    ABus --> Mem
    Mem -- "Read Data for LDR" --> DBus
    Ctl --> Mem
    ALU -. "Effective Address Calculation EA equals Rn plus Offset" .-> ABus
```

## 4.4 Instruction Format (32-bit Encoding Schematic)

The ARM single data transfer instruction is encoded as a **32-bit word**. The following Mermaid block visualizes the bit-field layout that students should be familiar with at a conceptual level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bits 31 to 28 Condition cond] --> B[Bits 27 to 26 01 Load Store opcode]
    B --> C[Bit 25 Immediate 0 or Register 1 offset]
    C --> D[Bit 24 Pre index 0 or 1 plus Writeback bit 21 P and W]
    D --> E[Bit 23 Up offset 0 or Down offset 1]
    E --> F[Bit 22 Byte 0 or Word 1 transfer size B]
    F --> G[Bit 21 Writeback W 0 or 1]
    G --> H[Bit 20 Load 1 or Store 0 L bit]
    H --> I[Bits 19 to 16 Base Register Rn]
    I --> J[Bits 15 to 12 Destination Rd for LDR or Source Rd for STR]
    J --> K[Bits 11 to 0 12-bit Offset or Shift specification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for the diagram:** The bit-fields from MSB (bit 31) to LSB (bit 0) of the 32-bit ARM instruction word are: **Cond (4 bits) → 01 (opcode, 2 bits) → I (1) → P (1) → U (1) → B (1) → W (1) → L (1) → Rn (4) → Rd (4) → Offset (12)**. The `I`, `P`, `U`, `B`, `W`, `L` bits collectively determine the addressing mode, transfer direction, and size.

## 4.5 Diagram: Stack Operations using Load-Store Multiple

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the Full Descending stack convention used by ARM for the `STMFD` / `LDMFD` pair.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Mem["Memory Address Space"]
        M1[Address SP plus 12 Slot 3]
        M2[Address SP plus 8 Slot 2]
        M3[Address SP plus 4 Slot 1]
        M4[Address SP Slot 0 Last Pushed]
    end
    subgraph Reg["Register File"]
        R0[R0]
        R1[R1]
        R2[R2]
        R3[R3]
    end
    R0 -- "STMFD SP exclamation" --> M1
    R1 -- "STMFD" --> M2
    R2 -- "STMFD" --> M3
    R3 -- "STMFD" --> M4
    M1 -- "LDMFD SP exclamation" --> R0
    M2 -- "LDMFD" --> R1
    M3 -- "LDMFD" --> R2
    M4 -- "LDMFD" --> R3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between Load-Store architecture and Register-Memory architecture with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Load-Store (RISC, e.g., ARM) | Register-Memory (CISC, e.g., 8086) |
|:--------|:------------------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| **Memory operands in ALU** | **Not allowed**; ALU operates only on registers | **Allowed**; ALU can directly use memory operands |
| **Instruction complexity** | Simple, fixed-length, single-cycle ideally | Complex, variable-length, multi-cycle |
| **Example** | `LDR R0, [R1]; ADD R0, R0, R2` (two instructions) | `ADD AX, [BX]` (single instruction) |
| **Pipelining** | Highly efficient, deep pipelines | Limited by variable instruction timing |
| **Code size** | Larger (more instructions) | Smaller (fewer instructions) |

**Example to illustrate:** To add a constant to a memory value, ARM requires:
```assembly
LDR R0, [R1]      ; Load memory into register
ADD R0, R0, #5    ; Add constant in register
STR R0, [R1]      ; Store result back to memory
```
whereas **8086** achieves the same with one instruction: `ADD WORD PTR [BX], 5`.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the difference between `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` and `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` with reference to the base register update.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental difference lies in the **writeback** of the base register `R1`:

- **`LDR R0, [R1, #4]`** — This is **Pre-indexed addressing without writeback**. The effective address is computed as $EA = R1 + 4$, the word at $EA$ is loaded into $R0$, and **$R1$ remains unchanged**.

- **`LDR R0, [R1, #4]!`** — This is **Pre-indexed addressing with writeback** (denoted by the `!` suffix). The effective address is computed as $EA = R1 + 4$, the word at $EA$ is loaded into $R0$, and **$R1$ is automatically updated to $EA$** ($R1 \leftarrow R1 + 4$).

**Numerical illustration:** If $R1 = 0x1000$ and $M[0x1004] = 0xAA$:

| Instruction | $R0$ After | $R1$ After |
|:------------|:-----------|:-----------|
| `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` | $0xAA$ | $0x1000$ (unchanged) |
| `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` | $0xAA$ | $0x1004$ (updated) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the four addressing modes of ARM Load/Store instructions with neat syntax and effective address calculation. Show how each mode affects the base register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following ARM instruction sequence. The initial register state is: $R0 = 0x40000000$, $R1 = 0x00000020$, $R2 = 0x00000005$, $R3 = 0x0000000C$. The memory contents are given below:

| Address | Value |
|:--------|:------|
| $0x40000020$ | $0xA1B2C3D4$ |
| $0x40000024$ | $0x55667788$ |
| $0x40000028$ | $0xDEADBEEF$ |
| $0x4000002C$ | $0x12345678$ |

Evaluate the sequence and find the final state of $R0$, $R1$, $R2$, $R3$, $R4$, and $R5$:

```assembly
LDR   R4, [R0, R1]          ; Instruction 1
LDR   R5, [R0, R1, LSL #2]  ; Instruction 2
STR   R2, [R0], #4          ; Instruction 3
STR   R3, [R0, #8]!         ; Instruction 4
``` **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The four ARM load-store addressing modes are:

**(i) Pre-Indexed Addressing:** Syntax `LDR Rd, [Rn, Offset]`. The offset is added to the base register **before** the memory access, but the base register is **not updated**.

$$
EA = Rn + \text{Offset}, \quad Rn \text{ is unchanged}
$$

**(ii) Pre-Indexed Addressing with Writeback:** Syntax `LDR Rd, [Rn, Offset]!`. The offset is added to the base register **before** the memory access, **and** the base register is updated with the new effective address (writeback).

$$
EA = Rn + \text{Offset}, \quad Rn \leftarrow EA
$$

**(iii) Post-Indexed Addressing:** Syntax `LDR Rd, [Rn], Offset`. The base register value is used **directly** as the memory address, and the offset is added to the base register **after** the memory access.

$$
EA = Rn, \quad Rn \leftarrow Rn + \text{Offset}
$$

**(iv) Immediate or Register Offset:** The offset can be a 12-bit immediate constant (`#imm`) or a register `Rm` (optionally shifted). The shift can be LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, or RRX with a 5-bit shift amount.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Instruction 1: `LDR R4, [R0, R1]`** (Pre-indexed, register offset, no writeback)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R0 + R1 \\
EA &= 0x40000000 + 0x00000020 \\
EA &= 0x40000020 \\
R4 &\leftarrow M[0x40000020] = 0xA1B2C3D4
\end{aligned}
$$

$R0$ remains $0x40000000$, $R1$ remains $0x00000020$.

**Instruction 2: `LDR R5, [R0, R1, LSL #2]`** (Pre-indexed, scaled register offset)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= R1 \ll 2 = 0x00000020 \times 4 = 0x00000080 \\
EA &= R0 + 0x00000080 \\
EA &= 0x40000000 + 0x00000080 \\
EA &= 0x40000080 \quad \text{(outside given memory map)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0x40000080$ is outside the table provided, we assume it contains an unknown value; the key point is that $R5$ receives $M[0x40000080]$ and $R0, R1$ are unchanged.

**Instruction 3: `STR R2, [R0], #4`** (Post-indexed with writeback)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R0 = 0x40000000 \quad \text{(original R0 used for address)} \\
M[0x40000000] &\leftarrow R2 = 0x00000005 \\
R0 &\leftarrow R0 + 4 \\
R0 &= 0x40000004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Instruction 4: `STR R3, [R0, #8]!`** (Pre-indexed with writeback)

Now $R0 = 0x40000004$ (from previous step).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R0 + 8 = 0x40000004 + 8 = 0x4000000C \\
M[0x4000000C] &\leftarrow R3 = 0x0000000C \\
R0 &\leftarrow EA = 0x4000000C
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Final Register State

| Register | Final Value | Hex Value | Reason |
|:---------|:------------|:----------|:-------|
| $R0$ | $17,825,292$ | $0x4000000C$ | Updated twice: $+4$ (Instruction 3), then overwritten by $EA$ in Instruction 4 |
| $R1$ | $32$ | $0x00000020$ | Unchanged in all instructions |
| $R2$ | $5$ | $0x00000005$ | Source for store, unchanged |
| $R3$ | $12$ | $0x0000000C$ | Source for store, unchanged |
| $R4$ | $2{,}713{,}572{,}820$ | $0xA1B2C3D4$ | Loaded from `0x40000020` |
| $R5$ | $M[0x40000080]$ | $M[0x40000080]$ | Loaded from `0x40000080` (unknown) |

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the ARM Load-Store architecture. Describe the role of the three instruction families: Single Data Transfer (`LDR`/`STR`), Multiple Data Transfer (`LDM`/`STM`), and Single Data Swap (`SWP`). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an ARM assembly code snippet to copy a 5-element integer array from a source location pointed by `R0` to a destination location pointed by `R1` using block transfer instructions. Show the register state and memory map before and after execution. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The **ARM Load-Store architecture** mandates a strict separation between **data processing** (register-to-register operations) and **data transfer** (register-to-memory operations). The CPU contains a **register file** of 16 visible 32-bit registers (`R0`–`R12`, `R13`/`SP`, `R14`/`LR`, `R15`/`PC`) and a **barrel shifter** in the datapath that performs shifts as part of the addressing calculation.

**Three instruction families:**

**(i) Single Data Transfer (`LDR`, `STR`, and variants):** Move a single 8-bit (`B`), 16-bit (`H`), or 32-bit (default) data item between memory and a single register. Supports pre-indexed, pre-indexed with writeback, and post-indexed addressing. Used for scalar variable access and stack push/pop.

**(ii) Multiple Data Transfer (`LDM`, `STM`):** Move a **block** of registers (any subset of the 16 registers, encoded in a 16-bit register list) between memory and the register file in a single instruction. Used for context switching, block copy, and stack frame management. The mode suffix (`IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB`, `FA`, `FD`, `EA`, `ED`) controls the addressing order and stack direction.

**(iii) Single Data Swap (`SWP`, `SWPB`):** Performs an **atomic (indivisible)** exchange of a register value with a memory location. During a single bus transaction, the processor reads from memory, writes the source register to memory, and returns the original memory value in the destination register. Used to implement semaphores, mutexes, and other synchronization primitives in multiprocessor systems.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Problem Statement:** Copy 5 integer words from source `[R0]` to destination `[R1]`.

**ARM Assembly Code:**

```assembly
; Assume R0 = 0x00002000 (source base)
; Assume R1 = 0x00003000 (destination base)
; Source array occupies 0x2000 to 0x2010
; Destination array occupies 0x3000 to 0x3010

        LDMIA   R0, {R2-R6}       ; Load 5 source words into R2-R6
        STMIA   R1, {R2-R6}       ; Store them into destination
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

The `LDMIA R0, {R2-R6}` instruction performs 5 successive word loads starting at the address in `R0`, incrementing the address by 4 after each load (IA = Increment After). Note that we deliberately omit the `!` writeback suffix so that `R0` retains the source base address (a common idiom for non-destructive reads).

| Cycle | Address Accessed | Destination Register | Address Increment |
|:------|:-----------------|:---------------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $M[0x2000]$ | $R2$ | next $= 0x2004$ |
| 2 | $M[0x2004]$ | $R3$ | next $= 0x2008$ |
| 3 | $M[0x2008]$ | $R4$ | next $= 0x200C$ |
| 4 | $M[0x200C]$ | $R5$ | next $= 0x2010$ |
| 5 | $M[0x2010]$ | $R6$ | end |

After `LDMIA`, the register file holds the five source values. The subsequent `STMIA R1, {R2-R6}` writes these five registers to consecutive addresses starting at `[R1] = 0x3000`.

| Cycle | Source Register | Destination Address |
|:------|:----------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | $R2$ | $M[0x3000] \leftarrow R2$ |
| 2 | $R3$ | $M[0x3004] \leftarrow R3$ |
| 3 | $R4$ | $M[0x3008] \leftarrow R4$ |
| 4 | $R5$ | $M[0x300C] \leftarrow R5$ |
| 5 | $R6$ | $M[0x3010] \leftarrow R6$ |

**Memory Map After Execution:**

| Source Address | Value | | Destination Address | Value |
|:---------------|:------|:--|:--------------------|:------|
| $0x2000$ | $R2$ (unchanged) | | $0x3000$ | $R2$ |
| $0x2004$ | $R3$ (unchanged) | | $0x3004$ | $R3$ |
| $0x2008$ | $R4$ (unchanged) | | $0x3008$ | $R4$ |
| $0x200C$ | $R5$ (unchanged) | | $0x300C$ | $R5$ |
| $0x2010$ | $R6$ (unchanged) | | $0x3010$ | $R6$ |

The source array remains intact (non-destructive `LDMIA` without `!`), and the destination array now holds an exact replica of the source — accomplished in just **2 instructions** (vs 10 instructions for a sequence of `LDR`/`STR` pairs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `!` distinction:** Many students write `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` and claim $R1$ is updated. The `!` suffix is **mandatory** for writeback in pre-indexed mode. Marks lost: **1–2 per occurrence**.
> 2. **Confusing `IA` with `IB`:** `IA` = Increment **After** (address used first, then incremented). `IB` = Increment **Before** (address incremented first, then used). Conflating these causes wrong memory addresses in block transfers. Marks lost: **2–3 per occurrence**.
> 3. **Misinterpreting `[Rn], Offset`:** The `Offset` in post-indexed mode is **NOT** part of the effective address. It is added to `Rn` for writeback **only after** the access. Marks lost: **2 per question**.
> 4. **Ignoring the suffix `S` in data-processing flags:** Load-store instructions do **not** set the condition flags (that is a data-processing feature). Marks lost: **1** if falsely claimed.
> 5. **Address alignment errors:** Forgetting that `LDR`/`STR` require 4-byte alignment. Marks lost: **1–2** if misaligned address is used without comment.
> 6. **Confusing Full Descending with Empty Descending:** In `FD`, the SP points to the last item pushed. In `ED`, the SP points to the first **free** slot. Marks lost: **2** if the final SP value is computed wrongly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Load-Store architecture mandates that the **ALU can only access registers**, never memory directly. All memory-to-register or register-to-memory moves require explicit `LDR`/`STR` instructions.
- **Three Instruction Families:** Single Data Transfer (`LDR`/`STR`), Multiple Data Transfer (`LDM`/`STM`), Single Data Swap (`SWP`).
- **Four Addressing Modes:** Pre-indexed, Pre-indexed with Writeback (`!`), Post-indexed, and the generic Immediate/Register Offset — all of which differ in **when** the base register is updated relative to the memory access.
- **Writeback Suffix `!`:** When present, the base register `Rn` is automatically updated with the effective address. When absent, `Rn` is preserved.
- **Post-Indexed `[Rn], Offset`:** Uses the **original** `Rn` as the address and updates `Rn` only **after** the access. The `!` suffix is **not used** here — the writeback is implicit.
- **Suffix Code Mapping:** `B` = byte, `H` = half-word, `T` = user-mode, `S` (in `LDRSH`/`LDRSB`) = sign-extend, no suffix = word.
- **Stack Mode Suffixes:** `FD` (Full Descending, ARM default), `ED` (Empty Descending), `FA` (Full Ascending), `EA` (Empty Ascending). The `^` symbol after the register list on `LDM` copies SPSR → CPSR (exception return).
- **Block Transfer Direction:** `IA` = Increment After, `IB` = Increment Before, `DA` = Decrement After, `DB` = Decrement Before.
- **Alignment Rules:** Word access = 4-byte aligned, half-word access = 2-byte aligned, byte access = any address.
- **Effective Address Formula (Pre-indexed):** $EA = Rn \pm \text{Offset}$, with sign determined by the **U bit** (1 = up/add, 0 = down/subtract).
- **Scaled Register Offset:** `Rm, LSL #n` means $R_m \cdot 2^{n}$. This is the canonical idiom for array indexing of 32-bit words (`LSL #2` = multiply by 4).
- **Engineering Relevance:** Load-store instructions form the basis of **context switching in RTOS**, **interrupt service routines**, **DMA programming**, and **DSP kernels** — making this topic a recurring high-weightage item in both KTU theory exams and embedded systems lab viva voce.
- **Exam-Favourite Problem Types:** (i) Tracing register/memory state after a sequence of `LDR`/`STR` instructions; (ii) distinguishing pre-indexed, post-indexed, and writeback variants; (iii) writing block-copy code using `LDM`/`STM`; (iv) explaining the role of `SWP` in mutual exclusion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Interrupt Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Interrupt Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor architecture**, **Software Interrupt Instructions** are a class of *Program Control* (or *Control Transfer*) instructions that explicitly invoke the 8086 interrupt mechanism from within a running program. They cause the processor to suspend the current instruction stream, push the **Flag Register (FR)**, **Code Segment (CS)**, and **Instruction Pointer (IP)** onto the stack, and branch execution to a predefined **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** whose 4-byte (IP:CS) address is fetched from the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** located in the first **1 KB (00000H – 003FFH)** of physical memory.

The four software interrupt instructions defined in the 8086 instruction set are:

1. **`INT n`** — Type-n software interrupt ($n = 0$ to $255$)
2. **`INT 3`** — One-byte breakpoint interrupt (Type 3)
3. **`INTO`** — Interrupt on overflow (Type 4)
4. **`BOUND reg16, mem32`** — Check array bounds (Triggers Type 5 on violation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic falls under **Module 4 – 8086 Instruction Set & Programming Model**, sub-unit *Control Transfer Instructions / Interrupts*. It is a **high-weightage topic** (frequently appears for 7–14 marks in End Semester Examinations).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Emergency Bell" of the CPU

Imagine the 8086 CPU as a **chef working in a busy restaurant kitchen** (executing your program). The chef is busy following a recipe line-by-line (sequential fetch–decode–execute). Now consider four "emergency bells" that can ring and force the chef to stop, save his exact place in the recipe, walk over to a **wall-mounted instruction card** (the Interrupt Vector Table), and execute a special procedure:

- **`INT n`** → A waiter punches a custom bell labeled *n* on the wall, and the chef follows card number $n$.
- **`INT 3`** → A special, tiny single-button bell always labeled "**3**" used by the head chef (debugger) to pause the recipe.
- **`INTO`** → A tripwire bell that rings **only if** the last arithmetic dish overflowed the kitchen counter (Overflow Flag = 1).
- **`BOUND`** → A picture-frame bell that rings if a labeled dish number (index) is **outside** the allowed range of menu items.

In all four cases, the chef:
1. Notes the current line and chapter of the recipe (pushes IP, CS).
2. Notes the current kitchen conditions (pushes Flags).
3. Walks to card $n$ in the Interrupt Vector Table.
4. Executes that card's instructions.
5. Eventually, returns ("**IRET**") and resumes the original recipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Constant to Remember:** The 8086 IVT contains **256 entries × 4 bytes = 1024 bytes (1 KB)** of fixed physical memory at the address range **00000H – 003FFH**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Software Interrupt Instructions — Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 `INT n` — Type-n Software Interrupt (2-byte instruction)

**Opcode Format:** `11001101 00000000 nnnnnnnn` (CDH followed by the type byte)

**Operational Steps (Why & How):**
- **Step 1 — PUSH Flags:** The 16-bit Flag Register (with TF=0, IF=0 set internally) is pushed to the stack. **Why:** To preserve CPU status and to disable further maskable interrupts during ISR execution.
- **Step 2 — PUSH CS:** The current Code Segment is pushed. **Why:** Required to restore the calling program segment after `IRET`.
- **Step 3 — PUSH IP:** The current Instruction Pointer is pushed. **Why:** Required to resume at the instruction following `INT n`.
- **Step 4 — Fetch New IP & CS:** The processor reads the 4-byte vector from physical address $4n$:

$$
\text{IP} \leftarrow \text{MEM}\big[4n \big], \quad \text{CS} \leftarrow \text{MEM}\big[4n + 2 \big]
$$

- **Step 5 — IF ← 0, TF ← 0:** Interrupt-Enable and Trap flags are cleared, disabling maskable hardware interrupts and single-step traps while in the ISR.

#### 2.1.2 `INT 3` — Breakpoint Interrupt (1-byte instruction = `CCH`)

- A **special optimized 1-byte form** of `INT 3`, allowing the debugger to insert it anywhere in code without disturbing other instruction alignments.
- Reserved exclusively as a **software breakpoint** for tools like DEBUG, CodeView, MASM, and modern emulators.
- Functionally identical to `INT 3` (which itself is 2 bytes: `CD 03`).
- Often called the **"Debug Trap"** instruction.

#### 2.1.3 `INTO` — Interrupt on Overflow (1-byte instruction = `CEH`)

- Conditionally invokes **Type 4 interrupt** (overflow service routine).
- **The 'Why':** Just after a signed arithmetic operation (e.g., `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `IMUL`), the Overflow Flag (OF) is set if the result exceeds the signed 16-bit range $-32768$ to $+32767$.
- If `OF = 1` → execute INT 4 (jump to IVT entry at $4 \times 4 = 16$).
- If `OF = 0` → no operation (NOP-like behavior).
- The typical pattern in KTU programming: `signed_op ; INTO`.

#### 2.1.4 `BOUND reg16, mem32` — Array Bound Check (2-byte instruction + addressing)

- Compares the **signed 16-bit value** in the register against **two signed 16-bit limits** stored in memory as a pair: `[lower_bound, upper_bound]`.
- **The 'How':**
  - If $reg < \text{lower\_bound}$ **OR** $reg > \text{upper\_bound}$ → trigger **Type 5 interrupt** (BOUND range exceeded).
  - Otherwise → continue to the next instruction.
- **Critical Limitation:** The register operand is **always 16-bit**, and the memory operand is **always 32-bit** (lower:upper). It is **invalid for 8-bit or 32-bit operands**.
- **The 'Why':** Used as a defensive programming guard to prevent buffer overruns—analogous to modern languages' runtime array-bound checks.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Symbol | Formula or Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) base address | $\text{00000H}$ | 1 KB block in physical memory |
| IVT end address | $\text{003FFH}$ | Last byte of IVT |
| Total IVT size | $256 \times 4 = 1024$ | Bytes (1 KB) |
| Vector address for type $n$ | $\text{Physical} = 4n$ | In bytes (multiply type by 4) |
| IP (offset of ISR) | $\text{IP} = \text{MEM}\big[4n \big]$ | Low word of vector |
| CS (segment of ISR) | $\text{CS} = \text{MEM}\big[4n+2 \big]$ | High word of vector |
| Stack growth (per push) | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2$ | Each push is 2 bytes |
| Total stack bytes used | $3 \times 2 = 6$ bytes | Flags + CS + IP (3 word pushes) |
| INT 3 opcode length | 1 byte | Unique: `CCH` |
| INT $n$ opcode length | 2 bytes | `CDH` + type byte |
| INTO opcode length | 1 byte | `CEH` |
| BOUND register width | 16-bit (word) | $reg = \text{AX, BX, CX, DX, ...}$ |
| BOUND memory width | 32-bit (dword) | Lower bound + upper bound |
| Flags after INT/INTO/BOUND | IF = 0, TF = 0 | Others unchanged |
| Type-3 reserved vector | Address $0000\text{CH}$ | INT 3 breakpoint |
| Type-4 reserved vector | Address $00010\text{H}$ | INTO overflow |
| Type-5 reserved vector | Address $00014\text{H}$ | BOUND violation |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes (`\vert`) are used inside the table above** to preserve markdown rendering. All set/bound notation uses standard English words.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Software Interrupts |
|---|---|
| **Operating Systems (DOS, BIOS)** | DOS function calls (e.g., `INT 21H`), BIOS services (e.g., `INT 10H` for video, `INT 13H` for disk, `INT 16H` for keyboard) |
| **Embedded Systems Debugging** | `INT 3` breakpoints for in-circuit emulators and JTAG debuggers |
| **High-Level Language Runtimes** | Compilers emit `INTO` after signed arithmetic; C/C++ array access translates to `BOUND` checks |
| **Real-Time OS Kernels** | Software-triggered system calls (SysCall) — modern 8086 descendants use `SYSCALL`/`SYSENTER` |
| **Safety-Critical Systems** | `BOUND` ensures memory access safety in aerospace/automotive control loops |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Vector Address Calculation for `INT n`

Let us rigorously derive the physical address from which the 8086 fetches the new IP and CS values when executing the instruction `INT n`, where $n$ is the 8-bit type byte.

**Given:**
- Type number $n$ is an 8-bit unsigned integer, $0 \le n \le 255$.
- The IVT begins at physical address $00000\text{H}$.
- Each IVT entry occupies exactly **4 bytes**: 2 bytes for IP (offset), 2 bytes for CS (segment).
- The physical memory model of 8086: a 20-bit address formed as $\text{PA} = (\text{CS} \ll 4) + \text{IP}$.

**Derivation:**

Each IVT entry is **4 bytes wide**. The 8086 contains a hardware multiplier that computes the byte-offset of the $n$-th entry by left-shifting $n$ two positions (equivalent to multiplying by 4):

$$
\text{Base\_Offset} = n \times 4 = n \ll 2
$$

The low word of the vector (2 bytes) at this base offset becomes the new IP:

$$
\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \big[\text{MEM}\big(00000\text{H} + (n \times 4)\big)\big]
$$

The high word of the vector (next 2 bytes) becomes the new CS:

$$
\text{CS}_{\text{new}} = \big[\text{MEM}\big(00000\text{H} + (n \times 4) + 2\big)\big]
$$

**Verification of the physical ISR address:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA}_{\text{ISR}} &= (\text{CS}_{\text{new}} \ll 4) + \text{IP}_{\text{new}} \\
&= \big[\text{MEM}(4n + 2)\big] \times 16 + \big[\text{MEM}(4n)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example — `INT 21H` (DOS Function Call):**

Let $n = 21\text{H} = 33_{10}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base\_Offset} &= 21\text{H} \times 4 \\
&= 33 \times 4 \\
&= 132_{10} \\
&= 84\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the 4-byte vector for INT 21H resides in the IVT at physical addresses:
- $\text{PA} = 00084\text{H}$ (low word) → contains IP of DOS dispatcher.
- $\text{PA} = 00086\text{H}$ (high word) → contains CS of DOS dispatcher.

**Step-by-step stack operations during `INT n` execution:**

Let initial stack state be: $\text{SS} = \text{F000H}$, $\text{SP} = \text{0100H}$ (so top of stack PA = F0100H).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: PUSH FR} \quad &\rightarrow \text{SP} = \text{00FEH}, \quad \text{Stack[00FEH..00FFH]} = \text{FR} \\
\text{Step 2: PUSH CS} \quad &\rightarrow \text{SP} = \text{00FCH}, \quad \text{Stack[00FCH..00FDH]} = \text{CS}_{\text{old}} \\
\text{Step 3: PUSH IP} \quad &\rightarrow \text{SP} = \text{00FAH}, \quad \text{Stack[00FAH..00FBH]} = \text{IP}_{\text{old}} \\
\text{Step 4: Clear IF, TF} \quad &\rightarrow \text{IF}=0, \; \text{TF}=0 \\
\text{Step 5: Fetch vector} \quad &\rightarrow \text{IP} = \text{MEM}[4n], \;\; \text{CS} = \text{MEM}[4n+2]
\end{aligned}
$$

Total stack decrement: $\Delta\text{SP} = -6$ bytes.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of the Four Instructions

| Attribute | `INT n` | `INT 3` | `INTO` | `BOUND` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Opcode | `CD n` (2 bytes) | `CC` (1 byte) | `CE` (1 byte) | `62 /r` (2 bytes) |
| Type Number | $n$ (0–255) | 3 (fixed) | 4 (fixed) | 5 (on violation) |
| Conditional? | No (always invokes) | No (always invokes) | **Yes** (only if OF=1) | **Yes** (only if out-of-range) |
| Effect on Flags | IF=0, TF=0 | IF=0, TF=0 | IF=0, TF=0 (if invoked) | IF=0, TF=0 (if invoked) |
| Vector Address | $4n$ | $0000\text{CH}$ | $00010\text{H}$ | $00014\text{H}$ |
| Common Use | DOS/BIOS calls | Debugger breakpoint | Signed overflow guard | Array bounds check |
| Operands | 8-bit immediate | None | None | reg16, mem32 |
| Stack Pushes | 3 (FR, CS, IP) | 3 (FR, CS, IP) | 3 (FR, CS, IP) | 3 (FR, CS, IP) |
| Return Instruction | `IRET` | `IRET` | `IRET` | `IRET` |
| Encoding class | Immediate | Fixed | Fixed | Memory-bound |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Example — `BOUND` Semantics

**Source Code (8086 Assembly):**
```asm
MY_ARRAY  DW  0010H, 00F0H      ; lower=0010H, upper=00F0H
          ...
          MOV  BX, 0050H        ; index = 50H (in range)
          BOUND BX, MY_ARRAY    ; OK → next instruction
          ...
          MOV  BX, 0200H        ; index = 200H (out of range)
          BOUND BX, MY_ARRAY    ; FAILS → INT 5 invoked
```

**Memory Layout of `MY_ARRAY`:**
- $\text{DS:0000H} = 10\text{H}$ (lower bound, low byte)
- $\text{DS:0001H} = 00\text{H}$ (lower bound, high byte)
- $\text{DS:0002H} = \text{F0H}$ (upper bound, low byte)
- $\text{DS:0003H} = 00\text{H}$ (upper bound, high byte)

**Comparison Logic:**
- Case 1: $\text{BX} = 0050\text{H}$. Check: $0010\text{H} \le 0050\text{H} \le 00\text{F0H}$ → **TRUE** → no interrupt.
- Case 2: $\text{BX} = 0200\text{H}$. Check: $0010\text{H} \le 0200\text{H}$ → **FALSE** → invoke INT 5.

### 3.4 Trap-Flag and Interrupt-Enable Side Effects — Mathematical Justification

The 8086 explicitly performs:
$$
\text{IF} \leftarrow 0, \quad \text{TF} \leftarrow 0
$$
**Why these specific flags?**

- **IF = 0:** Disables the **INTR pin** (maskable hardware interrupts). This prevents the ISR itself from being re-entered indefinitely on a still-pending hardware interrupt.
- **TF = 0:** Disables **single-step mode** (the trap generated after each instruction). This prevents the debugger's single-step feature from triggering one extra instruction inside the ISR prologue, which would corrupt the stack.

All other flags (CF, ZF, SF, OF, PF, AF, DF, etc.) are **left unchanged** — the 8086 preserves them so that the calling program (after `IRET`) sees identical arithmetic context.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow: Execution of `INT n`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: CPU fetches INT n] --> pushA[Step 1: PUSH Flags onto Stack]
    pushA --> pushB[Step 2: PUSH CS onto Stack]
    pushB --> pushC[Step 3: PUSH IP onto Stack]
    pushC --> clrA[Step 4: IF = 0 and TF = 0]
    clrA --> calcA[Step 5: Calculate vector offset = n x 4]
    calcA --> fetchA[Step 6: Read IP from MEM at 4n]
    fetchA --> fetchB[Step 7: Read CS from MEM at 4n plus 2]
    fetchB --> runA[Step 8: Begin ISR execution at new CS:IP]
    runA --> endA[End: ISR runs, terminates with IRET]
```

### 4.2 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mem00[00000H: INT 00 IP] --> mem01[00002H: INT 00 CS]
    mem01 --> mem02[00004H: INT 01 IP]
    mem02 --> mem03[00006H: INT 01 CS]
    mem03 --> mem04[00008H: INT 02 IP NMI]
    mem04 --> mem05[0000AH: INT 02 CS NMI]
    mem05 --> mem06[0000CH: INT 03 IP Breakpoint]
    mem06 --> mem07[0000EH: INT 03 CS Breakpoint]
    mem07 --> mem08[00010H: INT 04 IP Overflow]
    mem08 --> mem09[00012H: INT 04 CS Overflow]
    mem09 --> mem10[00014H: INT 05 IP Bound]
    mem10 --> mem11[00016H: INT 05 CS Bound]
    mem11 --> mem12[00018H: INT 06 IP Invalid Opcode]
    mem12 --> mem13[0001AH: INT 06 CS Invalid Opcode]
    mem13 --> mem14[...reserved...]
    mem14 --> mem15[003FEH: INT 255 IP]
    mem15 --> mem16[00400H: End of IVT]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Stack Frame Pushed by Software Interrupt

```mermaid
flowchart TD
