```mermaid
    highA[Higher Address] --> lowA[Lower Address]
    lowA --> cellA[IP of next instruction after INT]
    cellA --> cellB[CS of calling program]
    cellB --> cellC[Flags Register with IF=0 TF=0]
    cellC --> spA[Current SP points here]
    spA --> noteA[Stack grows downward]
    noteA --> highA
```

### 4.4 State Transition Diagram — `INTO` Conditional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB[INTO instruction fetched] --> ofCheck{OF flag equals 1?}
    ofCheck -- Yes --> actionA[Trigger INT 4 Push state and jump to vector 10H]
    ofCheck -- No --> actionB[NOP continue to next instruction]
    actionA --> returnB[ISR eventually executes IRET]
    actionB --> returnB
```

### 4.5 Decision Logic for `BOUND reg16, mem32`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC[BOUND instruction executed] --> readA[Read lower bound from mem32 low word]
    readA --> readB[Read upper bound from mem32 high word]
    readB --> cmpA{reg less than lower bound OR reg greater than upper bound?}
    cmpA -- Yes --> trapA[Trigger INT 5 push state and jump to vector 14H]
    cmpA -- No --> nextA[Continue to next instruction]
    trapA --> isrA[ISR executes IRET]
    nextA --> isrA
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric, letter-prefixed node identifiers and contain no reserved keywords, no bold/italic/HTML formatting, and no vertical pipes within labels — fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 schematic safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**  
**Explain the function of the `INT 3` instruction in 8086. Why is it encoded as a single byte, unlike the general `INT n` instruction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `INT 3` is a **1-byte software interrupt instruction (opcode `CCH`)** used as a **breakpoint** by debuggers. When executed, it pushes the Flag Register, CS, and IP onto the stack, clears the IF and TF flags, and transfers control to the **Type-3 ISR** whose vector is stored at physical address `0000CH` in the IVT. The ISR ends with an `IRET` instruction that restores the original state. (2 marks)
- Unlike `INT n` (2 bytes: `CDH` + type byte), `INT 3` is encoded as a single byte (`CCH`) to allow debuggers to **insert it into any memory location without disturbing the alignment of subsequent machine code instructions**. This makes it ideal for patching live programs. (1 mark)

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**  
**What is the role of the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) in 8086? How is the vector address calculated for a given interrupt type `n`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **IVT** is a 1 KB (256 × 4 bytes = 1024 bytes) data structure located at the **start of physical memory (`00000H` to `003FFH`)**. Each entry contains the starting `IP` and `CS` of an Interrupt Service Routine. (1.5 marks)
- For a given type $n$, the vector address is computed as $\text{Vector Address} = 4n$. The new IP is read from $\text{MEM}[4n]$ and the new CS is read from $\text{MEM}[4n + 2]$. The physical ISR address is then $\text{PA} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}$. (1.5 marks)

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the execution sequence of the `INT n` instruction in 8086. List the operations performed on the stack and on the flag register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `INT 3`, `INTO`, and `BOUND` instructions. Provide one practical application of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The `INT n` instruction is a 2-byte software interrupt (opcode `CDH` followed by the type byte $n$). The execution sequence is as follows:

1. **Push Flags Register** onto the stack. The 16-bit FR is stored at `SS:SP-2`, and SP is decremented by 2. (1 mark)
2. **Push CS** (current code segment) onto the stack. SP is again decremented by 2. (1 mark)
3. **Push IP** (address of the next instruction) onto the stack. SP is decremented by 2. After these three pushes, SP has moved down by 6 bytes. (1 mark)
4. **Clear IF and TF**: The Interrupt-Enable Flag (IF) and Trap Flag (TF) are both reset to 0, disabling maskable hardware interrupts and single-step trapping. (1 mark)
5. **Compute vector address**: The 8086 hardware computes $\text{offset} = n \times 4$. (1 mark)
6. **Fetch new IP and CS** from the IVT:

$$
\text{IP} \leftarrow \text{MEM}[0000\text{H} + 4n], \quad \text{CS} \leftarrow \text{MEM}[0000\text{H} + 4n + 2]
$$

7. **Transfer control** to the ISR at the new `CS:IP`. (1 mark)
8. The ISR terminates with the `IRET` instruction, which pops IP, CS, and Flags in reverse order. (1 mark)

**Diagram (describe in exam):** A memory diagram showing the stack growing downward with three 16-bit cells (Flags, CS, IP) and a separate IVT diagram at `00000H`–`003FFH` with the 4-byte vector for type $n$ highlighted.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Feature | `INT 3` | `INTO` | `BOUND` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opcode length | 1 byte (`CCH`) | 1 byte (`CEH`) | 2 bytes |
| Interrupt type | 3 (fixed) | 4 (fixed) | 5 (on violation) |
| Conditional? | No | **Yes** (OF = 1) | **Yes** (out-of-range) |
| Practical Application | **Debugger breakpoint** — used by DEBUG/DOS DEBUG to halt execution at a chosen line. (≈2.3 marks) | **Signed overflow detection** — placed after signed `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL` to detect arithmetic overflow and call an error handler. (≈2.3 marks) | **Array bound checking** — guards array index registers in high-level language runtimes to prevent buffer overflows. (≈2.4 marks) |

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the operation of the `BOUND` instruction in 8086. Show how it can be used to detect an out-of-range array index. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of software interrupt instructions on the 8086 flag register. Why are IF and TF specifically cleared, while other flags are preserved? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The `BOUND` instruction has the syntax:

```asm
BOUND reg16, mem32
```

It performs a **signed 16-bit bounds check** on the contents of `reg16` against two signed 16-bit limits stored contiguously in 32-bit memory: the **lower bound** at the low word of `mem32` and the **upper bound** at the high word. (2 marks)

**Step-by-step operation:** (3 marks)
1. The 8086 reads the lower bound from `[mem32]` (2 bytes).
2. It reads the upper bound from `[mem32 + 2]` (2 bytes).
3. It compares `reg16` to both limits using signed arithmetic.
4. If `reg16 < lower_bound` OR `reg16 > upper_bound` → triggers **INT 5 (BOUND range exceeded)**. The vector is at physical address `00014H`.
5. If `reg16` is within range → no operation; CPU proceeds to the next instruction.

**Example assembly program:** (2 marks)
```asm
.DATA
LIMIT_TABLE  DW  0000H, 0063H   ; Array of 100 elements (0 to 99)
.CODE
            MOV  BX, 0050H      ; Valid index = 80
            BOUND BX, LIMIT_TABLE   ; OK - continues
            MOV  BX, 0100H      ; Invalid index = 256
            BOUND BX, LIMIT_TABLE   ; TRIGGERS INT 5
```

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

All four software interrupt instructions (`INT n`, `INT 3`, `INTO`, `BOUND`) perform the following flag operations before transferring control: (3 marks)

$$
\text{IF} \leftarrow 0, \quad \text{TF} \leftarrow 0
$$

All other flags — Carry (CF), Parity (PF), Auxiliary (AF), Zero (ZF), Sign (SF), Overflow (OF), and Direction (DF) — are **left unchanged** on the stack and within the Flags Register.

**Why are IF and TF specifically cleared?** (4 marks)

1. **IF = 0 (Interrupt-Enable cleared):** During ISR execution, the CPU must not be re-interrupted by a maskable hardware interrupt (`INTR` pin) that could otherwise preempt the current service routine. Clearing IF provides a *protected execution environment* for the ISR. If nested interrupts are required, the ISR itself must explicitly execute `STI` to re-enable `INTR`.

2. **TF = 0 (Trap flag cleared):** TF = 1 causes the 8086 to generate an INT 1 (single-step trap) after **every instruction**. If TF remained set, the very first instruction of the ISR would itself trigger another INT 1, producing a runaway trap. Hence TF must be cleared.

3. **Other flags preserved:** Arithmetic state (CF, OF, ZF, SF, etc.) reflects the *result* of the last operation in the calling program. The calling program expects identical arithmetic context upon `IRET`, so these flags are simply pushed onto the stack and later popped back unchanged.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the vector address formula.** Some students write `n × 2` (forgetting each entry is **4 bytes**, not 2). The correct formula is **$4n$**, giving addresses $00000\text{H}$ to $003\text{FC}\text{H}$ for $n = 0$ to $255$.
> 2. **`BOUND` register width is strictly 16-bit.** Writing `BOUND AL, ...` or `BOUND EAX, ...` is an **invalid encoding** in 8086. Many students lose marks by assuming 8/32-bit forms exist.
> 3. **`INTO` does not clear OF.** It merely checks OF and branches. Several students incorrectly claim that INTO modifies OF.
> 4. **Stack order during INT is: PUSH Flags → PUSH CS → PUSH IP.** The `IRET` reversal is: POP IP → POP CS → POP Flags. Reversing this order in your answer loses up to 1 mark per question.
> 5. **Always state the IVT base address as `00000H`** (1 KB block). Writing "vector table is in low memory" is too vague and costs 0.5 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Software Interrupt Instructions**

- ⭐ **Four software interrupts in 8086:** `INT n` (any type 0–255), `INT 3` (1-byte breakpoint), `INTO` (overflow trap), `BOUND` (array bound check).
- ⭐ **IVT location:** Physical memory `00000H` to `003FFH`; **size = 1 KB = 256 × 4 bytes**.
- ⭐ **Vector address formula:** $\text{PA} = 4n$ → IP from `[4n]`, CS from `[4n + 2]`.
- ⭐ **Stack pushes (in order):** `PUSH FR → PUSH CS → PUSH IP`. **Total 6 bytes** of stack consumed.
- ⭐ **Flag side effects:** Only `IF = 0` and `TF = 0`; **all other flags preserved**.
- ⭐ **`INT 3` opcode:** `CCH` (1 byte); **`INT n` opcode:** `CDH nn` (2 bytes); **`INTO` opcode:** `CEH` (1 byte); **`BOUND` opcode:** `62H /r` (2 bytes).
- ⭐ **Reserved vector locations:** INT 3 → `0000CH`; INT 4 → `00010H`; INT 5 → `00014H`.
- ⭐ **`INTO` is conditional** on `OF = 1`; otherwise it acts like a NOP.
- ⭐ **`BOUND` operands are strictly `reg16, mem32`**: register must be 16-bit, memory must be 32-bit (lower:upper).
- ⭐ **Return from any software interrupt:** Use the `IRET` instruction (not `RET`).
- ⭐ **DOS / BIOS calls** are implemented as `INT n` software interrupts (e.g., `INT 21H` for DOS, `INT 10H` for video, `INT 13H` for disk, `INT 16H` for keyboard).
- ⭐ **Why IF and TF are cleared:** Prevent nested `INTR` and runaway single-step traps inside the ISR.
- ⭐ **Physical ISR address:** $\text{PA} = (\text{CS}_{\text{new}} \times 16) + \text{IP}_{\text{new}}$.
- ⭐ **KTU favorite question patterns:** (i) "Explain `INT n` execution with stack diagram" — 7 marks. (ii) "Differentiate the four software interrupts" — 7 marks. (iii) "Write a program using `INTO`/`BOUND`" — 7 marks. (iv) "Calculate vector address for type `n`" — 3 marks.
- ⭐ **Mnemonic to remember stack order:** **"**F**lags, **C**S, **I**P"** → pushed in this order, popped in reverse.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Status Register Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Status Register Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** processor architecture, the **Program Status Register (PSR)** is a **32-bit special-purpose register** that contains two distinct fields: the **condition code flags** (arithmetic/logic result status) and the **control bits** (processor mode, interrupt masks, and state). The ARM core maintains **two physical PSR instances**:

- **CPSR (Current Program Status Register):** Always active; holds the current condition flags and the current operating mode of the processor. There is exactly **one CPSR** accessible at any time.
- **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register):** A banked copy used only in privileged exception modes (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System). When an exception is taken, the CPSR is copied into the SPSR of the new mode so the original status can be restored on exception return.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The *Program Status Register Instructions* form a tiny but mandatory ARM instruction subset. Only two mnemonics — **MRS** (Move from Status Register) and **MSR** (Move to Status Register) — are permitted to legally access the PSR. Every other ARM data-processing instruction updates the flags *only* if the **'S' suffix** is used and the processor is in a privileged mode.

The formal ARM architecture reference splits CPSR bits into four regions:

| Field (top to bottom) | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Condition Flags | `[31:28]` | N, Z, C, V (and Q in ARMv5TE) |
| Q / Reserved | `[27]` | Sticky overflow (DSP) |
| Control Bits | `[7:0]` | I, F, T, M[4:0] |

> [!NOTE]
> A **banked register** in ARM means a register that is physically duplicated per mode. In User mode, **SPSR is undefined** and any read returns garbage. This is a high-frequency KTU exam trap.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Pilot's Instrument Panel"

Imagine the CPU as a **commercial airline pilot**. The CPSR is the **glareshield instrument panel** the pilot stares at constantly:

- The **four condition flags (N, Z, C, V)** are the **four engine dials** — they tell the pilot the *immediate outcome* of the most recent manoeuvre (was the last landing "Negative" too high, "Zero" perfect, "Carry" overshot, or "oVerflow" the runway?).
- The **control bits (I, F, T, M[4:0])** are the **autopilot and cockpit configuration switches** — they decide *how the plane is currently being flown* (are interrupts masked? is the plane in Thumb mode? is the pilot the Captain or the Co-pilot?).
- The **SPSR** is the **black-box flight recorder**: when an emergency interrupt (a "mayday") comes in, the current panel readings are *frozen and saved* so they can be restored after the emergency is handled.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **MRS** as the pilot *reading* the dials (Status → General-purpose register), and **MSR** as the pilot *twisting knobs* to change the configuration (General-purpose register → Status). You never touch the dials directly — the dials are read-only, only the knobs can be turned in privileged mode.

## 1.3 Why the PSR Exists — Engineering Rationale

A RISC machine that has a powerful conditional-execution model (`EQ`, `NE`, `CS`, `HI`, …) **must** have a tightly-defined flag-update protocol; otherwise every instruction would need a flag-update bit (wasting opcode space). ARM's compromise: PSR lives in its own register file, accessed only by **MRS/MSR**, and the **S-bit suffix** acts as the universal "update flags" flag for all data-processing and multiply instructions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the CPSR — Bit-by-Bit Decomposition

The 32-bit CPSR is laid out from bit **31 (MSB)** down to bit **0 (LSB)**. The standard ARMv4T (ARM7TDMI) layout used in KTU laboratories is:

$$
\boxed{\text{CPSR} = \underbrace{N\,Z\,C\,V}_{\text{Flags [31:28]}}\;\underbrace{\_}_{\text{Reserved [27:25]}}\;\underbrace{J}_{\text{Jazelle [24]}}\;\underbrace{\_}_{\text{Reserved [23:8]}}\;\underbrace{I\;F\;T}_{\text{Control [7:5]}}\;\underbrace{M_4\;M_3\;M_2\;M_1\;M_0}_{\text{Mode [4:0]}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **reversed flag naming convention** in the diagram above: the diagram shows the **logical** field grouping, but the actual bit-position is `N` at bit **31**, `Z` at bit **30**, `C` at bit **29**, `V` at bit **28**.

### 2.1.1 The Condition Flags (`[31:28]`)

| Flag | Bit | Set When | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** (Negative) | 31 | ALU result MSB = 1 | Result is negative in two's-complement |
| **Z** (Zero) | 30 | ALU result = 0 | Result is exactly zero (a branch test) |
| **C** (Carry) | 29 | Carry-out from unsigned addition / No borrow from subtraction | Used for **unsigned** overflow detection and multi-word arithmetic |
| **V** (oVerflow) | 28 | Signed overflow occurred | Used for **signed** overflow detection |

> [!NOTE]
> In **ARMv5TE** and later (ARM926EJ-S, Cortex-A), bit `[27]` is the **Q flag** (sticky overflow for the saturating DSP instructions `QADD`, `QSUB`). KTU boards using the **LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S)** typically do not implement Q.

### 2.1.2 The Control Bits (`[7:0]`)

| Bit | Name | Function | Read/Write Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **I** (IRQ disable) | 1 = IRQ masked, 0 = IRQ enabled | Privileged only |
| 6 | **F** (FIQ disable) | 1 = FIQ masked, 0 = FIQ enabled | Privileged only |
| 5 | **T** (Thumb state) | 1 = processor executing Thumb (16-bit) instructions, 0 = ARM (32-bit) | Privileged only |
| 4–0 | **M[4:0]** (Mode) | 5-bit processor mode code | Privileged only |

### 2.1.3 The Mode Encoding Table (Mandatory for KTU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10000_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{User (usr)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10001_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Fast Interrupt (fiq)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10010_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Interrupt (irq)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10011_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Supervisor (svc)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10111_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Abort (abt)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 11011_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Undefined (und)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 11111_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{System (sys)} \\
M_4\,M_3\,M_2\,M_1\,M_0 &= 10110_{2} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Secure Monitor (mon, TrustZone only)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Decimal value `0b10000` = 16 (User) is **NOT the same** as binary 16 with a different bit pattern. Many KTU students confuse the mode codes; **memorise the table above**.

## 2.2 The Two PSR Instructions — MRS and MSR

### 2.2.1 MRS — Move From Status Register

The `MRS` instruction **copies** the contents of the CPSR or an SPSR into a general-purpose register so that the flags can be *read*, *pushed onto the stack*, or *inspected bit-by-bit*.

**General syntax (ARM assembly):**

$$
\text{MRS} \;\langle Rd \rangle, \;\langle \text{CPSR} \mid \text{SPSR}\rangle
$$

**Examples (in Keil/ARM7 syntax):**

```assembly
MRS   R0, CPSR          ; Copy CPSR into R0
MRS   R1, SPSR          ; Copy SPSR of current mode into R1
```

**Operand constraints (KTU high-yield):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Encoding (ARM)} &: \text{cond}_{4}\,0000\,100\,1111\,1111\,1111\,0001\,\text{Rd}_{4} \\
\text{Register} &: \text{Rd} \neq \text{R15 (PC)} \\
\text{Access} &: \text{Unrestricted — User mode can read CPSR} \\
\text{Pipeline} &: \text{No flag update, no S-bit required}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reading CPSR **does not require** privileged mode. The flags are the public "result of last operation" and any application code is allowed to read them. *Writing* is the restricted operation.

### 2.2.2 MSR — Move To Status Register

The `MSR` instruction transfers the contents of a general-purpose register (or an immediate operand) into the **control byte** or **flag byte** (or both) of the CPSR or SPSR. The target byte(s) is selected by a 4-bit mask field `mask`.

**General syntax (two forms):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MSR} &\;\langle \text{CPSR} \mid \text{SPSR} \rangle\_\langle c \rangle, \;\langle Rm \rangle \\
\text{MSR} &\;\langle \text{CPSR} \mid \text{SPSR} \rangle\_\langle cxf \rangle, \;\#\text{immediate}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The underscore suffix `_<c>` and `_<x>` etc. is a **field mask** — a KTU-favourite exam point. Allowed field masks in ARMv4T are `c` (control byte `[7:0]`), `x` (extension byte `[15:8]`, unused/Reserved), `s` (status/flags byte `[23:16]`, partially reserved), and `f` (flags byte `[31:24]`, **only `N Z C V` writable**).

**Operand constraints (KTU high-yield):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source register} &: \text{Rm} \neq \text{R15 (PC)} \\
\text{Mode requirement} &: \text{Privileged mode required for writing control bits} \\
\text{User-mode rule} &: \text{User can write ONLY the flag byte} \;\big(f\big) \text{ of CPSR (and NOT SPSR)} \\
\text{Immediate form} &: 8\text{-bit value rotated right by an even amount}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Code examples (in Keil/ARM7 syntax):**

```assembly
MRS   R0, CPSR          ; Step 1: Read CPSR
BIC   R0, R0, #0x80     ; Step 2: Clear I-bit (bit 7) — re-enable IRQ
ORR   R0, R0, #0x40     ; Step 3: Set F-bit (bit 6) — keep FIQ masked
MSR   CPSR_c, R0        ; Step 4: Write back the CONTROL byte only
```

> [!NOTE]
> The classic **read-modify-write** idiom for any privileged PSR update is: `MRS → BIC/ORR → MSR`. Attempting to write a single bit directly with a single `MSR` instruction is impossible because the instruction writes the **whole selected byte**.

## 2.3 The 'S' Suffix — Indirect PSR Access

Almost all ARM data-processing, multiply, and barrel-shifter instructions accept an optional **S-suffix**. When the suffix is present **and** the processor is in a privileged mode, the **flag byte (`N Z C V`)** of the CPSR is updated by the result of that instruction.

$$
\text{E.g.:} \quad \text{ADDS} \;\text{R0},\text{R1},\text{R2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_0 = R_1 + R_2 \;\wedge\; \text{update N,Z,C,V}
$$

$$
\text{E.g.:} \quad \text{ADD} \;\text{R0},\text{R1},\text{R2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_0 = R_1 + R_2 \;\wedge\; \text{flags UNCHANGED}
$$

**The S-suffix rule (KTU board-favourite):**

$$
\text{In User mode:} \quad \text{ADD}S \text{ still updates flags (allowed)} \\
\text{However:} \quad \text{All flag updates from S-suffix instructions affect the } \mathbf{f} \text{ byte only}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Encoding Cheat Sheet

| Mnemonic | Class | Privileged? | Updates Flags? | Updates Mode? | Opcode Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MRS Rd, CPSR` | PSR Read | No | No | No | `0001 0` |
| `MRS Rd, SPSR` | PSR Read | Yes (only in privileged mode) | No | No | `0001 0` |
| `MSR CPSR_f, Rm` | PSR Write (flags) | **No** (User allowed) | **Yes** | No | `0001 0` |
| `MSR CPSR_c, Rm` | PSR Write (control) | **Yes** | No | **Yes** | `0001 0` |
| `MSR CPSR_cxsf, Rm` | PSR Write (all bytes) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | `0001 0` |
| `MSR SPSR_*, Rm` | PSR Write (saved) | **Yes** | Conditional | No | `0001 0` |
| `<op>S` (any DP) | Implicit PSR update | Conditional | **Yes** | No | `<op> + S` |

> [!TIP]
> The complete PSR instruction set is exactly **two mnemonics** (MRS and MSR), each with multiple addressing forms. This is one of the smallest instruction groups in the entire ARM ISA — yet it carries **8–14 marks** in KTU Module-4 questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Operating-System Kernel (μClinux, FreeRTOS context switch):** The scheduler reads CPSR with `MRS`, stores it on the task's stack, loads the new task's saved CPSR with `MSR SPSR_cxsf, Rm`, and then `SUBS PC, LR, #4` to atomically return and restore flags.
2. **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs):** The hardware automatically saves CPSR → SPSR on IRQ entry; the ISR prologue must preserve this on the user stack and restore it on exit using `MSR`.
3. **Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Emulation:** User-mode code uses the `CPSR_f` byte as a single 4-bit semaphore (`N Z C V`) to implement lock-free primitives on single-core ARM.
4. **Power Management:** Embedded firmware toggles the `F` and `I` bits to selectively mask fast interrupts before entering a critical timing section (e.g., ADC sampling, motor control loop).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

This section is the *exhaustive computation core*. Every algebraic and binary-arithmetic step is written explicitly. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Example 1 — Reading CPSR into R0 and Masking Out the Mode Bits

**Problem (KTU-style):** Write an ARM assembly sequence that copies the current CPSR into R0, then masks out the mode bits (M[4:0]) to leave only the flags and control byte visible. Show the binary trace assuming the processor is in Supervisor mode after an SWI exception.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Initial state after SWI exception (from real LPC2148 hardware):**

$$
\text{CPSR}_{\text{on entry}} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{flags}}\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;\underbrace{1\;1\;0}_{\text{I=1,F=1,T=0}}\;\underbrace{10011}_{\text{M=0x13 (Supervisor)}}
$$

(We write `XX` for the flags because they reflect the user's last instruction — assume `N=0, Z=1, C=0, V=0` for this worked example.)

**Step 2 — Convert to 32-bit hex for clarity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flags} &= 0000 \quad (N=0, Z=0, C=0, V=0) \\
\text{Middle} &= 00000000_{16} \\
\text{Control} &= 110 \;\, 10011_{2} = 0xD3 \\
\text{Full CPSR} &= 0x000000D3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute `MRS R0, CPSR`:**

$$
R_0 \;\leftarrow\; \text{CPSR} \;=\; 0x000000D3
$$

> This single instruction does **not** change CPSR. It is a *load* from the status register file to the general-purpose register file.

**Step 4 — Mask off the mode bits with a `BIC` instruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BIC R0, R0, \#0x1F} &\;\Rightarrow\; R_0 \;\leftarrow\; R_0 \;\text{AND}\; \sim 0x1F \\
\sim 0x1F &= 0xFFFFFFE0 \\
0x000000D3 \,\text{AND}\, 0xFFFFFFE0 &= 0x000000D0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final value in R0:**

$$
R_0 \;=\; 0x000000D0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{flags cleared, control byte = 0xD0, mode bits = 0}
$$

This value represents the *flag-and-control portion* of the CPSR, suitable for further bit-level inspection.

## 3.2 Example 2 — Re-enabling IRQ Inside an IRQ Handler

**Problem:** Inside an LPC2148 IRQ handler, you need to allow higher-priority FIQ to pre-empt, but still keep the current IRQ mask in place. Write the minimal MRS-MSR sequence.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Read CPSR:**

$$
\text{MRS R0, CPSR} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_0 = 0x000000D2 \;\;(\text{typical IRQ entry: I=1, F=1, mode=IRQ})
$$

(For IRQ mode, M[4:0] = 10010₂ = 0x12, so the control byte is 110 10010 = 0xD2.)

**Step 2 — Clear the I-bit (bit 7 of CPSR = 0x80):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BIC R0, R0, \#0x80} &\;\Rightarrow\; R_0 = 0x000000D2 \,\text{AND}\, 0xFFFFFF7F \\
&= 0x00000052
\end{aligned}
$$

(We only clear **I**, leaving **F** set so FIQ is still masked, and the mode bits are deliberately preserved as `10010` so the processor stays in IRQ mode.)

**Step 3 — Write back the control byte only:**

$$
\text{MSR CPSR\_c, R0} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{CPSR} = 0x00000052 \;\;(\text{I=0, F=1, mode=IRQ})
$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**

| Bit Position | 7 (I) | 6 (F) | 5 (T) | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Old value | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0xD2 |
| New value | **0** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x52 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The field mask `c` writes **only the control byte**. If we had used `cxsf`, the flags (which might have been set by the interrupt vector calculation) would have been **clobbered**. This is a classic KTU marker.

## 3.3 Example 3 — Computing C-flag Behaviour for a Multi-Word 64-bit Add

**Problem:** Show how a sequence of `ADC` (Add with Carry) instructions, combined with the `S`-suffix, propagates the carry across the upper 32 bits of a 64-bit add.

### Step-by-step solution:

Let $X = X_{H} \cdot 2^{32} + X_{L}$ and $Y = Y_{H} \cdot 2^{32} + Y_{L}$. We want $Z = X + Y$.

**Step 1 — Lower 32 bits (uses ordinary `ADD` + flags):**

$$
\text{ADDS R3, R0, R1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_3 = X_L + Y_L, \quad C_{\text{out}} \text{ written to CPSR.C}
$$

**Step 2 — Upper 32 bits (uses `ADC` which adds C-in):**

$$
\text{ADC R4, R2, R2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_4 = X_H + Y_H + \text{CPSR.C}
$$

**Step 3 — Mathematical verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= X + Y \\
  &= (X_H \cdot 2^{32} + X_L) + (Y_H \cdot 2^{32} + Y_L) \\
  &= (X_H + Y_H) \cdot 2^{32} + (X_L + Y_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, write $X_L + Y_L = \text{Result}_L + C_{\text{out}} \cdot 2^{32}$, where $\text{Result}_L \in [0, 2^{32}-1]$.

$$
Z = (X_H + Y_H) \cdot 2^{32} + \text{Result}_L + C_{\text{out}} \cdot 2^{32}
$$

$$
Z = (X_H + Y_H + C_{\text{out}}) \cdot 2^{32} + \text{Result}_L
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\; R_4 = X_H + Y_H + C_{\text{out}}, \quad R_3 = \text{Result}_L \;\blacksquare
$$

> This derivation proves that the `C` flag — the only **unsigned-overflow indicator** in CPSR — is the linkage between successive 32-bit ADDs that together form an N-bit (here 64-bit) integer addition. This is the canonical use of the C flag in multi-precision arithmetic.

## 3.4 Example 4 — Mode Switch from User to Supervisor via SWI

**Problem:** Show the complete PSR state transition when a User-mode program executes `SVC #0` (Software Interrupt) on an ARM7.

### Step-by-step derivation:

**Step 0 — Initial CPSR (User mode, IRQs enabled):**

$$
\text{CPSR}_{\text{init}} = \underbrace{N\,Z\,C\,V}_{\text{user flags}}\;0\ldots0\;\underbrace{0\;0\;0}_{\text{I=0, F=0, T=0}}\;\underbrace{10000}_{\text{M=User}}
$$

**Step 1 — Hardware atomically performs (on SVC instruction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SPSR}_{\text{svc}} &\;\leftarrow\; \text{CPSR} \quad \text{(snapshot the user flags and control)} \\
\text{CPSR.M[4:0]} &\;\leftarrow\; 10011_2 \quad (\text{SVC mode}) \\
\text{CPSR.I} &\;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad (\text{IRQ auto-disabled}) \\
\text{CPSR.F} &\;\leftarrow\; 1 \quad (\text{FIQ auto-disabled}) \\
\text{LR}_{\text{svc}} &\;\leftarrow\; \text{PC} - 4 \quad (\text{return address saved}) \\
\text{PC} &\;\leftarrow\; \text{0x00000008} \quad (\text{SVC exception vector})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Inside the SWI handler, the firmware reads SPSR for future return:**

$$
\text{MRS R9, SPSR} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_9 = \text{saved user CPSR}
$$

**Step 3 — To return, the firmware uses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOVS PC, LR} &\;\Rightarrow\; PC = LR_{\text{svc}}, \quad \text{and as a side effect} \\
\text{CPSR} &\;\leftarrow\; \text{SPSR}_{\text{svc}} \quad (\text{atomic restore of user mode + flags + I/F state})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The instruction `MOVS PC, LR` is a **special form** of the data-processing instruction that uses the `S` suffix to *simultaneously* transfer the saved PC and restore the SPSR into CPSR. KTU examiners love asking why this single instruction is enough to return from an exception.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each PSR Instruction

| Goal | Instruction | Privilege Required | Side Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Read flags for `if` logic in C | `MRS Rd, CPSR` | No | None |
| Save context on stack | `MRS Rd, CPSR` then `STMFD SP!, {Rd, ...}` | No | None |
| Write back the I-bit | `MRS; BIC; MSR CPSR_c, Rd` | **Yes** | Mode/IRQ change |
| Write back the N-bit only | `MRS; ORR; MSR CPSR_f, Rd` | No (User can write `f` byte) | Flag change |
| Switch to Supervisor (legal) | `SVC` instruction | No (exception entry is automatic) | Mode change, SPSR save |
| Switch to FIQ manually | `MSR CPSR_c, #0xD1` | **Yes** | Mode change |
| Return from exception | `MOVS PC, LR` (or `SUBS PC, LR, #4`) | **Yes** (restores CPSR from SPSR) | Atomic restore |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPSR Bit Layout (ARM7TDMI)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[CPSR 32-bit Register] --> B[Bits 31 to 28 : Condition Flags]
    A --> C[Bits 27 to 25 : Reserved or Q]
    A --> D[Bits 24 to 8 : Reserved]
    A --> E[Bits 7 to 0 : Control Byte]

    B --> B1["Bit 31 : N - Negative"]
    B --> B2["Bit 30 : Z - Zero"]
    B --> B3["Bit 29 : C - Carry"]
    B --> B4["Bit 28 : V - oVerflow"]

    C --> C1["Bit 27 : Q - Sticky overflow DSP"]

    E --> E1["Bit 7 : I - IRQ mask"]
    E --> E2["Bit 6 : F - FIQ mask"]
    E --> E3["Bit 5 : T - Thumb state"]
    E --> E4["Bits 4 to 0 : M4 M3 M2 M1 M0 - Mode"]
```

> The diagram above maps each **logical field** of the CPSR to its **bit positions**. Students should be able to draw a similar labelled box and fill in the bit numbers from memory.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MRS/MSR Data-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[General Purpose Register File] -->|MSR destination write| B[PSR Mux / Demux]
    B -->|byte select c x s f mask| C1[CPSR control byte 7 to 0]
    B --> C2[CPSR flags byte 31 to 24]
    B --> C3[CPSR status byte 23 to 16]
    B --> C4[CPSR ext byte 15 to 8]
    B --> C5[Banked SPSR per mode]

    C1 -->|MRS read| A
    C2 -->|MRS read| A
    C3 -->|MRS read| A
    C5 -->|MRS read| A

    style B fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The **PSR Mux/Demux** block is the conceptual hardware that enforces the byte-mask rule: a single `MSR` with `_cxsf` writes all four bytes; a `MSR` with `_c` writes only the control byte. The mask bits are decoded from the 4-bit `mask` field in the instruction word.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — The Read-Modify-Write Idiom

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need to update CPSR control bits] --> B["MRS Rd, CPSR"]
    B --> C{Rd now holds full PSR}
    C --> D["BIC Rd, Rd, #mask_clr"]
    C --> E["ORR Rd, Rd, #mask_set"]
    D --> F["MSR CPSR_c, Rd"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Check privilege}
    G -->|User and writes f byte only| H[Update allowed, no mode change]
    G -->|Privileged and writes c or cxsf| I[Update allowed, mode may change]
    G -->|User attempts c write| J[UNDEFINED exception raised]
    H --> K[End]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> The bottom branch "**UNDEFINED exception raised**" is exactly what happens on a Keil-simulated LPC2148 if a user-mode program tries `MSR CPSR_c, R0`. The CPU takes the Undefined Instruction vector. KTU questions frequently test this exception behaviour.

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Context Switch Between Two Tasks

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Task 1 running
    participant K as Kernel / Scheduler
    participant T2 as Task 2 ready

    T1->>K: SWI (SVC) for OS service
    Note over K: Hardware saves CPSR into SPSR_svc
    Note over K: Switches to SVC mode, masks IRQ and FIQ
    K->>K: MRS R0, CPSR
    K->>K: STMFD SP_user!, {R0, R1 ... R12, LR}
    Note over K: Task 1 full context now on stack
    K->>K: LDMFD SP_user2!, {R0, R1 ... R12, LR}
    K->>K: MSR CPSR_cxsf, R_saved
    K->>T2: MOVS PC, LR
    Note over T2: Atomic PSR restore, resumes Task 2
```

> This sequence diagram is the **canonical OS context switch** taught in every KTU module-4 lecture. The single `MSR CPSR_cxsf` is the keystone — without it, restoring the task's flags and mode would require multiple instructions and the system would not be atomic.
