# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. (3 marks)** Differentiate between **CPSR** and **SPSR** in the ARM processor. Which one is accessible in User mode?

**Model Answer (3 marks — full distribution):**

The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is the single, always-active status register that holds the live **condition flags (N, Z, C, V)** and the **control byte (I, F, T, M[4:0])** of the ARM core. There is exactly one CPSR per processor.

The **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is a **banked copy** of the CPSR that exists only in the **privileged exception modes** (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System). When an exception is taken, the hardware atomically copies the current CPSR into the SPSR of the *new* mode; the original CPSR values can be restored on exception return with a `MOVS PC, LR` instruction.

**User mode access:** The CPSR can be **read** in User mode using the `MRS` instruction, and the flag byte (`N Z C V`) can be **written** using `MSR CPSR_f, Rm`. However, the **SPSR is undefined** in User mode and any read returns garbage; any write to SPSR in User mode is not permitted. *(3 marks: 1 for CPSR definition, 1 for SPSR definition, 1 for User-mode access rule.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2. (3 marks)** Explain the function of the **'S' suffix** in ARM data-processing instructions. How is it related to the PSR?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `'S'` suffix is a single bit in the ARM data-processing instruction encoding (bit 20 of the instruction word). When set, and provided the processor is in a **privileged mode**, it causes the **condition flags byte (`N Z C V`) of the CPSR** to be updated according to the result of the operation.

For example, `ADDS R0, R1, R2` computes $R_0 = R_1 + R_2$ *and* updates the flags; the identical instruction without the suffix, `ADD R0, R1, R2`, computes the same result but leaves the flags unchanged. The S-suffix is therefore the **only legal mechanism other than `MRS`/`MSR`** to interact with the PSR. The alternative path is the explicit read-modify-write sequence `MRS → BIC/ORR → MSR` which is used when bits in the *control byte* need to be modified. *(3 marks: 1 for meaning of S, 1 for flag-update behaviour, 1 for relation to MRS/MSR.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Q3. (a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of the ARM CPSR**. List all five processor modes and their M[4:0] encodings.
>
> **Q3. (b) (7 marks)** Write the ARM assembly sequence to **read the CPSR, disable the IRQ (set the I-bit), and switch from User mode to Supervisor mode**. Show the binary content of R0 before and after the modification.

### Model Answer — Question 3(a) (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the CPSR split into two halves — the upper `flags byte (N Z C V)` at bits `[31:28]` and the lower `control byte (I F T M4 M3 M2 M1 M0)` at bits `[7:0]`, with the middle `[27:8]` marked as reserved. *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a labelled figure.)*

**Mode table (4 marks):**

| Mode Name | M[4:0] binary | M[4:0] decimal | Privilege |
|---|---|---|---|
| User (usr) | 10000 | 16 | Unprivileged |
| FIQ (fiq) | 10001 | 17 | Privileged |
| IRQ (irq) | 10010 | 18 | Privileged |
| Supervisor (svc) | 10011 | 19 | Privileged |
| Abort (abt) | 10111 | 23 | Privileged |
| Undefined (und) | 11011 | 27 | Privileged |
| System (sys) | 11111 | 31 | Privileged |

*(Award 0.5 mark per row, rounded up; the mandatory five are User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, System.)*

### Model Answer — Question 3(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Read CPSR (1 mark):**

```assembly
MRS   R0, CPSR      ; R0 = 0x000000D0   (assume User, I=0, F=0, T=0, M=10000)
```

Initial $R_0$ in binary:

$$
R_0 = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;1101\;0000_2 = 0x000000D0
$$

**Step 2 — Set the I-bit (bit 7) (2 marks):**

```assembly
ORR   R0, R0, #0x80 ; R0 = R0 OR 0x80
```

Calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x000000D0 &= \ldots\;1101\;0000_2 \\
0x00000080 &= \ldots\;1000\;0000_2 \\
\text{OR}   &= \ldots\;1101\;0000_2 \;\;(\text{bit 7 was already 1 in 0xD0})
\end{aligned}
$$

After ORR, $R_0$ still equals `0x000000D0`. The I-bit was already 1; this is a re-affirming write.

**Step 3 — Change mode bits to Supervisor (10011₂ = 0x13) (2 marks):**

```assembly
BIC   R0, R0, #0x1F ; Clear old mode bits (M[4:0])
ORR   R0, R0, #0x13 ; Insert new mode 0x13
```

Calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x000000D0 \,\text{AND}\, 0xFFFFFFE0 &= 0x000000D0 \quad(\text{mode was already 0x00 in our example}) \\
0x000000D0 \,\text{OR}\, \, 0x00000013 &= 0x000000D3
\end{aligned}
$$

Final $R_0$ in binary:

$$
R_0 = \ldots\;1101\;0011_2 = 0x000000D3
$$

**Step 4 — Write back to CPSR (1 mark):**

```assembly
MSR   CPSR_c, R0    ; Write control byte only (1 mark)
```

> Note: The hardware raises an **Undefined exception** in real User-mode execution because User mode cannot write the control byte. In a Keil-supervised lab, the developer is usually running in **Privileged (System/Supervisor)** mode, so the write succeeds.

**Step 5 — Note on automatic CPSR save (1 mark):** Had this mode switch been done by the CPU itself (e.g., via `SVC`), the old CPSR would have been automatically saved into `SPSR_svc` — so the sequence above is for *manual* switching during an OS service routine.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **Q4. (a) (7 marks)** Explain the instruction format and operand restrictions of the **MRS** instruction. Show the 32-bit machine encoding of `MRS R5, CPSR` when `cond = AL` (0xE).
>
> **Q4. (b) (7 marks)** In an ARM7-based embedded system, the FIRQ handler saves the context with:
>
> ```assembly
> SUB   LR, LR, #4
> STMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}
> MRS   R0, SPSR
> STMFD SP!, {R0}
> ```
>
> At the end of the handler, the return sequence is:
>
> ```assembly
> LDMFD SP!, {R0}
> MSR   SPSR_cxsf, R0
> LDMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}
> ADD   LR, LR, #4
> MOVS  PC, LR
> ```
>
> Explain **line by line** why this return sequence correctly restores the **interrupted User-mode context**.

### Model Answer — Question 4(a) (7 marks)

**Instruction format (3 marks):**

$$
\text{MRS} \; \langle Rd \rangle,\; \langle \text{CPSR} \mid \text{SPSR} \rangle
$$

**Encoding (4 marks):**

The ARM encoding for `MRS` (with `cond`, `Rd = R5 = 0101₂`):

$$
\underbrace{1110}_{\text{cond = AL}}\;0001\;0\;00\;1111\;1111\;1111\;0001\;\underbrace{0101}_{\text{Rd = R5}}
$$

Grouped as 8 hex digits:

$$
\text{0xE10F1005}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> A common student error is writing `0xE10F0015` — the **`Rd` field occupies bits `[15:12]`** of the instruction word, not bits `[3:0]`. Always double-check the bit position of the destination register in PSR instructions. The `[3:0]` field is hardwired to `0001` for `MRS` (an architectural quirk that distinguishes it from `MSR` which uses `0000`).
>
> A second common error: writing `Rd = PC` (R15) is **not allowed** in `MRS`. Using R15 raises an Undefined exception on real hardware.

### Model Answer — Question 4(b) (7 marks)

**Line 1 — `LDMFD SP!, {R0}`** *(1 mark)*
Pops the previously saved **SPSR copy** (which contains the original User-mode CPSR snapshot) from the stack into R0.

**Line 2 — `MSR SPSR_cxsf, R0`** *(2 marks)*
Loads the full 32-bit value into the **SPSR of the FIQ mode**. Since we are in privileged FIQ mode, the `cxsf` mask is permitted. The SPSR now holds the **exact pre-interrupt state** (User-mode flags, I=0, F=0, T=0, M=10000).

**Line 3 — `LDMFD SP!, {R0-R12, LR}`** *(1 mark)*
Restores all the **general-purpose registers and the link register** that the interrupted User-mode program was using.

**Line 4 — `ADD LR, LR, #4`** *(1 mark)*
The hardware subtracted 4 from PC when taking the FIQ exception (so that `LR_fiq = PC_at_exception + 4` actually points to the *next* instruction to execute). Adding 4 here corrects the return address so that the `MOVS PC, LR` on the next line jumps to the *exact* instruction that was interrupted.

**Line 5 — `MOVS PC, LR`** *(2 marks)*
The most important line. This is a special form of the data-processing `MOV` instruction with the `S` suffix. It performs two atomic operations:
1. Writes the value of LR into PC, causing a branch to the return address.
2. As a side effect of the `S` suffix, **the SPSR of the current mode is copied into the CPSR**. Because we wrote the original User-mode CPSR into SPSR in line 2, the CPSR is now atomically restored to User mode with IRQs re-enabled, original flags intact, and the original mode bits.

> The `MOVS PC, LR` form exists **only** for exception return — it cannot be used in any other context in a non-privileged way to write the PSR. This is a KTU high-yield fact. *(2 marks: 1 for atomic PSR restore, 1 for the privilege context.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Common Marks-Loss Hotspots for PSR Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `c` mask on `MSR`:** Writing `MSR CPSR, R0` — invalid syntax. You must specify at least one of `c`, `x`, `s`, `f` (the field mask).
> 2. **Using `MSR` to write flags from privileged code when the assignment only wanted a flag set:** Use `ADDS`, `SUBS` with the `S` suffix, not `MSR`.
> 3. **Writing SPSR in User mode:** It is undefined / permission-faulted. Always transition to Supervisor or System first.
> 4. **Confusing `MOVS PC, LR` with `MOV PC, LR`:** Only the `S` suffix triggers the SPSR → CPSR restore. The plain `MOV` is just a branch.
> 5. **Omitting the `BIC` step when changing mode:** Many students jump straight to `ORR` — but if the old mode bits are non-zero, you must first **clear** them or you will set *two* modes simultaneously (which is architecturally impossible and causes Undefined).
> 6. **Bit-number errors:** IRQ is bit **7**, FIQ is bit **6**, Thumb is bit **5** of CPSR. Many students get I and F swapped.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PSR** = the ARM status register file; consists of one **CPSR** and one **SPSR per privileged mode**.
- **MRS** = *Move from Status to Register* (read PSR). Format: `MRS Rd, CPSR` or `MRS Rd, SPSR`. No S-suffix needed. Rd ≠ R15.
- **MSR** = *Move from Register to Status* (write PSR). Format: `MSR CPSR_<mask>, Rm` or `MSR SPSR_<mask>, #imm`. Rm ≠ R15. Mask ∈ {`c`, `x`, `s`, `f`, combinations}.
- **CPSR byte structure:** Flags `[31:28]` = `N Z C V`; Control `[7:0]` = `I F T M4 M3 M2 M1 M0`. Middle bytes reserved.
- **Mode encodings:** User = 10000, FIQ = 10001, IRQ = 10010, SVC = 10011, ABT = 10111, UND = 11011, SYS = 11111.
- **The 'S' suffix** is the *implicit* PSR-update mechanism for data-processing and multiply instructions. It updates only the `N Z C V` byte in privileged mode (always in User mode for data-processing).
- **Privilege rule:** User can **read** CPSR, **write** only the flag byte of CPSR; cannot access SPSR; cannot change control byte.
- **The read-modify-write idiom** — `MRS → BIC/ORR → MSR` — is the only safe way to change a single PSR bit.
- **Exception return** uses the special `MOVS PC, LR` (or `SUBS PC, LR, #4` for IRQ/FIQ) — the S-suffix atomically restores SPSR → CPSR.
- **Two's-complement flag rules:** `V = 1` when signed overflow occurs; `C = 1` when unsigned overflow (carry-out) occurs. They are **independent** and can be 1 simultaneously.
- **Carry propagation in multi-word arithmetic** relies on the `C` flag in CPSR being preserved by the `S` suffix across successive `ADC` instructions.
- **Typical exam lines:** "List the two PSR instructions" (2 marks), "Explain CPSR structure with diagram" (7 marks), "Show the MRS-MSR sequence to enable/disable interrupts" (7 marks), "Explain the role of `MOVS PC, LR` in exception return" (7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loading Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loading Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture**, *Loading Constants* refers to the assembly-level operation of transferring a **literal numerical value** (an *immediate operand*) directly into a CPU register, an internal Special Function Register (SFR), or a directly-addressable data memory (RAM) location. This operation is performed using the **Immediate Addressing Mode**, where the constant itself is embedded within the instruction's machine code and is fetched along with the opcode from program memory (ROM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> "Loading Constants is the process of initializing CPU registers, SFRs, and RAM locations with fixed values (literals) using immediate addressing mode instructions such as `MOV A, #data`, `MOV Rn, #data`, `MOV direct, #data`, and the 16-bit `MOV DPTR, #data16` for pointer/look-up-table initialization."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pigeonhole Postman" Intuition

Imagine a postman (the **CPU**) who has a wall of numbered pigeonholes (the **Registers/RAM**). Normally, he walks to another office to pick up a letter and then places it in a hole. But for *constants*, the letter is **tucked into his satchel along with the delivery slip** — the value is part of the instruction itself.

| Real-World Object | 8051 Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Postman | Program Counter (PC) | Fetches instruction |
| Satchel pocket | Opcode + Operand byte | Carries constant |
| Pigeonhole | Register A, R0–R7, RAM | Destination |
| Delivery slip | Opcode (e.g., `74H`) | Tells what to do |
| Letter inside slip | `#data` byte | The constant itself |

When you write `MOV A, #25H`, the `25H` is the letter stapled to the instruction — it is **not** in memory somewhere waiting to be fetched. The CPU grabs both in a single memory read cycle.

### 1.3 Categories of Constants in the 8051

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Three Constant Categories**
> The 8051 recognizes three distinct numerical "sizes" of constants, each requiring a different instruction and consuming a different number of program memory bytes:
>
> 1. **8-bit Unsigned Constants** — Range: `00H` to `FFH` (0 to 255 decimal)
> 2. **16-bit Unsigned Constants** — Range: `0000H` to `FFFFH` (0 to 65,535 decimal)
> 3. **ASCII / Character Constants** — Encoded using single quotes, e.g., `'A'` = `41H`

### 1.4 The `#` Symbol — The "Hash of Immediacy"

The hash symbol (`#`) is the assembly syntax flag that tells the assembler: *"The number that follows is a literal value, not a memory address."* Without `#`, the assembler would interpret `25H` as a memory location `25H` in the internal RAM, triggering an **Indirect** or **Direct** addressing interpretation instead of an immediate load.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Forgetting the `#` symbol converts a constant load into a memory-to-memory move, which either produces a **wrong result** or triggers an **assembler error** if the source is not declared as an address.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Canonical Constant-Load Instructions

The 8051 instruction set provides five explicit "immediate load" instructions that the KTU examiner expects every student to memorize:

#### 2.1.1 `MOV A, #data` — Load Accumulator with 8-bit Constant
- **Opcode**: `74H`
- **Size**: 2 bytes (1 opcode + 1 data byte)
- **Machine Cycles**: 1
- **Affected Flags**: None (no PSW bits change)
- **Logic**: The 8-bit literal `#data` is copied into the accumulator `A`.

#### 2.1.2 `MOV Rn, #data` — Load Register R0–R7 with 8-bit Constant
- **Opcode**: `78H` to `7FH` (8 variants for R0 through R7)
- **Size**: 2 bytes
- **Machine Cycles**: 1
- **Affected Flags**: None
- **Logic**: The 8-bit literal is loaded into the selected working register `Rn`.

#### 2.1.3 `MOV direct, #data` — Load Internal RAM or SFR with 8-bit Constant
- **Opcode**: `75H`
- **Size**: 3 bytes (1 opcode + 1 address byte + 1 data byte)
- **Machine Cycles**: 2
- **Affected Flags**: None
- **Logic**: The 8-bit literal is written into any internal RAM address (00H–7FH) or SFR address (80H–FFH).

#### 2.1.4 `MOV DPTR, #data16` — Load Data Pointer with 16-bit Constant
- **Opcode**: `90H`
- **Size**: 3 bytes (1 opcode + 2 data bytes — high byte first, then low byte)
- **Machine Cycles**: 2
- **Affected Flags**: None
- **Logic**: A 16-bit address/constant is loaded into the DPTR (DPH contains the high byte, DPL contains the low byte). This is **the only 16-bit constant-load instruction** in the 8051 base instruction set.

#### 2.1.5 `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — Code Memory Read (Indirect Constant Access)
- **Opcode**: `93H`
- **Size**: 1 byte
- **Machine Cycles**: 2
- **Logic**: Not a *load* in the strict sense, but it allows reading a **lookup-table constant** from program ROM. The 16-bit base is in `DPTR`, the 8-bit offset is in `A`, and the resulting byte is fetched from code memory and placed in `A`.

### 2.2 KTU Formula / Boundary Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 8-bit constant range | `00H – FFH` (0–255) | Single-byte load operations |
| 16-bit constant range | `0000H – FFFFH` (0–65,535) | DPTR load only |
| Program memory per `MOV A, #data` | 2 bytes | Code-size planning |
| Program memory per `MOV direct, #data` | 3 bytes | Code-size planning |
| Program memory per `MOV DPTR, #data16` | 3 bytes | Code-size planning |
| Instruction fetch time (12 MHz crystal) | 1 µs per machine cycle | Timing calculations |
| ASCII `'0'` constant | `30H` | Character I/O |
| ASCII `'A'` constant | `41H` | Character I/O |
| ASCII `'\n'` (newline) | `0AH` | Serial routines |
| ASCII `'\r'` (carriage return) | `0DH` | Serial routines |
| ASCII `'\0'` (null) | `00H` | String termination |
| Maximum immediate value for `ADD A, #data` | `127` if signed | Avoid PSW overflow surprises |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: The 8051's accumulator accepts both signed (-128 to +127) and unsigned (0 to 255) interpretations. When loading constants intended for arithmetic, always declare intent using `EQU` directives and comments to avoid validation loss.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Five-Instruction Limitation

The 8051 ISA restricts constant loads to five patterns because of the **8-bit internal data bus width**. Loading a 16-bit value into any register other than DPTR would require two sequential byte-loads and an internal concatenation — a design the original Intel engineers avoided to keep the instruction set orthogonal but compact. DPTR, being the only **16-bit register** in the base architecture, is the sole beneficiary of a single-instruction 16-bit load.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Spotlight**
> - **Embedded firmware initialization**: Setting port directions (`MOV P1, #0FFH`), configuring timers (`MOV TMOD, #01H`), enabling interrupts (`MOV IE, #82H`).
> - **LCD / Seven-Segment displays**: Sending ASCII or BCD command codes (`MOV A, #38H` for 8-bit LCD init).
> - **Lookup Tables for Sensor Calibration**: Storing calibration constants in code ROM and accessing via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.
> - **State Machines in Washing Machines / Microwave Ovens**: Initial state variables loaded at power-on reset.
> - **Communication Protocol Headers**: Loading `0AAH` (sync byte) or `0CCH` (frame delimiter) into the SBUF.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Loading `25H` into Accumulator

Consider the instruction: `MOV A, #25H`

**Step 1 — Assembly Translation**

The assembler reads the source line, recognizes `MOV` (mnemonic), `A` (destination operand — implicit accumulator), `#` (immediate flag), and `25H` (hexadecimal literal).

**Step 2 — Opcode Lookup**

The assembler consults its opcode table and retrieves `74H` as the opcode for `MOV A, #data`.

**Step 3 — Encoding into Machine Code**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0 (Program Memory location PC)} &= \texttt{74H} \quad \text{(Opcode)} \\
\text{Byte 1 (Program Memory location PC+1)} &= \texttt{25H} \quad \text{(Immediate data)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — CPU Fetch Phase**

The Program Counter (PC) points to the first byte. The CPU reads `74H` from code memory, decodes it via the instruction decoder, and recognizes "load immediate to A". The internal address generation logic increments PC to point to the next byte.

**Step 5 — Operand Fetch Phase**

Because the 8051 uses a **single-byte opcode + 1-byte immediate** format here, the operand fetch happens in the **same machine cycle** as the opcode fetch. The byte `25H` is latched directly into the instruction register's operand field.

**Step 6 — Execution Phase**

The operand byte `25H` is routed via the internal data bus to the accumulator register file. The accumulator is overwritten:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A}_{\text{before}} &= \text{XXH (previous value, irrelevant)} \\
\text{A}_{\text{after}}  &= \text{25H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Program Counter Update**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{new}} &= \text{PC}_{\text{old}} + 2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The PC advances by **2** because 2 bytes were consumed from program memory.

**Step 8 — Flag Verification**

The PSW register's flag bits (`CY`, `AC`, `F0`, `RS1`, `RS0`, `OV`, `P`) are **all unaffected**. The 8051 explicitly does not modify any flag on a `MOV` operation, regardless of the value loaded. This is a **guaranteed, non-destructive** operation.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Loading 16-bit `1234H` into DPTR

Consider the instruction: `MOV DPTR, #1234H`

**Step 1 — Opcode Retrieval**

The opcode for `MOV DPTR, #data16` is `90H` (a 1-byte opcode).

**Step 2 — Encoding into Machine Code**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0} &= \texttt{90H} \quad \text{(Opcode)} \\
\text{Byte 1} &= \texttt{12H} \quad \text{(High byte of constant — comes FIRST in memory)} \\
\text{Byte 2} &= \texttt{34H} \quad \text{(Low byte of constant — comes SECOND in memory)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Trap**: Intel stores multi-byte data in **little-endian / high-byte-first** order in program memory when the **immediate** is part of the instruction. (This is *opposite* to how the 8051 stores general 16-bit values in data memory, which is **little-endian / low-byte-first**.) Examiners frequently test this asymmetry.

**Step 3 — Fetch and Execute**

The opcode `90H` tells the CPU to perform a 16-bit immediate load. The CPU reads the next two bytes in sequence: first `12H` goes into the **DPH** (Data Pointer High) register, then `34H` goes into the **DPL** (Data Pointer Low) register.

**Step 4 — Final Register State**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPH} &= \text{12H} \\
\text{DPL} &= \text{34H} \\
\text{DPTR (logical 16-bit)} &= \text{1234H} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Memory Footprint**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes consumed from program ROM} &= 3 \\
\text{Machine cycles required} &= 2 \\
\text{Time at 12 MHz oscillator} &= 2 \, \mu s \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Correct Load Instruction

| Situation You Face | Correct Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Load a small number (0–255) into A for arithmetic | `MOV A, #data` | 2 | 1 | Fastest path; A is the implicit ALU operand |
| Load a small number into R0–R7 for loop counter | `MOV Rn, #data` | 2 | 1 | Rn is the implicit `@Rn` indirect address holder |
| Configure an SFR (P1, TMOD, SCON, IE) | `MOV direct, #data` | 3 | 2 | Direct addressing is the only way to access SFRs by name |
| Set up a base address for a lookup table | `MOV DPTR, #data16` | 3 | 2 | 16-bit base is required to span full 64 KB code memory |
| Initialize the stack pointer to a custom RAM top | `MOV SP, #data` (using direct form: `MOV 81H, #data`) | 3 | 2 | SP is SFR at address 81H |
| Load two separate bytes into DPH and DPL manually | `MOV DPH, #data` + `MOV DPL, #data` | 2 + 2 | 1 + 1 | Alternative to `MOV DPTR` when high and low come from different sources |
| Load a constant derived from another register's value | (Not possible via immediate) | — | — | Must use `MOV` from RAM or arithmetic to derive |

### 3.4 ASCII Constant Loading Derivation

To display the character `'K'` on an LCD, the CPU must transmit `4BH` (the ASCII code for `'K'`).

**Derivation of ASCII `'K'`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASCII `'K'`} &= \text{uppercase letters begin at `'A' = 41H'} \\
\text{Position of K} &= \text{K} - \text{A} = 10 \text{ (decimal)} \\
\text{ASCII `'K'`} &= 41H + 0AH = 4BH \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The assembly code becomes:

```
MOV   A,   #'K'      ;  ; A ← 4BH
MOV   SBUF, A         ;  ; Transmit to serial port
```

The assembler converts `'K'` to `4BH` automatically. The machine code stored in ROM is `74 4B`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Constant Loading Data Path

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal data path traversed when an immediate-load instruction is executed:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROM[Program ROM] --> PC[Program Counter]
    PC --> FETCH[Instruction Fetch Unit]
    FETCH --> DECODE[Instruction Decoder]
    DECODE --> IDENTIFY{Is opcode a MOV immediate instruction?}
    IDENTIFY -->|Yes| BYTES[Determine operand byte count 1 or 2]
    IDENTIFY -->|No| OTHER[Route to other instruction handler]
    BYTES --> ALUINTERNAL[Route data byte via internal data bus]
    ALUINTERNAL --> DEST[Destination Register Selector]
    DEST --> ACC[Accumulator A]
    DEST --> REGS[Register Bank R0 to R7]
    DEST --> RAM[Internal RAM 00H to 7FH]
    DEST --> SFR[SFR Area 80H to FFH]
    DEST --> DPTR[DPTR 16 bit DPH and DPL]
    DEST --> DONE[Operation complete PC incremented]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Load Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need to load a constant] --> Q1{Is the value 16 bit or larger?}
    Q1 -->|Yes 16 bit| DPTR1[Use MOV DPTR data16]
    Q1 -->|No 8 bit| Q2{Destination is Accumulator A}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ALOAD[Use MOV A data]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Destination is R0 to R7}
    Q3 -->|Yes| RLOAD[Use MOV Rn data]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Destination is SFR or RAM byte}
    Q4 -->|Yes| DLOAD[Use MOV direct data]
    Q4 -->|No| ERR[Re-evaluate destination]
    DPTR1 --> DONE[Encoding complete]
    ALOAD --> DONE
    RLOAD --> DONE
    DLOAD --> DONE
    ERR --> DONE
```

### 4.3 Memory Map — Where Loaded Constants Land

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CODE[Program Memory 0000H to FFFFH]
        OP1[Opcode 74H]
        OP2[Opcode 78H to 7FH]
        OP3[Opcode 75H]
        OP4[Opcode 90H]
    end
    subgraph DATAMEM[Internal Data Memory 00H to FFH]
        R0LOC[Register Bank 00H to 1FH]
        BITLOC[Bit Addressable 20H to 2FH]
        GENRAM[General Purpose 30H to 7FH]
        SFRLOC[SFR Area 80H to FFH]
    end
    OP1 --> ACCDEST[Accumulates into SFR E0H]
    OP2 --> RDEST[Loads into R0 to R7 at 00H to 1FH]
    OP3 --> DDEST[Loads into any direct address 00H to FFH]
    OP4 --> DPRDEST[Loads into DPTR 82H and 83H]
    ACCDEST --> DATAMEM
    RDEST --> DATAMEM
    DDEST --> DATAMEM
    DPRDEST --> DATAMEM
```

### 4.4 Timing Diagram — Instruction Fetch and Execute Cycle

```mermaid
gantt
    title Machine Cycle Breakdown for MOV A, data
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Machine Cycle 1
    Fetch Opcode 74H        :a1, 0, 1
    Fetch Immediate data    :a2, 0, 1
    section Machine Cycle 2
    Idle or Next Instruction :a3, 1, 2
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*What is meant by "loading a constant" in the context of the 8051 microcontroller? List the different instructions used to load 8-bit and 16-bit constants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Loading a constant in 8051 assembly language refers to the operation of transferring a fixed numerical value (an *immediate operand*) directly into a CPU register, SFR, or internal RAM location using the **immediate addressing mode**. The value is embedded within the instruction itself and is not fetched from a memory address.

The 8051 provides the following instructions:

| Instruction | Purpose | Size |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, #data` | Load 8-bit constant into accumulator | 2 bytes |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | Load 8-bit constant into R0–R7 | 2 bytes |
| `MOV direct, #data` | Load 8-bit constant into RAM/SFR | 3 bytes |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | Load 16-bit constant into DPTR | 3 bytes |

The `#` symbol before the value signals the assembler that the operand is a literal constant.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of the `#` symbol in 8051 assembly language. What happens if it is omitted in a `MOV` instruction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `#` (hash) symbol is the **immediate addressing mode indicator** in 8051 assembly language. It informs the assembler that the operand following it is a **literal numerical value**, not a memory address or a label.

**Example:**

```
MOV A, #25H     ; Load constant 25H into A  (correct)
MOV A,  25H     ; Copy CONTENTS of RAM address 25H into A  (different operation)
```

If the `#` is **omitted**:
1. The assembler interprets the operand as a **direct memory address** in the internal data memory.
2. The CPU will perform a **memory read** from that address and copy its contents into the destination.
3. If the address is not a valid RAM/SFR location, the behavior is **undefined** and may corrupt system state.
4. For 8051 variants, the assembler may issue a **syntax error** or warning at compile time, but for valid addresses, it will silently produce incorrect logic.

Hence, `#` is **mandatory** for any true "load constant" operation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] — (7 marks)**
*Explain the four major instructions used to load 8-bit constants in the 8051 microcontroller. For each, give the opcode, instruction size, and one example use case.*

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 provides four instructions for loading 8-bit constants:

**1. `MOV A, #data` (7 marks breakdown begins)**

- **Opcode**: `74H`
- **Instruction size**: 2 bytes
- **Example**: `MOV A, #0F0H` — Loads the constant `F0H` into the accumulator.
- **Use case**: Preparing the accumulator as an operand for an arithmetic or logical operation, such as masking bits with `ANL A, #data` or rotating with `RL A`.

**2. `MOV Rn, #data`**

- **Opcode**: `78H` (R0), `79H` (R1), …, `7FH` (R7)
- **Instruction size**: 2 bytes
- **Example**: `MOV R0, #50H` — Loads `50H` into register R0.
- **Use case**: Initializing a loop counter before a `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) loop.

**3. `MOV direct, #data`**

- **Opcode**: `75H`
- **Instruction size**: 3 bytes
- **Example**: `MOV P1, #0FFH` — Configures all 8 pins of Port 1 as inputs (writing 1s to a port bit configures it as input in 8051 quasi-bidirectional I/O).
- **Use case**: Configuring SFRs (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #01H` to set Timer 0 in 16-bit mode) or initializing internal RAM variables.

**4. `MOV @R0, #data` (Indirect Immediate Load)**

- **Opcode**: `76H` (for `@R0`) and `77H` (for `@R1`)
- **Instruction size**: 2 bytes
- **Example**: `MOV R0, #30H` followed by `MOV @R0, #0AAH` — Stores `AAH` into RAM address `30H` using R0 as a pointer.
- **Use case**: Filling arrays or buffers where the destination address is computed dynamically.

**Summary Table:**

| Instruction | Opcode | Bytes | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, #data` | 74H | 2 | ALU prep |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | 78H–7FH | 2 | Counters, pointers |
| `MOV direct, #data` | 75H | 3 | SFR config |
| `MOV @Ri, #data` | 76H/77H | 2 | Array fill |

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] — (7 marks)**
*Write an 8051 assembly program to load the constant `0AAH` into the accumulator, store it into internal RAM location `30H`, then load `1234H` into DPTR. Show the final register state and the machine code generated for each instruction.*

**Model Solution:**

**Assembly Source Code:**

```
ORG     0000H           ; Program starts at reset vector
        MOV     A, #0AAH     ; Load 0AAH into A
        MOV     30H, A       ; Copy A into RAM[30H]
        MOV     DPTR, #1234H ; Load 16-bit constant 1234H into DPTR
HERE:   SJMP    HERE         ; Infinite loop to halt
        END
```

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

**Instruction 1: `MOV A, #0AAH`**
- Opcode: `74H`, Operand: `AAH`
- Machine code stored at ROM[0000H]: `74 AA`
- Bytes consumed: 2
- After execution: `A = AAH`

**Instruction 2: `MOV 30H, A`**
- Opcode: `F5H`, Operand (address): `30H`
- Machine code stored at ROM[0002H]: `F5 30`
- Bytes consumed: 2
- After execution: Internal RAM[30H] = `AAH`, A remains `AAH`

**Instruction 3: `MOV DPTR, #1234H`**
- Opcode: `90H`, Operands: `12H` (high byte first), `34H` (low byte)
- Machine code stored at ROM[0004H]: `90 12 34`
- Bytes consumed: 3
- After execution: `DPH = 12H`, `DPL = 34H`, logical `DPTR = 1234H`

**Instruction 4: `SJMP HERE`**
- Opcode: `80H`, Operand: relative offset `FEH` (jump back -2 from next PC)
- Machine code stored at ROM[0007H]: `80 FE`

**Final Register State:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulator A} &= \text{AAH} \\
\text{Internal RAM address 30H} &= \text{AAH} \\
\text{DPH} &= \text{12H} \\
\text{DPL} &= \text{34H} \\
\text{DPTR} &= \text{1234H} \\
\text{PC} &= \text{0009H} \quad \text{(continuously executing SJMP loop)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**ROM Hex Dump:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROM[0000H]} &= \text{74} \\
\text{ROM[0001H]} &= \text{AA} \\
\text{ROM[0002H]} &= \text{F5} \\
\text{ROM[0003H]} &= \text{30} \\
\text{ROM[0004H]} &= \text{90} \\
\text{ROM[0005H]} &= \text{12} \\
\text{ROM[0006H]} &= \text{34} \\
\text{ROM[0007H]} &= \text{80} \\
\text{ROM[0008H]} &= \text{FE} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] — (7 marks)**
*Explain the instruction `MOV DPTR, #data16` in detail. Why is it the only 16-bit constant load instruction in the 8051? Provide two engineering use cases.*

**Model Solution:**

**Instruction Format:**

```
MOV DPTR, #data16
```

- **Opcode**: `90H` (1 byte)
- **Operands**: 16-bit immediate constant (2 bytes — high byte first, then low byte)
- **Total instruction size**: 3 bytes
- **Machine cycles**: 2
- **Affected flags**: None
- **Encoding example** for `MOV DPTR, #4500H` → machine code `90 45 00`

**Internal Operation:**

The 8051's instruction decoder recognizes opcode `90H` and routes the next two bytes to the DPTR register pair: DPH (SFR address 83H) receives the high byte, and DPL (SFR address 82H) receives the low byte. After execution, DPTR logically represents a 16-bit address ranging from `0000H` to `FFFFH`.

**Why is it the only 16-bit constant load instruction?**

1. The 8051 base architecture has only **one 16-bit register** — the DPTR. All other registers (A, B, R0–R7, SP) are 8-bit wide. The 8-bit internal data bus and 8-bit ALU can only natively operate on 8-bit quantities.
2. Loading 16 bits into an 8-bit register would require **two instructions** to be concatenated (e.g., `MOV R0, #HIGH(val)` followed by `MOV R1, #LOW(val)`), which is inefficient.
3. DPTR was specifically designed as a **16-bit pointer** to address the 64 KB external data memory space and to serve as a base register for `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` code-memory reads and `MOVX A, @DPTR` external data reads.
4. Hence, the designers provided a single 3-byte instruction that loads both bytes in one atomic operation, optimized for the most common use case: **table lookup and external memory access**.

**Engineering Use Cases:**

**Use Case 1: Lookup Table for Sine Wave Generation**
In digital signal generation (e.g., a function generator), a 256-entry sine table is stored in code ROM. The DPTR is initialized to the table's base address:
```
MOV     DPTR, #SINE_TABLE   ; 16-bit base address of sine table
MOVC    A, @A+DPTR          ; A now holds sine[index]
```
Here, `#SINE_TABLE` is resolved by the assembler to its 16-bit ROM address (e.g., `#0200H`), and `MOV DPTR` loads it in a single instruction.

**Use Case 2: External Data Memory Access**
To read from an external RAM chip at address `4000H`:
```
MOV     DPTR, #4000H
MOVX    A, @DPTR            ; Read byte from external RAM[4000H]
```
The 16-bit load is essential because the 8051 can address up to 64 KB of external data memory.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] — (7 marks)**
*Compare the instructions `MOV A, #data` and `MOV direct, #data` with respect to opcode, instruction size, addressing mode, number of machine cycles, and a real-time embedded application scenario where each is preferred.*

**Model Solution:**

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Feature | `MOV A, #data` | `MOV direct, #data` |
|---|---|---|
| Opcode | `74H` | `75H` |
| Addressing mode (operand) | Immediate (1 byte) | Immediate data + Direct address (1+1) |
| Instruction size | 2 bytes | 3 bytes |
| Machine cycles | 1 | 2 |
| Destination | Accumulator A (SFR E0H) | Any direct address (00H–FFH) |
| Flag effect | None | None |
| Destination encoding | Implicit (no address byte) | Explicit (1 address byte) |
| Example | `MOV A, #55H` → `74 55` | `MOV 30H, #55H` → `75 30 55` |

**Detailed Analysis:**

- **`MOV A, #data`** is the most efficient immediate load because the accumulator has an **implicit encoding** in the opcode — no additional byte is needed to specify the destination. This makes it 1 cycle faster and 1 byte shorter than the general-purpose direct form.

- **`MOV direct, #data`** requires an **extra byte for the destination address**, making it 3 bytes total. However, it can write to **any** RAM location or SFR, not just the accumulator. This generality is essential for peripheral configuration.

**Real-Time Embedded Application Scenarios:**

**Scenario 1 — `MOV A, #data` is preferred:**

In a **fast bit-manipulation routine** for toggling bits during high-speed serial communication, every microsecond matters. Example:
```
MOV     A, #80H       ; 1 cycle — load mask for bit 7
ANL     P1, A          ; Clear all bits of P1 except bit 7 (wait, this clears - reverse logic)
ORL     P1, A          ; Set bit 7 of P1
```
Using `MOV A, #data` saves 1 byte and 1 cycle per mask load compared to `MOV 0E0H, #data` (the explicit form), which is critical in tight ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) loops running every few microseconds.

**Scenario 2 — `MOV direct, #data` is preferred:**

During **system initialization** at power-on, the firmware must configure multiple SFRs to set up timers, serial port, and interrupts. Example:
```
ORG     0000H
        MOV     TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0 in 16-bit mode
        MOV     SCON, #50H   ; Serial port mode 1
        MOV     IE,   #82H   ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        MOV     TCON, #10H   ; Start Timer 0
```
Here, writing directly to SFRs (TMOD, SCON, IE, TCON) by their symbolic names (which the assembler translates to their direct addresses) is the **only way** to configure them. The accumulator-first pattern would be slower and would require a temporary A register swap. The `MOV direct, #data` form is therefore the canonical initialization idiom in 8051 firmware.

**Performance Trade-off Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialization phase:} \quad & \text{Code size and clarity matter more than speed} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Use } \texttt{MOV direct, \#data} \\
\text{ISR / hot loop phase:} \quad & \text{Speed and code density matter more} \\
& \Rightarrow \text{Use } \texttt{MOV A, \#data} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Constant-Loading Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol (–1 to –2 marks)**: Writing `MOV A, 25H` instead of `MOV A, #25H` is a **fatal conceptual error**. The first loads the *contents* of address 25H; the second loads the *value* 25H.
> 2. **Wrong byte order in DPTR encoding (–1 mark)**: The high byte is stored first in program memory, e.g., `MOV DPTR, #1234H` → `90 12 34`, **not** `90 34 12`. This is a **little-known asymmetry** the 8051 has with respect to its data memory.
> 3. **Misstating instruction size (–1 mark)**: `MOV A, #data` is **2 bytes**, not 1. Some students incorrectly say it is 1 byte.
> 4. **Confusing flag effects (–1 mark)**: `MOV` instructions **never** modify any PSW flag. If a question asks about flag behavior after a constant load, the correct answer is "no flags are affected."
> 5. **Wrong opcode for `MOV @Ri, #data` (–1 mark)**: This is `76H` (for `@R0`) and `77H` (for `@R1`) — **not** `74H`. The `74H` opcode is reserved for the accumulator.
> 6. **Forgetting `ORG` and `END` directives (–1 mark)**: KTU expects well-structured assembly programs. Always start with `ORG 0000H` and end with `END`.
> 7. **Failing to show machine code byte-by-byte (–1 to –2 marks)**: When a question asks for the machine code, **every byte** must be shown with its address. A summary like "machine code is `74AA`" without the per-byte breakdown loses marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition**: Loading a constant = transferring an immediate literal value into a register, SFR, or RAM using the **immediate addressing mode**.
> - **Four 8-bit load instructions**:
>   * `MOV A, #data` → opcode `74H`, 2 bytes, 1 cycle
>   * `MOV Rn, #data` → opcode `78H–7FH`, 2 bytes, 1 cycle
>   * `MOV direct, #data` → opcode `75H`, 3 bytes, 2 cycles
>   * `MOV @Ri, #data` → opcode `76H/77H`, 2 bytes, 1 cycle
> - **One 16-bit load instruction**:
>   * `MOV DPTR, #data16` → opcode `90H`, 3 bytes, 2 cycles, **high byte first** in program memory
> - **The `#` symbol** is mandatory to indicate an immediate constant — without it, the operand is treated as a memory address.
> - **`MOV` instructions never modify PSW flags.**
> - **The accumulator (A)** is the only register with implicit encoding — saves 1 byte and 1 cycle.
> - **DPTR** is the only 16-bit register, used for code-memory lookup tables and external data memory access.
> - **8-bit constant range**: `00H` to `FFH`. **16-bit constant range**: `0000H` to `FFFFH`.
> - **ASCII constants** use single quotes: `'A' = 41H`, `'0' = 30H`, `'\n' = 0AH`.
> - **Assembler directives**:
>   * `ORG addr` — set program counter origin
>   * `END` — mark end of source file
>   * `EQU` — assign symbolic name to a constant
>   * `DB` — define byte constant in code memory
> - **Look-up table access pattern**:
>   1. `MOV DPTR, #TABLE_BASE` — load 16-bit base
>   2. `MOV A, #INDEX` — load 8-bit offset
>   3. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — read byte from code ROM
> - **Code-size optimization tip**: When loading the same constant into both A and a direct location, do it via A first, then `MOV direct, A` (2+2 = 4 bytes total) **only if A is not needed afterwards**; otherwise, use two separate `MOV direct, #data` (3+3 = 6 bytes).
> - **Real-world initialization sequence** almost always uses `MOV direct, #data` for SFRs in the order: `TMOD`, `SCON`, `IE`, `TCON`, `IP`, `PCON`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECIT615 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conditional Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of the **ARM processor architecture** (a primary focus of Module 4 in the KTU 2024 scheme), **Conditional Execution** is a hardware feature where **every data-processing instruction can be made to execute only if a specified condition code (cond) evaluates to TRUE** based on the current state of the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register) flags** — namely, the Negative (N), Zero (Z), Carry (C), and Overflow (V) flags.

Unlike conventional processors (e.g., 8086, 8051) where conditional logic is implemented using **branch/jump instructions** that alter the Program Counter (PC), ARM embeds the condition directly into the **opcode** of the instruction itself. This eliminates the need for explicit branch instructions for simple if-then scenarios, drastically reducing branch penalties and pipeline flushes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> In the 32-bit ARM instruction set, the **top 4 bits [31:28]** of every instruction are reserved for the **condition field (cond)**. If the condition is FALSE, the instruction becomes a **NOP (No Operation)** without stalling the pipeline.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **security guard at a gate** who only allows entry to a person (instruction) carrying a specific **color-coded ticket** (condition code) that matches today's shift duty roster (CPSR flags).

- If the **ticket color matches** the roster → the person (instruction) **enters and acts** (executes).
- If the **ticket color does NOT match** → the person is **politely turned away** (instruction is skipped/NOP) — but the guard immediately checks the **next person** without any disruption to the line (no pipeline stall!).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "ticket" is the 4-bit `cond` field in the ARM instruction word, and the "roster" is the live state of N, Z, C, V flags in the CPSR.

### 1.3 Why Conditional Execution Matters in Engineering
- **Reduces code size**: Eliminates explicit branch instructions for short if-then constructs.
- **Improves pipeline efficiency**: Avoids branch prediction failures and pipeline flushes.
- **Enhances real-time performance**: Critical in embedded control systems (automotive ECUs, IoT devices, robotics).
- **Power efficiency**: Skipped instructions consume only minimal decode-cycle power — vital for **battery-operated ARM Cortex-M** devices.

### 1.4 Comparison with 8086 / 8051 Conditional Logic
| Feature | ARM | 8086 / 8051 |
|---|---|---|
| Method | Condition field embedded in opcode | Conditional Jump instructions (e.g., `JE`, `JZ`) |
| Pipeline Impact | None (no flush) | Branch penalty possible |
| Instruction Width | 32-bit (with cond) | 8/16-bit jumps |
| Flag Source | CPSR | FLAGS / PSW register |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of an ARM Instruction (with Condition Field)
A standard ARM data-processing instruction has the format:

$$
\text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{cond}}_{[31:28]} \;\; \underbrace{000}_{[27:25]} \;\; \underbrace{\text{opcode}}_{[24:21]} \;\; \underbrace{\text{S}}_{[20]} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Rn}}_{[19:16]} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Rd}}_{[15:12]} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Operand2}}_{[11:0]}
$$

- **`cond` [31:28]**: 4-bit condition code (16 possible conditions).
- **`S` [20]**: If set, updates CPSR flags based on result.

### 2.2 The Condition Code Logic
The ARM evaluates the **cond field** against the **CPSR flags** using the boolean relation for each condition. The instruction executes **only if the relation is TRUE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 16 condition codes are split into:
> - **15 usable conditions** (codes `0000` to `1110`)
> - **1 reserved / always-execute** (code `1111` — used for unconditional instructions or as `NV`)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet: ARM Condition Codes

| Cond Code | Mnemonic | Meaning (Test) | CPSR Flag Logic | Tested Flag Bits |
|:---------:|:--------:|:---------------|:----------------|:-----------------|
| `0000` | **EQ** | Equal | $Z = 1$ | Z |
| `0001` | **NE** | Not Equal | $Z = 0$ | Z |
| `0010` | **CS / HS** | Carry Set / Unsigned Higher or Same | $C = 1$ | C |
| `0011` | **CC / LO** | Carry Clear / Unsigned Lower | $C = 0$ | C |
| `0100` | **MI** | Minus / Negative | $N = 1$ | N |
| `0101` | **PL** | Plus / Positive or Zero | $N = 0$ | N |
| `0110` | **VS** | Overflow Set | $V = 1$ | V |
| `0111` | **VC** | Overflow Clear | $V = 0$ | V |
| `1000` | **HI** | Unsigned Higher | $C = 1 \;\text{AND}\; Z = 0$ | C, Z |
| `1001` | **LS** | Unsigned Lower or Same | $C = 0 \;\text{OR}\; Z = 1$ | C, Z |
| `1010` | **GE** | Signed Greater Than or Equal | $N = V$ | N, V |
| `1011` | **LT** | Signed Less Than | $N \neq V$ | N, V |
| `1100` | **GT** | Signed Greater Than | $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$ | Z, N, V |
| `1101` | **LE** | Signed Less Than or Equal | $Z = 1 \;\text{OR}\; N \neq V$ | Z, N, V |
| `1110` | **AL** | Always (unconditional default) | $1$ (always true) | — |
| `1111` | **NV** | Never / Reserved | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Confusing `HI` (unsigned higher) with `GT` (signed greater than). Remember: `HI` uses **C and Z** only (no sign concept), whereas `GT` uses **N, V, Z** for signed arithmetic.

### 2.4 Logical Breakdown of Conditional Execution
1. **Fetch Stage**: The 32-bit instruction is fetched from memory. Bits [31:28] are decoded.
2. **Decode Stage**: The condition code is compared with the current CPSR flags.
3. **Execution Decision**:
   - If the boolean test is **TRUE** → the ALU performs the operation, registers are written, and (if `S=1`) flags are updated.
   - If the boolean test is **FALSE** → the instruction is **discarded** (treated as a NOP). **No flags are updated**, and no registers are written.
4. **Pipeline Continues**: The next instruction in sequence is fetched immediately — no flush occurs.

### 2.5 Conditional vs. Unconditional Instructions
- The assembler defaults to the **`AL`** (always) condition when no condition suffix is specified.
- Example: `ADDEQ R0, R1, R2` → adds only if Z=1 (i.e., previous result was zero).
- Example: `ADD R0, R1, R2` → equivalent to `ADDAL R0, R1, R2` → always executes.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive Braking Systems (ABS)**: Branch-free conditional math reduces response time by up to **30%**.
- **ARM Cortex-M3/M4 (Thumb-2 subset)**: A limited form of conditional execution exists via the **IT (If-Then) block**, but full conditional execution is the hallmark of classic 32-bit ARM mode.
- **DSP Filtering**: Real-time FIR/IIR filters avoid branch mispredictions on data-dependent decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How CPSR Flags are Set for the GT (Signed Greater Than) Condition

The condition code **`GT`** is widely tested in KTU exams. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Signed comparison fundamentals.**
For two signed 32-bit integers $A$ and $B$, the result of $A - B$ determines the relationship:

$$
A > B \iff (A - B) > 0
$$

**Step 2 — Relating to CPSR flags.**
When the ALU computes $A - B$:

- The **N flag** = MSB of the result. If result $\geq 0$, $N = 0$; if result $< 0$, $N = 1$.
- The **Z flag** = 1 if result is exactly zero; else 0.
- The **V flag** = 1 if signed overflow occurred during subtraction; else 0.

**Step 3 — Condition for $A > B$ (strictly greater).**
The result must be **non-zero AND non-negative** under proper signed interpretation:

$$
A > B \iff Z = 0 \;\;\text{AND}\;\; (N = V)
$$

**Step 4 — Final GT boolean expression.**

$$
\text{GT}_{\text{execute}} = (Z = 0) \;\wedge\; (N = V)
$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic verification with examples.**

| $A$ | $B$ | $A-B$ (hex) | N | Z | V | N=V? | Z=0? | GT? |
|:---:|:---:|:-----------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:----:|:----:|:---:|
| 5 | 3 | 0x00000002 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | **TRUE** |
| -3 | -5 | 0x00000002 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | **TRUE** |
| 3 | 5 | 0xFFFFFFFE | 1 | 0 | 0 | ✗ | ✓ | FALSE |
| 0x7FFFFFFF | 1 | 0x7FFFFFFE | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ | ✓ | **TRUE** |
| 0x7FFFFFFF | -1 | 0x80000000 | 1 | 0 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | **TRUE** (overflow, but N=V means treated as positive) |

### 3.2 Worked Example: Conditional ADD Execution

**Scenario**: An ARM processor must add 10 to register `R0` **only if the previous operation produced a zero result** (Z = 1).

**Instruction Encoding** (conceptual):

$$
\texttt{ADDEQ R0, R0, \#10}
$$

- Opcode: `ADDEQ` → `cond = 0000` (EQ)
- Operation: $R_0 \leftarrow R_0 + 10$
- Condition check: $Z = 1$?

**Step-by-step execution:**

1. Fetch the 32-bit word from memory.
2. Decode bits [31:28] → `0000` (EQ).
3. Read CPSR → check Z flag.
4. **Case 1**: If Z = 1 → TRUE → ALU computes $R_0 + 10$, writes back to R0, S-bit update may occur.
5. **Case 2**: If Z = 0 → FALSE → Instruction becomes NOP. R0 unchanged. No flags updated.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Condition

| Engineering Scenario | Comparison Type | Recommended Cond Code | Flag Test |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Loop counter reached limit? | Unsigned equality | `EQ` | Z=1 |
| Error flag triggered? | Any non-zero | `NE` | Z=0 |
| Byte overflow during addition? | Carry | `CS` | C=1 |
| Result is negative? | Sign | `MI` | N=1 |
| Two unsigned magnitudes compared? | Unsigned > | `HI` | C=1 & Z=0 |
| Two signed values compared? | Signed > | `GT` | Z=0 & N=V |
| No comparison (default)? | Always | `AL` | always TRUE |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Conditional Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch from Memory] --> B[Decode 32-bit Instruction Word]
    B --> C{Extract cond field from bits 31 to 28}
    C --> D[Read CPSR Flags: N Z C V]
    D --> E{Evaluate Condition Code against CPSR}
    E -- TRUE --> F[Execute ALU Operation]
    F --> G[Write Result to Destination Register]
    G --> H{S bit = 1?}
    H -- YES --> I[Update CPSR Flags]
    H -- NO --> J[Preserve CPSR Flags]
    E -- FALSE --> K[Discard Instruction as NOP]
    K --> L[No Register Write / No Flag Update]
    I --> M[Fetch Next Instruction]
    J --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: ARM Condition Code Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Condition Code] --> Q1{What type of comparison?}
    Q1 -- Equality --> Q2{Test Z flag}
    Q2 -- Z equals 1 --> CondEQ[Use EQ]
    Q2 -- Z equals 0 --> CondNE[Use NE]
    Q1 -- Unsigned comparison --> Q3{Use C and Z flags}
    Q3 -- C equals 1 AND Z equals 0 --> CondHI[Use HI Higher]
    Q3 -- C equals 0 OR Z equals 1 --> CondLS[Use LS Lower or Same]
    Q3 -- C equals 1 --> CondCS[Use CS]
    Q3 -- C equals 0 --> CondCC[Use CC]
    Q1 -- Signed comparison --> Q4{Use N and V flags}
    Q4 -- N equals V --> CondGE[Use GE Greater or Equal]
    Q4 -- N not equal V --> CondLT[Use LT Less Than]
    Q4 -- Z equals 0 AND N equals V --> CondGT[Use GT Greater Than]
    Q4 -- Z equals 1 OR N not equal V --> CondLE[Use LE Less or Equal]
    Q1 -- Single flag test --> Q5{Which flag?}
    Q5 -- N equals 1 --> CondMI[Use MI Negative]
    Q5 -- N equals 0 --> CondPL[Use PL Positive]
    Q5 -- V equals 1 --> CondVS[Use VS Overflow Set]
    Q5 -- V equals 0 --> CondVC[Use VC Overflow Clear]
    Q1 -- Always execute --> CondAL[Use AL Default]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow: Conditional vs. Branch-Based Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BranchBased[8086 / 8051 Branch Model]
        A1[Compare A and B] --> B1{Flag Set?}
        B1 -- YES --> C1[Jump to THEN block]
        B1 -- NO --> D1[Fall through to ELSE]
        C1 --> E1[Pipeline Flush Occurs]
    end
    subgraph Conditional[ARM Conditional Model]
        A2[Compare A and B] --> B2{Flag Set?}
        B2 -- YES --> C2[Execute instruction in place]
        B2 -- NO --> D2[Instruction becomes NOP]
        C2 --> E2[Next instruction fetched immediately]
        D2 --> E2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The ARM model (right) avoids the pipeline flush (`E1`) seen in the branch model (left), which is the core reason for ARM's superior branch efficiency in tight control-flow code.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define conditional execution in ARM. Where is the condition field located in an ARM instruction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Conditional execution in ARM is a hardware feature that allows an instruction to execute only if a specified 4-bit condition code evaluates to TRUE against the current CPSR flags (N, Z, C, V). If the condition is FALSE, the instruction is treated as a NOP. The condition field is located in the **top 4 bits [31:28]** of the 32-bit ARM instruction word. This eliminates the need for explicit branch instructions for simple if-then logic and avoids pipeline flushes, improving execution efficiency.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Explain the difference between the condition codes `HI` and `GT` in ARM. When would you use each?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The condition code `HI` (Unsigned Higher) tests for $C = 1 \;\text{AND}\; Z = 0$, and is used for **unsigned comparisons** (e.g., comparing memory addresses, array indices, or byte counts where negative values are not meaningful). In contrast, `GT` (Signed Greater Than) tests for $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$, and is used for **signed comparisons** (e.g., comparing temperature readings, sensor deltas, or financial values that can be negative). Choosing the wrong condition code leads to incorrect program behavior on mixed-sign data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Value Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`

**a)** Explain the ARM condition codes in detail. List all 16 condition codes with their mnemonics, meanings, and the corresponding CPSR flag tests. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat diagram, explain how conditional execution is implemented in the ARM pipeline. Compare its advantages over the traditional branch-based approach used in 8086. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The 16 ARM condition codes occupy the **bits [31:28]** of every instruction. They determine whether the instruction executes based on the current N, Z, C, V flags of the CPSR register.

**Complete Condition Code Table:**

| Code [31:28] | Mnemonic | Meaning | Flag Test |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0000 | EQ | Equal | Z = 1 |
| 0001 | NE | Not Equal | Z = 0 |
| 0010 | CS / HS | Carry Set / Unsigned ≥ | C = 1 |
| 0011 | CC / LO | Carry Clear / Unsigned < | C = 0 |
| 0100 | MI | Negative | N = 1 |
| 0101 | PL | Positive or Zero | N = 0 |
| 0110 | VS | Overflow | V = 1 |
| 0111 | VC | No Overflow | V = 0 |
| 1000 | HI | Unsigned Higher | C = 1 AND Z = 0 |
| 1001 | LS | Unsigned Lower or Same | C = 0 OR Z = 1 |
| 1010 | GE | Signed ≥ | N = V |
| 1011 | LT | Signed < | N ≠ V |
| 1100 | GT | Signed > | Z = 0 AND N = V |
| 1101 | LE | Signed ≤ | Z = 1 OR N ≠ V |
| 1110 | AL | Always | TRUE |
| 1111 | NV | Reserved / Never | — |

The condition is evaluated by the decode stage. If FALSE, the instruction is dropped as NOP. The assembler defaults to `AL` (always) when no suffix is specified, ensuring backward compatibility with non-conditional code. **(7 marks distributed across table, definition, and execution note)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Diagram (refer Section 4.1 above)** — The ARM pipeline integrates a **condition evaluation unit** between the decode and execute stages. Bits [31:28] of the fetched instruction are routed to this unit, where they are tested against the live CPSR flags.

**Pipeline Flow:**

1. **Fetch**: 32-bit instruction retrieved from memory.
2. **Decode**: Opcode + cond field extracted. CPSR flags read.
3. **Condition Check**: Boolean test applied.
4. **Execute** (only if TRUE): ALU performs operation, result written to register file, flags optionally updated (if S-bit = 1).
5. **Discard** (if FALSE): Instruction treated as NOP. No register write, no flag change.

**Advantages over 8086 branch-based execution:**

| Aspect | ARM Conditional | 8086 Conditional Jump |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Pipeline Flush | **None** | Required (branch penalty) |
| Code Size | Smaller (no extra branch instruction) | Larger (separate jump) |
| Cycle Count | 1 cycle (skipped) | Multiple (flush + refetch) |
| Power Consumption | Lower (skipped instruction uses minimal power) | Higher |

In 8086, conditional logic requires instructions like `JE label` or `JNE label`, which modify the IP and cause pipeline stalls in pipelined variants. ARM's embedded condition eliminates this overhead, which is why ARM dominates in **real-time embedded systems** and **mobile SoCs**. **(7 marks distributed across diagram description, pipeline stages, comparison table, and conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**a)** Write an ARM assembly code snippet to find the **largest of three numbers** stored in registers R0, R1, and R2, using **only conditional execution** (no explicit branch instructions). Store the result in R3. Explain each line. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the boolean condition tested by the ARM condition code `LE` (Signed Less Than or Equal). Verify your derivation with two numerical examples covering boundary cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

```asm
        CMP     R0, R1          ; Compare R0 and R1, set CPSR flags
        MOVGT   R3, R0          ; If R0 > R1 (signed), move R0 to R3
        MOVLE   R3, R1          ; If R1 >= R0 (signed), move R1 to R3
        CMP     R3, R2          ; Compare current max (R3) with R2
        MOVGT   R3, R3          ; (no-op, R3 already > R2) - illustrative
        MOVLE   R3, R2          ; If R2 >= R3, update R3 with R2
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**

1. **`CMP R0, R1`**: Performs $R_0 - R_1$ internally, sets N, Z, C, V flags, discards the result. This sets up the CPSR for the next conditional instructions.
2. **`MOVGT R3, R0`**: Moves R0 into R3 **only if GT is true** (i.e., $Z = 0 \;\text{AND}\; N = V$, meaning signed R0 > R1). If FALSE, this becomes a NOP.
3. **`MOVLE R3, R1`**: Moves R1 into R3 **only if LE is true** (i.e., $Z = 1 \;\text{OR}\; N \neq V$, meaning signed R1 ≥ R0). Since exactly one of GT/LE must be true after a CMP, the larger of R0 and R1 ends up in R3.
4. **`CMP R3, R2`**: Sets flags for comparison between current max and R2.
5. **`MOVLE R3, R2`**: If R2 ≥ R3, R3 is updated with R2 (R2 is the new max).

**Final result**: R3 contains the largest of {R0, R1, R2}. **No branch instructions used** — purely conditional execution. **(7 marks for code correctness, comments, and explanation)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Definition.**
The condition `LE` (Less Than or Equal) for signed integers $A$ and $B$ tests:

$$
A \le B
$$

**Step 2 — Subtraction approach.**
The ALU computes $A - B$ and sets flags:

- $Z = 1$ if $A = B$ (equal)
- $N \neq V$ if $A - B$ produced a negative result (signed less than)

**Step 3 — Boolean derivation.**

$$
\text{LE}_{\text{execute}} = (A = B) \;\vee\; (A < B) = (Z = 1) \;\vee\; (N \neq V)
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with numerical examples.**

**Example 1: Boundary case $A = B = 5$**
- $A - B = 0$ → $N = 0$, $Z = 1$, $V = 0$
- $Z = 1$ → first clause TRUE → LE = TRUE ✓ (since 5 ≤ 5 is true)

**Example 2: Boundary case $A = -5, B = -5$**
- $A - B = 0$ → $N = 0$, $Z = 1$, $V = 0$
- $Z = 1$ → first clause TRUE → LE = TRUE ✓

**Example 3: Signed less than, $A = 3, B = 7$**
- $A - B = -4$ (0xFFFFFFFC) → $N = 1$, $Z = 0$, $V = 0$
- $Z = 0$ but $N \neq V$ (since $1 \neq 0$) → second clause TRUE → LE = TRUE ✓

**Example 4: Signed greater, $A = 7, B = 3$ (LE should be FALSE)**
- $A - B = 4$ → $N = 0$, $Z = 0$, $V = 0$
- $Z = 0$ AND $N = V$ (both 0) → both clauses FALSE → LE = FALSE ✓ **(7 marks for derivation steps, examples, and final boolean expression)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Loss Area 1 (–2 marks)**: Students often write the `LE` condition as "$N = 1$" only, confusing it with `MI` (minus). The correct form uses **N ≠ V** because a signed-overflow subtraction can yield $N = 0$ with a negative mathematical result.
> - **Common Loss Area 2 (–2 marks)**: Forgetting to update or mention the role of the **Z flag** in the `LE` test. LE requires $Z = 1 \;\text{OR}\; N \neq V$, not just $N \neq V$.
> - **Common Loss Area 3 (–1 mark)**: Not specifying the **bit position [31:28]** of the condition field — examiners expect this explicit location reference.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Conditional Execution** = instructions execute only if a 4-bit `cond` field matches the current CPSR flag state.
- The condition field resides in **bits [31:28]** of every 32-bit ARM instruction.
- **16 condition codes** exist: 15 usable + 1 reserved (`1111` = NV).
- Key flags tested: **N (Negative), Z (Zero), C (Carry), V (Overflow)**.
- **Unsigned comparisons** → use `HI`, `LS`, `CS`, `CC` (rely on C and Z only).
- **Signed comparisons** → use `GT`, `LT`, `GE`, `LE` (rely on N, V, Z).
- **Equality tests** → use `EQ` (Z=1) and `NE` (Z=0).
- **Sign-only tests** → use `MI` (N=1) and `PL` (N=0).
- **Overflow-only tests** → use `VS` (V=1) and `VC` (V=0).
- A **FALSE condition** makes the instruction a **NOP** — no register write, no flag update, **no pipeline flush**.
- `AL` (always execute) is the **default** when no condition suffix is given by the programmer.
- **Advantage over 8086/8051**: avoids branch penalty, reduces code size, lowers power consumption.
- The `S` bit in the instruction determines whether flags are updated after a conditional execution.
